experimental_stimulus: Moving dark rectangles of varying sizes and speeds (two-photon imaging); aIPg activation (behavioral silencing)
neural_activity: Tuned to medium-sized moving objects (15–30° angular width/height); prolonged calcium response to slow dark looming
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic inactivation during aIPg activation significantly decreases aggressive behaviors and increases inter-fly distance
ecological_context: Tracking conspecifics during close-range female aggression
summary: LC10a neurons detect medium-sized moving objects and are required for maintaining close-range aggression in females.

paper_title: Social state alters vision using three circuit mechanisms in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Moving dark rectangles of varying sizes (two-photon imaging)
neural_activity: Preferentially tuned to smaller moving objects (~4.5° width, 2° height); prolonged response to slow dark looming
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic inactivation during aIPg activation does not decrease aggressive behaviors
ecological_context: Detection of smaller moving objects at varying distances
summary: LC9 neurons detect small moving objects but are not required for aIPg-mediated female aggression.

paper_title: Social state alters vision using three circuit mechanisms in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Moving objects (behavioral silencing)
neural_activity: Tuned to small moving objects (~4.5° angular width/height)
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic inactivation during aIPg activation does not decrease aggressive behaviors
ecological_context: Detection of small moving objects during social interactions
summary: LC11 neurons detect small moving objects but are not required for aIPg-mediated female aggression.

paper_title: Social state alters vision using three circuit mechanisms in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: aIPg activation (calcium imaging/electrophysiology); optogenetic silencing
neural_activity: Excitatory input from aIPg; glutamatergic (presumed inhibitory); outputs primarily to lobula interneuron Li22
behavioral_consequence: Inactivation results in a prolonged decrease in aggression and touch behaviors
ecological_context: Centrifugal modulation of visual inputs to the brain during aggression
summary: IB112 is a centrifugal neuron that mediates visual gain modulation, required for sustained female aggression.

paper_title: Social state alters vision using three circuit mechanisms in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: aIPg activation (electrophysiology/calcium imaging); optogenetic silencing/activation
neural_activity: Excitatory response to aIPg stimulation; glutamatergic; receives input from LC10a
behavioral_consequence: Inhibition transiently decreases aggression during aIPg activation; no effect reported in absence of aIPg
ecological_context: Part of a toggle switch mechanism gating LC10a/LC10c transmission during aggression
summary: TuTuA_1 is excited by aIPg and inhibits LC10c, acting as one half of a visual pathway toggle switch.

paper_title: Social state alters vision using three circuit mechanisms in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: aIPg activation (electrophysiology/calcium imaging); optogenetic silencing/activation; visual target (males)
neural_activity: Inhibitory response to aIPg (disynaptic via SMP054); glutamatergic; activity decreases during male courtship pursuit
behavioral_consequence: Activation reduces aggression; inactivation increases aggression in the absence of aIPg activation
ecological_context: Toggle switch mechanism in females; gate visual processing during male courtship pursuit
summary: TuTuA_2 is inhibited by aIPg and inhibits LC10a, acting as the other half of the toggle switch that regulates social behavior in both sexes.

paper_title: Synaptic enrichment and dynamic regulation of the two opposing dopamine receptors within the same neurons
experimental_stimulus: Starvation (24 hours); Appetitive olfactory conditioning (Odor + Sucrose reward)
neural_activity: Dop2R protein levels significantly increased in PPL1-α3 terminals after starvation; Dop1R1 levels did not change
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of Dop2R in PPL1 neurons reduced 3-hour appetitive memory performance in T-maze assay
ecological_context: Starvation state-dependent modulation of appetitive behavior expression
summary: Starvation increases Dop2R expression in PPL1 terminals; Dop2R knockdown in these neurons impairs appetitive memory.

paper_title: Synaptic enrichment and dynamic regulation of the two opposing dopamine receptors within the same neurons
experimental_stimulus: Starvation (10-48 hours)
neural_activity: Dop1R1 protein levels significantly increased in PAM-γ5 terminals after starvation; Dop2R levels tended to decrease
ecological_context: Starvation-dependent regulation of presynaptic dopamine signaling balance
summary: Starvation shifts the Dop1R1/Dop2R balance in PAM terminals, suggesting regulation of appetitive behaviors.

paper_title: Synaptic enrichment and dynamic regulation of the two opposing dopamine receptors within the same neurons
neural_activity: Dop1R1 and Dop2R proteins enriched in MB lobes and associated with presynaptic active zones (Brp puncta)
summary: Dopamine receptors are presynaptically localized in Kenyon cell axon terminals.

paper_title: Connectomic reconstruction predicts visual features used for navigation
cell_type: MeTu neurons (MeTu1, MeTu2, MeTu3, MeTu4 classes)
experimental_stimulus: None (connectomic reconstruction)
neural_activity: Electron microscopy reconstruction identified four major classes (MeTu1-4) and ten subtypes based on morphology and synaptic connectivity. MeTu1 forms a homogeneous group. MeTu2 innervates the dorsal rim area (DRA) and connects to polarization-sensitive ER neurons. MeTu3 includes subtypes with chromatic input (MeTu3b/c) and polarization input (MeTu3a). MeTu4 receives widefield inputs from large interneurons. MeTu3c is unique in encoding elevation.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested
ecological_context: Part of the anterior visual pathway (AVP) which encodes visual features essential for navigation, including landmarks, skylight polarization, and the two-dimensional organization of the scene.
summary: Dense EM reconstruction classified MeTu neurons into four classes and ten subtypes based on medulla input and AOTU output connectivity, revealing distinct information channels for visual feature extraction.

paper_title: Connectomic reconstruction predicts visual features used for navigation
experimental_stimulus: Square-shaped dot stimuli projected on a screen
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging showed dendritic calcium activity in the superior bulb. Receptive fields are vertically elongated (76° height, 31° width) and tile the visual space horizontally. Responded to ipsilateral visual stimuli.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested
ecological_context: Vertical receptive fields are suitable for detecting visual landmarks or the azimuthal location of visual features for setting a heading during navigation.
summary: Physiological testing confirmed predicted receptive fields: ER4d neurons exhibit vertically elongated receptive fields, supporting a role in detecting vertical stimuli or azimuthal location.

paper_title: Connectomic reconstruction predicts visual features used for navigation
experimental_stimulus: Square-shaped dot stimuli projected on a screen
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging showed dendritic calcium activity in the superior bulb. Receptive fields are smaller (59° height, 30° width) and tile the visual space in two dimensions.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested
ecological_context: Two-dimensional receptive fields support processing the two-dimensional organization of the surrounding environment or tracking the position of celestial bodies.
summary: Physiological testing confirmed predicted receptive fields: ER2 neurons exhibit smaller, two-dimensionally tiled receptive fields, suitable for processing scene organization.

paper_title: Functional imaging and connectome analyses reveal organizing principles of taste circuits in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: sucrose, glucose, arabinose, NaCl, bitter tastants, water
neural_activity: Responds primarily to sucrose with variable responses across subjects; dose-dependent activation by sucrose; activity increases with starvation relative to refed controls.
ecological_context: Sweet taste processing; modulated by hunger state (feeding context).
summary: peafowl TPNs are sweet-responsive and indirectly connected via cholinergic LNs; their activity is modulated by hunger state (starvation increases response to sucrose).

paper_title: Functional imaging and connectome analyses reveal organizing principles of taste circuits in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: lobeline, other bitter tastants, NaCl, water
neural_activity: Responds to multiple bitter tastants with clear peaks at stimulus onset and removal; dose-dependent activation by lobeline; receives direct and indirect inputs from bitter GRNs.
ecological_context: Bitter taste processing (aversive compounds).
summary: mlSEZt TPNs are bitter-responsive, receiving direct and indirect inputs from bitter GRNs, and show dose-dependent activation by bitter tastants.

paper_title: Functional imaging and connectome analyses reveal organizing principles of taste circuits in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: NaCl, other salts, water, sucrose, bitter tastants
neural_activity: Responds primarily to salts (monovalent and divalent) with dose-dependent activation; minor response to water; salt response depends on IR76b; receives almost entirely direct inputs from IR94e GRNs.
ecological_context: Salt taste processing.
summary: GNG.SLP.T1 TPNs are salt-responsive and receive direct inputs from IR94e GRNs; salt responses are validated as genuine taste responses dependent on IR76b.

paper_title: Functional imaging and connectome analyses reveal organizing principles of taste circuits in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: water, 1 M sucrose, 50 mM CaCl2, bitter mixture, ATP
neural_activity: 62% singly tuned, 38% multiply tuned; low proportion respond to both sucrose and bitter; tuning predictable by direct GRN inputs; subsets (e.g., rattle) show no hunger modulation.
ecological_context: General taste processing; integration of sensory information; some subsets modulated by internal state.
summary: SEZ cholinergic LNs receive inputs from GRNs, are largely singly tuned, and serve as nodes for feedforward information transfer to TPNs.

paper_title: Functional imaging and connectome analyses reveal organizing principles of taste circuits in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: water, 1 M sucrose, 50 mM CaCl2, bitter mixture, ATP
neural_activity: 67% singly tuned, 33% multiply tuned; bitter and IR94e GRNs share connectivity with specific GABAergic LNs; low proportion respond to both sucrose and bitter.
ecological_context: General taste processing; inhibition and integration of sensory information.
summary: SEZ GABAergic LNs receive inputs from GRNs, are largely singly tuned, and show shared connectivity between bitter and IR94e GRNs.

paper_title: Functional imaging and connectome analyses reveal organizing principles of taste circuits in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: 1 M sucrose
neural_activity: Responds to sucrose; response not modulated by hunger state (no difference between starved and refed conditions).
ecological_context: Sweet taste processing; organizational node not sensitive to hunger state.
summary: rattle LNs are cholinergic, respond to sucrose, but are not modulated by hunger, distinguishing them from other sweet-responsive LNs.

paper_title: Functional imaging and connectome analyses reveal organizing principles of taste circuits in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: 1 M sucrose
neural_activity: Responds to sucrose; response increases with starvation relative to refed controls; postsynaptic inputs from sweet/water GRNs differ from rattle LNs.
ecological_context: Sweet taste processing; modulated by hunger state (feeding context).
summary: marge LNs are cholinergic, respond to sucrose, and show hunger modulation (increased response with starvation), passing modulation to downstream TPNs.

paper_title: Columnar cholinergic neurotransmission onto T5 cells of Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: OFF edges (dark edges) moving in 16 directions
neural_activity: Calcium responses (ΔF/F) monitored in T5c axonal terminals (LOP3). Knockdown of nAChRα4 or nAChRα5 increased responses to non-preferred directions. Knockdown of nAChRα7 or mAChR-B increased responses to preferred and/or null directions.
behavioral_consequence: Fly-on-ball optomotor response. Knockdown of nAChRα5 or nAChRα7 decreased angular velocity amplitude to horizontal OFF edges. Knockdown of nAChRα4 or mAChR-B increased angular velocity amplitude to horizontal OFF edges.
ecological_context: Motion vision is important for survival and navigation. Optomotor response involves corrective steering maneuvers in flight or walking trajectories.
techniques_used: ['Two-photon calcium imaging', 'Fly-on-ball optomotor response assay', 'RNAi knockdown', 'Confocal microscopy', 'Immunohistochemistry']
summary: T5 cells are primary OFF-motion sensing neurons. Specific AChR knockdowns altered T5 directional tuning (calcium responses) and the optomotor response (angular velocity), indicating nAChRα5/α7 are required for normal amplitude while α4/mAChR-B influence gain.

paper_title: Columnar cholinergic neurotransmission onto T5 cells of Drosophila
summary: Tm1, Tm2, and Tm4 are excitatory cholinergic input neurons to T5 primarily using nAChRα5 subunits at synapses. Tm9 is an excitatory cholinergic input neuron primarily using nAChRα7 subunits at synapses.

paper_title: Neural circuit mechanisms underlying context-specific halting in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson), silencing (GtACR1), and co-activation with walking neurons (P9, BPN); sugar gustatory receptor neuron (GRN) activation
neural_activity: GABAergic (inhibitory) SEZ neurons; strongly recruited by sugar GRN stimulation; inhibits descending neurons oDN1 and BDN2
behavioral_consequence: Drives halting during walking by suppressing forward walking components of P9 and BPN; silencing reduces stopping bouts when encountering sucrose
ecological_context: Recruited during feeding and foraging contexts to halt locomotion upon food detection
summary: FG neurons are GABAergic SEZ neurons that mediate halting during feeding by inhibiting forward walking-promotion nodes oDN1 and BDN2.

paper_title: Neural circuit mechanisms underlying context-specific halting in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson), silencing (GtACR1), and co-activation with walking neurons (P9, BPN); sugar GRN activation
neural_activity: GABAergic (inhibitory) SEZ neurons; weakly recruited by sugar GRN stimulation compared to FG; inhibits turning components of P9 pathway
behavioral_consequence: Drives halting interspersed with leg movements; suppresses forward turning induced by P9; silencing affects stopping duration and velocity near food
ecological_context: Recruited during feeding and foraging contexts to constrain forward and angular velocities following food encounter
summary: BB neurons are GABAergic SEZ neurons that mediate halting during feeding, specifically suppressing forward turning components of the walking circuit.

paper_title: Neural circuit mechanisms underlying context-specific halting in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson), silencing (GtACR1), and in vivo VNC imaging; grooming induction via powder
neural_activity: Excitatory (potentially cholinergic) ascending neurons with somata in VNC neuromeres; recruits inhibitory neurons in SEZ; drives resistance reflex-like muscle activity
behavioral_consequence: Drives strong, freeze-like halting by locking leg joints in stance phase and increasing resistance at leg joints; silencing causes destabilization during grooming
ecological_context: Recruited segment-specific manner during grooming to halt and stabilize the posture of non-grooming legs
techniques_used: ['optogenetics', 'in vivo calcium imaging', '3D leg kinematics', 'muscle imaging', 'behavioral assays']
summary: BRK neurons are ascending VNC neurons that drive active halting during grooming by inhibiting walking commands and increasing joint resistance.

paper_title: Neural circuit mechanisms underlying context-specific halting in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (ChrimsonR)
neural_activity: Strongly recruit FG neurons in SEZ; weakly recruit BB neurons in SEZ
behavioral_consequence: Activation leads to recruitment of halting pathways (FG) to induce stopping behavior
ecological_context: Mediate detection of sucrose during feeding and foraging
summary: Sugar GRNs are sensory neurons that recruit halting neurons (specifically FG) upon sucrose detection to initiate stopping.

paper_title: Neural circuit mechanisms underlying context-specific halting in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson) and silencing (GtACR1); in vivo calcium imaging
neural_activity: Activity strongly correlated with forward velocity but not angular velocity; inhibited by FG but not BB
behavioral_consequence: Activation drives walking initiation; silencing reduces forward velocity during spontaneous walking
ecological_context: Critical for spontaneous forward walking
summary: BDN2 is a descending neuron whose activity correlates with forward velocity and is a key node inhibited by halt neurons to reduce walking.

paper_title: Neural circuit mechanisms underlying context-specific halting in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson) and silencing (GtACR1)
neural_activity: Strongly inhibited by FG and BRK; recruited by P9 pathway
behavioral_consequence: Activation drives walking initiation; decapitated flies show robust forward walking upon activation
ecological_context: Mediates forward walking initiation
summary: oDN1 is a descending neuron that drives forward walking and is a specific target of FG-mediated halting.

paper_title: Comparative connectomics of the descending and ascending neurons of the Drosophila nervous system: stereotypy and sexual dimorphism
experimental_stimulus: Not experimentally tested in this study
neural_activity: Not experimentally tested in this study
behavioral_consequence: Not experimentally tested in this study
ecological_context: Not tested in this study
summary: DNp13 is identified as a sexually dimorphic descending neuron. Connectomic analysis reveals distinct downstream circuits in males (targeting TN1a) versus females (targeting abdominal motor neurons and IN06B neurons). Authors propose potential female wing phenotypes based on connectivity.

paper_title: Comparative connectomics of the descending and ascending neurons of the Drosophila nervous system: stereotypy and sexual dimorphism
experimental_stimulus: Not experimentally tested in this study
neural_activity: Not experimentally tested in this study
behavioral_consequence: Not experimentally tested in this study
ecological_context: Not tested in this study
summary: DNa12/aSP22 is a sexually dimorphic descending neuron. Connectomic analysis shows 8 downstream partners are shared between males and females, while others differ (e.g., sex-specific TN1c in males, abdominal targets in females). Sex-specific AN08B031 is identified as a connection partner.

paper_title: Comparative connectomics of the descending and ascending neurons of the Drosophila nervous system: stereotypy and sexual dimorphism
experimental_stimulus: Not experimentally tested in this study
neural_activity: Not experimentally tested in this study
behavioral_consequence: Not experimentally tested in this study
ecological_context: Not tested in this study
summary: DNx02 is identified as a sensory descending neuron. EM reconstruction shows strong output onto neck and haltere motor neurons, and a strong connection to ascending neuron AN06B025. Authors predict mechanosensory function and potential role in head grooming sequences based on connectivity.

paper_title: Comparative connectomics of the descending and ascending neurons of the Drosophila nervous system: stereotypy and sexual dimorphism
experimental_stimulus: Not experimentally tested in this study
neural_activity: Not experimentally tested in this study
behavioral_consequence: Not experimentally tested in this study
ecological_context: Not tested in this study
summary: AN hemilineage 08B contains sex-specific ascending neurons in both males (7 types) and females (6 types). Male types connect to male song circuit neurons (pMP2, pIP10, dPR1). Female types have distinct upstream circuits. Authors suggest male types may provide corollary discharge during song production.

paper_title: Comparative connectomics of the descending and ascending neurons of the Drosophila nervous system: stereotypy and sexual dimorphism
experimental_stimulus: Not experimentally tested in this study
neural_activity: Not experimentally tested in this study
behavioral_consequence: Not experimentally tested in this study
ecological_context: Not tested in this study
summary: oviDNs are identified as female-specific descending neurons. Six female types and one male type were defined via EM reconstruction. They target abdominal ganglion regions. Connectivity analysis suggests requirement for direct fast feedback via dimorphic ascending neurons.

paper_title: Asymmetric activity of NetrinB controls laterality of the Drosophila brain
experimental_stimulus: Olfactory aversive conditioning (3-octanol or 4-methylcyclohexanol paired with electric shock); Courtship conditioning (exposure to unreceptive mated females)
neural_activity: Not electrophysiologically measured. In wild-type (ASYM) flies, H-neurons project axons asymmetrically into the Right Asymmetrical Body (RAB). In netB or unc-5 loss-of-function, H-neurons project bilaterally to Left and Right ABs (SYM phenotype).
behavioral_consequence: Loss of H-neuron asymmetry (SYM phenotype) leads to specific impairment in Long-Term Memory (LTM) formation 24 h after spaced training in both olfactory aversive and courtship paradigms. Short-term memory (30 min to 3 h) and massed training memory are preserved.
ecological_context: Linked to cognitive functions and long-term memory; no specific natural ecological context explicitly discussed.
techniques_used: ['Behavioral assays (T-maze olfactory conditioning, Courtship suppression)', 'Confocal microscopy and immunostaining (Fas2, Nc82, 72A10-Gal4)', 'Genetic RNAi (per-GAL4, 72A10-Gal4)', 'Mutant analysis (netB, unc-5, netA)', 'Time-course pupal development analysis']
summary: H-neurons normally project asymmetrically to the Right Asymmetrical Body (RAB). Genetic disruption of the NetB pathway or unc-5 receptor causes bilateral projection (SYM phenotype). This loss of asymmetry specifically impairs long-term memory formation (24 h post-spaced training) in olfactory and courtship behaviors, while short-term memory remains intact.

paper_title: Asymmetric activity of NetrinB controls laterality of the Drosophila brain
experimental_stimulus: None (Developmental requirement)
neural_activity: Unilateral NetB ligand activity on the right side of the brain is required for H-neuron asymmetry. This activity is non-autonomous to the H-neurons themselves.
behavioral_consequence: Required for establishing H-neuron asymmetry; loss of activity leads to symmetric H-neuron projection and subsequent long-term memory defects.
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed.
summary: NetB ligand activity is required specifically on the right side within the LALv1A lineage (excluding H-neurons) to drive H-neuron asymmetry, which is necessary for long-term memory.

paper_title: Spatial readout of visual looming in the central brain of Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Dark looming, receding dark, bright looming, looming annulus, luminance-matched, small object motion, vertical bar motion
neural_activity: Prefers dark looming stimuli; responds to edge motion of looming annulus and darkening in luminance-matched stimuli; does not respond to receding dark or bright looming; responds to motion of non-looming objects
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation elicits escape take-off behavior (referenced from Wu et al., 2016)
ecological_context: Visual systems use spatial information to direct escape away from predators; LC6 acts as a nexus between feature detection and behavioral control
summary: LC6 neurons respond preferentially to dark looming and non-looming object motion. Optogenetic activation elicits escape take-off behavior. They project to the LC6 glomerulus in the central brain.

paper_title: Spatial readout of visual looming in the central brain of Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Visual looming stimuli (grid positions), panel of visual stimuli (looming, receding, annulus, etc.)
neural_activity: Bilateral neurons respond to looming across most of the stimulated ipsilateral hemifield with strongest responses near midline and equator; stimulus selectivity similar to LC6 population (responds to looming, bar, small object motion)
behavioral_consequence: Not tested in this study
ecological_context: Serves as a broad spatial readout summarizing LC6 population responses
summary: LC6G1 neurons are bilaterally projecting downstream targets of LC6 with broad visual receptive fields and stimulus selectivity similar to LC6.

paper_title: Spatial readout of visual looming in the central brain of Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Visual looming stimuli (grid positions), panel of visual stimuli, bilateral looming (ipsilateral and contralateral)
neural_activity: Ipsilateral neurons respond within a restricted portion of the visual field; enhanced selectivity for looming stimuli compared to non-looming; responses significantly reduced by simultaneous contralateral looming stimulation
behavioral_consequence: Not tested in this study
ecological_context: Spatially selective readout and contralateral suppression enhance detection of looming stimuli over confounding visual cues
summary: LC6G2 neurons are ipsilateral downstream targets of LC6 with restricted receptive fields, enhanced looming selectivity, and contralateral suppression.

paper_title: Distinct subpopulations of mechanosensory chordotonal organ neurons elicit grooming of the fruit fly antennae
experimental_stimulus: Antennal mechanical displacement (push and pull) and vibrations (40-800 Hz)
neural_activity: Increased GCaMP6f fluorescence in response to push and pull stimuli; no response to vibration frequencies tested (200 Hz)
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation elicits antennal grooming lasting the duration of the stimulus and wing flapping movements
ecological_context: Discussed in relation to detection of antennal vibrations and sustained displacements; implicated in flight modulation (wing beats) and grooming responses
summary: JO-C/E neurons respond to antennal push and pull with increased calcium signals. Optogenetic activation triggers antennal grooming and wing flapping behavior.

paper_title: Distinct subpopulations of mechanosensory chordotonal organ neurons elicit grooming of the fruit fly antennae
experimental_stimulus: Antennal mechanical displacement (push and pull) and vibrations (40-800 Hz)
neural_activity: No GCaMP6f response to push, pull, or vibration frequencies tested in immobilized flies; competence confirmed with KCl
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation elicits antennal grooming (shorter duration than JO-C/E) and backward locomotion (avoidance response)
ecological_context: Discussed in relation to grooming responses to antennal displacement and backward locomotor avoidance triggered by mechanical stimuli
summary: JO-F neurons do not respond to tested mechanical stimuli in immobilized flies. Optogenetic activation triggers antennal grooming and backward locomotion.

paper_title: A connectome of a learning and memory center in the adult Drosophila brain
experimental_stimulus: Red light optogenetic activation (Chrimson) during training or memory recall; paired with odor (3-octanol or 4-methylcyclohexanol)
neural_activity: Photostimulation triggers slow depolarization in postsynaptic MBON-α1 via direct synapses; electrophysiology confirms monosynaptic excitatory transmission
behavioral_consequence: Pairing activation with odor induces appetitive long-term memory; activation during memory recall suppresses the conditioned approach response
ecological_context: Formation of appetitive long-term memories of nutritional foods
techniques_used: ['FIBSEM reconstruction', 'Optogenetics (Chrimson)', 'Calcium imaging (GCaMP6s)', 'Whole-cell electrophysiology', 'Olfactory learning behavioral assay']
summary: PAM-α1 DANs form direct excitatory synapses onto MBON-α1 and are required for appetitive long-term memory formation; their activation during recall suppresses conditioned approach.

paper_title: A connectome of a learning and memory center in the adult Drosophila brain
experimental_stimulus: Red light optogenetic activation (Chrimson) during training or memory recall; paired with odor
neural_activity: Activation induces slow depolarization in MBONs; electrophysiology confirms monosynaptic excitatory transmission
behavioral_consequence: Pairing activation with odor induces aversive long-term memory; activation during recall suppresses conditioned avoidance; activation alone in untrained flies produces attraction
ecological_context: Formation of aversive long-term memories associated with punishment
techniques_used: ['FIBSEM reconstruction', 'Optogenetics (Chrimson)', 'Calcium imaging (GCaMP6s)', 'Whole-cell electrophysiology', 'Olfactory learning behavioral assay']
summary: PPL1-α3 DANs form direct excitatory synapses onto MBONs and mediate aversive long-term memory; activation during recall suppresses conditioned avoidance.

paper_title: A connectome of a learning and memory center in the adult Drosophila brain
experimental_stimulus: Photostimulation of PAM-α1 DANs
neural_activity: Slow depolarization and calcium rise in dendrites (peaking seconds after stimulation); response persists with spiking blockers indicating monosynaptic transmission
behavioral_consequence: DAN activation suppressing MBON activity correlates with suppression of memory recall behavior
ecological_context: Output of associative learning circuits guiding memory-based action selection
summary: MBON-α1 receives direct monosynaptic excitatory input from DANs; activation of this pathway is linked to suppression of memory recall.

paper_title: A connectome of a learning and memory center in the adult Drosophila brain
experimental_stimulus: None (Anatomical reconstruction only)
neural_activity: EM reconstruction confirms each KC makes multiple en passant synapses in each α lobe compartment
behavioral_consequence: Sparse activity encodes sensory information (established context); direct behavioral manipulation not tested in this paper
ecological_context: Encoding of sensory identity for associative learning
summary: 949 KCs reconstructed; each makes en passant synapses to MBONs and DANs in all α lobe compartments, supporting parallel processing.

paper_title: A connectome of a learning and memory center in the adult Drosophila brain
experimental_stimulus: None (Anatomical reconstruction only)
neural_activity: EM reconstruction shows axonal projections to α lobe compartments targeting other MBON dendrites and each other
behavioral_consequence: Postulated to mediate interaction between memories of opposing valence/time scales (based on prior work, not new data in this paper)
ecological_context: Coordinated expression of parallel memory modules
summary: Feedforward MBONs project from other lobes to α lobe; primarily target other MBONs, suggesting they modify output rather than sensory input.

paper_title: Visual projection neurons in the Drosophila lobula link feature detection to distinct behavioral programs
experimental_stimulus: Dark looming disk, bright looming disk, luminance-matched stimulus, small moving object
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging reveals robust calcium increases in response to dark looming stimuli. Selective for edge motion; smaller response to luminance-matched stimulus lacking edge motion.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation evokes jumping (takeoff) resembling natural avoidance behavior. Unilateral activation was not explicitly tested for turning in this paper, but bilateral activation triggers jumping.
ecological_context: Resembles natural avoidance behavior triggered by a predator-mimicking visual loom.
summary: LC6 neurons respond to dark looming stimuli with calcium increases and optogenetic activation evokes jumping behavior resembling natural avoidance.

paper_title: Visual projection neurons in the Drosophila lobula link feature detection to distinct behavioral programs
experimental_stimulus: Dark looming disk, bright looming disk, luminance-matched stimulus, small moving object
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging reveals robust calcium increases in response to dark looming stimuli. Selective for edge motion; smaller response to luminance-matched stimulus lacking edge motion.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation evokes backward walking resembling natural avoidance behavior. Unilateral activation induces aversive turning away from the activated side.
ecological_context: Resembles natural avoidance behavior triggered by a predator-mimicking visual loom.
summary: LC16 neurons respond to dark looming stimuli with calcium increases; activation evokes backward walking, and unilateral activation causes aversive turning.

paper_title: Visual projection neurons in the Drosophila lobula link feature detection to distinct behavioral programs
experimental_stimulus: Dark looming disk, bright looming disk, small moving object (9°x9° spot), moving bar (9°x72°)
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging shows no response to looming stimuli. Strong response to small moving object motion; moderate response to moving bar.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation did not result in a coherent behavioral response in the behavioral assay screen.
ecological_context: Encodes presence of small moving objects.
summary: LC11 neurons selectively respond to small moving object motion rather than looming, but activation does not trigger a coherent behavioral response.

paper_title: Visual projection neurons in the Drosophila lobula link feature detection to distinct behavioral programs
experimental_stimulus: Not tested in this paper
neural_activity: Anatomically characterized; axonal projections to AOTu preserve retinotopy for azimuthal positions. Layer patterns in lobula differ by subtype.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation of LC10a or LC10d evokes reaching behavior. Unilateral activation induces attractive turning towards the activated side.
ecological_context: Reaching resembles natural gap-climbing behavior and aggressive fly-fly interactions.
summary: LC10a and LC10d activation evokes reaching behavior; unilateral activation causes attractive turning towards the activated side.

paper_title: Visual projection neurons in the Drosophila lobula link feature detection to distinct behavioral programs
experimental_stimulus: Not tested in this paper
neural_activity: Not tested in this paper
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation drives highly penetrant jumping.
ecological_context: Associated with escape responses.
summary: LC4 activation drives jumping behavior.

paper_title: Visual projection neurons in the Drosophila lobula link feature detection to distinct behavioral programs
experimental_stimulus: Not tested in this paper
neural_activity: Not tested in this paper
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation drives highly penetrant jumping.
ecological_context: Associated with escape responses.
summary: LPLC1 activation drives jumping behavior.

paper_title: Visual projection neurons in the Drosophila lobula link feature detection to distinct behavioral programs
experimental_stimulus: Not tested in this paper
neural_activity: Not tested in this paper
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation elicits both jumping and backward walking behaviors.
ecological_context: Associated with avoidance responses.
summary: LPLC2 activation elicits jumping and backward walking.

paper_title: Neural dynamics for landmark orientation and angular path integration
cell_type: EBw.s neurons (ellipsoid body wedge neurons, R60D05-GAL4)
experimental_stimulus: Single vertical stripe, complex visual scenes (multiple features), two identical stripes, darkness; self-motion via walking on an air-supported ball in a virtual reality arena
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6f) revealed a localized 'bump' of activity in the ellipsoid body. The bump rotates to track visual landmark azimuth. In darkness, the bump tracks self-motion (angular path integration) but drifts over time. Activity persists when the fly stops walking in darkness (standing), maintaining orientation representation for >30s.
ecological_context: Navigation using a combination of visual landmarks and path integration
summary: EBw.s neurons exhibit a localized calcium activity bump in the ellipsoid body that tracks visual landmark orientation and self-motion for angular path integration, maintaining orientation representation during rest.

paper_title: The GABAergic anterior paired lateral neuron suppresses and is suppressed by olfactory learning
experimental_stimulus: Odors (methylcyclohexanol, 3-octanol), electric shock, and paired odor-shock conditioning
neural_activity: Increased intracellular calcium and neurotransmitter release detected by functional optical imaging (G-CaMP and synapto-pHluorin) upon odor or shock presentation; reduced calcium response specifically to the trained odor after conditioning (memory trace)
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of GABA synthesis (Gad-RNAi) in APL enhanced olfactory learning performance index; no difference in naive responses to odors or electric shock
ecological_context: Olfactory associative learning (laboratory conditioning paradigm)
techniques_used: ['Functional optical imaging (G-CaMP, synapto-pHluorin)', 'Immunohistochemistry (anti-GABA)', 'RNA interference (Gad-RNAi)', 'Olfactory classical conditioning (T-maze)', 'Clonal analysis (FLP-out)']
summary: The GABAergic APL neuron innervates mushroom bodies, responds to odors and shock with increased calcium and neurotransmitter release, and forms a memory trace of reduced calcium response to trained odors. Reducing GABA synthesis in APL enhances olfactory learning.

paper_title: From perception to valence: a pair of interneurons that assign positive valence to sweet sensation in Drosophila
cell_type: fox interneurons (pair of subesophageal zone interneurons)
experimental_stimulus: sweet taste and other gustatory stimuli
neural_activity: activity is necessary and sufficient to drive appetitive behaviors; transmit positive valence to mushroom body
behavioral_consequence: drive appetitive behaviors; override a tastant's intrinsic valence without impairing taste quality discrimination; support learned sugar responses
ecological_context: crucial to survival; guide flexible behaviors; support learned sugar responses
summary: fox interneurons impart positive valence to sweet taste, are necessary and sufficient to drive appetitive behaviors, and can override a tastant's intrinsic valence without impairing taste quality discrimination.

paper_title: From perception to valence: a pair of interneurons that assign positive valence to sweet sensation in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Not explicitly stated (receives signal from fox neurons)
neural_activity: Receive positive valence signal transmitted from fox neurons
behavioral_consequence: Mediate appetitive memory formation
ecological_context: Mediate appetitive memory formation (linked to survival and flexible behaviors)
summary: Specific dopaminergic neurons receive positive valence signals from fox neurons and mediate appetitive memory formation.

paper_title: Hunger Recruits a Parallel Circuit Encoding Alcohol Reward
experimental_stimulus: Food deprivation (hunger state); odor cue previously paired with ethanol
neural_activity: Hunger increased baseline activity in this neuron, making it more responsive to an odor cue previously paired with ethanol
behavioral_consequence: Required for ethanol seeking only when Drosophila are food-deprived; drives learned ethanol preference
ecological_context: Internal states like hunger can bias behavior; hunger recruits a parallel circuit that drives learned ethanol preference
techniques_used: ['intersectional genetic tools for manipulating neurons', 'genetic and connectome analyses']
summary: A single octopamine neuron shows increased baseline activity and odor responsiveness under hunger conditions; this neuron is required for ethanol seeking and drives learned ethanol preference only in food-deprived flies.

paper_title: Neuropeptide-mediated synaptic plasticity regulates context-dependent mating behaviors in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Social context (group-reared/naïve, single-reared, sexually experienced); Optogenetic activation of upstream SIFa neurons
neural_activity: Calcium-dependent activity (CaLexA/GCaMP) increases in optic lobe with social isolation and in abdominal ganglion with sexual experience. Synaptic input from SIFa neurons decreases with social isolation/sexual experience (GRASP).
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of SIFaR in these neurons disrupts both Longer-Mating-Duration (LMD) and Shorter-Mating-Duration (SMD) behaviors. Overexpression rescues SIFaR mutant lethality and restores interval timing behaviors.
ecological_context: Regulates male mating duration adaptations: LMD is believed to be an adaptation to male mating competition; SMD is believed to allow energy conservation by mating quickly.
summary: GAL424F06-expressing SIFaR neurons are necessary for LMD and SMD behaviors. Their synaptic connectivity with SIFa neurons and calcium activity change with social and sexual experience.

paper_title: Neuropeptide-mediated synaptic plasticity regulates context-dependent mating behaviors in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson + red light)
neural_activity: Activation induces prolonged elevation in intracellular Ca2+ concentrations in postsynaptic SIFaR neurons in the abdominal ganglion (AG) region.
behavioral_consequence: Essential for generating distinct interval timing behaviors (LMD/SMD) via the SIFa-SIFaR/Crz-CrzR neuropeptide relay pathway.
ecological_context: Integrates information to orchestrate multiple behaviors including interval timing and mating duration in response to internal states.
summary: SIFa neurons project to and activate SIFaR neurons in the AG, forming a relay pathway essential for context-dependent mating duration behaviors.

paper_title: Neuropeptide-mediated synaptic plasticity regulates context-dependent mating behaviors in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation of SIFa neurons; Genetic knockdown
neural_activity: Crz neurons in optic lobe (OL) and abdominal ganglion (AG) show sustained Ca2+ increase (activation) upon SIFa activation. Crz neurons in Super Intermediate Protocerebrum (SIP) show Ca2+ decrease (inhibition).
behavioral_consequence: Crz expression in SIFaR24F06 cells is required for both LMD and SMD. SIFaR expression in Crz neurons is only required for SMD.
ecological_context: Acts as a relay signal in the neuropeptide circuitry governing interval timing for mating behaviors.
summary: Crz neurons relay SIFa signaling to SIFaR and CrzR-expressing populations; their activity is differentially modulated by SIFa depending on brain region.

paper_title: Neuropeptide-mediated synaptic plasticity regulates context-dependent mating behaviors in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Genetic manipulation (CrzR-RNAi via repo-GAL4)
neural_activity: Receptor expression identified in glial population; no direct activity measured.
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of CrzR in glial population disrupts LMD but not SMD behaviors. Knockdown in oenocytes or neurons had no impact.
ecological_context: Critical component of the SIFa-SIFaR/Crz-CrzR relay pathway for regulating interval timing behaviors.
summary: Non-neuronal glial cells expressing CrzR are critical for regulating LMD behavior, highlighting a role for glia in neuropeptide relay.

paper_title: Serotonin selectively modulates visual responses of object motion detectors in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: visual stimuli including motion-defined bar and varying object velocity
neural_activity: Serotonin increases amplitude of calcium responses to visual stimuli; potentiates responses to motion-defined bar; tunes gain of responses to varying object velocity; no influence on contrast sensitivity; effects observed in presynaptic terminals of the glomerulus, not postsynaptic dendrites
summary: LC15 neurons express 5-HT1A and 5-HT1B receptors; serotonin application increases calcium response amplitude and potentiates motion-defined bar responses specifically in presynaptic terminals, tuning response gain to object velocity without affecting contrast sensitivity.

paper_title: Serotonin selectively modulates visual responses of object motion detectors in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: visual stimuli
neural_activity: Serotonin application does not influence visual responses
summary: LC12 neurons express inhibitory 5-HT1A and excitatory 5-HT2A receptors but show no change in visual responses following serotonin application; connectomics confirms they share neither presynaptic inputs nor postsynaptic outputs with LC15.

paper_title: Neural circuit mechanisms for steering control in walking Drosophila.
experimental_stimulus: spontaneous walking on spherical treadmill; lateralized fictive odor (antennal light); lateralized fictive heat (antennal light); chemogenetic stimulation of PEN1 neurons (ATP puff)
neural_activity: Whole-cell electrophysiology: firing rate increases predict steering; activity is more transient than DNa01; right-left firing rate difference linearly predicts rotational velocity; increased firing before compensatory turns after PEN1 stimulation; responds to lateralized stimuli even during immobility
behavioral_consequence: Unilateral optogenetic activation (ReaChR) causes ipsilateral rotational bias; bilateral silencing (GtACR1) reduces sideways speed and alters inner back leg stance duration during body rotation, though rotational speed remains unchanged
ecological_context: flexible and precise control of an organism’s orientation in space; orienting toward/attracting stimuli; head direction-based navigation
summary: DNa02 neurons predict high-gain, transient steering during walking and are recruited by both sensory stimuli and head direction system errors via right-left asymmetric activity.

paper_title: Neural circuit mechanisms for steering control in walking Drosophila.
experimental_stimulus: spontaneous walking on spherical treadmill; lateralized fictive odor/heat
neural_activity: Whole-cell electrophysiology: firing rate predicts steering with lower gain and more sustained dynamics than DNa02; right-left difference predicts steering
behavioral_consequence: Bilateral silencing (GtACR1) reduces sideways speed and alters inner back leg stance duration during body rotation
ecological_context: low-gain steering control; orientation stabilization
summary: DNa01 neurons predict low-gain, sustained steering and contribute to sideways movement control during walking.

paper_title: Neural circuit mechanisms for steering control in walking Drosophila.
experimental_stimulus: walking on spherical treadmill
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging: activity forms a 'bump' of activity that tracks heading direction; bump jumps following PEN1 stimulation
behavioral_consequence: Perturbation of EPG (via PEN1) causes bump jump, followed by compensatory behavioral turn to restore heading direction
ecological_context: working memory of the fly’s heading direction; navigation
summary: EPG neurons encode heading direction as a bump of activity and trigger compensatory turning when heading errors are detected.

paper_title: Neural circuit mechanisms for steering control in walking Drosophila.
experimental_stimulus: ATP puff (chemogenetic)
neural_activity: Chemogenetic activation (P2X2) causes depolarization and bump jump in EPG ensemble
behavioral_consequence: Induces head direction error, triggering compensatory steering to correct heading
ecological_context: angular velocity integration; heading maintenance
summary: PEN1 neurons relay angular velocity to EPG neurons and can be stimulated to induce heading errors that drive steering corrections.

paper_title: The foraging gene coordinates brain and heart networks to modulate socially cued interval timing in Drosophila
cell_type: Pdfr-positive neurons (c61-positive subset in Ellipsoid Body)
experimental_stimulus: Social context (group-reared vs. isolated); Mating duration assay (competitor-induced LMD)
neural_activity: Elevated CaLexA signals in group-reared males compared to isolated; foraging expression required for this activity
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of foraging in Pdfr-positive cells impairs Longer Mating Duration (LMD); genetic rescue restores LMD
ecological_context: Competitor-induced prolonged mating duration (socially cued interval timing)
techniques_used: ['RNAi knockdown (Pdfr2A-GAL4, c61-GAL4)', 'CaLexA imaging', 'LMD assay', 'Single-cell RNA sequencing (SCOPE)', 'Immunostaining']
summary: Pdfr-positive neurons, specifically a c61-positive subset in the Ellipsoid Body, require foraging expression to mediate Longer Mating Duration (LMD) behavior, with calcium signals modulated by social context (group vs. isolation).

paper_title: The foraging gene coordinates brain and heart networks to modulate socially cued interval timing in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Social context (group-housed vs. socially isolated)
neural_activity: Robust CaLexA signals in group-housed flies; signals abolished by knockdown of foraging or Pdfr in the heart
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of foraging or Pdfr in fru-positive heart cells disrupts LMD behavior
ecological_context: Integration of social cues with physiological states to regulate mating duration
techniques_used: ['RNAi knockdown (Hand-GAL4, fru-GAL4)', 'CaLexA imaging', 'LMD assay', 'Single-cell RNA sequencing (SCOPE)', 'Immunostaining']
summary: fru-positive heart cells express foraging and Pdfr, and their calcium dynamics are modulated by social context; this pathway is essential for LMD behavior.

paper_title: The foraging gene coordinates brain and heart networks to modulate socially cued interval timing in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: N/A (General neuronal function)
neural_activity: foraging gene expression detected; required for LMD
behavioral_consequence: Neuronal knockdown of foraging (elavc155, nSyb) disrupts LMD; Glial knockdown (repo) has no effect
ecological_context: N/A
summary: Foraging gene function in general neurons is indispensable for LMD behavior, whereas glial function is not required.

paper_title: The foraging gene coordinates brain and heart networks to modulate socially cued interval timing in Drosophila
cell_type: Memory-related brain neurons (Mushroom Body, Fan-shaped Body, Ellipsoid Body general, Pars Intercerebralis)
experimental_stimulus: Mating duration assay (LMD)
neural_activity: foraging gene expression detected but not required
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of foraging in MB, FB, EB (c547), or PI did not affect LMD behavior
ecological_context: N/A
summary: Foraging expression in key memory-related brain regions (MB, FB, EB general, PI) is dispensable for LMD generation, contrasting with the requirement in Pdfr-positive neurons.

paper_title: Proprioceptive limit detectors mediate sensorimotor control of the Drosophila leg
cell_type: CxHP8 (hair plate on the fly coxa)
experimental_stimulus: Limits of anterior leg movement (physical range of limb)
neural_activity: Detected limits of anterior leg movement (measured via calcium imaging in behaving flies)
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation elicited posterior postural reflexes; silencing altered the swing-to-stance transition during walking
techniques_used: ['Calcium imaging in behaving flies', 'Optogenetic activation', 'Optogenetic silencing', 'Connectome reconstruction']
summary: CxHP8 hair plate neurons detect limits of anterior leg movement; optogenetic activation elicits posterior postural reflexes, while silencing alters the swing-to-stance transition during walking.

paper_title: Molecular characterization of gustatory second-order neurons reveals integrative mechanisms of gustatory and metabolic information
experimental_stimulus: Fed vs. 24-hour starved metabolic states; Sweet (Gr64f) and Bitter (Gr66a) GRN inputs
neural_activity: Transcriptomic profiles differ between Gr64fG2N and Gr66aG2N populations. Starvation significantly influences gene expression. Leucokinin (Lk) expression significantly elevated in Gr66aG2N population under starvation. Genes involved in nervous system development and synapse organization differentially expressed.
behavioral_consequence: Molecular profile changes suggest adaptation to metabolic state impacting subsequent information processing. Specific behavioral role of SELK subset tested (see below).
ecological_context: Balancing food intake during starvation with avoidance of toxic substances. Transcriptomic changes suggest synaptic remodeling and stress response during starvation.
techniques_used: ['trans-Tango', 'Fluorescent Activated Cell Sorting (FACS)', 'Bulk RNAseq', 'Gene Ontology (GO) analysis']
summary: G2Ns receiving sweet or bitter inputs exhibit distinct molecular profiles that change with the fly's metabolic state (fed vs. starved), suggesting metabolic modulation of sensory processing.

paper_title: Molecular characterization of gustatory second-order neurons reveals integrative mechanisms of gustatory and metabolic information
cell_type: SELK neurons (Leucokinin-expressing neurons in the SEZ)
experimental_stimulus: Fed vs. 24-hour starved metabolic states; Sucrose (sweet), Caffeine (bitter), and Sucrose+Caffeine mixtures
neural_activity: Leucokinin (Lk) mRNA and protein expression increased significantly in starved flies compared to fed flies. Receive direct synaptic input from both Gr64fGRNs (sweet) and Gr66aGRNs (bitter) confirmed by trans-Tango, GRASP, BacTrace, and EM connectome (FAFB). No in vivo calcium imaging recorded (unsuccessful).
behavioral_consequence: Silencing Lk neurons (including SELK) did not affect PER response to sucrose alone. In PER assays with sucrose+Caffeine, silenced flies showed a greater decrease in response compared to controls at high caffeine concentrations. In flyPAD two-choice assays, silenced flies showed altered preference when choosing between high sucrose and high sucrose+Caffeine, indicating SELK involvement in integrating bitter and sweet information for feeding initiation.
ecological_context: Facilitates feeding initiation on sweet food laced with bitter compounds during starvation. Increased Leucokinin in starved flies promotes acceptance of potentially toxic but nutritious food.
techniques_used: ['qPCR', 'Immunohistochemistry', 'Trans-Tango', 'GRASP', 'BacTrace', 'Electron Microscopy (FAFB)', 'Proboscis Extension Response (PER)', 'flyPAD two-choice assay', 'Tetanus Toxin (TNT) silencing']
summary: SELK neurons integrate sweet and bitter gustatory inputs and upregulate Leucokinin expression during starvation, playing a role in initiating feeding on bitter-laced food when starved.

paper_title: Synaptic Targets of Circadian Clock Neurons Influence Core Clock Parameters
experimental_stimulus: Silencing via tetanus toxin (TNT) expression; hyper-excitation via Na+ channel NaChBac expression; light-dark cycles
neural_activity: Not directly measured; manipulation via genetic tools (TNT, NaChBac)
behavioral_consequence: Silencing via TNT shortens the free-running period; hyper-excitation via NaChBac results in period lengthening; under light-dark cycles, silencing causes lower daytime activity and higher daytime sleep
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed in relation to natural ecological contexts
techniques_used: ['tetanus toxin (TNT) expression', 'NaChBac expression', 'free-running period measurement', 'light-dark cycle experiments']
summary: SLP316 neurons are postsynaptic partners of Drosophila pacemaker neurons (sLNvs). Genetic manipulation of SLP316 neurons alters circadian period (shortening with silencing, lengthening with hyper-excitation) and affects daytime activity/sleep patterns under light-dark cycles.

paper_title: Synaptic Targets of Circadian Clock Neurons Influence Core Clock Parameters
experimental_stimulus: Not directly manipulated in this study
neural_activity: Not directly measured in this study
behavioral_consequence: Not directly tested; described as critical pacemakers in the circadian clock network
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed
summary: sLNvs serve as critical pacemakers in the Drosophila circadian clock network and form strong synaptic connections with SLP316 neurons.

paper_title: Driver lines for studying associative learning in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson, 627 nm LED); Odor presentation (Pentyl acetate/Ethyl lactate) paired with activation
neural_activity: Neurons activated via optogenetics; specific expression in labellum and tarsal sensory neurons (ascending and non-ascending segment-specific types)
behavioral_consequence: Formed robust appetitive memories in olfactory conditioning; reduced walking speed during activation; increased local search behavior (revisiting activation area); proboscis extension upon activation
ecological_context: Substitutes sugar reward in associative learning; encodes appetitive food-related taste information
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics (CsChrimson)', 'Olfactory conditioning arena', 'LED choice arena', 'Confocal microscopy', 'Tethered walking assay']
summary: SS87269 labels specific sugar sensory neurons. Optogenetic activation drives appetitive learning, reduced locomotion, local search, and proboscis extension, making it suitable for reward substitution.

paper_title: Driver lines for studying associative learning in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson, 627 nm LED); Odor presentation paired with activation
neural_activity: Neurons activated via optogenetics; labels stGRNs and/or labial sensory neurons
behavioral_consequence: Induced appetitive learning and reduced locomotion during activation; did not induce significant local search behaviors
ecological_context: Appetitive food-related taste processing
summary: SS88801 activation drives appetitive learning and reduced locomotion but lacks the local search behavior observed with SS87269.

paper_title: Driver lines for studying associative learning in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson, 627 nm LED); Odor presentation paired with activation
neural_activity: Neurons activated via optogenetics; expression in abdominal ganglion and body surface (class IV multidendritic neurons)
behavioral_consequence: Increased walking speed during activation; reduced probability of return at offset (avoidance); progressive immobility during inter-trial intervals; negative preference to paired odor after extended training
ecological_context: Conveys aversive/nociceptive signals; potential role in punishment pathways
summary: SS87278/SS87279 label abdominal nociceptive neurons. Activation induces aversive behaviors including increased speed, avoidance, and negative associative learning.

paper_title: Driver lines for studying associative learning in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson, 627 nm LED)
neural_activity: Neurons activated via optogenetics; labels a group of ascending neurons from body/legs to brain
behavioral_consequence: Robust avoidance of LED quadrants; backward walking, turning, increased speed, and jumping upon activation
ecological_context: Nociceptive signals from body and legs to brain
summary: SS01159 labels ascending nociceptive neurons. Activation triggers robust avoidance and characteristic nociceptive behaviors like jumping and turning.

paper_title: Driver lines for studying associative learning in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: None (Morphological characterization)
neural_activity: Morphological analysis via Confocal microscopy and MCFO; EM connectome matching
behavioral_consequence: None tested in this paper (behavioral function cited from previous work)
ecological_context: Memory-based actions (inferred from connectome and literature)
summary: MBON08 and MBON09 exhibit striking individuality and asymmetry. MB083C driver labels two cells per hemisphere; MBON09 is common (65% probability), MBON08 is rare and appears in only one hemisphere.

paper_title: Driver lines for studying associative learning in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: None (Connectivity analysis)
neural_activity: EM connectome analysis; integrates over 2,000 inputs from 9 types of MBONs; cholinergic output to reward DANs
behavioral_consequence: None tested in this paper
ecological_context: MB-extrinsic circuit integration (inferred)
summary: CRE011 (SS45245) shows the highest number of synaptic inputs from MBONs (2,000+ from 9 types) and connects cholinergically to reward DANs.

paper_title: Driver lines for studying associative learning in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: None (Connectivity analysis)
neural_activity: EM connectome analysis; receives inputs from glutamatergic MBONs; highest number of cholinergic connections to DANs
behavioral_consequence: None tested in this paper
ecological_context: Appetitive memory pathways (inferred)
summary: SMP108 (SS48900) receives inputs from multiple glutamatergic MBONs and makes the highest number of cholinergic connections to DANs involved in appetitive memory.

paper_title: Aminergic and peptidergic modulation of insulin-producing cells in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation of modulatory neurons (DANs, OANs, AstANs, LKNs, MSNs, etc.); baseline conditions
neural_activity: Heterogeneous responses across the population: excitation (depolarization, increased spiking), inhibition (hyperpolarization, reduced spiking), or unaffected. Heterogeneous receptor expression profiles identified via single-nucleus RNA-seq. Calcium influx observed during imaging, sometimes decoupled from spiking (subthreshold depolarization).
ecological_context: Regulation of metabolic homeostasis and insulin release tailored to changing internal metabolic demands
techniques_used: ['Single-nucleus RNA sequencing', 'Anatomical receptor mapping (T2A-GAL4)', 'Whole-cell patch-clamp recordings', 'In vivo widefield calcium imaging', 'Optogenetics']
summary: IPCs exhibit heterogeneous receptor expression and responses to modulatory inputs, allowing differential modulation of the population to regulate insulin release for metabolic homeostasis.

paper_title: Aminergic and peptidergic modulation of insulin-producing cells in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson)
neural_activity: Activation shifts IPC population activity towards excitation (strongest excitatory effect among tested lines)
ecological_context: Primes IPC system for higher metabolic demands
summary: Activation of DANs strongly excites the IPC population, suggesting a role in priming the system for metabolic demand.

paper_title: Aminergic and peptidergic modulation of insulin-producing cells in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson)
neural_activity: Activation shifts IPC population activity towards excitation (weaker than DANs)
ecological_context: Primes IPC system for metabolic demands
summary: Activation of OANs drives IPC excitation, contributing to the overall excitatory shift of the IPC population.

paper_title: Aminergic and peptidergic modulation of insulin-producing cells in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson)
neural_activity: Heterogeneous effects on IPCs: majority inhibited in patch-clamp, but calcium influx observed in majority during calcium imaging
ecological_context: Modulates IPC activity to regulate metabolism/feeding (inferred from IPC function)
summary: AstAN activation elicits heterogeneous responses in IPCs, inhibiting most via electrophysiology but causing calcium influx in imaging, likely due to receptor heterogeneity.

paper_title: Aminergic and peptidergic modulation of insulin-producing cells in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson)
neural_activity: Strong inhibition of IPC population activity (strongest inhibitory effect in patch-clamp)
ecological_context: Linked to starvation-induced suppression of sleep and metabolic homeostasis
summary: Activation of LKNs strongly inhibits IPCs, supporting their role in metabolic homeostasis and sleep regulation during starvation.

paper_title: Aminergic and peptidergic modulation of insulin-producing cells in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson)
neural_activity: Inhibits IPC population activity
ecological_context: Linked to mating-related food consumption and post-mating behaviors
summary: MSNs inhibit IPC activity, potentially facilitating increased food consumption and reducing re-mating drive in mated females.

paper_title: Convergent olfactory circuits for courtship in Drosophila revealed by ds-Tango
experimental_stimulus: Olfactory cues in the context of courtship and sex recognition
behavioral_consequence: Silencing this convergence node results in deficits in sex recognition of potential partners; required for proper courtship behavior
ecological_context: Male Drosophila melanogaster courtship and sex-specific behaviors
summary: Lateral horn output neurons serve as a convergence node for three sexually dimorphic olfactory pathways. Silencing these neurons causes deficits in sex recognition during courtship behavior.

paper_title: Neurons Underlying Aggression-Like Actions That Are Shared by Both Males and Females in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (617 nm red light, CsChrimson)
neural_activity: Activation of Chrimson-expressing neurons (spatially targeted DMD or whole-brain L320 line)
behavioral_consequence: Elicits wing threat, wing extension, thrusting, and alert stance in isolated males and females. Sexual dimorphism in habituation: male wing threat persists longer, females habituate faster. Actions occur without a target.
ecological_context: Actions resemble natural aggressive behaviors (wing threat elevation >45 degrees distinguishes from courtship). Aggression aids in securing territory, mates, or food.
summary: CL062 neurons, which do not express fruitless, mediate aggression-like actions (wing threat, thrusting, alert stance) in both male and female Drosophila when optogenetically activated, functioning independently of known dimorphic aggression circuits.

paper_title: Neurons Underlying Aggression-Like Actions That Are Shared by Both Males and Females in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (DMD, 617 nm light)
neural_activity: Activation of Chrimson-expressing neurons in L320-labeled LH clusters
behavioral_consequence: Did not elicit aggressive actions (wing threat, extension, lunging, or alert stance)
ecological_context: N/A
summary: Activation of LH neurons labeled by the L320 line did not drive the aggression-like behaviors observed with CL062 activation.

paper_title: Neurons Underlying Aggression-Like Actions That Are Shared by Both Males and Females in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (DMD, 617 nm light)
neural_activity: Activation of Chrimson-expressing neurons in L320-labeled PVLP clusters
behavioral_consequence: Did not elicit aggressive actions
ecological_context: N/A
summary: Activation of PVLP neurons labeled by the L320 line did not drive the aggression-like behaviors observed with CL062 activation.

paper_title: Heterogeneity in the projections and excitability of tyraminergic/octopaminergic neurons that innervate the Drosophila reproductive tract
cell_type: Tyraminergic/octopaminergic neurons (J39942-Gal4 subset, PC6 and PC7)
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (~470 nm LED) using ChR2-XXM::tdTomato
neural_activity: Stimulation followed by repetitive lateral oviduct contractions
behavioral_consequence: Repetitive lateral oviduct contractions observed in dissected preparations
ecological_context: Activities required for fertility (oviduct contractility)
summary: Optogenetic activation of the J39942-Gal4 labeled subset (PC6 and PC7) stimulates lateral oviduct contractions, indicating specific subsets within the posterior cluster can mediate this activity.

paper_title: Heterogeneity in the projections and excitability of tyraminergic/octopaminergic neurons that innervate the Drosophila reproductive tract
experimental_stimulus: Step current injections, Picrotoxin (100 μM), Ivermectin (1 μM)
neural_activity: PC1 more excitable than PC2 (more action potentials per current step); both tonically inhibited; Ivermectin affected PC1 excitability more than PC2
ecological_context: Innervate posterior common oviduct and anterior uterus
summary: PC1 and PC2 neurons are tonically inhibited but show differential intrinsic excitability and response to GluCl agonist activation, with PC1 being more excitable than PC2.

paper_title: Heterogeneity in the projections and excitability of tyraminergic/octopaminergic neurons that innervate the Drosophila reproductive tract
experimental_stimulus: Co-labeling with GluCl-MiMIC-Gal4
neural_activity: GluCl receptor expression detected at relatively low levels in the cluster
ecological_context: N/A
summary: GluCl appears expressed at relatively low levels in tyraminergic/octopaminergic neurons within the posterior cluster, suggesting indirect regulation of their excitability.

paper_title: Light and dopamine impact two circadian neurons to promote morning wakefulness
cell_type: ITP+ LNs (ITP+ sLNv and ITP+ LNd)
experimental_stimulus: Red light optogenetic activation (CsChrimson), dopamine perfusion, genetic inhibition (Kir2.1, TNT), Dop1R1 CRISPR/Cas9 knock-out
neural_activity: Dopamine perfusion increases cAMP levels (EPAC-H187 and cAMPFIRE sensors); response is mediated by Dop1R1. Dop1R1 mRNA levels cycle with a peak at ZT02 in 12:12 LD but not in constant darkness.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation increases activity and reduces sleep (p-wake increases, p-doze decreases) across the day. Inhibition or Dop1R1 knock-out decreases morning activity (ZT00-06) and increases sleep depth (p-doze increases). Inhibition also causes a small, statistically significant decrease in evening activity peak (with TNT).
ecological_context: Regulates locomotor activity under 12:12 light-dark conditions, specifically promoting wakefulness in the morning (ZT00-06). The paper notes this mimics the effect of light on morning wakefulness in humans.
techniques_used: ['Electron microscopy (NeuPrint, FlyWire)', 'Immunohistochemistry', 'Optogenetics', 'CRISPR/Cas9 mutagenesis', 'Ex vivo confocal imaging (cAMP sensors)', 'Locomotor activity monitoring (FlyBox, DAM)']
summary: ITP+ LNs (one sLNv and one LNd) sit at the top of the evening cell connectomic hierarchy. Dopamine increases their cAMP via Dop1R1, which peaks in the morning. Activation promotes wakefulness, while inhibition or Dop1R1 loss specifically reduces morning activity and sleep depth.

paper_title: Light and dopamine impact two circadian neurons to promote morning wakefulness
experimental_stimulus: Red light optogenetic activation (intersectional strategy: Clk856-GAL4 + Trissin-LexA + flipper)
neural_activity: Not explicitly measured in this study.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation alone did not change activity, sleep, p-wake, or p-doze. Inhibition combined with ITP+ LNs showed similar effects to ITP+ LN inhibition alone.
ecological_context: Part of the evening cell group, regulated by ITP+ LNs in the connectomic hierarchy.
summary: Trissin+ LNds are downstream of ITP+ LNs in the connectomic hierarchy. Activation of Trissin+ LNds alone does not alter activity or sleep, indicating they are not primary drivers of wakefulness compared to ITP+ LNs.

paper_title: Descending networks transform command signals into population motor control
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson, 21 μW laser) of neck connective
neural_activity: Recruits large population of GNG DNs (32 direct downstream partners); recruitment lost in headless animals
behavioral_consequence: Drives forwards walking in intact flies; drives abdomen contraction in headless flies
ecological_context: Locomotion (forwards walking)
summary: DNp09 activates a large downstream DN network in the brain; network recruitment is required for forwards walking, while isolated activation drives only simple movements.

paper_title: Descending networks transform command signals into population motor control
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson, 41.6 μW laser) of neck connective
neural_activity: Recruits GNG DNs (23 direct downstream partners); recruitment lost in headless animals
behavioral_consequence: Drives antennal grooming in intact flies; drives front leg approach in headless flies
ecological_context: Grooming (to remove debris from antennae)
summary: aDN2 recruits downstream DN populations for antennal grooming; network recruitment is necessary for the full behavior.

paper_title: Descending networks transform command signals into population motor control
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson, 21 μW laser) of neck connective
neural_activity: Recruits GNG DNs (14 direct downstream partners); recruitment persists in headless animals
behavioral_consequence: Drives backwards walking in intact and headless flies; behavior relies on active movement of hindlegs
ecological_context: Locomotion (backwards walking)
summary: MDN drives backwards walking without requiring downstream DN network recruitment in the brain.

paper_title: Descending networks transform command signals into population motor control
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation
neural_activity: Connects to 20 downstream DNs
behavioral_consequence: Drives turning in intact flies; lost in headless flies (replaced by front leg flexion)
ecological_context: Steering during locomotion
summary: DNb02 acts as a broadcaster requiring downstream DN recruitment for turning behaviors.

paper_title: Descending networks transform command signals into population motor control
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation
neural_activity: Connects to 29 downstream DNs
behavioral_consequence: Drives backing up/retreat in intact flies; lost in headless flies
ecological_context: Escape/Retreat
summary: DNp42 recruits downstream DNs to drive retreat behavior.

paper_title: Descending networks transform command signals into population motor control
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation
neural_activity: Connects to 26 downstream DNs
behavioral_consequence: Drives grooming in intact flies; uncoordinated front leg movements in headless flies
ecological_context: Grooming
summary: aDN1 recruits downstream DNs for grooming.

paper_title: Descending networks transform command signals into population motor control
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation
neural_activity: Connects to 25 downstream DNs
behavioral_consequence: Drives in-place turning in intact flies; lost in headless flies
ecological_context: Steering
summary: DNa01 recruits downstream DNs to drive turning.

paper_title: Descending networks transform command signals into population motor control
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation
neural_activity: Connects to 18 downstream DNs
behavioral_consequence: Drives turning in intact flies; lost in headless flies
ecological_context: Steering
summary: DNa02 recruits downstream DNs to drive turning.

paper_title: Descending networks transform command signals into population motor control
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation
neural_activity: No direct downstream DN partners
behavioral_consequence: Drives abdomen dip and vibration in both intact and headless flies
ecological_context: Simple motor control
summary: DNg14 drives simple stereotyped movements without downstream DN recruitment.

paper_title: Descending networks transform command signals into population motor control
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation
neural_activity: Connects to 4 downstream DNs
behavioral_consequence: Drives abdomen curling and ovipositor extension in both intact and headless flies
ecological_context: Reproduction
summary: oviDN drives reproductive motor actions independently of large DN networks.

paper_title: Descending networks transform command signals into population motor control
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation
neural_activity: No monosynaptic connections to other DNs
behavioral_consequence: Drives ovipositor extension in both intact and headless flies
ecological_context: Reproduction
summary: Mute DNs drive simple motor actions without downstream DN recruitment.

paper_title: Descending networks transform command signals into population motor control
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation
neural_activity: Connects to 4 downstream DNs
behavioral_consequence: Drives foreleg rubbing in both intact and headless flies
ecological_context: Grooming
summary: DNg11 drives simple motor actions like foreleg rubbing independently of large networks.

paper_title: Descending networks transform command signals into population motor control
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation of comDNs (DNp09, aDN2, MDN)
neural_activity: Co-activated in spatially distinct regions of cervical connective depending on comDN type; recruitment strength correlates with connectivity
behavioral_consequence: Recruitment necessary for complete behaviors (walking/grooming) driven by broadcasters; not necessary for standalone behaviors
ecological_context: Active control of multiple joint degrees of freedom during locomotion and grooming
summary: GNG DNs form interconnected clusters that are recruited by command-like DNs to orchestrate complex motor behaviors.

paper_title: Hue selectivity from recurrent circuitry in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Gamut of fly colors defined by Rh3-6 opsin captures, single wavelength stimuli, contrast stimuli
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6f) of axonal terminals in the medulla. pR8/yR8 show opponent responses (inhibitory in UV/long wavelengths). R7 opponency not clearly evident from gamut responses due to background.
summary: Photoreceptor axons (pR7, yR7, pR8, yR8) show opponent response properties in color space, with pR8/yR8 exhibiting inhibitory responses in specific wavelength ranges.

paper_title: Hue selectivity from recurrent circuitry in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Gamut of fly colors, single wavelength stimuli
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging. pDm8 excited by Rh4-6 combinations (violet), inhibited by Rh3-4-6. yDm8 broadly inhibited, positive near Rh6. Responses fit linear model.
summary: Dm8 interneurons (pDm8, yDm8) exhibit linear color responses distinct from photoreceptor inputs, with pDm8 tuned to violet and yDm8 broadly inhibited.

paper_title: Hue selectivity from recurrent circuitry in Drosophila
