experimental_stimulus: Gamut of fly colors, single wavelength, contrast stimuli
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging in lobula. Tm5a tuned to Rh3 vertex (UV), Tm5b to Rh4/Rh5 edge (violet), Tm20 to Rh4/Rh5/Rh6 face (blue/green). All show nonlinear hue selectivity and sparse responses. Silencing outputs with TeNT broadens tuning and linearizes responses.
techniques_used: ['two-photon calcium imaging', 'genetic silencing (TeNT)', 'electron microscopy connectomics', 'computational modeling']
summary: Tm5a, Tm5b, and Tm20 are hue-selective projection neurons with nonlinear processing of photoreceptor inputs. Recurrent connections are required for this selectivity.

paper_title: Hue selectivity from recurrent circuitry in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Gamut of fly colors, single wavelength, contrast stimuli
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging in lobula. Broad preference near Rh6 vertex (green). Linear properties. Silencing outputs with TeNT does not change tuning.
summary: Tm5c neurons show broad color tuning and linear properties, acting as an interneuron within the circuit rather than a hue-selective projection neuron.

paper_title: Light and dopamine impact two circadian neurons to promote morning wakefulness
cell_type: ITP+ LNs (ITP+ sLNv and ITP+ LNd)
experimental_stimulus: Red light (optogenetic activation with CsChrimson), Genetic silencing (Kir2.1, TNT), Dopamine perfusion (ex vivo), CRISPR/Cas9 Dop1R1 knock-out, 12:12 light-dark cycles
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation increases activity levels. Dopamine perfusion increases cAMP levels (EPAC sensor), with response more robust in light phase (morning). Dop1R1 mRNA peaks in morning (ZT02) under light-dark conditions. Knocking out Dop1R1 eliminates cAMP response to dopamine.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation dramatically increases activity and reduces sleep (day and night). Inhibition (Kir2.1/TNT) decreases morning activity (ZT00-06) and increases sleep (ZT00-06). Dop1R1 knock-out decreases morning activity (ZT00-06) and increases sleep depth (p-doze).
ecological_context: Promotes morning wakefulness and reduces sleep inertia upon light exposure (dawn), mimicking the effect of light on morning wakefulness in humans.
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics (CsChrimson)', 'Locomotor activity monitoring (FlyBox, DAM)', 'CRISPR/Cas9 mutagenesis', 'Ex vivo live imaging (cAMP sensors)', 'Single-cell RNA sequencing analysis', 'Electron microscopy connectome analysis']
summary: ITP+ LNs promote morning wakefulness; their activity is enhanced by dopamine via Dop1R1 and cAMP signaling, which peaks in the morning under light conditions. Optogenetic activation increases activity and reduces sleep, while inhibition or Dop1R1 knockdown specifically reduces morning activity.

paper_title: Light and dopamine impact two circadian neurons to promote morning wakefulness
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (intersectional strategy with Clk856-GAL4 and Trissin-LexA)
neural_activity: No specific neural response measured during activation; behavioral readout showed no change.
behavioral_consequence: No change in activity, sleep, p-wake, or p-doze observed when activated alone.
summary: Trissin+ LNds do not promote wakefulness when activated alone, unlike ITP+ LNs.

paper_title: Miniature linear and split-belt treadmills reveal mechanisms of adaptive motor control in walking Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Genetic silencing using iav-GAL4 > UAS-Kir2.1
behavioral_consequence: Silencing drastically reduced locomotion in freely walking flies. On the linear treadmill, flies with silenced chordotonal neurons walked at wider speed ranges and lower bout frequencies. Step frequency was lower and step length was greater across speeds compared to controls. Inter-leg coordination remained intact.
techniques_used: ['Genetic silencing (UAS-Kir2.1)', 'High-speed video tracking (5 cameras, 180 fps)', '3D kinematics analysis (DeepLabCut/Anipose)']
summary: Silencing chordotonal neurons alters step kinematics (frequency and length) across walking speeds but does not affect inter-leg coordination.

paper_title: Taste cells expressing Ionotropic Receptor 94e reciprocally impact feeding and egg laying in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (red light/ATR), chemogenetic activation (capsaicin/VR1), chemical stimulation (tryptone, glutamate, aspartate, sucrose, salts, water, yeast extract, pheromones)
neural_activity: In vivo calcium imaging (GCaMP6f in SEZ axon terminals) reveals strong activation by tryptone and amino acids (glutamate, aspartate). No response to sucrose, bitter compounds, water, or high salt. Response abolished in IR94e, IR25a, or IR76b mutants.
behavioral_consequence: Activation (optogenetic/chemogenetic) causes mild suppression of feeding (reduced PER, preference for non-activated food in binary choice) and enhanced egg laying (increased eggs on capsaicin substrates). IR94e mutants show increased consumption of tryptone and decreased egg laying on grape juice.
ecological_context: Mated females prioritize oviposition on protein-rich substrates containing amino acids to support offspring development; this system may discourage adult feeding on the same substrate to reduce larval competition.
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics (CsChrimson)', 'Chemogenetics (VR1)', 'In vivo calcium imaging (GCaMP6f)', 'FLIC feeding assay', 'Dye-based feeding assay', 'Egg-laying assay', 'IR94e mutants', 'Connectomics (FlyWire)']
summary: IR94e GRNs located in labellar L-type sensilla detect amino acids (glutamate, aspartate) via an IR94e/IR25a/IR76b receptor complex. Activation of these neurons leads to mild feeding aversion and increased egg laying, while loss of function increases feeding and decreases egg laying on amino acid substrates.

paper_title: Descending control and regulation of spontaneous flight turns in Drosophila.
experimental_stimulus: spontaneous flight (in darkness), looming visual stimuli (expanding spots at different azimuths)
neural_activity: 2-photon imaging (GCaMP7f) showed activity correlates with differential wing stroke amplitude (L-R) and ipsilaterally directed saccades. Activity levels between left and right cells followed an inverse relationship. Looming stimulus increased activity in cells on the opposite visual hemisphere.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson) of unilateral cells reliably elicited syndirectional saccades. Genetic ablation significantly reduced spontaneous saccade frequency (from 0.48 Hz to 0.09 Hz) and altered temporal pattern (long sequences of straight flight interspersed with bursts). Ablation also reduced saccade speed.
ecological_context: Spontaneous turns (saccades) occur during local search foraging and are suppressed during long-distance dispersal or odor tracking.
summary: DNae014 neurons correlate with spontaneous saccades and looming responses. Unilateral activation elicits syndirectional saccades, while ablation significantly reduces saccade frequency and alters flight dynamics, suggesting a role in generating turns during local search.

paper_title: Descending control and regulation of spontaneous flight turns in Drosophila.
experimental_stimulus: spontaneous flight (in darkness), looming visual stimuli (expanding spots at different azimuths)
neural_activity: 2-photon imaging (GCaMP7f) showed activity correlates with differential wing stroke amplitude and ipsilaterally directed saccades. Activity levels between left and right cells followed an inverse relationship. Looming stimulus increased activity in cells on the opposite visual hemisphere.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson) of unilateral cells elicited syndirectional saccades (smaller amplitude and longer duration than DNae014). Genetic ablation significantly reduced spontaneous saccade frequency (from 0.48 Hz to 0.12 Hz) and altered temporal pattern (long sequences of straight flight interspersed with bursts). Ablation reduced saccade speed.
ecological_context: Spontaneous turns (saccades) occur during local search foraging and are suppressed during long-distance dispersal or odor tracking.
summary: DNb01 neurons correlate with spontaneous saccades and looming responses. Unilateral activation elicits syndirectional saccades with different dynamics than DNae014, while ablation significantly reduces saccade frequency, suggesting a complementary role to DNae014 in saccade generation.

paper_title: Descending control and regulation of spontaneous flight turns in Drosophila.
experimental_stimulus: None tested (connectivity and activation only)
neural_activity: Connectomics analysis identified as inhibitory interneuron with direct monosynaptic input to DNae014 and DNb01 cells of both saccade-generating units. Predicted to be GABAergic based on machine learning.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson) strongly suppressed spontaneous saccades in the magnotether arena (reducing rate from ~0.5-0.7 Hz to ~0.1 Hz) and in rigidly tethered flight, promoting straight flight.
ecological_context: Promotes straight flight to regulate the transition between local search (turning) and long-distance dispersal.
summary: VES041 interneurons inhibit the saccade network via connections to DNae014 and DNb01. Optogenetic activation suppresses saccades, suggesting a regulatory role in switching between search and dispersal behaviors.

paper_title: Presynaptic inhibition selectively suppresses leg proprioception in behaving Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Passive leg movement, walking, grooming, resting
neural_activity: Active across behaviors. Calcium signals tracked joint position deviation from ~80° and were not suppressed during self-generated leg movements. Signals increased/decreased as predicted by passive movement models during transitions into/out of movement.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested experimentally (imaging only).
ecological_context: Position feedback contributes to postural reflexes during standing and ensures proper leg placement in space during walking and grooming.
summary: Claw axons encode leg joint position and remain active during self-generated movements like walking and grooming, suggesting position feedback is transmitted across behavioral contexts.

paper_title: Presynaptic inhibition selectively suppresses leg proprioception in behaving Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Passive leg movement, walking, grooming, resting
neural_activity: Suppressed during self-generated movements (walking, grooming). Calcium signals were absent or weak during active leg movements but present during passive movements (e.g., leg moved by hind legs).
behavioral_consequence: Not tested experimentally (imaging only).
ecological_context: Movement feedback helps detect external perturbations and suppress reflexes that would disrupt ongoing movements.
summary: Hook axons encode leg movement and are selectively suppressed during self-generated movements via presynaptic inhibition, allowing transmission of externally-generated signals.

paper_title: Presynaptic inhibition selectively suppresses leg proprioception in behaving Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Leg contact with treadmill, active leg movements
neural_activity: Not suppressed during active leg movements. Baseline activity elevated when legs contacted the treadmill.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested experimentally (imaging only).
ecological_context: Function as exteroceptors that mediate external substrate vibrations.
summary: Club axons encode high-frequency vibrations and are not suppressed during active leg movements, consistent with a role in detecting external substrate vibrations.

paper_title: Presynaptic inhibition selectively suppresses leg proprioception in behaving Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Active leg movements (walking, grooming), passive leg movements
neural_activity: High activity during self-generated movements. Weak activity during passive leg movements or resting. Leg-specific recruitment observed.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation changed femur-tibia movement range and caused flies to slow down. Silencing had weaker effects.
ecological_context: Contribute to local feedback control of the femur-tibia joint and suppress proprioceptive feedback to prevent disruption of ongoing movements.
summary: 9A interneurons are active during self-generated leg movements and provide GABAergic presynaptic inhibition to hook axons; their activation alters leg kinematics and walking speed.

paper_title: Presynaptic inhibition selectively suppresses leg proprioception in behaving Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Active leg movements (walking, grooming), resting
neural_activity: High activity during resting. Low activity during active leg movements. Weak when front leg at rest while other legs moved actively.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested experimentally (imaging only).
ecological_context: Releases 9A neurons from inhibition during self-generated leg movements, part of a predictive signaling circuit from the brain.
summary: The web neuron is a descending neuron active during resting and inactive during self-generated movement, suggesting it disinhibits 9A interneurons during active walking and grooming.

paper_title: Flexible circuit mechanisms for context-dependent song sequencing
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (csChrimson, varying irradiance and duty cycle)
neural_activity: In vivo patch-clamp recordings show spike-frequency adaptation. Activation drives stimulus-locked pulse song. Strong activation drives rebound sine song following offset in solitary males.
behavioral_consequence: In solitary males, drives pulse song. In presence of female, drives complex song (pulse followed by rebound sine). Inactivation (TNT/Kir2.1) reduces simple pulse-only bouts. Co-activation with P1a drives long complex bouts.
ecological_context: Song production near versus far from the female fly (context-dependent song sequencing).
summary: pIP10 descending neurons drive pulse song and rebound sine song depending on activation strength and context (female presence), utilizing spike-frequency adaptation.

paper_title: Flexible circuit mechanisms for context-dependent song sequencing
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (csChrimson); pIP10 optogenetic activation while imaging
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging reveals diverse response patterns. Subsets are positively correlated with pIP10 stimulus (pulse) or anti-correlated (rebound sine). Some show post-inhibitory rebound activity.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation drives sine song. Rebound sine song production is facilitated by female sensory cues. Knockdown of Ih or Rdl in TN1 reduces song complexity.
ecological_context: Generation of simple versus complex song bouts during courtship.
summary: TN1 neurons in the VNC generate sine song and rebound dynamics through mutual inhibition and rebound excitability, modulated by female proximity.

paper_title: Flexible circuit mechanisms for context-dependent song sequencing
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (csChrimson); female tapping (natural stimulus)
neural_activity: Calcium imaging shows activity in downstream GABAergic F1 follower neurons and inhibition of F2 follower neurons. Tapping rate correlates with P1a activity estimate.
behavioral_consequence: Activation alone produces persistent, variable song and suppresses wing extension during stimulus. Co-activation with pIP10 drives long complex bouts. Acute activation promotes complex bout generation.
ecological_context: Disinhibition of VNC circuit for complex song near the female; associated with tapping behavior during courtship.
summary: P1a neurons mediate functional disinhibition of the VNC rebound circuit, enabling complex song sequences during courtship near females.

paper_title: Flexible circuit mechanisms for context-dependent song sequencing
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (csChrimson); synaptic silencing (TNT)
neural_activity: Silencing reduces overall song activity. Activation drives pulse song followed by rebound sine.
behavioral_consequence: Silencing reduces song amount, increases simple pulse bouts, and reduces complexity. Duration of activity determines pulse type selection (brief drives Pfast, sustained drives Pslow).
ecological_context: Integration of visual and auditory cues to determine song composition (simple vs complex) during courtship.
summary: pC2 neurons serve as a main determinant of song composition, integrating sensory cues to drive simple or complex song bouts via pIP10 and P1a pathways.

paper_title: Flexible circuit mechanisms for context-dependent song sequencing
experimental_stimulus: P1a optogenetic activation
neural_activity: F1 follower neurons show activity immediately following P1a activation. F2 follower neurons show inhibition (suppressed activity) during F1 activity.
behavioral_consequence: Disinhibitory motif supports complex song generation; F2 followers are dispersed suggesting multiple disinhibitory circuits.
ecological_context: Supports context-dependent gating of the song circuit during courtship.
summary: GABAergic neurons downstream of P1a form a disinhibitory motif, with F1 followers active and F2 followers inhibited, facilitating complex song production.

paper_title: Overlap and divergence of neural circuits mediating distinct behavioral responses to sugar
experimental_stimulus: Red light optogenetic activation (Chrimson) at low, medium, and high intensities (4-35 µW/mm²)
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation used to drive behavior; mimics sugar taste
behavioral_consequence: Strong locomotor suppression (transient in fed, prolonged in starved flies). Elicits positional preference in starved flies at low light intensity (aversion at high intensity). Drives appetitive learning (odor preference) in starved flies. Responses blunted by high-sugar diet.
ecological_context: Promotes caloric intake and food-finding; hunger state gates these responses. Bitter taste overrides sugar responses to prevent toxin ingestion.
summary: Gr64f-Gal4 sugar-sensing neurons drive locomotor suppression, positional preference, and appetitive learning upon activation, with responses modulated by hunger state and high-sugar diet.

paper_title: Overlap and divergence of neural circuits mediating distinct behavioral responses to sugar
experimental_stimulus: Red light optogenetic activation (Chrimson)
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation used to drive behavior
behavioral_consequence: Causes strong locomotor suppression at all light intensities and positional preference in starved flies at low intensity. Did not observe learned preference.
ecological_context: Sugar sensing for feeding initiation and location; subset of Gr64f-expressing neurons.
summary: Gr5a-Gal4 sugar-sensing neurons suppress locomotion and drive positional preference in starved flies but do not support learned preference under tested conditions.

paper_title: Overlap and divergence of neural circuits mediating distinct behavioral responses to sugar
experimental_stimulus: Red light optogenetic activation (Chrimson)
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation used to drive behavior; mimics bitter taste
behavioral_consequence: Increases forward velocity and turning (locomotor enhancement). Causes positional aversion. Drives learned aversion (enhanced in starved flies). Overrides sugar-evoked behaviors during co-activation.
ecological_context: Prevents ingestion of toxins; aversive behaviors are important regardless of hunger state.
summary: Gr33a-Gal4 bitter-sensing neurons drive locomotor enhancement and aversion, overriding sugar-evoked behaviors during co-activation.

paper_title: Overlap and divergence of neural circuits mediating distinct behavioral responses to sugar
cell_type: Zorro, Rattle, FMIn, G2N-1 (Second-order sugar interneurons)
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (Chrimson) via split-Gal4 lines
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation used to drive behavior
behavioral_consequence: Ellicit proboscis extension. Cause significant locomotor suppression. Rattle and FMIn also cause significant positional preference. Form core excitatory subcircuit with shared targets.
ecological_context: Mediate feeding initiation and stopping locomotion to feed; diverge from sensory neurons in circuit processing.
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics', 'Proboscis extension assay', 'Locomotion tracking', 'Positional preference assay', 'Connectome analysis']
summary: These second-order interneurons mediate proboscis extension and locomotor suppression, with Rattle and FMIn also driving positional preference.

paper_title: Overlap and divergence of neural circuits mediating distinct behavioral responses to sugar
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (Chrimson) via split-Gal4 lines
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation used to drive behavior; predicted to be inhibitory
behavioral_consequence: Usnea did not elicit proboscis extension in this study. Phantom and Usnea do not affect locomotion or positional preference. Share targets with each other but few with excitatory neurons.
ecological_context: Part of inhibitory subcircuit within sugar pathway, distinct from locomotion/preference circuits.
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics', 'Proboscis extension assay', 'Locomotion tracking', 'Positional preference assay', 'Connectome analysis']
summary: These second-order interneurons form an inhibitory subcircuit distinct from excitatory neurons driving locomotion or preference.

paper_title: Overlap and divergence of neural circuits mediating distinct behavioral responses to sugar
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (Chrimson)
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation used to drive behavior
behavioral_consequence: Ellicits proboscis extension. Does not affect locomotion or positional preference. Shares targets with Phantom/Usnea and excitatory neurons.

techniques_used: ['Optogenetics', 'Proboscis extension assay', 'Locomotion tracking', 'Positional preference assay', 'Connectome analysis']
summary: Clavicle mediates proboscis extension and has intermediate connectivity between excitatory and inhibitory subcircuits.

paper_title: Overlap and divergence of neural circuits mediating distinct behavioral responses to sugar

behavioral_consequence: Ellicits proboscis extension. Causes significant locomotor suppression. Shows trends in positional preference. Receives input from Rattle, FMIn, G2N-1.
ecological_context: Critical node connecting sugar circuit to locomotor suppression pathways.
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics', 'Proboscis extension assay', 'Locomotion tracking', 'Positional preference assay', 'Connectome analysis']
summary: Fdg drives proboscis extension and locomotor suppression, acting as a critical node in the sugar circuit.

paper_title: Overlap and divergence of neural circuits mediating distinct behavioral responses to sugar

ecological_context: Contributes to innate preference in parallel with other neurons.
summary: Bract drives proboscis extension and contributes to innate positional preference.

paper_title: Overlap and divergence of neural circuits mediating distinct behavioral responses to sugar

summary: Roundup mediates proboscis extension without affecting locomotion or positional preference.

paper_title: Nested neural circuits generate distinct acoustic signals during Drosophila courtship
experimental_stimulus: Green light (525 nm) silencing in courting males; Red light (625 nm) activation in isolated males
neural_activity: Optogenetic silencing reduced sine song production; Activation elicited sine song in isolated males (persisted throughout 10s stimulus)
behavioral_consequence: Silencing significantly reduced sine song without altering pulse song; Activation in courting males increased sine song at the expense of pulse song
ecological_context: Pulse and sine song signal species identity and male quality; Sine song is the simpler, more recent evolutionary form; Females evaluate songs to recognize conspecific males
summary: TN1A neurons are cholinergic, fru+/dsx+ interneurons essential for sine song production. Silencing reduces sine song, while activation is sufficient to elicit it.

paper_title: Nested neural circuits generate distinct acoustic signals during Drosophila courtship
experimental_stimulus: Green light (525 nm) silencing in courting males; Red light (625 nm) activation in isolated males
neural_activity: Optogenetic silencing variably but consistently reduced sine song; Activation elicited normal sine trains in isolated males (intermittent)
behavioral_consequence: Silencing significantly reduced sine song without affecting pulse song; Activation elicited sine song but little or no pulse song
ecological_context: Sine song is used during courtship to signal species identity and male quality
summary: dMS2 neurons are cholinergic, fru+ interneurons. The bilateral subtype is central to sine song production, as silencing reduces sine song and activation elicits it.

paper_title: Nested neural circuits generate distinct acoustic signals during Drosophila courtship
experimental_stimulus: Green light (525 nm) silencing in courting males; Red light (625 nm) activation in isolated and courting males
neural_activity: Optogenetic silencing tended to reduce pulse song (significant at one intensity); Activation in isolated males drove normal pulse trains
behavioral_consequence: Silencing reduced pulse song; Activation increased pulse song and reduced sine song in courting males
ecological_context: Pulse song consists of trains of discrete pulses that recur every 35 ms; Used to attract females and signal quality
summary: pMP2 neurons are cholinergic, fru+ descending neurons. They contribute to pulse song production, as silencing reduces pulse and activation induces it.

paper_title: Nested neural circuits generate distinct acoustic signals during Drosophila courtship
experimental_stimulus: Green light (525 nm) silencing in courting males; Red light (625 nm) activation in isolated and courting males
neural_activity: Optogenetic silencing significantly reduced pulse song; Activation produced normal pulse trains in isolated males
behavioral_consequence: Silencing reduced pulse song and indirectly increased sine song; Activation increased pulse song and decreased sine song
ecological_context: Neurons may contribute to encoding species identity via inter-pulse interval (tuned to 35ms in female brain)
summary: dPR1 neurons are cholinergic, fru+ dsx+ VNC interneurons. They are essential for pulse song production and indirectly inhibit sine song.

paper_title: Nested neural circuits generate distinct acoustic signals during Drosophila courtship
experimental_stimulus: Green light (525 nm) silencing in courting males; Red light (625 nm) activation in isolated and courting males
neural_activity: Optogenetic silencing significantly reduced pulse production; Activation produced song with pulse and sine trains (up to 2s)
behavioral_consequence: Silencing reduced pulse song; Activation variably increased pulse or sine song depending on driver line
ecological_context: Pulse song is the more complex, ancestral song form; Song features vary across species for recognition
summary: dMS9 neurons are cholinergic, fru+ bilaterally paired neurons. They are essential for pulse song and may contribute to sine song.

paper_title: Nested neural circuits generate distinct acoustic signals during Drosophila courtship
experimental_stimulus: Green light (525 nm) silencing in courting males; Red light (625 nm) activation in isolated and courting males
neural_activity: Optogenetic silencing reduced pulse song (some lines); Activation variably increased pulse and sine song
behavioral_consequence: Silencing reduced pulse song; Activation increased sine song in courting males
ecological_context: Indirect control muscles (ps1) innervated by vMS12 are active during pulse song
summary: vMS12 neurons are cholinergic, fru+ interneurons. They are required for pulse song production and may contribute to sine song.

paper_title: Nested neural circuits generate distinct acoustic signals during Drosophila courtship
experimental_stimulus: Green light (525 nm) silencing in courting males; Red light (625 nm) activation in isolated and courting males
neural_activity: Optogenetic silencing significantly reduced both pulse and sine song; Activation elicited normal pulse trains interspersed with sine song
behavioral_consequence: Silencing reduced both song types; Activation increased both at low intensity, pulse at expense of sine at high intensity
ecological_context: Receives input from neurons integrating auditory and visual courtship-related input in the brain
summary: pIP10 neurons are bilaterally paired, cholinergic, fru+ descending neurons. They are necessary and sufficient for both song types and influence song choice.

paper_title: Nested neural circuits generate distinct acoustic signals during Drosophila courtship
experimental_stimulus: Green light (525 nm) silencing in courting males; Red light (625 nm) activation in courting males
neural_activity: Optogenetic silencing (vPR9-SS5) significantly reduced both pulse and sine song; Activation reduced song types depending on subtype
behavioral_consequence: Silencing reduced both song types; vPR9a activation preferentially reduced pulse song; vPR9b/c activation reduced sine song
ecological_context: Inter-pulse interval is critical for species recognition; vPR9 may mediate switching between song modes
summary: vPR9 neurons are GABAergic, fru+ VNC interneurons. They are required for producing both song types, with subtypes preferentially suppressing pulse or sine song.

paper_title: Nested neural circuits generate distinct acoustic signals during Drosophila courtship
experimental_stimulus: Red light (625 nm) co-activation with pIP10 in isolated males
neural_activity: Optogenetic manipulation alone did not affect song amount; Co-activation increased sine song ratio
behavioral_consequence: Co-activation of pIP10 and DNp13 increased sine song and reduced pulse production compared to pIP10 alone
ecological_context: Descending inputs may mediate switching between song modality based on sensory cues
summary: DNp13 is a descending neuron. Activity is required to generate sine song in conjunction with pIP10, supporting a model of alternative descending inputs mediating song mode switching.

paper_title: Networks of descending neurons transform command-like signals into population-based behavioral control
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (640 nm laser, 10 µW on neck connective)
neural_activity: Recruited GNG-DN populations recorded via two-photon calcium imaging; connectome shows 32 downstream DN partners
behavioral_consequence: Intact: Drives forward walking. Headless: Does not drive forward walking, but drives stereotyped abdomen contraction
ecological_context: Flexible, goal-directed walking behavior
summary: DNp09 activation drives flexible forward walking in intact flies via recruitment of downstream DN networks; in headless flies, only simple abdomen contractions remain.

paper_title: Networks of descending neurons transform command-like signals into population-based behavioral control
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (640 nm laser, 20 µW on neck connective)
neural_activity: Recruited GNG-DN populations recorded via two-photon calcium imaging; connectome shows 23 downstream DN partners
behavioral_consequence: Intact: Drives anterior grooming. Headless: Does not drive anterior grooming, but drives front leg approach
ecological_context: Anterior grooming, a somewhat flexible behavior involving sequential control
summary: aDN2 activation drives flexible anterior grooming in intact flies via recruitment of downstream DN networks; in headless flies, only front leg approach remains.

paper_title: Networks of descending neurons transform command-like signals into population-based behavioral control
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (640 nm laser, 10 µW on neck connective)
neural_activity: Recruited GNG-DN populations recorded via two-photon calcium imaging; connectome shows 14 downstream DN partners
behavioral_consequence: Intact: Drives backward walking. Headless: Still drives backward walking
ecological_context: Stereotyped backward walking behavior arising from stereotyped oscillations
summary: MDN activation drives stereotyped backward walking in intact flies; this behavior persists in headless flies without network recruitment.

paper_title: Networks of descending neurons transform command-like signals into population-based behavioral control
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation
neural_activity: Connectome analysis shows 20 downstream DN partners
behavioral_consequence: Intact: Drives turning. Headless: Turns lost, drives flexion of front legs
ecological_context: Turning behavior
summary: DNb02 requires downstream DN network recruitment to drive turning; in headless flies, only simple leg flexion occurs.

summary: DNa01 requires downstream DN network recruitment to drive turning; turning is lost in headless flies.

paper_title: Networks of descending neurons transform command-like signals into population-based behavioral control

behavioral_consequence: Intact: Drives in-place turning. Headless: Turning behavior lost

summary: DNa02 requires downstream DN network recruitment to drive turning; turning is lost in headless flies.

paper_title: Networks of descending neurons transform command-like signals into population-based behavioral control

paper_title: Networks of descending neurons transform command-like signals into population-based behavioral control

paper_title: Networks of descending neurons transform command-like signals into population-based behavioral control

summary: Mute drives simple ovipositor extension in both intact and headless flies without requiring downstream DN network recruitment.

paper_title: Neural mechanisms to incorporate visual counterevidence in self motion estimation
experimental_stimulus: Full-field stationary or flickering checkerboard patterns; islands of motion stimuli
neural_activity: Exhibited sustained, bipolar calcium responses to stationary checkerboards with higher trial-to-trial variability than flickering stimuli; presynaptic to T4 neurons
behavioral_consequence: Silencing Mi4 synaptic outputs (using shibirets) significantly reduced optomotor suppression by stationary patterns, resulting in increased turning responses
ecological_context: Detection of stationary visual patterns as negative evidence against self-rotation to stabilize course orientation during walking
summary: Mi4 neurons encode stationary patterns via sustained responses and are necessary for suppressing rotational optomotor responses in the presence of stationary visual cues.

paper_title: Neural mechanisms to incorporate visual counterevidence in self motion estimation
experimental_stimulus: Islands of motion stimuli (stationary, flicker, or uniform foreground) presented in a single circular aperture centered on receptive fields
neural_activity: T4 responses were not affected by stationary patterns; T5 responses were suppressed by ~25% by stationary patterns; flickering patterns suppressed both T4 and T5 activities
behavioral_consequence: Not tested via manipulation in this study; imaging suggests suppression of optomotor response occurs downstream of these neurons
ecological_context: Elementary motion detection necessary for rotational optomotor responses and course stabilization
summary: T4 and T5 neurons are elementary motion detectors; stationary patterns suppress T5 but not T4 activity, and suppression of turning occurs downstream.

paper_title: Neural mechanisms to incorporate visual counterevidence in self motion estimation
cell_type: Lobula Plate Tangential Cells (LPTCs: HS, H2, CH, Hx, FD1)
experimental_stimulus: Islands of motion stimuli (uniform, flicker, stationary) and full-field stationary, flickering, or translating checkerboards
neural_activity: Stationary foreground patterns did not significantly affect LPTC responses (except slightly increasing HS responses); flickering patterns suppressed activity
behavioral_consequence: Not tested via manipulation in this study; results suggest information integration occurs in the central brain downstream of LPTCs
ecological_context: Optic flow detection and yaw tuning for rotational optomotor responses
summary: LPTCs respond directionally to optic flow; stationary patterns do not suppress their activity, indicating central integration for suppression.

paper_title: Neural mechanisms to incorporate visual counterevidence in self motion estimation
experimental_stimulus: Islands of motion stimuli
neural_activity: Not tested in this study
behavioral_consequence: Silencing synaptic outputs resulted in no significant reduction in optomotor suppression by stationary patterns
ecological_context: Heading direction estimation and navigation
summary: Silencing heading circuitry neurons did not affect the suppression of optomotor responses by stationary patterns.

paper_title: Neural mechanisms to incorporate visual counterevidence in self motion estimation
cell_type: Populations targeted by R13E12, R15C05, R75H07 Gal4 lines
experimental_stimulus: Islands of motion stimuli
neural_activity: Not tested in this study
behavioral_consequence: Silencing synaptic outputs resulted in significantly reduced optomotor suppression by stationary patterns
ecological_context: Stationary pattern detection or suppression of optomotor response
summary: Silencing these populations reduced optomotor suppression, but off-target labeling prevented definitive identification of specific contributing cell types.

paper_title: A leaky integrate-and-fire computational model based on the connectome of the entire adult Drosophila brain reveals insights into sensorimotor processing

behavioral_consequence: Proboscis extension and feeding initiation
ecological_context: Feeding initiation in response to appetitive substances in hungry flies
summary: Sugar GRNs activate MN9 to drive proboscis extension during feeding.

paper_title: A leaky integrate-and-fire computational model based on the connectome of the entire adult Drosophila brain reveals insights into sensorimotor processing

behavioral_consequence: Proboscis extension to water
ecological_context: Consumption in hungry and thirsty flies
summary: Water GRNs activate MN9 to drive proboscis extension for water consumption.

paper_title: A leaky integrate-and-fire computational model based on the connectome of the entire adult Drosophila brain reveals insights into sensorimotor processing

neural_activity: Inhibited MN9 firing
behavioral_consequence: Inhibited proboscis extension to sucrose (eliminated consumption at 1M)
ecological_context: Avoidance of aversive substances

paper_title: A leaky integrate-and-fire computational model based on the connectome of the entire adult Drosophila brain reveals insights into sensorimotor processing

neural_activity: Inhibited MN9 firing
behavioral_consequence: Inhibited proboscis extension to 50 mM sucrose, but not 1M sucrose
ecological_context: Low salt detection (identified as inhibitory to feeding initiation)

paper_title: A leaky integrate-and-fire computational model based on the connectome of the entire adult Drosophila brain reveals insights into sensorimotor processing

neural_activity: Output of the feeding initiation circuit

paper_title: A leaky integrate-and-fire computational model based on the connectome of the entire adult Drosophila brain reveals insights into sensorimotor processing
experimental_stimulus: Tastant application (water, sugar, bitter)
neural_activity: Calcium response to water and sugar
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic silencing significantly decreased proboscis extension to water
ecological_context: Water feeding initiation
summary: Fudog responds to water and sugar and is required for water-induced proboscis extension.

paper_title: A leaky integrate-and-fire computational model based on the connectome of the entire adult Drosophila brain reveals insights into sensorimotor processing
experimental_stimulus: Tastant application (water, sugar, bitter) in thirsty-like flies
neural_activity: Calcium response to water
behavioral_consequence: Silencing phenotype tested among water-responsive neurons
ecological_context: Water feeding initiation in thirsty state
summary: Zorro responds to water in thirsty-like flies as confirmed by calcium imaging.

paper_title: A leaky integrate-and-fire computational model based on the connectome of the entire adult Drosophila brain reveals insights into sensorimotor processing
experimental_stimulus: Tastant application (water, sugar)
neural_activity: Responds to water and sugar
behavioral_consequence: Required for proboscis extension to water (silencing decreased extension)
ecological_context: Processing of both sugar and water tastes
summary: Usnea responds to both water and sugar and is required for water feeding initiation.

paper_title: A leaky integrate-and-fire computational model based on the connectome of the entire adult Drosophila brain reveals insights into sensorimotor processing
experimental_stimulus: Tastant application (water, sugar)
neural_activity: Respond to both water and sugar
behavioral_consequence: Required for feeding initiation to water (silencing decreased extension)
ecological_context: Shared components in sugar and water pathways
summary: Bract and Roundup are required for water feeding initiation and respond to both water and sugar.

paper_title: A leaky integrate-and-fire computational model based on the connectome of the entire adult Drosophila brain reveals insights into sensorimotor processing
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation
neural_activity: Robustly activates aBN1 interneurons (calcium imaging)
behavioral_consequence: Sufficient to trigger antennal grooming
ecological_context: Antennal grooming control
summary: JO-CE neurons robustly activate aBN1 and trigger antennal grooming.

paper_title: A leaky integrate-and-fire computational model based on the connectome of the entire adult Drosophila brain reveals insights into sensorimotor processing

behavioral_consequence: Sufficient to trigger antennal grooming
ecological_context: Antennal grooming control
summary: JO-F neurons trigger grooming despite not robustly activating aBN1.

paper_title: A leaky integrate-and-fire computational model based on the connectome of the entire adult Drosophila brain reveals insights into sensorimotor processing

neural_activity: Activated by JO-CE neurons, not by JO-F neurons
behavioral_consequence: Sufficient for antennal grooming (validated circuit node)

summary: aBN1 responds selectively to JO-CE mechanosensory input and mediates grooming.

paper_title: GABA-mediated inhibition in visual feedback neurons fine-tunes Drosophila male courtship
experimental_stimulus: Visual feedback during courtship pursuits (tracking a mating partner)
neural_activity: Activity modulated by GABARAP regulating GABAA receptors; in vivo two-photon imaging shows functional connection to circuits mediating visually-guided courtship pursuits; hyperactivation and inhibition performed
behavioral_consequence: Hyperactivation induces male courtship toward other males; inhibition delays copulation by impairing the ability of males to follow females; Knockdown of GABARAP or GABAA receptors in Lat neurons induces male courtship toward other males
ecological_context: Vision is critical for the regulation of mating behaviors; neurons track a mating partner during courtship
summary: Lat neurons are visual feedback neurons where GABARAP fine-tunes activity to track mating partners; hyperactivation or GABARAP/GABAA knockdown induces courtship toward males, while inhibition impairs following females during courtship.

paper_title: Synaptic gradients transform object location to action
experimental_stimulus: Looming visual stimuli (dark expanding discs) at varying azimuths (0, 90, 180 degrees; electrophysiology used 32.5, 45, 57.5, 70 degrees)
neural_activity: Anterior LC4 neurons provide relatively more synaptic drive to DNp02; posterior LC4 neurons provide more drive to DNp11. Antiparallel synaptic number gradients along the anterior-posterior axis of the visual field.
behavioral_consequence: LC4 activation is critical for fast escape takeoffs through direct synapses onto the giant fibre (GF) and other descending neurons.
ecological_context: Looming visual cues indicate an impending collision or predator attack; flies orient their escape takeoff away from the direction of the stimulus to survive.
techniques_used: ['Electron microscopy (EM)', 'Connectomics (hemibrain, FAFB)', 'Modeling of receptive fields', 'Behavioral tracking (FlyPEZ)']
summary: LC4 neurons exhibit antiparallel synaptic number gradients along the anterior-posterior axis connecting to DNp02 and DNp11, transforming retinal location into directional escape signals.

paper_title: Synaptic gradients transform object location to action
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson); indirect activation via looming stimuli
neural_activity: Electrophysiology (whole-cell patch-clamp) shows more action potentials to anterior looming stimuli compared to posterior.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation promotes backward leaning and backward escape takeoffs. Silencing impairs backward takeoff in response to frontal looming (flies jump forwards towards the stimulus).
ecological_context: Controls the direction of escape takeoffs away from looming threats.
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics (CsChrimson)', 'Whole-cell patch-clamp electrophysiology', 'Kir2.1 silencing', 'Behavioral tracking (FlyPEZ, Animal Part Tracker)']
summary: DNp02 is required for backward escape takeoffs; activation drives backward postural shifts and takeoffs, while silencing impairs the ability to escape frontal threats.

paper_title: Synaptic gradients transform object location to action
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson); indirect activation via looming stimuli
neural_activity: Electrophysiology (whole-cell patch-clamp) shows more action potentials to posterior looming stimuli compared to anterior.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation drives forward leaning and forward escape takeoffs. Silencing impairs forward takeoff control (flies jump forwards in response to frontal looming).
ecological_context: Controls the direction of escape takeoffs away from looming threats.
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics (CsChrimson)', 'Whole-cell patch-clamp electrophysiology', 'Kir2.1 silencing', 'Behavioral tracking (FlyPEZ, Animal Part Tracker)']
summary: DNp11 is required for forward escape takeoffs; activation drives forward postural shifts and takeoffs, while silencing impairs escape directionality.

paper_title: Synaptic gradients transform object location to action
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson); Looming stimuli; ATP puffing (for LPLC2 input)
neural_activity: Electrophysiology shows depolarization to looming. Local LPLC2 dorsal activation produces stronger GF response than ventral.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation produces high takeoff rates (>90%) and 'short-mode' escapes (no prior wing-raising).
ecological_context: Drives rapid escape motor actions in response to threats.
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics (CsChrimson)', 'Whole-cell patch-clamp electrophysiology', 'ATP puffing (P2X2 receptor activation)', 'Behavioral tracking (FlyPEZ)']
summary: GF activation drives high-rate, short-mode escape takeoffs; receives graded input from LPLC2 with dorsal-ventral synaptic gradients.

paper_title: Synaptic gradients transform object location to action
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson); indirect activation via looming stimuli
neural_activity: Electrophysiology shows bursts of action potentials without significant azimuth tuning.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation alone results in omnidirectional takeoffs. Co-activation with DNp02 enhances backward shifts.
ecological_context: Part of the descending network for escape behaviors.
summary: DNp04 drives omnidirectional takeoffs and enhances backward shifts when co-activated with DNp02, though lacks strong azimuth tuning.

paper_title: Synaptic gradients transform object location to action
experimental_stimulus: Local activation via ATP puffing in dorsal/ventral lobula
neural_activity: Synaptic gradients onto GF along the dorsoventral axis. Dorsal LPLC2 activation produces stronger GF response than ventral. No axonal topography observed in glomerulus.
behavioral_consequence: Functional validation shown via GF response to local VPN activation; direct behavioral output of LPLC2 not explicitly tested.
ecological_context: Visuomotor transformation for spatially directed motor outputs.
techniques_used: ['ATP puffing (P2X2 receptor activation)', 'Whole-cell patch-clamp electrophysiology (GF)', 'Electron microscopy (EM)', 'Connectomics (hemibrain)']
summary: LPLC2 exhibits dorsoventral synaptic gradients onto GF that correlate with functional response strength, despite lacking axonal topography in the glomerulus.

paper_title: Combined patterns of activity of major neuronal classes underpin a global change in brain state during spontaneous and forced walk in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Spontaneous walk, flailing, grooming
neural_activity: Increased global brain activity correlated with walk; activity closely followed individual walk bouts
summary: GABAergic neurons show increased activity during spontaneous walking compared to rest or grooming.

paper_title: Combined patterns of activity of major neuronal classes underpin a global change in brain state during spontaneous and forced walk in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Spontaneous walk, flailing, grooming
neural_activity: Increased global brain activity correlated with walk; activity closely followed individual walk bouts
summary: Glutamatergic neurons show increased activity during spontaneous walking compared to rest or grooming.

paper_title: Combined patterns of activity of major neuronal classes underpin a global change in brain state during spontaneous and forced walk in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Spontaneous walk, flailing, grooming
neural_activity: Increased global brain activity correlated with walk; activity closely followed individual walk bouts
summary: Cholinergic neurons show increased activity during spontaneous walking compared to rest or grooming.

paper_title: Combined patterns of activity of major neuronal classes underpin a global change in brain state during spontaneous and forced walk in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Spontaneous walk, forced walk, turning
neural_activity: Increased activity during walk. Specific components (M-Omega, WE-DA-like, MB compartments) active before walk onset in spontaneous walk. PPM2-LW component active during turning (lateralized). CX components (PB-EB, EB-DA) active at walk onset
summary: Dopaminergic neurons show increased activity during walking. Some subpopulations show activity before walk onset, while others correlate with turning.

paper_title: Combined patterns of activity of major neuronal classes underpin a global change in brain state during spontaneous and forced walk in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Spontaneous walk, forced walk, turning
neural_activity: Increased activity during walk. OL-PENP component strongly correlated. Activity similar to dopaminergic neurons during walk
summary: Octopaminergic neurons show increased global activity during walking.

paper_title: Combined patterns of activity of major neuronal classes underpin a global change in brain state during spontaneous and forced walk in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Spontaneous walk, forced walk, turning
neural_activity: Overall less globally activated than dopaminergic/octopaminergic neurons. AVLPshell component inhibited during walk. Other components showed variable activation
summary: Serotonergic neurons show heterogeneous activity; a specific component (AVLPshell) is inhibited during walk.

paper_title: Combined patterns of activity of major neuronal classes underpin a global change in brain state during spontaneous and forced walk in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Forced walk, forced turn
neural_activity: Strong axonal activity in GNG, AMMC, and AVLP during forced walk. Activity absent in brain when VNC severed
techniques_used: ['Light field microscopy', 'Synaptically tagged calcium imaging (sytGCaMP)', 'VNC severing']
summary: Ascending neurons from the VNC convey behavioral state signals to the brain during walking, as activity in GNG/AVLP depends on VNC connectivity.

paper_title: Taste quality and hunger interactions in a feeding sensorimotor circuit
experimental_stimulus: 50 mM sucrose, water, optogenetic activation, food-deprived vs fed state
neural_activity: Calcium imaging (GCaMP6s) showed responses to sugar taste. G2N-1 and Clavicle activation elicited higher proboscis extension rates in food-deprived flies compared to fed flies.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation elicited proboscis extension (PER). Inhibition reduced PER to 50 mM sucrose. Hunger state specifically amplified activation of G2N-1 and Clavicle, not other sugar neurons.
ecological_context: Hunger signals amplify sugar pathway activation to promote feeding initiation in food-deprived animals.
techniques_used: ['Electron Microscopy reconstruction', 'Calcium Imaging', 'Optogenetics (CsChrimson/GtACR1)', 'Proboscis Extension Response assay']
summary: G2N-1 and Clavicle are second-order sugar neurons that are modulated by hunger state; activation increases PER in food-deprived flies, and inhibition reduces PER to sucrose.

paper_title: Taste quality and hunger interactions in a feeding sensorimotor circuit
cell_type: Other Sugar Second-order Neurons (Zorro, FMIn, Rattle)
experimental_stimulus: 50 mM sucrose, optogenetic activation
neural_activity: Calcium imaging showed responses to sugar taste in food-deprived flies.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation elicited proboscis extension. Inhibition reduced PER to 50 mM sucrose. Activation rates did not differ between fed and food-deprived flies.
ecological_context: Contribute to feeding initiation robustness alongside hunger-modulated neurons.
summary: These second-order sugar neurons respond to sugar and elicit proboscis extension, but are not modulated by hunger state during activation.

paper_title: Taste quality and hunger interactions in a feeding sensorimotor circuit
experimental_stimulus: Water, sugar taste solutions
neural_activity: Calcium imaging showed Usnea responded specifically to water, Phantom responded to both water and sugar. Usnea and Phantom are reciprocally connected with GRNs.
behavioral_consequence: Not explicitly tested for PER behavior in isolation in main results, but implicated in tuning GRN responses and thirst-driven feeding.
ecological_context: Water taste detection and thirst state modulation of feeding decisions.
summary: Usnea responds specifically to water, Phantom responds to water and sugar; these neurons may tune GRN responses to water.

paper_title: Taste quality and hunger interactions in a feeding sensorimotor circuit
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation, sugar GRN activation
neural_activity: Fdg did not respond to proboscis taste stimulation but responded to optogenetic activation of sugar-sensing GRNs. Bract is a descending neuron.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation elicited robust proboscis extension. Inhibition did not influence PER to 50 mM sucrose. Activation rates did not differ between fed and food-deprived flies.
ecological_context: Coordinate proboscis extension with other feeding subprograms via descending output.
summary: Third-order neurons including Fdg elicit proboscis extension upon activation but are not modulated by hunger state.

paper_title: Taste quality and hunger interactions in a feeding sensorimotor circuit
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation, sugar/bitter GRN activation
neural_activity: Roundup responded to sugar GRN activation but not bitter GRN activation; Roundup response was dramatically decreased upon co-activation of sugar and bitter GRNs.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation elicited proboscis extension. Inhibition did not influence PER. Bitter signals suppress Roundup activity to prevent feeding.
ecological_context: Local integration of sweet and bitter taste to reject sweet foods laced with bitter compounds.
summary: Premotor neurons integrate sweet and bitter taste; bitter signals suppress activity to inhibit feeding initiation.

paper_title: Taste quality and hunger interactions in a feeding sensorimotor circuit
experimental_stimulus: Bitter GRN activation
neural_activity: Receives over 150 synapses from bitter GRNs. Synapses directly onto Roundup and Rounddown.
behavioral_consequence: Inhibits feeding initiation by suppressing Roundup activity in the presence of bitter compounds.
ecological_context: Bitter taste avoidance mechanism to prevent ingestion of noxious compounds.
summary: Scapula is a second-order bitter neuron that synapses onto premotor neurons to inhibit feeding initiation.

paper_title: Taste quality and hunger interactions in a feeding sensorimotor circuit
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation
neural_activity: N/A
behavioral_consequence: Activation elicited proboscis extension. Activation rates were the same in food-deprived and fed flies.
ecological_context: Essential for extension of the rostrum during feeding initiation.
summary: MN9 is necessary and sufficient for rostrum extension; its activation rate is not modulated by hunger state.

paper_title: Taste quality and hunger interactions in a feeding sensorimotor circuit
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation, food-deprived vs fed state
neural_activity: Hunger modulates sugar GRN activity.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation caused higher proboscis extension rates in food-deprived flies than in fed flies.
ecological_context: Hunger signals act on sensory neurons to increase detection sensitivity for sugar.
summary: Sugar-sensing GRNs show higher feeding initiation rates upon activation in food-deprived flies compared to fed flies.

paper_title: Mapping the Neural Dynamics of Locomotion across the Drosophila Brain
experimental_stimulus: Walking in darkness (tethered, treadmill ball)
neural_activity: Synapses spatially overlap with voxels correlated with ipsilateral turning velocity
behavioral_consequence: Identified as candidate neurons underlying rotational velocity signals
ecological_context: Walking maneuvers involving turning
summary: DNa02, DNa03, and DNa04 descending neurons were identified as candidates underlying turning velocity signals based on overlap with functional voxels correlated with angular velocity.

paper_title: Mapping the Neural Dynamics of Locomotion across the Drosophila Brain

behavioral_consequence: Identified as candidate neurons underlying forward velocity signals
ecological_context: Walking maneuvers involving forward movement
summary: MBONs were identified as candidates underlying forward velocity signals based on overlap with functional voxels correlated with forward velocity.

paper_title: Mapping the Neural Dynamics of Locomotion across the Drosophila Brain
experimental_stimulus: Walking in darkness (tethered, treadmill ball)
neural_activity: Identified as part of the forward velocity network; synapses align with voxels correlated with forward velocity
behavioral_consequence: Identified as candidate neurons underlying forward velocity signals

paper_title: Mapping the Neural Dynamics of Locomotion across the Drosophila Brain
