paper_title: A functionally ordered visual feature map in the Drosophila brain.
experimental_stimulus: Moving objects of varied size and speed, flicker stimuli, contrast changes (ON/OFF transitions)
neural_activity: Detects very small objects smaller than the lens resolution; responds strongly to fast sequential contrast changes (Dt ≈ 60 ms); selective for intermediate contrast objects; not directionally selective
ecological_context: Detecting very small objects may be critical for early initiation of behaviors, such as orienting toward food sources or conspecifics
techniques_used: ['in vivo two-photon calcium imaging', 'computational modeling', 'electron microscopy (connectome)']
summary: LC18 neurons detect very small moving objects by comparing local contrast changes in time at the same location, utilizing a temporal-contrast mechanism rather than spatial comparisons.

paper_title: A functionally ordered visual feature map in the Drosophila brain.
experimental_stimulus: Moving objects, looming rings vs. disks, thin bars
neural_activity: Encodes motion of thin bars but not edges; responds to looming ring but not looming disk; requires simultaneous opposite contrast polarity changes across neighboring spatial positions
summary: LC25 neurons detect line-like features and respond to thin bar motion, requiring opposite contrast polarity changes across neighboring positions.

paper_title: A functionally ordered visual feature map in the Drosophila brain.
experimental_stimulus: Looming objects, moving objects
neural_activity: Exhibits strongest responses to looming; inhibited for anything but centered looming; dark over bright selective
ecological_context: Downstream integration of looming signals serves to detect approaching objects such as predators
summary: LPLC2 neurons are ultra-selective detectors for centered looming motion with strong inhibition for non-centered stimuli.

paper_title: A functionally ordered visual feature map in the Drosophila brain.
experimental_stimulus: Moving dark objects, looming, directionally tuned motion
neural_activity: Shows directionally selective responses; exhibits strong responses to looming; dark over bright selective
summary: LC4 neurons respond to looming and show directionally selective responses to motion, unlike most other LC types.

paper_title: A functionally ordered visual feature map in the Drosophila brain.
experimental_stimulus: Moving objects, looming
neural_activity: Selective for small objects; responds strongly to moving objects but weakly to looming; size selectivity maintained across speeds with peak shifting
ecological_context: Detection of very small objects may be critical for orienting toward food sources or conspecifics
summary: LC11 neurons are tuned to small moving objects with weak looming responses, maintaining size selectivity across a range of speeds.

paper_title: A functionally ordered visual feature map in the Drosophila brain.
experimental_stimulus: Moving objects, looming, moving bars
neural_activity: Selective for long, thin bars; responds strongly to moving objects, weakly to looming
summary: LC15 neurons are tuned to long, thin bar motion rather than general object motion or looming.

paper_title: A functionally ordered visual feature map in the Drosophila brain.
experimental_stimulus: Looming, moving objects
neural_activity: Responds strongly to looming; not strongly size tuned (responds strongly to any object smaller than receptive-field size)
summary: LC12 neurons are strongly looming-responsive and exhibit minimal size tuning for object motion.

paper_title: A functionally ordered visual feature map in the Drosophila brain.
experimental_stimulus: Looming, moving objects
neural_activity: Responds strongly to looming; exhibits minimal responses to object motion stimuli
summary: LC17 neurons are primarily looming-responsive with minimal responses to non-looming object motion.

paper_title: A functionally ordered visual feature map in the Drosophila brain.
experimental_stimulus: Looming, moving objects
neural_activity: Responds to looming; dark over bright selective
summary: LPLC1 neurons are looming-responsive and selective for dark object motion.

paper_title: A functionally ordered visual feature map in the Drosophila brain.
experimental_stimulus: Moving objects, looming
neural_activity: Responds selectively to both small objects and thin bars; weak looming response
summary: LC21 neurons respond selectively to small objects and thin bars in either vertical or horizontal orientations.

paper_title: The anterior paired lateral neuron normalizes odour-evoked activity in the Drosophila mushroom body calyx
experimental_stimulus: Odours (4-methylcyclohexanol, 3-octanol, delta-decalactone, pentyl acetate, methyl palmitate, farnesol) presented as 5 s puffs
neural_activity: Odour-evoked calcium transients detected in the calyx via in vivo imaging. Response scales with Projection Neuron (PN) input strength (e.g. higher for Oct than Mch). Response is regionalized within the calyx volume corresponding to PN input distribution.
ecological_context: To identify and memorize discrete but similar environmental inputs; navigating through a complex and variable environment.
techniques_used: ['Two-photon in vivo calcium imaging', 'Electron microscopy (EM) connectomics']
summary: APL neurons respond to odours with variable calcium transients that scale with PN input strength and are regionally localized within the calyx. EM data confirm reciprocal synapses with PNs and KCs.

paper_title: The anterior paired lateral neuron normalizes odour-evoked activity in the Drosophila mushroom body calyx
experimental_stimulus: Odours (4-methylcyclohexanol, 3-octanol, delta-decalactone, pentyl acetate, methyl palmitate, farnesol) presented as 5 s puffs
neural_activity: Odour-evoked calcium transients in antennal lobe dendrites and calyx boutons. Response strength varies with odour identity (e.g. higher for Oct than Mch) while number of responding glomeruli/boutons remains similar. PN boutons show regionalized distribution in the calyx (e.g. PA activates posterior, MP/FA activates anterior).
ecological_context: Delivers sensory information from 51 distinct olfactory glomeruli to the mushroom body for memory formation and retrieval.
techniques_used: ['Two-photon in vivo calcium imaging', 'Electron microscopy (EM) connectomics']
summary: PNs exhibit odour-dependent variability in calcium response strength in the calyx, which correlates with APL activation levels.

paper_title: The anterior paired lateral neuron normalizes odour-evoked activity in the Drosophila mushroom body calyx
experimental_stimulus: Odours (4-methylcyclohexanol, 3-octanol, delta-decalactone, pentyl acetate, methyl palmitate, farnesol) presented as 5 s puffs
neural_activity: Odour-evoked calcium transients in KC claws (microglomeruli). Average postsynaptic response is normalized (homogeneous) across odours when APL is active. When APL is silenced (Tetanus toxin), KC claw responses become variable, mimicking PN bouton activity. Number of active MGs varies with odour strength.
ecological_context: Supports pattern separation and sparse coding required for stimuli discrimination and efficient associative memory formation.
techniques_used: ['Two-photon in vivo calcium imaging', 'APL silencing (Tetanus toxin light chain)']
summary: KC claw responses are normalized by APL-mediated inhibition. Silencing APL output removes this normalization, leading to variable odour representations at KC postsynapses.

paper_title: Flygenvectors: The spatial and temporal structure of neural activity across the fly brain
experimental_stimulus: Spontaneous locomotion (running on spherical treadmill; suspension/flailing)
neural_activity: Calcium imaging (nls-GCaMP6s) showed reliably higher activity during bouts of running compared to quiescence; little activity during quiescence.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested via manipulation. No egg-laying behavior was observed while flies were on the treadmill; activity correlated with running did not trigger egg-laying in this context.
ecological_context: Suggests neurons with highly specified functions receive knowledge of the larger behavioral context (locomotion), requiring gating mechanisms to ensure behaviors occur at the right time and place.
summary: oviDN neurons show increased calcium activity during running compared to quiescence but do not trigger egg-laying during treadmill locomotion, suggesting gating mechanisms regulate behavior.

paper_title: Flygenvectors: The spatial and temporal structure of neural activity across the fly brain
experimental_stimulus: Spontaneous running
neural_activity: Calcium imaging showed many Dilp neurons exhibited an inverse relationship (anticorrelation) with running activity.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested (observational imaging only).
ecological_context: Not explicitly tested in this paper.
summary: Dilp neurons in the Pars Intercerebralis show anticorrelated activity with running compared to the majority of brain neurons.

paper_title: Flygenvectors: The spatial and temporal structure of neural activity across the fly brain
experimental_stimulus: Spontaneous running
neural_activity: Calcium imaging showed Dh44 neurons exhibited an inverse relationship (anticorrelation) with running activity.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested (observational imaging only).
ecological_context: Not explicitly tested in this paper.
summary: Dh44 neurons in the Pars Intercerebralis show anticorrelated activity with running compared to the majority of brain neurons.

paper_title: Cell types and neuronal circuitry underlying female aggression in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation using CsChrimson with continuous or pulsed light (0.04–0.4 mW/mm2); Genetic inactivation using Tetanus toxin (TNTe)
neural_activity: No natural stimulus response measured; expression confirmed as acetylcholine and sNPF via RNAseq; activity induced via optogenetics
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation increased aggression (head butting, fencing), chasing, and touching in females, even without food restriction or opponent presence; Inactivation reduced aggression under food-restricted conditions; Aggression reduced in dark arena
ecological_context: Paper notes natural female aggression increases with nutrient limitation and near egg-laying sites; aIPg activation drives aggression independent of these environmental cues
summary: aIPg neurons (types 1-3) optogenetic activation increases female aggression and social interaction regardless of food status, while inactivation reduces aggression under food restriction; circuit connectomics shows visual gating role.

paper_title: Cell types and neuronal circuitry underlying female aggression in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation using CsChrimson with continuous light; Genetic inactivation using Tetanus toxin (TNTe)
neural_activity: No natural stimulus response measured; expression confirmed as acetylcholine via RNAseq; activity induced via optogenetics
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation increased aggression (head butting, fencing), chasing, and touching; Inactivation did not reduce aggression significantly
ecological_context: Paper notes links to oviposition and mating circuits (pC1a, vpoDN); aggression naturally associated with egg-laying sites and resource competition
summary: pC1d activation promotes female aggression, but is not essential for aggression; pC1d is interconnected with aIPg and other social behavior neurons.

paper_title: Cell types and neuronal circuitry underlying female aggression in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation using CsChrimson with continuous light
neural_activity: No natural stimulus response measured; expression confirmed as acetylcholine via RNAseq
behavioral_consequence: Activation did not significantly alter aggression or social behaviors compared to controls
ecological_context: N/A
summary: pC1e activation alone does not promote female aggression under tested conditions, despite connectivity with aIPg neurons.

paper_title: Neural circuit mechanisms of sexual receptivity in Drosophila females
experimental_stimulus: Male courtship song (pulse song), optogenetic activation/silencing, mating status (mated vs virgin)
neural_activity: Robust elevation of calcium levels in soma and neurites upon playback of male courtship song. Song responses attenuated upon mating. Depolarized upon photoactivation of vpoENs and pC1 neurons (whole-cell recordings).
behavioral_consequence: Photoactivation reliably triggered Vaginal Plate Opening (VPO) in isolated females. Acute optogenetic silencing or genetic ablation prevented copulation and dramatically reduced frequency of VPO in paired females. Response to song reduced in mated females.
ecological_context: VPO signals willingness to mate. Drosophila melanogaster uses a strategy where females mate infrequently but use sperm from a single copulation over an extended period. Wild-caught females are often multiply mated, suggesting the circuit may allow re-mating if courted by a male of higher quality.
summary: vpoDNs are female-specific descending neurons that trigger VPO. They integrate excitatory input from auditory neurons and mating status signals. Song-evoked calcium responses are attenuated in mated females.

paper_title: Neural circuit mechanisms of sexual receptivity in Drosophila females
experimental_stimulus: Male courtship song (pulse, sine, white noise, aggressive), artificial songs with varying Inter-Pulse Intervals (IPI)
neural_activity: Robust calcium response to pulse songs with IPI near 35ms. No response to sine song, white noise, or antagonistic sounds. Basal activity and song responses not lower in mated females compared to virgins.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic silencing significantly reduced frequency of copulation and VPO. Optogenetic activation elicited VPO in isolated females. vpoENs were significantly less potent at eliciting VPO in mated than in virgin females.
ecological_context: Tuning to conspecific song features (IPI near 35ms) ensures species recognition and appropriate sexual receptivity responses.
summary: vpoENs are cholinergic auditory neurons tuned to conspecific pulse song. They provide excitatory input to vpoDNs and promote VPO, though their potency is reduced in mated females.

paper_title: Neural circuit mechanisms of sexual receptivity in Drosophila females
experimental_stimulus: Male courtship song, artificial songs
neural_activity: Broadly tuned calcium response to pulse song across wide IPIs, aggressive sound, and weakly to sine song/white noise. Basal activity and song responses indistinguishable between mated and virgin females.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation suppressed mating and VPO. Silencing had no obvious effect on receptivity or VPO.
ecological_context: Broad tuning combined with vpoEN specificity may ensure vpoDNs are finely tuned to the conspecific courtship song.
summary: vpoINs are GABAergic inhibitory neurons with broad sound tuning that suppress VPO and receptivity when activated.

paper_title: Neural circuit mechanisms of sexual receptivity in Drosophila females
experimental_stimulus: Mating status (mated vs virgin), optogenetic activation/silencing
neural_activity: Activity lower in mated females than virgins due to silencing of SPSN-SAG pathway. Photoactivation elicited strong depolarization and action potentials in vpoDNs (whole-cell recordings). Transient activation rendered vpoDNs more sensitive to courtship song.
behavioral_consequence: Silencing pC1 neurons in virgin females suppressed VPO to mated levels. Photoactivation of pC1 cells in mated females restored VPO and sexual receptivity. Effect persisted for up to 25 seconds after photoactivation.
ecological_context: Encode the female's mating status (presence of sperm via Sex Peptide pathway), modulating sexual receptivity based on internal physiological state.
summary: pC1 neurons encode mating status and provide excitatory input to vpoDNs, integrating internal state with external song signals to control mating decisions.

paper_title: Localized inhibition in the Drosophila mushroom body
experimental_stimulus: Electric shock (abdomen and legs), Odor (isoamyl acetate), Local ATP application (via P2X2 channel), Heat (via dTRPA1 activation of Kenyon cells)
neural_activity: Spatially restricted Ca2+ responses measured by volumetric two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6f). Electric shock evokes strong response in vertical lobe tip, weaker in other regions. Odor evokes stronger response in vertical lobe stalk. Local ATP activation evokes strong local response that decays to undetectable levels within ~100–250 μm. Activation of specific Kenyon cell subsets (αβ vs α′β′) activates corresponding APL lobes without cross-propagation. Low expression of voltage-gated Na+ and Ca2+ channel mRNAs compared to other mushroom body neurons.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested in this study.
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed in terms of natural ecological context; stimuli relate to olfactory aversive conditioning paradigms (electric shock, odor).
techniques_used: ['Two-photon volumetric calcium imaging', 'RNA-seq analysis', 'Local pressure ejection of ATP/GABA', 'Heat activation (dTRPA1)']
summary: APL neurons exhibit spatially restricted Ca2+ activity in response to sensory stimuli and local activation, with limited activity propagation along neurites, consistent with low voltage-gated ion channel expression.

paper_title: Localized inhibition in the Drosophila mushroom body
experimental_stimulus: Odor (isoamyl acetate), Local APL activation (ATP), Local GABA application, Spontaneous activity
neural_activity: Exhibit spontaneous and odor-evoked Ca2+ influx measured by volumetric two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6f). Local APL activation (ATP) strongly reduces baseline and odor-evoked KC Ca2+ activity locally, with inhibition extending to regions where APL activity is undetectable. Local GABA application mimics this inhibitory profile.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested in this study.
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed in terms of natural ecological context; stimuli relate to olfactory processing and learning paradigms.
techniques_used: ['Two-photon volumetric calcium imaging', 'Local pressure ejection of ATP/GABA']
summary: Kenyon cells show spontaneous and odor-evoked Ca2+ activity that is locally inhibited by APL activation, with inhibitory effects spreading further than APL activity itself.

paper_title: The neural basis for a persistent internal state in Drosophila females
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation using ReaChR for 5 minutes with variable delay (0, 3, 6 min) before male introduction; Silencing using TNT during interaction
neural_activity: Behavioral experiments measured output; calcium imaging used specific subtypes (pC1-A/S) rather than this pool directly
behavioral_consequence: Silencing diminishes receptivity and response to male song. Activation increases receptivity (at 0 min delay) and persistent shoving/chasing (at 3 min delay)
ecological_context: Modulates female receptivity and responses to male courtship song during social interactions, impacting mating decisions and reproduction
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics', 'Behavioral Tracking (JAABA, SVM, SLEAP)', 'Cox proportional hazards regression']
summary: pC1-Int neurons modulate female receptivity and song responses; specific subtypes drive persistent aggressive and male-like behaviors following activation.

paper_title: The neural basis for a persistent internal state in Drosophila females
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation using ReaChR for 5 minutes; Whole-brain calcium imaging (GCaMP6s); Patch clamp recordings
neural_activity: Patch clamp shows depolarization to courtship song features. Calcium imaging shows persistent activity in Dsx+ pC1 somas and Fru+ cells lasting minutes after activation offset. EM reveals recurrent connectivity with aIPg-b/c neurons
behavioral_consequence: Drives persistent shoving and chasing. Does not affect female receptivity
ecological_context: Drives aggressive and male-like courtship behaviors during interactions with males; male song is predicted to drive these neurons during natural courtship interactions
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics', 'Two-photon Calcium Imaging', 'Patch Clamp', 'Electron Microscopy (FlyWire, FAFB)', 'Behavioral Tracking']
summary: pC1d/e neurons drive persistent shoving and chasing behaviors without affecting receptivity; they show persistent neural activity and recurrent connectivity with aIPg neurons.

paper_title: The neural basis for a persistent internal state in Drosophila females
cell_type: pC1-S (Other pC1 neurons, R71G01.AD x Dsx.DBD)
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation using ReaChR for 5 minutes
neural_activity: Not directly imaged in calcium imaging experiments
behavioral_consequence: No effect on receptivity, shoving, or chasing. Contains cells that control female receptivity (as pC1-Int cells other than pC1d/e drive receptivity)
ecological_context: Contributes to female receptivity state during social interactions
summary: pC1-S neurons do not drive shoving or chasing; they contain the cells within pC1-Int that control female receptivity.

paper_title: The neural basis for a persistent internal state in Drosophila females
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation using ReaChR for 5 minutes; Silencing using TNT
neural_activity: No persistent activity in pCd1 cells following pC1d/e activation (calcium imaging)
behavioral_consequence: Silencing reduces receptivity. Activation has no persistent effect on receptivity, shoving, or chasing
ecological_context: Involved in receptivity but not the persistent aggressive/male-like state
summary: pCd1 neurons are involved in receptivity but do not drive persistent shoving or chasing behaviors following activation.

paper_title: The neural basis for a persistent internal state in Drosophila females
experimental_stimulus: None for behavior in this paper
neural_activity: Persistent calcium activity detected in Fru+ cells (likely aIPg) following pC1d/e activation. Reciprocal connectivity with pC1d/e found via EM
behavioral_consequence: Not tested in this paper
ecological_context: Part of recurrent circuit linking pC1 activation to persistent activity
summary: aIPg neurons form recurrent connections with pC1d/e and exhibit persistent neural activity following pC1 activation.

paper_title: The neural basis for a persistent internal state in Drosophila females
experimental_stimulus: None for behavior in this paper
neural_activity: No persistent activity in Dsx+ pC2 cells following pC1d/e activation (calcium imaging)
behavioral_consequence: Not tested in this paper
ecological_context: N/A
summary: pC2 neurons do not show persistent activity following pC1d/e activation.

paper_title: Octopamine neuron dependent aggression requires dVGLUT from dual-transmitting neurons
experimental_stimulus: Social interaction (male-male pairing for aggression; male-female pairing for courtship)
behavioral_consequence: Reducing dVGLUT in brain OGNs decreases aggression (lower lunge number, wing threats, increased latency to lunge). Reducing dVGLUT does not increase inter-male courtship (unlike OA deficiency). dVGLUT in VNS OGNs is required for normal male-female courtship parameters. OA and Glu signals converge on shared aggression-promoting pathway.
ecological_context: Aggression defends or obtains resources. OA responds to external chemosensory cues and male-specific pheromone information.
techniques_used: ['Immunohistochemistry', 'Genetic manipulation (RNAi, B3RT conditional null allele)', 'Behavioral assays (aggression/courtship scoring)', 'Confocal microscopy']
summary: OGNs require dVGLUT for aggression promotion; glutamate and OA signals are separable for aggression vs courtship; VMAT and dVGLUT puncta show partial spatial segregation.

paper_title: ON selectivity in the Drosophila visual system is a multisynaptic process involving both glutamatergic and GABAergic inhibition
experimental_stimulus: 5 s full-field flashes (ON/OFF steps)
neural_activity: Calcium signals (GCaMP6f) hyperpolarize to light onset (ON) and depolarize to light offset (OFF). iGluSnFR signals at Mi1/Tm3 dendrites indicate glutamatergic input from L1. Responses to visual stimuli persist even at high picrotoxin (PTX) concentrations (100 µM).
ecological_context: Visual system processing of behaviorally relevant information such as contrast and direction of motion.
techniques_used: ['In vivo two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6f)', 'Glutamate sensor imaging (iGluSnFR)']
summary: L1 lamina neurons show OFF-selective calcium signals and release glutamate. Their visual responses are resistant to PTX application, indicating they remain functional when ON pathway postsynaptic neurons are blocked.

paper_title: ON selectivity in the Drosophila visual system is a multisynaptic process involving both glutamatergic and GABAergic inhibition
experimental_stimulus: 5 s full-field flashes (ON/OFF steps)
neural_activity: Calcium signals (GCaMP6f) increase to ON steps. Responses abolished by systemic PTX (2.5-100 µM) or GluClα null mutation. Partially rescued by PTX-insensitive GluClα and Rdl alleles. Responses preserved despite cell-type-specific GluClα knockdown in Mi1.
ecological_context: Visual system processing of behaviorally relevant information such as contrast and direction of motion.
techniques_used: ['In vivo two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6f)', 'Pharmacology (Picrotoxin, MPEP)', 'Genetic manipulation (CRISPR, RNAi, FlpStop, GluClα/Rdl alleles)']
summary: Mi1 medulla neurons exhibit ON selectivity mediated by a multisynaptic process involving GluClα and Rdl receptors. ON selectivity is robust to cell-type-specific loss of GluClα in Mi1.

paper_title: ON selectivity in the Drosophila visual system is a multisynaptic process involving both glutamatergic and GABAergic inhibition
experimental_stimulus: 5 s full-field flashes (ON/OFF steps)
neural_activity: Calcium signals (GCaMP6f) increase to ON steps. Responses abolished by systemic PTX (2.5-100 µM) or GluClα null mutation. Partially rescued by PTX-insensitive GluClα and Rdl alleles. Responses preserved despite cell-type-specific GluClα knockdown in Tm3.
ecological_context: Visual system processing of behaviorally relevant information such as contrast and direction of motion.
techniques_used: ['In vivo two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6f)', 'Pharmacology (Picrotoxin, MPEP)', 'Genetic manipulation (CRISPR, RNAi, FlpStop, GluClα/Rdl alleles)']
summary: Tm3 medulla neurons exhibit ON selectivity mediated by GluClα and Rdl receptors. Like Mi1, ON selectivity is distributed and robust to cell-type-specific loss of GluClα.

paper_title: ON selectivity in the Drosophila visual system is a multisynaptic process involving both glutamatergic and GABAergic inhibition
experimental_stimulus: Moving ON edges (8 directions), 5 s full-field flashes
neural_activity: Calcium signals (GCaMP6f) in axon terminals and dendrites. Respond to ON edges and flashes. ON responses completely abolished in GluClα null mutants. Reveals GABAergic inhibition (calcium decrease) to ON flashes in GluClα null background.
ecological_context: Visual system processing of behaviorally relevant information such as contrast and direction of motion.
summary: T4 direction-selective cells require GluClα for ON responses to edges and flashes. Loss of GluClα unmasks GABAergic inhibition onto T4 dendrites.

paper_title: ON selectivity in the Drosophila visual system is a multisynaptic process involving both glutamatergic and GABAergic inhibition
experimental_stimulus: Moving OFF edges (8 directions), 5 s full-field flashes
neural_activity: Calcium signals (GCaMP6f) in axon terminals. Respond to OFF edges and flashes. OFF responses unaffected in GluClα null mutants.
ecological_context: Visual system processing of behaviorally relevant information such as contrast and direction of motion.
summary: T5 direction-selective cells mediate OFF responses which remain intact despite GluClα loss of function, demonstrating pathway specificity.

paper_title: Auditory activity is diverse and widespread throughout the central brain of Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Auditory stimuli (pulse, sine, white noise)
neural_activity: Activity reduced by 99% in iav1 mutant flies compared to wild-type; activity is transduced through these mechanoreceptor neurons.
ecological_context: Courtship song is the main auditory cue to which flies respond.
summary: JONs are identified as the primary transducers of auditory activity, as 99% of auditory-evoked responses were eliminated in iav1 mutant flies.

paper_title: Auditory activity is diverse and widespread throughout the central brain of Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Auditory stimuli (pulse, sine, white noise)
neural_activity: Auditory responses detected in somas of AL PNs using GH146-Gal4 genetic lines.
ecological_context: Olfactory pathway integration with auditory signals.
summary: AL PNs exhibit auditory-evoked activity, demonstrating cross-modal integration in the olfactory pathway.

paper_title: Auditory activity is diverse and widespread throughout the central brain of Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Auditory stimuli (pulse, sine, white noise)
neural_activity: Auditory responses detected in somas of MB Kenyon cells using OK107-Gal4 genetic lines.
ecological_context: Olfactory pathway integration with auditory signals.
summary: MB Kenyon Cells show auditory-evoked activity, indicating auditory representation in higher-order olfactory processing regions.

paper_title: Auditory activity is diverse and widespread throughout the central brain of Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Auditory stimuli (pulse, sine, white noise, natural song)
neural_activity: Robust auditory activity found in 33 out of 36 central brain neuropils; 18 distinct temporal response types identified; activity is stereotyped in early mechanosensory regions and more variable in downstream regions.
ecological_context: Courtship song features (pulse/sine) are tuned; activity in motor regions (central/lateral complex) noted but behavioral role not determined.
techniques_used: ['Two-photon microscopy', 'Volumetric neural imaging (GCaMP6s)', 'Pan-neuronal expression', 'Volumetric registration to IVIA atlas']
summary: Auditory activity is widespread throughout the central brain, spanning olfactory, visual, and pre-motor pathways, with responses tuned to courtship song features.

paper_title: Convergence of monosynaptic and polysynaptic sensory paths onto common motor outputs in a Drosophila feeding connectome
experimental_stimulus: None (Structural reconstruction of synaptic connectivity)
neural_activity: Monosynaptic connections mapped via EM; ~90% of neurons in these compartments form direct synapses with output neurons
behavioral_consequence: Circuit identified as underlying food intake behavior and feeding motor program
ecological_context: Larval feeding behavior involving food intake and ingestion
techniques_used: ['serial section transmission electron microscopy (ssTEM)', 'Immunohistochemistry with GAL4 drivers']
summary: Seven distinct sensory synaptic compartments were reconstructed. ACa, AVa, and AVp compartments contain sensory neurons that predominantly form monosynaptic connections with feeding output neurons.

paper_title: Convergence of monosynaptic and polysynaptic sensory paths onto common motor outputs in a Drosophila feeding connectome
experimental_stimulus: None (Structural reconstruction of synaptic connectivity)
neural_activity: Receive monosynaptic inputs from sensory compartments (ACa, AVa, AVp) and polysynaptic routes
behavioral_consequence: Innervate muscles for pharyngeal pumping and head tilting; part of elemental feeding circuit
ecological_context: Food intake behavior in Drosophila larvae
summary: PMNs innervate cibarial dilator muscles for pharyngeal pumping, mouth hooks, and dorsal protractor muscles. They receive direct and polysynaptic inputs from sensory neurons.

paper_title: Convergence of monosynaptic and polysynaptic sensory paths onto common motor outputs in a Drosophila feeding connectome
experimental_stimulus: None (Structural reconstruction of synaptic connectivity)
neural_activity: Four neurons (Se0ph and Se0ens) receive substantial monosynaptic sensory inputs (~40% of input synapses)
behavioral_consequence: Innervate enteric nervous system and pharynx; proposed neuromodulatory effects on feeding system
ecological_context: Metabolic homeostasis and food intake regulation
summary: Se0 neurons project to the enteric nervous system (posterior) and pharynx (anterior). They receive dense monosynaptic inputs from enteric and pharyngeal sensory compartments.

paper_title: Convergence of monosynaptic and polysynaptic sensory paths onto common motor outputs in a Drosophila feeding connectome
experimental_stimulus: None (Structural reconstruction of synaptic connectivity)
neural_activity: Receive monosynaptic inputs from sensory neurons (ACa, AVa, AVp) and polysynaptic inputs via interneurons
behavioral_consequence: Target ring gland; regulate metabolic homeostasis and growth via Dilps, DH44, DMS
ecological_context: Metabolic regulation during feeding
summary: Three neurosecretory cell clusters express Dilps, DH44, and DMS. They receive direct sensory inputs and are targets of polysynaptic pathways involving hugin interneurons.

paper_title: Convergence of monosynaptic and polysynaptic sensory paths onto common motor outputs in a Drosophila feeding connectome
experimental_stimulus: None (Structural reconstruction of synaptic connectivity)
neural_activity: Receive inputs primarily from ACa and AVa sensory compartments (enteric origin); target mNSCs
behavioral_consequence: Part of polysynaptic pathway linking sensory inputs to neuroendocrine outputs
ecological_context: Feeding circuit integration and metabolic regulation
summary: Hugin neurons act as interneurons integrating sensory information from enteric compartments to target neuroendocrine cells, facilitating polysynaptic pathways onto feeding outputs.

paper_title: Convergence of monosynaptic and polysynaptic sensory paths onto common motor outputs in a Drosophila feeding connectome
experimental_stimulus: None (Structural reconstruction of synaptic connectivity)
neural_activity: Make monosynaptic connections to interneurons that target feeding output neurons (mNSCs, Se0, PMNs)
behavioral_consequence: Proposed 'psychomotor' pathway connecting memory circuits to feeding reflex circuits
ecological_context: Integration of memory/experience with feeding behavior
summary: MBON-f1 connects to interneurons that also comprise layers of the monosynaptic feeding circuit, suggesting a mechanism for memory circuits to modulate feeding reflexes.

paper_title: An Array of Descending Visual Interneurons Encoding Self-Motion in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Rotational visual stimuli (optic flow) around 13 horizontal axes, translational stimuli, moving gratings, yaw rotation
neural_activity: Responded with graded changes in membrane potential (no action potentials). Tuned maximally to rotation around an azimuthal axis at -139.9° relative to the fly's midline (rightward roll/down). Slow ramp in activity during stimulus epoch.
behavioral_consequence: No direct activation or inactivation tested (electrophysiological current insufficient to influence output; specific drivers unavailable). Neural tuning correlated with an increase in contralateral wing amplitude and deviation during flight.
ecological_context: Projects to the neck motor region; likely enables precise compensatory head movements to reduce motion blur during flight.
summary: DNOVS1 is a non-spiking descending neuron tuned to specific roll axes, with activity correlated with wing amplitude during flight, projecting to neck motor neuropil.

paper_title: An Array of Descending Visual Interneurons Encoding Self-Motion in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Rotational visual stimuli (optic flow) around 13 horizontal axes, translational stimuli, moving gratings, yaw rotation
neural_activity: Produced small spikes riding on graded changes in membrane potential. Tuned maximally to rotation around an azimuthal axis at +144.3° relative to the fly's midline (leftward roll/down). Increased baseline activity during flight.
behavioral_consequence: No direct activation or inactivation tested (electrophysiological current insufficient to influence output; specific drivers unavailable). Neural tuning correlated with wing amplitude; peak response axis similar to initial roll axis of body saccades.
ecological_context: Projects to neck, wing, and haltere motor neuropils; suggested role in body saccades (rapid banked turns) during flight.
summary: DNOVS2 is a spiking descending neuron tuned to a distinct roll axis, with activity correlated with wing amplitude and potential involvement in body saccades.

paper_title: An Array of Descending Visual Interneurons Encoding Self-Motion in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Rotational visual stimuli (optic flow) around 13 horizontal axes, translational stimuli, moving gratings, yaw rotation
neural_activity: Produced small spikes riding on graded changes in membrane potential. Tuned maximally to rotation around an azimuthal axis at -7.5° (roll down toward ipsilateral side). Responded more strongly to pure yaw motion compared to other descending neurons.
behavioral_consequence: No direct activation or inactivation tested (electrophysiological current insufficient to influence output; specific drivers unavailable). Neural tuning correlated with head yaw and abdominal ruddering during flight.
ecological_context: Projects to neck and haltere motor neuropils; suggests potential role in providing visual input to the haltere motor system.
summary: DNHS1 is a spiking descending neuron tuned to yaw/roll axes, with activity correlated with head yaw and abdominal ruddering during flight.

paper_title: Direct neural pathways convey distinct visual information to Drosophila mushroom bodies
experimental_stimulus: Blue and green light stimuli; 5 different odors (OCT, MCH, HEP, isoamyl acetate, apple cider vinegar)
neural_activity: Spiking responses to blue and green light stimuli; slow inhibitory responses to odors (no excitatory responses). Measured via in vivo whole-cell current-clamp recordings.
behavioral_consequence: Blockade with UAS-shits1 significantly impaired aversive color discrimination memory and green intensity learning. Olfactory memory remained intact.
ecological_context: Dendrites form a ventral accessory calyx (vAC); arrangement consistent with the specialization of the ventral retina for color processing of landmarks during foraging.
techniques_used: ['Split-GAL4 drivers (MB607B, MB419B)', 'UAS-shits1 blockade', 'Behavioral color discrimination learning assay', 'Whole-cell current-clamp recordings', 'Immunohistochemistry']
summary: γd KCs selectively respond to visual stimuli with spiking activity and are required for visual memory but not olfactory memory.

paper_title: Direct neural pathways convey distinct visual information to Drosophila mushroom bodies
experimental_stimulus: Visual input from optic lobe medulla layer M8
neural_activity: Dendrites arborize in medulla layer M8; axons project to the ventral accessory calyx (vAC) forming presynaptic boutons.
behavioral_consequence: Blockade with MB425B-GAL4/UAS-shits1 significantly impaired color discrimination memory.
ecological_context: Medulla layer M8 contains terminals of Tm5 neurons involved in color vision, consistent with ventral retina specialization for landmark color processing.
techniques_used: ['Split-GAL4 drivers (MB425B, VT008475)', 'UAS-shits1 blockade', 'Behavioral color discrimination learning assay', 'Immunohistochemistry (3D reconstruction)']
summary: VPN-MB1 neurons connect medulla layer M8 to the mushroom body vAC and are required for color discrimination memory.

paper_title: Direct neural pathways convey distinct visual information to Drosophila mushroom bodies
experimental_stimulus: Visual input from optic lobe medulla layer M7
neural_activity: Dendrites arborize in medulla layer M7; axons project to the ventral accessory calyx (vAC) forming presynaptic boutons.
behavioral_consequence: Blockade with MB334C-GAL4/UAS-shits1 significantly impaired intensity (brightness) discrimination learning but did not affect color discrimination.
ecological_context: Position of dendrites suggests light intensity encoding in medulla layer M7.
techniques_used: ['Split-GAL4 drivers (MB334C)', 'UAS-shits1 blockade', 'Behavioral intensity discrimination learning assay', 'Immunohistochemistry (3D reconstruction)']
summary: VPN-MB2 neurons connect medulla layer M7 to the mushroom body vAC and are required for intensity discrimination learning.

paper_title: Direct neural pathways convey distinct visual information to Drosophila mushroom bodies
experimental_stimulus: Odors (implied by GH146-GAL4 expression)
neural_activity: Dendrites in antennal lobe; axons project to the main calyx.
behavioral_consequence: Blockade with GH146-GAL4/UAS-shits1 did not significantly alter visual color conditioning memory.
ecological_context: Provides olfactory input to main calyx, distinct from visual input pathway to vAC.
techniques_used: ['Split-GAL4 driver (GH146)', 'UAS-shits1 blockade', 'Behavioral color discrimination learning assay']
summary: OPNs are not required for visual color discrimination memory, indicating segregation of visual and olfactory input pathways.

paper_title: Direct neural pathways convey distinct visual information to Drosophila mushroom bodies
experimental_stimulus: Blue and green light stimuli; 5 different odors
neural_activity: Many cells responded to odors but none to visual stimuli. Measured via whole-cell current-clamp recordings.
behavioral_consequence: Not explicitly tested for visual memory blockade in this study; used as contrast to γd response profile.
ecological_context: Represent typical olfactory processing in main calyx.
summary: α/β KCs show clear modality segregation compared to γd KCs, responding to odors but not visual stimuli.

paper_title: Direct neural pathways convey distinct visual information to Drosophila mushroom bodies
experimental_stimulus: N/A
neural_activity: Co-expressed with VPN-MB2 in MB334C-GAL4 driver.
behavioral_consequence: Blockade did not significantly impair brightness discrimination learning.
ecological_context: MB output pathway shared by different sensory modalities.
summary: MBON-α1 is not responsible for the intensity discrimination learning deficit caused by VPN-MB2 blockade.

paper_title: Sparse, Decorrelated Odor Coding in the Mushroom Body Enhances Learned Odor Discrimination
experimental_stimulus: Odors (ethyl acetate, δ-decalactone, isoamyl acetate:ethyl butyrate mixtures)
neural_activity: Odor-evoked Ca2+ influx in mushroom body lobes (α, α′, β, β′, γ) measured via two-photon imaging (GCaMP3). Responses are sparse (~5-10% of population). Sparseness decreases and inter-odor correlations increase when Kenyon cell or APL output is blocked (shits1, TeTx).
behavioral_consequence: Kenyon cells are the major site of olfactory associative memory storage. Disrupting the Kenyon cell-APL feedback loop prevents flies from learning to discriminate similar, but not dissimilar, odors.
ecological_context: To adapt to their environments, animals must distinguish reinforced stimuli from similar but irrelevant ones; sparse coding augments memory capacity for odor-specific memories.
summary: Kenyon cells exhibit sparse, decorrelated odor coding maintained by feedback inhibition from APL; disrupting this loop impairs learned discrimination of similar odors.

paper_title: Sparse, Decorrelated Odor Coding in the Mushroom Body Enhances Learned Odor Discrimination
experimental_stimulus: Odors (ethyl acetate, δ-DL, IA:EB mixtures), thermal activation (dTRPA1)
neural_activity: GABAergic interneuron responding to odors/KC activation with Ca2+ influx and synaptic vesicle release (spH). Inhibits Kenyon cells. Blocking APL output increases Kenyon cell odor responses.
behavioral_consequence: Blocking APL synaptic output (shits1) impairs learned discrimination of similar odors (IA:EB 1:4 vs 4:1) at 32°C but not dissimilar odors. Partial GABA knockdown (GAD RNAi) has no significant behavioral effect.
ecological_context: Feedback inhibition maintains sparse coding to support odor discrimination in environments with similar stimuli.
techniques_used: ['two-photon Ca2+ imaging (GCaMP3, spH)', 'synaptic blockade (shits1, TeTx)', 'optogenetics/thermogenetics (dTRPA1)', 'intersectional genetics', 'behavioral T-maze']
summary: The APL neuron provides all-to-all feedback inhibition to Kenyon cells; blocking it reduces coding sparseness and impairs discrimination of similar odors.

paper_title: Sparse, Decorrelated Odor Coding in the Mushroom Body Enhances Learned Odor Discrimination
experimental_stimulus: Odors (ethyl acetate)
neural_activity: Odor-evoked Ca2+ influx in calyx measured via two-photon imaging (GCaMP3). Responses did not increase when Kenyon cell output was blocked.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested directly in this paper.
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed.
summary: PN odor responses are not modulated by Kenyon cell feedback, indicating the feedback loop operates directly on the mushroom body.

paper_title: Synchronized Bilateral Synaptic Inputs to Drosophila melanogaster Neuropeptidergic Rest/Arousal Neurons
experimental_stimulus: Pharmacological application of neurotransmitters (ACh, nicotine, GABA, glutamate) and receptor antagonists (curare, alpha-bungarotoxin, picrotoxin); current injection; whole-brain explant recording (light phase ZT22–ZT23)
neural_activity: Exhibit synchronous rhythmic membrane activity (burst firing, 5–10 s periodicity) in ipsilateral and contralateral pairs. Activity requires cholinergic synaptic inputs (nAChR); blocked by TTX or nAChR antagonists. nAChR activation depolarizes membrane and increases firing. GABA and glutamate application inhibits firing via GABAA and GluCl receptors. Synchrony lost with cholinergic block, maintained with GABAergic block.
ecological_context: Drosophila exhibit daily rhythms of rest and activity (crepuscular bouts, afternoon siesta). lLNvs regulate rest and arousal patterns and are wake-promoting.
summary: lLNvs display synchronous rhythmic membrane activity dependent on bilateral cholinergic inputs; they possess excitatory nAChRs and inhibitory GABAA and GluCl receptors that modulate firing patterns.

paper_title: Synchronized Bilateral Synaptic Inputs to Drosophila melanogaster Neuropeptidergic Rest/Arousal Neurons
experimental_stimulus: Paired whole-cell patch-clamp recordings with lLNvs (whole-brain explant)
neural_activity: Exhibit partially synchronized membrane activity with lLNvs, sharing one common rhythmic peak but also demonstrating an additional peak of rhythmic activity not seen in lLNvs. Degree of synchrony significantly lower than lLNv–lLNv pairs.
summary: 201Y+ neurons receive some common rhythmic inputs with lLNvs but also unique inputs, resulting in partial synchrony compared to robust lLNv–lLNv synchrony.

paper_title: Adaptation to visual sparsity enhances responses to isolated stimuli.
experimental_stimulus: Full-field ternary noise (sparse vs. dense fill fractions), moving bars (sparse vs. dense), one-dimensional ternary noise for receptive field mapping
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium (GCaMP6f) and voltage (ArcLight) imaging showed strong sparsity adaptation. Sparse stimuli elicited biphasic filters and positive responses to both light and dark flashes. Dense stimuli elicited monophasic filters and opposite-sign responses to light and dark flashes. Receptive field center adapted, surround did not. Acetylcholine release (GACh3.0) mirrored calcium signals.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested in this study; discussion suggests potential to enhance optomotor response strength to sparse objects.
ecological_context: Natural scenes exhibit varying sparsity (e.g., sky vs. cluttered foreground); adaptation may enhance salience of infrequent objects.
techniques_used: ['Two-photon calcium imaging', 'Two-photon voltage imaging', 'Two-photon neurotransmitter imaging', 'Optogenetics (Chrimson)']
summary: Mi1 neurons show strong sparsity adaptation where sparse stimuli activate both ON and OFF pathways (light and dark), while dense stimuli recruit pathways with opposite signs.

paper_title: Adaptation to visual sparsity enhances responses to isolated stimuli.
experimental_stimulus: Moving bars (sparse vs. dense), single moving bar (velocity tuning)
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6f) showed sparse stimuli activated T4 by both light and dark bars with delayed non-preferred contrast responses. Dense stimuli elicited opposite responses to light and dark bars. Velocity tuning differed between preferred and non-preferred contrast.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested in this study; discussion suggests contribution to optomotor rotational responses and speed regulation.
ecological_context: Motion detection for flight stabilization and navigation; enhanced response to sparse moving objects may aid bottom-up saliency.
summary: T4 neurons adapt to stimulus sparsity, responding to both light and dark moving bars in sparse conditions, enhancing motion signals to infrequent stimuli.

paper_title: Adaptation to visual sparsity enhances responses to isolated stimuli.
experimental_stimulus: Moving bars (sparse vs. dense), single moving bar (velocity tuning)
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6f) showed similar sparsity adaptation to T4 with inverted polarity (OFF-pathway). Sparse stimuli activated T5 by both light and dark bars; dense stimuli elicited opposite signs.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested in this study; discussion suggests contribution to optomotor rotational responses and speed regulation.
ecological_context: Motion detection for flight stabilization and navigation; enhanced response to sparse moving objects may aid bottom-up saliency.
summary: T5 neurons adapt to stimulus sparsity, responding to both light and dark moving bars in sparse conditions, mirroring upstream ON and OFF pathway properties.

paper_title: Adaptation to visual sparsity enhances responses to isolated stimuli.
experimental_stimulus: Full-field ternary noise, moving bars
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6f) showed strong sparsity adaptation. Filters changed from biphasic (sparse) to monophasic (dense). Impulse responses showed qualitative similarity to Mi1.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested in this study.
ecological_context: Early visual processing stage; adaptation reflects sparsity statistics present in natural scenes.
summary: L1 neurons exhibit robust sparsity adaptation, changing filter shapes and impulse responses based on stimulus sparsity.

paper_title: Adaptation to visual sparsity enhances responses to isolated stimuli.
experimental_stimulus: Full-field ternary noise
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6f) showed minimal shape change in linear filters when stimulus changed from sparse to dense. Did not adapt to stimulus sparsity.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested in this study.
ecological_context: Proposed role as a luminance-sensitive neuron; lacks sparsity adaptation seen in other lamina cells.
summary: L3 neurons show minimal sparsity adaptation compared to L1 and L5, suggesting a different functional role in luminance processing.

paper_title: Adaptation to visual sparsity enhances responses to isolated stimuli.
experimental_stimulus: Full-field ternary noise, moving bars
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6f) showed sparsity adaptation similar to Mi1. Filters changed shape with sparsity; light and dark bar responses differed in shape under sparse conditions.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested in this study.
ecological_context: Parallel input to motion detectors; adaptation properties inherited by T4.
summary: Tm3 neurons show strong sparsity adaptation, mirroring Mi1 response properties and suggesting input inheritance to downstream motion detectors.

paper_title: Adaptation to visual sparsity enhances responses to isolated stimuli.
experimental_stimulus: Full-field ternary noise, moving bars
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6f) showed robust sparsity adaptation in the OFF pathway. Tm1 showed stronger filter shape changes than Tm2. Light and dark bar responses differed characteristically under sparse conditions.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested in this study.
ecological_context: Parallel inputs to motion detectors; adaptation enhances responses to sparse stimuli in the OFF pathway.
summary: Tm1 and Tm2 neurons adapt to stimulus sparsity, showing distinct filter changes and impulse responses between sparse and dense conditions.

paper_title: Small-field visual projection neurons detect translational optic flow and support walking control
experimental_stimulus: Drifting gratings (four cardinal directions), translational optic flow (Focus of Expansion at various azimuths), rotational optic flow
neural_activity: Calcium imaging in LPC glomerulus showed strong response to ipsilateral back-to-front visual motion. Responses were enhanced by contralateral back-to-back motion and suppressed by contralateral front-to-back motion.
behavioral_consequence: Silencing (Shibirets1, Kir2.1) significantly reduced forward walking speed in response to back-to-front translational motion but did not affect turning or optomotor response to rotation. Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson) reduced forward walking speed (to approximate stop) without affecting turning.
ecological_context: Regulating forward walking during navigation; detecting translational optic flow is critical for goal-directed behavior towards objects such as conspecifics, food sources, or refuge.
techniques_used: ['Two-photon calcium imaging', 'Neuronal silencing (Shibirets1, Kir2.1)', 'Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson)', 'Fly Vision Box assay', 'Tethered walking on air-supported ball']
summary: LPC1 neurons selectively encode ipsilateral back-to-front translational visual motion and regulate forward walking speed; silencing eliminates slowing in response to back-to-front motion without affecting turning.

paper_title: Small-field visual projection neurons detect translational optic flow and support walking control
experimental_stimulus: Drifting gratings (four cardinal directions), translational optic flow (Focus of Expansion at various azimuths), rotational optic flow
neural_activity: Calcium imaging in glomerulus showed selective activation by ipsilateral front-to-back visual motion. Responses were enhanced by contralateral front-to-back motion and suppressed by contralateral back-to-front motion.
behavioral_consequence: Silencing (Shibirets1) did not yield statistically significant behavioral phenotypes in forward walking or turning components in Fly Vision Box or tethered walking assays.
ecological_context: Detecting the visual consequence of forward self-motion; mechanism for discarding confounding visual motion during turning in any environment.
techniques_used: ['Two-photon calcium imaging', 'Neuronal silencing (Shibirets1)', 'Fly Vision Box assay', 'Tethered walking on air-supported ball']
summary: LLPC1 neurons selectively encode ipsilateral front-to-back translational visual motion with contralateral modulation, though silencing did not produce significant behavioral deficits.

paper_title: Small-field visual projection neurons detect translational optic flow and support walking control
experimental_stimulus: Moving gratings (four cardinal directions)
neural_activity: Calcium imaging showed primary encoding of ipsilateral front-to-back motion. The cell is GABAergic (GAD1+) and presynaptic in the LPC1 glomerulus.
behavioral_consequence: No behavioral consequences tested in this study.
ecological_context: Provides cascaded inhibition from oppositely tuned neurons to sharpen selectivity for translational motion over rotation.
techniques_used: ['Two-photon calcium imaging', 'Anatomical analysis (MCFO, EM connectome matching)', 'FISH (GAD1)']
summary: LPC-IN is a GABAergic interneuron that encodes ipsilateral front-to-back motion and provides inhibitory input to LPC1 neurons, contributing to translational motion selectivity.

paper_title: A visuomotor circuit for evasive flight turns in Drosophila.
experimental_stimulus: Laterally moving dark spot (various velocities and sizes), expanding looming disc, grating patterns
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP7f) showed responses to both translating spot and looming disc. Response amplitude was consistent across spot velocities (15-225 deg/s). Stronger response to back-to-front motion than front-to-back motion. Inputs integrate features from Lobula and Lobula Plate (LP).
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson) induced robust flight turns away from the activated side. Thermogenetic silencing (ShiTS) and synaptic silencing (TNT) reduced wing responses (L-R WBA) to both translating spot and looming disc.
ecological_context: Drives evasive flight turns in response to visual patterns indicative of imminent threats (approaching or laterally moving objects).
summary: LPLC2 neurons respond to both translating spots and looming discs, integrating features from early visual structures. Their activation drives avoidance flight turns, while silencing impairs this behavior.

paper_title: A visuomotor circuit for evasive flight turns in Drosophila.
experimental_stimulus: Visual patterns (translating spot, looming disc, grating)
neural_activity: Whole-cell patch clamping showed membrane potential and spike rate responses similar to LPLC2. Responses were phasic to bars and spots. Showed direction-selective responses and sensitivity to contralateral visual field.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation induced steering responses away from the activated side. Thermogenetic silencing (ShiTS) significantly reduced wing responses to translating spot and looming disc.
ecological_context: Postsynaptic to LPLC2 and drives evasive flight maneuvers in response to visual threats.
summary: DNp06 neurons inherit visual selectivity from LPLC2 and drive avoidance flight turns when activated; silencing reduces avoidance behavior to approaching and moving objects.

paper_title: A visuomotor circuit for evasive flight turns in Drosophila.
experimental_stimulus: Red light pulses (optogenetic activation)
neural_activity: Expression of CsChrimson in LC10 neurons.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation induced strong attraction (turning toward the activated side), contrasting with other VPNs that induced avoidance.
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed in ecological terms within this paper's results.
summary: LC10 neurons are uniquely associated with attraction behavior upon optogenetic activation, unlike other VPNs tested.

paper_title: A visuomotor circuit for evasive flight turns in Drosophila.
experimental_stimulus: Red light pulses (activation); Spot and Looming patterns (silencing)
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation induced avoidance turns. Visual responses to spot/loom not explicitly imaged for LC4 in this study.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation induced flight turns away from the activated side. Thermogenetic silencing (ShiTS) did not yield significant changes in spot- or looming-induced avoidance flight turns.
ecological_context: Involved in evasive locomotive control in flight conditions.
summary: LC4 neurons can drive avoidance turns when activated but do not appear essential for spot- or looming-induced flight turns compared to LPLC2.

paper_title: A visuomotor circuit for evasive flight turns in Drosophila.
experimental_stimulus: Spot and Looming patterns (silencing)
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging showed response to translating spot (most sensitive to slowest speed).
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation induced avoidance turns. Thermogenetic silencing reduced wing responses to translating spot; TNT silencing did not yield significant response changes to translating spot.
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed in ecological terms.
summary: LC13 neurons respond to translating spots and drive avoidance turns when activated, though their silencing effect on behavior was variable between methods.

paper_title: A visuomotor circuit for evasive flight turns in Drosophila.
experimental_stimulus: Spot and Looming patterns (silencing)
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging showed response to visual patterns.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation induced avoidance turns. Thermogenetic silencing significantly enhanced reduction in wing responses to looming disc; TNT silencing increased wing responses to looming disc.
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed in ecological terms.
summary: LC22 neurons induce avoidance turns and contribute to looming-evoked wing responses, though silencing effects varied by method.

paper_title: A visuomotor circuit for evasive flight turns in Drosophila.
experimental_stimulus: Spot and Looming patterns (silencing)
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging showed strong visual responses to looming and spot.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation induced strongest avoidance wingbeat changes. Silencing reduced wing responses to translating spot but did not significantly enhance reduction to looming disc compared to controls.
ecological_context: Discussed in context of distinct motor circuits compared to LPLC2 (e.g., walking vs flight).
summary: LPLC1 neurons can drive avoidance turns when activated but show less critical contribution to flight avoidance behavior upon silencing compared to LPLC2.

paper_title: A visuomotor circuit for evasive flight turns in Drosophila.
experimental_stimulus: Spot (various speeds), Looming disc, Grating
neural_activity: Silencing (TNT/ShiTS) significantly reduced calcium responses of LPLC2 neurons to fast-moving spot and looming disc.
behavioral_consequence: Silencing reduced wing responses to translating spot and looming disc.
ecological_context: Provide motion-sensitive inputs to LPLC2 for detecting moving objects.
summary: T4/T5 neurons provide motion inputs to LPLC2; their silencing reduces LPLC2 activity and impairs avoidance flight turns to moving objects.

paper_title: A visuomotor circuit for evasive flight turns in Drosophila.
experimental_stimulus: Red light pulses (activation); Spot and Looming patterns (silencing)
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation tested.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation decreased L-R WBA signal but not significantly compared to control. Thermogenetic silencing did not significantly affect wing response to spot or loom.

paper_title: Dynamic nonlinearities enable direction opponency in Drosophila elementary motion detectors
experimental_stimulus: Sinusoidal gratings (PD, ND, PD+ND), random dot stimuli, varying contrast and temporal frequency, orthogonal motion stimuli
neural_activity: Calcium signals (GCaMP6f) showed positive response to PD motion and little to ND motion. Response was reduced by approximately half when ND motion was added to PD motion (counterphase). Opponency persisted when chemical synaptic transmission was blocked with tetanus toxin. Suppression was tuned to temporal frequency and specific to null direction, not orthogonal motion.
behavioral_consequence: Silencing T4 synaptic output with tetanus toxin suppressed optomotor turning responses in behaving flies.
ecological_context: Local motion detection; opponency improves motion discriminability by suppressing noise generated by the local structure of natural scenes.
techniques_used: ['in vivo 2-photon imaging', 'calcium indicator (GCaMP6f)', 'tetanus toxin silencing', 'quantitative optomotor behavior']
summary: T4 neurons are first-order direction-selective and exhibit direction opponency in calcium signals that arises feedforward, independent of synaptic output, improving local motion coding fidelity.

paper_title: Glutamate Signaling in the Fly Visual System
experimental_stimulus: 1-dimensional white noise stimulus consisting of vertical bars (180° azimuth)
neural_activity: Glutamatergic phenotype confirmed by VGlut localization in axon terminals (medulla layers M1, M5). Receptive field has an OFF center (9-11°) with antagonistic ON surround (40-50°). Temporal response fits a band-pass filter (iGluSnFR low-pass 70 ms, high-pass ~400 ms). Glutamate release kinetics significantly faster than calcium signals (GCaMP6f).
ecological_context: Center-surround antagonism acts as a spatial band-pass filter, which reduces redundancy in natural images.
summary: L1 neurons are glutamatergic with OFF-center receptive fields and band-pass temporal filtering; glutamate release kinetics are faster than calcium signals.

Temporal response fits a low-pass filter (iGluSnFR time constant 75 ms). Glutamate release kinetics significantly faster than calcium signals (GCaMP6f).
