experimental_stimulus: Low concentration ethyl acetate (1×10−6 dilution) delivered as 40 ms pulses at 5-10 Hz; direct current injection to DM1 soma.
neural_activity: Spike rates adapt modestly over time; encodes temporal odor patterns. Axons diverge to form excitatory synapses onto LHN1 and LHN2. Synapses onto LHN1 show depression, while synapses onto LHN2 show facilitation.
behavioral_consequence: Activation of DM1 (via optogenetic activation of cognate ORNs) evokes time-locked increases in upwind walking velocity in freely walking flies.
ecological_context: Odor-guided upwind walking in a wind tunnel; odor pulse frequencies loosely model intermittent patterns of natural odor plumes and signal proximity to the odor source.
summary: DM1 PNs encode temporal odor patterns and diverge onto LHN1 and LHN2 with distinct synaptic dynamics (depression vs. facilitation), driving upwind walking behavior when activated.

paper_title: Divergent synaptic dynamics originate parallel pathways for computation and behavior in an olfactory circuit.
experimental_stimulus: Ethyl acetate odor pulses (5-10 Hz); current injection; pharmacological/genetic perturbations (dnATPase expression).
neural_activity: Generates sustained responses to odor with positive steady-state spike rates. Adapts divisively (gain control) via Na+/K+ ATPase. Filter width decreases with adaptation.
behavioral_consequence: Silencing LHN1 synaptic output abolishes low-intensity maintenance of upwind walking velocity (during 2.5 Hz stimulation) but not the high-intensity surge (during 10 Hz stimulation).
ecological_context: Contributes to sustained tracking of odor sources during walking navigation in odor plumes.
summary: LHN1 neurons provide sustained responses and divisively adapt to odor, contributing specifically to maintenance of upwind velocity during low-intensity stimulation.

paper_title: Divergent synaptic dynamics originate parallel pathways for computation and behavior in an olfactory circuit.
experimental_stimulus: Ethyl acetate odor pulses (5-10 Hz); current injection; presynaptic perturbations (Unc13B knockdown in DM1, EGTA dialysis).
neural_activity: Generates transient responses that return to zero after adaptation. Adapts subtractively (tuning curve shift). DM1-LHN2 synapses show short-term facilitation dependent on Unc13B and presynaptic calcium.
behavioral_consequence: Silencing LHN2 synaptic output abolishes the high-intensity surge of upwind walking velocity (during 10 Hz stimulation) but preserves low-intensity maintenance (during 2.5 Hz stimulation).
ecological_context: Contributes to detecting rapid increases in odor intensity (temporal novelty) during walking navigation in odor plumes.
summary: LHN2 neurons provide transient responses and subtractively adapt to odor, contributing specifically to the surge in upwind velocity during high-intensity stimulation via presynaptic facilitation.

paper_title: Dynamics of glia and neurons regulate homeostatic rest, sleep and feeding behavior in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Walking and immobility behavior, CO2 gas exposure, Optogenetic activation
neural_activity: Calcium levels increase during active states (walking/effort) and decrease during rest/sleep. Dynamics follow a homeostatic model (exponential rise/fall). Calcium saturates during sleep deprivation. Fast calcium peaks occur at walking onset after immobility. CO2 exposure induces a slow calcium increase and overshoot upon offset.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation is sufficient to induce immobility (rest) and proboscis extension. Sleep deprivation induces rebound sleep and calcium reset.
ecological_context: Regulates short, naturally occurring rest and sleep bouts in Drosophila. Links homeostatic rest/sleep to metabolic activity (CO2/pH).
summary: EG calcium levels track behavioral activity and rest, saturate during sleep deprivation, and optogenetic activation induces immobility, linking metabolic activity to homeostatic rest.

paper_title: Dynamics of glia and neurons regulate homeostatic rest, sleep and feeding behavior in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Walking and immobility behavior, CO2 gas exposure, Optogenetic activation
neural_activity: Calcium levels increase during activity and decrease during rest. Show stronger circadian modulation and faster time constants than EG. Respond to CO2 with slow increase and overshoot similar to EG.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation is sufficient to induce immobility (rest) and proboscis extension.
ecological_context: Act as locally acting homeostats for short, naturally occurring rest and sleep bouts.
summary: AL calcium levels show homeostatic and circadian dynamics, and optogenetic activation induces immobility, suggesting a role in homeostatic rest.

paper_title: Dynamics of glia and neurons regulate homeostatic rest, sleep and feeding behavior in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Walking and immobility behavior
neural_activity: Calcium activity correlates with active/rest epochs but correlations are considerably lower than EG. Calcium activity does not fit the sleep homeostat model as well as EG.
behavioral_consequence: No optogenetic manipulation tested in this paper; dynamics compared to glia.
ecological_context: Previously described homeostatic sleep-regulating neurons in the ellipsoid body.
summary: R5 neurons show weaker correlation with sleep homeostasis dynamics compared to EG, suggesting EG better represent sleep homeostasis.

paper_title: Dynamics of glia and neurons regulate homeostatic rest, sleep and feeding behavior in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Walking and immobility behavior, Feeding (hungry/fed epochs), Sleep deprivation
neural_activity: Calcium activity correlates with walking speed and rapidly resets during feeding. Activity increases with time since last feeding (hunger). Activity fits a 'hunger-walk' model better than a sleep homeostat model.
behavioral_consequence: No optogenetic manipulation tested in this paper; dynamics compared to homeostatic models.
ecological_context: Encodes feeding homeostasis rather than sleep regulation.
summary: dFB neurons encode hunger and walking state, with activity better described by a hunger-walk model than a sleep homeostat model.

paper_title: Inhibitory control explains locomotor statistics in walking Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (Chrimson) and silencing (GtACR) with light; Odor pulse (1% apple cider vinegar)
neural_activity: Not measured in this study; neurons predicted to be glutamatergic based on connectomics
behavioral_consequence: Activation decreases ground speed, increases angular velocity, and increases curvature (mimicking offset search signatures). Activation increases angular velocity at odor offset. Silencing decreases angular velocity at odor offset. No significant effect on baseline motor output.
ecological_context: Regulates local search behavior following odor loss during foraging to relocate food sources.
summary: SS49952-labeled LAL neurons (LAL089, LAL091, LAL093) modulate locomotor statistics to induce offset search signatures (slower speed, higher turning) upon activation and regulate angular velocity at odor offset.

paper_title: Inhibitory control explains locomotor statistics in walking Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (Chrimson) and silencing (GtACR) with light; Odor pulse
neural_activity: Not measured in this study; predicted to be glutamatergic
behavioral_consequence: Activation decreases mean ground speed and shifts ground speed distribution to lower values. Silencing increases mean ground speed and shifts distribution to higher values. Bidirectionally regulates walking speed and scales length of odor-evoked runs.
ecological_context: Modulates walking speed and run duration during foraging responses to olfactory cues.
summary: LAL073 neurons bidirectionally regulate ground speed, with activation slowing walking and silencing speeding it, thereby influencing odor-evoked run length.

paper_title: Inhibitory control explains locomotor statistics in walking Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (Chrimson) and silencing (GtACR) with light
neural_activity: Not measured in this study
behavioral_consequence: Activation reduces groundspeed. Silencing had no significant effect on motor output.
ecological_context: Contributes to walking speed control alongside LAL073 in premotor circuits.
summary: SMP092 neurons contribute to speed control, with activation reducing ground speed, though less robustly than the full Throttle-Gal4 population.

paper_title: Rewiring an olfactory circuit by altering the combinatorial code of cell-surface proteins
experimental_stimulus: male pheromone
neural_activity: not explicitly detailed in abstract
behavioral_consequence: normal function inhibits male-male courtship; after rewiring to new PN partner, connection promotes courtship behavior
ecological_context: male pheromone sensing in Drosophila olfactory circuit
techniques_used: ['synaptic partner matching', 'alteration of combinatorial code of cell-surface proteins']
summary: A single ORN type that senses male pheromone normally inhibits male-male courtship. When its CSP combinatorial code was altered to switch connection to a new PN type, this changed the odor response and markedly increased male-male courtship.

paper_title: Rewiring an olfactory circuit by altering the combinatorial code of cell-surface proteins
experimental_stimulus: not explicitly detailed in abstract
neural_activity: not explicitly detailed in abstract
behavioral_consequence: associated with inhibiting male-male courtship under normal conditions
ecological_context: normal Drosophila olfactory circuit function
summary: Endogenous PN partner of the ORN type is involved in inhibiting male-male courtship behavior.

paper_title: Rewiring an olfactory circuit by altering the combinatorial code of cell-surface proteins
experimental_stimulus: not explicitly detailed in abstract
neural_activity: changed odor response after anatomical rewiring
behavioral_consequence: promotes courtship behavior when connected to ORN after CSP manipulation
ecological_context: rewired Drosophila olfactory circuit
techniques_used: ['synaptic partner matching', 'alteration of combinatorial code of cell-surface proteins']
summary: New PN partner that the ORN was rewired to connect with; this anatomical switch changed the odor response and markedly increased male-male courtship.

paper_title: Rewiring an olfactory circuit by altering the combinatorial code of cell-surface proteins
experimental_stimulus: not explicitly detailed in abstract
neural_activity: not explicitly detailed in abstract
behavioral_consequence: successfully rewired to multiple distinct PN types using generalized manipulation strategies
ecological_context: Drosophila olfactory circuit
techniques_used: ['increasing repulsion with old partner', 'decreasing repulsion with new partner', 'matching attraction with new partner']
summary: A second ORN type was successfully rewired to multiple distinct PN types using three manipulation strategies: increasing repulsion with old partner, decreasing repulsion with new partner, and matching attraction with new partner.

paper_title: A neural correlate of individual odor preference in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: 12 odors + air (including 3-octanol and 4-methylcyclohexanol)
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6m) and immunohistochemistry (Brp-Short presynaptic density) revealed idiosyncratic calcium dynamics and structural variation across individuals.
behavioral_consequence: Natural calcium responses (PC1) predicted odor-vs-air preference (total response inversely correlated with preference for 3-octanol). ORN calcium responses did not predict odor-vs-odor preference. Presynaptic density (DM2 vs DC2) predicted odor-vs-odor preference. Activation/inactivation not tested.
ecological_context: Behavioral variation supported by this circuit variation may be beneficial, supporting a bet-hedging strategy to counter environmental fluctuations.
summary: ORN calcium responses and presynaptic density predict individual odor preferences; ORN activity predicts odor-vs-air choice, while structural density predicts odor-vs-odor choice.

paper_title: A neural correlate of individual odor preference in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: 12 odors + air (including 3-octanol and 4-methylcyclohexanol)
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6m) revealed idiosyncratic calcium dynamics, with responses more variable than ORNs.
behavioral_consequence: Natural calcium responses (PC1) predicted odor-vs-air preference. Natural calcium responses (PC2, specifically DM2 vs DC2 contrast) predicted odor-vs-odor preference. Activation/inactivation not tested.
ecological_context: Behavioral variation supported by this circuit variation may be beneficial, supporting a bet-hedging strategy to counter environmental fluctuations.
summary: PN calcium responses predict individual odor preferences; specific contrast in DM2 vs DC2 activation predicts odor-vs-odor choice.

paper_title: Nonlinear high-activity neuronal excitation enhances odor discrimination
experimental_stimulus: Odors (ethyl propionate, 1-hexanol, MCH, OCT) at varying concentrations (10^-5 to 10^-1 dilution)
neural_activity: mAChR-B knockdown (KD) reduced odor-induced Ca2+ transients in ORN axons (GCaMP6f). mAChR-B overexpression increased responses. Effect was nonlinear, occurring primarily at high neuronal firing rates (high odor concentrations).
behavioral_consequence: mAChR-B KD impaired flies’ odor discrimination capabilities, shown by reduced learning scores in MCH vs OCT conditioning, with greater deficits in difficult odor mixtures (e.g., 60:40 ratio).
ecological_context: Odor discrimination is crucial for survival (e.g., distinguishing edible from poisonous berries), as stated in the introduction.
techniques_used: ['Functional imaging (two-photon calcium imaging)', 'RNAi knockdown', 'Behavioral olfactory conditioning']
summary: ORNs express mAChR-B which mediates intraglomerular excitation; KD of mAChR-B reduces ORN activity at high firing rates and impairs odor discrimination behavior.

paper_title: Nonlinear high-activity neuronal excitation enhances odor discrimination
experimental_stimulus: Odors
neural_activity: mAChR-B KD in ORNs dramatically decreased iLN odor responses (GCaMP3/6f, 449-QF driver).
behavioral_consequence: Not directly tested via manipulation of iLNs.
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed for iLNs specifically.
summary: iLN odor responses depend on ORN mAChR-B activity; knocking down mAChR-B in ORNs significantly reduces iLN activity.

paper_title: Nonlinear high-activity neuronal excitation enhances odor discrimination
experimental_stimulus: Odors
neural_activity: mAChR-B KD in ORNs resulted in a mild decrease in PN responses (GCaMP7b, GH146-LexA driver), consistent with reduced ORN activity.
behavioral_consequence: Not directly tested via manipulation of PNs.
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed for PNs specifically.
summary: PN responses mildly decreased when ORN mAChR-B was knocked down, indicating functional synaptic transmission from ORNs.

paper_title: Temperature cues are integrated in a flexible circadian neuropeptidergic feedback circuit to remodel sleep-wake patterns in flies
experimental_stimulus: High temperature (29°C), optogenetic activation (CsChrimson), thermogenetic activation (dTrpA1), thermogenetic inhibition (ShibireTS), optogenetic activation of DN1p
neural_activity: Activation increases sleep; DN1p activation increases LPN calcium via Glutamate-NMDAR; LPN activation decreases LNd calcium
behavioral_consequence: Activation increases sleep and reduces daily locomotion; inhibition decreases sleep and increases locomotion; AstC knockdown blocks high-temperature-induced evening sleep increase
ecological_context: Maintaining appropriate sleep and activity levels across different seasonal environments is crucial for animal health and survival; adaptive behavioral responses to different thermal environments
summary: LPNs integrate circadian and temperature signals to promote sleep, particularly under high-temperature conditions, by inhibiting LNds via the AstC pathway.

paper_title: Temperature cues are integrated in a flexible circadian neuropeptidergic feedback circuit to remodel sleep-wake patterns in flies
experimental_stimulus: High temperature (29°C), optogenetic activation (CsChrimson)
neural_activity: Activation increases LPN calcium
behavioral_consequence: Inhibition blocks high-temperature-induced evening sleep increase; activation mimics high-temperature effect by increasing evening sleep and decreasing locomotion
ecological_context: Heat-induced changes in daytime activity; high-temperature-sensitive cells respond to thermal changes to relay signals to higher brain regions
summary: High-temperature-sensitive ACs activate LPNs to promote sleep and reshape evening activity under high-temperature conditions.

paper_title: Temperature cues are integrated in a flexible circadian neuropeptidergic feedback circuit to remodel sleep-wake patterns in flies
experimental_stimulus: Low temperature (19°C), optogenetic activation (CsChrimson), LPN activation, genetic manipulation (AstCR1/2 RNAi/Mutation)
neural_activity: Activation reduces sleep (promotes wakefulness); LPN activation decreases LNd calcium; LNds express NPF and activate LPNs via NPFR
behavioral_consequence: Activation promotes wakefulness; AstCR1/2 knockdown/mutation blocks high-temperature-induced evening sleep increase; NPF/NPFR knockdown blocks low-temperature-induced evening sleep decrease and locomotion increase
ecological_context: Regulation of evening locomotion peak is crucial for energy balance and overall survival; fine-tuning the evening peak increase at lower temperatures to prevent excessive movement
summary: LNds control evening locomotion; they receive inhibitory signals from LPNs via AstC and activate LPNs via NPF to form a negative feedback loop limiting LNd activation.

paper_title: Temperature cues are integrated in a flexible circadian neuropeptidergic feedback circuit to remodel sleep-wake patterns in flies
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson), VGLUT RNAi
neural_activity: Activation increases LPN calcium
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of glutamate transmission impairs basal sleep
ecological_context: Integrates temperature information into the central circadian circuit; circadian neurons monitor environmental temperature to set sleep timing
summary: DN1p neurons signal to LPNs via glutamate to contribute to sleep-promoting effects and circadian temperature regulation.

paper_title: Temperature cues are integrated in a flexible circadian neuropeptidergic feedback circuit to remodel sleep-wake patterns in flies
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson)
neural_activity: N/A
behavioral_consequence: Activation increases sleep and decreases locomotion
ecological_context: Interlinked sleep-promoting DN3-LPN circuit
summary: sCPDN3 neurons strongly promote sleep, forming an interlinked sleep-promoting circuit with LPNs.

paper_title: Ancestral neural circuits potentiate the origin of a female sexual behavior in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson); Decapitation to isolate VNC neurites
neural_activity: Forced activation via optogenetics; inferred to integrate external (song) and internal (mating status) signals
behavioral_consequence: D. santomea: Induces robust Wing Spreading (WS) and abdomen extension (VPO). D. melanogaster: Induces VPO in all females; latent WS in 21.9% of females (increases to 71.3% at 29°C). D. yakuba: Contributes to abdomen extension via dsx-GAL4 activation (vpoDN specific activation not tested).
ecological_context: Controls female sexual receptivity signaling (VPO, WS) during courtship; WS promotes prolonged male song and copulation success
summary: vpoDN descending neurons drive VPO across D. santomea, D. yakuba, and D. melanogaster. In D. santomea, vpoDN activation drives a novel Wing Spreading behavior; in D. melanogaster, vpoDN has a latent potential to drive WS that is enhanced by high developmental temperature.

paper_title: Ancestral neural circuits potentiate the origin of a female sexual behavior in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson) via CRISPR-generated dsx-GAL4 lines
neural_activity: Forced activation via optogenetics
behavioral_consequence: D. santomea: Abdomen extension + Robust Wing Spreading. D. yakuba: Abdomen extension + Wing inward movement + Female polycyclic song. D. melanogaster: Abdomen extension + Latent Wing Spreading.
ecological_context: Integration of sensory and motivational cues for female reproductive behaviors across species
summary: Optogenetic activation of dsx-expressing brain neurons induces VPO in all three species. Species-specific outputs include Wing Spreading in D. santomea, female song in D. yakuba, and latent Wing Spreading in D. melanogaster.

paper_title: Exploring the Asymmetric Body’s Influence on Interval Timing Behaviors of Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Mating duration assay (virgin female; rival-induced or sexual experience context)
neural_activity: Genetic inhibition (UAS-KCNJ2) or hyperexcitation (UAS-NaChBac) via temperature-controlled GAL4/UAS system
behavioral_consequence: Inhibition disrupts both longer mating duration (LMD) and shorter mating duration (SMD); hyperexcitation perturbs SMD
ecological_context: Interval timing behaviors essential for Drosophila, involving rival-induced prolonged mating and sexual experience-mediated shorter mating
techniques_used: ['Genetic manipulation (GAL4/UAS, UAS-KCNJ2, UAS-NaChBac, tub-GAL80ts)', 'Mating duration assay', 'Single-cell RNA-sequencing']
summary: Inhibition of GAL4R38D01-labeled AB neurons disrupts both LMD and SMD, while hyperexcitation perturbs SMD.

paper_title: Exploring the Asymmetric Body’s Influence on Interval Timing Behaviors of Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Mating duration assay (virgin female; rival-induced or sexual experience context)
neural_activity: Genetic inhibition (UAS-KCNJ2) or hyperexcitation (UAS-NaChBac) via temperature-controlled GAL4/UAS system
behavioral_consequence: Inhibition disrupts SMD without affecting LMD; hyperexcitation perturbs both SMD and LMD
ecological_context: Interval timing behaviors essential for Drosophila, involving rival-induced prolonged mating and sexual experience-mediated shorter mating
techniques_used: ['Genetic manipulation (GAL4/UAS, UAS-KCNJ2, UAS-NaChBac, tub-GAL80ts)', 'Mating duration assay']
summary: GAL4R72A10-labeled AB neurons specifically regulate SMD upon inhibition, while hyperexcitation affects both SMD and LMD.

paper_title: Exploring the Asymmetric Body’s Influence on Interval Timing Behaviors of Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Mating duration assay (virgin female; rival-induced or sexual experience context)
neural_activity: Genetic inhibition (UAS-KCNJ2) or knockdown (5-HT1B-RNAi)
behavioral_consequence: Inhibition disrupts both LMD and SMD; knockdown disrupts LMD behavior
ecological_context: Interval timing behaviors essential for Drosophila, involving rival-induced prolonged mating and sexual experience-mediated shorter mating
summary: 5-HT1B neurons, predominantly within Abd-B expressing AB neuronal populations, are required for LMD generation and SMD regulation.

paper_title: Exploring the Asymmetric Body’s Influence on Interval Timing Behaviors of Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Mating duration assay (virgin female; rival-induced context)
neural_activity: Pdfr knockdown in 5-HT1B neurons; Pdfr reduction via RNAi
behavioral_consequence: Pdfr knockdown affects LMD behavior; Pdfr reduction causes disappearance of the AB-l structure
ecological_context: Interval timing behaviors essential for Drosophila, involving rival-induced prolonged mating
summary: Pdfr is a critical factor for AB-l structure formation and LMD generation, with projections predominantly from cholinergic neurons.

paper_title: Male cuticular pheromones stimulate removal of the mating plug and promote re-mating through pC1 neurons in Drosophila females
experimental_stimulus: 2-methyltetracosane (2MC) applied to filter paper; presence of naive wild-type males
neural_activity: Silencing with tetanus toxin light chain (TNT) abolished male-induced EHP shortening (MIES). Thermogenetic activation with dTRPA1 resulted in significant EHP shortening even in the absence of males.
behavioral_consequence: Activation shortens Ejaculate Holding Period (EHP). Essential for MIES (male-induced EHP shortening).
ecological_context: Detection of male cuticular hydrocarbons in a changing social-sexual context; potential indicator of male age and resilience.
summary: Or47b+ ORNs detect the male pheromone 2MC. Silencing these neurons abolishes MIES, while their activation shortens the Ejaculate Holding Period.

paper_title: Male cuticular pheromones stimulate removal of the mating plug and promote re-mating through pC1 neurons in Drosophila females
experimental_stimulus: 7-Tricosene (7-T) applied to filter paper; presence of mated females
neural_activity: Silencing with tetanus toxin light chain (TNT) almost completely abolished MIES.
behavioral_consequence: Essential for MIES (male-induced EHP shortening) induced by 7-T. Does not affect EHP when ppk29 or ppk28 are deficient.
ecological_context: Detection of male pheromones (7-T) transferred during copulation; part of social-sexual context assessment.
summary: ppk23+ gustatory neurons detect 7-T. Silencing these neurons abolishes MIES, linking contact chemosensation to sperm ejection timing.

paper_title: Male cuticular pheromones stimulate removal of the mating plug and promote re-mating through pC1 neurons in Drosophila females
experimental_stimulus: 2-methyltetracosane (2MC) and 7-tricosene (7-T); cVA and courtship pulse song (after cAMP elevation)
neural_activity: Exposure to 2MC and 7-T increased cAMP levels (measured via CRE-luciferase reporter). Optogenetic cAMP elevation (PhotoAC) increased Ca2+ transients in response to cVA/courtship song (measured via GCaMP6m). Silencing with GtACR1 increased EHP.
behavioral_consequence: Silencing increased EHP. Optogenetic cAMP elevation shortened EHP. Elevated cAMP reinstated responsiveness to male courtship cues in mated females and increased re-mating rate.
ecological_context: Processing male courtship cues and regulating female mating receptivity after copulation; responding to social-sexual context changes.
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics (GtACR1 silencing, PhotoAC activation)', 'Calcium imaging (GCaMP6m)', 'CRE-luciferase reporter (cAMP measurement)', 'Behavioral assays (EHP, re-mating rate)']
summary: pC1b and pC1c neurons integrate pheromone signals to increase cAMP. This shortens EHP, reinstates courtship responsiveness, and promotes re-mating.

paper_title: Male cuticular pheromones stimulate removal of the mating plug and promote re-mating through pC1 neurons in Drosophila females
experimental_stimulus: 2-methyltetracosane (2MC) and 7-tricosene (7-T)
neural_activity: No increase in CRE-luciferase reporter activity (cAMP) upon exposure to 2MC or 7-T. cAMP elevation via PhotoAC did not shorten EHP.
behavioral_consequence: Silencing pC1a did not result in increased EHP. Silencing pC1d and pC1e had a limited effect on EHP.
ecological_context: Not explicitly distinguished from pC1b/c in ecological function within the results, though pC1a is associated with mating receptivity and pC1d/e with aggression in cited connectome data.
summary: Unlike pC1b/c, silencing pC1a, d, or e does not significantly increase EHP, and cAMP elevation in these subtypes does not shorten the Ejaculate Holding Period.

paper_title: Reorganization of circadian activity and the pacemaker circuit under novel light regimes
experimental_stimulus: LDimLDim 5:7:5:7 light regime (70 lux: 1 lux alternating bright and dim phases)
neural_activity: Knockdown of CRYPTOCHROME (CRY) expression using Clock856GAL4 and UAS-cryRNAi; cry01 null mutants
behavioral_consequence: Significant loss of activity bifurcation (bifurcation index significantly lower than 0.5); activity restricted to photophases or single bout
ecological_context: Novel light regimes mimicking anthropogenic artificial light and shift work conditions
techniques_used: ['Locomotor activity monitoring (DAM system)', 'Genetic manipulation (RNAi, Null mutants)']
summary: Cell-autonomous CRYPTOCHROME photoreception in circadian pacemaker neurons is necessary for activity bifurcation under novel dim light regimes.

paper_title: Reorganization of circadian activity and the pacemaker circuit under novel light regimes
experimental_stimulus: LDimLDim 5:7:5:7 light regime
neural_activity: Overexpression of DBT s (faster molecular clock speed) using LNdGAL4 driver
behavioral_consequence: Significant advancement of the mean phase of both activity bouts (SP1 and SP2)
ecological_context: Regulation of activity timing under novel light regimes; circadian reorganisation of pacemaker circuit
techniques_used: ['Locomotor activity monitoring', 'Center of Mass (CoM) phase analysis']
summary: Evening neurons (LNds) regulate the timing of both activity bouts under novel light regimes, as evidenced by phase advancement upon clock speed manipulation.

paper_title: Reorganization of circadian activity and the pacemaker circuit under novel light regimes
experimental_stimulus: LDimLDim 5:7:5:7 light regime
neural_activity: Overexpression of DBT s (faster molecular clock speed) using pdfGAL4 driver
behavioral_consequence: No difference in activity waveform compared to controls under LDimLDim (unlike LD 12:12 where morning bout advanced)
ecological_context: Regulation of activity timing under novel light regimes
summary: Morning neurons (PDF+ve) do not modulate the phasing of both activity bouts under novel light regimes, unlike their role in standard LD conditions.

paper_title: Reorganization of circadian activity and the pacemaker circuit under novel light regimes
cell_type: Canonical circadian clock neurons (per, tim, clk, cyc)
experimental_stimulus: LDimLDim 5:7:5:7 light regime
neural_activity: Null mutations in period (per01), timeless (tim01), Clock (Clkjrk), and cycle (cyc01)
behavioral_consequence: Bifurcation present (index > 0.5), but overall waveform altered (timing of peaks and offsets changed); startle responses to transitions intact
ecological_context: Novel light regimes; circadian clock function in waveform regulation
summary: Canonical circadian clock genes are required for determining the precise timing of activity peaks and offsets under novel light regimes, though dim light-induced activity remains intact.

paper_title: Metabolic constraints on growth explain how developmental temperature scales synaptic connectivity relevant for behavior
experimental_stimulus: Odor stimuli (2-butanone, vinegar/benzaldehyde) and optogenetic activation (CsChrimson)
neural_activity: Single sensillum recordings show transduction currents and firing rates are invariant to developmental temperature (18°C vs 25°C). In vivo calcium imaging shows higher presynaptic calcium responses in glomeruli DM1 and DM4 at 18°C. Trans-Tango labeling shows more postsynaptic partners and higher Brp puncta intensity at 18°C.
behavioral_consequence: Increased presynaptic activity and connectivity at 18°C correlate with stronger odor-driven increases in walking speed and higher odor occupancy in free-walking assays.
ecological_context: Olfaction mediates key behaviors including foraging and mating; flies inhabit climates with thermal ranges between 11 and 32°C.
techniques_used: ['Single sensillum electrophysiology', 'In vivo calcium imaging', 'Trans-Tango', 'Brp immunostaining']
summary: ORNs show invariant firing rates to odors regardless of developmental temperature, but exhibit larger presynaptic calcium responses and increased synaptic partners at lower temperatures (18°C).

paper_title: Metabolic constraints on growth explain how developmental temperature scales synaptic connectivity relevant for behavior
experimental_stimulus: Odor stimuli (2-butanone, vinegar/benzaldehyde)
neural_activity: Calcium imaging (GCaMP6f) shows odor responses are robust and similar between flies developed at 18°C and 25°C, despite ORN input differences.
behavioral_consequence: Odor representations remain invariant at the uPN output level, but downstream behavioral output varies with developmental temperature.
ecological_context: uPNs target the Lateral Horn to drive innate behavior, ensuring robust odor coding across temperatures.
summary: uPNs maintain invariant odor representations across developmental temperatures through compensatory mechanisms despite ORN input scaling.

paper_title: Metabolic constraints on growth explain how developmental temperature scales synaptic connectivity relevant for behavior
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation of upstream or42b-ORNs
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation drives higher activity in the medial lateral antennal lobe tract (mlALT) at 18°C. Trans-Tango indicates more synaptic partners recruited in the ventral cluster at 18°C.
behavioral_consequence: Increased recruitment of inhibitory mPNs at 18°C correlates with stronger odor-driven approach behavior (vinegar and 2-butanone).
ecological_context: mPNs modulate downstream circuits in the Lateral Horn; their differential wiring contributes to temperature-dependent behavioral outcomes.
summary: mPNs recruit more partners and show higher activity at lower developmental temperatures (18°C), correlating with enhanced odor-driven approach behavior.

paper_title: Metabolic constraints on growth explain how developmental temperature scales synaptic connectivity relevant for behavior
experimental_stimulus: None (structural connectivity analysis)
neural_activity: Connectivity from uPNs changes with temperature; probability of VM2 and VM3 glomeruli connection is significantly lower in flies developed at 18°C compared to 25°C.
behavioral_consequence: Differential wiring correlates with temperature-dependent odor preference and occupancy behavior observed in whole-animal assays.
ecological_context: Innate odor preference is determined by uPN-LH wiring, which is contingent on partner availability during development.
summary: PD2a1/b1 LHNs show temperature-dependent changes in uPN input patterns, contributing to altered odor preference behaviors in flies developed at different temperatures.

paper_title: The function of juvenile–adult transition axis in female sexual receptivity of Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: None (developmental manipulation)
neural_activity: Not measured directly; identified via immunohistochemistry and GAL4/LexA drivers
behavioral_consequence: Inactivation during 3rd-instar larval stage significantly increased virgin female copulation rate. Activation during 3rd-instar larval stage significantly decreased virgin female copulation rate. Activation or inactivation during pupal or adult stages did not change copulation rate.
ecological_context: Regulates establishment of virgin female sexual receptivity during the juvenile–adult transition (neural maturation), analogous to the HPG axis in mammals.
techniques_used: ['Genetic manipulation (Kir2.1, dTrpA1)', 'GAL4/LexA drivers', 'Immunohistochemistry (dsx, PTTH)', 'Behavioral assays (copulation rate, latency)']
summary: dsx-positive PG neurons expressing PTTH negatively regulate virgin female receptivity specifically during the 3rd-instar larval stage; inactivation enhances receptivity while activation reduces it.

paper_title: The function of juvenile–adult transition axis in female sexual receptivity of Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: None (developmental manipulation)
neural_activity: Calcium imaging (GCaMP6s) in lateral junction of LPC showed no significant change in calcium responses when EcR-A was reduced.
behavioral_consequence: Reducing EcR-A in pC1 neurons decreased virgin female copulation rate, increased latency to copulation, decreased vaginal plate opening, and increased ovipositor extrusion. Reduction in pC1-a/c/e did not change receptivity, implicating pC1b. Morphology showed abnormal elaboration (e.g., extra vertical projection in pC1d).
ecological_context: Controls virgin female sexual receptivity established during metamorphosis; necessary for successful copulation.
summary: pC1 neurons, particularly pC1b, require EcR-A for normal morphological development and virgin female copulation rate; reduced EcR-A leads to abnormal morphology and reduced receptivity without altering calcium activity.

paper_title: The function of juvenile–adult transition axis in female sexual receptivity of Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: None (developmental manipulation)
neural_activity: Express EcR-A and EcR-B1 (confirmed via immunohistochemistry)
behavioral_consequence: Reducing EcR-A or EcR-B1 expression in vpoDN neurons did not affect virgin female receptivity (copulation rate or latency).
ecological_context: Involved in vaginal plate opening, but EcR expression is not required for this function in virgin females in this study.
summary: vpoDN neurons express EcR receptors, but reducing EcR-A or EcR-B1 in these neurons does not alter virgin female receptivity behaviors.

paper_title: Evolution of connectivity architecture in the Drosophila mushroom body
experimental_stimulus: Olfactory odors (farnesol, hexanoic acid, 4-methylcyclohexanol, 3-octanol) presented during learning assays; odors known to activate specific glomeruli (DC3, DL2d, VM5v, DP1l).
neural_activity: Connectivity frequencies with Kenyon cells mapped; presynaptic bouton volume and neuron number quantified. DL2d and DP1l PNs showed increased bouton volume in D. sechellia; DC3 and VM5v PNs showed decreased neuron number in D. sechellia.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested via direct manipulation; cumulative connectivity frequencies of activated glomeruli correlated with associative learning performance (Performance Index).
ecological_context: Species-specific connectivity biases reflect chemical ecology: D. sechellia (noni specialist) prioritizes acid-sensing channels (DL2d, DP1l); generalists (D. melanogaster, D. simulans) prioritize fermenting fruit channels (DC3, VM5v).
techniques_used: ['Photo-labeling', 'Dye electroporation', 'Confocal microscopy', 'Morphological quantification', 'T-maze aversive learning assay']
summary: Projection neurons innervating DL2d, DP1l, DC3, and VM5v glomeruli show species-specific morphological differences (bouton volume or neuron number) correlating with chemical ecology; increased connectivity correlates with higher learning performance.

paper_title: Evolution of connectivity architecture in the Drosophila mushroom body
experimental_stimulus: Indirect olfactory input via Projection Neurons activated by odors (farnesol, hexanoic acid, etc.).
neural_activity: Input connectivity frequencies from PNs mapped; confirmed biased connectivity (some glomeruli over/underrepresented) and random convergence. Connectivity biases varied across species.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested via direct manipulation; KC connectivity patterns correlated with associative learning performance (Performance Index) across species.
ecological_context: Connectivity architecture changes in KCs reflect species' ecological niches (noni specialist vs. generalists), prioritizing ecologically relevant odors.
techniques_used: ['Photo-labeling', 'Dye electroporation', 'Confocal imaging', 'Connectivity matrix analysis', 'T-maze aversive learning assay']
summary: Kenyon cells receive biased inputs from Projection Neurons; species-specific connectivity biases align with ecological niches and correlate with learning performance in associative tasks.

paper_title: Reorganisation of circadian activity and the pacemaker circuit under novel light regimes
experimental_stimulus: LDimLDim 5:7:5:7 (70 lux bright / 1 lux dim); LDimLDim 700:10 lux
neural_activity: Not measured directly
behavioral_consequence: CRY null mutants (cry01) and clock-neuron specific RNAi knockdown (Clk > cryRNAi) failed to display activity bifurcation (activity not restricted to dim phases). Higher light intensities (700 lux) did not rescue bifurcation in mutants.
ecological_context: Artificial light at night and shift work regimes; Drosophila are crepuscular organisms.
summary: Cryptochrome in circadian pacemaker neurons is necessary for locomotor activity bifurcation under novel dim light regimes.

paper_title: Reorganisation of circadian activity and the pacemaker circuit under novel light regimes
experimental_stimulus: LDimLDim 5:7:5:7 (70 lux bright / 1 lux dim)
neural_activity: Not measured directly
behavioral_consequence: Overexpression of fast clock allele DBT s in LNds advanced the mean phase of both activity bouts (scotophase 1 and 2) compared to controls.
ecological_context: Circadian timing under novel light regimes.
summary: Evening neurons (LNds) regulate the timing of both activity bouts under bifurcated light regimes.

paper_title: Reorganisation of circadian activity and the pacemaker circuit under novel light regimes
experimental_stimulus: LDimLDim 5:7:5:7 (70 lux bright / 1 lux dim)
neural_activity: Not measured directly
behavioral_consequence: Overexpression of fast clock allele DBT s in PDF+ve neurons did not advance both bouts under bifurcation (unlike LD 12:12 where only morning bout advanced).
ecological_context: Circadian timing.
summary: Morning neurons do not regulate both activity bouts under bifurcated light regimes.

paper_title: A subclass of evening cells promotes the switch from arousal to sleep at dusk.
cell_type: E1 neurons (E1 subclass of evening lateral neurons)
experimental_stimulus: Thermogenetic activation (dTrpA1), electrical silencing (Kir2.1 via AGES), light/dark cycles (12:12 LD, constant darkness), genetic manipulation of clock genes (tim, per)
neural_activity: In vivo voltage imaging (Voltron2) revealed spiking rate does not vary between dawn (ZT0-1) and dusk (ZT12-13). Silencing and activation were performed to manipulate activity directly.
behavioral_consequence: Silencing E1 neurons significantly delayed sleep onset after dusk. Activation produced time- and light-dependent effects: reduced afternoon sleep (arousal) and advanced sleep onset after dusk (sleep), but had no effect in constant darkness. Timing of output depends on a non-cell-autonomous clock (per01 affects timing, but E1-specific per knockout does not).
ecological_context: Promotes the switch from arousal to sleep at dusk in crepuscular animals. Facilitates behavioral transitions associated with the light-to-dark transition at twilight.
techniques_used: ['Thermogenetics (dTrpA1)', 'Chemical genetics (Kir2.1/AGES)', 'In vivo voltage imaging (Voltron2)', 'Drosophila Activity Monitoring', 'CRISPR (tim, per knockout)', 'Sleep assays']
summary: E1 neurons promote the transition from arousal to sleep at dusk. Their output is light-dependent and timed by a non-cell-autonomous clock, yet their own electrical firing rate does not vary between dawn and dusk.

paper_title: A subclass of evening cells promotes the switch from arousal to sleep at dusk.
cell_type: E2 neurons (E2 subclass of evening lateral neurons)
experimental_stimulus: Thermogenetic activation (dTrpA1), electrical silencing (Kir2.1 via AGES), molecular clock manipulation (tim CRISPR knockout, DBTS overexpression), 12:12 light:dark cycles
neural_activity: Silencing or activating E2 neurons had no significant effect on evening anticipation, suggesting clock-dependent rather than activity-dependent control. Molecular clocks were manipulated to alter phase.
behavioral_consequence: The cell-autonomous clocks of E2 neurons alone are required to drive and adjust the phase of evening anticipation. Knocking out tim or overexpressing DBTS in E2 neurons altered evening anticipation phase and slope.
ecological_context: Drives evening anticipation, the canonical behavior associated with 'evening' clock neurons in crepuscular animals.
techniques_used: ['Thermogenetics (dTrpA1)', 'Chemical genetics (Kir2.1/AGES)', 'CRISPR (tim knockout, DBTS overexpression)', 'Drosophila Activity Monitoring', 'Sleep assays']
summary: E2 neurons drive evening anticipation via their cell-autonomous molecular clocks. Their electrical activity is not required for this behavior, as silencing or activation did not impair anticipation.

paper_title: Divergent neural circuits for proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensing of the Drosophila leg
experimental_stimulus: Not directly tested in this study; physiological profiles referenced from Mamiya et al. (2018/2023) indicate encoding of femur-tibia joint position and movement.
neural_activity: Connectomic reconstruction reveals axonal projections in the ventral nerve cord (VNC); found to synapse primarily onto local VNC interneurons and leg motor neurons.
behavioral_consequence: Connectivity suggests a role in fast feedback control of leg motor output (proprioception); consistent with leg posture stabilization.
ecological_context: Leg motor control during walking and posture maintenance.
summary: FeCO Claw neurons project to local VNC interneurons and motor neurons, suggesting a proprioceptive function for leg motor control.

paper_title: Divergent neural circuits for proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensing of the Drosophila leg
experimental_stimulus: Not directly tested in this study; physiological profiles referenced from Mamiya et al. (2018/2023) indicate encoding of femur-tibia joint movement.
neural_activity: Connectomic reconstruction shows axonal projections in the VNC; found to synapse primarily onto local VNC interneurons and leg motor neurons.
behavioral_consequence: Connectivity suggests a role in fast feedback control of leg motor output (proprioception); hook axons receive inhibitory input from 9A neurons during voluntary movements.
ecological_context: Leg motor control and reflexes during walking.
summary: FeCO Hook neurons connect to local leg motor control circuits, indicating a proprioceptive function similar to Claw neurons.

paper_title: Divergent neural circuits for proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensing of the Drosophila leg
experimental_stimulus: Not directly tested in this study; physiological profiles referenced from Mamiya et al. (2018/2023) indicate encoding of low-amplitude, high-frequency tibia vibration.
neural_activity: Connectomic reconstruction shows axonal projections to intersegmental neurons (10B, 8B, 0A/0B, 9A hemilineages) and ascending neurons to the brain; no direct synapses onto leg motor neurons.
behavioral_consequence: Connectivity suggests exteroceptive function; indirect connection to premotor neurons (PSI, LTM) suggests potential pathway for startle or escape behaviors (freezing, take-off).
ecological_context: Detection of substrate vibrations for courtship communication, predator detection, or escape responses.
techniques_used: ['Connectomic reconstruction', 'Electron microscopy (FANC dataset)', 'Connectome analysis', 'FlyWire brain connectome integration']
summary: FeCO Club neurons connect to intersegmental and ascending pathways rather than local motor circuits, supporting a vibration-sensing exteroceptive function.

paper_title: Divergent neural circuits for proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensing of the Drosophila leg
experimental_stimulus: Input from FeCO Club neurons (vibration signals).
neural_activity: Connectomic reconstruction shows integration of club neuron inputs across multiple legs (e.g., 10B project to contralateral/adjacent legs; 8B arborize in all neuromeres); high recurrent connectivity among interneuron classes.
behavioral_consequence: Connectivity suggests capacity to localize substrate vibrations in the external environment; relay vibration information to the brain.
ecological_context: Spatial localization of external mechanical vibrations.
summary: VNC interneurons receive input from club neurons and integrate signals across legs, suggesting a role in localizing substrate vibrations.

paper_title: Divergent neural circuits for proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensing of the Drosophila leg
experimental_stimulus: Leg vibration signals (inferred from club input).
neural_activity: Connectomic reconstruction shows projections to anterior ventrolateral protocerebrum (AVLP), wedge (WED), saddle (SAD), and gnathal ganglion (GNG); convergence with antennal mechanosensory neurons (JONs).
behavioral_consequence: Connectivity suggests integration of leg vibration information with auditory/mechanosensory information from antennae in the brain.
ecological_context: Detection and localization of mechanical vibrations in the external environment; potential role in courtship communication.
summary: Ascending neurons relay club neuron signals to the brain, where they integrate with antennal mechanosensory information.

paper_title: Mating proximity blinds threat perception
experimental_stimulus: Visual threat (predator-like moving shadow), non-threat light, female sensory cues, dopamine application
neural_activity: Substantial calcium influx upon visual threat (two-photon imaging, GCaMP6f/7b). Calcium signal decreased during late courtship with dopamine application. Dopamine application completely suppressed threat-driven LC16 response.
behavioral_consequence: Silencing LC16 (TNT) prevents flies from stopping courtship in response to visual threat. Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson) interrupts courtship mimicking threat. Knockdown of Dop2R receptors in LC16 restored threat response during late courtship.
ecological_context: Detects visual threats (predators) to prioritize defensive responses (escape/freezing) over courtship when danger is imminent.
summary: LC16 neurons detect visual threats via calcium influx and trigger defensive responses. Dopamine signaling inhibits LC16 output via Dop2R, reducing threat perception during advanced courtship.

paper_title: Mating proximity blinds threat perception
experimental_stimulus: Visual threat, optogenetic activation of LC16 neurons, artificial activation
neural_activity: Significant calcium influx upon threat exposure and LC16 stimulation (two-photon imaging, GCaMP6s). Response abolished by dopamine application.
behavioral_consequence: Silencing (TNT, TRH-RNAi) prevents stopping courtship upon visual threat. Optogenetic activation terminates courtship and induces defensive behaviors in absence of threat.
ecological_context: Integrate threats of different modalities to act as general effectors of threat responses, prioritizing survival over sex.
summary: 5-HTPMPD neurons respond to visual threats and LC16 activity. They inhibit central courtship nodes (P1, plP10) via serotonin to prioritize escape over courtship.

paper_title: Mating proximity blinds threat perception
experimental_stimulus: Visual threat, serotonin (5-HT) application
neural_activity: Calcium activity decreased upon 5-HT application (two-photon imaging, GCaMP6s). Inhibitory effect abolished by knocking down 5-HT7 receptors.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation allows males to continue courtship despite threat. Knockdown of 5-HT7 in P1 reduces threat response and increases courtship levels.
ecological_context: Central mating regulation hub that integrates competing drives (sex vs. survival) to regulate courtship initiation and persistence.
summary: P1 neurons are inhibited by 5-HT via 5-HT7 receptors during threats, suppressing courtship. Activation overrides threat response.

paper_title: Mating proximity blinds threat perception
experimental_stimulus: Visual threat, serotonin (5-HT) application
neural_activity: Calcium activity significantly decreased upon 5-HT application (two-photon imaging, GCaMP6m). Inhibitory effect abolished by reducing 5-HT2B expression.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation resulted in high, sustained courtship levels throughout threat delivery. Knockdown of 5-HT2B resulted in males continuing courtship despite threat.
ecological_context: Target wing motor region and are crucial for courtship song; sustain threat-induced inhibition of courtship.
summary: plP10 neurons are inhibited by 5-HT via 5-HT2B receptors during threats, contributing to sustained suppression of courtship.

paper_title: Mating proximity blinds threat perception
experimental_stimulus: Artificial optogenetic activation, threat delivery during late courtship
neural_activity: Activation drives dopamine ramping in PPM1/2 neurons (indirectly measured via dopamine activity).
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson) diminished threat responses. Silencing (TNT) made flies reduce courtship in response to late threats.
ecological_context: Activity linked to abdomen bending, which indicates proximity to expected copulation and courtship progression.
summary: OvAbg neurons signal abdomen bending during courtship, which drives dopamine ramping and reduces threat responsiveness in late courtship.

paper_title: Mating proximity blinds threat perception
experimental_stimulus: Female presence (courtship progression), threat delivery, optogenetic activation
neural_activity: Gradual increase in calcium signal (ramping) during courtship progression, correlated with abdomen bending (two-photon imaging, GCaMP7b). Dopamine release onto LC16 terminals increased during courtship (GRABDA sensor). Activity decreased when abdomen fixed.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation during early courtship caused males to ignore threats and continue courting.
ecological_context: Activity reflects courtship progress and goal proximity (mating success), integrating proprioceptive feedback from abdomen bending to bias sensory perception.
summary: PPM1/2 dopamine neurons ramp activity as courtship progresses (driven by abdomen bending), inhibiting visual threat detection to prioritize mating.

paper_title: Mating proximity blinds threat perception
experimental_stimulus: Dopamine application, threat delivery
neural_activity: Calcium activity decreased by dopamine application (two-photon imaging, GCaMP6f). Knockdown prevented LC16 inhibition by dopamine.
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of Dop2R in LC16 shifted behavior from courtship to defensive responses during late court stages.
ecological_context: Mediates dopaminergic inhibition of outgoing activity from LC16 neurons, allowing persistence in courtship despite danger.
summary: Dop2R receptors on LC16 axon terminals mediate dopamine-induced inhibition of visual threat responses during late courtship.

paper_title: Long-term neuropeptide modulation of female sexual drive via the TRP channel in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Male courtship cues (cVA pheromone, pulse song); Sexual maturation (age); Mating (Sex Peptide)
neural_activity: Increased Ca2+ transients (GCaMP6m imaging) in 3-day-old mature virgins vs 1-day-old immature virgins. Increased CREB activity (Luciferase reporter) during sexual maturation, decreased after mating. Expression of pyrexia (pyx) channel correlates with excitability. Pyx RNAi reduced Ca2+ responses.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation (dTrpA1, CsChrimson) increased mating receptivity in immature females. Silencing (RNAi, GtACR1) blocked mating receptivity. Pyx overexpression induced precocious receptivity in 1-day-old females. Activation in mated females enabled second round of mating.
ecological_context: Female sexual drive increases 2-3 days after eclosion (sexual maturation) and is suppressed for days/weeks after mating (post-mating response).
summary: pC1 neurons show increased Ca2+ and CREB activity during sexual maturation which drives excitability via Pyx expression, increasing mating receptivity. Mating suppresses this activity and receptivity.

paper_title: Long-term neuropeptide modulation of female sexual drive via the TRP channel in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Light (optogenetic activation/silencing); Sexual maturation
neural_activity: Express Dh44 neuropeptide and Dh44 receptors (Dh44R1/2). Activation of Dh44R1-CA increased CREB activity. Silencing reduced receptivity.
behavioral_consequence: Activation during maturation increased receptivity in 1-day-old females (precocious). Silencing during maturation reduced receptivity. Enabled second mating in mated females via Pyx expression.
ecological_context: Regulates long-term shifts in sexual drive via neuropeptide feedback loops during maturation and post-mating.
summary: A subset of pC1 neurons (pC1b/c) secretes Dh44, activating a Dh44R-CREB-Pyx pathway to increase excitability and drive sexual maturation and receptivity.

paper_title: Long-term neuropeptide modulation of female sexual drive via the TRP channel in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Sexual maturation (endogenous); Mating (Sex Peptide)
neural_activity: Silencing via Kir2.1 or GtACR1 inhibited pC1-CREB activity. Silencing during maturation reduced pC1 activity.
behavioral_consequence: Silencing during maturation reduced mating receptivity (approx 60% vs 90% in controls). Silencing during mating assay reduced receptivity.
ecological_context: Modulates pC1 activity to regulate sexual drive during maturation; silenced by mating to suppress drive.
summary: SAG neurons provide excitatory inputs to pC1 neurons; their silencing reduces pC1 CREB activity and mating receptivity during sexual maturation.

paper_title: Ancestral neural circuits potentiate the origin of a female sexual behavior
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson, red light, 0.4-4.1 μW/mm2); Natural male courtship pulse song (endogenous context)
neural_activity: Not tested (endogenous response to stimuli not recorded in this study)
behavioral_consequence: In D. santomea, optogenetic activation drives robust Wing Spreading (WS) and Vaginal Plate Opening (VPO); decapitated flies retain WS/VPO. In D. melanogaster, activation drives VPO robustly; WS occurs in 21.9% (SS2 line) to 71.3% (29°C rearing). In D. yakuba, activation drives VPO and female song production. WS probability increases with higher vpoDN activity intensity in D. melanogaster.
ecological_context: Female sexual receptivity signaling during courtship; promotes male song prolongation and copulation success in D. santomea
summary: vpoDN descending neurons drive conserved VPO across Drosophila species and species-specific Wing Spreading (WS) in D. santomea; WS function in D. melanogaster is latent and plastic, influenced by developmental temperature.

paper_title: Interactions between specialized gain control mechanisms in olfactory processing.
experimental_stimulus: Odors (2-heptanone, farnesol, blends) at various concentrations; Optogenetic stimulation (CsChrimson)
neural_activity: Nonspiking neurons with graded potentials. Modest increases in depolarization and calcium signals with increasing odor concentration. Calcium signals largely confined to specific glomeruli (e.g., DC3 for farnesol). Optogenetic activation inhibits Projection Neuron (PN) odor responses with minimal changes to PN response dynamics or frequency tuning.
ecological_context: Part of a mechanism to minimize temporal distortions and improve odor discrimination when stimuli vary over a vast dynamic range.
techniques_used: ['In vivo patch-clamp recording', 'Two-photon calcium imaging', 'Optogenetics (CsChrimson)', 'Computational modeling']
summary: Patchy LNs are nonspiking, compartmentalized neurons that perform intra-glomerular gain control. They are recruited by specific odors and inhibit PNs without altering network dynamics significantly.

paper_title: Interactions between specialized gain control mechanisms in olfactory processing.
experimental_stimulus: Odors (2-heptanone, farnesol, blends) at various concentrations; Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson) and suppression (GtACR1)
neural_activity: Robust increases in depolarization and calcium signals with increasing odor concentration. Calcium signals spread throughout the arbor rather than being confined. Optogenetic activation inhibits PN odor responses and minimizes temporal distortion (band-pass filtering) in PNs, making PN responses more faithful to the odor stimulus. Optogenetic suppression disinhibits PN responses at odor onset.
ecological_context: Mediates global presynaptic gain control to manage the tradeoff between temporal fidelity and sensitivity, improving odor discrimination across varying odor intensities.
techniques_used: ['In vivo patch-clamp recording', 'Two-photon calcium imaging', 'Optogenetics (CsChrimson, GtACR1)', 'Computational modeling']
summary: Full LNs are Broad LNs with widespread arbors that perform global presynaptic gain control. They are recruited by strong, widespread network input and reduce temporal distortions in PN output.

paper_title: Interactions between specialized gain control mechanisms in olfactory processing.
experimental_stimulus: Odors (2-heptanone) at various concentrations
neural_activity: Individual cells could be excited or inhibited by odor. Many cells showed little change in response with increasing odor concentration, suggesting balanced excitation and inhibition.
ecological_context: Likely perform functions other than gain control for high odor intensity, potentially suppressing background noise when odor stimuli are absent.
summary: LN1 and LN2S/R cells do not show monotonic depolarization with increasing odor intensity, suggesting they are not primarily positioned to perform intensity-dependent gain control.

paper_title: Cell type-specific contributions to a persistent aggressive internal state in female Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation using 655 nm light (5 Hz, 30 seconds) via CsChrimson.
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP) showed time-locked responses to stimulation. Neither short (30s) nor long (300s) stimulation triggered persistent neuronal activity in aIPg neurons. Stimulation of pC1d neurons triggered time-locked responses in aIPg neurons but not persistent activity.
behavioral_consequence: 30-second stimulation promoted aggressive behaviors (head butting and shoving) lasting at least 10 minutes post-stimulation (Sliding door assay). Aggression persisted in mixed pair assays with wild-type targets. Inactivation of pC1d did not suppress aIPg-induced persistent aggression.
ecological_context: Female Drosophila melanogaster aggressive internal states; internal states are important for maintaining survival-promoting behaviors such as aggression.
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics', 'Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP)', 'Sliding door assay', 'Mixed pair assay', 'Behavioral epistasis']
summary: aIPg neurons promote a persistent aggressive internal state lasting at least 10 minutes after brief optogenetic stimulation, despite showing only time-locked neural activity during stimulation.

paper_title: Cell type-specific contributions to a persistent aggressive internal state in female Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation using 655 nm light (5 Hz, 30 seconds) via CsChrimson; also tested with co-stimulation of pC1e.
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP) showed time-locked responses to stimulation. pC1d neurons did not exhibit persistent neuronal activity during or after stimulation. Stimulation of pC1d triggered time-locked responses in aIPg neurons.
behavioral_consequence: Stimulation alone generated only time-locked aggression, not a persistent internal state. Simultaneous stimulation with pC1e promoted persistent aggression. Inactivation (Kir2.1) did not significantly reduce aIPg-evoked persistent aggression.
ecological_context: Female Drosophila melanogaster aggressive internal states; part of sexually dimorphic cell types controlling aggression.
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics', 'Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP)', 'Sliding door assay', 'Mixed pair assay', 'Behavioral epistasis']
summary: pC1d neurons alone do not promote persistent aggression (only time-locked), but co-stimulation with pC1e promotes persistent aggression; they do not show persistent neural activity.

paper_title: Cell type-specific contributions to a persistent aggressive internal state in female Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation alone and combined with pC1d (655 nm light, 5 Hz, 30 seconds).
neural_activity: Not explicitly measured in this study's results section; referenced previous work for stimulation alone effects.
behavioral_consequence: Stimulation alone does not evoke aggression. Simultaneous stimulation with pC1d promotes persistent aggression, suggesting an unexpected synergy between these cell types.
ecological_context: Female Drosophila melanogaster aggressive internal states; part of sexually dimorphic cell types controlling aggression.
summary: pC1e neurons do not evoke aggression when stimulated alone, but co-stimulation with pC1d promotes a persistent aggressive internal state.

paper_title: Rapid threat assessment in the Drosophila thermosensory system
experimental_stimulus: Rapid heating and cooling steps (~1 °C/s), optogenetic activation of hot/cold TRNs
neural_activity: Display transient ON responses (burst of action potentials) at stimulus onset, independent of direction (heating/cooling) and absolute temperature. Firing rate correlates with rate of temperature change.
behavioral_consequence: Genetic silencing (Kir2.1) produces defects in both hot and cold avoidance in 2-choice tests.
ecological_context: Rapid temperature change can quickly turn comfortable conditions into dangerous or lethal temperatures for a tiny poikilotherm fly.
techniques_used: ['2-photon guided patch clamp electrophysiology', 'Optogenetics (Chrimson in TRNs)', 'Genetic silencing (Kir2.1)', 'Split-Gal4 driver creation', '2-choice behavioral assays']
summary: TPN-IIIs display ON transients to rapid temperature changes regardless of direction, and silencing them impairs heat and cold avoidance behavior.

paper_title: Rapid threat assessment in the Drosophila thermosensory system
experimental_stimulus: Rapid heating and cooling steps with varying rates of temperature change
neural_activity: ON responses to heating/cooling bursts that track the rate of temperature change with a threshold (>0.2 °C/s). Steady-state firing modulated by absolute temperature (higher in cold).
behavioral_consequence: Silencing reduces hot and cold avoidance, reduces dry air avoidance, and reduces 'early turns' during single-fly navigation in thermal gradients. Does not affect olfactory avoidance.
ecological_context: Function as an early warning system to alert the fly that dangerous heat may be looming, priming locomotor behavior for rapid escape maneuvers.
techniques_used: ['2-photon guided patch clamp electrophysiology', 'Genetic silencing (Kir2.1)', 'Genetic activation (P2X2)', 'Calcium imaging (GCaMP)', 'Connectomics (Hemibrain)', '2-choice and single-fly behavioral assays']
summary: TLHONs fire ON transients to rapid thermal changes, silencing them impairs heat avoidance and early escape turns, but not olfactory responses.

paper_title: Rapid threat assessment in the Drosophila thermosensory system
experimental_stimulus: Implied thermal gradient context in behavioral assays
neural_activity: Receives indirect synaptic input from TLHONs mediated by LHAD1 and PVLP076 (connectomics analysis).
behavioral_consequence: Genetic silencing reduces 'early turns' in heat avoidance (25°C vs 35°C/40°C), producing a phenotype similar to TLHON silencing.
ecological_context: DNp06 contributes to propagate thermal signals to centers controlling fast locomotion, turning, and escape, relevant for evasive maneuvers.
summary: DNp06 is an indirect downstream target of TLHONs; silencing it reduces early escape turns in heat avoidance contexts.

paper_title: Rapid threat assessment in the Drosophila thermosensory system
experimental_stimulus: Implied thermal gradient context in behavioral assays
neural_activity: Receives weaker indirect synaptic input from TLHONs via intermediates (connectomics analysis).
behavioral_consequence: Genetic silencing produced no defects in heat avoidance behavior.
ecological_context: None explicitly discussed for this specific control finding.
summary: Silencing the downstream DNp09 does not affect heat avoidance, unlike DNp06 silencing.

paper_title: Multilevel visual motion opponency in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Moving gratings, moving dots, transparent motion (local and global), stochastic motion noise
neural_activity: Depolarize to preferred direction (PD) motion, hyperpolarize to null direction (ND) motion (motion-opponent). Strong motion-opponent suppression to transparent motion. Large receptive fields and slow temporal dynamics compared to other cell types. Conductance changes measured via electrophysiology.
ecological_context: Tuned to particular optic flow fields that match specific patterns of self-motion of the fly.
techniques_used: ['Two-photon voltage imaging', 'Whole-cell patch clamp electrophysiology', 'Calcium imaging', 'Computational modeling']
summary: VS cells exhibit motion-opponent responses and suppress responses to transparent motion. Their large receptive fields and slow dynamics contrast with earlier circuit stages, and opponent inhibition at this level generates flow field selectivity.

paper_title: Multilevel visual motion opponency in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Moving gratings, moving dots, transparent motion
neural_activity: Reciprocal motion-opponent responses: LPi3-4 depolarizes to upward motion and hyperpolarizes to downward motion; LPi4-3 shows opposite tuning. Strong motion-opponent inhibition mediated by GluClα receptors. Inhibition is balanced with excitation despite fewer synapses.
ecological_context: Motion opponency at this level enhances the responsiveness of LPTCs under noisy conditions.
techniques_used: ['Two-photon voltage imaging', 'Two-photon calcium imaging', 'Optogenetics (TeNT block)', 'RNA interference (GluClα-RNAi)', 'Connectomics (EM)']
summary: LPi neurons implement motion-opponent inhibition at multiple network levels, including reciprocal inhibition between LPi types and feedback to T4/T5 terminals, mediated by GluClα receptors.

paper_title: Multilevel visual motion opponency in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Moving gratings, moving dots, annulus motion, transparent motion
neural_activity: Axon terminals depolarize to preferred direction motion and hyperpolarize to null direction motion. Motion opponency and direction selectivity are higher in axon terminals than in dendrites. Receive inhibitory input from LPi cells.
ecological_context: Cancels locally opponent signals that arise from local luminance fluctuations in natural scenes.
summary: T4c axon terminals show motion-opponent inhibition from LPi cells, contributing to a multilevel opponent architecture that enhances circuit resilience to noise.

paper_title: A rotational velocity estimate constructed through visuomotor competition updates the fly’s neural compass
experimental_stimulus: Walking in darkness; Optic flow stimuli (square wave gratings, CW/CCW); Closed-loop visual experience (gains -2 to 2); Open-loop optic flow (50 deg/s); Leg removal to impair proprioception.
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP). Activity correlates with rotational velocity (Right GLNO active during right turns; Left GLNO active during left turns). Flip-flop activity pattern. Motor signal overrides congruent optic flow (positive gain). During conflict (negative gain/open-loop immobility), neurons toggle between following motor or visual signals. Habituation to prolonged visual motion.
behavioral_consequence: Inactivation via shibirets uncouples EPG bump from fly's head direction (path integration failure) without preventing bump movement or altering shape. Active during optomotor responses to counter external optic flow.
ecological_context: Updating the fly's head direction representation (compass) via angular path integration. Reliance on motor cues during active turns and visual cues during immobility or unexpected rotations (e.g., wind).
techniques_used: ['Two-photon calcium imaging', 'Temperature-sensitive shibirets inactivation', 'Virtual reality spherical treadmill', 'Whole-brain connectomics analysis']
summary: GLNO neurons encode rotational velocity for the head direction network using motor information primarily, but can incorporate optic flow during conflict or immobility. Inactivation impairs path integration accuracy.

paper_title: A rotational velocity estimate constructed through visuomotor competition updates the fly’s neural compass
experimental_stimulus: Walking in darkness or closed-loop with visual landmark.
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging. Population activity forms a bump in the ellipsoid body encoding head direction. Bump position tracked via population vector average.
behavioral_consequence: Bump position tracks fly's head direction accurately under normal conditions. When GLNO synaptic transmission is reduced, the EPG bump uncouples from the fly's actual head direction.
summary: EPG neurons encode head direction as a traveling bump of activity. Accurate tracking of head direction requires functional input from GLNO neurons.

paper_title: A rotational velocity estimate constructed through visuomotor competition updates the fly’s neural compass
experimental_stimulus: N/A (Identified via connectomics and previous physiology).
neural_activity: Two-photon imaging in previous work cited. Conjunctively tuned to head direction and rotational velocity. GLNO neurons identified as sole highly lateralized input to PEN populations.
behavioral_consequence: GLNO neurons inhibit contralateral PEN populations to differentially control left and right PEN bump amplitudes, driving compass updates.
summary: PEN neurons are the primary targets of GLNO input and drive the differential modulation of the head direction bump to update compass representation.

paper_title: Examining Sleep Modulation by Drosophila Ellipsoid Body Neurons
experimental_stimulus: Thermogenetic activation (UAS-TrpA1, 30-31°C); Optogenetic activation (UAS-CsChrimson); GABA synthesis inhibition (UAS-Gad1RNAi)
neural_activity: Confirmed GABAergic expression via immunostaining and Gad1-KI-Gal80 subtraction; Presynaptic active zone abundance (BRP::smFP_V5) unchanged after sleep deprivation
behavioral_consequence: Activation shortens sleep bout length and increases probability of awakening (P(wake)); reduces total sleep (especially nighttime); wake-promoting effect requires GABA synthesis
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed in natural ecological context
summary: ER3d neurons are GABAergic and promote wakefulness by reducing sleep maintenance; their activation shortens sleep bouts and increases awakening probability, a process dependent on GABA synthesis.

paper_title: Examining Sleep Modulation by Drosophila Ellipsoid Body Neurons
experimental_stimulus: Thermogenetic activation (UAS-TrpA1); Optogenetic activation (UAS-CsChrimson); GABA synthesis inhibition (UAS-Gad1RNAi); Antennal injury
neural_activity: Confirmed GABAergic expression via immunostaining; Presynaptic active zone intensity (BRP::smFP_V5/STaR) increases following sleep deprivation
behavioral_consequence: Activation increases total sleep time and mean sleep bout duration; decreases probability of waking (P(wake)); GABA synthesis is required for postinjury sleep response
ecological_context: Required for increased sleep following antennal transection (postinjury sleep)
summary: ER3m neurons are GABAergic and promote sleep consolidation; their activation increases sleep duration and bout length, and they are required for the sleep response to antennal injury.

paper_title: Examining Sleep Modulation by Drosophila Ellipsoid Body Neurons
experimental_stimulus: None (connectivity mapping only)
neural_activity: Provide putative synaptic contacts onto ER3m and ER3d neurons as shown by GRASP and Trans-Tango
behavioral_consequence: Not tested in this study
ecological_context: Cited as arousal-encoding in previous literature
summary: Helicon/ExR1 neurons form putative synaptic contacts onto ER3m and ER3d neurons in the ellipsoid body, though their behavioral function was not tested in this paper.

paper_title: A subclass of evening cells promotes the switch from arousal to sleep at dusk
experimental_stimulus: Light/dark cycles (12:12 LD), constant darkness (DD), thermogenetic activation (dTrpA1), electrical silencing (Kir2.1)
neural_activity: In vivo voltage imaging using Voltron2 revealed spiking rate does not vary between dawn and dusk. Electrical silencing and thermogenetic activation were used to manipulate output.
behavioral_consequence: Conditional silencing causes significant delay in sleep onset after dusk. Activation reduces sleep (promotes arousal) in light conditions (afternoon) but induces sleep after dusk. Activation effects are light-dependent (no effect in constant darkness). E1-specific clock ablation (per/tim CRISPR) has no effect on timing of this output.
ecological_context: Promotes the switch from arousal to sleep at dusk, a major transition in behavioral state for crepuscular animals.
techniques_used: ['Activity monitoring (Trikinetics)', 'In vivo voltage imaging (Voltron2)', 'Thermogenetics (dTrpA1)', 'Electrical silencing (Kir2.1/AGES)', 'CRISPR (tim/per knockout)', 'Split-GAL4']
summary: E1 neurons promote the transition from wakefulness to sleep at dusk. Their activation promotes arousal in light and sleep in dark in a light-dependent manner. Their firing rate is constant between dawn and dusk, and their timing relies on non-cell-autonomous clocks.

paper_title: A subclass of evening cells promotes the switch from arousal to sleep at dusk
experimental_stimulus: Clock gene manipulation (tim CRISPR, DBTS), thermogenetic activation (dTrpA1), electrical silencing (Kir2.1)
neural_activity: Electrical silencing and activation had no significant effect on evening anticipation (suggesting activity-independent control for this behavior).
behavioral_consequence: Ablating cell-autonomous clocks (tim) in E2 neurons reduces evening anticipatory behavior. Shortening clock period (DBTS) advances the phase of evening activity. Electrical manipulation of E2 neurons does not impair evening anticipation.
ecological_context: Drives and adjusts the phase of evening anticipation, the canonical behavior associated with 'evening' clock neurons prior to dusk.
summary: E2 cell-autonomous clocks drive evening anticipation. Electrical activity of E2 neurons is dispensable for evening anticipation.

paper_title: Shallow networks run deep: Peripheral preprocessing facilitates odor classification
experimental_stimulus: Odorant mixtures (Methyl acetate and Ethyl 3-hydroxy butyrate) at different dilution ratios
neural_activity: Firing rates measured via electrophysiology showed transient decay and suppression of neighbor activity due to ephaptic inhibition. Asymmetry parameter q was fitted to 0.019.
behavioral_consequence: Valence information (positive/negative) referenced from prior work (Ref 12) was used to interpret activity; no new behavioral tests performed in this study.
ecological_context: Insects encounter high-dimensional, transient odor stimuli in turbulent environments.
summary: Electrophysiological recordings from the ab2 sensillum validated a model of ephaptic inhibition between coupled ORNs, showing asymmetric suppression and transient valence amplification.

paper_title: Shallow networks run deep: Peripheral preprocessing facilitates odor classification
