cell_type: Uniglomerular Projection Neurons (uPNs) and Lateral Horn Neurons (LHNs)
experimental_stimulus: None; analyzed via connectome data (Hemibrain)
neural_activity: Connectome analysis revealed structured AL-to-LH connectivity. uPNs postsynaptic to positive-valence ORNs had significantly larger PC1 coefficients than those from negative-valence ORNs.
behavioral_consequence: Lateral Horn is known to mediate innate odor-guided behaviors (cited); connectivity structure predicts valence readout.
ecological_context: Mediating innate olfactory behaviors in response to odor mixtures.
summary: Connectome analysis of AL-to-LH projections demonstrates structured connectivity where uPN-LHN weights align with peripheral ephaptic interactions to compute odor valence.

paper_title: Shallow networks run deep: Peripheral preprocessing facilitates odor classification
experimental_stimulus: Simulated odor stimuli (N=50 dimensions, uniform private odorants)
neural_activity: Simulated sparse responses via random expansion and sparsification from AL inputs.
behavioral_consequence: Simulated classification error (SVM) showed marked improvement in odor classification when peripheral ephaptic preprocessing was included.
ecological_context: Novel odor classification in higher brain centers without requiring clustered input-layer representations.
summary: Computational simulations demonstrated that peripheral ephaptic preprocessing markedly improves odor classification performance in the Mushroom Body via random expansion and sparsification.

paper_title: A neuronal substrate for translating nutrient state and resource density estimations into foraging decisions
experimental_stimulus: Nutritional state (protein deprivation vs fully fed); Foraging environment (high vs low patch density/short vs long travel time); Yeast patch encounter
neural_activity: Whole-cell patch-clamp recordings show significantly lower firing rate and resting membrane potential in protein-deprived flies compared to fully fed flies; activity modulated by protein state
behavioral_consequence: Silencing increases probability to engage with yeast patches; Optogenetic activation decreases probability to engage when approaching patches; Silencing abolishes the adaptation of engagement probability to travel time (patch density) but does not affect patch leaving duration or total food intake
ecological_context: Regulates the transition from exploration to exploitation in foraging, integrating internal nutrient needs with external resource density estimates to optimize foraging decisions
techniques_used: ['High-throughput video tracking', 'Neurogenetic silencing (Kir2.1)', 'Optogenetics (CsChrimson)', 'Whole-cell patch-clamp electrophysiology', 'Split-GAL4 intersection', 'Confocal microscopy', 'Electron microscopy connectome matching']
summary: FB6A.1 neurons in the dorsal fan-shaped body control the decision to engage with food patches. Protein deprivation decreases their firing rate. Silencing increases engagement probability and prevents adaptation to patch density (travel time), without affecting total intake or patch leaving duration.

paper_title: Stimulus-Specific Modulation is Enabled by Differential Serotonin Receptor Expression
experimental_stimulus: Farnesol, benzaldehyde; 5-HT receptor agonists (ipsapirone) and antagonists (WAY100635, metitepine)
neural_activity: Calcium imaging revealed ipsapirone (5-HT1A/1B agonist) significantly reduced odor-evoked responses in AL processes but not LH processes. 5-HT application did not significantly affect LH responses. 5-HT1A RNAi eliminated ipsapirone-induced reduction in AL.
ecological_context: Innervate glomeruli (DC3, VL2a) responsive to plant-derived odors (e.g., farnesol); project to ventral LH region associated with pheromone processing.
summary: R24H08 v-PNs express 5-HT1A and 5-HT1B receptors; 5-HT1A/1B activation suppresses odor-evoked responses in the AL but not the LH.

paper_title: Stimulus-Specific Modulation is Enabled by Differential Serotonin Receptor Expression
experimental_stimulus: 1-octen-3-ol, 1-hexanol; 5-HT receptor agonists (AS-19) and antagonists (SB269970, metitepine)
neural_activity: Calcium imaging in LH axon terminals showed 5-HT and AS-19 (5-HT7 agonist) significantly enhanced odor-evoked responses. 5-HT7 RNAi eliminated enhancement for 1-hexanol (direct modulation) but not 1-octen-3-ol (polysynaptic). AS-19 suppressed responses when 5-HT7 RNAi was expressed.
ecological_context: Multiglomerular neurons projecting to dorsomedial LH; innervate distinct glomeruli compared to R24H08 v-PNs.
summary: R86G06 v-PNs express 5-HT7 receptors; 5-HT7 activation enhances odor-evoked responses, with direct modulation for 1-hexanol and polysynaptic enhancement for 1-octen-3-ol.

paper_title: Stimulus-Specific Modulation is Enabled by Differential Serotonin Receptor Expression
experimental_stimulus: None (Anatomical analysis of receptor expression and innervation)
neural_activity: All five 5-HT receptors co-expressed within v-PN and lv-PN populations. Co-expression patterns differ; 5-HT7 receptors primarily expressed in v-PNs innervating variable glomeruli, while other receptors (1A, 1B, 2A, 2B) associated with more specific glomerular innervation patterns.
ecological_context: Subsets innervate different combinations of glomeruli and LH regions, enabling stimulus-specific modulation of olfactory coding.
summary: v-PNs and lv-PNs display heterogeneous 5-HT receptor co-expression patterns that correlate with distinct glomerular innervation and downstream connectivity.

paper_title: PDF neuropeptide signals independently of Bruchpilot-labelled active zones in daily remodelled terminals of Drosophila clock neurons
experimental_stimulus: Circadian time points (ZT2 vs ZT14); RNAi-mediated knockdown of synaptic/vesicle genes (brp, cadps, syx, etc.) in sLNvs; Constant Darkness (DD) and Constant Light (LL) conditions
neural_activity: Expansion microscopy showed the number of BRP-labelled punctae remained stable between ZT2 and ZT14, while density increased at ZT14 (compaction). ssTEM analysis of the FAFB dataset showed spontaneous dense-core vesicle release profiles lacked spatial correlation with BRP-organised active zones. Transcriptomics revealed brp and Pdf mRNA expression peaks were out of phase. RNAi knockdown of brp did not impair PDF-dependent activity, while cadps and SNARE knockdown impaired activity.
behavioral_consequence: Locomotor rhythmicity in DD remained normal after brp RNAi but was impaired after cadps or pdf RNAi. In LL, brp RNAi reduced rhythmicity, likely affecting glycine transmission.
ecological_context: PDF serves as a major clock output modulating locomotor activity and aligning the molecular clockwork of other central clock neurons. PDF signalling is required for normal diel morphological plasticity of sLNv terminals.
techniques_used: ['Expansion microscopy', 'Confocal microscopy', 'Serial section transmission electron microscopy (ssTEM)', 'Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) analysis', 'RNAi-mediated knockdown', 'Locomotor activity monitoring (DAM-2)']
summary: PDF release from sLNvs is independent of BRP-organised active zones, supported by stable BRP punctae numbers despite terminal remodeling, lack of spatial correlation with DCV release in EM, and normal locomotor rhythmicity in DD after brp knockdown. PDF release requires CADPS and SNARE proteins.

paper_title: The RNA-binding protein, Imp specifies olfactory navigation circuitry and behavior in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Not tested directly in this study (odor presented to fly)
neural_activity: Not tested (firing/calcium); Expresses Tachykinin (TK) neuropeptide (immunostaining)
behavioral_consequence: Loss of these neurons (via Imp knockdown in Type II NSCs) abolishes upwind orientation to attractive odor
ecological_context: Olfactory navigation in hungry flies tracking attractive odors upwind
summary: VT029515 neurons are derived from Type II neural stem cells and express Tachykinin. Imp knockdown in Type II NSCs eliminates these neurons and abolishes upwind orientation behavior.

paper_title: The RNA-binding protein, Imp specifies olfactory navigation circuitry and behavior in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Not tested directly in this study (wind direction presented to fly)
neural_activity: Not tested (firing/calcium)
behavioral_consequence: Loss of these neurons (via Imp knockdown in Type II NSCs) abolishes upwind orientation to attractive odor
ecological_context: Olfactory navigation in hungry flies tracking attractive odors upwind
summary: Ventral P-FNs arise from Type II neural stem cells. Imp knockdown in Type II NSCs eliminates these neurons and abolishes upwind orientation behavior.

paper_title: The RNA-binding protein, Imp specifies olfactory navigation circuitry and behavior in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Not tested directly in this study (odor and wind presented to fly)
neural_activity: Not tested (firing/calcium); Morphology altered by Imp levels (axonal projections span multiple layers)
behavioral_consequence: Loss of function of circuit containing these neurons (via Imp knockdown in Type II NSCs) impairs persistent upwind tracking
ecological_context: Olfactory navigation in hungry flies tracking attractive odors upwind
summary: hΔC/K neurons arise from Type II neural stem cells. Imp knockdown alters their morphology and abolishes upwind orientation behavior.

paper_title: The RNA-binding protein, Imp specifies olfactory navigation circuitry and behavior in Drosophila
cell_type: Type II Neural Stem Cells (DM1-DM6, DL1-DL2)
experimental_stimulus: Developmental heat shock (0h, 24h, 48h, 72h ALH), Imp RNAi/overexpression
neural_activity: Express Imp (RNA-binding protein); Regulate neuron number and morphology
behavioral_consequence: Imp knockdown abolishes upwind orientation to attractive odor while leaving locomotion intact
ecological_context: Olfactory navigation in hungry flies tracking attractive odors upwind
techniques_used: ['Heat shock birth dating', 'Imp RNAi/overexpression', 'Immunostaining', 'Behavioral assays (Wind tunnel)']
summary: Type II neural stem cells generate CX olfactory navigation circuitry. Imp expression in these cells is required for neuron specification (number/morphology) and upwind orientation behavior.

paper_title: Heterogeneous receptor expression underlies non-uniform peptidergic modulation of olfaction in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Apple cider vinegar (ACV), 1-hexanol, 1-octen-3-ol, benzaldehyde, geranyl acetate, ammonium hydroxide
neural_activity: ACV reliably and consistently activated MIPergic LN neurites in every identifiable glomerulus. Other odors (geranyl acetate, 1-octen-3-ol) evoked inhibitory responses in MIPergic LN neurites.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested in this study.
ecological_context: ACV is a food-related odor; MIPergic LNs innervate food-odor associated glomeruli.
techniques_used: ['In vivo calcium imaging (GCaMP6f)', 'Immunohistochemistry (anti-MIP, anti-GAD1)', 'Electron microscopy (hemibrain connectome)']
summary: MIPergic LNs are GABAergic patchy local interneurons in the Drosophila antennal lobe that are robustly activated by food odor (ACV) but inhibited by other diverse odors, forming reciprocal connections with principal neurons.

paper_title: Heterogeneous receptor expression underlies non-uniform peptidergic modulation of olfaction in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Apple cider vinegar (ACV) at 10^-2 and 10^-6 concentrations
neural_activity: Synthetic MIP application increased responses in DM1 and DM4 glomeruli OSNs and decreased responses in DM2 and DM5 glomeruli OSNs. SPR knockdown abolished MIP-induced decreases in DM2 and DM5 but not increases in DM1 and DM4.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested in this study.
ecological_context: DM1, DM2, DM4, and DM5 are food-odor associated glomeruli involved in food search and attraction.
techniques_used: ['In vivo calcium imaging (GCaMP6f)', 'Synthetic MIP pressure injection', 'RNAi knockdown (UAS-SPR-RNAi)']
summary: OSNs in food-associated glomeruli show differential gain modulation by MIP application, with direct inhibition in DM2/DM5 and indirect enhancement in DM1/DM4, mediated by the SPR receptor.

paper_title: Alcohol tolerance encoding in sleep regulatory circadian neurons in Drosophila
cell_type: Mushroom Body α/β lobe intrinsic neurons
experimental_stimulus: 55% ethanol vapor exposure (E1 and E2 separated by 3.5 hours)
neural_activity: Silencing synaptic output (UAS-TeTx) or decreased expression of presynaptic genes cac and Cdk5 via RNAi
behavioral_consequence: Decreased rapid ethanol tolerance; silencing did not affect naive ethanol sensitivity
ecological_context: Known site of learning and memory, sleep, and locomotor navigation; overlaps with sleep regulatory circuitry
summary: Silencing synaptic output or decreasing presynaptic gene expression in MB α/β lobes reduces rapid ethanol tolerance without affecting sensitivity, indicating a role in tolerance encoding.

paper_title: Alcohol tolerance encoding in sleep regulatory circadian neurons in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: 55% ethanol vapor exposure
neural_activity: Decreased glutamate release via vGlut RNAi (R16C05-Gal4 pattern); PDF receptor (Pdfr) reduction had no effect
behavioral_consequence: Decreased rapid ethanol tolerance
ecological_context: Circadian clock neurons that regulate arousal by light at night, temperature-dependent sleep timing, and night-time sleep; organize locomotor activity states
techniques_used: ['Genetic manipulation (vGlut RNAi, Pdfr RNAi)', 'Behavioral ethanol sedation assay', 'Immunohistochemistry (CCHa1, DH31)']
summary: DN1a circadian clock neurons promote rapid ethanol tolerance through glutamatergic signaling, independent of PDF receptor input.

paper_title: Alcohol tolerance encoding in sleep regulatory circadian neurons in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: 55% ethanol vapor exposure
neural_activity: Decreased glutamate release via vGlut RNAi (R51H05-Gal4 pattern)
behavioral_consequence: No effect on rapid ethanol tolerance
ecological_context: Circadian clock neurons regulating sleep; distinct from DN1a in tolerance role
summary: Glutamatergic signaling in DN1p neurons is not required for rapid ethanol tolerance development, unlike DN1a neurons.

paper_title: Alcohol tolerance encoding in sleep regulatory circadian neurons in Drosophila
cell_type: LNd6 and 5th LNv neurons (E2 evening neurons)
experimental_stimulus: 55% ethanol vapor exposure
neural_activity: Decreased metabotropic glutamate receptor (mGluR) expression via DvPdf-Gal4 pattern
behavioral_consequence: Decreased rapid ethanol tolerance
ecological_context: Postsynaptic to DN1a neurons; regulate evening activity; part of circadian clock circuitry
summary: Postsynaptic E2 evening neurons (LNd6, 5th LNv) require mGluR signaling to promote rapid ethanol tolerance downstream of DN1a neurons.

paper_title: Alcohol tolerance encoding in sleep regulatory circadian neurons in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: 55% ethanol vapor exposure
neural_activity: Decreased glutamate release via vGlut RNAi
behavioral_consequence: Decreased rapid ethanol tolerance
ecological_context: Sparse neurons distinct from mushroom bodies, ellipsoid body, perineurial glia, and DN1 clusters
summary: R82F12-Gal4 neurons are a distinct population of glutamatergic neurons that promote rapid ethanol tolerance, separate from DN1 neurons.

paper_title: Alcohol tolerance encoding in sleep regulatory circadian neurons in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: 55% ethanol vapor exposure
neural_activity: Decreased glutamate release via vGlut RNAi
behavioral_consequence: No effect on rapid ethanol tolerance
ecological_context: Sparse neurons identified in forward screen; distinct glutamatergic tolerance neurons not found in this pattern
summary: R79H04-Gal4 neurons contain non-glutamatergic tolerance neurons, as vGlut reduction did not reduce tolerance.

paper_title: Modulation and Neural Correlates of Postmating Sleep Plasticity in Drosophila Females
experimental_stimulus: Mating (Sex Peptide transfer from males)
neural_activity: Impaired neurotransmitter release via tetanus toxin (TNT)
behavioral_consequence: Prevented postmating nighttime sleep changes in female flies on normal food
ecological_context: Mediates postmating sleep suppression in response to mating status on nutrient-rich food
summary: SPSN neurons are required for postmating nighttime sleep suppression on normal food; silencing them prevents the sleep loss seen in mated females.

paper_title: Modulation and Neural Correlates of Postmating Sleep Plasticity in Drosophila Females
experimental_stimulus: Mating (Sex Peptide signal)
neural_activity: Impaired neurotransmitter release via tetanus toxin (TNT)
behavioral_consequence: Prevented postmating nighttime sleep changes in female flies on normal food
ecological_context: Acts downstream of SPSN to mediate postmating sleep suppression
summary: SAG neurons are required for postmating nighttime sleep suppression on normal food; silencing them abolishes the sleep loss difference between mated and virgin females.

paper_title: Modulation and Neural Correlates of Postmating Sleep Plasticity in Drosophila Females
cell_type: Female-specific pC1 Neurons (subtypes a, c, e)
experimental_stimulus: Mating status; Temperature shift (22°C to 27°C for TrpA1); ATP perfusion (for P2X2)
neural_activity: Calcium imaging showed reduced calcium levels in dFB neurons upon activation; Silenced via TNT
behavioral_consequence: Silencing attenuated postmating nighttime sleep loss; Activation suppressed sleep in virgin and mated females on normal food
ecological_context: Sleep suppression is food-dependent; activation has little effect on sucrose-only food
summary: pC1 subtypes a, c, e are required for postmating sleep plasticity; activation suppresses sleep on normal food but not sucrose-only, and inhibits dFB neurons.

paper_title: Modulation and Neural Correlates of Postmating Sleep Plasticity in Drosophila Females
experimental_stimulus: Mating status; Temperature shift (22°C to 27°C for TrpA1)
neural_activity: Silenced via TNT
behavioral_consequence: Silencing attenuated postmating nighttime sleep loss; Activation suppressed sleep in both virgins and mated females on normal food
ecological_context: Food context modulates sleep suppression; little sleep effect on sucrose-only food
summary: All pC1 subtypes contribute to postmating sleep loss; activation suppresses sleep in a food-dependent manner.

paper_title: Modulation and Neural Correlates of Postmating Sleep Plasticity in Drosophila Females
experimental_stimulus: Mating status; Temperature shift (22°C to 29°C for TrpA1)
neural_activity: Calcium imaging showed reduced calcium levels in dFB neurons upon activation; Silenced via TNT
behavioral_consequence: Silencing had little effect on postmating sleep changes; Activation suppressed sleep on normal food; modest reduction on sucrose
ecological_context: Diet-dependent sleep suppression suggests integration of mating status and food context
summary: pC1e activation suppresses sleep in a diet-dependent manner and inhibits dFB neurons, though silencing had minimal effect possibly due to redundancy.

paper_title: Modulation and Neural Correlates of Postmating Sleep Plasticity in Drosophila Females
experimental_stimulus: Mating status
neural_activity: Impaired neurotransmitter release via TNT
behavioral_consequence: Did not prevent nighttime sleep suppression in mated flies
ecological_context: Oviposition circuit components are dispensable for postmating sleep suppression
summary: OviIN neurons are dispensable for postmating nighttime sleep suppression despite being part of the oviposition circuit.

paper_title: Modulation and Neural Correlates of Postmating Sleep Plasticity in Drosophila Females
experimental_stimulus: Mating status
neural_activity: Impaired neurotransmitter release via TNT
behavioral_consequence: Did not prevent nighttime sleep suppression in mated flies
ecological_context: Oviposition circuit components are dispensable for postmating sleep suppression
summary: OviEN neurons are dispensable for postmating nighttime sleep suppression despite being part of the oviposition circuit.

paper_title: Modulation and Neural Correlates of Postmating Sleep Plasticity in Drosophila Females
experimental_stimulus: Mating status
neural_activity: Impaired neurotransmitter release via TNT
behavioral_consequence: Did not prevent nighttime sleep suppression in mated flies
ecological_context: Oviposition circuit components are dispensable for postmating sleep suppression
summary: OviDN neurons are dispensable for postmating nighttime sleep suppression despite being part of the oviposition circuit.

paper_title: Modulation and Neural Correlates of Postmating Sleep Plasticity in Drosophila Females
experimental_stimulus: Sleep deprivation; Mating status; pC1 activation (ATP)
neural_activity: Calcium levels reduced when pC1 activated (GCaMP7b); Silenced (TNT) or activated (NaChBac)
behavioral_consequence: Inhibition abolished postmating nighttime sleep loss difference; Activation increased sleep more in mated females than virgins
ecological_context: Integrates mating status, food content, and sleep drive to regulate female sleep
techniques_used: ['Genetic silencing (TNT)', 'Genetic activation (NaChBac)', 'Calcium imaging (GCaMP7b)', 'Mechanical sleep deprivation', 'Drosophila Activity Monitoring (DAM)']
summary: dFB neurons are required for postmating sleep plasticity; they are inhibited by pC1 activation and integrate mating, food, and sleep drive signals to regulate sleep.

paper_title: Behavioral state-dependent modulation of insulin-producing cells in Drosophila.
experimental_stimulus: Nutritional state (starved vs. fed), Glucose refeeding (400 mM), Glucose perfusion (40 mM extracellular saline), Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson)
neural_activity: Starved IPCs were quiescent (0 Hz firing) compared to fed IPCs (0-1.4 Hz). Glucose perfusion did not increase activity. Glucose refeeding increased activity after 6 hours. Optogenetic activation increased spiking.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation in starved flies reduced starvation-induced hyperactivity (satiety-like effect) but did not abolish it. Activation in fed flies had no notable effect on walking activity.
ecological_context: Maintaining metabolic homeostasis and modulating foraging behavior in response to nutritional state shifts (e.g., starvation-induced hyperactivity).
techniques_used: ['In vivo patch-clamp electrophysiology', 'Optogenetics', 'Universal Fly Observatory (UFO) behavioral tracking']
summary: IPCs are quiescent in starved flies and active in fed flies. They respond to glucose ingestion but not extracellular glucose perfusion. Optogenetic activation reduces starvation-induced hyperactivity in starved flies.

paper_title: Behavioral state-dependent modulation of insulin-producing cells in Drosophila.
cell_type: DH44-expressing neurons in the pars intercerebralis (DH44PINs)
experimental_stimulus: Nutritional state (starved vs. fed), Glucose perfusion (40 mM extracellular saline), Optogenetic activation (DH44PI-GAL4)
neural_activity: Baseline spike rates were similar in fed and starved flies, but Inter-Spike Intervals shifted after starvation. Glucose perfusion significantly increased spike rate in starved flies. Activation did not affect IPC activity.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation increased forward velocity overall compared to controls but did not cause stopping behavior or proboscis extension.
ecological_context: Glucose sensing and post-feeding regulation of metabolic homeostasis in the brain.
techniques_used: ['In vivo patch-clamp electrophysiology', 'Optogenetics', 'Universal Fly Observatory (UFO) behavioral tracking']
summary: DH44PINs are glucose-sensitive in starved flies (extracellular perfusion) but do not inhibit IPCs. Their optogenetic activation increases walking speed without stopping behavior.

paper_title: Behavioral state-dependent modulation of insulin-producing cells in Drosophila.
cell_type: DH44-expressing neurons outside the pars intercerebralis (DH44Ns)
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (broad DH44-GAL4 driver line)
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation of broad DH44Ns drove immediate hyperpolarization and reduced spiking in IPCs (strong inhibition).
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation dramatically increased locomotor activity (forward velocity) followed by abrupt stopping behavior and proboscis extension. Post-activation activity increased over cycles, mimicking starvation-induced hyperactivity.
ecological_context: Promoting feeding and foraging behavior; signaling hunger states to ensure reciprocal inhibition of satiety and hunger pathways.
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics combined with patch-clamp electrophysiology (functional connectivity)', 'Optogenetics with Universal Fly Observatory (UFO) behavioral tracking']
summary: DH44Ns outside the PI inhibit IPCs. Optogenetic activation drives locomotor activity, stopping behavior, and proboscis extension, phenocopying starvation-induced hyperactivity.

paper_title: Behavioral state-dependent modulation of insulin-producing cells in Drosophila.
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (TDC2-GAL4; CsChrimson)
neural_activity: Not measured electrophysiologically in this study; used as a reference for behavioral effects.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation caused a strong, significant increase in walking activity comparable to starvation-induced hyperactivity. Stopping behavior was observed during acute activation onset.
ecological_context: Mediating starvation-induced hyperactivity to enable food search and foraging behavior.
summary: Optogenetic activation of OANs drives locomotor activity comparable to starvation-induced hyperactivity and induces stopping behavior during activation.

paper_title: Dopamine promotes head direction plasticity during orienting movements
experimental_stimulus: Rotational speed (on spherical treadmill in darkness), wide-field rotational optic flow (stationary fly), visual cue rotation
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (jGCaMP7f) shows activity scales linearly with moment-to-moment rotational speed; robustly activated by wide-field rotational optic flow; lateralized activity in lateral accessory lobe correlates with ipsilateral rotational velocity.
behavioral_consequence: Chemogenetic activation (ATP/P2X2) or dopamine application strengthens the association between visual cues and head direction neurons (increased mutual information between cue and EPG bump, more consistent tuning); inhibition (Kir2.1) decreases cue influence; no effect on fly walking behaviour or self-motion tracking when inhibited.
ecological_context: Rotational movements coincide with internal cues (motor commands/proprioception) that can be compared with external head direction cues (visual patterns, wind); dopamine compresses learning into these data-rich epochs to prevent over-learning stationary views during straight-line navigation.
techniques_used: ['Two-photon calcium imaging', 'Electrophysiology (whole-cell patch clamp)', 'Chemogenetics (ATP/P2X2)', 'Optogenetics (Chrimson)', 'Pharmacology (dopamine bath)', 'Spherical treadmill/virtual reality']
summary: ExR2 dopamine neurons are active during rotational movements, and their activity promotes synaptic plasticity at visual-to-head-direction synapses, strengthening cue associations without affecting locomotion.

paper_title: Dopamine promotes head direction plasticity during orienting movements
experimental_stimulus: Rotating visual cue (vertical bar), closed-loop visual cue controlled by fly rotation
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (jGCaMP7f) and electrophysiology show a 'bump' of activity in the ellipsoid body that tracks visual cue position; visual response amplitude correlates with rotational speed.
behavioral_consequence: ExR2 activation paired with visual cue increases consistency of visual cue responses (decreased cycle-to-cycle jitter in preferred position) and increases bump amplitude; ExR2 inhibition reduces mutual information between cue position and bump position.
ecological_context: Part of the head direction system used to enforce straight-line paths while navigating towards distant goal destinations; relies on updating the map during orienting movements.
techniques_used: ['Two-photon calcium imaging', 'Electrophysiology (whole-cell patch clamp)', 'Virtual reality panorama']
summary: EPG head direction neurons update their visual cue associations based on rotational speed signals modulated by ExR2 dopamine activity.

paper_title: Dopamine promotes head direction plasticity during orienting movements
experimental_stimulus: Not directly manipulated in this paper (inferred inputs)
neural_activity: Not directly recorded in this study; known to provide visual input to EPG neurons; express dopamine receptors
behavioral_consequence: Synapses from ER to EPG are plastic; their plasticity is gated by ExR2 dopamine activity
ecological_context: Provide visual input to the head direction map; ER-to-EPG synapses are in close proximity to dopamine release sites
summary: ER neurons provide visual input to EPG neurons, and their synapses onto EPG neurons are modulated by ExR2 dopamine activity to facilitate learning.

paper_title: Converting an allocentric goal into an egocentric steering signal
experimental_stimulus: Menotaxis task with visual bar (virtual reality); virtual bar rotations (+/- 90 degrees); wind-induced angular memory task (airflow)
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP7f): EPG neurons express a calcium activity bump in the ellipsoid body and protocerebral bridge. The phase of the bump rotates +/- 90 degrees in lockstep with virtual bar rotations, tracking the allocentric heading angle.
behavioral_consequence: Genetic silencing (shibirets) prevents flies from orienting along a previously experienced allocentric wind direction after wind is turned off (wind-induced angular memory task). Does not impair upwind orientation (anemotaxis) while wind is on.
ecological_context: Encodes the fly's moment-to-moment angular orientation (heading angle) required for navigation and maintaining a stable bearing (menotaxis).
techniques_used: ['Two-photon calcium imaging', 'Genetic silencing (shibirets)', 'Behavioral assays (menotaxis, wind memory task)']
summary: EPG neurons encode allocentric heading angle via a calcium bump phase that tracks heading. Silencing impairs memory of allocentric wind direction after wind cessation.

paper_title: Converting an allocentric goal into an egocentric steering signal
experimental_stimulus: Menotaxis task with visual bar; virtual bar rotations; focal optogenetic activation (CsChrimson) of fan-shaped body columns
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (sytGCaMP7f): FC2 neurons express a calcium bump in the fan-shaped body. The phase remains stable during virtual bar rotations (unlike EPG), tracking the allocentric goal angle.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation induces flies to stabilize a consistent heading angle corresponding to the stimulated fan-shaped body location, effectively changing the fly's goal direction.
ecological_context: Signals the allocentric goal direction (desired progress direction) to downstream circuits for navigation.
summary: FC2 neurons signal allocentric goal angle via a stable calcium bump phase. Optogenetic activation biases flies to orient along experimenter-defined goal directions.

paper_title: Converting an allocentric goal into an egocentric steering signal
experimental_stimulus: Menotaxis task with visual bar; virtual bar rotations; focal optogenetic activation of left/right LAL terminals; genetic silencing (TNT/shibirets) of subsets
neural_activity: Whole-cell patch-clamp: Conjunctive spike-rate tuning to both heading and goal angles. Two-photon imaging of axon terminals in LAL: Right-minus-left activity asymmetry precedes turning. Optogenetic activation causes ipsilateral turning.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation of left/right LAL causes ipsilateral turning. Silencing PFL3 neurons increases wind-direction error and reduces the number of goal directions stabilized in a wind-induced angular memory task.
ecological_context: Generates an egocentric steering signal by comparing allocentric heading and goal signals to guide locomotor turns during navigation.
techniques_used: ['Whole-cell patch-clamp electrophysiology', 'Two-photon calcium imaging (LAL)', 'Focal optogenetics (LAL stimulation)', 'Genetic silencing (TNT, shibirets)', 'Behavioral assays (menotaxis, wind memory task)']
summary: PFL3 neurons compare allocentric heading and goal signals to produce egocentric steering signals. Optogenetic activation causes ipsilateral turning; silencing impairs memory-guided navigation.

paper_title: Converting an allocentric goal into an egocentric steering signal
experimental_stimulus: Focal optogenetic activation (CsChrimson) of left/right LAL terminals
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP): Showed strong activation during stimulation.
behavioral_consequence: Did not cause an increase in ipsilateral turning velocity during stimulation compared to controls, despite strong GCaMP signal.
ecological_context: Serves as a control demonstrating that ipsilateral turning is specific to PFL3 activity and not an inevitable outcome of asymmetric LAL stimulation.
summary: PFL1 neurons show no turning bias upon optogenetic activation of LAL terminals, unlike PFL3 neurons.

paper_title: Identification of a GABAergic neuroblast lineage modulating sweet and bitter taste sensitivity.
experimental_stimulus: Sweet tastants (1, 10, 35, 100, 1000 mM sucrose) and bitter tastants (L-canavanine, denatonium) applied to the proboscis/labellum under fed and starved conditions.
neural_activity: Calcium imaging (GCaMP6s) showed increased GCaMP signal in somata and neurites upon sucrose stimulation. A subset of neurons responded to L-canavanine. Immunohistochemistry confirmed GABA expression and lack of ChAT expression. GRASP showed synaptic contacts with sweet GRNs (Gr5a) but no direct GRASP signal with bitter GRNs (Gr66a).
behavioral_consequence: Silencing TRdm neurons (via Tetanus toxin) increased the Proboscis Extension Response (PER) to low sucrose concentrations in satiated flies and increased sweet sensitivity in starved flies. It also alleviated the inhibitory effect of L-canavanine on feeding behavior (reduced aversive sensitivity). No effect was observed on tarsal PER assays.
ecological_context: Modulates taste sensitivity involved in the initiation or inhibition of feeding behavior, potentially acting as a gain control mechanism for gustatory input.
techniques_used: ['Calcium imaging (GCaMP6s)', 'Immunohistochemistry (anti-GABA, anti-ChAT)', 'GRASP (GFP reconstitution across synaptic partners)', 'Proboscis Extension Response (PER) assays', 'Neuronal silencing (Tetanus toxin)', 'Clonal analysis (MARCM/FLP-out)']
summary: TRdm lineage GABAergic local interneurons in the subesophageal zone respond to sweet and bitter tastants via calcium imaging. Silencing these neurons increases appetitive feeding behavior to sucrose and reduces aversive inhibition by bitter compounds during labellar taste perception.

paper_title: Visual and motor signatures of locomotion dynamically shape a population code for feature detection in Drosophila
cell_type: Small Object Detecting Visual Projection Neurons (e.g., LC11, LC21, LC18, LC12, LC15)
experimental_stimulus: 15° dark moving spot probe, natural images with rotation, visual saccades, walking behavior (air-suspended ball)
neural_activity: Calcium responses (syt1GCaMP6f) strongly suppressed during walking and visual rotation/saccades; suppression strongest when saccade coincides with probe response
behavioral_consequence: Not tested (response gain modulated by walking behavior; no activation/inactivation behavior tested)
ecological_context: Transiently reduces sensitivity during locomotor saccades to avoid corrupting signals, allowing sampling during intersaccadic heading stability
techniques_used: ['Two-photon volumetric calcium imaging', 'Connectome-based glomerulus mapping', 'Air-suspended ball tracking', 'Virtual reality stimulation']
summary: Small object detecting VPNs show trial-to-trial gain modulation correlated with walking behavior and visual rotation, suppressing sensitivity during saccades to maintain feature detection fidelity.

paper_title: Visual and motor signatures of locomotion dynamically shape a population code for feature detection in Drosophila
cell_type: Loom Detecting Visual Projection Neurons (e.g., LC4, LPLC2, LC9)
experimental_stimulus: Dark looming spot probe, walking behavior (air-suspended ball)
neural_activity: Calcium responses not significantly modulated by walking behavior
behavioral_consequence: Not tested (response gain modulated by walking behavior; no activation/inactivation behavior tested)
ecological_context: Maintains sensitivity to large visual features (e.g., predators) during locomotion
summary: Loom detecting VPNs are not suppressed by walking behavior, unlike small object detectors, preserving sensitivity to large features during locomotion.

paper_title: Visual and motor signatures of locomotion dynamically shape a population code for feature detection in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Synthetic visual stimuli (spots, looming, bars, gratings, natural images)
neural_activity: Calcium responses (syt1GCaMP6f) show strong trial-to-trial covariance across population; decoding accuracy improves with correlated variability
behavioral_consequence: Not tested (trial-to-trial gain correlated with walking behavior)
ecological_context: Joint representation and shared gain improve coding fidelity for feature detection during self-motion
techniques_used: ['Connectome-based volumetric imaging', 'Multinomial logistic regression decoding', 'Trial shuffling analysis']
summary: A shared gain factor modulates trial-to-trial variability across the VPN population, enhancing stimulus decoding fidelity during locomotion.

paper_title: Postsynaptic plasticity of cholinergic synapses underlies the induction and expression of appetitive and familiarity memories in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Odor exposure (OCT, MCH) during T-maze training protocols (appetitive and aversive)
neural_activity: Blocking neurotransmitter release using temperature-sensitive Shibire (Shi ts) at 32°C. No calcium transients observed in KC axons following focal acetylcholine application in explant preparations.
behavioral_consequence: Blocking KC output during training abolishes 30-min appetitive memory performance. Blocking KC output during training does not significantly impact 30-min aversive memory performance.
ecological_context: Olfactory association formation required for learning about food rewards (sugar) or aversive stimuli (electric shock) in the environment.
techniques_used: ['Temperature-sensitive Shibire (Shi ts)', 'T-maze behavioral assay', 'Calcium imaging (GCaMP6f)', 'Explant brain preparation']
summary: KC neurotransmitter release is required for the acquisition of appetitive memories but is dispensable for the formation of aversive memories in Drosophila.

paper_title: Postsynaptic plasticity of cholinergic synapses underlies the induction and expression of appetitive and familiarity memories in Drosophila
cell_type: M4/6 MBONs (MBON-γ5β'2a, MBON-β'2mp, MBON-β2β'2a, MBON-β'2mp bilateral)
experimental_stimulus: Odor exposure paired with sugar (appetitive) or shock (aversive); Focal acetylcholine application and optogenetic DAN activation in explant; Repeated odor exposure.
neural_activity: Dendritic calcium transients measured via GCaMP6f imaging show depression after in vivo appetitive training. Postsynaptic calcium responses to acetylcholine increase following paired acetylcholine and dopaminergic neuron activation in explant. α2 dynamics stalling observed after paired training via FRAP.
behavioral_consequence: RNAi knockdown of α5 nAChR subunit impairs immediate appetitive memory. Knockdown of α2, α1, or Dlg impairs 3-hour appetitive memory. Knockdown of α2 or α5 does not affect aversive memory performance.
ecological_context: Encoding appetitive and aversive associations by weighing up valence signals to guide behavioral choices.
techniques_used: ['RNAi knockdown (α2, α5, α1, Dlg)', 'T-maze behavioral assay', 'In vivo calcium imaging (GCaMP6f)', 'Brain explant preparation (optogenetics + focal ACh application)', 'Fluorescence Recovery After Photobleaching (FRAP)']
summary: Postsynaptic nAChR subunits α5 and α2 are required for induction and expression of appetitive memories in M4/6 MBONs, respectively, with plasticity traces induced independently of the presynapse.

paper_title: Postsynaptic plasticity of cholinergic synapses underlies the induction and expression of appetitive and familiarity memories in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Repeated odor exposure (familiarity learning paradigm)
neural_activity: Postsynaptic calcium transients depress following repeated odor application (not observed at presynaptic KC level). α2 nAChR subunit dynamics increase following odor training via FRAP.
behavioral_consequence: RNAi knockdown of α5 or α2 nAChR subunits impairs familiarity learning. α5 knockdown flies fail to stop grooming to novel odor (act as if odor is familiar). α2 knockdown delays learning (decreased efficacy).
ecological_context: Non-associative familiarity learning (habituation) allows adaptation to new odors and distinguishes familiar from novel stimuli.
techniques_used: ['RNAi knockdown (α2, α5)', 'Grooming behavior assay (dust removal)', 'In vivo calcium imaging (GCaMP6f)', 'Fluorescence Recovery After Photobleaching (FRAP)']
summary: Postsynaptic plasticity at α'3 MBONs, dependent on α5 and α2 nAChR subunits, underlies non-associative familiarity learning and odor habituation.

paper_title: Postsynaptic plasticity of cholinergic synapses underlies the induction and expression of appetitive and familiarity memories in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Red light pulses (optogenetic activation)
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation triggers calcium transients. Pairing activation with acetylcholine application induces postsynaptic plasticity in MBONs.
behavioral_consequence: Induction of persistent postsynaptic plasticity trace (increased calcium response to acetylcholine) in M6 MBONs in explant preparation, bypassing KC input.
ecological_context: Convey information on reward (sugar) or relative valence of stimuli to drive memory formation.
summary: DAN activation paired with postsynaptic activation induces persistent postsynaptic plasticity traces in MBONs independent of KC presynaptic output.

paper_title: Circadian clock disruption promotes the degeneration of dopaminergic neurons
experimental_stimulus: Oxidative stress (10% H2O2 for 4 hours) applied at different circadian timepoints (ZT8 vs ZT20).
neural_activity: Show rhythmic vulnerability to cell death (maximal loss at ZT20 in LD, CT4 in DD). Exhibit bimodal intracellular Ca2+ rhythms (peaks at CT5 and CT17) in wild-type flies; pattern disrupted in per0 mutants.
behavioral_consequence: Neurodegeneration leads to increased total sleep duration, specifically during the night. Loss does not affect startle-induced climbing behavior.
ecological_context: Regulates sleep-wake cycles; degeneration mimics Parkinson's disease non-motor symptoms related to sleep disturbance.
techniques_used: ['Split-GAL4 labeling (MB299B)', 'In vivo Ca2+ imaging (GCaMP7s)', 'Immunohistochemistry (anti-TH)', 'Sleep Assay (Drosophila Activity Monitoring System)', 'Startle-induced negative geotaxis assay']
summary: PAM-α1 neurons show circadian rhythmicity in vulnerability to oxidative stress and Ca2+ activity. Their degeneration increases nighttime sleep but does not impair climbing ability.

paper_title: Circadian clock disruption promotes the degeneration of dopaminergic neurons
experimental_stimulus: Genetic manipulation of circadian clock genes (per null mutants, genetic rescue) and oxidative stress (H2O2) on the animal.
neural_activity: Identified as presynaptic to PAM neurons via trans-Tango tracing and connectome analysis. PER expression in these neurons modulates PAM neuron survival.
behavioral_consequence: PER expression in circadian neurons restores PAM neuron survival, indirectly preventing sleep changes associated with PAM loss and premature death in per0 mutants.
ecological_context: Controls circadian timing of vulnerability to stress and regulates sleep-wake cycles.
techniques_used: ['trans-Tango (anterograde transsynaptic tracing)', 'Connectome data analysis (hemibrain/NeuPrint)', 'Genetic Rescue (UAS-per + DvPdf-GAL4/Pdf-GAL4)', 'Immunohistochemistry']
summary: LNds are directly presynaptic to vulnerable PAM neurons. PER expression in circadian pacemaker neurons is required to protect PAM neurons from oxidative stress-induced degeneration.

paper_title: Circadian clock disruption promotes the degeneration of dopaminergic neurons
cell_type: PDF-positive Lateral Ventral Neurons (s- and l-LNvs)
experimental_stimulus: Genetic rescue of per using Pdf-GAL4 in per0 mutants. Oxidative stress (H2O2) challenge.
neural_activity: PER expression in these neurons is sufficient to prevent age-dependent and oxidative stress-induced PAM neuron loss.
behavioral_consequence: Rescue of PER in PDF-positive LNvs restores PAM neuron survival and prevents premature death when combined with oxidative stress.
ecological_context: Synchronizes rhythmicity across the pacemaker circuit and regulates daily physiological robustness.
summary: PER expression within PDF-positive LNvs is sufficient to protect PAM neurons from oxidative stress and age-dependent degeneration.

paper_title: Nonsynaptic Transmission Mediates Light Context-Dependent Odor Responses in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: cVA odor (3s air puff); Blue light (470-495 nm, 1.5s transient or constant)
neural_activity: Firing rate decreased at light onset, increased during sustained light, and increased at light offset; no change in constant light/dark conditions
ecological_context: Discussion suggests mechanism may help flies switch attention to visual cues (light on) or olfactory cues (light off)
techniques_used: ['Single-sensillum extracellular recording', 'Genetic mutants (eya2, norpA36, hdcJK910)', 'Eye transplantation', 'Current injection into optic lobe']
summary: Or67d OSNs show transient changes in firing rate during odor stimulation when light conditions change, mediated by ephaptic transmission from retinal photoreceptor cells.

paper_title: Nonsynaptic Transmission Mediates Light Context-Dependent Odor Responses in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Blue light stimulation
neural_activity: Evokes extracellular field potential changes that mediate ephaptic transmission to distant OSNs; synaptic transmission not required
ecological_context: Enables context-dependent modulation of olfactory sensitivity (multimodal sensory integration)
techniques_used: ['Genetic mutants (eya2, norpA36, hdcJK910, longGMR-GAL4/UAS-TNT)', 'Eye transplantation', 'Current injection into optic lobe']
summary: Retinal photoreceptor cells mediate light-evoked firing rate changes in distant OSNs via ephaptic transmission independent of synaptic relay, reaching distances over 200 mm.

paper_title: Visual and motor signatures of locomotion dynamically shape a population code for visual features in Drosophila
cell_type: Projection neurons (subset tuned to small objects)
experimental_stimulus: Local visual features, small objects, self-movement signals (motor-related and visual motion), locomotion (saccades and intersaccadic intervals)
neural_activity: Visually evoked activity jointly represents local features across the population; shared gain factor improves trial-to-trial coding fidelity; subset tuned to small objects modulated by motor-related and visual motion signals; sensitivity adjusted across locomotor cycle with gain reduced during saccades and restored during intersaccadic intervals
ecological_context: Natural vision during locomotion to extract local features from input dominated by self-generated signals
summary: Projection neurons jointly represent local visual features with a shared gain factor improving fidelity; a subset tuned to small objects is modulated by motor and motion signals, reducing gain during saccades to ensure reliable feature detection during locomotion.

paper_title: Generating parallel representations of position and identity in the olfactory system
experimental_stimulus: Male fly at distances from 5 mm to 0.25 mm; male fly presented ipsilaterally or contralaterally; male fly approach at varying speeds
neural_activity: Respond reliably at 5 mm distance with widest dynamic range; responses increase as distance decreases (sigmoidal tuning); faster rise-time with higher speed; stronger ipsilateral activation
behavioral_consequence: Not directly manipulated for behavior in this paper; activity correlates with male distance and contributes to orienting behavior
ecological_context: cVA on a male fly can only be detected when flies are less than 5 mm apart; social interactions occur in low light where visual distinction is difficult
techniques_used: ['In vivo two-photon calcium imaging', 'Behavioral assays (turning behavior, opposite-sex preference)']
summary: Or67d ORNs detect cVA from male flies within a 5 mm range, showing distance-dependent and side-biased responses that inform spatial orientation.

paper_title: Generating parallel representations of position and identity in the olfactory system
experimental_stimulus: Male fly at distances from 5 mm to 0.25 mm; male fly presented ipsilaterally or contralaterally; male fly approach at varying speeds
neural_activity: Sigmoidal distance tuning plateauing at 1 mm; adapt strongly during 10s stimulus; stronger response to ipsilateral stimulus; bilateral contrast increased by contralateral inhibition
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation in males decreased courtship and copulation rate with females; reduced male-male courtship; no effect on female receptivity
ecological_context: cVA suppresses male courtship of other males; processing contributes to spatial orientation near a target male
techniques_used: ['In vivo two-photon calcium imaging', 'Optogenetics (CsChrimson)', 'Genetic ablation (Hid/Reaper)', 'Behavioral assays (courtship, aggression)']
summary: DA1 lPNs signal male distance with adaptation, suppress male-male courtship via activation, and show enhanced bilateral contrast for position coding.

paper_title: Generating parallel representations of position and identity in the olfactory system
experimental_stimulus: Male fly at distances from 5 mm to 0.25 mm; male fly presented ipsilaterally or contralaterally
neural_activity: Weak mid-range response, sharp increase below 2 mm; no adaptation during 10s stimulus; stronger response to ipsilateral stimulus
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation in females increased copulation rate (receptivity); activation in males increased male-male aggression; ablation reduced mating success in females
ecological_context: cVA promotes female receptivity and male aggression; relays cVA information to pC1 central integrators
techniques_used: ['In vivo two-photon calcium imaging', 'Optogenetics (CsChrimson)', 'Genetic ablation (Hid/Reaper)', 'Behavioral assays (courtship, aggression)']
summary: DA1 lvPNs signal male proximity without adaptation and promote female receptivity and male aggression upon activation.

paper_title: Generating parallel representations of position and identity in the olfactory system
experimental_stimulus: Male fly presented contralaterally or ipsilaterally
neural_activity: Contralateral stimulation evokes larger responses; specifically activates DA1 glomerulus; response abolished by contralateral antenna block
behavioral_consequence: Chemogenetic block (Ort + Histamine) reduced bilateral contrast in lPN responses
ecological_context: Enhances bilateral contrast in cVA sensing to signal male angular position
techniques_used: ['In vivo two-photon calcium imaging', 'Chemogenetic block (Ort + Histamine)', 'Connectomics (EM)']
summary: il3LN6 provides contralateral inhibition to lPNs, increasing bilateral contrast for precise position encoding of male flies.

paper_title: Generating parallel representations of position and identity in the olfactory system
experimental_stimulus: Male fly approaching at different speeds (1.41, 4.30, 8.04 mm/s)
neural_activity: Phasic ON-response selective to onset; response rise-time and peak depend on male approach speed; correlates with differential of lPN response
behavioral_consequence: Constant silencing reduced female receptivity; optogenetic activation had no effect on receptivity
ecological_context: Detects male approach speed; required for normal courtship behavior
techniques_used: ['In vivo two-photon calcium imaging', 'Genetic silencing (Kir2.1)', 'Optogenetics', 'Behavioral assays (courtship)']
summary: AV2a2 neurons encode male approach speed via phasic responses and are required for normal female receptivity.

paper_title: Generating parallel representations of position and identity in the olfactory system
experimental_stimulus: Male fly
neural_activity: Sustained and slowly decaying (tonic) response to male presence; responds to lvPN optogenetic activation
behavioral_consequence: Genetic ablation suppressed female receptivity
ecological_context: Controls female mating decisions and receptivity
techniques_used: ['In vivo two-photon calcium imaging', 'Optogenetics (lvPN activation)', 'Genetic ablation (Hid/Reaper)', 'Behavioral assays (courtship)']
summary: pC1 neurons respond tonically to male presence and are necessary for female receptivity, receiving input from lvPNs.

paper_title: Generating parallel representations of position and identity in the olfactory system
experimental_stimulus: Male fly; male genitals (taste); cVA olfactory stimulus
neural_activity: Phasic ON-response to male (habituates); integrates cVA (lPN) and gustatory inputs (Ir94e)
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation increased female receptivity and female-female aggression; blocking decreased receptivity; activation partially rescued receptivity in pC1-ablated females
ecological_context: Multimodal integration of cVA and taste cues promotes female receptivity and aggression; controls binary decision to accept a mate
techniques_used: ['In vivo two-photon calcium imaging', 'Optogenetics (activation/blocking)', 'Genetic ablation (pC1)', 'Behavioral assays (courtship, aggression)']
summary: aSP-g neurons integrate cVA and taste cues to bidirectionally regulate female receptivity and aggression, operating in parallel with pC1.

paper_title: Generating parallel representations of position and identity in the olfactory system
experimental_stimulus: Labellar stimulation with water, NaCl, male genitals, female genitals, male dorsal cuticle
neural_activity: Strongest response to compounds on male genitals; water and NaCl also activate
behavioral_consequence: Activation alone no effect on receptivity; simultaneous activation with cVA (lPN) increased female receptivity
ecological_context: Contact pheromones on male genitals contribute to female receptivity when combined with cVA
summary: Ir94e GRNs respond to male genital compounds, and their activation combined with cVA promotes female receptivity via aSP-g integration.

paper_title: Lateral Axonal Modulation is Required for Stimulus-Specific Olfactory Conditioning in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Odors (4-methylcyclohexanol/MCH, 3-octanol/OCT), electric shock (aversive conditioning), optogenetic activation (CsChrimson), dopamine application
neural_activity: mAChR-B knockdown increased odor-evoked Ca2+ responses in axons (2-photon imaging). mAChR-B overexpression decreased odor-evoked Ca2+ responses. mAChR-B activation suppressed dopamine-induced cAMP elevation (cAMPr imaging). Optogenetic activation of a KC subpopulation suppressed odor responses in neighboring KCs (lateral suppression). Connectome analysis showed $\gamma$ KCs have the most robust axo-axonic connections (average 190 other KCs).
behavioral_consequence: mAChR-B knockdown impaired short-term aversive olfactory learning. Specifically caused unspecific conditioning: flies showed increased aversion to an untrained odor (OCT) after training with MCH, whereas controls showed increased attraction to the untrained odor. No change in shock sensitivity or naive odor preference.
ecological_context: Effective and stimulus-specific learning is essential for animals' survival. An animal's survival critically depends on its capacity for sensory discrimination. Unspecific learning is described as detrimental.
summary: $\gamma$ Kenyon Cells express mAChR-B in their axons, which mediates lateral inhibition to suppress odor-evoked Ca2+ and dopamine-induced cAMP. Knockdown of mAChR-B in $\gamma$ KCs impairs olfactory learning specificity, causing unspecific conditioning to untrained odors.

paper_title: Lateral Axonal Modulation is Required for Stimulus-Specific Olfactory Conditioning in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Odors (MCH, OCT), mAChR-B knockdown/overexpression
neural_activity: mAChR-B is expressed in axons. mAChR-B knockdown increased odor-evoked Ca2+ responses in axons. No effect on Ca2+ responses in dendrites.
behavioral_consequence: mAChR-B knockdown in $\alpha/\beta$ KCs did not impair aversive olfactory conditioning learning scores.
ecological_context: Blank
summary: $\alpha/\beta$ Kenyon Cells express mAChR-B in axons and show increased Ca2+ responses upon knockdown, but mAChR-B is not required for aversive learning in this subtype.

paper_title: Lateral Axonal Modulation is Required for Stimulus-Specific Olfactory Conditioning in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: mAChR-B knockdown
neural_activity: mAChR-B expression is weak or not detected.
behavioral_consequence: mAChR-B knockdown in $\alpha'/\beta'$ KCs did not impair aversive olfactory conditioning learning scores.
ecological_context: Blank
summary: $\alpha'/\beta'$ Kenyon Cells show weak mAChR-B expression and mAChR-B knockdown does not affect learning, distinguishing them from $\gamma$ KCs in this function.

paper_title: Interneurons of fan-shaped body promote arousal in Drosophila
cell_type: FB interneurons (NP2320-Gal4 labeled; vDeltaB, C, D and hDeltaC subtypes)
experimental_stimulus: Thermo-genetic activation using dTrpA1 at 29°C vs 22°C; ATP application to upstream R52B10 neurons (ex vivo)
neural_activity: Activation of dTrpA1 at 29°C; Ca2+ imaging showed substantial increase in GCaMP signals when R52B10 neurons were activated by ATP (indicating excitatory input). Confirmed as cholinergic by suppression with Cha-Gal80.
behavioral_consequence: Significant decrease in sleep time (promoted arousal) when activated at 29°C; phenotype suppressed by Cha-Gal80.
ecological_context: Regulation of arousal state and sleep-wake cycle.
summary: Cholinergic FB interneurons promote arousal when activated; they receive excitatory signals from R52B10 neurons.

paper_title: Interneurons of fan-shaped body promote arousal in Drosophila
cell_type: R52B10 neurons (PB neurons, output of PB-FB pathway)
experimental_stimulus: ATP application via P2X2 channel (ex vivo)
neural_activity: Activation led to a substantial increase in GCaMP signals in FB interneurons, indicating a functional excitatory connection.
summary: R52B10 neurons form an excitatory connection with FB interneurons.

paper_title: The Neuronal Circuit of the Dorsal Circadian Clock Neurons in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Not tested
neural_activity: Not tested
behavioral_consequence: Not tested
ecological_context: Circadian clock neurons fine-tune activity under light and temperature cycles to anticipate 24-h rhythms.
summary: Two neurons located in the anterior superior cell body rind dorsal to the superior lateral protocerebrum. Projections extend to the superior lateral protocerebrum, lateral horn, accessory medulla, and mushroom body calyx. Hemibrain data indicates strong input from antennal lobe temperature sensing neurons. Trans-Tango and hemibrain connectivity reveal output to LNd, 5th LN, s-LNvs, and dorsal fan-shaped body neurons.

paper_title: The Neuronal Circuit of the Dorsal Circadian Clock Neurons in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Not tested
neural_activity: Not tested
behavioral_consequence: Not tested
ecological_context: Dorsal neurons play essential roles in linking output from lateral neurons to downstream circuits controlling sleep and metabolism.
summary: Heterogeneous group of ~15 neurons per hemisphere. Identified subtypes include Type A (project contralaterally via middle dorsal commissure) and Type B (project ipsilaterally to anterior optic tubercle). Type A DN1ps show output to ITP-positive LNd and 5th LN. Type B DN1ps signal to tubercle bulb neurons and DN2s. Input from LNd and 5th LN observed in Type A.

paper_title: The Neuronal Circuit of the Dorsal Circadian Clock Neurons in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Not tested
neural_activity: Not tested
behavioral_consequence: Not tested
ecological_context: Important for temperature entrainment in larvae and temperature preference in adults.
summary: Two CRY-negative neurons with somata near dorsal terminals of s-LNvs. Projections bifurcate to contralateral hemisphere (middle dorsal commissure) and loop around superior lateral protocerebrum toward anterior optic tubercle without reaching it. Strong input from ipsilateral superior lateral protocerebrum (DN1ps). Trans-Tango identifies output to mushroom bodies and pars intercerebralis neurosecretory cells.

paper_title: The Neuronal Circuit of the Dorsal Circadian Clock Neurons in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Not tested
neural_activity: Not tested
behavioral_consequence: Not tested
ecological_context: Involved in light perception and sensory integration of temperature cues into the circadian system.
summary: Largest cluster (~40 neurons), distinguishable by large and small somata. Large DN3s project to anterior optic tubercle and accessory medulla. Small DN3s terminate in posterior lateral protocerebrum hub. Projections overlap with all other clock neurons in superior lateral protocerebrum, superior medial protocerebrum, and posterior lateral protocerebrum. Hemibrain data identifies three morphological subtypes.

paper_title: Sensorimotor transformation underlying odor-modulated locomotion in walking Drosophila
cell_type: Olfactory Receptor Neurons (ORNs) - Or42b, Or92a, Ir64a, Ir75a, Or42a, Orco, Ir8a
experimental_stimulus: Red light (617 nm) optogenetic activation via CsChrimson in a circular arena with a central light-zone; flies walked in darkness or light conditions
neural_activity: Firing rate (f) and change in firing rate (df) measured via single-sensillum recording; responses shaped by locomotion dynamics (entry/exit from light zone); two-stage linear filter cascade used to model stimulus-response relationship
behavioral_consequence: Activation modulates locomotor parameters (speed, curvature, turn duration, turn optimality) distinctively based on ORN class combination and sensory context (f, df); attraction (time in light zone) increases with number of ORN classes activated; locomotor strategy adapts over time (short-term memory via f, df and longer timescale changes); flies turn preferentially at border (optimal turns) to stay near or return to stimulated region
ecological_context: Odor-guided locomotion to locate ecologically important resources (food, mates) in environments with incomplete information (turbulent flow, dissipating gradients) requiring exploration and search behavior
summary: ORNs activated by red light optogenetics show firing rate and rate of change dependent on locomotion. Activation modulates locomotor parameters (speed, curvature, turns) distinctively based on ORN class combination and sensory context. Attraction increases with number of classes. Flies adapt behavior over time and exhibit turn optimality at borders.

paper_title: A neural circuit linking learning and sleep in Drosophila long-term memory
experimental_stimulus: Courtship learning with mated females (1, 2, 4, 6 hours); optogenetic activation
neural_activity: Increased luminescence (Lola reporter) and Ca++ (GCaMP7b) activity in TP1-3 (1-3h post-learning) after 4h and 6h training; no increase after 1h or 2h training; optogenetic activation induces sleep
behavioral_consequence: Silencing blocks post-learning sleep enhancement and impairs LTM (courtship suppression) after 6h training; normal STM after 1h training; activation promotes sleep
ecological_context: Facilitates consolidation of courtship long-term memory (LTM) after prolonged futile courtship experience with unreceptive mated females
techniques_used: ['luminescence assay (Lola)', 'two-photon Ca++ imaging (GCaMP7b)', 'optogenetics (Chrimson)', 'neuronal silencing (Shits)', 'SleepTracker (video tracking)', 'courtship assay']
summary: vFBs are selectively activated by prolonged courtship learning experiences (4-6h), promoting post-learning sleep and essential for LTM consolidation, but not required for short-term memory.

paper_title: A neural circuit linking learning and sleep in Drosophila long-term memory
experimental_stimulus: Courtship learning (1-6h); optogenetic activation
neural_activity: Activity increases linearly with learning duration (1-6h) measured by luminescence (Lola); optogenetic activation elicits excitatory Ca++ response in vFBs
behavioral_consequence: Silencing blocks post-learning sleep enhancement and LTM formation after 6h training; optogenetic activation acutely induces sleep
ecological_context: Encodes the amount/duration of learning experience to promote sleep and memory consolidation only after prolonged courtship
summary: MBONs-γ2α’1 activity reflects the length of courtship learning and provides excitatory input to vFBs to promote sleep and LTM after prolonged training.

paper_title: A neural circuit linking learning and sleep in Drosophila long-term memory
experimental_stimulus: Courtship learning (1-6h); optogenetic activation/silencing
neural_activity: Activity peaks after 2h training and decreases after 4-6h training (luminescence); optogenetic activation elicits inhibitory Ca++ response in vFBs
behavioral_consequence: Silencing after 2h training induces post-learning sleep and LTM (normally STM); optogenetic activation after 6h training suppresses sleep and impairs LTM
ecological_context: Suppresses sleep after short learning experiences (STM) to ensure only significant learning experiences induce sleep and consolidation
summary: MBONs-β’2mp activity peaks after short learning and inhibits vFBs, preventing sleep and LTM consolidation for short experiences, but releases inhibition after prolonged learning.

paper_title: A neural circuit linking learning and sleep in Drosophila long-term memory
experimental_stimulus: Courtship learning (1-6h); optogenetic activation of inputs or SFSs
neural_activity: Excited by MBONs-γ2α’1 activation; inhibited by MBONs-β’2mp activation; activated after 4 and 6h training (Ca++ imaging); optogenetic activation excites vFBs
behavioral_consequence: Silencing blocks post-learning sleep enhancement and LTM after prolonged training
ecological_context: Integrates opposing MB outputs to gate post-learning sleep for LTM consolidation based on learning intensity
summary: SFS neurons integrate excitatory input from MBONs-γ2α’1 and inhibitory input from MBONs-β’2mp to selectively activate vFBs for post-learning sleep after prolonged learning.

paper_title: A neural circuit linking learning and sleep in Drosophila long-term memory
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation
neural_activity: Enhances sleep upon light stimulus; no immediate connection or response in vFBs upon activation (Ca++ imaging)
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation acutely induces sleep
ecological_context: Sleep-promoting MBON class, but not involved in the learning-duration specific circuit described
summary: MBONs-calyx neurons promote sleep upon optogenetic activation but do not connect to vFBs and are not part of the learning-duration gated circuit.

paper_title: A neural circuit linking learning and sleep in Drosophila long-term memory
experimental_stimulus: Courtship learning (6h)
neural_activity: No increased activity after courtship training (luminescence); no response to MBONs-γ2α’1 activation (Ca++ imaging)
behavioral_consequence: No role in courtship LTM or post-learning sleep enhancement
ecological_context: Involved in sleep homeostasis, distinct from learning-induced sleep
summary: dFBs are not activated by courtship learning and are not involved in post-learning sleep or courtship LTM, serving homeostatic sleep regulation instead.

paper_title: A neural correlate of individual odor preference in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: 12-odor panel plus air, specifically 3-octanol (OCT) vs air and OCT vs 4-methylcyclohexanol (MCH) assays
neural_activity: Calcium dynamics (GCaMP6m) measured via two-photon imaging; presynaptic density (Brp-Short) measured via immunohistochemistry and expansion microscopy
behavioral_consequence: ORN calcium PC1 predicts individual odor-vs-air preference (explaining ~23% variance). ORN calcium responses do not predict odor-vs-odor preference. ORN presynaptic density variation (specifically DM2 vs DC2) predicts individual odor-vs-odor preference
ecological_context: Discussion suggests behavioral variation may support a bet-hedging strategy to counter environmental fluctuations
techniques_used: ['two-photon calcium imaging', 'behavioral tunnel assay', 'immunohistochemistry', 'confocal microscopy', 'expansion microscopy']
summary: ORN calcium dynamics predict odor-vs-air preference, while ORN presynaptic density variation predicts odor-vs-odor preference.

paper_title: A neural correlate of individual odor preference in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: 12-odor panel plus air, specifically 3-octanol (OCT) vs air and OCT vs 4-methylcyclohexanol (MCH) assays
neural_activity: Calcium dynamics (GCaMP6m) in antennal lobe glomeruli measured via two-photon imaging
behavioral_consequence: PN calcium PC1 predicts individual odor-vs-air preference. PN calcium PC2 (DM2 vs DC2 contrast) predicts individual odor-vs-odor preference (explaining ~26% variance in test set)
ecological_context: Discussion suggests behavioral variation may support a bet-hedging strategy to counter environmental fluctuations
summary: PN calcium responses predict both odor-vs-air and odor-vs-odor preferences, with DM2 vs DC2 contrast specific to odor-vs-odor choice.

paper_title: Neural mechanisms to exploit positional geometry for collision avoidance
experimental_stimulus: Back-to-front and front-to-back moving objects (parallel/approach stimuli), translational gratings
neural_activity: Synaptic silencing reduced slowing; optogenetic activation resulted in large LPLC1 calcium transients
behavioral_consequence: Silencing significantly reduced slowing in response to back-to-front stimuli and abolished direction selectivity in the behavior
ecological_context: Motion detection required for direction selective collision avoidance behavior during walking
summary: T4 and T5 neurons are necessary for direction selective collision avoidance slowing; their activity drives LPLC1 calcium responses.

paper_title: Neural mechanisms to exploit positional geometry for collision avoidance
experimental_stimulus: Back-to-front and front-to-back moving objects, looming, rotating backgrounds, size and azimuth sweeps
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging showed direction selectivity for back-to-front motion, size tuning (peak 10 deg height), and spatial bias in frontolateral field; iGluSnFR imaging revealed glutamatergic inputs
behavioral_consequence: Synaptic silencing reduced slowing to back-to-front stimuli; optogenetic activation caused duration-dependent slowing in blind flies
ecological_context: Collision avoidance exploiting positional geometry; frontolateral visual field is where back-to-front motion predicts near collision
summary: LPLC1 neurons are necessary and sufficient for collision avoidance slowing; they pool motion and object inputs and exhibit spatially biased direction selectivity.

paper_title: Neural mechanisms to exploit positional geometry for collision avoidance
experimental_stimulus: Horizontally translating objects of varying heights
neural_activity: Functional silencing tested; complementary vertical size tuning compared to LPLC1
behavioral_consequence: Silencing reduced slowing caused by tall objects moving back-to-front
ecological_context: Complementary tuning to LPLC1 for object height in collision avoidance behavior
summary: LPC1 neurons contribute to slowing behavior for tall objects, showing complementary size tuning to LPLC1.

paper_title: Neural mechanisms to exploit positional geometry for collision avoidance
experimental_stimulus: Moving bars, squares, expanding discs (imaging); parallel stimuli (behavior)
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging showed response patterns matching LPLC1 axon terminals (moving bars, squares, expanding discs)
behavioral_consequence: Silencing had no significant change in slowing; optogenetic activation caused slowing
ecological_context: Central brain pathway mediating collision avoidance
summary: PLP219 is sufficient to trigger slowing and inherits LPLC1 response properties, but is not necessary.

paper_title: Neural mechanisms to exploit positional geometry for collision avoidance
experimental_stimulus: Parallel stimuli (behavior); moving bars, squares, looming (imaging for PVLP112/113)
neural_activity: PVLP112/113 responded to broader set of stimuli including drifting gratings (calcium imaging)
behavioral_consequence: Silencing resulted in no significant change in slowing; optogenetic activation did not cause slowing
ecological_context: Downstream targets of LPLC1 not required for collision avoidance slowing
summary: These downstream neurons are not necessary for slowing; their activation is insufficient to trigger the behavior.

paper_title: Neural mechanisms to exploit positional geometry for collision avoidance
experimental_stimulus: Connectomic identification only (no direct behavioral test in this paper)
neural_activity: Connectomic analysis identified them as putative excitatory inputs to LPLC1
behavioral_consequence: Inferred to drive LPLC1 object-selective responses
ecological_context: Object detection inputs contributing to collision avoidance circuitry
summary: T2 and T3 neurons identified via connectomics as providing excitatory inputs to LPLC1.

paper_title: Drosulfakinin signaling modulates female sexual receptivity in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: neuronal activity manipulation (Dsk and DSK neuronal activity)
neural_activity: receive input signal from R71G01GAL4 neurons
behavioral_consequence: promote female sexual behavior; impact female sexual receptivity
ecological_context: female sexual behavior is of prominent biological importance for survival and reproduction
summary: Dsk-expressing neurons promote female sexual behavior and receive input from R71G01GAL4 neurons; manipulation of their activity impacts female sexual receptivity.

paper_title: Drosulfakinin signaling modulates female sexual receptivity in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: neuronal activity manipulation (intersectional technique)
neural_activity: modulate female sexual behavior by acting on receptor CCKLR-17D3
behavioral_consequence: regulation of female sexual behavior relies mainly on these neurons (rather than lateral DSK neurons)
ecological_context: female sexual behavior is of prominent biological importance for survival and reproduction
summary: Medial DSK neurons are the primary regulators of female sexual behavior, modulating it via the receptor CCKLR-17D3.

paper_title: Drosulfakinin signaling modulates female sexual receptivity in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: neuronal activity manipulation (intersectional technique)
neural_activity: compared to medial DSK neurons
behavioral_consequence: regulation of female sexual behavior relies mainly on medial rather than lateral DSK neurons
ecological_context: female sexual behavior is of prominent biological importance for survival and reproduction
summary: Lateral DSK neurons show less reliance in regulating female sexual behavior compared to medial DSK neurons.

paper_title: Drosulfakinin signaling modulates female sexual receptivity in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: neuronal activity manipulation (intersectional technique)
neural_activity: provide input signal to Dsk-expressing neurons
behavioral_consequence: promote female sexual receptivity downstream of DSK signaling
ecological_context: female sexual behavior is of prominent biological importance for survival and reproduction
summary: R71G01GAL4 neurons provide input signals to Dsk-expressing neurons to promote female sexual receptivity.

paper_title: Flexible Circuit Mechanisms for Context-Dependent Song Sequencing
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (csChrimson) at varying irradiance and duty cycle; patch-clamp current injection
neural_activity: Activation drives pulse song; strong activation produces 'rebound' sine song after offset. Patch-clamp shows spike-frequency adaptation.
behavioral_consequence: In solitary males: low activation drives pulse song, strong activation drives pulse + rebound sine. With female: drives long complex song bouts. Headless males: still produces rebound song. Knockdown (Kir2.1/TNT): reduces simple pulse bouts; TNT increases sine-leading bouts.
ecological_context: Drives simple song far from female and complex song near female. Context-dependent song patterning is linked to proximity to female for mating.
summary: pIP10 descending neurons drive pulse song via optogenetic activation, with strong stimulation enabling rebound sine song. Female presence or co-activation with P1a promotes complex song bouts, and pIP10 activity is required for leading pulse song in natural courtship.

paper_title: Flexible Circuit Mechanisms for Context-Dependent Song Sequencing
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (csChrimson); Optogenetic activation of pIP10 during recording
neural_activity: Calcium imaging (GCaMP6s) shows correlated activity (pulse) and anti-correlated activity (sine/rebound) with pIP10 activation. Subtypes TN1A drive sine song.
behavioral_consequence: Activation in solitary males drives sine song (with some pulse). With female, drives long complex bouts. Knockdown of Ih or Rdl reduces song complexity.
ecological_context: VNC neurons involved in generating complex song (pulse-sine alternations) when near female. Rebound circuitry in VNC enables sine production without direct excitation.
summary: TN1 VNC neurons exhibit mutual inhibition and rebound excitability. Their activity correlates with song mode (pulse/sine) and is required for complex song complexity in the presence of sensory cues.

paper_title: Flexible Circuit Mechanisms for Context-Dependent Song Sequencing
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (csChrimson); Tactile tapping during courtship
neural_activity: Calcium imaging shows activation of downstream GABAergic 'F1 follower' neurons. Tap rate correlates with activity estimates.
