ecological_context: Flies derived from a wild orchard population in Winters, CA; fecundity variation is heritable and largely independent of other life history traits; QTL effect on fecundity greater in flies reared in 0.6% yeast vs 0.2% yeast
techniques_used: ['Immunohistochemistry', 'Quantitative PCR (qPCR)', 'RNA interference (RNAi)', 'Quantitative Trait Locus (QTL) mapping', 'Deficiency mapping']
summary: Corazonin-producing neurons in the dorsolateral protocerebrum express the aquaporin Drip. Natural variation in Drip mRNA levels in the head correlates with fecundity. Knockdown of Drip in these neurons using Crz-Gal4 reduces female fecundity by approximately 20%.

paper_title: The Dopaminergic System in the Aging Brain of Drosophila
cell_type: Dopaminergic (DA) Neuron Clusters (PPL1, PPL2, PPM1/2, PPM3, PAL)
experimental_stimulus: Aging (Day 2 to Day 70); Startle (tap to vial bottom); Open-field arena (undisturbed walking)
neural_activity: Cell numbers quantified via confocal microscopy and anti-TH labeling (no age-related degeneration found); Projection patterns mapped via MARCM clones (PPL1 to mushroom bodies, PPM3 to central complex); No electrophysiological firing activity measured.
behavioral_consequence: Startle-induced locomotion and negative geotaxis decline significantly with age; Walking activity and exploration behavior increase at late stages of life (Day 60+); TH-specific Dcr2 expression reduces walking activity without affecting exploration or centrophobism.
techniques_used: ['Confocal microscopy', 'MARCM (Mosaic Analysis with a Repressible Cell Marker)', 'Immunohistology (anti-TH, anti-bruchpilot)', 'Startle-induced negative geotaxis assay', 'Video-assisted movement tracking']
summary: Dopaminergic neuron clusters (PPL1, PPM3, etc.) project to distinct brain regions (mushroom bodies, central complex) but do not degenerate with age in wildtype flies. Startle-induced climbing declines with age while walking activity increases in late life. TH-specific Dcr2 expression selectively reduces walking activity, suggesting the siRNA pathway modulates distinct DA behaviors.

paper_title: A modular circuit coordinates the diversification of courtship strategies
experimental_stimulus: Contact/taste of conspecific and heterospecific female targets; perfumed females (7-T, 7,11-HD)
neural_activity: Robust calcium responses (GCaMP6s) in foreleg afferents; broadly tuned to all targets in D. yakuba (conspecific and heterospecific)
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation triggers courtship of otherwise unattractive/heterospecific targets; ppk23 mutants lose aversion to heterospecific females and cannot sustain courtship in the dark
ecological_context: Mediates chemosensory detection for mate recognition, enabling courtship in the absence of visual feedback
techniques_used: ['Functional calcium imaging', 'Optogenetics', 'CRISPR mutant analysis', 'Behavioral courtship assays']
summary: Ppk23+ sensory neurons detect pheromones in the foreleg; in D. yakuba they broadly respond to targets, and optogenetic activation drives courtship of unattractive females.

paper_title: A modular circuit coordinates the diversification of courtship strategies
experimental_stimulus: 7-tricosene (7-T) pheromone
neural_activity: Selective calcium activation in foreleg afferents by 7-T in D. yakuba; responses attenuated in ppk25 mutants
behavioral_consequence: ppk25 mutants show diminished courtship towards conspecifics in the dark; retain aversion to D. melanogaster females
ecological_context: Enables D. yakuba males to use sexually monomorphic 7-T as an excitatory cue for courtship in the dark
techniques_used: ['Functional calcium imaging', 'CRISPR mutant analysis', 'Behavioral courtship assays']
summary: Ppk25+ neurons in D. yakuba are tuned to 7-T, allowing males to use this pheromone to promote courtship in darkness.

paper_title: A modular circuit coordinates the diversification of courtship strategies
experimental_stimulus: Consipic/heterospecific female touch/pheromones; Optogenetic activation
neural_activity: Calcium responses (GCaMP6s) in lateral protocerebral complex (LPC); activated by conspecific female taste in D. yakuba, D. melanogaster, D. erecta
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation triggers persistent courtship pursuit of unattractive/heterospecific females
ecological_context: Central node integrating sensory inputs to regulate courtship arousal and mate recognition across species
summary: P1 neurons integrate pheromone signals to drive courtship; activation is sufficient to trigger pursuit of unattractive females.

paper_title: A modular circuit coordinates the diversification of courtship strategies
experimental_stimulus: Conspecific female pheromones (7-T); Optogenetic activation; Silencing
neural_activity: Strongly activated by taste of conspecific females in D. yakuba via 7-T; unresponsive in D. simulans
behavioral_consequence: Activation triggers courtship/singing towards heterospecific females; constitutive silencing attenuates courtship in the dark
ecological_context: Integrates excitatory pheromone signals (7-T) to underlie distinct tuning for male arousal in D. yakuba
summary: Dsx∩P1 neurons are selectively excited by 7-T in D. yakuba and are required for sustaining courtship in the dark.

paper_title: A modular circuit coordinates the diversification of courtship strategies
experimental_stimulus: Conspecific/heterospecific female pheromones; Optogenetic activation; Silencing
neural_activity: Largely unresponsive to pheromones in D. yakuba; in D. melanogaster, excited by conspecific and D. erecta females
behavioral_consequence: Activation triggers courtship/singing towards heterospecific females; silencing has little impact on courtship dynamics in D. yakuba
ecological_context: Provides a modular unit for sensory integration, though less critical for 7-T mediated courtship in D. yakuba
summary: Fru∩P1 neurons trigger courtship upon activation but show distinct pheromone tuning and are less critical for dark courtship in D. yakuba compared to Dsx∩P1.

paper_title: A modular circuit coordinates the diversification of courtship strategies
experimental_stimulus: cis-vaccenyl acetate (cVA)
neural_activity: cVA perfuming suppresses P1 neuron responses
behavioral_consequence: cVA perfuming suppresses courtship pursuit towards females; Or67d mutants show no impact on male-male courtship in D. yakuba
ecological_context: Serves as a male-specific inhibitory cue to support sex discrimination
summary: Or67d+ neurons mediate detection of cVA, which suppresses courtship to aid sex discrimination in D. yakuba.

paper_title: Ascending neurons convey behavioral state to integrative sensory and action selection brain regions
experimental_stimulus: Spontaneous behavior (resting epochs)
neural_activity: Calcium activity (OpGCaMP6f) increases during resting epochs (two-photon imaging).
ecological_context: Conveys self-motion state (rest) to AVLP for contextualizing sensory cues (distinguishing exafference from reafference).
summary: Rest ANs encode resting behavior and project to the anterior ventrolateral protocerebrum (AVLP).

paper_title: Ascending neurons convey behavioral state to integrative sensory and action selection brain regions
experimental_stimulus: CO2 and air puffs
neural_activity: Calcium activity increases in response to gust-like puffs. Does not encode backward walking (two-photon imaging).
ecological_context: Conveys gust-like stimuli to AVLP for contextualizing sensory cues.
summary: Puff-responsive ANs encode gust-like stimuli (CO2/air) and project to the AVLP, similar to Rest ANs but with distinct encoding.

paper_title: Ascending neurons convey behavioral state to integrative sensory and action selection brain regions
experimental_stimulus: Spontaneous behavior (forward/backward walking)
neural_activity: Calcium activity correlates with timing and onset of walking epochs (two-photon imaging).
ecological_context: Conveys global locomotor signals to the gnathal ganglia (GNG) for action selection.
summary: Walk ANs encode general locomotor states (walking) and project bilaterally to the GNG.

paper_title: Ascending neurons convey behavioral state to integrative sensory and action selection brain regions
experimental_stimulus: Turning (spherical treadmill roll velocity)
neural_activity: Pairwise difference in activity correlates with turning direction (left vs right turns) (two-photon imaging).
ecological_context: Conveys specific locomotor dimensions to GNG/WED for steering control.
summary: Turn ANs encode turning direction and project unilaterally to the GNG and WED.

paper_title: Ascending neurons convey behavioral state to integrative sensory and action selection brain regions
experimental_stimulus: Foreleg-dependent behaviors (walking, pushing, grooming)
neural_activity: Calcium activity correlates with multiple foreleg-dependent behaviors (two-photon imaging).
ecological_context: Conveys foreleg state to multiple brain regions (GNG, AVLP) to ensure future behaviors are compatible with ongoing ones.
summary: Foreleg ANs encode general foreleg-dependent behaviors and project bilaterally to GNG, AVLP, and other regions.

paper_title: Ascending neurons convey behavioral state to integrative sensory and action selection brain regions
experimental_stimulus: Eye grooming
neural_activity: Calcium activity correlates with eye grooming epochs (two-photon imaging).
ecological_context: Conveys discrete action state to GNG to guide action selection.
summary: Eye Groom ANs encode specific eye grooming and project unilaterally to the GNG.

paper_title: Ascending neurons convey behavioral state to integrative sensory and action selection brain regions
experimental_stimulus: Proboscis Extension (PE) trains
neural_activity: Activity ramps up over PE trains, integrating number of PEs over >10s (two-photon imaging).
ecological_context: Conveys temporal integration of PE events to GNG, possibly associated with deep rest states.
summary: PE-ANs integrate the number of proboscis extensions over time and project to the GNG.

paper_title: Descending neurons coordinate anterior grooming behavior in Drosophila.
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (csChrimson), bilateral and unilateral activation
neural_activity: Not directly measured; activity driven by optogenetic stimulation
behavioral_consequence: Bilateral activation induces coupled alternation of head sweeps and front leg rubbing. Unilateral activation induces single-sided (ipsilateral) head sweeps.
ecological_context: Anterior grooming behavior to remove dust/debris from the head in natural conditions
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics', 'Fly-on-a-ball preparation', 'Automatic Behavior Recognition System (ABRS)', 'Genetic Reconstitution of Synaptic Protein (GRASP)']
summary: DNg12 neurons command coupled anterior grooming subroutines (head sweeps and leg rubs) when bilaterally activated, and can decouple leg movements for single-sided head sweeps when unilaterally activated.

paper_title: Descending neurons coordinate anterior grooming behavior in Drosophila.
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation and inactivation (csChrimson, GtAcr1), unilateral activation
neural_activity: Not directly measured; activity driven by optogenetic stimulation
behavioral_consequence: Activation induces isolated front leg rubbing. Unilateral activation recruits bilateral leg rubbing. Inactivation reduces but does not eliminate rubbing.
ecological_context: Anterior grooming behavior involving front leg rubbing to remove accumulated dust
summary: DNg11 neurons command front leg rubbing; unilateral activation results in bilateral leg coordination via commissural brain circuits.

paper_title: Descending neurons coordinate anterior grooming behavior in Drosophila.
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation, unilateral activation
neural_activity: Not directly measured; activity driven by optogenetic stimulation
behavioral_consequence: Activation induces head sweeps targeting the antenna, rarely followed by leg rubbing. Unilateral activation induces ipsilateral head sweeps.
ecological_context: Anterior grooming, specifically targeting antenna cleaning after dust exposure
summary: aDNs command antenna-specific head sweeps; unilateral activation produces single-sided sweeping movements.

paper_title: Descending neurons coordinate anterior grooming behavior in Drosophila.
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (csChrimson)
neural_activity: Glutamatergic (confirmed by co-labeling with glutamatergic reporter); identified as inhibitory via behavioral suppression
behavioral_consequence: Activation suppresses front leg rubbing in dusted flies but not head sweeps, arbitrating between grooming actions. Silencing has no significant effect.
ecological_context: Conflict resolution between head sweeps and leg rubbing during grooming
summary: JO-in neurons are inhibitory and suppress front leg rubbing to favor antenna grooming head sweeps.

paper_title: Descending neurons coordinate anterior grooming behavior in Drosophila.
cell_type: JOs (Johnston's Organ chordotonal neurons, JO-E subtype)
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (referenced in text regarding JO-in pathway)
neural_activity: Detect static deflection of the antenna
behavioral_consequence: Activation induces antennal grooming; connects to JO-in which inhibits DNg11
ecological_context: Detection of mechanical forces on the antenna during grooming initiation
summary: JO-E neurons detect mechanical forces on the antenna and contribute to antenna grooming circuits via JO-in inhibition of DNg11.

paper_title: Descending neurons coordinate anterior grooming behavior in Drosophila.
experimental_stimulus: None; identified via anatomical tracing
neural_activity: Not measured
behavioral_consequence: Identified as an indirect commissural circuit connecting left and right DNg11 neurons
ecological_context: Coordination of bilateral leg movements during front leg rubbing
summary: LNR1 and LNR2 form an indirect brain circuit linking left and right DNg11 neurons, explaining bilateral leg coordination during unilateral DNg11 activation.

paper_title: Descending neurons coordinate anterior grooming behavior in Drosophila.
experimental_stimulus: None; anatomical connectivity tested
neural_activity: Not measured
behavioral_consequence: GRASP shows synaptic contact with DNg12 dendrites in the gnathal ganglion
ecological_context: Sensory input for detecting debris to trigger grooming
summary: MBSNs provide sensory input to DNg12 neurons, confirmed by synaptic contact in the gnathal ganglion.

paper_title: State-dependent decoupling of sensory and motor circuits underlies behavioral flexibility in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Frontal looming stimuli, front-to-back moving bars, lateral looming, air puffs (mechanosensory)
neural_activity: Increased spike rate to visual motion; visual responses attenuated during non-flight (70% reduction in spike rate). Octopamine application restores visual responses in non-flight. Baseline membrane potential unaffected by flight state. Inhibited by air puffs in non-flight. Stronger contralateral visual responses than DNp10.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation drives landing leg extensions of all six legs. Silencing reduces landing response rates to frontal looming and moving bars. Silencing does not impair takeoff response rates. Spike rate correlates with leg extension amplitude.
ecological_context: Enables appropriate behavioral response to ambiguous looming cues: landing during flight (self-motion) versus escape when perched (predator approach).
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics (CsChrimson activation, Kir2.1 silencing)', 'In vivo whole-cell patch-clamp electrophysiology', 'High-speed video behavioral tracking', 'Pharmacology (Octopamine bath application)']
summary: DNp07 neurons respond to visual motion with increased spike rates, with activity gated by flight state via octopaminergic modulation. Activation drives landing leg extensions, while silencing reduces landing behavior without affecting escape.

paper_title: State-dependent decoupling of sensory and motor circuits underlies behavioral flexibility in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Frontal looming stimuli, front-to-back moving bars, lateral looming, air puffs (mechanosensory)
neural_activity: Increased spike rate to visual motion; visual responses completely eliminated during non-flight. Octopamine and Dopamine application have no effect on visual responses. Baseline membrane potential tonically depolarized during flight. Excited by air puffs in non-flight. Flight-dependence not mediated by antennae, wings, or halteres mechanoreceptors.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation drives landing leg extensions of all six legs (middle/hind legs directed more laterally than DNp07). Silencing reduces landing response rates to frontal looming and moving bars. Spike rate correlates with leg extension amplitude.
ecological_context: Enables appropriate behavioral response to ambiguous looming cues: landing during flight (self-motion) versus escape when perched (predator approach).
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics (CsChrimson activation, Kir2.1 silencing)', 'In vivo whole-cell patch-clamp electrophysiology', 'High-speed video behavioral tracking', 'Pharmacology (Octopamine/Dopamine bath application)', 'Mechanosensory ablation (antennae, wings, halteres)']
summary: DNp10 neurons respond to visual motion with increased spike rates, with activity gated by flight-dependent membrane depolarization independent of neuromodulators or primary mechanoreceptors. Activation drives landing leg extensions, while silencing reduces landing behavior.

paper_title: Threshold-Based Ordering of Sequential Actions during Drosophila Courtship.
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation using CsChrimson with 635 nm light (intensities 50–300 mW/mm²); Genetic ablation using UAS-Diphtheria Toxin (DTI); Natural courtship observation
neural_activity: Whole-cell patch-clamp recordings showed spike frequency ramping up over behaviorally relevant optogenetic stimulus intensities and time. Cumulative spike count at behavioral latency was consistent across varying stimulus intensities, whereas spike frequency was not, suggesting spike count encodes action thresholds.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation triggered a stereotyped sequence of actions: proboscis extension, followed by abdomen bending, then foreleg lifting (thresholds increased for successive actions). Inactivation (ablation) reduced the frequency of proboscis extension, abdomen bending, and foreleg lifting during courtship but did not affect proboscis extension during feeding or locomotion. Sequence was partially preserved after ablation.
ecological_context: Controls a stereotyped 'engagement' motif in male courtship behavior as the male approaches a female. Actions are sexually dimorphic (males show abdomen flexion, females show extension). Distinct from feeding behavior.
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics (CsChrimson)', 'Genetic ablation (DTI)', 'Whole-cell patch-clamp electrophysiology', 'High-resolution video tracking (two cameras)', 'Immunohistochemistry/Confocal microscopy']
summary: aSP22 descending neurons control a sequence of courtship engagement actions (proboscis extension, abdomen bending, leg lifting) via a ramp-to-threshold mechanism based on cumulative spike count. They are necessary for these actions during courtship but not feeding.

paper_title: Drosophila Ovipositor Extension in Mating Behavior and Egg Deposition Involves Distinct Sets of Brain Interneurons
experimental_stimulus: Temperature increase to 35°C (dTrpA1 activation in MARCM clones)
neural_activity: Activity induced via dTrpA1 thermogenetics (temperature increase)
behavioral_consequence: Females: Mating-type extrusion of the ovipositor (rejection behavior toward courting males). Males: Courtship behavior (wing vibration, tapping, licking, abdominal bending)
ecological_context: Female rejection behavior prevents remating with inferior males and allows decision to oviposit; affects fecundity and fitness
summary: pC2l neurons are sexually dimorphic brain interneurons; activation induces mating-type ovipositor extrusion (rejection) in females and courtship behavior in males.

paper_title: Drosophila Ovipositor Extension in Mating Behavior and Egg Deposition Involves Distinct Sets of Brain Interneurons
experimental_stimulus: Temperature increase to 35°C (dTrpA1 activation in MARCM clones)
neural_activity: Activity induced via dTrpA1 thermogenetics (temperature increase)
behavioral_consequence: Females: Oviposition-type extension of the ovipositor (abdomen bending) and egg ejection. Males: Absent in wild-type (eliminated by DsxM cell death); ectopically produced if cell death inhibited (p35)
ecological_context: Egg deposition directly affects fecundity and fitness; mated females engage in oviposition when they decide not to remate
summary: pMN2 neurons are female-specific brain interneurons; activation induces oviposition-type ovipositor extension and egg laying in females.

paper_title: Drosophila Ovipositor Extension in Mating Behavior and Egg Deposition Involves Distinct Sets of Brain Interneurons
experimental_stimulus: Temperature increase (29-32°C) in decapitated females or brain-restricted repression (Otd-GAL80)
neural_activity: Activity induced via dTrpA1 thermogenetics (temperature increase)
behavioral_consequence: Females: Oviposition-type extension and egg ejection (motor program activation without brain input)
ecological_context: Part of the motor program for egg laying, contributing to reproductive success
summary: dsx-expressing neurons in the ventral nerve cord alone can generate the motor patterns for oviposition-type extension and egg ejection.

paper_title: Drosophila Ovipositor Extension in Mating Behavior and Egg Deposition Involves Distinct Sets of Brain Interneurons
experimental_stimulus: Temperature increase to 35°C (dTrpA1 activation in MARCM clones)
neural_activity: Activity induced via dTrpA1 thermogenetics (temperature increase)
behavioral_consequence: Males: Significant correlation with courtship behavior (wing vibration, tapping, licking, abdominal bending) in Responder-C group
ecological_context: Male courtship behavior is a component of reproductive fitness
summary: pC1 neurons, which contain fru-expressing P1 neurons, are significantly correlated with the execution of courtship behavior in males.

paper_title: Hsp90 buffers behavioral variability by regulating Pdf transcription in clock neurons of Drosophila melanogaster.
experimental_stimulus: Hsp83 depletion (Hsp8308445 mutants or CRISPR knock-out in clock neurons); Light:Dark (LD) and Constant Darkness (DD) conditions
neural_activity: Reduced Pdf transcription (measured via Pdf-Red reporter fluorescence intensity in soma and dorsal projections); Reduced PDF peptide accumulation (immunostaining), with altered oscillation amplitude (morning peak, lower trough at ZT14); No change in neuron development or circuit formation
behavioral_consequence: Increased inter-individual behavioral variability; Reduced phase coherence in DD; Reduced synchronization to LD cycles (reduced Entrainment Index, particularly morning peak); Reduced rhythmicity when Hsp83 knocked out in all clock neurons
ecological_context: Circadian rhythms restrict activity to species-specific 'temporal niches' to avoid predators or increase foraging success; Relaxation of temporal niche constriction (increased variability) could be beneficial under stressful environmental conditions (bet hedging strategy)
techniques_used: ['Locomotor activity monitoring (DAM system)', 'Immunohistochemistry (anti-PDF, anti-RFP)', 'Confocal microscopy', 'Hidden Markov Models (HMM) analysis']
summary: Hsp83 depletion in s-LNv and l-LNv clock neurons reduces Pdf transcription and PDF peptide accumulation, leading to increased inter-individual behavioral variability and reduced phase coherence.

paper_title: Hsp90 buffers behavioral variability by regulating Pdf transcription in clock neurons of Drosophila melanogaster.
experimental_stimulus: Hsp83 depletion (Hsp8308445 mutants); Constant Darkness (DD)
neural_activity: Altered PERIOD (PER) expression rhythms; 8.0-luc reporter expression (restricted to PDF-negative neurons) shows high-amplitude, short-period (23.6h) oscillations; PER staining detects signals in all six LNd and 5th s-LNv
behavioral_consequence: Short-period behavioral rhythms observed in 8.0-luc background; Overall increased behavioral variability similar to Pdf01 mutants
ecological_context: Circadian rhythms restrict activity to species-specific 'temporal niches'; Altered molecular oscillations in subsets of clock neurons contribute to behavioral variability under stress
summary: Hsp83 depletion in clock neurons affects synchronized oscillations of the clock protein PERIOD in PDF-negative neurons (LNd, DN) similar to flies without PDF, resulting in high-amplitude, short-period molecular rhythms.

paper_title: Hsp90 buffers behavioral variability by regulating Pdf transcription in clock neurons of Drosophila melanogaster.
experimental_stimulus: Hsp83 depletion (Hsp8308445 mutants)
neural_activity: RFP intensity in soma and projections (using CCAP-Gal4 > UAS-myr-mRFP1) showed no difference compared to controls
behavioral_consequence: None tested (control for RFP stability)
ecological_context: Not tested (control for technical artifact)
summary: CCAP neurons were used as a control to show HSP83 is not involved in RFP folding or stability, as no signal intensity differences were observed upon Hsp83 depletion.

paper_title: Shaker potassium channel mediates an age-sensitive neurocardiac axis regulating sleep and cardiac function in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Shaker gene knockdown via RNAi (Elav-Gal4 > UAS-ShRNA); Environmental conditions: Light/Dark (LD) vs Constant Light (LL); Feeding: Ad Libitum (ALF) vs Time-Restricted Feeding (TRF); Aging: 3-week-old and 5-week-old cohorts
neural_activity: Regulates neuronal excitability and electrical signaling (inferred from behavioral and cardiac phenotypes); knockdown associated with sleep loss and hyperactivity
behavioral_consequence: Severe sleep loss (reduced sleep duration), hyperactivity (increased activity), and impaired cardiac function (arrhythmia, reduced output) via a neurocardiac axis; neuronal knockdown alone was sufficient to impair cardiac behavior
ecological_context: Aging processes, circadian entrainment (light cycles), and metabolic regulation (feeding timing)
techniques_used: ['Elav-Gal4/UAS-RNAi genetic manipulation', 'Drosophila Activity Monitoring System (DAMS)', 'Semi-intact heart dissection', 'High-speed video recording of heart physiology', 'SOHA software analysis']
summary: Pannuclear neuronal knockdown of the Shaker channel causes sleep loss, hyperactivity, and cardiac dysfunction, establishing a neurocardiac axis where neuronal signaling regulates heart function in an age-dependent manner.

paper_title: Octopamine signaling from clock neurons plays dual roles in Drosophila long-term memory
behavioral_consequence: Temporally restricted knockdown of Tyramine β hydroxylase (Tbh) disrupted long-term memory (LTM) maintenance. Tbh knockdown had minimal effects on circadian behavioral rhythms or sleep.
techniques_used: ['Temporally restricted knockdown of Tyramine β hydroxylase (Tbh)']
summary: Octopamine signaling from LNds is essential for LTM maintenance, with minimal impact on circadian rhythms or sleep.

paper_title: Octopamine signaling from clock neurons plays dual roles in Drosophila long-term memory
cell_type: Pdf neurons (small and large ventral–lateral neurons)
behavioral_consequence: Temporally restricted knockdown of Tyramine β hydroxylase (Tbh) impaired LTM consolidation. Tbh knockdown had minimal effects on circadian behavioral rhythms or sleep.
techniques_used: ['Temporally restricted knockdown of Tyramine β hydroxylase (Tbh)']
summary: Octopamine signaling from Pdf neurons is essential for LTM consolidation, with minimal impact on circadian rhythms or sleep.

paper_title: The circadian genes are required in DAL neurons for Drosophila long-term memory formation
experimental_stimulus: Olfactory avoidance training (3-octanol or 4-methylcyclohexanol paired with 70V electric shock); Heat-shock induction (29°C) to activate UAS-transgenes
neural_activity: Oscillatory transcriptional activity (~24h periodicity) detected via LABL reporter (period promoter-driven luciferase); Transcriptional disruption of clk/cyc via dominant-negative proteins or RNAi
behavioral_consequence: Post-training disruption of clk or cyc in DAL neurons disrupts 3-day memory (reduced Performance Index). Global tim or cyc mutants do not affect 3-day memory. Induction prior to testing does not affect retrieval. Locomotor activity and sleep changes are unlikely responsible for memory deficits.
techniques_used: ['LABL reporter assay (luminescence)', 'Olfactory avoidance behavior assay', 'Heat-shock induction (UAS/GAL80ts)', 'Drosophila Activity Monitor (DAM)']
summary: DAL neurons exhibit 24h oscillatory transcriptional activity. Post-training disruption of clock genes (clk, cyc) in these neurons impairs 3-day olfactory avoidance memory, independent of locomotor or sleep changes.

paper_title: Circadian rhythms are more resilient to pacemaker neuron disruption in female Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Locomotor activity monitored under LD 12:12 and Constant Darkness (DD). Genetic manipulations included Pdf null mutation, CRISPR-Cas9 Pdf knockout in LNvs, UAS-DBTs expression (speeding up molecular clock), and UAS-TeTxLC expression (blocking synaptic transmission).
neural_activity: PDF peptide levels in cell bodies and dorsal projections quantified via immunohistochemistry and confocal microscopy. PDF cycling amplitude in dorsal termini analyzed via COSINOR curve fitting (no significant sex differences in wild-type amplitude).
behavioral_consequence: Loss of Pdf or CRISPR knockout caused greater arrhythmicity in males than females. Speeding up molecular clock advanced morning peak phase in males but not females; significantly shortened FRP in males (~18.5h) while females retained ~24h period or showed bimodal rhythms. Blocking transmission lengthened FRP more in males.
ecological_context: Discussion suggests sex differences in oscillator hierarchy may serve an adaptive purpose ensuring precise timing of female reproductive behaviors (sexual receptivity, egg laying) for fitness.
summary: s-LNvs exhibit sexually dimorphic control over circadian rhythms; male activity is more disrupted by PDF loss or clock speeding, whereas female rhythms remain more resilient, suggesting distributed timekeeping in females.

paper_title: Circadian rhythms are more resilient to pacemaker neuron disruption in female Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Locomotor activity under LD 12:12. Genetic manipulation: UAS-DBTs expression via MB122B-Gal4 (targeting E1+E2 subsets).
neural_activity: Not directly measured; inferred via behavioral clock phase changes.
behavioral_consequence: Speeding up clock advanced evening peak phase in both sexes, with a more pronounced effect in females. No significant alteration of FRP or rhythmic power in DD.
ecological_context: Regulates timing of evening activity and rest transitions within the circadian cycle.
summary: E1+E2 LNds regulate evening peak phase, showing a more pronounced effect in females when molecular clock speed is increased.

paper_title: Circadian rhythms are more resilient to pacemaker neuron disruption in female Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Locomotor activity under LD 12:12 and DD. Genetic manipulation: UAS-DBTs expression via Clk856-Gal4.
neural_activity: Not directly measured; inferred via behavioral clock phase changes.
behavioral_consequence: Speeding up molecular clock advanced both morning and evening peaks and shortened FRP in both sexes. No significant sex differences in rhythmicity, rhythmic power, or FRP shortening observed.
ecological_context: General circadian timekeeping across the entire clock network.
summary: Global clock speeding affects both sexes similarly, contrasting with the sex-specific effects observed with s-LNv manipulation.

paper_title: Circadian control of a sex-specific behaviour in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Genetic downregulation of per (RNAi) in PDF+ cells
neural_activity: Molecular clock disruption in LNv neurons
behavioral_consequence: Egg-laying rhythmicity not abolished; period slightly shortened
ecological_context: Egg-laying is a female-specific behavior with a peak near night onset impacting fitness
summary: Disruption of the molecular clock in PDF+ LNv neurons does not abolish circadian egg-laying rhythmicity in Drosophila females.

paper_title: Circadian control of a sex-specific behaviour in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Genetic downregulation of per (RNAi) or electrical silencing (Kir2.1) in DN1p
neural_activity: Molecular clock disruption or neuronal silencing in DN1p neurons
behavioral_consequence: Egg-laying rhythmicity not altered compared to controls
ecological_context: Egg-laying exhibits circadian rhythmicity with a peak near night onset
summary: Disruption or silencing of DN1p neurons does not affect circadian egg-laying rhythmicity in Drosophila females.

paper_title: Circadian control of a sex-specific behaviour in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Genetic downregulation of per (RNAi) using MB122B or Mai179;PdfG80 drivers
neural_activity: Molecular clock disruption in Cry+ LNd subset
behavioral_consequence: Drastic reduction of circadian egg-laying rhythmicity; locomotor activity rhythmicity in mated females remained unaltered
ecological_context: Egg-laying rhythmicity is endogenously driven; locomotor activity differs in mated females
techniques_used: ['RNAi (MB122B-splitGal4 or Mai179-Gal4;pdf-Gal80)', 'Automatic oviposition device', 'Video tracking for locomotor activity', 'Periodogram analysis']
summary: The molecular clock in Cry+ LNd neurons is necessary for rhythmic egg-laying but is not required for locomotor rhythmicity in mated females.

paper_title: Circadian control of a sex-specific behaviour in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Genetic downregulation of per (RNAi) using Clk856 driver
neural_activity: Molecular clock disruption in all clock neurons in the brain
behavioral_consequence: Drastic reduction of rhythmic oviposition compared to controls
ecological_context: Egg-laying rhythm is endogenously driven by the central clock rather than peripheral clocks
summary: Disruption of the molecular clock in all brain clock neurons significantly reduces circadian egg-laying rhythmicity.

paper_title: Circadian control of a sex-specific behaviour in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Connectome analysis (observational)
neural_activity: N/A
ecological_context: Part of the neural circuit controlling the motor program leading to egg deposition
summary: Hemibrain connectome analysis reveals bidirectional synaptic connections between Cry+ LNd (E2) and oviIN neurons.

paper_title: Circadian control of a sex-specific behaviour in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Connectome analysis (observational)
neural_activity: N/A
ecological_context: Known to control female receptivity (cited); connected to circadian circuit
summary: Hemibrain connectome analysis reveals synaptic contacts between Cry- LNd (E3) and pC1 neurons.

paper_title: Circadian control of a sex-specific behaviour in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Connectome analysis (observational)
neural_activity: N/A
ecological_context: Circadian clock neurons
summary: Hemibrain connectome analysis reveals strong bidirectional connections between LPN and oviIN neurons.

paper_title: Synaptic connectome of a neurosecretory network in the Drosophila brain
summary: Identified 16-18 neurons in the medial protocerebrum based on connectome clustering and DILP2 antibody quantification. Receive the largest number of inputs from central neurons and descending neurons. Single-cell transcriptomics reveals expression of insulin receptor (InR) and receptors for sNPF and Leucokinin.

paper_title: Synaptic connectome of a neurosecretory network in the Drosophila brain
techniques_used: ['FlyWire connectome analysis', 'Retrograde trans-synaptic labelling (retro-Tango)', 'Electron microscopy (Dense Core Vesicles)']
summary: Identified as 6 neurons in the medial protocerebrum via retro-Tango and connectome clustering. Receive majority of inputs from neurons in the subesophageal zone (SEZ). Ultrastructural analysis of Dense Core Vesicles supported classification against m-NSCDMS.

paper_title: Synaptic connectome of a neurosecretory network in the Drosophila brain
summary: 6 adult neurosecretory cells identified that project via the nervii corpora cardiaca (NCC). 4 additional Gr64a-expressing CRZ neurons do not project via NCC and are not neurosecretory. Receive inputs from descending neurons and provide strong synaptic output to DNg27 descending neurons innervating the wing neuropil.

paper_title: Synaptic connectome of a neurosecretory network in the Drosophila brain
summary: Identified as 2 large neurons in the subesophageal zone (about twice as large as other NSC). Receive direct inputs from gustatory and mechanosensory neurons as well as olfactory projection neurons. Co-express Capa and Myosuppressin (Ms).

paper_title: Synaptic connectome of a neurosecretory network in the Drosophila brain
summary: 6 neurons in the lateral protocerebrum receiving the largest number of strong synaptic inputs (16 connections, >1500 synapses). Inputs originate from SEZ and lateral horn. Strong olfactory inputs from aversive and food-related ORNs relayed via projection neurons.

paper_title: Hunger- and thirst-sensing neurons modulate a neuroendocrine network to coordinate sugar and water ingestion
experimental_stimulus: Hunger signals (AKH) and thirst signals (high hemolymph osmolality); Optogenetic activation; RNAi knockdown of dILP3 and amontillado
neural_activity: AKH increases activity; high hemolymph osmolality decreases activity. Optogenetic activation increases activity.
behavioral_consequence: Activation promotes sugar ingestion and inhibits water ingestion. Knockdown of dILP3 or amontillado decreases sugar ingestion and increases water ingestion.
ecological_context: Coordinate competing hunger and thirst needs to maintain internal nutrient homeostasis for survival.
techniques_used: ['RNAi screen', 'Temporal consumption assay', 'Optogenetics', 'In vivo functional imaging']
summary: ISNs sense hunger and thirst signals to reciprocally regulate sugar and water ingestion via dILP3 release.

paper_title: Hunger- and thirst-sensing neurons modulate a neuroendocrine network to coordinate sugar and water ingestion
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation/inhibition; Optogenetic stimulation of ISNs
neural_activity: ISN activation inhibits BiT (voltage imaging showed hyperpolarization).
behavioral_consequence: Activation decreases sugar ingestion and increases water ingestion. Inhibition increases sugar ingestion and decreases water ingestion.
ecological_context: Serves as a critical node in the ISN network to coordinate nutrient-specific ingestion based on internal needs.
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics', 'Temporal consumption assay', 'In vivo voltage imaging', 'Electron microscopy reconstruction']
summary: BiT reciprocally regulates sugar and water ingestion downstream of ISNs, acting as a key node in the peptidergic network.

paper_title: Hunger- and thirst-sensing neurons modulate a neuroendocrine network to coordinate sugar and water ingestion
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation; Optogenetic stimulation of BiT
neural_activity: BiT activation inhibits IPCs (calcium imaging).
behavioral_consequence: Acute activation increases sucrose ingestion and decreases water ingestion.
ecological_context: Integrate nutrient status signals to regulate feeding and metabolism for homeostasis.
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics', 'Temporal consumption assay', 'In vivo calcium imaging', 'Electron microscopy reconstruction']
summary: IPCs reciprocally regulate sugar and water ingestion, receiving inhibitory input from BiT.

paper_title: Hunger- and thirst-sensing neurons modulate a neuroendocrine network to coordinate sugar and water ingestion
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation/inhibition; Optogenetic stimulation of ISNs
neural_activity: ISN stimulation causes robust calcium responses (likely via BiT disinhibition). BiT stimulation response not detected (likely inhibition).
behavioral_consequence: Activation decreases water ingestion but does not change sugar ingestion. Inhibition increases water ingestion but does not change sugar ingestion.
ecological_context: Modulate water ingestion downstream of ISNs to coordinate hydration needs.
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics', 'Temporal consumption assay', 'In vivo calcium imaging', 'Electron microscopy reconstruction']
summary: CCHa2R-RA neurons modulate water ingestion specifically downstream of the ISN-BiT pathway.

paper_title: Hunger- and thirst-sensing neurons modulate a neuroendocrine network to coordinate sugar and water ingestion
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation/inhibition; Optogenetic stimulation of ISNs
neural_activity: Activated upon ISN optogenetic stimulation (calcium imaging).
behavioral_consequence: Activation decreases water ingestion and increases sugar ingestion. Silencing decreases sugar ingestion and increases water ingestion.
ecological_context: Contribute to the broader peptidergic network coordinating sugar and water ingestion based on nutrient need.
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics', 'Temporal consumption assay', 'In vivo calcium imaging', 'Electron microscopy reconstruction']
summary: CCAP neurons reciprocally regulate sugar and water ingestion downstream of the ISNs.

paper_title: Thirst interneurons that promote water seeking and limit feeding behavior in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Water deprivation (thirst state) vs water replete; Hunger state tested; Humidity gradient in open-field assay
neural_activity: Janu-GABA1 neurons were selectively activated in thirsty flies using TRIC calcium indicator; hunger did not change activity of any Janu neuron
behavioral_consequence: Activation (TrpA1/Chrimson) promotes water seeking in water replete flies and induces place preference; Inactivation (Shibirets) decreases thirsty water seeking; Knockdown of Gad1/VGAT decreases thirsty water seeking and reduces length of first intake bout; No effect on total water consumption, pumping rate, or feeding behavior
ecological_context: Thirsty seeking up a humidity gradient is rewarding; Flies use hygrosensory and taste environmental cues to locate and drink water
techniques_used: ['TRIC calcium imaging', 'TrpA1 thermogenetics', 'Chrimson optogenetics', 'Shibirets inactivation', 'RNAi knockdown', 'Open-field behavioral assay']
summary: Janu-GABA1 neurons are GABAergic local interneurons in the SEZ that are selectively activated by thirst, promote water seeking, and are not involved in feeding behavior or water intake metrics.

paper_title: Thirst interneurons that promote water seeking and limit feeding behavior in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Water deprivation vs water replete; Hunger state tested
neural_activity: No state-dependent changes in Janu-AstA neuronal activity detected with TRIC calcium indicator under thirst or hunger conditions
behavioral_consequence: Activation (TrpA1) promotes water seeking in water replete flies; Inactivation decreases thirsty water seeking; Knockdown of AstA reduces thirsty water seeking and increases food occupancy (inhibits feeding); No effect on water consumption time, first intake bout, or pumping rate
ecological_context: Coordinates thirst and hunger behaviors; Inhibits feeding while promoting water seeking similar to dehydration-anorexia
techniques_used: ['TRIC calcium imaging', 'TrpA1 thermogenetics', 'RNAi knockdown', 'Open-field behavioral assay']
summary: Janu-AstA neurons are projection interneurons from SEZ to SMP that release Allatostatin A to promote water seeking and inhibit feeding behavior, though they do not show state-dependent activity changes.

paper_title: Thirst interneurons that promote water seeking and limit feeding behavior in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Thirst state vs replete; Hunger state vs fed
neural_activity: Only P1 and L1-L NPF neurons showed activity; Both increased activity in thirsty flies; Hunger also selectively increases activity of these two neurons
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of NPF increases water seeking; Knockdown of AstA-R2 in NPF decreases thirsty water seeking, increases feeding behavior, decreases total water consumption time, and reduces duration of first intake bout
ecological_context: Reciprocally regulate thirst and hunger behaviors
techniques_used: ['TRIC calcium imaging', 'RNAi knockdown (NPF, AstA-R2)', 'Open-field behavioral assay', 'Synaptic GRASP']
summary: NPF neurons (P1 and L1-L) receive AstA signals from Janu-AstA neurons via AstA-R2 and reciprocally regulate water seeking and feeding behavior, showing increased activity during both thirst and hunger.

paper_title: Regulation of Olfactory Associative Memory by the Circadian Clock Output Signal Pigment-Dispersing Factor (PDF)
experimental_stimulus: Olfactory associative memory training (odor + sucrose/shock); Time of Day (ZT1, ZT13); Bath-applied PDF; Optogenetic activation (P2X2 + ATP)
neural_activity: PDF release required for STM; Optogenetic activation of LNvs did not evoke cAMP response in MB calyx; PDF bath application evoked cAMP response in DN1 neurons
behavioral_consequence: Loss of PDF ($pdf^{01}$) significantly decreases appetitive STM and aversive STM; PDF signaling stabilizes memory formation across the day (WT stable, mutants show morning deficit)
ecological_context: Stabilizes appetitive sucrose/odor memory across the day to optimize responses to changing conditions
summary: PDF-producing LNv neurons regulate olfactory associative memory; their signaling stabilizes appetitive STM across the day, though LNv activation does not directly signal to MB Kenyon cells.

paper_title: Regulation of Olfactory Associative Memory by the Circadian Clock Output Signal Pigment-Dispersing Factor (PDF)
experimental_stimulus: Olfactory memory training; Bath-applied PDF; LNv optogenetic activation
neural_activity: Confocal imaging showed minimal colocalization of PDFR and KC markers; EPAC-cAMP imaging showed no significant cAMP response to PDF or LNv activation
behavioral_consequence: PDF acts extrinsically to MB; KCs are not direct targets of PDF for appetitive STM regulation
ecological_context: Site of olfactory memory integration
summary: MB Kenyon cells are not direct targets of PDF signaling for appetitive STM, despite their role in memory integration.

paper_title: Regulation of Olfactory Associative Memory by the Circadian Clock Output Signal Pigment-Dispersing Factor (PDF)
experimental_stimulus: Bath-applied PDF
neural_activity: EPAC-cAMP imaging showed significant ~30% average cAMP increase upon PDF application
behavioral_consequence: Not tested for behavior in this study
ecological_context: Circadian clock circuit
summary: DN1 clock neurons show significant cAMP response to PDF, serving as a positive control for PDF responsiveness in the clock circuit.

paper_title: Regulation of Olfactory Associative Memory by the Circadian Clock Output Signal Pigment-Dispersing Factor (PDF)
experimental_stimulus: Appetitive memory training
neural_activity: Not directly measured; PDFR expression identified via rescue
behavioral_consequence: Pan-neuronal and adult-specific neuronal PDFR expression rescues han5304 appetitive STM deficit; Clock-specific PDFR expression does not
ecological_context: Necessary for equal learning ability at all times of day
summary: PDFR in adult neurons outside the clock and MB is sufficient to rescue appetitive STM, distinct from clock-based PDFR function.

paper_title: Regulation of Olfactory Associative Memory by the Circadian Clock Output Signal Pigment-Dispersing Factor (PDF)
experimental_stimulus: Locomotor activity; Appetitive memory training
neural_activity: Clock-specific PDFR rescues locomotor rhythms but not memory-related cAMP signaling in MB
behavioral_consequence: Clock-specific PDFR expression (clk856-GAL4) rescues LD/DD locomotor activity but fails to rescue appetitive STM deficit
ecological_context: Distinguishes clock output functions for locomotion versus memory
summary: PDFR expression within the core clock circuit regulates locomotor activity but is insufficient for appetitive memory formation.

paper_title: A glucose-sensing neuron pair regulates insulin and glucagon in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Nutritive sugars (D-glucose, D-trehalose, D-fructose); nonnutritive sugars (L-glucose, sucrose); amino acids; feeding state (fed, starved, refed)
neural_activity: Ex vivo calcium imaging (GCaMP6s) showed robust calcium oscillations with D-glucose; In vivo CaLexA showed increased GFP signal in fed/refed flies; Synaptic markers (Syt-GFP) changed with feeding state
behavioral_consequence: Inactivation (Kir2.1, TNT) reduced preference for D-glucose over L-glucose in starved flies in two-choice assay
ecological_context: Mediates starvation-induced selection of nutritive sugar; maintains glucose homeostasis
techniques_used: ['Two-choice assay', 'Ex vivo calcium imaging (GCaMP6s)', 'In vivo CaLexA', 'Immunostaining', 'Mass spectrometry', 'GRASP']
summary: CN neurons are glucose-excited neurons that respond to nutritive sugars, regulate insulin and glucagon release, and are required for selecting nutritive sugar after starvation.

paper_title: A glucose-sensing neuron pair regulates insulin and glucagon in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: D-glucose (in vivo), CN neuron activation (ATP), sNPF application; feeding state (fed, starved)
neural_activity: Ex vivo calcium imaging (GCaMP6s) showed increased activity in response to D-glucose and sNPF; Activity dependent on CN neurons
ecological_context: Insulin secretion regulates glucose homeostasis
summary: IPCs respond to glucose and CN neuron activation via sNPF, leading to dilp2 secretion.

paper_title: A glucose-sensing neuron pair regulates insulin and glucagon in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: CN neuron activation (ATP), sNPF application; feeding state (fed, starved)
neural_activity: Ex vivo calcium imaging (GCaMP6s) and Arclight imaging showed decreased activity and hyperpolarization upon CN activation/sNPF
ecological_context: Glucagon analog secretion regulates glucose homeostasis
techniques_used: ['Ex vivo calcium imaging (GCaMP6s)', 'Arclight imaging', 'GRASP', 'Mass spectrometry', 'Dot blot']
summary: AKH-producing cells are inhibited by CN neuron activation via sNPF, suppressing AKH secretion.

paper_title: Peptidergic signaling from clock neurons regulates reproductive dormancy in Drosophila melanogaster
cell_type: PDF-expressing neurons (PDF+), including small ventral Lateral Neurons (s-LNvs)
experimental_stimulus: Genetic manipulation using Pdf-Gal4 or R6-Gal4 drivers (overexpression of Na+ChBac, PDF, sNPF, Ork, hid; Pdf01 mutation)
neural_activity: Enhanced or reduced release of neuropeptides PDF and sNPF (based on excitability manipulation preventing or enhancing action potential firing)
behavioral_consequence: Modulation of reproductive dormancy (ovarian arrest); enhanced activity or overexpression reduced arrest, reduced activity or mutation increased arrest
ecological_context: Regulation of reproductive arrest during overwintering in response to low temperatures and short photoperiods
summary: PDF-expressing neurons, including s-LNvs, regulate reproductive dormancy; enhanced neuropeptide release reduces ovarian arrest, while reduced activity or mutation increases arrest.

paper_title: Peptidergic signaling from clock neurons regulates reproductive dormancy in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Bath application of synthetic PDF and sNPF; Genetic manipulation (PDFR overexpression, sNPFR1 inhibition)
neural_activity: Increased cAMP levels (FRET imaging) upon PDF and sNPF application; Increased Ca2+ levels (GCaMP imaging) upon sNPF application; Synergistic cAMP increase with co-application
behavioral_consequence: Modulation of reproductive dormancy; PDFR overexpression reduced arrest, sNPFR1 inhibition increased arrest
ecological_context: Maintain active state to antagonize dormancy and allow reproductive growth; IPCs release dILPs
techniques_used: ['Live optical imaging (cAMP FRET, Ca2+ GCaMP)', 'Bath application of peptides', 'Genetic manipulation (Gal4/UAS)', 'Dormancy assay']
summary: IPCs respond directly to PDF and sNPF with increased cAMP and sNPF-induced Ca2+, modulating reproductive dormancy levels.

paper_title: Peptidergic signaling from clock neurons regulates reproductive dormancy in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Genetic manipulation using Crz-Gal4 drivers (overexpression of Na+ChBac, sNPF)
neural_activity: Not directly measured in this study
behavioral_consequence: No significant effect on reproductive dormancy compared to controls
ecological_context: Investigated for role in dormancy regulation but found not robust
summary: DLPs were tested for involvement in dormancy regulation but showed no robust effect compared to PDF+ neurons.

paper_title: The HisCl1 histamine receptor acts in photoreceptors to synchronize Drosophila behavioral rhythms with light-dark cycles
experimental_stimulus: Light-Dark (LD) cycles (phase advanced/delayed), Red Light-Dark (RD) cycles
neural_activity: Expression of HisCl1 mRNA and protein confirmed via RNAscope in situ hybridization and immunolabeling; receives histaminergic inputs from other photoreceptors (Rh1, R7, Rh5)
behavioral_consequence: Rescue of circadian synchronization in cry HisCl1 ort triple mutants when HisCl1 expressed specifically in Rh6 cells; knockdown of HisCl1 in Rh6 cells slows synchronization; mutant HisCl1 eyes fail to synchronize with delayed LD cycles
ecological_context: Synchronizing rest-activity rhythms with environmental light-dark cycles
techniques_used: ['Locomotor activity monitoring (TriKinetics)', 'GAL4/UAS rescue', 'RNA interference (RNAi)', 'Mutant analysis (HisCl1134)', 'RNAscope in situ hybridization', 'Immunolabeling']
summary: Rh6-expressing photoreceptors express HisCl1 and receive histaminergic inputs from other photoreceptors to support Cry-independent circadian entrainment.

paper_title: The HisCl1 histamine receptor acts in photoreceptors to synchronize Drosophila behavioral rhythms with light-dark cycles
experimental_stimulus: Light-Dark (LD) cycles, Red Light-Dark (RD) cycles
neural_activity: Expression of Ort protein and mRNA confirmed in lamina and medulla/lobula neuropils; does not express HisCl1
behavioral_consequence: Rescue of circadian synchronization in cry HisCl1 ort triple mutants when Ort expressed in these interneurons; HisCl1 cannot replace Ort function here
ecological_context: Synchronizing rest-activity rhythms with environmental light-dark cycles
summary: Ort-expressing interneurons in the optic lobe mediate Cry-independent circadian entrainment through a distinct pathway from HisCl1.

paper_title: The HisCl1 histamine receptor acts in photoreceptors to synchronize Drosophila behavioral rhythms with light-dark cycles
experimental_stimulus: Red Light-Dark (RD) cycles
neural_activity: Histaminergic outer photoreceptors; project to lamina
behavioral_consequence: Inputs from Rh1 cells contribute to entrainment mediated by HisCl1 in Rh6 cells; absence of Rh1 slows synchronization in flies rescued for HisCl1 in Rh6 cells
ecological_context: Circadian photoreception via red light
summary: Rh1-expressing outer photoreceptors provide histaminergic inputs to Rh6-expressing photoreceptors to support circadian entrainment.

paper_title: The HisCl1 histamine receptor acts in photoreceptors to synchronize Drosophila behavioral rhythms with light-dark cycles
cell_type: Clock Neurons (LNvs, DN1, DN2, DN3, etc.)
experimental_stimulus: Light-Dark (LD) cycles
neural_activity: Express Clk:gal4 or tim-gal4; receive indirect histaminergic inputs
behavioral_consequence: Direct expression of HisCl1 or Ort in clock neurons did not rescue synchronization in cry HisCl1 ort triple mutants
ecological_context: Control of circadian clock network
summary: Direct histaminergic inputs to clock neurons are not sufficient for circadian entrainment; an indirect pathway via photoreceptors/interneurons is required.

paper_title: Hub-organized parallel circuits of central circadian pacemaker neurons for visual photoentrainment in Drosophila
cell_type: Central circadian pacemaker neurons (l-LNv, s-LNv, 5th s-LNv, ITP-LNd, DN1a, DN3a, DN3p)
experimental_stimulus: Full field light stimulation (470 nm) via compound eyes or H-B eyelets
neural_activity: Excitation with increased action potential firing and inward currents (patch-clamp recordings)
behavioral_consequence: Silencing or ablation of subsets (e.g., PDF-LNvs or evening neurons) in Cry02 flies did not eliminate photoentrainment to dim LD cycles
ecological_context: Synchronization of the master clock to external light/dark cycles for photoentrainment
summary: Most central circadian pacemaker neurons are excited by light inputs through eyes and function in a parallel circuit to receive visual signals for photoentrainment.

paper_title: Hub-organized parallel circuits of central circadian pacemaker neurons for visual photoentrainment in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Light stimulation
neural_activity: Increased action potential firing with longer latency compared to other pacemaker neurons
behavioral_consequence: Light responses abolished when 5th s-LNv and ITP-LNd are silenced
ecological_context: Receive visual inputs from other light-sensitive pacemaker neurons (5th s-LNv and ITP-LNd) rather than directly from the aMe
summary: sNPF-LNd neurons receive visual inputs from 5th s-LNv and ITP-LNd via excitatory chemical synaptic transmission.

paper_title: Hub-organized parallel circuits of central circadian pacemaker neurons for visual photoentrainment in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Brief flash of light
neural_activity: Inward current causing graded depolarization without action potentials (patch-clamp)
behavioral_consequence: Contribute to light responses of central pacemaker neurons; responses reduced/abolished in Rh6 mutants or after axonal ablation
ecological_context: Detect light via Rh6 through PLC-mediated phototransduction for visual photoentrainment
summary: H-B eyelet photoreceptors send monosynaptic inputs to central circadian pacemaker neurons via the accessory medulla (aMe).

paper_title: Hub-organized parallel circuits of central circadian pacemaker neurons for visual photoentrainment in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Light (compound eyes), ATP (chemogenetic activation)
neural_activity: Graded depolarization to light; ATP activation excites pacemaker neurons (LNvs) (patch-clamp/imaging)
behavioral_consequence: Axonal ablation significantly reduces light responses of LNvs mediated by compound eyes
ecological_context: Relay visual signals from compound eyes to central circadian pacemaker neurons via the accessory medulla
summary: AstC/CcapR interneurons relay light signals from compound eyes to central circadian pacemaker neurons in the accessory medulla.

paper_title: A post-ingestive amino acid sensor promotes food consumption in Drosophila
cell_type: DH44+ neurons (Diuretic Hormone 44 expressing neurons in the pars intercerebralis)
experimental_stimulus: L-amino acids (L-Glu, L-Ala, L-Asp, 5 mM), amino acid mixture (17 L-amino acids), D-amino acids (D-Glu, D-Ala, D-Asp), yeast extract (5%), sucrose (400 mM), glucose (dose-dependent)
neural_activity: Rapid calcium transients observed via in vivo and ex vivo GCaMP6m imaging upon perfusion or feeding of L-Glu, L-Ala, L-Asp, and yeast extract. Response was dose-dependent. Response to D-amino acids was significantly lower than L-amino acids. Calcium responses were resistant to tetrodotoxin (TTX), indicating direct activation without synaptic transmission. In the presence of glucose, calcium responses to amino acids were eliminated.
behavioral_consequence: Genetic silencing (Kir2.1 expression) abolished the increase in food consumption (sucrose intake) induced by dietary amino acids and yeast extract in the MAFE assay, and reduced food contact duration in the FLIC assay. Genetic activation (NaChBac expression) was sufficient to promote sucrose consumption. DH44, DH44-R1, and DH44-R2 mutants exhibited no increase in food consumption with amino acids. Knockdown of CG13248 transporter in these neurons blocked amino acid-induced feeding and calcium responses.
ecological_context: Yeast is the major protein source for fruit flies in their natural habitats. L-Glu, L-Ala, and L-Asp are among the most abundant amino acids in yeast. The sensor is tuned to these amino acids to facilitate protein intake from protein-rich food sources.
techniques_used: ['MAFE assay (Manual Feeding)', 'FLIC assay', 'In vivo calcium imaging', 'Ex vivo calcium imaging', 'Immunostaining', 'Single-cell transcriptome analysis (RNAseq)', 'Genetic manipulation (RNAi, CRISPR mutants, UAS-GAL4)']
summary: DH44+ neurons in the pars intercerebralis are a central amino acid sensor that is rapidly and directly activated by L-Glu, L-Ala, and L-Asp via the putative transporter CG13248. These neurons mediate the promotion of food consumption by dietary amino acids and yeast extract, independent of peripheral Ir76b signaling or internal nutritional status.

paper_title: Cryptochrome-Dependent and -Independent Circadian Entrainment Circuits in Drosophila
cell_type: Evening (E) Oscillator Clock Neurons (fifth s-LNv and LNd)
experimental_stimulus: 16/8 h light/dark (LD) cycles with an 8 h LD phase delay; brief light pulses
neural_activity: PDP1 molecular oscillations phase-shifted in response to LD phase delay in cry0 mutants (visual system dependent). CRY rescue restored PDP1 cycling phase to approximately ZT 18–21 and enabled phase delay response. TIM degradation measured in LN groups via immunostaining.
behavioral_consequence: CRY rescue in E cells (via mai-gal4/Pdf-gal80) enabled approximately 6 h phase shift on day 1 after 8 h LD shift (fast re-entrainment). cry0 mutants showed limited re-entrainment (~2 h/d).
ecological_context: Entrainment to environmental light/dark (LD) cycles which serve as the most important environmental time cue.
techniques_used: ['Locomotor activity monitoring (DAM)', 'Immunohistochemistry (TIM, PDP1, PDF)', 'Confocal imaging', 'Genetic rescue (uas-cry, gal4 drivers)']
summary: E cells (fifth s-LNv and LNd) are the primary targets of light input via CRY and the visual system, required for fast light entrainment and PDP1 cycling phase shifts.

paper_title: Cryptochrome-Dependent and -Independent Circadian Entrainment Circuits in Drosophila
cell_type: Morning (M) Oscillator Clock Neurons (s-LNv and l-LNv)
experimental_stimulus: 16/8 h LD cycles with an 8 h LD phase delay
neural_activity: PDP1 cycling in s-LNv and l-LNv did not follow the LD phase delay in cry0 mutants or E-cell-specific CRY rescue flies. CRY rescue in M cells alone resulted in slower phase shifts.
behavioral_consequence: CRY rescue in M cells (via Pdf-gal4) improved phase shift to approximately 3 h/d (statistically significant vs mutants) but was slower than E cell rescue.
ecological_context: Entrainment to environmental light/dark (LD) cycles.
summary: M cells contribute to light entrainment but are less critical than E cells for fast re-entrainment to LD phase shifts.

paper_title: Cryptochrome-Dependent and -Independent Circadian Entrainment Circuits in Drosophila
cell_type: Dorsal Neurons (DN groups: DN1a, DN1p, DN2, DN3)
experimental_stimulus: 8 h LD phase delay
neural_activity: DN1a showed phase shifts similar to LN groups. DN1p subset phase shifted. DN2 and DN3 showed no difference or slow resynchronization in PDP1 cycling.
behavioral_consequence: CRY rescue in DN groups (via Clk4.1M-gal4) did not improve re-entrainment speed compared to cry0 mutants.
ecological_context: Entrainment to environmental light/dark (LD) cycles.
summary: DN groups are not major players in CRY-dependent fast light entrainment to LD phase delays.

paper_title: Cryptochrome-Dependent and -Independent Circadian Entrainment Circuits in Drosophila
cell_type: Visual Photoreceptors (Compound eyes, ocelli, H-B eyelets)
experimental_stimulus: LD cycles; Genetic ablation of photoreceptors
neural_activity: Visual system input mediates residual light response in E cells (PDP1 cycling) in cry0 mutants. This response disappeared when all external photoreceptors were ablated.
behavioral_consequence: Ablation of all external photoreceptors in cry0 mutants abolished synchronization to LD cycles.
ecological_context: Entrainment to environmental light/dark (LD) cycles.
summary: The visual system mediates non-CRY light input to E cells, which is essential for synchronization to LD cycles in the absence of CRY.

paper_title: Metabolic Stress Responses in Drosophila Are Modulated by Brain Neurosecretory Cells That Produce Multiple Neuropeptides
experimental_stimulus: Starvation (water only) and Desiccation (no food or water)
neural_activity: Not measured directly; peptide levels (ITP, DTK, sNPF) quantified via immunocytochemistry
behavioral_consequence: RNAi knockdown of DTK or sNPF reduced survival during desiccation (median 17.5-23h vs 28-33h controls) and starvation (median ~30h vs 39h controls); increased body water loss at desiccation; no change in locomotor activity during starvation
ecological_context: Metabolic stress responses and homeostasis during starvation and desiccation
techniques_used: ['Immunocytochemistry', 'Gal4-UAS RNAi', 'Survival assays', 'Water content measurement', 'Trikinetics locomotor monitoring']
summary: ipc-1 and ipc-2a neurosecretory cells co-express ITP, DTK, and sNPF; RNAi knockdown of DTK or sNPF in these cells reduced survival during metabolic stress (starvation/desiccation) and increased water loss, without affecting locomotor activity at starvation.

paper_title: Functional Analysis of Circadian Pacemaker Neurons in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Light/Dark (LD) cycle; Constant Light (LL) at 500 µW/cm² and 0.25 µW/cm²; *cry^b* mutants
neural_activity: Nuclear PER/TIM at peak of short-period activity component; cytoplasmic at trough. Significant cycling in subcellular location under LL.
behavioral_consequence: Associated with short-period free-running component (22.5h in *cry^b*). Suggests control of both M and E activity bouts.
ecological_context: Adaptation to seasonal changes in day length; helps avoid midday heat in long summer days.
summary: PDF-positive s-LNv cells cycle with the short-period behavioral component and are proposed to control both M and E activity bouts.

paper_title: Functional Analysis of Circadian Pacemaker Neurons in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Constant Light (LL) at 500 µW/cm²; *cry^b* mutants
neural_activity: Nuclear PER/TIM at peak of long-period activity component. Cycles in subcellular location.
behavioral_consequence: Linked to long-period free-running component (25.2h) and E activity bout.
ecological_context: Adaptation to seasonal changes in day length.
summary: 5th s-LNv cycles with the long-period behavioral component and is linked to the E activity bout.

paper_title: Functional Analysis of Circadian Pacemaker Neurons in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Constant Light (LL) at 500 µW/cm²; *cry^b* mutants
neural_activity: More strongly stained with PER/TIM than remaining LNd. Nuclear PER/TIM at long-period activity peak.
behavioral_consequence: Linked to long-period free-running component (25.2h) and E activity bout.
ecological_context: Adaptation to seasonal changes in day length.
summary: Extra LNd shows strong molecular cycling associated with the long-period behavioral component.

paper_title: Functional Analysis of Circadian Pacemaker Neurons in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Constant Light (LL) at 500 µW/cm²; *cry^b* mutants
neural_activity: PER nuclear, TIM cytoplasmic at both time points. No clock protein cycling observed.
behavioral_consequence: Not associated with period splitting or desynchronization.
ecological_context: Not explicitly linked to ecological adaptation in this study.
summary: l-LNv show no molecular cycling under constant light conditions.

paper_title: Functional Analysis of Circadian Pacemaker Neurons in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Constant Light (LL) at 500 µW/cm²; *cry^b* mutants
neural_activity: Variable staining intensity and location (nuclear/cytoplasmic mix). Suggests subclusters. DN2 cycles on Day 1 but not significant on Day 5.
behavioral_consequence: No direct link established to period splitting in this study.
ecological_context: Not explicitly linked to ecological adaptation in this study.
summary: DN1 and DN3 show heterogeneous molecular states suggesting subclusters; no cycling in LL.

paper_title: Functional Analysis of Circadian Pacemaker Neurons in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Constant Light (LL) at 500 µW/cm²; *cry^b* vs *cl^e^ya; cry^b* (eyeless) mutants
neural_activity: N/A (Input to clock)
behavioral_consequence: Required for internal desynchronization and period lengthening/shortening. Absence results in single period (23.8h).
ecological_context: Necessary for adequate timing of M and E activity bouts in long summer days and short winter days.
summary: Compound eyes transmit light information to pacemaker neurons to drive period adaptation.

paper_title: Memory-like states created by the first ethanol experience are encoded into the Drosophila mushroom body learning and memory circuitry in an ethanol-specific manner
experimental_stimulus: Acute sedating ethanol exposure (55% ethanol vapor); Chronic low concentration ethanol exposure (16% ethanol for 48h)
neural_activity: Amnesiac (amn) expression required for Anesthesia-Sensitive Tolerance (AST); Sirt1 expression suppresses a labile trace; GABAA receptor (Rdl) expression promotes chronic tolerance
behavioral_consequence: RNAi against amn in gamma Kenyon cells reduced rapid tolerance (4 hr); RNAi against Sirt1 in gamma Kenyon cells enhanced chronic tolerance (suppressed labile trace) which became insensitive to cold shock after 24 hr; Cold shock reverted enhanced tolerance in Sirt1 mutants early but not late
ecological_context: First ethanol experience creates memory-like states; Sirt1 suppresses formation of a labile trace that consolidates into enhanced chronic tolerance
techniques_used: ['RNA interference (RNAi)', 'Cold shock anesthesia', 'ST50 sedation assay', 'Sirt1 null mutants']

paper_title: Memory-like states created by the first ethanol experience are encoded into the Drosophila mushroom body learning and memory circuitry in an ethanol-specific manner
experimental_stimulus: Acute sedating ethanol exposure; Chronic low concentration ethanol exposure
neural_activity: GABAA receptor (Rdl) expression required for chronic tolerance; Synaptic release dispensable for rapid and chronic tolerance; Protein synthesis dispensable for chronic tolerance
behavioral_consequence: RNAi against Rdl in alpha/beta Kenyon cells reduced chronic tolerance and sedation sensitivity; Inactivation of synaptic release in alpha/beta lobes did not affect rapid tolerance; Dominant negative Mef2 and constitutively active CaMKII in Kenyon cells did not affect chronic tolerance
ecological_context: Chronic tolerance differs from classical long-term memory in that key encoding steps reside outside classical Kenyon cell pathways
techniques_used: ['RNA interference (RNAi)', 'Temperature-sensitive Shibire inactivation', 'ST50 sedation assay', 'Dominant negative constructs']

paper_title: Memory-like states created by the first ethanol experience are encoded into the Drosophila mushroom body learning and memory circuitry in an ethanol-specific manner
experimental_stimulus: Acute sedating ethanol exposure (55% ethanol vapor); Chronic low concentration ethanol exposure
neural_activity: Amnesiac (amn) expression supports AST; NMDA receptor (Nmdar1) expression supports AST; Protein synthesis in all Kenyon cells dispensable for chronic tolerance
behavioral_consequence: RNAi against amn in all Kenyon cells reduced rapid tolerance; RNAi against Nmdar1 in all Kenyon cells decreased rapid tolerance but not chronic tolerance; Blocking new protein synthesis in all adult Kenyon cells did not affect chronic tolerance
ecological_context: First ethanol experience creates memory-like states; Kenyon cells play a less central role than previously thought for chronic tolerance formation and storage
techniques_used: ['RNA interference (RNAi)', 'Ricin toxin protein synthesis inhibition', 'ST50 sedation assay', 'Gal4/UAS expression system']

paper_title: Memory-like states created by the first ethanol experience are encoded into the Drosophila mushroom body learning and memory circuitry in an ethanol-specific manner
experimental_stimulus: Acute sedating ethanol exposure (55% ethanol vapor)
neural_activity: Activity required during ethanol exposure acquisition promotes Anesthesia-Resistant Tolerance (ART); Constitutive GABA and octopamine release limits AST
behavioral_consequence: Inactivation of APL neurons decreased rapid tolerance (4 hr and 24 hr) and increased sedation sensitivity; RNAi against Gad1 (GABA) increased rapid tolerance and sensitivity; RNAi against Tbh (octopamine) increased rapid tolerance
ecological_context: APL neurons regulate the initial development of tolerance during the first ethanol exposure
techniques_used: ['Temperature-sensitive Shibire inactivation', 'RNA interference (RNAi)', 'ST50 sedation assay', 'Cold shock anesthesia']

paper_title: Memory-like states created by the first ethanol experience are encoded into the Drosophila mushroom body learning and memory circuitry in an ethanol-specific manner
experimental_stimulus: Chronic low concentration ethanol exposure (16% ethanol for 48h)
neural_activity: Synaptic release required during chronic ethanol exposure for tolerance; GABA synthesis required; Serotonin synthesis dispensable; CREB dispensable; Protein synthesis required
behavioral_consequence: Inactivation of DPM synaptic release reduced chronic tolerance; RNAi against Gad1 reduced chronic tolerance and sedation sensitivity; RNAi against Ddc (serotonin) did not affect tolerance; Protein synthesis inhibition in DPMs reduced chronic tolerance
ecological_context: DPM neurons hold a protein synthesis dependent step for chronic tolerance encoding
techniques_used: ['Temperature-sensitive Shibire inactivation', 'RNA interference (RNAi)', 'ST50 sedation assay', 'Cold shock anesthesia']

paper_title: A neurotrophin functioning with a Toll regulates structural plasticity in a dopaminergic circuit
experimental_stimulus: Neuronal activation (TrpA1 at 30°C, CsChrimson with 588nm laser)
neural_activity: Increased DNT-2 vesicle production and cleavage (Western blot/GFP counting); Increased Homer+ PSDs (synapse formation); Caused GCaMP increase in PAMs (calcium signaling); Expressed vGlut (glutamatergic)
behavioral_consequence: Increased locomotion speed in open arena; Enhanced appetitive long-term memory (olfactory conditioning)
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed in natural ecological terms; linked to experience-dependent behavior
techniques_used: ['TrpA1 thermogenetics', 'CsChrimson optogenetics', 'Western blot', 'Homer-GCaMP imaging', 'GCaMP6s calcium imaging', 'Behavioral assays']
summary: DNT-2A neurons are glutamatergic; activation increases DNT-2 levels and synapse formation, and modulates locomotion and memory.

paper_title: A neurotrophin functioning with a Toll regulates structural plasticity in a dopaminergic circuit
experimental_stimulus: Activation of DNT-2 neurons (Optogenetics); Genetic knockdown of DNT-2/Toll-6/Kek-6 (RNAi/Mutants)
neural_activity: Increased GCaMP signal upon DNT-2 activation; Reduced cell number/survival with DNT-2/Toll-6 loss (TH staining/Histone-YFP); Expressed Toll-6 and Kek-6
behavioral_consequence: Impaired climbing (startle-induced negative geotaxis) with PAM loss; Enhanced memory with DNT-2 overexpression
ecological_context: Essential for locomotion and learning/memory (experience-dependent behavior)
techniques_used: ['GCaMP6s calcium imaging', 'Immunostaining (Tyrosine Hydroxylase)', 'Cell counting (DeadEasy DAN)', 'Climbing assay', 'Appetitive long-term memory test']
summary: PAMs receive input from DNT-2 neurons; DNT-2/Toll-6 are required for PAM survival. DAN loss impairs climbing and memory.

paper_title: A neurotrophin functioning with a Toll regulates structural plasticity in a dopaminergic circuit
experimental_stimulus: Neuronal activation (TrpA1 at 30°C)
neural_activity: Decreased dendrite complexity and size (structural homeostasis); Increased synapse density (structural synaptic plasticity); Activity-dependent synapse formation requires DNT-2, Toll-6, and Kek-6
behavioral_consequence: Circuit modification associated with altered memory performance
ecological_context: Required for long-term memory
summary: DAL neurons undergo structural homeostasis and synaptic plasticity upon activation, requiring DNT-2, Toll-6, and Kek-6.

paper_title: A neurotrophin functioning with a Toll regulates structural plasticity in a dopaminergic circuit
experimental_stimulus: Knockdown of Toll-6/Kek-6 (RNAi)
neural_activity: Axonal misrouting away from mushroom body peduncle (FlyBow imaging); Expressed Toll-6 and Kek-6
behavioral_consequence: Circuit connectivity required for long-term memory
ecological_context: Required for long-term memory
summary: PPL1 axonal targeting requires Toll-6 and Kek-6; circuit required for memory.

paper_title: A neurotrophin functioning with a Toll regulates structural plasticity in a dopaminergic circuit
cell_type: Kenyon Cells (KCs) and Mushroom Body Output Neurons (MBONs)
experimental_stimulus: None directly stimulated; Receptor expression analysis
neural_activity: Express Toll-6 and Kek-6 receptors (Reporter lines/RNAseq)
behavioral_consequence: Part of associative learning circuit required for learning and long-term memory
ecological_context: Required for associative learning and long-term memory
summary: KCs and MBONs express DNT-2 receptors and are part of the associative learning circuit.

paper_title: Drosophila larvae form appetitive and aversive associative memory in response to thermal conditioning
cell_type: Cool-sensing and warm-sensing neurons (Ir25a-expressing, Dorsal Organ Ganglion)
experimental_stimulus: Thermal conditioning (20°C or 27°C paired with Fructose or NaCl)
neural_activity: Not measured directly; mutant phenotype used to infer function
behavioral_consequence: Ir25a2 mutant larvae failed to show conditioned thermotaxis (navigation index similar to naive controls), whereas wild-type larvae navigated towards/away from conditioned temperatures depending on reward/punishment pairing
ecological_context: Temperature is of vital importance to nearly every animal, especially small, slow ectotherms like the Drosophila larva
summary: Ir25a2 mutant larvae lacking thermal sensing receptors did not form associative thermal memory, indicating Ir25a-expressing cool/warm-sensing neurons are necessary for thermal conditioning, though direct neural activity was not measured.

paper_title: Olfactory sensory neuron population expansions influence projection neuron adaptation and enhance odour tracking
