paper_title: Pain is so close to pleasure: the same dopamine neurons can mediate approach and avoidance in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic stimulation with yellow or red light (CsChrimson)
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation via CsChrimson; no direct neural activity measured
behavioral_consequence: Initially showed consistent preference for light in T-maze (red), Y-maze, and Joystick screens. In Joystick rescreen, showed avoidance of light; valence opposite to original screen.
ecological_context: Flies tested were satiated, unlike starved/thirsty conditions where appetitive DAN functions are typically found.
summary: TH-D1 DANs (PPL1, PPM3, PPM1 clusters) showed variable valence: consistent preference in initial screens but avoidance in Joystick rescreen, suggesting context-dependent function.

paper_title: Pain is so close to pleasure: the same dopamine neurons can mediate approach and avoidance in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic stimulation with yellow or red light (CsChrimson)
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation via CsChrimson; no direct neural activity measured
behavioral_consequence: Initially showed consistent avoidance in T-maze and Y-maze screens. In Joystick rescreen, showed initial avoidance followed by a switch to preference for light over time.
ecological_context: Flies tested were satiated; initial aversive function generalized to other operant experiments, but valence switched over time during prolonged operant control.
summary: TH-D' DANs (PPL1, PPM3, PPM1 clusters) mediated avoidance initially but switched to preference in Joystick rescreen, demonstrating temporal flexibility in valence.

paper_title: Pain is so close to pleasure: the same dopamine neurons can mediate approach and avoidance in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic stimulation with yellow or red light (CsChrimson)
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation via CsChrimson; no direct neural activity measured
behavioral_consequence: Showed consistent attraction to light across all four original screens.
ecological_context: Flies tested were satiated; this line was one of the few showing consistency across screens.
summary: TH-G1 DANs were the only line to show consistent attraction across all four operant screens, though rescreening suggested limited reproducibility.

paper_title: Pain is so close to pleasure: the same dopamine neurons can mediate approach and avoidance in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic stimulation with yellow or red light (CsChrimson)
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation via CsChrimson; no direct neural activity measured
behavioral_consequence: No lines sustained consistent behavioral valence across all experiments. NP47 mediated approach in T-maze (red) and avoidance in Y-maze. Most lines showed weak or inconsistent preferences compared to controls.
ecological_context: Flies tested were satiated; results contradict a 'common currency' dopamine function in flies.
summary: Screening ~50 DAN subpopulations revealed heterogeneous, context-dependent valence with no common currency function in operant self-stimulation.

paper_title: Pain is so close to pleasure: the same dopamine neurons can mediate approach and avoidance in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic stimulation with red light (T-maze)
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation via CsChrimson; no direct neural activity measured
behavioral_consequence: Showed robust avoidance of the arm with stimulating light (positive control).
ecological_context: Bitter taste neurons mediate aversion; used as a validated positive control for operant avoidance.
summary: Gr66a-positive bitter taste neurons mediated robust avoidance in T-maze, serving as a reliable positive control for optogenetic aversion.

paper_title: Pain is so close to pleasure: the same dopamine neurons can mediate approach and avoidance in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic stimulation with yellow or red light (T-maze, Y-maze, Joystick)
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation via CsChrimson; no direct neural activity measured
behavioral_consequence: Showed robust avoidance of the arm/area with stimulating light (positive control); stronger aversion than Gr66a.
ecological_context: Heat sensing neurons mediate aversion; used as a validated positive control for operant avoidance in multiple setups.
summary: Gr28bd+TrpA1-positive heat sensitive neurons mediated robust avoidance across multiple operant setups, validating the optogenetic method.

paper_title: Postsynaptic plasticity of cholinergic synapses underlies the induction and expression of appetitive memories in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Olfactory conditioning (odor paired with sugar or electric shock) in T-maze; focal acetylcholine application in vitro
neural_activity: Blocking neurotransmitter release using temperature-sensitive Shibirets (Shits1) at 32°C abolished signaling. Calcium imaging showed no KC activation by focal acetylcholine to MBON dendrites.
behavioral_consequence: Blocking KC output during training abolished 30-min appetitive memory performance but did not significantly impact 30-min aversive memory performance.
summary: KC neurotransmitter release is required for the acquisition of appetitive memories but is less crucial for aversive memories, as blocking release during training abolished appetitive but not aversive memory performance.

paper_title: Postsynaptic plasticity of cholinergic synapses underlies the induction and expression of appetitive memories in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Odor presentation (Octanol, Methylcyclohexanol), sugar feeding, optogenetic dopamine activation
neural_activity: Dendritic calcium transients measured with GCaMP6f. α5 knockdown increased initial odor-evoked calcium transients. Paired training induced postsynaptic plasticity in vitro and depressed axonal signals in vivo. Knockdown of α5 impaired immediate memory, α2 impaired 3-hr memory.
behavioral_consequence: RNAi knockdown of α5 impaired immediate appetitive memory. RNAi knockdown of α2 impaired 3-hr appetitive memory. Knockdowns did not impact aversive memory. Blocking output during training did not affect memory.
techniques_used: ['RNAi knockdown', 'T-maze memory assay', 'Calcium imaging (dendritic/axonal)', 'Fluorescence recovery after photobleaching (FRAP)', 'Optogenetics (CsChrimson)', 'Explant brain preparation']
summary: Postsynaptic nicotinic acetylcholine receptor subunits α5 and α2 are required for induction and expression of appetitive memories in M4/6 MBONs respectively, with α5 acting upstream of α2.

paper_title: Postsynaptic plasticity of cholinergic synapses underlies the induction and expression of appetitive memories in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Repeated odor application (familiarity learning paradigm)
neural_activity: Dendritic calcium transients decreased over repeated odor presentations. α2 subunit dynamics (FRAP) increased after training.
behavioral_consequence: Control flies stopped grooming upon repeated odor exposure. α5 knockdown prevented grooming cessation. α2 knockdown delayed grooming cessation.
ecological_context: Allows the animal to adapt its behavioral responses to new odors.
techniques_used: ['Grooming behavior assay', 'Calcium imaging', 'Fluorescence recovery after photobleaching (FRAP)', 'RNAi knockdown']
summary: α5 and α2 nAChR subunits in α'3 MBONs are required for non-associative familiarity learning, with α5 governing induction and α2 dynamics accompanying postsynaptic depression.

paper_title: Postsynaptic plasticity of cholinergic synapses underlies the induction and expression of appetitive memories in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Red light pulses (optogenetic activation)
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation induced dopamine release. Paired with odor to induce plasticity.
behavioral_consequence: Paired with odor to induce postsynaptic plasticity in MBONs.
summary: DAN activation paired with odor application is sufficient to induce postsynaptic plasticity in MBONs, supporting the role of postsynaptic mechanisms in memory storage.

paper_title: Regulation of Drosophila Long-Term Courtship Memory by Ecdysis Triggering Hormone
experimental_stimulus: Genetic manipulation of secretion (ablation via hid,rpr; block via shibire; activation via dTrpA1)
neural_activity: ETH secretion manipulated; in vitro functional imaging not reported for Inka cells themselves in this study
behavioral_consequence: Ablation or secretion block of Inka cells suppresses long-term courtship memory (LTM); thermogenetic activation of Inka cells during training enhances LTM and reduces the minimum training period required
ecological_context: Critical for survival and propagation; enables males to suppress courtship towards mated females after rejection to avoid wasted reproductive effort
techniques_used: ['Genetic ablation (UAS-hid,rpr)', 'Temperature-sensitive secretion block (UAS-Shits)', 'Thermogenetic activation (UAS-dTrpA1)', 'Courtship conditioning assay (Courtship Index measurement)']
summary: Inka cells release ecdysis triggering hormone (ETH) which is an obligatory enabler of long-term courtship memory; secretion is necessary for LTM and enhanced activity improves memory performance.

paper_title: Regulation of Drosophila Long-Term Courtship Memory by Ecdysis Triggering Hormone
experimental_stimulus: ETH receptor (ETHR) knockdown (RNAi); Juvenile Hormone receptor (Met) knockdown (RNAi); ETH application
neural_activity: ETH application mobilizes calcium in MB γ lobe neuropils (GCaMP5 imaging)
behavioral_consequence: ETHR knockdown or Met knockdown in MB γ lobe impairs courtship LTM; ETHR silencing does not affect short-term memory (STM)
ecological_context: Part of the neural circuit for persistent behavioral changes resulting from aversive social experience (rejection by mated females)
techniques_used: ['RNAi knockdown (GAL4/UAS system)', 'Functional in vitro Ca2+ imaging (GCaMP5)', 'Courtship conditioning assay']
summary: ETH and JH signaling converge on MB γ lobe neurons; ETH mobilizes calcium here and both ETHR and Met expression are required for courtship LTM formation.

paper_title: Regulation of Drosophila Long-Term Courtship Memory by Ecdysis Triggering Hormone
experimental_stimulus: ETH receptor (ETHR) knockdown (RNAi); ETH application
neural_activity: ETH application mobilizes calcium in DAL neurons (GCaMP5 imaging)
behavioral_consequence: ETHR knockdown in DAL neurons impairs courtship LTM performance
ecological_context: Involved in memory consolidation and retrieval of social experience-based behavioral modification
techniques_used: ['RNAi knockdown (G0338 and G0431 drivers)', 'Functional in vitro Ca2+ imaging (GCaMP5)', 'Courtship conditioning assay']
summary: ETH targets DAL neurons directly via ETHR to regulate courtship LTM; ETH induces calcium mobilization in these neurons.

paper_title: Regulation of Drosophila Long-Term Courtship Memory by Ecdysis Triggering Hormone
experimental_stimulus: ETH receptor (ETHR) knockdown (RNAi); JH analog methoprene treatment
neural_activity: ETH signaling regulates JH production; calcium mobilization in CA mentioned in context of previous work, JH levels measured indirectly via deficiency phenotype
behavioral_consequence: ETHR knockdown in CA creates JH deficiency which suppresses LTM; memory performance rescued by topical JH analog (methoprene)
ecological_context: Endocrine state determines learning and memory; hormonal convergence enables social context-dependent behavioral plasticity
summary: ETH regulates courtship LTM indirectly by promoting JH production in the Corpus Allatum; JH is necessary for LTM maintenance.

paper_title: Regulation of Drosophila Long-Term Courtship Memory by Ecdysis Triggering Hormone
experimental_stimulus: Juvenile Hormone receptor (Met and gce) knockdown (RNAi)
neural_activity: Not explicitly measured in this study
behavioral_consequence: Met/gce silencing in TH-positive (dopaminergic) neurons does not affect courtship LTM performance
ecological_context: Distinguishes parallel pathways for STM and LTM; STM requires JH receptors in DA neurons but LTM does not
summary: JH receptors in dopaminergic neurons are required for short-term memory but not long-term courtship memory.

paper_title: Switch-like and persistent memory formation in individual Drosophila larvae
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson + Red LEDs) paired with Carbon Dioxide (CO2) presentation; Unpaired CO2 presentation; Repeated unrewarded CO2 presentation (extinction); Massed vs. Spaced training protocols
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation via CsChrimson using red LEDs (15-second intervals). Endogenous neural activity not directly measured in this study.
behavioral_consequence: Paired activation with CO2 eliminates innate avoidance (larvae become indifferent to CO2). Unpaired or reverse-paired activation does not change preference. Repeated unrewarded CO2 exposure extinguishes learned indifference. Massed training forms Cycloheximide-insensitive memory; Spaced training forms Cycloheximide-sensitive Long-Term Memory (LTM) that persists overnight.
ecological_context: Associative learning allows animals to use past experience to predict future events, such as changes in environmental conditions. CO2 is innately aversive, likely signaling predators or decay.
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics', 'Y-maze behavioral assay', 'Computer vision tracking', 'Genetic manipulation (Gal4/UAS)', 'Cycloheximide feeding', 'Overnight memory testing']
summary: DAN-i1 neurons act as a reward signal; optogenetic activation paired with CO2 presentation eliminates innate CO2 avoidance in larvae. Learning is switch-like (all-or-none) and can form overnight memories dependent on training protocol (massed vs. spaced) and protein synthesis.

paper_title: Switch-like and persistent memory formation in individual Drosophila larvae
experimental_stimulus: Exposure to Carbon Dioxide (CO2) at various concentrations (6.5% to 18%); Genetic loss-of-function (Gr63a[1] mutant)
neural_activity: Not directly measured. Function inferred via genetic mutant analysis (Gr63a[1] lacking functional CO2 receptor).
behavioral_consequence: Wild-type larvae innately avoid CO2 (choosing CO2 channel ~25% of the time). Gr63a[1] mutant larvae are indifferent to CO2, confirming Gr63a mediates CO2 detection and avoidance.
ecological_context: Innate avoidance of CO2 suggests these neurons detect ecologically relevant cues (e.g., predators, decay) to guide navigation away from harmful environments.
summary: Gr63a olfactory sensory neurons mediate innate CO2 avoidance; larvae lacking functional Gr63a receptors show no avoidance of CO2.

paper_title: Switch-like and persistent memory formation in individual Drosophila larvae
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson + Red LEDs) paired with CO2 presentation
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation via CsChrimson using red LEDs.
behavioral_consequence: Activation of the 58E02 driver line did not produce an increased preference for CO2 compared to DAN-i1 activation alone. Training reduced avoidance but did not induce attraction.
ecological_context: Same as DAN-i1 (appetitive reinforcement in associative learning context).
summary: Activation of the 58E02 driver line (including DAN-i1, DAN-h1, DAN-j1) during training reduces CO2 avoidance but does not produce stronger attraction than DAN-i1 activation alone.

paper_title: Voluntary intake of psychoactive substances is regulated by the dopamine receptor Dop1R1 in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Ethanol exposure (reward context)
neural_activity: Function tested via blockade of neurotransmission; activity not directly measured in this study.
behavioral_consequence: Blockade of neurotransmission abolished the acquired ethanol preference (Fig. 4c).
ecological_context: Part of the reward circuit conveying natural reward such as sugar or water to the mushroom body.
summary: PAM cluster dopamine neurons are necessary for acquired ethanol preference; blocking their neurotransmission abolishes this preference.

paper_title: Voluntary intake of psychoactive substances is regulated by the dopamine receptor Dop1R1 in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Repeated ethanol intake (3 days) vs Methamphetamine intake
neural_activity: Dop1R1 protein level significantly increased in the medial lobe of the mushroom body after ethanol intake, but not after methamphetamine intake (Fig. 5).
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of Dop1R1 in the mushroom body abolished acquired ethanol preference and suppressed methamphetamine preference; genetic overexpression of Dop1R1 enhanced ethanol preference (Fig. 4b,e; 5e).
ecological_context: Ethanol preference may function as an indicator of fermented rotten fruit, a major source of ethanol in the wild.
summary: Dop1R1 protein levels in the mushroom body increase after repeated ethanol intake, and Dop1R1 signaling is required for acquired ethanol preference and basal methamphetamine preference.

paper_title: Voluntary intake of psychoactive substances is regulated by the dopamine receptor Dop1R1 in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Ethanol and Methamphetamine exposure
neural_activity: Not measured directly.
behavioral_consequence: DopEcR mutant flies showed continuously high preference for both ethanol and methamphetamine, suggesting a role in drug aversion (Fig. 4a,d).
ecological_context: May mediate aversive effects of drugs, such as ethanol sedation, acting as a 'brake' for further drug intake.
summary: DopEcR signaling appears to suppress drug intake, as mutants show higher preference for ethanol and methamphetamine.

paper_title: Differential conditioning produces merged long-term memory in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Aversive differential conditioning (3-Octanol or 4-methylcyclohexanol paired with 12 pulses of 60 V electric shock as CS+; unpaired odor as CS–)
neural_activity: Synaptic output blocked using UAS-Shibirets1 during training
behavioral_consequence: Blocking output impaired formation of merged long-term memory (mLTM), resulting in loss of avoidance to both CS+ and CS–. Blocking PAM DANs had no effect.
ecological_context: Necessary for encoding merged long-term memory guiding avoidance during occasional threatening experiences (single-trial training)
summary: PPL1 DANs are required for mLTM formation; their synaptic output during single-trial aversive conditioning is necessary for subsequent avoidance of both shock-paired and unpaired odors.

paper_title: Differential conditioning produces merged long-term memory in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Aversive differential conditioning
neural_activity: Synaptic output blocked using UAS-Shibirets1 during training
behavioral_consequence: Blocking output did not affect mLTM formation (avoidance of CS+ and CS– remained intact)
summary: PAM DANs are not required for the formation of merged long-term memory following single-trial aversive conditioning.

paper_title: Differential conditioning produces merged long-term memory in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Odor exposure during memory retrieval test
neural_activity: Synaptic output blocked using UAS-Shibirets1 during testing
behavioral_consequence: Blocking output impaired mLTM retrieval (reduced avoidance of CS+ and CS–). Blocking α'β' or γ KCs had no significant effect.
ecological_context: Required for the expression of merged long-term memory guiding avoidance during occasional threatening experiences
summary: αβ KCs are necessary for mLTM retrieval; their synaptic output is required to guide avoidance of both CS+ and CS–.

paper_title: Differential conditioning produces merged long-term memory in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Odor exposure during memory retrieval test
neural_activity: Synaptic output blocked using UAS-Shibirets1 during testing
behavioral_consequence: Blocking output had no significant effect on mLTM retrieval
summary: α'β' KCs are not required for the retrieval of merged long-term memory.

paper_title: Differential conditioning produces merged long-term memory in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Odor exposure during memory retrieval test
neural_activity: Synaptic output blocked using UAS-Shibirets1 during testing
behavioral_consequence: Blocking output had no significant effect on mLTM retrieval
summary: γ KCs are not required for the retrieval of merged long-term memory.

paper_title: Differential conditioning produces merged long-term memory in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Presentation of CS+ odor, CS– odor, and novel odor
neural_activity: Depressed odor-evoked calcium responses (jGCaMP7f) to CS+ and CS– 1 day after training compared to naive. Synaptic output blocked using UAS-Shibirets1 or Kir2.1 during testing
behavioral_consequence: Blocking output impaired mLTM expression (reduced avoidance of CS+ and CS–). Depressed responses signaled the mLTM
ecological_context: Signals merged aversive memory for single-trial threats, facilitating avoidance of all potentially dangerous cues
summary: α2sc MBONs express mLTM via depressed responses to CS+ and CS–; their output is necessary for avoidance behavior.

paper_title: Differential conditioning produces merged long-term memory in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Odor exposure during memory retrieval test
neural_activity: Synaptic output blocked using UAS-Shibirets1 during testing
behavioral_consequence: Blocking output did not affect mLTM expression
summary: α3 MBONs are not required for the expression of merged long-term memory.

paper_title: The Drosophila Split Gal4 System for Neural Circuit Mapping
experimental_stimulus: Genetic activation
neural_activity: Activation of a single pair of neurons
behavioral_consequence: Command wing expansion in newly eclosed flies
ecological_context: Post-emergence wing hardening and expansion
summary: Activation of a single pair of Bursicon-expressing SEZ neurons is sufficient to command wing expansion in newly eclosed flies.

paper_title: The Drosophila Split Gal4 System for Neural Circuit Mapping
experimental_stimulus: Visual motion stimuli
neural_activity: Response properties characterized
behavioral_consequence: Four of 12 types contribute to motion detection
ecological_context: Visual processing and motion detection
summary: Activity suppression experiments using cell-type-specific drivers established that four of 12 lamina neuron types contribute to motion detection.

paper_title: The Drosophila Split Gal4 System for Neural Circuit Mapping
experimental_stimulus: Visual stimuli (looming)
neural_activity: Visual responses to looming
behavioral_consequence: Activation triggers moonwalker-like phenotype (backward walking)
ecological_context: Evasive locomotor responses to threats
summary: LC16 neurons mediate visual responses to looming and are sufficient to activate moonwalker descending neurons to induce backward walking.

paper_title: The Drosophila Split Gal4 System for Neural Circuit Mapping
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic/Genetic activation
neural_activity: Activation induces backward locomotion
behavioral_consequence: Evokes backward walking in adults; backward crawling ('mooncrawler') in larvae
ecological_context: Evasive locomotor responses to visual and mechanosensory input
summary: MDNs act as central coordinators of evasive locomotor responses; activation induces backward walking/crawling in both adults and larvae.

paper_title: The Drosophila Split Gal4 System for Neural Circuit Mapping
experimental_stimulus: Genetic activation/silencing
neural_activity: Facilitates backward walking
behavioral_consequence: Inhibits the program for forward walking
ecological_context: Reversal of locomotion direction
summary: MAN neurons facilitate backward walking by inhibiting the forward walking program.

paper_title: The Drosophila Split Gal4 System for Neural Circuit Mapping
experimental_stimulus: Mechanosensory input (touch)
neural_activity: Activated by contact with barrier
behavioral_consequence: Mediates mechanosensitive input to MDNs to govern reversal of walking
ecological_context: Reversal of walking direction when confronting obstacles
summary: TLA neurons mediate mechanosensitive input to MDNs to govern reversal of walking when confronting a dead end.

paper_title: The Drosophila Split Gal4 System for Neural Circuit Mapping
experimental_stimulus: UV light
neural_activity: Substrates governing attraction to UV light
behavioral_consequence: Attraction to UV light
ecological_context: UV preference and spectral discrimination
summary: Dm8 amacrine neurons are the substrates governing flies' attraction to UV light.

paper_title: The Drosophila Split Gal4 System for Neural Circuit Mapping
experimental_stimulus: Odor presentation
neural_activity: Receive input from Kenyon Cells and translate into approach/avoidant behaviors
behavioral_consequence: Approach or avoidant behaviors based on learned valence
ecological_context: Experience-dependent responses to odors
summary: MBONs receive synaptic input from Kenyon Cells and broadly translate it into approach or avoidant behaviors associated with rewards/punishments.

paper_title: The Drosophila Split Gal4 System for Neural Circuit Mapping
experimental_stimulus: Environmental conditions (reward/aversion)
neural_activity: Reflects the rewarding or aversive impact of environmental conditions
behavioral_consequence: Modulates KC-MBON synapses to associate rewards/punishments with odors
ecological_context: Associative learning and memory formation
summary: DANs modulate KC-MBON synapses, associating rewards and punishments with particular odors in the MB network.

paper_title: The Drosophila Split Gal4 System for Neural Circuit Mapping
experimental_stimulus: Odorants and pheromones
neural_activity: Display stereotyped response profiles to odorants
behavioral_consequence: Innate behavioral responses to odors and pheromones
ecological_context: Genetic encoding of olfactory information
summary: LHONs display stereotyped response profiles to odorants consistent with innate encoding of olfactory information.

paper_title: The Drosophila Split Gal4 System for Neural Circuit Mapping
experimental_stimulus: Odor plumes
neural_activity: Required for tracking odor plumes
behavioral_consequence: Neurons involved in chemotaxis and stopping behavior
ecological_context: Tracking odor plumes in larvae
summary: PDM-DN neurons are identified in a synaptic suppression screen as neurons involved in tracking odor plumes in larvae.

paper_title: The Drosophila Split Gal4 System for Neural Circuit Mapping
experimental_stimulus: Genetic manipulation
neural_activity: Inhibitory neuron in SEZ
behavioral_consequence: Blocks forward locomotion
ecological_context: Regulation of forward crawling
summary: Pair1 neurons are inhibitory neurons in the SEZ that block forward locomotion by inhibiting A27 h premotor neurons.

paper_title: The Drosophila Split Gal4 System for Neural Circuit Mapping
experimental_stimulus: Genetic manipulation
neural_activity: Excitatory premotor neurons
behavioral_consequence: Required for forward peristalsis
ecological_context: Larval forward crawling
summary: A27 h neurons are excitatory premotor neurons required for forward peristalsis and are connected to known motor neurons.

paper_title: The Drosophila Split Gal4 System for Neural Circuit Mapping
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation
neural_activity: Diverse projections to motor neuropils
behavioral_consequence: Selectively produced walking, tapping, reaching, or grooming phenotypes
ecological_context: Sensorimotor control and behavioral output
summary: A collection of 133 DN drivers targets 54 distinct types; individual activation produces specific motor behaviors like walking, tapping, reaching, or grooming.

paper_title: Social attraction in Drosophila is regulated by the mushroom body and serotonergic system
experimental_stimulus: Social cues (visual and olfactory), social experience (group-reared vs isolated flies)
neural_activity: CaLexA imaging revealed higher fluorescence intensity in group-reared flies compared to isolated flies in the calyx region
behavioral_consequence: Silencing with TNT dramatically reduced social approach behavior; optogenetic activation with CsChrimson elevated levels of social attraction
ecological_context: Social attraction is ubiquitous and robust in Drosophilidae; active social gathering may increase the likelihood of finding food and mating, as well as the spreading of information
summary: KCγ neurons in the mushroom body exhibit increased activity after social experience and are necessary for social approach; their activation enhances motivation for social affiliation.

paper_title: Social attraction in Drosophila is regulated by the mushroom body and serotonergic system
experimental_stimulus: Social approach paradigm
neural_activity: Not directly measured; silenced to test function
behavioral_consequence: TNT-induced silencing resulted in decreased social approach behavior
ecological_context: Serotonin system critical for social affinity, similar to mechanisms in humans and rodents
summary: Serotonergic neurons are required for normal social approach behavior in Drosophila.

paper_title: Social attraction in Drosophila is regulated by the mushroom body and serotonergic system
experimental_stimulus: Social approach paradigm
neural_activity: Silenced to test function
behavioral_consequence: Social approach behavior was dramatically decreased when silencing DPM neurons with TNT
ecological_context: DPM neurons suggested as a source of serotonin to the peduncles and lobes
summary: DPM neurons contribute to social motivation, likely through serotonin modulation.

paper_title: Social attraction in Drosophila is regulated by the mushroom body and serotonergic system
experimental_stimulus: Visual cues (tested in anosmic flies)
neural_activity: Synaptic connections with KCγ visualized via t-GRASP in accessory calyx
behavioral_consequence: Silencing abolished social approach behavior under visual-only conditions
ecological_context: Relays visual information to the mushroom body for social attraction
summary: F5 neurons in the fan-shaped body relay visual inputs to the mushroom body and are necessary for visual-mediated social approach.

paper_title: Social attraction in Drosophila is regulated by the mushroom body and serotonergic system
experimental_stimulus: Olfactory cues (tested in darkness)
neural_activity: Synaptic connections with KCγ visualized via t-GRASP in main calyx
behavioral_consequence: Silencing significantly decreased social attraction under olfactory-only conditions
ecological_context: Relays olfactory information to the mushroom body for social attraction
summary: KCα/β surface neurons relay olfactory inputs to the mushroom body and are necessary for olfactory-mediated social approach.

paper_title: Social attraction in Drosophila is regulated by the mushroom body and serotonergic system
experimental_stimulus: cVA pheromone (tested in darkness)
neural_activity: Silenced to test function
behavioral_consequence: Social approach response was strongly suppressed when Or67d receptor neurons were silenced
ecological_context: Involved in tracking conspecific social cues and deposits of other flies
summary: Or67d neurons mediate cVA perception and are essential for social approach behavior.

paper_title: Availability of food determines the need for sleep in memory consolidation
experimental_stimulus: Appetitive olfactory conditioning (sucrose reward + odor), post-training in fed vs. starved conditions
neural_activity: Calcium imaging (CaLexA) showed reduced activity in trained-starved flies compared to trained-fed. Thermogenetic activation (UAS-TrpA1) resulted in substantial sleep gain. Inactivation (UAS-shibirets1) eliminated post-training sleep increase in fed flies.
behavioral_consequence: Required for sleep-dependent long-term memory in fed flies (silencing impaired memory). Dispensable for memory in starved flies. Promotes sleep when activated. Activity required for post-training sleep increase in fed flies.
ecological_context: Food availability induces sleep-dependent memory formation. Starvation triggers a distinct mechanism to allow foraging (wakefulness) while preserving memory capability for survival.
summary: Anterior/posterior α’/β’ neurons mediate sleep-dependent memory in fed flies and promote sleep. Their activity is reduced in starved flies.

paper_title: Availability of food determines the need for sleep in memory consolidation
experimental_stimulus: Appetitive olfactory conditioning (sucrose reward + odor), post-training in fed vs. starved conditions
neural_activity: Calcium imaging (CaLexA) showed increased activity after training in starved flies. Thermogenetic activation (UAS-TrpA1) significantly reduced sleep.
behavioral_consequence: Required for sleep-independent long-term memory in starved flies (silencing impaired memory). Dispensable for memory in fed flies.
ecological_context: Starvation allows foraging activity at the expense of sleep, yet memory is preserved via sleep-independent circuit.
summary: Medial α’/β’ neurons mediate sleep-independent memory in starved flies and reduce sleep when activated.

paper_title: Availability of food determines the need for sleep in memory consolidation
experimental_stimulus: Appetitive olfactory conditioning (sucrose reward + odor), post-training in fed vs. starved conditions
neural_activity: Inactivation (UAS-shibirets1) impaired memory consolidation.
behavioral_consequence: Required for long-term memory in fed flies. Dispensable for memory in starved flies.
ecological_context: Circuit selection determined by hunger level to switch between sleep-dependent and sleep-independent memory.
summary: MB-MV1 dopaminergic neurons are required for long-term memory in fed flies but not starved flies.

paper_title: Availability of food determines the need for sleep in memory consolidation
experimental_stimulus: Appetitive olfactory conditioning (sucrose reward + odor), post-training in fed vs. starved conditions
neural_activity: Inactivation (UAS-shibirets1) impaired memory consolidation.
behavioral_consequence: Required for long-term memory in starved flies. Dispensable for memory in fed flies.
ecological_context: Circuit selection determined by hunger level to switch between sleep-dependent and sleep-independent memory.
summary: MB-MP1 dopaminergic neurons are required for long-term memory in starved flies but not fed flies.

paper_title: Availability of food determines the need for sleep in memory consolidation
experimental_stimulus: Appetitive olfactory conditioning (sucrose reward + odor), post-training in fed vs. starved conditions
neural_activity: Inactivation (UAS-shibirets1) impaired memory. Identified as sleep-promoting neurons.
behavioral_consequence: Required for long-term memory in fed flies. Dispensable for memory in starved flies.
ecological_context: Fed flies form sleep-dependent memory requiring this circuit.
summary: MBON-γ2α’1 neurons are required for sleep-dependent memory in fed flies and promote sleep.

paper_title: Availability of food determines the need for sleep in memory consolidation
experimental_stimulus: Appetitive olfactory conditioning (sucrose reward + odor), post-training in fed vs. starved conditions
neural_activity: Inactivation (UAS-shibirets1) impaired memory.
behavioral_consequence: Required for long-term memory in starved flies. Dispensable for memory in fed flies.
ecological_context: Starved flies form sleep-independent memory requiring this circuit.
summary: MBON-γ1pedc neurons are required for sleep-independent memory in starved flies.

paper_title: Availability of food determines the need for sleep in memory consolidation
experimental_stimulus: Appetitive olfactory conditioning, post-training in starved vs. fed conditions
neural_activity: Loss of NPF signaling (npf RNAi in NPF-positive cells, npfr mutants) increased sleep quantity and quality post-training in starved flies.
behavioral_consequence: Starved flies lacking NPF signaling required sleep for long-term memory (switched from sleep-independent to sleep-dependent). Memory impaired by sleep deprivation in these mutants.
ecological_context: Hunger signals (NPF) determine circuit selection to allow foraging (wake) while retaining memory capability in changing environments.
summary: NPF signaling is essential for sleep-independent memory in starved flies; loss of NPF renders starved flies dependent on sleep for memory.

paper_title: Transsynaptic mapping of Drosophila mushroom body output neurons
ecological_context: Integrates environmental cues with associated valence (Highlight section)
summary: MBONs identified as sending divergent projections to other MBONs, the fan shaped body (FSB), and the lateral accessory lobe (LAL). Inhibitory and excitatory MBONs converge on the same genetic subset of FSB and LAL neurons.

paper_title: Transsynaptic mapping of Drosophila mushroom body output neurons
summary: Identified as a downstream target receiving convergent input from MBONs (both inhibitory and excitatory).

paper_title: Transsynaptic mapping of Drosophila mushroom body output neurons
summary: Identified as a downstream target receiving convergent input from MBONs (both inhibitory and excitatory).

paper_title: A gut-secreted peptide controls arousability through modulation of dopaminergic neurons in the brain
experimental_stimulus: Dietary protein (peptone supplementation)
neural_activity: Increased intracellular calcium activity (measured with CaLexA reporter) and increased CCHa1 peptide and mRNA levels in the posterior midgut.
behavioral_consequence: Conditional silencing (shits) caused hyper-arousability (easier to wake from sleep) and increased responsiveness during wakefulness. Conditional activation (TrpA1) caused hypo-arousability and decreased responsiveness during wakefulness. Effects were CCHa1-dependent.
ecological_context: Gut cells assess food quality; abundance of food molecules signals an opportunity to sleep more deeply since there is no need to forage.
summary: Gut enteroendocrine cells increase CCHa1 production in response to dietary protein, which suppresses sensory arousability from sleep and wakefulness.

paper_title: A gut-secreted peptide controls arousability through modulation of dopaminergic neurons in the brain
experimental_stimulus: Dietary protein (peptone feeding); Mechanical vibrations
neural_activity: Protein-enriched diet increased GFP signal (CaLexA) in a CCHa1R-dependent manner. Mechanical vibrations inhibited neuron activity (decreased CaLexA signal).
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of CCHa1R increased arousability during sleep. Conditional silencing (shits) increased arousability; activation (TrpA1) decreased arousability. Knockdown of Tyrosine Hydroxylase (TH) increased arousability. No effect on sleep duration or responsiveness during wakefulness.
ecological_context: Nutritional status signals opportunities for deep sleep; integrates physiological signals with sensory information to tune arousal thresholds.
summary: PAMMB441B dopaminergic neurons integrate gut-derived CCHa1 and mechanical sensory information to regulate sleep arousability without affecting sleep duration.

paper_title: A gut-secreted peptide controls arousability through modulation of dopaminergic neurons in the brain
experimental_stimulus: None explicitly tested as input
neural_activity: Not measured
behavioral_consequence: Conditional silencing (shits) increased arousability during sleep. Conditional activation (TrpA1) decreased arousability. Modulated sleep duration bidirectionally. No effect on responsiveness during wakefulness.
ecological_context: Represents convergence of circuits controlling sleep amount and sleep depth.
summary: MBONMB083C neurons modulate sleep arousability and duration, integrating inputs from upstream dopaminergic circuits.

paper_title: Individual bitter-sensing neurons in Drosophila exhibit both ON and OFF responses that influence synaptic plasticity
experimental_stimulus: Bitter compounds (quinine, denatonium, lobeline, caffeine, L-canavanine) applied to labellum; sugar and osmolarity controls
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP) showed strong ON responses (stimulus onset) and OFF responses (stimulus offset). OFF response magnitude often similar or larger than ON. ON response exhibited rapid adaptation. OFF response persisted when synaptic transmission was silenced (Shits) and required bitter receptors (Gr33a, Gr66a, Gr93a).
behavioral_consequence: Not experimentally tested in this study. Discussion suggests potential role in suppressing feeding or reinforcing aversive learning, but behavior was not measured.
ecological_context: Natural feeding involves brief, repeated labellar contacts. Labellar neurons signal bitter onset and offset during food sampling.
techniques_used: ['two-photon calcium imaging', 'Shibire synaptic silencing', 'receptor mutants', 'optogenetics (Chrimson for control)']
summary: Individual labellar bitter neurons exhibit cell-intrinsic ON and OFF calcium responses to bitter compounds, unlike sugar or leg bitter neurons which show only ON responses.

paper_title: Individual bitter-sensing neurons in Drosophila exhibit both ON and OFF responses that influence synaptic plasticity
experimental_stimulus: Bitter compounds (quinine, denatonium, lobeline) applied to leg (tarsus)
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP) showed strong ON responses but no OFF responses. ON response adaptation was far less than in labellar bitter neurons.
behavioral_consequence: Not experimentally tested in this study.
ecological_context: Legs are used for continuous substrate sampling; signaling presence/absence of bitter may mediate locomotor repulsion.
summary: Leg bitter neurons encode bitter stimuli with sustained ON responses and lack OFF responses, unlike labellar bitter neurons.

paper_title: Individual bitter-sensing neurons in Drosophila exhibit both ON and OFF responses that influence synaptic plasticity
experimental_stimulus: Bitter stimulation of labellum or leg; Optogenetic activation (Chrimson) timed to mimic ON/OFF responses
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP) showed both ON and OFF responses to labellar bitter, but only ON responses to leg bitter. Dynamics mirrored labellar bitter neurons (ligand and experience-dependent).
behavioral_consequence: Direct behavioral consequences not tested. Optogenetic mimicry of timing induced opposing synaptic plasticity in MBONs (ON response drove depression, OFF response drove facilitation).
ecological_context: Mediate aversive learning; timing of activity (ON vs OFF) determines direction of synaptic plasticity during associative learning.
summary: PPL1 DANs propagate labellar bitter ON/OFF responses, and the presence of OFF responses influences timing-based synaptic plasticity in mushroom body output neurons.

paper_title: Individual bitter-sensing neurons in Drosophila exhibit both ON and OFF responses that influence synaptic plasticity
experimental_stimulus: Odor (CS+) paired with bitter onset or offset
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging of dendrites showed odor response changes after pairing. Pairing with bitter onset enhanced MBON response (facilitation). Pairing with bitter offset depressed MBON response (depression).
behavioral_consequence: Direct behavioral consequences not tested. Changes in calcium response indicate synaptic plasticity.
ecological_context: Mushroom body output neurons encode valence and guide memory-based action selection.
summary: Repeated pairing of odor with bitter onset versus offset leads to opposing effects on g2a'1 MBON synaptic plasticity, driven by the bitter OFF response.

paper_title: Rac1 Impairs Forgetting-Induced Cellular Plasticity in Mushroom Body Output Neurons
experimental_stimulus: Aversive olfactory conditioning (MCH odor paired with 90V electric shocks); intrinsic time passage (5, 15, 30 min); interfering electric shocks (12, 90V); reversal learning (CS+ and CS- swapped)
neural_activity: In vivo calcium imaging (GCaMP6f) showed aversive conditioning induced a depression of calcium responses to the trained odor (MCH) at 5 min. In control flies, responses recovered to preconditioning levels by 15-30 min (intrinsic forgetting). Responses were restored by interfering shocks or reversal learning. Expression of dominant negative Rac1 (RacN17) in Kenyon Cells impaired this recovery (memory trace persisted).
ecological_context: The paper explicitly discusses forgetting as advantageous in a dynamic environment to prevent memory saturation, allow efficient recall of useful memories, engender behavioral flexibility, and required for generalization.
techniques_used: ['In vivo calcium imaging (confocal microscopy)', 'Genetic manipulation (UAS-RacN17, Gal4 drivers)', 'Odor and shock presentation']
summary: MBON-γ1pedc>α/β shows calcium response depression following aversive conditioning that recovers via intrinsic forgetting, shock-induced forgetting, or reversal learning. Genetic inhibition of Rac1 in Kenyon Cells impairs this restoration of plasticity.

paper_title: Rac1 Impairs Forgetting-Induced Cellular Plasticity in Mushroom Body Output Neurons
experimental_stimulus: Genetic expression of dominant negative Rac1 (RacN17) in adulthood (R13F02-gal4 driver)
ecological_context: The paper discusses forgetting as advantageous for behavioral flexibility and generalization in a dynamic environment.
summary: Expression of dominant negative Rac1 in Kenyon Cells impairs the restoration of depressed odor responses in MBON-γ1pedc>α/β following aversive conditioning, indicating Rac1 is required for forgetting-induced cellular plasticity.

paper_title: A neural signature of choice under sensory conflict in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Sweet (50 mM sucrose) and bittersweet (500 mM sucrose + 1 mM quinine) taste applied to forelegs; water applied to forelegs
neural_activity: Calcium imaging (GCaMP6f) showed FBl6 neurons were strongly inhibited by the taste of the rejected food choice. Inhibition depended on hunger state (naïve food-deprived vs fed) and decision outcome (chose sweet vs chose bittersweet). Inhibition by water was observed in flies that chose sweet or neither, but not those that chose bittersweet.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic inhibition (GtACR1) shifted food preference towards bittersweet. Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson) did not affect preference. Knockdown of Lkr, AstA-R1, or DH44-R1 receptors in FBl6 shifted preference towards bittersweet.
ecological_context: Foraging on decomposing fruits where flies must balance obtaining essential nutrition (high sucrose) with avoiding toxins (quinine).
summary: FBl6 neurons encode food choice through inhibition by the rejected taste stimulus, modulated by hunger state and experience. Optogenetic inhibition of FBl6 shifts preference towards bittersweet food.

paper_title: A neural signature of choice under sensory conflict in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: None (optogenetically manipulated)
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation suppressed feeding in food-deprived flies.
behavioral_consequence: Activation suppressed feeding (consumption decreased). Inhibition shifted preference towards bittersweet (higher calorie) food. RNAi knockdown of Lk in Lk neurons abolished feeding suppression during activation.
ecological_context: Regulation of metabolic state information (hunger/satiety) during foraging.
summary: Lk neurons encode metabolic state; their activation suppresses feeding, while inhibition shifts preference towards higher calorie food.

paper_title: A neural signature of choice under sensory conflict in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: None (optogenetically manipulated)
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation and inhibition tested.
behavioral_consequence: Activation shifted preference towards sweet food. Inhibition shifted preference towards bittersweet food. RNAi knockdown of AstA abolished the preference shift induced by activation.
ecological_context: Integration of value estimates for food choice.
summary: AstA neurons influence food choice preference; activation promotes sweet choice while inhibition promotes bittersweet choice.

paper_title: A neural signature of choice under sensory conflict in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: None (optogenetically manipulated)
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation and inhibition tested.
behavioral_consequence: Activation shifted preference towards sweet food. Inhibition had no significant effect. RNAi knockdown of NPF abolished the preference shift induced by activation.
ecological_context: Integration of value estimates for food choice.
summary: NPF neuron activation promotes sweet food preference in decision tasks.

paper_title: A neural signature of choice under sensory conflict in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: None (optogenetically manipulated)
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation and inhibition tested.
behavioral_consequence: Activation had no significant effect on preference. Inhibition shifted preference towards bittersweet food.
ecological_context: Integration of value estimates for food choice.
summary: DH44 neuron inhibition shifts preference towards bittersweet food, while activation has no significant effect.

paper_title: A neural signature of choice under sensory conflict in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: None (optogenetically manipulated)
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation and inhibition tested.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation of these subsets shifted preference towards bittersweet food.
ecological_context: Modulation of reward, aversive memory, and taste conditioning signals.
summary: Specific dopaminergic subsets involved in memory and conditioning influence food choice when activated.

paper_title: A neural signature of choice under sensory conflict in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: None (optogenetically manipulated)

behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation of various subsets did not affect food preference.
ecological_context: Higher-order behaviors and learning, but not involved in this specific decision task.
summary: Optogenetic manipulation of mushroom body neurons did not alter food preference in the decision assay.

paper_title: A neural signature of choice under sensory conflict in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: None (optogenetically manipulated)
neural_activity: Optogenetic inhibition and chronic inhibition (Tetanus toxin) tested.
behavioral_consequence: Acute optogenetic and chronic inhibition of TPN3 did not change food preference at the equal-preference condition.
ecological_context: Relay of bitter taste information, but not essential for the specific sweet-bittersweet tradeoff decision tested.
summary: Inhibition of TPN3 bitter taste projection neurons did not alter food preference in the decision task.

paper_title: Mushroom body subsets encode CREB2-dependent water-reward long-term memory in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Water-reward conditioning (odor + water) in thirsty flies
neural_activity: In vivo calcium imaging (GCaMP6m) showed increased calcium response to training odor 24 hours post-conditioning
behavioral_consequence: Blocking neurotransmission (shits) during retrieval disrupted 24-hour water-reward long-term memory (wLTM); expression of dCREB2b disrupted wLTM formation
ecological_context: Thirsty flies associating specific odors with water reward
techniques_used: ['In vivo calcium imaging', 'Temperature-sensitive shibire (shits)', 'dCREB2b expression', 'T-maze behavioral assay']
summary: MB αβ surface neurons form a CREB2-dependent memory trace with increased calcium response 24 hours after odor/water conditioning and are required for wLTM retrieval and formation.

paper_title: Mushroom body subsets encode CREB2-dependent water-reward long-term memory in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Water-reward conditioning (odor + water) in thirsty flies
neural_activity: In vivo calcium imaging (GCaMP6m) showed decreased calcium response to training odor 24 hours post-conditioning
behavioral_consequence: Blocking neurotransmission (shits) during retrieval disrupted 24-hour wLTM; expression of dCREB2b disrupted wLTM formation
ecological_context: Thirsty flies associating specific odors with water reward
techniques_used: ['In vivo calcium imaging', 'Temperature-sensitive shibire (shits)', 'dCREB2b expression', 'T-maze behavioral assay']
summary: MB γ dorsal neurons form a CREB2-dependent memory trace with decreased calcium response 24 hours after odor/water conditioning and are required for wLTM retrieval and formation.

paper_title: Mushroom body subsets encode CREB2-dependent water-reward long-term memory in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Water-reward conditioning (odor + water) in thirsty flies

behavioral_consequence: Blocking neurotransmission (shits) during retrieval did not affect 24-hour wLTM

techniques_used: ['In vivo calcium imaging', 'Temperature-sensitive shibire (shits)', 'T-maze behavioral assay']

paper_title: Dopamine neurons promote arousal and wakefulness via Dop1R receptor in the Drosophila mushroom body
experimental_stimulus: Strong wake-inducing stimuli that reduces GABA transmission; mated females; presence or absence of CBZ (Carbamazepine)
neural_activity: Activity is independent of sleep-need; not directly modulated by GABA signaling
behavioral_consequence: Inhibition of specific subsets projecting to the Mushroom Body (MB) increases sleep
ecological_context: Regulation of sleep-wake transition and ability to engage in behaviors critical for survival
techniques_used: ['Genetic inhibition (Shits1 expression)', 'Sleep behavior analysis (total sleep, daytime/nighttime sleep, bout length, latency, activity)']
summary: Inhibition of PAM and PPL1 DAN subsets projecting to the MB increases sleep, particularly under strong wake-inducing stimuli. These DANs' activity is independent of sleep-need and not modulated by GABA signaling.

paper_title: Dopamine neurons promote arousal and wakefulness via Dop1R receptor in the Drosophila mushroom body
cell_type: Kenyon Cells (KCs) and Mushroom Body Output Neurons (MBONs)
experimental_stimulus: Sleep-need conditions; dopamine signaling via DopR receptors
neural_activity: Activity is modulated by sleep-need
behavioral_consequence: Function as wake-promoting neurons; required for dopamine-mediated sleep circuit function
ecological_context: Regulation of sleep, wakefulness, and behavioral arousal within common MB circuits
techniques_used: ['Receptor requirement analysis (DopR1 and DopR2)', 'Sleep behavior analysis']
summary: Wake-promoting KCs and MBONs express DopR1 and DopR2 receptors required for dopamine-mediated sleep regulation. Their activity is modulated by sleep-need, unlike the PAM-DAN activity.

paper_title: Differential Role for a Defined Lateral Horn Neuron Subset in Naïve Odor Valence in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Various odors (methyl acetate, 2-butanone, ethyl acetate, ACV, geranyl acetate, hexyl acetate, 2-heptanone, 3-octanol, 1-hexanol, ethyl benzoate, MCH, ethyl lactate) at concentrations ranging from 10^-3 to 5x10^-2; flies were tested in satiated or starved states; exposure times from 0 to 120 seconds.
neural_activity: Two-photon functional imaging (GCaMP6f) revealed varied response dynamics (peak response, % responding cells, persistence, time to peak) dependent on odor identity and concentration. Activity increased linearly with odor concentration. Starvation did not alter neural activity in the somata.
behavioral_consequence: Silencing neurons (UAS-Shibirets1) in satiated flies at 10^-2 concentration caused a significant decrease in avoidance behavior for odors eliciting strong responses (e.g., 2-butanone, ACV, geranyl acetate). No behavioral effect was observed for odors with weak responses. In starved flies, silencing abolished the contribution to aversion despite unchanged neural activity. Effect on valence developed over time, becoming significant after 80 seconds of exposure. Silencing improved aversive conditioning for 2-butanone by reducing innate aversion.
ecological_context: Context-dependent valence coding is crucial for survival and evaluating food resources. High odor concentrations (even for food odors) are associated with aversion, while starvation enhances attraction to food odors. The neurons' role shifts based on internal state (satiation) and external factors (concentration, exposure time).
techniques_used: ['Two-photon functional imaging', 'Genetic silencing (UAS-Shibirets1)', 'Behavioral assay (Flywalk/linear chambers)', 'trans-Tango mapping']
summary: PD2a1/b1 lateral horn output neurons mediate odor avoidance in satiated flies at high concentrations, a function dependent on exposure time and abolished by starvation without changes in neural activity. These neurons contribute to value coding for survival-critical food evaluation.

paper_title: Neonicotinoids disrupt memory, circadian behaviour and sleep
cell_type: Mushroom Body (Kenyon cells and output neurons)
experimental_stimulus: Olfactory shock conditioning (pairing odour with electric shock); field-relevant neonicotinoid exposure (10 µg/L imidacloprid, clothianidin, thiamethoxam).
neural_activity: Knockdown of Dα1 and Dβ2 nicotinic acetylcholine receptor subunits via RNAi (OK107-Gal4) significantly reduced memory performance; sensory controls confirmed intact odour and shock sensation.
behavioral_consequence: Reduced 1-hour olfactory associative memory performance compared to controls.
ecological_context: Memory formation is essential for efficient foraging and pollination in beneficial insects.
summary: Knockdown of Dα1 and Dβ2 nAChR subunits in the mushroom body reduced 1h memory, confirming nAChR-mediated memory deficits similar to neonicotinoid exposure.

paper_title: Neonicotinoids disrupt memory, circadian behaviour and sleep
experimental_stimulus: Neonicotinoid exposure (50 µg/L imidacloprid or clothianidin); circadian time (Day ZT2 vs Night ZT14).
neural_activity: Immunohistochemistry showed loss of day/night differences in s-LNv dorsal terminal branching complexity and PDF accumulation; knockdown of Dα1/Dβ2 in PDF neurons recapitulated this structural disruption.
behavioral_consequence: Disrupted circadian rhythmicity and sleep fragmentation in flies with neonicotinoid exposure or subunit knockdown.
ecological_context: Circadian rhythmicity is essential for efficient foraging and pollination; disruption reduces daytime activity opportunities.
summary: Neonicotinoids and subunit knockdown removed day/night remodelling of s-LNv dorsal terminals and PDF cycling, disrupting circadian output and sleep.

paper_title: Neonicotinoids disrupt memory, circadian behaviour and sleep
experimental_stimulus: Neonicotinoid exposure (50 µg/L imidacloprid or clothianidin).
neural_activity: Knockdown of Dα1 or Dβ2 nAChR subunits in tim-expressing cells reduced rhythmicity; no additive effect observed when neonicotinoids were added to knockdown flies.
behavioral_consequence: Reduced circadian rhythmicity and sleep fragmentation (shorter night-time sleep episodes, increased number of sleep episodes).
ecological_context: Sleep and circadian behaviour are essential for memory consolidation and efficient foraging.
summary: Knockdown of Dα1/Dβ2 in clock neurons mimicked neonicotinoid-induced deficits in rhythmicity and sleep, confirming nAChR subunits mediate these effects in the clock.

paper_title: Concerted Actions of Octopamine and Dopamine Receptors Drive Olfactory Learning
experimental_stimulus: Olfactory conditioning (Odor CS paired with 90 V electric shock US for aversive; Odor CS paired with 2 M sucrose US for appetitive)
neural_activity: Knockdown of octopamine biosynthetic enzyme tyramine beta hydroxylase (TbH) using RNAi; expression of Octb1R receptor required for signaling
behavioral_consequence: TbH RNAi in tdc2-GAL4 or APL-GAL4 neurons led to significantly impaired aversive olfactory learning (poor avoidance of shock-associated odor); TbH knockdown during adulthood specifically impaired learning
ecological_context: Animals make flexible behavioral choices vital for responding to cues predicting benefit or harm for fitness and survival
summary: Octopamine neurons (tdc2-GAL4+ and APL-GAL4+) are required for aversive olfactory learning, as TbH knockdown in these neurons impairs avoidance of shock-associated odor.

paper_title: Concerted Actions of Octopamine and Dopamine Receptors Drive Olfactory Learning
experimental_stimulus: Olfactory conditioning (Odor CS paired with 90 V electric shock US)
neural_activity: Expression of Octb1R receptor; Octb1R rescue experiments; cAMP imaging (Epac1-camps) in MB lobes
behavioral_consequence: Restoration of Octb1R expression in MB ab neurons fully rescued the aversive learning defect in octb1r mutants; Octb1R expression in other MB substructures (a9b9, g) did not rescue aversive learning
ecological_context: Processing aversive learning to avoid noxious cues
techniques_used: ['GAL4/UAS targeted gene expression (rescue)', 'T-maze olfactory conditioning', 'Confocal microscopy (cAMP imaging)']
summary: Octb1R function in MB ab neurons is required for aversive learning, as rescue expression here restored avoidance behavior in octb1r mutants.

paper_title: Concerted Actions of Octopamine and Dopamine Receptors Drive Olfactory Learning
experimental_stimulus: Olfactory conditioning (Odor CS paired with 2 M sucrose US)
neural_activity: Expression of Octb1R receptor; cAMP imaging showed OA-induced cAMP increase in PN dendrites and axons (dampened in octb1r mutant)
behavioral_consequence: Restoration of Octb1R expression in PNs fully rescued the appetitive learning defect in octb1r mutants; Octb1R expression in MB did not fully rescue appetitive learning
ecological_context: Processing appetitive learning to approach beneficial cues
techniques_used: ['GAL4/UAS targeted gene expression (rescue)', 'T-maze olfactory conditioning', 'Confocal microscopy (cAMP imaging)']
summary: Octb1R function in Projection Neurons is the major site for appetitive learning, as rescue expression here restored sugar-rewarded odor preference in octb1r mutants.

paper_title: Concerted Actions of Octopamine and Dopamine Receptors Drive Olfactory Learning
cell_type: Mushroom Body g and a9b9 Neurons
experimental_stimulus: Olfactory conditioning (Odor CS paired with 90 V electric shock US)
neural_activity: Expression of Octb1R receptor; Octb1R rescue experiments
behavioral_consequence: Octb1R re-expression in g or a9b9 neurons had no effect on rescuing the aversive learning phenotype in octb1r mutants
ecological_context: Distinct MB substructures process different learning tasks
summary: Octb1R expression in MB g or a9b9 neurons is not sufficient for aversive learning, as rescue in these substructures failed to restore avoidance behavior.

paper_title: Carbon Monoxide, a Retrograde Messenger Generated in Postsynaptic Mushroom Body Neurons, Evokes Noncanonical Dopamine Release
experimental_stimulus: Coincident activation by cholinergic inputs from Antennal Lobe (AL) and glutamatergic inputs (NMDA bath application or AFV stimulation)
neural_activity: Calcium imaging (G-CaMP2/R-GECO1) shows robust Ca2+ increase upon coincident stimulation. CO imaging (COP-1 probe) shows CO production specifically in MB lobes receiving coincident activation. dHO (heme oxygenase) is expressed in MBs.
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of dHO in MBs impairs short-term olfactory aversive memory (performance index decreases) without affecting naive responses to odors or electrical shocks.
ecological_context: Olfactory associative memories regulate approach and avoidance behaviors in Drosophila.
techniques_used: ['calcium imaging', 'CO imaging (COP-1 probe)', 'RNAi knockdown', 'olfactory aversive memory assay (T-maze)']
summary: Coincident AL and NMDA activation of Kenyon cells evokes Ca2+ signaling and CO production via dHO, which is required for olfactory associative memory but not naive sensory responses.

paper_title: Carbon Monoxide, a Retrograde Messenger Generated in Postsynaptic Mushroom Body Neurons, Evokes Noncanonical Dopamine Release
experimental_stimulus: Coincident MB activation (AL + NMDA), direct CO application (CO-saturated saline or CORM-3), RyR activation (4-chloro-3-methylphenol)
neural_activity: Vesicle release imaging (spH) and DA imaging (DA1m probe) show release specifically onto a3/a93 compartments of vertical MB lobes upon coincident MB activation or CO application. Calcium imaging (G-CaMP3) shows Ca2+ increase in terminals via internal stores. RyR is expressed in DA terminals.
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of RyR in DA neurons impairs short-term olfactory memory (1h) without affecting naive responses to odors or electrical shocks. Pharmacological blockade of RyR inhibits DA release and Long-Term Enhancement (LTE).
ecological_context: Memory-associated plasticity regulates approach and avoidance behaviors via dopamine-dependent learning.
techniques_used: ['vesicle release imaging (spH)', 'dopamine imaging (DA1m)', 'calcium imaging (G-CaMP3)', 'RNAi knockdown', 'pharmacological inhibition (dantrolene, TTX, thapsigargin)', 'olfactory aversive memory assay (T-maze)']
summary: DA neurons release dopamine via a noncanonical mechanism requiring internal Ca2+ efflux through RyR, triggered by CO from postsynaptic Kenyon cells, to support olfactory memory formation.

paper_title: Stochastic and Arbitrarily Generated Input Patterns to the Mushroom Bodies Can Serve as Conditioned Stimuli in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Thermogenetic activation (30°C) paired with electric shocks (90V DC, 1.25s duration) during training; Temperature gradient (18-35°C) during test phase.
neural_activity: Stochastic, idiosyncratic activation patterns induced by mCherry-dTRPA1 expression in GH146-Gal4 targeted OPNs; channel opens at ~25°C.
behavioral_consequence: Trained flies avoided temperatures >24°C (which activate OPNs) compared to controls; reduced locomotion speed after training; avoidance restricted to first 5 minutes of test. Learning threshold reached at ~31% OPN activation.
ecological_context: Learning arbitrary patterns is essential for survival as a human commensal adapting to environments differing from ancestral habitat; reflects evolutionary unpredictability of stimuli to be learned.
techniques_used: ['Thermogenetics (dTRPA1)', 'Flippase-mediated stochastic expression', 'Electric shock conditioning', 'Temperature gradient behavioral assay', 'Immunohistochemistry', 'Video tracking']
summary: OPNs expressing mCherry-dTRPA1 can serve as a conditioned aversive stimulus when their stochastic activity is paired with punishment, leading to avoidance behavior independent of glomerular identity, valence, or symmetry.

paper_title: Activation of specific mushroom body output neurons inhibits proboscis extension and sucrose consumption
cell_type: Mushroom Body Output Neurons (MBONs) [7 types: γ4>γ1γ2, α1, β'1, γ2α'1, α'2, α2sc, calyx]
experimental_stimulus: Sucrose (10 mM to 1000 mM) presented to tarsi
neural_activity: Not explicitly measured endogenously; function tested via optogenetic activation (CsChrimson/Chrimson88) and silencing (Shibirets, gtACR1, Kir2.1)
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation significantly decreased proboscis extension (PER) to sucrose. Activation of specific lines (MB078C, MB311C, MB242A) also decreased sucrose consumption. Inhibition did not consistently increase PER.
ecological_context: Relevant to innate feeding behavior and survival; modulates food acceptance based on prior experience (e.g., conditioned taste aversion). Flies were starved (20-26 hours) to induce feeding motivation.
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics (CsChrimson, Chrimson88)', 'Thermogenetics (Shibirets)', 'Optogenetic silencing (gtACR1)', 'Genetic silencing (Kir2.1)', 'Proboscis extension response assay', 'Temporal consumption assay', 'Locomotor assay']

paper_title: Appetitive Memory with Survival Benefit Is Robust Across Aging in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Aging (10 vs 30 days); Thermogenetic activation (dTrpA1 at 31°C); Two-choice sugar preference (sucrose vs arabinose)
neural_activity: Expression of dNPF significantly upregulated in aged fly heads (qRT-PCR); GFP intensity upregulated around subesophageal ganglion (SOG) area (Immunohistochemistry); Artificial depolarization via dTrpA1
behavioral_consequence: Activation induces higher preference for nutritious sugar (sucrose) over non-nutritious sugar (arabinose); Aged flies show stronger preference for nutritious sugar correlating with upregulation
ecological_context: Aged flies have high mortality rates during starvation; enhanced preference for nutritious sugar correlates with survival benefit
summary: dNPF-expressing neurons show age-dependent upregulation of expression. Their activation increases preference for nutritious sugar over non-nutritious sugar, potentially aiding survival during starvation.

paper_title: Appetitive Memory with Survival Benefit Is Robust Across Aging in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Thermogenetic activation (dTrpA1) paired with odor presentation during appetitive conditioning
neural_activity: Artificial activation induces appetitive memory; functional capacity decreases with aging (30 d vs 10 d); no Long-Term Memory (LTM) formed
behavioral_consequence: Induces appetitive Short-Term Memory (3 min) in young flies, but significantly reduced in aged flies; does not form LTM in either age group
ecological_context: Mediates memory for sweet taste (non-nutritious sugar); impairment contributes to fragile memory with non-nutritious sugar in aged flies
summary: PAM-δ4 DANs signal sweet taste. Thermogenetic activation induces short-term memory, but function decreases with aging, impairing memory for non-nutritious sugar.

paper_title: Appetitive Memory with Survival Benefit Is Robust Across Aging in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Thermogenetic activation (dTrpA1) paired with odor presentation during appetitive conditioning
neural_activity: Artificial activation induces appetitive memory; functional capacity maintained with aging
behavioral_consequence: Induces appetitive Long-Term Memory (24 h) in both young and aged flies
ecological_context: Mediates memory for nutritional value (nutritious sugar); preservation allows robust memory with nutritious sugar in aged flies
summary: PAM-ε1 DANs signal nutritional value. Thermogenetic activation induces long-term memory, which is maintained with aging, allowing robust memory for nutritious sugar.

paper_title: Neuropeptide relay between SIFa signaling controls the experience-dependent mating duration of male Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Socio-sexual environment (singly-reared vs group-reared for LMD; sexually experienced vs naive for SMD)
neural_activity: Electrophysiological manipulation: Hyperpolarization (KCNJ2) disrupts LMD; Depolarization (NachBac) disrupts SMD; Knockdown or synaptic blockade (TNT) disrupts both
behavioral_consequence: SIFa neurons act as a 'molecular switch'. Hyperpolarization prevents Longer-Mating-Duration (LMD). Depolarization prevents Shorter-Mating-Duration (SMD). Knockdown or synaptic blockade disrupts both LMD and SMD
ecological_context: Modulates mating duration in response to rival threats (LMD) or prior sexual experience (SMD)
summary: SIFa neurons function as a molecular switch controlling mating duration; hyperpolarization disrupts LMD while depolarization disrupts SMD, and general knockdown disrupts both behaviors.

paper_title: Neuropeptide relay between SIFa signaling controls the experience-dependent mating duration of male Drosophila

neural_activity: Genetic manipulation: RNAi knockdown of SIFaR; Genetic rescue of SIFaRB322 mutant
behavioral_consequence: SIFaR expression in 49087-labeled cells is necessary and sufficient for both LMD and SMD behaviors
ecological_context: Essential subpopulation for SIFa-mediated neuropeptide relay in male mating duration plasticity
summary: SIFaR-positive cells labeled by GAL4 49087 are necessary and sufficient for both LMD and SMD behaviors.

paper_title: Neuropeptide relay between SIFa signaling controls the experience-dependent mating duration of male Drosophila

paper_title: Aversive Training Induces Both Presynaptic and Postsynaptic Suppression in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Aversive olfactory conditioning (odor + electric shock); RNAi knockdown of Rdl GABAA receptor
neural_activity: Suppressed calcium responses (GCaMP6f) to the conditioned odor (CS+) in axon segments; reduced acetylcholine release (GACh sensor); suppression persisted for ~30 min
behavioral_consequence: Rdl knockdown enhanced 3 min memory performance with 30V shock (not 90V); neurons required for memory acquisition, consolidation, and early memory retrieval (cited)
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed
techniques_used: ['In vivo functional imaging', 'In vivo ACh imaging', 'RNAi knockdown', 'T-maze behavioral assay']
summary: Aversive conditioning suppresses Ca2+ responses and ACh release in $\alpha'3$ and $\alpha'2$ MBn axon segments. Rdl knockdown in these neurons enhanced early memory performance.

paper_title: Aversive Training Induces Both Presynaptic and Postsynaptic Suppression in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Aversive olfactory conditioning (odor + electric shock); Temperature-sensitive shibire ts inactivation
neural_activity: Suppressed calcium responses (GCaMP6f) to the conditioned odor (CS+) in dendrites; reduced ACh release; suppression persisted for ~30 min
behavioral_consequence: Blocking synaptic output impaired 15 min memory expression; aversive conditioning promotes avoidance behavior through suppression of this circuit
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed
techniques_used: ['In vivo functional imaging', 'Temperature-sensitive shibire ts inactivation', 'T-maze behavioral assay']
summary: Aversive conditioning suppresses Ca2+ responses and ACh release in $\alpha'3$ MBOn dendrites. Blocking output impairs early memory expression, suggesting this circuit drives avoidance behavior.

paper_title: Selective suppression and recall of long-term memories in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Aversive olfactory conditioning (3-Octanol or 4-Methyl-cylohexanol paired with electric shock); Unpaired conditioning; Food-odor reduced environment; Red light (optogenetics)
neural_activity: Sparse, nonstereotypic labeling via 6xCRE-GFP; Increased GCaMP6f calcium response to conditioned stimulus (CS+) odor after spaced training compared to unpaired training; No response increase for CS- or unrelated odors; Labeling increases in 14-21 day-old flies compared to 7-day-olds
behavioral_consequence: Silencing (Tetanus toxin) reduces aversive olfactory memory performance 2 days and 5 days after spaced training; No effect on Mid-Term Memory (3h) or Anesthesia-Resistant Memory (2 days massed); Artificial optogenetic activation (Chrimson + red light) after conditioning elicits red light avoidance behavior (aversive recall) 2 days and 4 days after training; No effect in unpaired controls
ecological_context: Adaptive decision-making depends on memory formation essential for survival; Innate CAMEL activity increases in first week of adult life in a sensory experience-dependent manner; Food-odor associations may contribute to endogenous CAMEL activity
techniques_used: ['Immunohistochemistry (GFP/EGFP)', 'In vivo calcium imaging (GCaMP6f)', 'T-maze olfactory conditioning assay', 'Optogenetics (Chrimson)', 'Light preference assay']
summary: CAMEL-tagged Kenyon Cells specifically encode aversive olfactory long-term memory, responding to the conditioned odor after training. Silencing these cells impairs memory recall at 2-5 days, while artificial activation mimics aversive memory recall.

paper_title: Selective suppression and recall of long-term memories in Drosophila
