paper_title: Regulation of sleep by cholinergic neurons located outside the central brain in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (627 nm LED + retinal)
neural_activity: Anatomical analysis identified 2 neurons with processes in leg ganglia and brain
behavioral_consequence: Activation had no effect on sleep quantity or bout duration
ecological_context: Processes extend into leg ganglia, suggesting sensory integration potential, though sleep function was not supported by experiments
summary: TPN1 neurons within the 23E10-GAL4 driver pattern do not promote sleep when activated.

paper_title: DYRK1A antagonists rescue degeneration and behavioural deficits of in vivo models based on amyloid-β, Tau and DYRK1A neurotoxicity
experimental_stimulus: Developmental and adult overexpression of human Tau 0N4R, human Aβ42, or fly mnb (GMR-Gal4 driver)
neural_activity: Not tested (structural degeneration observed)
behavioral_consequence: Loss of photoreceptor neurons, 'rough eye' phenotype, significant reduction in eye surface area (~60% Tau, ~50% Aβ42, ~30% mnb); rescue of eye size by DYR219 and DYR533
summary: Overexpression of disease-associated genes in photoreceptor neurons causes neurodegeneration and reduced eye size, which is suppressed by DYRK1A inhibitors.

paper_title: DYRK1A antagonists rescue degeneration and behavioural deficits of in vivo models based on amyloid-β, Tau and DYRK1A neurotoxicity
experimental_stimulus: Pan-neuronal overexpression of human Tau 0N4R, human Aβ42, or fly mnb (elav-Gal4 driver)
neural_activity: Reduced phosphorylation of human Tau at pT231, pS262, and pS396 measured via Western blot upon drug treatment
behavioral_consequence: Shortened lifespan (38% Tau, 50% Aβ42, 25% mnb), reduced locomotion (negative geotaxis climbing assay); lifespan and climbing performance partially or fully rescued by DYR219 and DYR533
summary: Pan-neuronal overexpression shortens lifespan and impairs locomotion; DYRK1A inhibitors reduce Tau phosphorylation and rescue these phenotypes.

paper_title: DYRK1A antagonists rescue degeneration and behavioural deficits of in vivo models based on amyloid-β, Tau and DYRK1A neurotoxicity
experimental_stimulus: Clock-wide overexpression of human Tau, Aβ42, or mnb (Tim-Gal4 driver) under 12h Light:12h Dark cycles
neural_activity: Not tested (sleep behavior assessed)
behavioral_consequence: Reduced total, day, and night sleep duration; DYR219 increased sleep in mnb overexpressors, DYR533 increased sleep in Aβ42 overexpressors
summary: Clock neuron overexpression reduces sleep duration; specific DYRK1A inhibitors rescue sleep loss in specific genotypes.

paper_title: DYRK1A antagonists rescue degeneration and behavioural deficits of in vivo models based on amyloid-β, Tau and DYRK1A neurotoxicity
experimental_stimulus: Mushroom body overexpression of human Tau, Aβ42, or mnb (OK107-Gal4 driver); Olfactory shock conditioning (Octanol/MCH + Shock)
neural_activity: Not tested (memory assessed)
behavioral_consequence: Decreased 1h olfactory memory (Performance Index); sensory avoidance of odors and shock unchanged; DYR219 rescued Tau and mnb memory, DYR533 rescued Aβ42 memory
summary: Mushroom body overexpression impairs olfactory memory; inhibitors rescue memory deficits without affecting sensory response.

paper_title: Lipophorin receptors regulate mushroom body development and complex behaviors in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Odorants (3-octanol, 4-methyl cyclohexanol) paired with electric shocks (in vivo); Light/dark cycles (in vivo); Mammalian Reelin (30 nM, in primary culture)
neural_activity: Mutants and RNAi knockdowns showed impaired mid-term memory formation (Performance Index near 0), increased sleeping time during light phase, and altered MB anatomical organization (mislocalized lobes, reduced beta lobe distance). In primary culture, wild-type MB neurons increased neurite arborization complexity (max length, critical value) with Reelin treatment; LpR/Dab-deficient neurons failed to respond to Reelin or showed impaired basal neurite development.
behavioral_consequence: LpR1/LpR2/Dab mutants and MB-specific knockdowns exhibited deficits in aversive olfactory memory and altered sleep architecture (increased sleep time).
techniques_used: ['Olfactory conditioning assay (T-maze)', 'Locomotor activity monitoring (Drosophila Activity Monitoring System)', 'Immunohistochemistry (Confocal microscopy)', 'Primary neuronal culture (Sholl analysis)', 'Ligand internalization assay (in S2 cells)']
summary: Mushroom Body neurons require Lipophorin receptors (LpR1, LpR2) and Dab for proper anatomical development and function. Genetic deficiency impairs olfactory memory and increases sleep duration. In vitro, these neurons respond to mammalian Reelin by increasing neurite complexity, a response dependent on LpRs and Dab.

paper_title: Sleep-promoting neurons remodel their response properties to calibrate sleep drive with environmental demands
experimental_stimulus: Dopamine (DA), Allatostatin-A (AstA), Sleep deprivation (12h), Starvation (18h), Time-restricted feeding (7 days), Courtship conditioning (spaced training)
neural_activity: Live-brain imaging (Epac1-camps) showed DA increases cAMP levels, while AstA reduces cAMP levels. Response to AstA increased after sleep deprivation. Response to DA reduced after social enrichment and courtship conditioning. CaLexA imaging showed higher GFP signal (activity) after courtship training. Dop1R1 receptors recruited following metabolic challenges.
behavioral_consequence: Activation (TrpA1) increased sleep. Knockdown of AstA-R1 or AstA-R2 increased sleep. Knockdown of Dop1R1 increased sleep only after time-restricted feeding or starvation recovery (not baseline). Sleep rebound observed after starvation recovery was dependent on Dop1R1.
ecological_context: Plasticity allows animals to maintain wakefulness in dangerous or life-threatening conditions (e.g., during sleep deprivation). Dop1R1 recruitment allows animals to be more alert and productive during their primary wake period following metabolic challenges.
summary: R23E10 neurons are sleep-promoting dFB neurons that intrinsically regulate their response to DA and AstA based on waking history. They exhibit plasticity in receptor expression (Dop1R1 recruitment) after metabolic challenges, allowing them to prioritize waking behaviors when sleep drive conflicts with environmental demands.

paper_title: Sleep-promoting neurons remodel their response properties to calibrate sleep drive with environmental demands
experimental_stimulus: Activation (TrpA1/NaChBac), Dopamine (DA), Allatostatin-A (AstA), Time-restricted feeding, Starvation recovery
neural_activity: Live-brain imaging (Epac1-camps) showed robust response to AstA (reduced cAMP) but reduced or absent response to DA. Similar to R23E10, Dop1R1 receptors were recruited following metabolic challenges.
behavioral_consequence: Activation increased sleep. Knockdown of Dop1R1 increased sleep following time-restricted feeding or starvation recovery (not baseline).
ecological_context: Represents an independent set of sleep-promoting neurons that remodel response properties to calibrate sleep drive with environmental demands, similar to R23E10.
summary: R55B01 neurons form an independent set of dFB sleep-promoting neurons distinct from R23E10. They show robust AstA responses and low baseline DA responses, but recruit Dop1R1 to support waking after metabolic challenges.

paper_title: Sleep-promoting neurons remodel their response properties to calibrate sleep drive with environmental demands
experimental_stimulus: Activation (TrpA1 on AstA-GAL4 or R65D05-LexA lines)
neural_activity: Not directly measured in live-brain imaging for this specific cell type in the Results section, but shown to project to dFB dendritic fields. Co-release of glutamate and AstA onto R23E10/R55B01.
behavioral_consequence: Activation substantially increased sleep. Knockdown of AstA receptors on R23E10/R55B01 increased sleep, suggesting AstA exerts a wake-promoting inhibitory tone on these neurons.
ecological_context: Co-release of inhibitory AstA with excitatory glutamate may attenuate overexcitement of sleep-promoting neurons, allowing animals to maintain wakefulness in dangerous conditions.
summary: AstA-expressing neurons project to the dFB and release glutamate and AstA. While glutamate promotes sleep, AstA exerts a wake-promoting inhibitory tone on R23E10/R55B01 neurons, likely to prevent excessive excitation during high sleep drive.

paper_title: DYRK1a Inhibitor Mediated Rescue of Drosophila Models of Alzheimer’s Disease-Down Syndrome Phenotypes
experimental_stimulus: Genetic overexpression of human Tau 0N4R, human tandem oligomerising Aβ42, or minibrain (mnb) using GMR-GAL4
behavioral_consequence: Degeneration of photoreceptor neurons resulting in 'rough eye' phenotype and reduced eye surface area; PST-001 treatment increased eye size, rescuing degeneration
summary: Overexpression of AD-associated genes in photoreceptor neurons causes degeneration and reduced eye size, which is partially or fully rescued by the DYRK1A inhibitor PST-001.

paper_title: DYRK1a Inhibitor Mediated Rescue of Drosophila Models of Alzheimer’s Disease-Down Syndrome Phenotypes
experimental_stimulus: Genetic overexpression of human Tau 0N4R, human tandem oligomerising Aβ42, or minibrain (mnb) using tim-GAL4
behavioral_consequence: Reduction in total sleep, day sleep, and night sleep; PST-001 treatment increased total sleep to wild type levels for Tau and mnb overexpressing flies, but not Aβ42
summary: Overexpression of AD-associated genes in clock neurons disrupts sleep patterns, which is rescued by PST-001 treatment for Tau and mnb models.

paper_title: DYRK1a Inhibitor Mediated Rescue of Drosophila Models of Alzheimer’s Disease-Down Syndrome Phenotypes
experimental_stimulus: Genetic overexpression of human Tau 0N4R, human tandem oligomerising Aβ42, or minibrain (mnb) using OK107-GAL4; Olfactory conditioning (odor + electric shock)
behavioral_consequence: Reduced intermediate-term memory (Performance Index); sensory controls confirmed intact odor and shock detection; PST-001 treatment improved memory performance to control levels
summary: Overexpression of AD-associated genes in Mushroom Body neurons impairs associative memory, which is restored by PST-001 treatment.

paper_title: DYRK1a Inhibitor Mediated Rescue of Drosophila Models of Alzheimer’s Disease-Down Syndrome Phenotypes
experimental_stimulus: Genetic overexpression of human Tau 0N4R, human tandem oligomerising Aβ42, or minibrain (mnb) using elav-GAL4
behavioral_consequence: Shortened lifespan and reduced climbing performance (motor impairment); PST-001 extended lifespan for Tau and Aβ42 models and rescued climbing performance for Tau and mnb models
summary: Pan-neuronal overexpression of AD-associated genes shortens lifespan and impairs motor function, with PST-001 treatment showing rescue effects on these phenotypes.

paper_title: Biological functions of α2‐adrenergic‐like octopamine receptor in Drosophila melanogaster
cell_type: Octα2R-expressing neurons in the Pars intercerebralis (PI)
experimental_stimulus: Starvation conditions; General locomotor monitoring in 12h:12h light/dark cycles
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown in PI (dilp2-Gal4, 30Y-Gal4) decreased total daily locomotor counts and evening peak activity. Octα2R hypomorphic mutants failed to show starvation-induced hyperactivity seen in wild-type flies.
ecological_context: Regulation of locomotor activity and starvation-induced hyperactivity (potentially food-searching behavior)
techniques_used: ['Genetic knockdown (Gal4/UAS RNAi)', 'Piggy Bac transposon insertion mutants (Octα2R hypo)', 'Drosophila Activity Monitoring (DAM) System', 'qPCR for mRNA levels']
summary: Octα2R-expressing neurons in the PI regulate locomotor activity. Loss of function reduces general locomotion and prevents starvation-induced hyperactivity.

paper_title: Biological functions of α2‐adrenergic‐like octopamine receptor in Drosophila melanogaster
cell_type: Octα2R-expressing neurons in the Ventral Nerve Cord (VNC)
experimental_stimulus: Octopamine (10 mM) application to exposed nerve cord in decapitated flies
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown increased the time and frequency of grooming. Wild-type flies showed increased foreleg grooming time with OA application, whereas Octα2R mutants did not, indicating Octα2R signaling inhibits grooming.
ecological_context: Grooming behavior regulation (self-maintenance)
techniques_used: ['Decapitation assay', 'Multibeam monitor (MB5)', 'Video analysis of grooming', 'Immunohistochemistry (GFP expression)']
summary: Octα2R signaling in the VNC inhibits grooming behavior. Octα2R hypomorphs exhibit increased grooming time and fail to increase grooming upon octopamine application.

paper_title: Under warm ambient conditions, Drosophila melanogaster suppresses nighttime activity via the neuropeptide pigment dispersing factor
cell_type: Ventrolateral Neurons (LNvs) / PDF-expressing circadian clock neurons
experimental_stimulus: Constant ambient temperatures of 25°C (LD25) and 30°C (LD30) under 12:12 Light:Dark cycles (12 hours Light: 12 hours Dark)
neural_activity: Not directly recorded (e.g., no electrophysiology or calcium imaging); function inferred via genetic manipulation (knockout, RNAi, hyperpolarization, ablation). PDF neuropeptide levels quantified via immunocytochemistry using anti-PDF antibodies.
behavioral_consequence: Under LD30, control flies suppress locomotor activity during the midnight window (ZT16-20). Flies lacking PDF (*Pdf01*), lacking PDF receptors (*Pdfr* mutants), or lacking LNvs (*Pdf-GAL4 > UAS-Dti*) fail to suppress midnight activity, showing significantly increased locomotor activity and reduced sleep during ZT16-20 compared to controls. Under LD25, this specific midnight activity deficit is not observed. Total activity levels were not significantly different between genotypes.
ecological_context: In tropical wild environments, ambient temperatures can reach 30°C or higher, while flies prefer 23-25°C. Suppressing activity at night under warm conditions is a behavioral strategy to mitigate harsh temperatures and potentially conserve energy.
techniques_used: ['Locomotor activity monitoring (Drosophila Activity Monitors - DAM)', 'Immunocytochemistry (anti-PER, anti-PDF antibodies)', 'Genetic manipulation (Mutants: *Pdf01*, *Pdfr*3369, *Pdfr*5304; RNAi: UAS-Pdf-RNAi; Hyperpolarization: UAS-Kir2.1; Ablation: UAS-Dti)', 'Imaging (Zeiss LSM 880 microscope)']
summary: PDF-expressing LNvs are necessary to suppress nighttime locomotor activity specifically under warm ambient conditions (30°C). Flies lacking PDF, PDF receptors, or LNvs fail to suppress activity during the middle of the night (ZT16-20) at 30°C, leading to reduced sleep, whereas activity suppression remains intact under cooler conditions (25°C).

paper_title: Age-Related Unstructured Spike Patterns and Molecular Localization in Drosophila Circadian Neurons
experimental_stimulus: Light-dark cycles (12h:12h); Zeitgeber Time comparisons (ZT6-8 mid-day vs ZT18-20 mid-night); Aging comparison (young 2-4 days vs aged 60-67 days); Current injections (evoked spiking and hyperpolarizing current for PSPs)
neural_activity: Young DN1p neurons exhibited irregular firing at ZT6-8 but regular/structured firing at ZT18-20. Aged DN1p neurons showed unstructured spike patterns at both ZT6-8 and ZT18-20. Aged neurons had increased CV and LV of interspike intervals, reduced spike frequency adaptation at ZT18-20, and reduced PSP frequency at ZT18-20. Young neurons showed circadian cycling of SLOB-HA and NaKβ-myc targeting the plasma membrane, whereas aged neurons showed disrupted localization patterns with increased perinuclear signals and reduced heterogeneity.
behavioral_consequence: Not experimentally tested in this study (paper discusses link to sleep quality decline based on established regulatory role)
ecological_context: Circadian regulation of sleep quality, particularly structured nighttime activity (ZT18-20) associated with sleep quality in Drosophila
techniques_used: ['Perforated patch-clamp electrophysiology', 'Intracellular sharp electrode recordings', 'STED microscopy (immunocytochemistry)']
summary: DN1p neurons in aged flies exhibit unstructured spike patterns during nighttime compared to young flies, associated with disrupted subcellular localization of SLOB and NaKβ proteins and altered membrane properties.

paper_title: Intrinsic maturation of sleep output neurons regulates sleep ontogeny in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Juvenile vs mature adulthood (developmental stage)
neural_activity: Transcriptomic analysis reveals dynamic transcriptional states at different ages; genes involved in distinct molecular processes implicated in sleep control of juvenile and mature flies
behavioral_consequence: Functional manipulations suggest these neurons differentially regulate sleep in juvenile and mature flies; increased sleep in juvenile flies driven by decreased probability of transitioning to wake and more time in deeper sleep states
summary: Sleep-promoting neurons in the dFB exhibit dynamic transcriptional states across ages and differentially regulate sleep in juvenile versus mature flies, with juvenile sleep characterized by increased duration and deeper sleep states driven by reduced transition to wake.

paper_title: Protocerebral Bridge Neurons That Regulate Sleep in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Thermogenetic activation (dTrpA1, 29°C) in constant dark (DD); mechanical stimulation during sleep
behavioral_consequence: Activation significantly increased total sleep amount; activation increased arousal threshold (fewer flies awakened by mechanical stimuli)
techniques_used: ['dTrpA1 thermogenetic activation', 'Drosophila Activity Monitoring', 'Video tracking', 't-GRASP', 'Immunohistochemistry', 'RNAi (Dop2R knockdown)']
summary: PB interneurons labeled by R59E08-Gal4 promote sleep; activation increases sleep duration and arousal threshold.

paper_title: Protocerebral Bridge Neurons That Regulate Sleep in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Thermogenetic activation (dTrpA1, 29°C) in constant dark (DD)
behavioral_consequence: Activation significantly decreased total sleep amount; increased waking activity index
summary: PFN neurons labeled by R52B10-Gal4 promote wakefulness; activation decreases sleep.

paper_title: Protocerebral Bridge Neurons That Regulate Sleep in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Thermogenetic activation (dTrpA1, 29°C) in constant dark (DD) and light:dark (LD)
behavioral_consequence: Activation significantly decreased total sleep amount
techniques_used: ['dTrpA1 thermogenetic activation', 'Drosophila Activity Monitoring', 'GRASP', 'RNAi (Dop2R knockdown in PB)']
summary: T1 DA neurons project to PB; activation decreases sleep and regulates sleep via D2 receptor signaling on PB interneurons.

paper_title: Regulation of circadian rhythm and sleep by miR‐375‐timeless interaction in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Genetic overexpression/knockout of miR-375; Light/Dark (LD) and Constant Darkness (DD) cycles
neural_activity: TIM protein levels decreased significantly (31.1% at ZT18, 54.37% at ZT24) in miR-375 overexpression flies (measured by immunostaining/confocal microscopy); miR-375 expression oscillated with peak at ZT12 in wild-type (measured by qRT-PCR)
behavioral_consequence: miR-375 overexpression in tim neurons caused 100% arrhythmicity in locomotor activity under LD and DD; altered sleep patterns (increased daytime sleep, decreased nighttime sleep); miR-375 knockout caused 33% arrhythmicity and reduced total sleep
ecological_context: Regulates daily rhythms in physiology and behavior, which are entrained by environmental stimuli such as light and temperature
summary: l-LNv neurons express miR-375 and TIM; miR-375 overexpression reduces TIM levels, causing arrhythmicity and sleep changes, indicating miR-375 modulates circadian rhythm and sleep via targeting timeless.

paper_title: AKH-FOXO pathway regulates starvation-induced sleep loss through remodeling of the small ventral lateral neuron dorsal projections
experimental_stimulus: Starvation (food deprivation) for 16-24 hours, specifically around the first light-dark transition
neural_activity: s-LNv dorsal projections become more open (defasciculated); Defasciculation Index (DI) significantly increased compared to non-starved flies
behavioral_consequence: Starvation prevents flies from going to sleep after the first light-dark transition; LNvs are required for starvation-induced sleep loss
ecological_context: Food inanition is a common survival challenge; energy deficiency drives a complex behavioral program to forage and ingest food; metabolism and sleep are inextricably linked
techniques_used: ['Immunofluorescence (confocal microscopy)', 'Drosophila Activity Monitoring System (sleep behavior)', 'Genetic manipulation (Gal4/UAS, mutants, RNAi)']
summary: Starvation causes structural remodeling (defasciculation) of s-LNv dorsal projections, and these neurons are required for starvation-induced sleep loss to facilitate foraging behavior.

paper_title: AKH-FOXO pathway regulates starvation-induced sleep loss through remodeling of the small ventral lateral neuron dorsal projections
experimental_stimulus: Starvation (food deprivation)
neural_activity: akh mRNA levels significantly increased at ST15 (ZT3) following light/dark shift
behavioral_consequence: Loss of AKH function blocks starvation-induced sleep loss; Overexpression of akh increases starvation-induced sleep loss
ecological_context: AKH functions to mobilize stored energy for maximizing survival when food is scarce; starvation-induced sleep loss is a response to energy deficiency
techniques_used: ['qRT-PCR (gene expression)', 'Sleep monitoring (Drosophila Activity Monitoring System)', 'Genetic manipulation (Akh mutants, UAS-akh overexpression)']
summary: AKH-expressing neurons increase akh mRNA during starvation, and AKH signaling is indispensable for starvation-induced sleep loss and s-LNv remodeling.

paper_title: Neurofibromin 1 in mushroom body neurons mediates circadian wake drive through activating cAMP–PKA signaling
experimental_stimulus: Circadian cycles (12 h light–dark, constant darkness), Genetic RNAi knockdown (Nf1, Pka-C1)
neural_activity: Rhythmic expression of hundreds of genes (RNA-seq) including Nf1 and Pka-C1; Rhythmic cAMP levels peaking during daytime (Epac1-camps FRET imaging); Rhythmic calcium levels peaking during daytime (CaLexA imaging); Rhythmic PKA-C1 protein accumulation (immunostaining). Knockdown reduces calcium and cAMP levels.
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of Nf1 or Pka-C1 increases total sleep duration and sleep consolidation (maximum episode duration) specifically during the daytime. Circadian locomotor activity patterns remain largely normal. Wake activity levels are not reduced.
ecological_context: Regulates daytime wakefulness (Process C of sleep regulation). Helps offset the increase in sleep debt build-up during the day. Genes cycling in both LD and DD may represent functionally important genes in the natural environment.
techniques_used: ['RNA-seq (FACS sorted MB neurons)', 'Drosophila Activity Monitor (DAM) sleep assay', 'Epac1-camps FRET imaging', 'CaLexA calcium imaging', 'Immunohistochemistry']
summary: MB neurons exhibit circadian rhythms in cAMP and calcium levels peaking during the daytime, mediated by Nf1-cAMP-PKA signaling, which promotes daytime wakefulness.

paper_title: Neurofibromin 1 in mushroom body neurons mediates circadian wake drive through activating cAMP–PKA signaling
cell_type: Mushroom Body Neuron Subtypes (α/β and γ lobes)
experimental_stimulus: Genetic RNAi knockdown (Nf1, Pka-C1) using specific drivers (c739 for α/β, 201Y for α/β and γ, c309 for all lobes)
neural_activity: Calcium levels in these subregions are elevated by NF1 and PKA-C1. Knockdown reduces calcium levels across MB lobes.
behavioral_consequence: Nf1 knockdown in α/β and γ lobes (201Y) increases daytime sleep. Pka-C1 knockdown in α/β and γ lobes (201Y) increases daytime sleep. Knockdown in α/β lobes alone (c739) does not consistently increase daytime sleep for Pka-C1. Effect emerges from simultaneous activation of α/β and γ KCs.
ecological_context: Contributes to daytime wakefulness, a state regulated by the interaction between sleep homeostasis and circadian processes in bimodal activity patterns.
summary: The NF1–cAMP/PKA pathway promotes daytime wakefulness primarily through the simultaneous activation of α/β and γ Kenyon cells.

paper_title: Down-regulation of a cytokine secreted from peripheral fat bodies improves visual attention while reducing sleep in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Nutritional state (fed ad libitum, acute starvation, upd2 mutant starved-like state)
neural_activity: Increased CaLexA signal intensity (GFP) in upd2 mutants and starved flies compared to fed controls; activity negatively correlated with feeding events in control flies
behavioral_consequence: Activity serves as a neural signature of nutritional status/hunger; not experimentally manipulated
ecological_context: Reflects chronic starvation signal in the brain associated with sleep and feeding regulation
summary: R4 neurons in the Ellipsoid Body show increased activity in upd2 mutants and starved flies, correlating with nutritional status rather than sleep duration.

paper_title: Down-regulation of a cytokine secreted from peripheral fat bodies improves visual attention while reducing sleep in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Knockdown of upd2 receptor (dome) via RNAi (Ilp2-GAL4 > UAS-domeRNAi)
neural_activity: Not directly measured; receptor knockdown used to test signaling pathway integration
behavioral_consequence: Decreased daytime sleep, increased nighttime feeding events, and improved visual attention (reduced distractibility)
ecological_context: Integrates peripheral starvation signals (Upd2 from fat body) to regulate sleep and foraging behavior
techniques_used: ['RNA interference', 'sleep tracking (DART)', 'open-field behavioral tracking', "visual attention paradigm (Buridan's)"]
summary: Knockdown of the upd2 receptor in Ilp2 neurons recapitulates the sleep reduction, hyperphagia, and improved visual attention phenotypes seen in fat body upd2 knockdown.

paper_title: Dopamine Signaling in Wake-Promoting Clock Neurons Is Not Required for the Normal Regulation of Sleep in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Bath application of 1 mM dopamine
neural_activity: Increased cAMP levels (imaging); depolarization of membrane potential (patch-clamp); no significant increase in spontaneous action potential firing rate.
behavioral_consequence: Downregulation of Dop1R1 receptors specifically in l-LNvs/s-LNvs resulted in a moderate decrease in daytime sleep (fragmented sleep).
ecological_context: Circadian regulation of sleep during the daytime phase.
summary: l-LNvs respond to dopamine with cAMP increase and depolarization without increased firing; dopamine signaling via Dop1R1 promotes daytime sleep.

paper_title: Dopamine Signaling in Wake-Promoting Clock Neurons Is Not Required for the Normal Regulation of Sleep in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Endogenous dopamine (inferred from presynaptic connectivity and receptor knockdown effects)
neural_activity: Respond to dopamine with cAMP increase (imaging difficult due to l-LNv overlap); inferred to utilize Dop1R2 receptors based on behavioral knockdown data.
behavioral_consequence: Downregulation of Dop1R2 receptors in s-LNvs resulted in a decrease in nighttime sleep.
ecological_context: Circadian regulation of sleep during the nighttime phase.
summary: s-LNvs respond to dopamine via Dop1R2 receptors; dopamine signaling via s-LNvs promotes nighttime sleep.

paper_title: Dopamine Signaling in Wake-Promoting Clock Neurons Is Not Required for the Normal Regulation of Sleep in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: None (Anatomical mapping only)
neural_activity: Presynaptic terminals located in the accessory medulla (AME) contacting l-LNvs and s-LNvs.
behavioral_consequence: N/A (Receptors targeted in clock neurons, not dopaminergic neurons themselves)
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed
techniques_used: ['Immunostaining (TH-Gal4)', 'GRASP (GFP reconstitution across synaptic partners)', 'Synaptic marker expression (SytI/II::GFP, Dscam::GFP)']
summary: Dopaminergic neurons form presynaptic contacts with l-LNvs and s-LNvs in the accessory medulla.

paper_title: Dopamine Signaling in Wake-Promoting Clock Neurons Is Not Required for the Normal Regulation of Sleep in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Bath application of 1 mM dopamine
neural_activity: Increased cAMP levels (imaging); responses mediated by Dop1R1 and/or Dop1R2 (heterogeneous expression).
behavioral_consequence: Constitutive knockdown of Dop1R2 in all clock neurons reduced daytime activity and increased nighttime activity.
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed
summary: LNds respond to dopamine with increased cAMP; dopamine receptor knockdown affects activity levels but not significantly sleep in constitutive mutants.

paper_title: Dopamine Signaling in Wake-Promoting Clock Neurons Is Not Required for the Normal Regulation of Sleep in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Bath application of 1 mM dopamine
neural_activity: Increased cAMP levels (imaging); responses mediated primarily by Dop1R1.
behavioral_consequence: Constitutive knockdown of dopamine receptors in all clock neurons showed no significant changes in sleep pattern.
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed
summary: DN1s respond to dopamine with cAMP increase; dopamine receptor knockdown affects activity levels but not significantly sleep in constitutive mutants.

paper_title: High-Frequency Neuronal Bursting is Essential for Circadian and Sleep Behaviors in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Electrophysiological recording (spontaneous firing); Genetic downregulation of Ih channel (Ih RNAi or null mutant); Light/Dark cycles (LD 12:12) and Constant Darkness (DD)
neural_activity: sLNvs are bursting neurons. Ih is necessary to achieve high-frequency bursting firing pattern. Downregulation of Ih reduces bursting frequency. PDF immunoreactivity at axonal termini is reduced and clamped in a night-like state. Somatic PDF accumulates abnormally at night in Ih knockdown.
behavioral_consequence: Acute Ih downregulation increases total sleep (day and night) with more consolidated sleep (reduced bout number, longer bout duration). Constitutive Ih downregulation decreases circadian locomotor rhythmicity without altering free-running period.
ecological_context: Temporal organization of locomotor activity and sleep; anticipation of daily changes in environmental conditions associated with the rotation of the planet.
techniques_used: ['Whole-cell patch clamp electrophysiology', 'Cell-attached patch clamp', 'Drosophila Activity Monitor (DAM)', 'Immunofluorescence', 'Sholl analysis', 'Confocal microscopy']
summary: sLNvs are bursting neurons dependent on the Ih channel for high-frequency firing. Ih downregulation disrupts PDF transport to terminals and reduces circadian rhythmicity, while acute knockdown increases consolidated sleep.

paper_title: High-Frequency Neuronal Bursting is Essential for Circadian and Sleep Behaviors in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Electrophysiological recording (spontaneous firing); Genetic downregulation of Ih channel (Ih RNAi or null mutant); Light/Dark cycles (LD 12:12)
neural_activity: lLNvs fire action potentials in a bursting mode. Ih is necessary to achieve the high-frequency bursting firing pattern. Downregulation of Ih reduces bursting frequency.
behavioral_consequence: Acute Ih downregulation (using c929-Gal4) increases the number of sleep episodes resulting in increased nighttime sleep, with unchanged bout duration. Constitutive Ih downregulation decreases circadian locomotor rhythmicity.
ecological_context: Circadian organization of locomotor activity; arousal and sleep regulation to maintain temporal organization of behavior.
summary: lLNvs are bursting neurons dependent on Ih for high-frequency firing. Ih downregulation disrupts circadian rhythmicity and increases sleep fragmentation (more bouts).

paper_title: The voltage-gated potassium channel Shaker promotes sleep via thermosensitive GABA transmission
experimental_stimulus: Temperature shift (21°C to 29°C), Light conditions (LD, LL, DD)
neural_activity: Sh mutation elevated intracellular Ca2+ levels (CaLexA imaging). GABAergic synapses onto dFSB showed stronger activity at 21°C compared to 29°C (syb:GRASP imaging).
behavioral_consequence: Sh depletion in these neurons suppressed L sleep at 21°C but not at 29°C, phenocopying Sh mutants. Sh mutant sleep was short and fragmented at 21°C but partially rescued at 29°C.
ecological_context: Temperature-dependent plasticity of sleep behaviors is considered beneficial for animal fitness, consistent with nocturnal activities in wild-type flies at high temperature.
techniques_used: ['CaLexA imaging', 'syb:GRASP imaging', 'Sh RNAi (VGAT-Gal4)', 'Behavioral monitoring (DAM system)', 'Genetic crosses (GAD1-Gal80, VGAT-Gal4)']
summary: Sh-expressing GABAergic neurons promote sleep at low temperature; their function is attenuated at high temperature, contributing to adaptive sleep plasticity.

paper_title: The voltage-gated potassium channel Shaker promotes sleep via thermosensitive GABA transmission
experimental_stimulus: Temperature (21°C vs 29°C), Pharmacological application (GABA, Dopamine, ATP)
neural_activity: GABA suppressed excitability at 21°C; Dopamine silenced excitability at 29°C (P2X2 + sytGCaMP). Dopamine-induced cAMP elevation was more robust at 29°C (Epac1-camps). GABA repressed DA-induced cAMP at 21°C.
behavioral_consequence: Rdl depletion in dFSB neurons lengthened wild-type L sleep at 21°C and non-additively suppressed Sh mutant sleep at 21°C. Sh depletion in dFSB neurons negligibly affected sleep.
ecological_context: Temperature-dependent switching of synaptic transmission modalities adapts neural properties of dFSB neurons to temperature shifts and reorganizes sleep architecture for fitness.
techniques_used: ['Live-brain imaging (P2X2, sytGCaMP)', 'FRET imaging (Epac1-camps)', 'Rdl RNAi (23E10-Gal4)', 'Behavioral monitoring', 'syb:GRASP imaging']
summary: dFSB neurons integrate temperature-gated GABA and Dopamine signals; Rdl in dFSB acts downstream of Sh-expressing neurons to suppress sleep at low temperature.

paper_title: The voltage-gated potassium channel Shaker promotes sleep via thermosensitive GABA transmission
experimental_stimulus: Temperature (21°C vs 29°C)
neural_activity: Dopaminergic synapses onto dFSB showed comparable syb:GRASP signals at both temperatures (not temperature-sensitive).
behavioral_consequence: Not explicitly tested for temperature-dependent behavioral outcomes in this study.
ecological_context: Not explicitly tested in this study.
summary: Dopaminergic synapses onto dFSB neurons do not display temperature sensitivity in contrast to GABAergic synapses.

paper_title: Drosophila insulin-like peptide 2 mediates dietary regulation of sleep intensity
experimental_stimulus: Starvation (1% agar), Sucrose-only diet (5% sucrose), Standard food
neural_activity: Dilp2 protein levels were absent/reduced in knockdown and null mutants (Immunostaining); Dilp2 expression silenced acutely in adults using Dilp2-Geneswitch (RU486)
behavioral_consequence: Loss of Dilp2 function prevents starvation-induced increase in sleep depth (arousal threshold) and results in a homeostatic rebound of sleep duration and depth upon re-feeding (unlike controls which show no rebound)
ecological_context: Suggests a natural resilience to starvation-induced sleep loss, potentially allowing flies to forage at night without requiring rebound sleep the following day
techniques_used: ['DART system (arousal threshold)', 'Drosophila Activity Monitoring (DAM) system (sleep duration)', 'Immunohistochemistry', 'RNAi (UAS-Dilp2RNAi)', 'Genetic mutants (Dilp2null)', 'Geneswitch (Dilp2-GS-GAL4)']
summary: Dilp2-expressing IPCs are required for starvation-induced increases in sleep depth; loss of Dilp2 prevents this depth increase and causes a homeostatic rebound upon re-feeding.

paper_title: Neurocalcin regulates nighttime sleep and arousal in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Nca RNAi knockdown; TrpA1 activation (27°C); Vibration stimuli (50 Hz, 200 ms pulses)
neural_activity: Nca knockdown resulted in significantly enhanced synaptic release in MB αβ-lobes and AMMC (synaptopHluorin imaging). Enhanced release was suppressed in constant light. TrpA1 activation did not measure firing directly but promoted wakefulness.
behavioral_consequence: Nca knockdown reduced night sleep and increased responsiveness to vibration at night (lower arousal threshold). TrpA1 activation profoundly reduced night and day sleep (promoted wake). Combined C01/A05 activation further reduced night sleep compared to C01 alone.
ecological_context: Regulates night sleep and arousal thresholds, operating within a temporal window defined by circadian and light-sensing pathways (active at night, suppressed by light).
summary: C01-neurons promote wakefulness when activated and regulate night sleep and arousal thresholds when NCA is knocked down, acting synergistically with A05-neurons.

paper_title: Neurocalcin regulates nighttime sleep and arousal in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Nca RNAi knockdown; TrpA1 activation (27°C)
neural_activity: Nca knockdown alone did not significantly enhance synaptic release or sleep loss without C01 co-knockdown, but NCA is required in this domain for full night sleep regulation.
behavioral_consequence: Nca knockdown alone did not alter night sleep, but in combination with C01 knockdown, it significantly reduced night sleep. TrpA1 activation alone did not alter sleep, but synergized with C01 activation to further reduce night sleep.
ecological_context: Part of a dispersed wake-promoting neural network that regulates sleep specifically during the night, gated by light conditions.
summary: A05-neurons function in concert with C01-neurons to regulate night sleep and arousal, with activation synergistically promoting wakefulness alongside C01-neurons.

paper_title: Neurocalcin regulates nighttime sleep and arousal in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Nca RNAi knockdown; Vibration stimuli
neural_activity: Nca knockdown in MB-KCs significantly increased responsiveness to mechanical stimuli during the night (arousal).
behavioral_consequence: Nca knockdown reduced the arousal threshold at night but did not significantly reduce night sleep on its own. Sleep reduction occurred when combined with A05-neuron knockdown.
ecological_context: Contributes to the regulation of nocturnal arousal thresholds as part of a larger sleep-regulatory module.
summary: MB-KCs are a relevant component of the C01 expression domain involved in suppressing nocturnal arousal, though not sufficient alone to regulate night sleep duration.

paper_title: Neurocalcin regulates nighttime sleep and arousal in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Nca RNAi knockdown; Nca knockout (NcaKO)
neural_activity: Nca expression reduced in neurons; no direct neural activity measured for pan-neuronal population, but locomotor velocity enhanced during quiescent night periods.
behavioral_consequence: Reduced night sleep but not day sleep; increased locomotor activity during the night; reduced arousal threshold at night; no alteration in circadian rhythmicity.
ecological_context: Establishes the general role of NCA in promoting night sleep and suppressing nocturnal hyperactivity across the neuronal population.
techniques_used: ['Nca RNAi', 'Nca Knockout (homologous recombination)', 'DAM system', 'DART system', 'Video tracking']
summary: Pan-neuronal NCA knockdown or knockout specifically reduces night sleep and increases nighttime arousal and locomotor activity without affecting circadian rhythmicity.

paper_title: Differential regulation of the Drosophila sleep homeostat by circadian and arousal inputs
experimental_stimulus: Hyperactivation via NaChBac or TRPA1; glutamate or dopamine bath application; activation of upstream LPNAstA/SLPAstA or DAA neurons via ATP/P2X2
neural_activity: Glutamate application increases GCaMP6f fluorescence (Ca2+). Dopamine application reduces Ca2+ levels in axonal terminals (syt::GCaMP6s). Activation of LPNAstA/SLPAstA increases GCaMP3 fluorescence. Activation of DAA neurons reduces GCaMP3 fluorescence.
behavioral_consequence: Hyperactivation increases daytime sleep and sleep bout length. Inactivation (TNT) blocks sleep promotion from upstream neurons. Co-activation with DAA neurons leads to fragmented sleep with frequent shifts between sleep and wake states.
ecological_context: Proposed effector component of the sleep homeostat. Sleep trade-off involves critical behaviors like feeding, mating, and defense taking place when awake.
summary: dFB neurons promote sleep via GABA release. They receive glutamatergic input from sleep-promoting neurons and dopaminergic input that inhibits their axonal output. Hyperactivation increases sleep, while simultaneous arousal input fragments sleep.

paper_title: Differential regulation of the Drosophila sleep homeostat by circadian and arousal inputs
cell_type: LPNAstA neurons (Lateral Posterior Neurons expressing Allatostatin-A)
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson), thermogenetic activation (NaChBac), ATP/P2X2 channel activation
neural_activity: Activation increases GCaMP3 fluorescence in dFB neurons.
behavioral_consequence: Activation increases daytime sleep. Sleep-promoting effect is reduced by VGlut RNAi, AstALexA mutation, or dFB inactivation. Identified as circadian pacemaker neurons expressing Timeless and Period.
ecological_context: Function as circadian pacemaker neurons providing circadian input to the sleep homeostat.
summary: LPNAstA neurons are circadian pacemaker neurons that promote sleep via glutamatergic and AstA signaling onto dFB neurons.

paper_title: Differential regulation of the Drosophila sleep homeostat by circadian and arousal inputs
cell_type: SLPAstA neurons (Superior Lateral Protocerebrum neurons expressing Allatostatin-A)
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson), thermogenetic activation (NaChBac)
neural_activity: Activation increases GCaMP3 fluorescence in dFB neurons.
behavioral_consequence: Activation increases daytime sleep. Sleep-promoting effect depends on VGlut and dFB neurons.
ecological_context: Sleep-promoting neurons functioning upstream of the sleep homeostat.
summary: SLPAstA neurons are a second group of sleep-promoting neurons labeled by AstA reporters that activate dFB neurons via glutamate.

paper_title: Differential regulation of the Drosophila sleep homeostat by circadian and arousal inputs
experimental_stimulus: Hyperactivation via NaChBac, ATP/P2X2 channel activation
neural_activity: Activation reduces GCaMP3 fluorescence in dFB neurons. Dopamine application reduces Ca2+ levels in dFB axonal terminals.
behavioral_consequence: Hyperactivation drastically reduces total sleep time and fragments sleep (reduced bout length, increased bout number). Co-activation with LPNAstA/SLPAstA antagonizes sleep promotion.
ecological_context: Arousal-promoting neurons that provide an essential balance against sleep-promoting circuits.
summary: DAA neurons inhibit dFB output via dopamine, promoting wakefulness. Simultaneous activation with sleep-promoting neurons prevents consolidated sleep.

paper_title: Differential regulation of the Drosophila sleep homeostat by circadian and arousal inputs
experimental_stimulus: Hyperactivation via NaChBac, dFB neuron activation via ATP/P2X2
neural_activity: Activation of dFB neurons reduces GCaMP3 fluorescence in OAA neurons.
behavioral_consequence: Hyperactivation fragments nighttime sleep (increased bout number, reduced bout length). Inhibition by dFB neurons confers long sleep bouts at night.
ecological_context: Arousal-promoting neurons involved in the regulation of sleep-wake states.
summary: OAA neurons promote arousal and fragment sleep. They are inhibited by GABAergic dFB neurons to allow for long sleep bouts.

paper_title: Wakefulness Is Promoted during Day Time by PDFR Signalling to Dopaminergic Neurons in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Light/Dark cycles (LD12:12), varying light intensities (10, 300, 2000 lux), functional ablation (reaper), hyperexcitation (NachBac), dysfunction (Htt Q128)
neural_activity: PDF neuropeptide levels high during day (ZT4), low at night (ZT14). GRASP shows synaptic contacts with dopaminergic neurons in s-LNv axonal projections. PDFR signaling inhibits dopaminergic neurons.
behavioral_consequence: PDFR signaling promotes wakefulness during the day. Loss of pdfr increases day-time sleep. Ablation of both LNv subsets increases day-time sleep at low light intensity. Hyperexcitation reduces night-time sleep.
ecological_context: Organisms need to remain awake during appropriate times of the day to engage in activities such as feeding and mating that increase their chances of survival.
techniques_used: ['Sleep assays (DAM2 monitors)', 'GRASP (GFP reconstitution across synaptic partners)', 'Immunocytochemistry', 'Genetic ablation/activation (reaper, NachBac, Htt Q128)']
summary: s-LNv and l-LNv release PDF, which forms synaptic contacts with dopaminergic neurons. PDFR signaling promotes wakefulness during the day, and ablation of these neurons increases day-time sleep, especially at low light intensity.

paper_title: Wakefulness Is Promoted during Day Time by PDFR Signalling to Dopaminergic Neurons in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: pdfr downregulation (RNAi) or overexpression (OEX), time of day (ZT4 vs ZT14), thermal activation (dTRPA1 at 21°C vs 29°C)
neural_activity: Intracellular Ca2+ levels (CaLexA) lower during day (ZT4) than night (ZT14) in wild-type (PDFR functional). In pdfr mutants, Ca2+ remains high at both times. GRASP shows overlap between TH+ and PDFR+ neurons in PPM3 subset.
behavioral_consequence: pdfr downregulation in dopaminergic neurons increases day-time sleep. pdfr overexpression decreases day-time sleep (promotes wakefulness). dTRPA1 activation of TH-F3 (PPM3) neurons did not significantly change sleep, suggesting heterogeneity with some sleep-promoting neurons.
ecological_context: PDF/PDFR signaling inhibits sleep-promoting dopaminergic neurons during the day to promote wakefulness, bridging circadian and homeostatic regulation.
techniques_used: ['Sleep assays (DAM2 monitors)', 'CaLexA (calcium-dependent nuclear transport)', 'GRASP', 'dTRPA1 thermal activation', 'Immunocytochemistry']
summary: PDFR signaling inhibits PPM3 dopaminergic neurons specifically during the day (lower Ca2+ levels). Downregulation of pdfr in these neurons increases day-time sleep, while overexpression decreases it, indicating these neurons promote sleep.

paper_title: A Drosophila Model of Essential Tremor
experimental_stimulus: Voltage clamp depolarization steps (from -133 mV to -3 mV); current injection for spontaneous firing rate
neural_activity: Expression of hKv9.2 or hKv9.2-D379E altered native Shab (Kv2) channel kinetics, causing increased inactivation. Mutant hKv9.2-D379E caused significantly higher spontaneous action potential firing frequency compared to wild-type hKv9.2.
behavioral_consequence: Expression caused significant increase in night-time activity and reduction in night-time sleep.
ecological_context: Circadian locomotor rhythms and sleep regulation
techniques_used: ['Whole-cell voltage clamp', 'Current clamp recordings', 'Drosophila Activity Monitor (DAM)']
summary: l-LNv clock neurons expressing mutant hKv9.2-D379E show increased Shab inactivation and higher spontaneous firing, leading to increased night-time activity and reduced sleep.

paper_title: A Drosophila Model of Essential Tremor
experimental_stimulus: Pan-neural expression via Elav-Gal4; Ether anesthetization; Light/Dark cycles
neural_activity: Western blot verified expression of hKv9.2 and hKv9.2-D379E in Drosophila. Pan-neural expression altered Shab kinetics consistent with neuronal hyperexcitability.
behavioral_consequence: Flies displayed significantly faster climbing response throughout lifespan. Approximately 40% displayed abnormal wing posture (bilateral wing elevation). Ether anesthetization induced leg shaking, abdominal pulsations, and body shuddering. Post-developmental expression reduced lifespan.
ecological_context: General locomotor function and motor stability
techniques_used: ['Western Blot', 'Negative Geotaxis Climbing Assay', 'Ether anesthetization observation', 'Drosophila Activity Monitor (DAM)', 'Lifespan Assay']
summary: Pan-neural expression of hKv9.2 or mutant hKv9.2-D379E results in neuronal hyperexcitability, faster climbing, abnormal wing posture, tremor under ether, and reduced lifespan.

paper_title: Downregulation of DmMANF in Glial Cells Results in Neurodegeneration and Affects Sleep and Lifespan in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Downregulation of DmMANF using repo-Gal4 driver (pan-glial RNAi)
neural_activity: Not measured (structural and molecular assays performed)
behavioral_consequence: Reduced lifespan (median 70 days vs 83 days control); decreased activity during light phase; increased activity during dark phase; reduced sleep bins during dark phase; increased sleep bins at beginning of day
ecological_context: Visual system maintenance and circadian plasticity; histamine recycling at capitate projections; blood-brain barrier integrity for detoxification
techniques_used: ['RNAi', 'Immunohistochemistry', 'Confocal Microscopy', 'Transmission Electron Microscopy', 'Western Blotting', 'Drosophila Activity Monitoring (DAM2)', 'Lifespan Analysis']
summary: Silencing DmMANF in epithelial glial cells induces neurodegeneration in the lamina (holes, ER membrane folds), reduces lifespan, and alters circadian locomotor activity and sleep patterns.

paper_title: Downregulation of DmMANF in Glial Cells Results in Neurodegeneration and Affects Sleep and Lifespan in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Downregulation of DmMANF using elav-Gal4 (pan-neuronal), GMR-RH1 (photoreceptors), or 21D-Gal4 (L2 interneurons) drivers
neural_activity: Not measured (structural and molecular assays performed)
behavioral_consequence: Pan-neuronal silencing: decreased activity in light phase; no significant change in lifespan or total activity; Photoreceptor/L2 silencing: no impact on visual system structure
ecological_context: General neural homeostasis and visual information transmission
summary: Silencing DmMANF in neurons (pan-neuronal, photoreceptors, or L2 interneurons) did not cause visible neurodegeneration in the visual system, though pan-neuronal silencing reduced light-phase locomotor activity without affecting lifespan.

paper_title: Pharmacochaperoning in a Drosophila model system rescues human dopamine transporter variants associated with infantile/juvenile parkinsonism
experimental_stimulus: Expression of human DAT mutants (hDAT-V158F, hDAT-G327R) or pharmacological treatment with noribogaine and pifithrin-μ
neural_activity: Confocal microscopy visualized hDAT trafficking; wild-type hDAT delivered to axonal territories while mutants were confined to cell bodies; pharmacochaperoning restored trafficking to presynaptic terminals in the fan-shaped body; no electrophysiological activity reported
behavioral_consequence: DAT-deficient fumin mutants and hDAT-expressing mutants showed hyperlocomotion and reduced sleep (~300 min/day); pharmacochaperone treatment restored sleep duration (~1000 min/day), reduced locomotion, and increased grooming (specifically head grooming)
techniques_used: ['Confocal microscopy', 'Drosophila activity monitoring system (TriKinetics)', 'Video recording for grooming analysis']
summary: Dopaminergic neurons (PPL1/PPM3) expressing hDAT mutants show impaired trafficking to presynaptic terminals; pharmacochaperone treatment restores trafficking and normalizes sleep, locomotion, and grooming behaviors.

paper_title: The role of PDF neurons in setting the preferred temperature before dawn in Drosophila
cell_type: sLNvs (small Lateral Ventral Neurons / subset of PDF neurons)
experimental_stimulus: Circadian time (ZT1-24), ATP application (via P2X2 receptor expression)
neural_activity: GRASP showed physical contacts with DN2s peak before dawn (ZT22-24). Calcium imaging showed increased fluorescence in sLNvs upon bath application of 3 mM ATP (P2X2 activation).
behavioral_consequence: Inactivation (Kir2.1) or Clock disruption (ClockΔ) caused lower temperature preference before dawn (ZT19-24). Knockdown of 5HT1B receptor in PDF neurons (verified with R6-Gal4 for sLNvs) caused lower temperature preference at ZT22-24.
ecological_context: Setting preferred temperature before dawn (ZT22-24), coinciding with waking up and sleep regulation interactions.
techniques_used: ['Temperature preference behavior assay', 'GRASP (GFP Reconstitution Across Synaptic Partners)', 'Calcium imaging (GCaMP3.0)', 'Genetic inhibition (UAS-Kir2.1)', 'Genetic clock disruption (UAS-ClockΔ)', 'RNAi (5HT1B-RNAi)']
summary: sLNvs physically contact DN2s with peak connectivity before dawn and activate DN2s via ATP/P2X2. sLNv activity and 5HT1B receptor expression are required for normal temperature preference before dawn.

paper_title: The role of PDF neurons in setting the preferred temperature before dawn in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Indirect activation via sLNv-P2X2 pathway
neural_activity: Calcium imaging showed increased fluorescence in DN2s following sLNv activation (ATP/P2X2). GRASP showed contacts with sLNvs peak before dawn.
behavioral_consequence: Inactivation (Kir2.1) caused lower preferred temperatures at all times of the day. Inhibition changed the set-point of Temperature Preference Rhythm (TPR) but did not eliminate rhythmicity.
ecological_context: Main clock cells regulating Temperature Preference Rhythm (TPR) and temperature set-point.
summary: DN2s receive input from sLNvs peaking before dawn and are required for proper temperature preference set-point, though not for TPR rhythmicity alone.

paper_title: The role of PDF neurons in setting the preferred temperature before dawn in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Temperature (targeted as warmth sensors)
neural_activity: Described as serotonergic neurons. GRASP showed contacts with sLNvs in the dorsal protocerebrum.
behavioral_consequence: Inactivation (Kir2.1) or TRPA1 knockdown caused significantly lower temperature preference before dawn (ZT22-24). Not required for temperature entrainment to cycles.
ecological_context: Thermosensory input regulating pre-dawn temperature preference behavior.
summary: ACs contact sLNvs and their activity (via TRPA1) is required for normal temperature preference before dawn, but not for circadian temperature entrainment.

paper_title: Amyloid Precursor Protein in Drosophila Glia Regulates Sleep and Genes Involved in Glutamate Recycling
experimental_stimulus: Inhibition (RNAi) or overexpression of Amyloid Precursor Protein (Appl); Sleep deprivation (25h)
neural_activity: No electrophysiological activity measured. Molecular phenotype: Inx2 protein distribution became interrupted and clumpy upon Appl inhibition; Inx2 levels increased upon Appl overexpression; GS protein expression in calyx reduced upon Appl inhibition.
behavioral_consequence: Appl inhibition resulted in higher sleep amounts and longer sleep bout duration during the night. Appl overexpression reduced sleep amounts. Sleep deprivation increased Inx2 and GS expression levels.
ecological_context: Regulation of sleep-wake vigilance states.
summary: Appl expression in cortex glia is required for sleep/wake regulation; its inhibition increases night sleep, while overexpression reduces sleep, correlated with changes in Inx2 protein distribution and levels.

paper_title: Amyloid Precursor Protein in Drosophila Glia Regulates Sleep and Genes Involved in Glutamate Recycling
experimental_stimulus: Inhibition of Appl (via dEaat1-Gal4 driver); Inhibition of dEaat1 transporter; Inhibition of Glutamine Synthetase (GS)
neural_activity: No electrophysiological activity measured. Molecular phenotype: GS protein expression reduced in calyx upon Appl inhibition; GS levels increased following sleep deprivation; dEaat1 transcript levels upregulated upon Appl inhibition.
behavioral_consequence: Appl inhibition in this subtype (when combined with cortex glia) led to higher sleep amounts. Inhibition of dEaat1 transporter specifically led to increased wakefulness and disrupted circadian patterning. Combined GS and Inx2 inhibition increased sleep.
ecological_context: Glutamate recycling and regulation of sleep-wake vigilance states.
summary: Astrocyte-like glia contribute to sleep regulation via glutamate recycling genes; Appl inhibition reduces GS expression, while dEaat1 inhibition increases wakefulness.

paper_title: MicroRNA-92a is a circadian modulator of neuronal excitability in Drosophila
cell_type: PDF cells (Pigment Dispersing Factor pacemaker neurons)
experimental_stimulus: 10-min light pulses (ZT15, ZT21), high concentration KCl perfusion, nicotine application
neural_activity: mir-92a levels cycle (peak at night). mir-92a overexpression suppresses KCl-induced depolarization (ArcLight) and lowers basal Ca2+ levels (CaLexA, GCaMP6). mir-92a knockdown increases Ca2+ levels. mir-92a levels increase after ZT15 light pulse and decrease after ZT21 light pulse.
behavioral_consequence: mir-92a overexpression maintains PDF projections in a fasciculated state; knockdown leads to defasciculation. mir-92a knockdown decreases sleep duration. mir-92a overexpression increases magnitude of phase shift responses to light pulses.
ecological_context: Circadian rhythms entrained by light:dark cycles; PDF cells dictate circadian period and regulate phase shift response.
techniques_used: ['RT-qPCR', 'Sholl analysis', 'ArcLight imaging', 'CaLexA bioluminescence', 'GCaMP6 imaging', 'Locomotor activity monitors (sleep/phase shift)']
summary: PDF cells are pacemaker neurons where mir-92a levels cycle and regulate neuronal excitability (Ca2+ levels) and projection morphology, affecting sleep duration and phase shift responses to light.

paper_title: MicroRNA-92a is a circadian modulator of neuronal excitability in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: mir-92a overexpression/knockdown, sirt2 RNAi
neural_activity: Not directly measured.
behavioral_consequence: mir-92a overexpression increases sleep duration. mir-92a knockdown reduces sleep duration. sirt2 RNAi phenocopies mir-92a overexpression (increases sleep).
ecological_context: Wake-promoting regulation of sleep-wake cycles.
summary: Dopaminergic neurons regulate wakefulness; mir-92a overexpression increases sleep while knockdown reduces sleep duration.

paper_title: MicroRNA-92a is a circadian modulator of neuronal excitability in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: mir-92a overexpression/knockdown
neural_activity: Not directly measured.
behavioral_consequence: mir-92a overexpression leads to significant decrease in sleep duration. Knockdown had no effect.
ecological_context: Sleep regulation in circadian clock network.
summary: Sleep-promoting dorsal clock neurons; mir-92a overexpression decreases sleep duration.

paper_title: TOR signaling pathway and autophagy are involved in the regulation of circadian rhythms in behavior and plasticity of L2 interneurons in the brain of Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Circadian time points (ZT1, ZT4, ZT13, ZT16) under LD 12:12 conditions
neural_activity: Structural plasticity measured via dendritic tree perimeter in distal lamina; rhythm with peak size at ZT13 (beginning of night) in wild-type flies
ecological_context: Circadian structural plasticity in the visual system (lamina) correlated with locomotor activity pattern
summary: L2 Monopolar Cells show circadian structural plasticity in dendritic tree perimeter (peak at ZT13). Silencing Tor or Atg5 in L2 cells abolishes this rhythm and enlarges trees; silencing Atg7 alters the pattern (peak at ZT1).

paper_title: TOR signaling pathway and autophagy are involved in the regulation of circadian rhythms in behavior and plasticity of L2 interneurons in the brain of Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Circadian conditions (LD 12:12, DD)
neural_activity: Cyclic expression of Tor, Atg5, and Atg7 genes in wild-type brains (abolished in per01 mutants)
behavioral_consequence: Silencing Tor shortened locomotor activity period (by 0.5h), reduced morning activity peak, enhanced evening peak, and increased sleep duration in dark phase. Silencing Tsc1 increased total activity. Silencing Atg7 increased sleep duration in dark phase.
ecological_context: Regulation of circadian locomotor activity and sleep rhythms
summary: Silencing Tor or autophagy genes in per-expressing clock cells alters circadian gene expression and locomotor activity rhythms, indicating involvement in behavioral regulation.

paper_title: Drosophila Clock Is Required in Brain Pacemaker Neurons to Prevent Premature Locomotor Aging Independently of Its Circadian Function
experimental_stimulus: Genetic inactivation of Clock (ClkRNAi) using pdf-Gal4 or R6-Gal4 drivers; Ablation using hid expression
behavioral_consequence: Clk inactivation accelerated age-related locomotor impairment (startle-induced negative geotaxis/SING assay); Ablation of PDF neurons had no effect on locomotor aging
techniques_used: ['Gal4-UAS system (RNAi, overexpression, ablation)', 'Startle-induced negative geotaxis (SING) assay', 'Immunohistochemistry', 'Confocal microscopy']
summary: Inactivation of Clock specifically in s-LNvs accelerates locomotor aging independently of circadian rhythmicity, whereas ablating PDF neurons does not alter this phenotype.

paper_title: Drosophila Clock Is Required in Brain Pacemaker Neurons to Prevent Premature Locomotor Aging Independently of Its Circadian Function
experimental_stimulus: Clock mutation (ClkAR), ClkRNAi in s-LNvs, Dopamine synthesis inhibition (TH-RNAi via MZ840-Gal4), Apoptosis inhibition (H99 deficiency)
behavioral_consequence: Loss of TH-IR cells in PPL1 cluster correlated with accelerated locomotor decline; Inhibiting TH in single MB-V1 pair accelerated locomotor decline
techniques_used: ['Immunohistochemistry (TH staining)', 'Confocal microscopy', 'Startle-induced negative geotaxis (SING) assay', 'Gal4-UAS system']
summary: PPL1 dopaminergic neurons, particularly MB-V1, are required for maintaining locomotor performance; their loss in ClkAR mutants is dependent on PDF receptor signaling and apoptotic pathways.

paper_title: Drosophila Clock Is Required in Brain Pacemaker Neurons to Prevent Premature Locomotor Aging Independently of Its Circadian Function
experimental_stimulus: Genetic inactivation of Clock (ClkRNAi) using C929-Gal4 driver
behavioral_consequence: Clk inactivation in l-LNvs had no effect on age-related locomotor impairment
summary: Clk inactivation in l-LNvs does not affect locomotor aging, distinguishing their function from s-LNvs in this context.

paper_title: When transporters fail to be transported: how to rescue folding-deficient SLC6 transporters
experimental_stimulus: Pharmacological treatment with noribogaine or HSP70 inhibitor pifithrin-μ
behavioral_consequence: Restoration of normal sleep time in mutant flies
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed
techniques_used: ['Fluorescence microscopy (GFP-tagged DAT, ER marker RFP-KDEL)', 'Sleep behavior assays']
summary: Pharmacochaperoning with noribogaine or HSP70 inhibition restores axonal delivery of folding-deficient DAT (dDAT-G108Q/hDAT-G140Q) in dopaminergic neurons and rescues the sleepless phenotype in Drosophila.

paper_title: Drosophila DH31 Neuropeptide and PDF Receptor Regulate Night-Onset Temperature Preference
experimental_stimulus: Circadian cycle (Light/Dark conditions, ZT1-ZT24)
neural_activity: Not directly measured
behavioral_consequence: Expression of PDFR or tethered-DH31 (t-DH31) in DN2s restores the preferred temperature decrease at night-onset (ZT10–12 to ZT13–15) in Pdfr and Dh31 mutants respectively. Expression in other clock neurons (e.g., s-LNvs, DN1ps) does not rescue this phenotype.
ecological_context: Regulates temperature preference rhythm (TPR) which generates body temperature rhythm in small ectotherms during the transition from day to night.
summary: DN2s are critical for night-onset temperature preference decrease; PDFR and t-DH31 expression in DN2s rescues the deficit in Pdfr and Dh31 mutants.

paper_title: Drosophila DH31 Neuropeptide and PDF Receptor Regulate Night-Onset Temperature Preference
experimental_stimulus: Circadian cycle (Light/Dark conditions, ZT1-ZT24)
neural_activity: Not directly measured
behavioral_consequence: DH31 is expressed in DN1ps (immunohistochemistry). Expression of PDFR or t-DH31 in DN1ps does not rescue the night-onset TPR defect in mutants.
ecological_context: DH31 is expressed here but not sufficient for night-onset TPR regulation; previously linked to sleep regulation in other contexts (cited).
summary: DN1ps express DH31 but are not sufficient to regulate night-onset temperature preference; rescue of TPR defect requires DN2 expression.

paper_title: Drosophila DH31 Neuropeptide and PDF Receptor Regulate Night-Onset Temperature Preference
experimental_stimulus: Circadian cycle (Light/Dark conditions, ZT1-ZT24)
neural_activity: Not directly measured
behavioral_consequence: Expression of t-DH31 in LNvs does not rescue the night-onset TPR defect in Dh31 mutants.
ecological_context: PDF is known to regulate locomotor activity rhythms but PDF expression in LNvs is not required for night-onset TPR.
summary: LNvs are not the site where DH31 regulates night-onset temperature preference; t-DH31 expression here fails to rescue mutant TPR phenotype.

paper_title: TfAP-2 is required for night sleep in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: TfAP-2 RNA interference (RNAi) knockdown
neural_activity: TfAP-2 mRNA reduced by ~60% in nervous system; TfAP-2 protein expression decreased in l-LNv neurons measured by antibody staining
behavioral_consequence: Pan-neuronal knockdown almost completely abolished night sleep but did not affect day sleep; adult-specific knockdown (using tubGal80ts) caused a modest reduction in night sleep
ecological_context: Night sleep is a prominent sleep behavior in Drosophila under 12h light-12h dark cycle conditions
techniques_used: ['RNA interference', 'Sleep monitoring (video and infrared beam)', 'RT-qPCR', 'Immunohistochemistry', 'Temperature-sensitive Gal80']
summary: TfAP-2 is required in the nervous system for night sleep; knockdown abolishes night sleep, while adult-specific knockdown causes a modest reduction.

paper_title: TfAP-2 is required for night sleep in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: TfAP-2 RNAi using 201yGal4 driver
neural_activity: TfAP-2 expressed in MB neuroblasts during pupal stage; low expression in larval MB lobes
behavioral_consequence: MB-specific knockdown did not cause a sleep phenotype; pan-neuronal knockdown affected MB morphology (slimmer/disrupted axonal projections)
ecological_context: MB lobes are implicated in sleep control and learning/memory in Drosophila
summary: TfAP-2 is expressed in MB neuroblasts and required for MB development; however, specific MB knockdown did not alter sleep behavior.

paper_title: TfAP-2 is required for night sleep in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: TfAP-2 RNAi using sNPFGal4 drivers
neural_activity: TfAP-2 protein expressed in sNPF neurons; TfAP-2 reduction did not affect sNPF mRNA or protein levels
behavioral_consequence: sNPF-specific knockdown did not cause a sleep phenotype
ecological_context: Short neuropeptide F (sNPF) is known to regulate sleep in Drosophila
summary: TfAP-2 is expressed in sNPF neurons but acts independently of sNPF levels to control sleep in this context.

paper_title: TfAP-2 is required for night sleep in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: TfAP-2 RNAi using TimGal4 and PDF-Gal4 drivers
neural_activity: TfAP-2 protein expressed in l-LNv neurons; TimGal4 > TfAP-2RNAi significantly reduced TfAP-2 antibody staining intensity
behavioral_consequence: TimGal4 and PDF-Gal4 specific knockdowns did not give a sleep phenotype
ecological_context: l-LNv neurons are circadian clock neurons associated with sleep regulation
summary: TfAP-2 is expressed in l-LNv circadian neurons, but specific knockdown in these neurons did not result in sleep defects.

paper_title: TfAP-2 is required for night sleep in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: TfAP-2 RNAi using gad-Gal4 driver
neural_activity: Expression not explicitly detailed, knockdown performed
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown did not cause a sleep phenotype
ecological_context: GABAergic neurons are known to play a role in sleep control
summary: Knockdown of TfAP-2 in GABAergic neurons did not produce a sleep phenotype.

paper_title: TfAP-2 is required for night sleep in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: TfAP-2 RNAi using tdc-Gal4 driver
neural_activity: Expression not explicitly detailed, knockdown performed
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown did not cause a sleep phenotype
ecological_context: Octopamine is known to regulate sleep in Drosophila
summary: Knockdown of TfAP-2 in octopaminergic neurons did not produce a sleep phenotype.

paper_title: Regulation of sleep plasticity by a thermo-sensitive circuit in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Elevated ambient temperature (30–31 °C)
neural_activity: TrpA1 channel signaling required; acute inhibition of synaptic output (UAS-shits) blocks function
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown or inhibition suppresses Prolonged Morning Wakefulness (PMW), specifically delaying siesta onset at high temperatures
ecological_context: Integrates temperature information to ensure sleep is not initiated during detrimental conditions in the wild
techniques_used: ['UAS-shits (acute inhibition)', 'TrpA1-RNAi knockdown', 'Drosophila Activity Monitoring (DAM) system', 'GRASP (GFP Reconstitution Across Synaptic Partners)']
summary: TrpA1[SH]-expressing neurons are required for temperature-induced delays in morning sleep onset (PMW), projecting to the dorsal posterior protocerebrum.

paper_title: Regulation of sleep plasticity by a thermo-sensitive circuit in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Elevated ambient temperature (30–31 °C)
neural_activity: TrpA1 expression required; acute inhibition of synaptic output (UAS-shits) blocks function
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown or inhibition suppresses PMW; distinct from mdIV neurons
ecological_context: Integrates temperature information to match sleep onset with environmental conditions
summary: A specific subset of ppk-expressing neurons projecting to the dorsal posterior protocerebrum is required for temperature-dependent PMW.

paper_title: Regulation of sleep plasticity by a thermo-sensitive circuit in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Elevated ambient temperature (30 °C); Blue-light (via CRY)
neural_activity: Synaptic output required for PMW; receives input from TrpA1-expressing neurons (GRASP)
behavioral_consequence: Inhibiting synaptic output suppresses PMW; loss of CRYPTOCHROME (CRY) in DN1p suppresses PMW
ecological_context: Integrates temperature, light, and time to dynamically control when sleep is initiated
summary: DN1p clock neurons integrate thermo-sensory and light information to promote wakefulness during hot mornings and delay siesta onset.

paper_title: Regulation of sleep plasticity by a thermo-sensitive circuit in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Elevated ambient temperature; Light
neural_activity: CRY expressed; neurotransmitter/neuropeptide release inhibition tested
behavioral_consequence: Inhibition of output did NOT suppress PMW
ecological_context: Part of the circadian network, but not the primary driver of temperature-induced sleep plasticity in this context
summary: Inhibition of s-LNvs and LNds output does not suppress PMW, indicating a privileged role for DN1p neurons in this circuit.

paper_title: Allatostatin A Signalling in Drosophila Regulates Feeding and Sleep and Is Modulated by PDF
experimental_stimulus: Thermogenetic activation via UAS-TrpA1 at 29°C; Conditional silencing via UAS-Kir2.1/ΔORK at 30°C via TARGET system; Ex vivo PDF peptide application (10 μM).
neural_activity: Increased intracellular cAMP upon PDF application (cAMP live imaging). Functional PDF receptor expression confirmed.
behavioral_consequence: Activation significantly reduced food intake (CAFE assay), reduced locomotor activity (DAM system), promoted sleep (increased total sleep and bout duration), and lowered arousal threshold. Conditional silencing increased locomotor activity and reduced sleep, particularly during the midday siesta.
ecological_context: Pleiotropic AstA signalling helps adapt the fly to a digestive energy-saving state, coordinating behavior and gut physiology for efficient digestion.
techniques_used: ['CAFE assay', 'DAM system', 'Video monitoring', 'Arousal assay (loudspeaker/shaker)', 'cAMP live imaging', 'Immunostaining', 'Thermogenetics']
summary: PLP neurons express functional PDF receptors and mediate AstA signalling; their activation reduces feeding and locomotion while promoting sleep, suggesting a role in energy conservation and digestion.

paper_title: Allatostatin A Signalling in Drosophila Regulates Feeding and Sleep and Is Modulated by PDF
experimental_stimulus: Thermogenetic activation via AstA34-Gal4/UAS-TrpA1 at 29°C.
neural_activity: Immunostaining confirms AstA expression in midgut EECs; unable to genetically dissociate from PLP neurons for behavioral testing in this study.
behavioral_consequence: Activation reduced food intake, reduced locomotor activity, and promoted sleep (similar to PLP neuron activation). Conditional silencing increased locomotor activity and decreased sleep.
ecological_context: Potentially signal nutritional information from the gut to brain centers regulating feeding and sleep to adapt the fly to a digestive energy-saving state.
summary: AstA-expressing EECs in the midgut contribute to feeding and sleep regulation, potentially signaling gut nutritional status to the brain.

paper_title: Allatostatin A Signalling in Drosophila Regulates Feeding and Sleep and Is Modulated by PDF
experimental_stimulus: Thermogenetic activation via AstA34-Gal4/UAS-TrpA1 at 29°C; Genetic restriction to brain neurons using tsh-Gal80.
neural_activity: Not directly measured.
behavioral_consequence: Activation reduced feeding and locomotion; however, restriction of activation to brain neurons (excluding DLAa via tsh-Gal80) reproduced the phenotype, indicating DLAa neurons are dispensable for feeding inhibition and sleep promotion in this context.
ecological_context: None explicitly stated for this specific subset in the results.
summary: DLAa neurons are not required for the observed AstA-mediated reduction in feeding and sleep promotion; PLP neurons and/or EECs are sufficient.

paper_title: Allatostatin A Signalling in Drosophila Regulates Feeding and Sleep and Is Modulated by PDF
experimental_stimulus: Thermogenetic activation via R6-Gal4/UAS-TrpA1 at 29°C.
neural_activity: Anatomical overlap with PLP neuron projections in the superior protocerebrum.
behavioral_consequence: Activation promoted sleep specifically during morning and evening activity peaks, but had a smaller effect than direct AstA cell activation. Did not significantly alter total sleep or period.
ecological_context: PDF is a major synchronisation and output factor of the circadian clock, affecting the timing of sleep and feeding.
summary: PDF-expressing sLNvs contact PLP neurons and modulate sleep, suggesting a circadian clock output pathway to AstA cells.

paper_title: Artificial Induction of Associative Olfactory Memory by Optogenetic and Thermogenetic Activation of Olfactory Sensory Neurons and Octopaminergic Neurons in Drosophila Larvae
experimental_stimulus: Blue light (468 nm) + All-trans-retinal (1 mM in food)
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation of specific ORN classes (e.g., Or24a, Or42b) expressing Channelrhodopsin 2 (ChR2)
behavioral_consequence: When paired with heat activation of OA neurons, induces associative memory resulting in increased olfactory response to specific odorants (Acetophenone for Or24a, Ethyl acetate for Or42b) and suppression of negative phototaxis toward blue light. Unpaired activation causes no behavioral alteration.
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed in this paper
summary: Optogenetic activation of specific ORNs substitutes natural odor signals. Concomitant activation with OA neurons induces associative olfactory memory, increasing response to specific odorants and suppressing phototaxis.

paper_title: Artificial Induction of Associative Olfactory Memory by Optogenetic and Thermogenetic Activation of Olfactory Sensory Neurons and Octopaminergic Neurons in Drosophila Larvae
experimental_stimulus: Heat (28°C agar surface)
neural_activity: Thermogenetic activation of Tdc2-expressing OA neurons via dTrpA1 channel
behavioral_consequence: When paired with ORN activation, substitutes natural reward signal to induce associative memory (increased olfactory response to odorants, suppression of negative phototaxis). Acute activation alone (60 s) does not alter phototactic or olfactory responses.
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed in this paper
summary: Thermogenetic activation of OA neurons substitutes natural reward signals. Concomitant activation with ORNs induces associative memory, whereas activation alone does not alter behavior.

paper_title: ADAR-mediated RNA editing suppresses sleep by acting as a brake on glutamatergic synaptic plasticity
experimental_stimulus: Genetic manipulation (Adar RNAi knockdown, NaChBac depolarization)
neural_activity: Upregulated DVGLUT protein, expanded reserve pool of synaptic vesicles (measured at larval NMJ), over-activation of NMDA receptors
behavioral_consequence: Increased total daily sleep, destabilization of waking state (shorter wake bouts), increased sleep pressure
ecological_context: Maintenance of sleep pressure and waking stability; sleep need linked to experience-dependent synaptic potentiation
techniques_used: ['GAL4/UAS system', 'RNAi', 'Sleep monitoring (Drosophila Activity Monitoring System)', 'Electrophysiology (NMJ recordings)', 'Western blotting']
summary: 40B03 sleep-promoting glutamatergic neurons require Adar to suppress sleep; loss of Adar leads to vesicle pool expansion and NMDAR over-activation, resulting in increased sleep.

paper_title: ADAR-mediated RNA editing suppresses sleep by acting as a brake on glutamatergic synaptic plasticity
experimental_stimulus: Adar RNAi knockdown
neural_activity: ADAR protein does not express in adult Kenyon cells
behavioral_consequence: No changes in sleep observed
ecological_context: None explicitly discussed
summary: Adar RNAi in mushroom bodies does not alter sleep, indicating the sleep phenotype is not mediated by Kenyon cells.

paper_title: Ca-α1T, a fly T-type Ca2+ channel, negatively modulates sleep
cell_type: Ca-α1T-expressing neurons (broadly distributed in adult fly brain)
experimental_stimulus: Genetic knockout or knockdown of Ca-α1T channel (Ca-α1TGal4, Ca-α1TRescue, pan-neuronal RNAi)
neural_activity: Loss of Ca-α1T channel function; not directly recorded from fly neurons but channel characterized as passing low-voltage-activated (LVA) Ca2+ currents in expression systems
