behavioral_consequence: Increased total sleep duration (most pronounced during subjective day under continuous dark); increased sleep bout length; elongated circadian period and reduced rhythmic power in constant darkness
ecological_context: Regulation of sleep homeostasis and circadian rhythm stability under light-dark and constant dark conditions
techniques_used: ['Genetic manipulation (mutants, RNAi)', 'Sleep monitoring (Drosophila Activity Monitoring)', 'Electrophysiology (Xenopus oocytes, HEK-293 cells)', 'Immunohistochemistry/Imaging (GFP tagging)']
summary: Ca-α1T-expressing neurons broadly in the adult brain negatively modulate sleep; loss of Ca-α1T leads to increased sleep duration and altered circadian rhythmicity, though specific neuronal subsets tested individually did not show significant sleep alterations.

paper_title: The Ion Transport Peptide Is a New Functional Clock Neuropeptide in the Fruit Fly Drosophila melanogaster
cell_type: ITP-expressing clock neurons (fifth sLNv and LNd)
experimental_stimulus: Light-Dark (LD 12:12) and Constant Darkness (DD) cycles; RNA interference (RNAi) knockdown of ITP; Overexpression of ITP
neural_activity: ITP peptide immunostaining in dorsal protocerebrum terminals cycles significantly (peaks around noon and midnight/ZT20) while cell body staining does not cycle; PER protein cycling in these neurons is dampened in amplitude during ITP overexpression
behavioral_consequence: ITP knockdown reduces evening locomotor activity relative to morning activity and increases nighttime activity; slightly lengthens free-running period in DD; Overexpression (with tim-GAL4) disrupts behavioral rhythms causing arrhythmicity in DD and impaired morning/evening activity in LD
ecological_context: Controls levels of daily locomotor activity (evening activity); does not influence adaptation to changing photoperiods unlike PDF
techniques_used: ['Immunocytochemistry', 'RNA interference (GAL4/UAS)', 'Locomotor Activity Monitoring system', 'Behavioral analysis (actograms, periodograms)']
summary: ITP-expressing fifth sLNv and LNd show rhythmic terminal ITP release. Knockdown reduces evening activity levels, while overexpression disrupts circadian behavioral rhythms.

paper_title: The Ion Transport Peptide Is a New Functional Clock Neuropeptide in the Fruit Fly Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Light-Dark (LD 12:12) and Constant Darkness (DD) cycles; RNA interference (RNAi) knockdown of PDF; Double knockdown (ITP + PDF)
neural_activity: PDF peptide immunostaining in dorsal protocerebrum terminals cycles significantly (peaks at ZT2) and continues in DD; PDF cycling is affected in ITP-overexpressing flies
behavioral_consequence: PDF knockdown reduces rhythmicity in DD (59% rhythmic), shortens free-running period, advances evening activity peak, and reduces morning activity; Double knockdown with ITP causes hyperactivity, almost complete arrhythmicity in DD, and reduced sleep
ecological_context: Promotes adaptation to long photoperiods and controls activity phase; cooperates with ITP to control flies' sleep
summary: PDF-expressing sLNv and lLNv show rhythmic terminal staining. Knockdown alters activity phase and rhythmicity; combined with ITP knockdown leads to severe disruption of rhythms and sleep.

paper_title: The Drosophila neuropeptides PDF and sNPF have opposing electrophysiological and molecular effects on central neurons.
experimental_stimulus: PDF (10 µM) or sNPF (20 µM) bath application
neural_activity: PDF treatment depolarized neurons and increased excitability (firing rate) and cAMP levels. sNPF treatment hyperpolarized neurons, decreased excitability, decreased cAMP levels, and blocked spontaneous calcium waves propagating along the ventral nerve cord.
ecological_context: Spontaneous calcium waves in the larval ventral ganglion likely reflect output of the locomotion central pattern generator.
techniques_used: ['Patch-clamp electrophysiology', 'Live optical imaging (FRET cAMP sensor)', 'Live optical imaging (GCaMP3 calcium sensor)']
summary: Larval motor neurons expressing transgenic PDFR or sNPFR show opposing electrophysiological responses: PDF causes depolarization and increased excitability via cAMP, while sNPF causes hyperpolarization and decreased excitability via Gαo, blocking spontaneous locomotor network activity.

paper_title: The Drosophila neuropeptides PDF and sNPF have opposing electrophysiological and molecular effects on central neurons.
experimental_stimulus: sNPF (20 µM) bath application
neural_activity: Transgenic sNPFR expression induced strong hyperpolarization in response to sNPF; endogenous sNPFR expression showed no reliable electrical response in larvae.
summary: DILP-expressing insulin-producing cells in third instar larvae hyperpolarize upon sNPF treatment only when sNPFR is transgenically overexpressed, dependent on Gαo signaling.

paper_title: The Drosophila neuropeptides PDF and sNPF have opposing electrophysiological and molecular effects on central neurons.
experimental_stimulus: PDF (10 µM) bath application
neural_activity: PDF treatment significantly increased cAMP levels in muscles expressing transgenic PDFR; no change observed without transgenic receptor.
summary: Body wall muscle cells express functional PDFR signaling machinery capable of increasing cAMP upon PDF application, confirming receptor functionality in non-neuronal tissue.

paper_title: A neuron-glia interaction involving GABA Transaminase contributes to sleep loss in sleepless mutants
experimental_stimulus: Genetic rescue of sleepless (sss) expression using Gad1-GAL4 or VGAT-GAL4 drivers
neural_activity: Function inferred via rescue; SSS expression restored sleep phenotype; general sss mutant background shows increased neuronal excitability (citing previous work)
behavioral_consequence: Significant restoration of daily sleep (80-90% of wild type), increased sleep bout duration, increased bout number, and reduced sleep latency to wild-type levels
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed
techniques_used: ['GAL4/UAS system', 'Drosophila Activity Monitoring System (sleep assays)', "Student's t-test/ANOVA for statistical analysis"]
summary: Expression of SSS in GABAergic neurons rescues the sleep loss phenotype of sss mutants, increasing total sleep and improving sleep consolidation.

paper_title: A neuron-glia interaction involving GABA Transaminase contributes to sleep loss in sleepless mutants
experimental_stimulus: Genetic manipulation of GABAT (CG7433) using Repo-GAL4; loss of GABAT or restoration of GABAT expression in sss; gabat background
neural_activity: GABAT protein levels increased post-transcriptionally in sss mutants; GABAT localizes to mitochondria; GABA levels reduced in sss brains
behavioral_consequence: Loss of GABAT increases daily sleep and consolidation; Restoration of GABAT in glia reduces sleep (promotes wakefulness) in sss mutants; loss of GABAT suppresses sss sleep phenotype
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed
techniques_used: ['GAL4/UAS system', 'Sleep assays (Drosophila Activity Monitoring System)', 'Immunohistochemistry (Confocal microscopy)', 'Western blot analysis', 'HPLC amino-acid analysis', '2D-DIGE proteomic analysis']
summary: GABAT acts in glia to promote wakefulness; loss of GABAT increases sleep, while restoration of GABAT in glia suppresses the sleep phenotype of sss mutants.

paper_title: Drosophila Neuroligin 4 Regulates Sleep through Modulating GABA Transmission
experimental_stimulus: Light/Dark cycle (Day ZT2-ZT5 vs Night ZT13-ZT16); Exogenous GABA application (0.1-10 mM puffs)
neural_activity: Wild-type: spontaneous AP firing during day, suppressed at night (tonic firing, rare bursts). Mutant: hyperactivated at night (frequent bursts). Significantly reduced GABA-gated currents. Reduced RDL receptor clustering in terminals.
behavioral_consequence: dnlg4 mutants exhibit reduced total night sleep, longer sleep onset latency, and fragmented sleep (more episodes, shorter duration). Genetic rescue of DNlg4 in l-LNvs restores sleep behavior.
ecological_context: Sleep is an essential and evolutionarily conserved behavior regulated by circadian and homeostatic mechanisms.
techniques_used: ['Immunostaining', 'Western blot', 'Locomotor activity monitoring (DAM5)', 'Whole-cell electrophysiology (I-clamp/voltage-clamp)', 'Gal4/UAS genetic manipulation (RNAi/rescue)']
summary: DNlg4 is highly expressed in l-LNvs and essential for sleep regulation; loss of DNlg4 leads to l-LNv hyperactivity at night, impaired GABA transmission, and fragmented sleep.

paper_title: Drosophila Neuroligin 4 Regulates Sleep through Modulating GABA Transmission
experimental_stimulus: Genetic expression of DNlg4 (104y-Gal4 driver)
neural_activity: Not measured
behavioral_consequence: Expression of DNlg4 failed to rescue sleep defects in dnlg4 mutants.
ecological_context: None explicitly discussed in this study
summary: Manipulation of DNlg4 expression in fan-shaped body neurons did not rescue sleep defects, indicating l-LNvs are the critical site for DNlg4-mediated sleep regulation.

paper_title: Regulation of Sleep by Neuropeptide Y-Like System in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Sleep deprivation (12 hours mechanical); Presence or absence of opposite sex (mating context); Gal4-UAS overexpression/knockdown
neural_activity: NPF transcript levels (qPCR) and protein immunoreactivity (immunohistochemistry); Transcript levels decreased after sleep deprivation in controls; Protein levels oscillated with peaks at ZT8 and ZT20; NPF expression was male-specific in 2-4 D1 noncircadian neurons
behavioral_consequence: Overexpression of npf/npfr1 in males increased nighttime sleep (increased episode duration, reduced sleep latency) and reduced sleep rebound after deprivation; In wild-type males, lower npf levels when females present (associated with increased activity for courtship); Higher npf levels when females absent (associated with sleep); No significant sleep changes observed in females with altered NPF signaling
ecological_context: Balancing sleep and mating behavior in males; Regulating sleep homeostasis after sleep loss
summary: Overexpression of NPF/NPFR1 in male flies increases nighttime sleep duration and reduces sleep latency, while also reducing sleep rebound after deprivation. Endogenous npf expression decreases after sleep deprivation and is lower in males when females are present (associated with courtship activity), suggesting NPF-expressing neurons (specifically male D1 neurons) regulate sleep homeostasis and balance sleep with mating behavior.

paper_title: Cul3 and the BTB Adaptor Insomniac Are Key Regulators of Sleep Homeostasis and a Dopamine Arousal Pathway in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: UAS-inc-RNAi or UAS-Cul3-RNAi knockdown
neural_activity: Not measured
behavioral_consequence: Reduced sleep duration, consolidation, and homeostasis; increased latency to sleep; hyper-arousability to mechanical stimulus (>85% wake vs 25% WT)
summary: Pan-neuronal knockdown of inc or Cul3 reduces sleep duration and homeostasis, and increases arousal threshold to mechanical stimuli.

paper_title: Cul3 and the BTB Adaptor Insomniac Are Key Regulators of Sleep Homeostasis and a Dopamine Arousal Pathway in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: UAS-inc expression (Rescue)
neural_activity: Not measured
behavioral_consequence: Rescue of baseline sleep duration and sleep homeostasis (rebound sleep after deprivation)
summary: Expression of inc in cholinergic neurons rescues sleep duration and homeostasis defects in inc mutants.

paper_title: Cul3 and the BTB Adaptor Insomniac Are Key Regulators of Sleep Homeostasis and a Dopamine Arousal Pathway in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: UAS-inc expression (Rescue)
neural_activity: Not measured
behavioral_consequence: No rescue of sleep duration or arousal phenotypes; TH protein levels unchanged in inc mutants
summary: Expression of inc in dopaminergic neurons does not rescue inc sleep phenotypes, indicating inc does not function primarily in these neurons.

paper_title: Cul3 and the BTB Adaptor Insomniac Are Key Regulators of Sleep Homeostasis and a Dopamine Arousal Pathway in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: UAS-inc expression (Rescue)
neural_activity: Not measured
behavioral_consequence: 30Y-Gal4 provides partial rescue of sleep homeostasis; 247-Gal4 (restricted) does not rescue; inc mutants show stochastic MB lobe branching defects (10/50 brains)
summary: 30Y-Gal4 partially rescues sleep homeostasis; inc mutants exhibit low-penetrance mushroom body morphological defects that do not correlate with sleep penetrance.

paper_title: Cul3 and the BTB Adaptor Insomniac Are Key Regulators of Sleep Homeostasis and a Dopamine Arousal Pathway in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Mechanical rotation of activity monitors (off horizontal)
neural_activity: Not measured
behavioral_consequence: Hyper-arousability: >85% of inc mutants and Cul3-RNAi flies wake vs 25% of WT; 3IY suppresses this phenotype
summary: inc and Cul3 mutants exhibit hyper-arousability to mechanical stimuli, which is suppressed by pharmacological inhibition of dopamine synthesis.

paper_title: Ih Current Is Necessary to Maintain Normal Dopamine Fluctuations and Sleep Consolidation in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Genetic deletion of Ih current (DmIh null mutant); Pharmacological dopamine reduction (3-iodotyrosine feeding)
neural_activity: DmIh expression confirmed in TH+ cells via FACS and RT-PCR; Dopamine levels measured in whole heads via HPLC showed daily cycling in wild-type, with loss of cycling in light and dramatic increase at night in mutants
behavioral_consequence: Sleep fragmentation (increased number of episodes, decreased duration); Altered locomotor activity (loss of bimodal pattern, reduced dawn peak); Altered circadian rhythms
ecological_context: Regulation of rest-activity patterns and sleep consolidation in natural light:dark cycles
techniques_used: ['Fluorescence Activated Cell Sorting (FACS)', 'RT-PCR', 'HPLC', 'Drosophila Activity Monitoring Systems (DAMS)', 'FlySiesta software', 'Drug feeding']
summary: DmIh channels are expressed in dopaminergic neurons and are necessary for maintaining normal dopamine fluctuations; loss of Ih current increases dopamine at night, causing sleep fragmentation that is rescued by reducing dopamine levels.

paper_title: Ih Current Is Necessary to Maintain Normal Dopamine Fluctuations and Sleep Consolidation in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Genetic deletion of Ih current (DmIh null mutant)
neural_activity: PDF secretion from dorsal projections measured via immunohistochemistry showed no difference between mutant and control flies
behavioral_consequence: Shortened circadian period and increased arrhythmicity in constant darkness
ecological_context: Endogenous circadian timekeeping and entrainment to light/dark cycles
summary: PDF clock output from LNvs is normal in DmIh mutants, suggesting circadian rhythm defects arise downstream of LNv pacemaker output.

paper_title: Ih Current Is Necessary to Maintain Normal Dopamine Fluctuations and Sleep Consolidation in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Genetic deletion of Ih current (DmIh null mutant); Light:Dark cycles
neural_activity: Light entrainment capability assessed via activity monitoring (not directly recorded)
behavioral_consequence: Light entrainment of circadian rhythms preserved despite Ih current loss
ecological_context: Visual processing and light-dependent circadian entrainment
summary: DmIh is expressed in retinal receptors and its loss does not prevent light entrainment, indicating mutant flies are not severely impaired for light detection.

paper_title: Pan-Neuronal Knockdown of Calcineurin Reduces Sleep in the Fruit Fly, Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Mechanical stimulation (during sleep)
neural_activity: Not directly measured; behavioral readouts (locomotor activity, arousal) used as proxies
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of CnA or CnB subunits reduced total daily sleep and sleep-bout length. CnA#1 RNAi flies exhibited hyporesponsiveness to mechanical stimuli (increased arousal threshold). CnA#1 RNAi impaired retention of aversive olfactory memory (2h). Misexpression of constitutively active CanA-14F increased sleep.
ecological_context: None explicitly discussed in ecological terms; findings linked to general physiological functions (plasticity, memory)
techniques_used: ['Locomotor activity monitoring', 'Mechanical stimulation', 'Olfactory conditioning', 'RT-PCR/qPCR', 'In situ hybridization', 'Immunohistochemistry']
summary: Pan-neuronal knockdown of Calcineurin reduces sleep, decreases sleep-bout length, and impairs olfactory memory retention, while constitutively active CnA increases sleep. Sleep-deprived flies show homeostatic rebound.

paper_title: SLEEPLESS, a Ly–6/Neurotoxin Family Member, Regulates Levels, Localization, and Activity of Shaker
experimental_stimulus: Genetic rescue of SLEEPLESS (SSS) expression in sss mutants using Cha-Gal4 driver
neural_activity: Restoration of Shaker expression and activity levels (cell-autonomous)
behavioral_consequence: Strong rescue of the short-sleeping phenotype (daily sleep amount restored to wild-type); little effect on ether-induced leg shaking
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed
techniques_used: ['Gal4-UAS system', 'Drosophila Activity Monitoring System (sleep)', 'Ether-induced leg shaking assay', 'Immunohistochemistry']
summary: Cholinergic neurons expressing SSS are required for sleep regulation; genetic rescue of SSS in these neurons restores sleep in sss mutants without affecting leg-shaking circuits.

paper_title: SLEEPLESS, a Ly–6/Neurotoxin Family Member, Regulates Levels, Localization, and Activity of Shaker
experimental_stimulus: Genetic rescue of SLEEPLESS (SSS) expression in sss mutants using vGlut-Gal4 driver
neural_activity: Restoration of Shaker expression specifically in visual projection neurons
behavioral_consequence: Rescue of ether-induced leg-shaking phenotype; weak rescue of sleep phenotype
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed
techniques_used: ['Gal4-UAS system', 'Drosophila Activity Monitoring System (sleep)', 'Ether-induced leg shaking assay', 'Immunohistochemistry']
summary: SSS expression in glutamatergic neurons rescues leg-shaking behavior but does not fully rescue sleep, indicating distinct circuits for sleep and motor control.

paper_title: SLEEPLESS, a Ly–6/Neurotoxin Family Member, Regulates Levels, Localization, and Activity of Shaker
experimental_stimulus: Genetic rescue of SLEEPLESS (SSS) expression in sss mutants using OK107-Gal4 driver
neural_activity: Increased Shaker expression in mushroom bodies (specifically alpha and alpha' lobes)
behavioral_consequence: Little or no rescue of the short-sleeping phenotype despite Shaker expression rescue
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed
summary: While SSS expression rescues Shaker levels in mushroom bodies, this does not translate to rescue of the sleep phenotype in sss mutants.

paper_title: Dopamine Is a Regulator of Arousal in the Fruit Fly
experimental_stimulus: Genetic mutation in dopamine transporter gene (dDAT/fmn) and transgenic rescue using TH-GAL4 (dopaminergic) and ELAV-GAL4 (pan-neuronal)
behavioral_consequence: Loss of dDAT (*fmn*) increases locomotor activity, reduces rest (sleep), enhances sensitivity to mechanical stimuli, prolongs arousal response, and abolishes sleep rebound; TH-GAL4 driven rescue of dDAT in dopaminergic neurons yields weaker phenotypic rescue compared to ELAV-GAL4
techniques_used: ['Activity monitoring (infrared beam crossings)', 'Mechanical stimulation', 'Genetic rescue (transgenic flies)', 'Rest deprivation', 'Life span analysis']
summary: Dopaminergic neurons are implicated in arousal regulation via the dopamine transporter gene (*dDAT*); *fmn* mutation in *dDAT* leads to hyperactivity, reduced rest, increased arousal sensitivity, and absent sleep rebound, while rescue in dopaminergic neurons (TH-GAL4) is less effective than pan-neuronal rescue.

paper_title: Multifaceted luminance gain control beyond photoreceptors in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Moving OFF edges of varying Weber contrasts (-50% to -100%), step contrasts (-25% to +50%), random luminance steps; background luminance varied using neutral density filters (ND 0 to ND 2.7) over 30s adaptation contexts.
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6f). Calcium signals negatively correlated with contrast. Gain scaled with adapting luminance; operating range increased with luminance. Adaptation time constants ranged 1.2s–1.7s. Responses remained luminance-dependent and were not fully adapted even after 30s.
behavioral_consequence: L2 response profiles (gain control limitations) predicted the turning response of L3-blocked flies, specifically underestimation of contrast in low luminance and overestimation in high luminance.
ecological_context: Relevant for navigation in natural environments where luminance changes occur slowly (day-night) or rapidly (open field-to-shadow), requiring stable contrast perception.
techniques_used: ['Two-photon calcium imaging', 'Behavioral arena (turning on air-cushioned ball)', 'Genetic expression (L221Dhh-Gal4/UAS-GCaMP6f)']
summary: L2 neurons encode contrast with luminance-dependent gain that is insufficient for behavioral invariance. Their response patterns explain the contrast underestimation and overestimation observed when L3 is silenced.

paper_title: Multifaceted luminance gain control beyond photoreceptors in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Moving OFF edges of varying Weber contrasts (-50% to -100%), luminance steps, adapting backgrounds; background luminance varied using neutral density filters (ND 0 to ND 2.7).
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6f). Increased calcium response to OFF steps. Steady-state response gradually decreased with luminance, encoding absolute luminance over three orders of magnitude.
behavioral_consequence: Acute silencing using shibirets (34°C) disrupted luminance invariance. L3-blocked flies showed bidirectional gain control deficits: underestimation of contrast in dim adaptation contexts and overestimation in bright adaptation contexts.
ecological_context: Ensures luminance-invariant visual behaviors critical for robust navigation in dynamic environments (e.g., Drosophila's crepuscular lifestyle, rapidly changing lighting during movement).
techniques_used: ['Two-photon calcium imaging', 'Behavioral arena (turning on air-cushioned ball)', 'Genetic silencing (L30595-Gal4/UAS-shibirets at 34°C)']
summary: L3 neurons relay absolute luminance information and implement bidirectional gain control (increments in dim, decrements in bright) at fast and slow timescales to correct for L2 limitations and achieve luminance-invariant behavior.

paper_title: First-order visual interneurons distribute distinct contrast and luminance information across ON and OFF pathways to achieve stable behavior
experimental_stimulus: Moving ON and OFF edges (varying luminance, 100% contrast), staircase luminance stimuli, randomized luminance flashes, A/B contrast steps from adapting background
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging of axon terminals in medulla layer M1. L1 shows transient negative responses (peak) encoding contrast and sustained responses encoding luminance. Luminance encoding is linear (negatively correlated with luminance). Hyperpolarizes to light onset, depolarizes to light offset.
behavioral_consequence: Silencing L1 alone has little effect on ON behavior at 100% contrast across luminances but disrupts luminance invariance in OFF behavior (underestimates dim OFF edges). L1 + L2 silencing fully disrupts ON behavior. L1 ort rescue restores ON and OFF behavior at low luminances. L1 provides luminance gain to both ON and OFF pathways.
ecological_context: Relevant for visual behavior in natural environments where illumination changes rapidly (e.g., sun to shade, saccades), ensuring contrast computation remains stable despite luminance changes.
techniques_used: ['Two-photon calcium imaging', 'Fly-on-a-ball behavioral assay', 'Genetic silencing (UAS-shibirets)', 'Genetic rescue (UAS-ort)']
summary: L1 neurons encode both contrast (transient) and luminance (sustained, linear) information. They contribute to luminance-invariant behavior in both ON and OFF pathways, with L1 providing essential contrast input to the ON pathway when L2 is silenced and luminance gain to both pathways.

paper_title: First-order visual interneurons distribute distinct contrast and luminance information across ON and OFF pathways to achieve stable behavior
experimental_stimulus: Moving ON and OFF edges (varying luminance), staircase luminance stimuli, randomized luminance flashes
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging. L2 shows transient responses only (no sustained component). Purely contrast sensitive. Encodes contrast with a steeper sigmoid response function than L1.
behavioral_consequence: Silencing L2 alone slightly reduces ON turning behavior. However, simultaneous silencing of L1 and L2 fully disrupts ON behavioral responses. L2 provides contrast-sensitive input to the ON pathway in addition to L1.
ecological_context: Contributes to stable visual behavior in natural environments by providing contrast information to the ON pathway, complementing luminance-sensitive inputs.
summary: L2 neurons are purely contrast-sensitive with transient responses. They provide contrast input to the ON pathway and are required together with L1 for robust ON-motion guided behavior.

paper_title: First-order visual interneurons distribute distinct contrast and luminance information across ON and OFF pathways to achieve stable behavior
experimental_stimulus: Moving ON and OFF edges (varying luminance), staircase luminance stimuli, randomized luminance flashes
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging. L3 shows sustained responses and is non-linearly tuned to luminance, strongly amplifying dim stimuli (darkest stimulus).
behavioral_consequence: Silencing L3 alters ON behavior luminance dependence (flies respond stronger but lose slight luminance correlation). L3 ort rescue restores ON behavior selectively at low luminances. L3 provides luminance gain to both ON and OFF pathways; losing L3 function leads to underestimation of dim stimuli.
ecological_context: Crucial for survival in changing visual environments where dim light stimuli need to be accurately detected despite rapid background luminance changes.
techniques_used: ['Two-photon calcium imaging', 'Fly-on-a-ball behavioral assay', 'Genetic silencing (UAS-shibirets)', 'Genetic rescue (UAS-ort)']
summary: L3 neurons encode luminance non-linearly (amplifying dim light) and provide behaviorally relevant luminance gain to both ON and OFF pathways, ensuring contrast constancy in varying illumination.

paper_title: A flexible geometry for panoramic visual and optogenetic stimulation during behavior and physiology.
experimental_stimulus: Drifting sine wave gratings (spatial wavelength 60° or 45°, temporal frequencies 0.25-32 Hz)
neural_activity: Direction-selective calcium responses, sustained during stimulus; peak response at 1-4 Hz temporal frequency (Two-photon imaging with GCaMP6f)
behavioral_consequence: Wildtype flies turned in direction of stimulus displacement and slowed walking speed. Optogenetic silencing (GtACR) in T4 and T5 reduced turning strength compared to genetic control flies
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed in this paper
summary: T4 and T5 neurons show direction-selective calcium responses to drifting gratings. Optogenetic silencing of these neurons reduces turning strength during visual optomotor behavior.

paper_title: Neural mechanisms underlying sensitivity to reverse-phi motion in the fly
experimental_stimulus: Reverse-phi motion (discretized square-wave grating with contrast reversals), phi motion, varying velocities (16-256 deg/s), varying spatial wavelengths (30, 90 deg)
neural_activity: Calcium imaging in dendrites showed strong tuning to motion in preferred direction. Pronounced reverse-phi responses for null direction at low velocities and preferred direction at high velocities (high-velocity re-inversion)
behavioral_consequence: Silencing T4 (with T5) fully abolished phi and reverse-phi turning responses. Silencing T4 alone reduced reverse-phi turning; high-velocity re-inversion remained in T4 block flies
ecological_context: Course control, spatial navigation, object tracking
techniques_used: ['Calcium imaging (GCaMP6m)', 'Tethered walking behavior', 'Thermogenetic silencing (shibirets)', 'Tetanus toxin (TNT) silencing']
summary: T4 dendrites show strong reverse-phi sensitivity and high-velocity re-inversion, and are necessary for optomotor turning responses.

paper_title: Neural mechanisms underlying sensitivity to reverse-phi motion in the fly
experimental_stimulus: Reverse-phi motion, phi motion, varying velocities, varying spatial wavelengths
neural_activity: Calcium imaging in dendrites showed phi tuning similar to T4. Under reverse-phi, directional selectivity was smaller; marginal inversion at low speeds, pronounced re-inversion at high velocities
behavioral_consequence: Silencing T5 (with T4) fully abolished phi and reverse-phi turning responses. Silencing T5 alone abolished high-velocity re-inversion
ecological_context: Course control, spatial navigation, object tracking
summary: T5 dendrites show marginal reverse-phi responses compared to T4 but are critical for high-velocity re-inversion in turning behavior.

paper_title: Neural mechanisms underlying sensitivity to reverse-phi motion in the fly
experimental_stimulus: Phi and reverse-phi motion stimuli (vertical/horizontal gratings)
neural_activity: In vivo patch-clamp recordings showed strong depolarization for phi in preferred direction. Under reverse-phi, null direction stimuli caused depolarization (response inversion)
ecological_context: Course control, spatial navigation, object tracking
summary: LPTCs exhibit partial reverse-phi sensitivity and are thought to control compensatory steering behavior based on T4/T5 input.

paper_title: A gut-derived hormone suppresses sugar appetite and regulates food choice in Drosophila
cell_type: NPF+ Enteroendocrine Cells (EECs) in the midgut
experimental_stimulus: Sugar ingestion (sucrose re-feeding after starvation), mating (via Sex Peptide signaling)
neural_activity: Increased calcium signaling measured by CaLexA reporter after sugar re-feeding; reduced NPF peptide levels in midgut after mating (indicating release); release regulated by Sut2 transporter and Mondo transcription factor
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of NPF leads to increased sugar intake and preference, and decreased preference for protein-rich yeast food; exogenous NPF injection suppresses sugar intake and increases yeast preference in virgin and mated females
ecological_context: Regulates food choice in mated females to promote protein intake for egg production and prevent excessive sugar consumption to maintain nutritional homeostasis
techniques_used: ['RNAi knockdown (voila-GAL4, NPFgut>', 'CaLexA calcium reporter', 'Immunostaining', 'Dye consumption assay', 'CAFÉ assay', 'FLIC assay', 'FlyPAD', 'Peptide injection']
summary: Midgut NPF+ EECs release NPF in response to sugar ingestion and mating; this signaling suppresses sugar appetite and promotes yeast consumption in mated females.

paper_title: A gut-derived hormone suppresses sugar appetite and regulates food choice in Drosophila
cell_type: AKH-Producing Cells (APCs) in the anterior midgut (Crop Constrictor cells)
experimental_stimulus: Nutritional state (fed vs. starvation), gut-derived NPF signaling (via NPFR), mating
neural_activity: NPF acts on APC NPFR to suppress AKH release (reduced AKH peptide levels in fed state); starvation increases AKH release and calcium activity; NPFR knockdown increases AKH peptide levels in fed females
behavioral_consequence: AKH promotes sugar appetite and suppresses protein intake; loss of AKH or NPFR in APCs increases sugar intake and decreases yeast preference; activation of APCs increases sugar intake
ecological_context: Mediates gut NPF signals to control nutrient-specific feeding decisions (sugar vs. protein) and energy mobilization during homeostatic regulation
techniques_used: ['NPFR RNAi (AKH-GAL4)', 'AKH RNAi', 'TrpA1 activation', 'Immunostaining (AKH levels)', 'Dye consumption assay', 'CAFÉ assay', 'FLIC assay', 'FlyPAD']
summary: APCs integrate gut NPF signals to suppress AKH release in fed state; this inhibition reduces sugar appetite and promotes protein intake, particularly in mated females.

paper_title: A gut-derived hormone suppresses sugar appetite and regulates food choice in Drosophila
cell_type: AstC+ Enteroendocrine Cells (EECs) in the midgut
experimental_stimulus: Starvation, gut-derived NPF signaling (via NPFR)
neural_activity: Express NPFR; NPF signaling inhibits these cells (reduced activity/peptide release); NPFR knockdown leads to increased AstC peptide release and increased calcium activity
behavioral_consequence: Increased AstC signaling promotes AKH release and energy mobilization; NPFR knockdown in these cells reduces starvation resistance
ecological_context: Part of the gut-AKH axis regulating metabolism and feeding under nutritional stress (starvation conditions)
techniques_used: ['NPFR RNAi (AstC-GAL4 + R57C10-GAL80)', 'CaLexA calcium reporter', 'Immunostaining', 'Starvation survival assay']
summary: AstC+ EECs release AstC during starvation to promote AKH release; gut NPF inhibits this release to suppress the AKH axis.

paper_title: A gut-derived hormone suppresses sugar appetite and regulates food choice in Drosophila
cell_type: PPK+ Neurons in the reproductive tract
experimental_stimulus: Mating (Sex Peptide signaling)
neural_activity: Express Sex Peptide Receptor (SPR); required for mating-induced changes in food preference
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of SPR in these neurons blocks the mating-induced increase in yeast preference; however, NPF injection can still increase yeast preference in these females, indicating NPF acts downstream
ecological_context: Mediates post-mating behavioral changes to increase protein consumption for egg production
summary: PPK+ neurons in the reproductive tract are required for mating-induced yeast preference, acting upstream of gut NPF release to regulate food choice.

paper_title: Developmental remodeling repurposes larval neurons for sexual behaviors in adult Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (red light, 10.5 µW/mm2, CsChrimson::mVenus); Natural context: Male courtship (endogenous trigger for ovipositor extrusion in mated females)
neural_activity: Not directly measured (activity inferred via optogenetic manipulation)
behavioral_consequence: Bilateral activation induces ovipositor extrusion in unmated and mated females; unilateral activation induces abdominal posture change without extrusion. Silencing (Kir2.1) reduces ovipositor extrusion frequency and egg laying in mated females.
ecological_context: Courtship rejection behavior performed by mated females to reject courting males (may block copulation or male courtship drive)
summary: These female-specific interneurons control ovipositor extrusion. Bilateral activation induces extrusion regardless of mating status; silencing reduces it in mated females.

paper_title: Developmental remodeling repurposes larval neurons for sexual behaviors in adult Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (red light, unilateral)
neural_activity: Not directly measured (activity inferred via optogenetic manipulation)
behavioral_consequence: Unilateral activation failed to evoke any obvious behavior. Bilateral activation did not modify postural change induced by DDAG_C/D neurons.
ecological_context: Contribution to female behavior is currently unclear
summary: Unilateral activation of DDAG_B neurons does not evoke obvious behavior; their contribution to female behavior is currently unclear.

paper_title: Developmental remodeling repurposes larval neurons for sexual behaviors in adult Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: None tested
neural_activity: None tested
behavioral_consequence: None tested
ecological_context: Function in larvae is currently unknown
summary: Larval interneurons present in both sexes; segments A5 and A6 homologs persist in females to become DDAG_C/D neurons, while undergoing dsx-dependent cell death in males during pupation.

paper_title: Mating activates neuroendocrine pathways signaling hunger in Drosophila females
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation of upstream pCd-2a/b neurons; Direct optogenetic activation of Lgr3+ cells
neural_activity: Calcium decreases in Lgr3+ cells upon pCd-2 activation; Calcium imaging and behavioral studies indicate Lgr3+ mBNs are more active in a mated state
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation of Lgr3+ mBNs promotes sucrose consumption, mimicking the mated state
ecological_context: Provides a neural mechanism to increase caloric intake in anticipation of the energetic costs associated with reproduction (offspring production)
techniques_used: ['Electron Microscopy (EM)', 'Optogenetics', 'In vivo calcium imaging', 'FLIC assay', 'CAFE assay']
summary: Lgr3+ mBNs receive inhibitory input from pCd-2 neurons. Activation of Lgr3+ mBNs promotes sucrose consumption and is more active in mated females, transforming the mating signal into a hunger-like signal.

paper_title: Mating activates neuroendocrine pathways signaling hunger in Drosophila females
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation of upstream pC1 neurons; Optogenetic silencing of pCd-2a/b in virgins and mated females
neural_activity: Significant calcium increases in pCd-2a and pCd-2b upon pC1 activation; Cells are predicted to be less active in mated females
behavioral_consequence: Silencing pCd-2a or pCd-2b in virgins significantly increases sucrose consumption; Activation of pCd-2a in mated females reduces sugar intake
ecological_context: Outputs of the mating status circuit that mediate postmating sucrose consumption independent of egg laying
summary: pCd-2 neurons are outputs of the mating status circuit. Silencing them mimics the mated state by increasing sucrose consumption, while activation reduces intake in mated females.

paper_title: Mating activates neuroendocrine pathways signaling hunger in Drosophila females
experimental_stimulus: Genetic silencing (TNT/KIR) in virgin females
neural_activity: Functionally silenced to mimic mating state
behavioral_consequence: Silenced virgin females consumed significantly more sucrose than virgin controls
ecological_context: Detection of male sex peptide during copulation initiates the postmating response
summary: SPSNs are the first-order neurons in the mating status circuit. Silencing them in virgins recapitulates the postmating increase in sucrose consumption.

paper_title: Mating activates neuroendocrine pathways signaling hunger in Drosophila females
cell_type: SAG Neurons (Sex Peptide Abdominal Ganglia neurons)
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic silencing (GtACR1) in virgin females
neural_activity: Functionally silenced
behavioral_consequence: Acute silencing of SAGs significantly increased sucrose consumption
ecological_context: Second-order relay neurons conveying mating status from uterus to central brain
summary: Silencing SAG neurons in virgins increases sucrose consumption, demonstrating their role in regulating postmating feeding behavior.

paper_title: Mating activates neuroendocrine pathways signaling hunger in Drosophila females
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic silencing (GtACR1) in virgin females
neural_activity: Functionally silenced
behavioral_consequence: Acute silencing of pC1 significantly increased sucrose consumption
ecological_context: Third-order neurons in the mating status circuit
summary: Silencing pC1 neurons in virgins increases sucrose consumption, indicating they regulate postmating sucrose feeding downstream of the mating status circuit.

paper_title: Mating activates neuroendocrine pathways signaling hunger in Drosophila females
experimental_stimulus: Sucrose stimulation on the proboscis
neural_activity: Similar GCaMP6s calcium responses in virgin and mated females
behavioral_consequence: Virgin and mated females have similar feeding initiation propensities (Proboscis Extension Response)
ecological_context: Taste detection of sugar, which is not altered by mating status
techniques_used: ['In vivo calcium imaging', 'Proboscis Extension Response (PER) assay']
summary: Gustatory sensory neurons do not differ in sugar sensitivity between mating states; increased sugar consumption is not due to changes in sensory sensitivity.

paper_title: Mating activates neuroendocrine pathways signaling hunger in Drosophila females
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation in virgin females
neural_activity: N/A
behavioral_consequence: No difference in sucrose consumption compared to controls
ecological_context: Mediates changes in sexual receptivity, distinct from feeding behavior
summary: Descending neurons regulating sexual receptivity do not influence sucrose consumption, indicating separate neural pathways.

paper_title: Mating activates neuroendocrine pathways signaling hunger in Drosophila females
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation in virgin females
neural_activity: N/A
behavioral_consequence: No difference in sucrose consumption compared to controls
ecological_context: Mediates changes in egg laying, distinct from feeding behavior
summary: Descending neurons regulating egg laying do not influence sucrose consumption, indicating separate neural pathways.

paper_title: Enteric neurons increase maternal food intake during reproduction
cell_type: PI Ms Neurons (Myosuppressin-positive neurons in the pars intercerebralis)
experimental_stimulus: Virgin vs. mated status; Starvation/Refeeding; Thermogenetic activation (TrpA1); Genetic silencing (Kir2.1); Hormone receptor modulation (EcR, Rk RNAi)
neural_activity: Mated females show higher cumulative calcium levels and reduced oscillations compared to virgins (GCaMP6 imaging, CaLexA reporter). Ms peptide levels in cell bodies are lower post-mating (indicating release). Express EcR and Rk receptors.
behavioral_consequence: Activation causes crop enlargement. Silencing prevents crop enlargement in starved-refed flies. Downregulation of receptors reduces crop enlargement, food intake, and fecundity in mated females.
ecological_context: Post-mating crop enlargement sustains increased food intake required for reproduction/gestation.
techniques_used: ['Calcium imaging (GCaMP6, CaLexA)', 'Immunohistochemistry (Ms staining)', 'Thermogenetics (TrpA1)', 'Genetic silencing (Kir2.1)', 'RNAi (EcR, Rk)', 'Crop quantification', 'Food intake assays (dye, FlyPAD)', 'Fecundity assays']
summary: PI Ms neurons increase activity post-mating (higher calcium, reduced oscillations) leading to Ms release, which drives crop enlargement via MsR1 on crop muscles, increasing food intake to support reproduction.

paper_title: fruitless tunes functional flexibility of courtship circuitry during development
experimental_stimulus: Genetic knockdown of fruM expression (UAS-fruMi) using temperature-sensitive tub-GAL80ts during different developmental stages
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown during pupation (stages 5-6) eliminates innate courtship towards females (CI < 10%). Knockdown during adulthood has no obvious effect on female-directed courtship (CI > 80%) but slightly increases male-male courtship and chaining behaviors
techniques_used: ['Genetic manipulation (UAS-fruMi, tub-GAL80ts)', 'Behavioral assays (Courtship Index, Mating success)']
summary: fruM expression in fruGAL4-labeled neurons is critical during pupation for innate courtship towards females, while expression during adulthood is dispensable for female courtship but plays a minor role in inhibiting male-male behaviors.

paper_title: fruitless tunes functional flexibility of courtship circuitry during development
experimental_stimulus: Spatial knockdown of fruM expression in brain-only neurons using Otd-Flp intersectional strategy
behavioral_consequence: Reduced courtship towards females (CI = 56.61%) but sexual orientation unchanged (low courtship to males). Low initial male chaining behavior that increases over 1-3 days
techniques_used: ['Intersectional genetics (Otd-Flp, UAS-fruMi)', 'Behavioral assays (Courtship Index, Chaining Index)']
summary: fruM function in brain neurons promotes male-female courtship and inhibits the experience-dependent progression of male chaining behavior.

paper_title: fruitless tunes functional flexibility of courtship circuitry during development
experimental_stimulus: Spatial knockdown of fruM expression in Otd-negative neurons using Otd-Flp intersectional strategy
behavioral_consequence: Intensive courtship towards both females (CI = 85.62%) and males (CI = 82.89%). High initial male chaining behavior that decreases over 1-3 days
techniques_used: ['Intersectional genetics (Otd-Flp, UAS-fruMi)', 'Behavioral assays (Courtship Index, Chaining Index)']
summary: fruM function in neurons outside the brain inhibits male-male courtship; its removal results in innately bisexual courtship behavior.

paper_title: Sex-determining genes distinctly regulate courtship capability and target preference via sexually dimorphic neurons
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson) with 850nm red LED; presence of wild-type male or mated female target flies
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation induced behavioral output; neurons visualized by tdTomato immunohistochemistry
behavioral_consequence: Toward male targets: induced wing extensions and lunges. Toward female targets: induced robust wing extensions but no lunges. Increased time orienting toward females compared to males. fru is required for enhancement of courtship toward females and for promoting aggression.
ecological_context: Successful male courtship behavior is vital for the propagation of offspring, whereas intra-specific aggressive behavior is often the key to ensure access to potential mates. Expression of either courtship or aggression to the wrong target sex can be costly.
summary: dsxGAL4 ∩ fruFLP neurons (P1/pC1 subset) promote courtship and aggression upon optogenetic activation; behavior is target-sex dependent, with dsx specifying the execution mechanism and fru required for female-target enhancement and aggression.

paper_title: Sex-determining genes distinctly regulate courtship capability and target preference via sexually dimorphic neurons
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson) with 850nm red LED; presence of wild-type male or mated female target flies
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation induced behavioral output; neurons visualized by tdTomato immunohistochemistry; identified as a subset of P1/pC1 cluster
behavioral_consequence: Toward male targets: induced lunges and wing extensions. Toward female targets: induced wing extensions but no lunges. fru-expressing subpopulation promotes both behaviors. fru required for aggression and female-target courtship enhancement.
ecological_context: Social behaviors are consequential for fitness; correct target sex selection prevents costly errors in reproductive behavior.
summary: NP2631 ∩ dsxFLP neurons promote courtship and aggression upon activation; behavior depends on target sex, with dsx specifying neuroanatomical dimorphism and fru required for female-target enhancement and aggression.

paper_title: Sex-determining genes distinctly regulate courtship capability and target preference via sexually dimorphic neurons
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson) with 850nm red LED; presence of wild-type male or mated female target flies
neural_activity: Optogenetic activation induced behavioral output; neurons visualized by tdTomato and GCaMP6f immunohistochemistry
behavioral_consequence: Toward male targets: induced lunges (mostly during inter-stimulus intervals). Toward female targets: induced wing extensions (mostly during inter-stimulus intervals). fruF males showed severely impaired wing extension capacity and no lunges.
ecological_context: Social behaviors are consequential for fitness; target sex specificity ensures appropriate reproductive investment.
summary: P1a neurons are morphologically and functionally distinct from NP2631 ∩ dsxFLP neurons; require both dsx and fru for specification and dimorphism; promote aggression and courtship with distinct temporal dynamics.

paper_title: Neural circuitry coordinating male copulation
experimental_stimulus: Genetic silencing (TNT), Thermoactivation (dTrpA1 at 31°C), Mechanical stimulation of genitalia
neural_activity: Project to muscles of genitalia and abdominal segment 6. Show calcium responses to genital touch (GCaMP6m).
behavioral_consequence: Silencing prevents genital coupling and copulation initiation. Pre-copulation activation prevents attachment. In-copulo activation prevents genital uncoupling/termination, resulting in a 'stuck' phenotype where males remain attached for extended periods while sperm transfer continues.
ecological_context: Mediate the mechanics of genital coupling and intromission required for reproductive success and evolutionary fitness.
summary: dsx/vGlut motor neurons innervate genital muscles and are necessary for genital coupling. Their inhibition or activation pre-copulation prevents mating, while activation in-copulo blocks termination.

paper_title: Neural circuitry coordinating male copulation
experimental_stimulus: Genetic silencing (TNT), Thermoactivation (dTrpA1 at 31°C), Mechanical stimulation of genitalia, GABA-B-R2 receptor knockdown
neural_activity: Arborize locally in Abg with presynaptic boutons overlapping dsx/vGlut dendrites. Show calcium responses to genital touch.
behavioral_consequence: Silencing prevents genital uncoupling ('stuck' phenotype) and reduces successful copulation rates. Pre-copulation activation prevents attachment. In-copulo activation causes immediate termination of copulation, leading to infertility. GABA-B-R2 knockdown partially recapitulates silencing phenotype.
ecological_context: Control genital uncoupling and termination of copulation, ensuring proper mating duration.
techniques_used: ['Split-GAL4', 'Tetanus toxin (TNT) silencing', 'dTrpA1 thermoactivation', 'RNAi knockdown', 'Immunohistochemistry', 'Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6m)']
summary: dsx/Gad1 interneurons functionally oppose motor neurons to promote genital uncoupling. Silencing blocks termination, while activation prematurely ends copulation.

paper_title: Neural circuitry coordinating male copulation
experimental_stimulus: Mechanical stimulation (minuten pin) of genitalia
neural_activity: Axons project to Abdominal Ganglion and Subesophageal Zone (SEZ). Arborizations interdigitate with dsx/vGlut dendrites in Abg. Drive calcium responses in dsx/vGlut and dsx/Gad1 neurons.
behavioral_consequence: Functional connectivity established; provide sensory feedback to the motor circuit. (Direct behavioral manipulation of these specific neurons was not tested in this study).
ecological_context: Provide sensory feedback to ensure coordinated sequence of motor events for successful genital attachment and mating posture.
summary: dsx mechanosensory neurons of the genitalia project to the Abdominal Ganglion and brain, functionally connecting to the motor circuit to provide sensory feedback during copulation.

paper_title: P1 interneurons promote a persistent internal state that enhances inter-male aggression in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Thermogenetic activation (dTrpA1 at 29-32°C); Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson/ReaChR) using light (530-685 nm) at frequencies 1-50 Hz; Solitary confinement behind barrier
neural_activity: Calcium imaging (2-photon, GCaMP6s) showed linear increase in response magnitude as photostimulation frequency increased (10-50 Hz); No persistent activity detected within P1 population during persistent behavioral state
behavioral_consequence: Low-frequency optogenetic activation (≤20 Hz) promoted lunging (aggression) without wing extension; High-frequency activation (≥30 Hz) promoted wing extension during light, suppressed aggression during light, aggression resumed post-light offset; Single photostimulation episode triggered persistent aggression lasting ≥10 min in solitary flies; Thermogenetic activation increased both lunging and wing extension
ecological_context: Aggression serves to establish dominance or defend resources, territory or mating partners; Male flies fight only in the presence of an appetitive resource; P1 activation promotes a persistent internal state facilitating future social behavior
techniques_used: ['Thermogenetics (dTrpA1)', 'Optogenetics (CsChrimson, ReaChR)', 'Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6s)', 'Split-GAL4 intersection', 'Behavioral tracking (CADABRA, FlyTracker, JAABA)', 'Barrier assay']
summary: P1 interneurons promote a persistent internal state that enhances aggression and courtship; low-threshold activation promotes aggression, high-threshold promotes wing extension, and single activation triggers aggression lasting minutes even in solitary flies.

paper_title: P1 interneurons promote a persistent internal state that enhances inter-male aggression in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Chemogenetic silencing (Kir2.1) during optogenetic P1 activation
neural_activity: Functionally silenced via Kir2.1 expression
behavioral_consequence: Silencing suppressed enhancement of lunging following termination of P1 photoactivation; Did not diminish wing extension during photostimulation but caused increase in wing extension during light OFF period
ecological_context: Aggression circuitry required for male-specific aggressive arousal
summary: TKFruM neurons are required for P1-induced aggression following photostimulation offset; silencing them suppresses post-stimulation lunging.

paper_title: Excitation and inhibition onto central courtship neurons biases Drosophila mate choice
experimental_stimulus: Female pheromones (7,11-heptacosadiene and 7,11-nonacosadiene); ATP via P2X2 channel activation; Heat (dTRPA1 activation)
neural_activity: Calcium increases (GCaMP6s) upon female pheromone application. Activation triggers calcium increases in PPN1 and P1 neurons.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic/thermogenetic activation (dTRPA1) at 30°C significantly increases unilateral wing extension rate (courtship behavior).
ecological_context: Detection of female pheromones is essential for productive mate selection and species propagation, driving courtship toward females.
summary: F cells respond to female pheromones and activate central PPN1 and P1 neurons, promoting courtship behavior.

paper_title: Excitation and inhibition onto central courtship neurons biases Drosophila mate choice
experimental_stimulus: Male pheromones (7-tricosene and cis-vaccenyl acetate); ATP via P2X2 channel activation; Heat (dTRPA1 activation)
neural_activity: Calcium increases (GCaMP6s) upon male pheromone application. Activation triggers calcium increases in mAL neurons but not P1 neurons.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic/thermogenetic activation (dTRPA1) at 30°C significantly decreases unilateral wing extension rate (suppresses courtship).
ecological_context: Detection of male pheromones prevents inappropriate courtship, ensuring males do not court other males.
summary: M cells respond to male pheromones and activate mAL neurons, inhibiting courtship behavior.

paper_title: Excitation and inhibition onto central courtship neurons biases Drosophila mate choice
experimental_stimulus: F cell activation (ATP); PPN1 activation (Chrimson); mAL activation (Chrimson); Retinal (Chrimson cofactor)
neural_activity: Calcium increases (GCaMP6s) upon F cell and PPN1 activation. Hyperpolarization (ArcLight) upon mAL activation.
behavioral_consequence: Chrimson-mediated activation triggers sustained courtship behaviors. Inactivation of GABA-A receptors (Rdl RNAi) induces male-male chaining. Co-activation with mAL suppresses P1-induced courtship.
ecological_context: Serve as command neurons for male courtship behavior, integrating sensory cues to decide whether to court.
summary: P1 neurons integrate excitatory (F cell/PPN1) and inhibitory (mAL) inputs to trigger or suppress courtship decisions.

paper_title: Excitation and inhibition onto central courtship neurons biases Drosophila mate choice
experimental_stimulus: F cell activation (ATP); Light (Chrimson activation)
neural_activity: Calcium increases (GCaMP6s) in axons upon F cell stimulation. Activation triggers calcium increases in P1 neurons.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic/thermogenetic activation (dTRPA1) increases unilateral wing extension rate (courtship behavior).
ecological_context: Transmits F cell activation to P1 to promote courtship decisions during mate selection.
summary: PPN1 neurons act as an excitatory pathway from F cells to P1 neurons to drive courtship behavior.

paper_title: Excitation and inhibition onto central courtship neurons biases Drosophila mate choice
experimental_stimulus: F cell/M cell activation (ATP); Light (Chrimson activation)
neural_activity: Calcium increases (GCaMP6s) upon both F and M cell stimulation. Activation causes hyperpolarization of P1 neurons (ArcLight).
behavioral_consequence: Activation (dTRPA1) suppresses courtship toward females. Inactivation (TNT/vGAT RNAi) causes male-male chaining.
ecological_context: Provides an inhibitory drive to suppress courtship, balancing excitation to prevent mating with males.
summary: mAL neurons receive input from both F and M cells and inhibit P1 neurons to suppress courtship behavior.

paper_title: Hierarchical chemosensory regulation of male-male social interactions in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Male cuticular hydrocarbons (specifically (Z)-7-tricosene or 7-T); Capsaicin (for TRPV1 activation)
behavioral_consequence: Required for 7-T to promote male-male aggression and suppress male-male courtship; *Gr32a* mutant males show diminished aggression towards wild-type males; Artificial activation of these neurons promotes aggression and suppresses courtship towards CH-depleted males
ecological_context: Regulating the balance between male-male aggression and courtship during social interactions
summary: Gr32a+ GRNs mediate the reciprocal regulation of aggression and courtship by male CHs, specifically 7-T.

paper_title: Hierarchical chemosensory regulation of male-male social interactions in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: 7-T (in presence of cVA context); Synthetic cVA (cited from previous work for cVA sensitivity)
behavioral_consequence: Not required for 7-T-induced aggression; Sensitivity to cVA is required for cVA to promote aggression (context of hierarchical regulation)
ecological_context: Regulating male-male aggression intensity via cVA detection, interacting with gustatory signals
summary: Or67d+ ORNs mediate cVA-induced aggression but are not required for 7-T-induced aggression, indicating hierarchical interaction.

paper_title: Hierarchical chemosensory regulation of male-male social interactions in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Male CH depletion (oe- target males)
behavioral_consequence: Required for elevated male-male courtship towards CH-depleted males; Normal male-female courtship is unaffected; Does not affect aggression
ecological_context: Detecting cues that promote courtship, normally suppressed by male CHs
techniques_used: ['Behavioral assays', 'Genetic manipulation (Null alleles)', 'RNA in situ hybridization', 'Projection imaging']
summary: Or47b+ ORNs mediate courtship promotion towards males lacking CHs, indicating gustatory dominance over olfactory courtship cues.

paper_title: Genomic and Functional Studies of Drosophila Sex Hierarchy Regulated Gene Expression in Adult Head and Nervous System Tissues
cell_type: dpr-expressing cells (Ascending median bundle neurons, Subesophageal Ganglion, Pars Intercerebralis, VNC, Proboscis/Forelegs)
experimental_stimulus: Courtship assay with virgin female; multiple male courtship context
neural_activity: Not measured directly; gene expression validated by RT-PCR and immunohistochemistry (colocalization with FRUM)
behavioral_consequence: Loss of dpr function or reduced fru P1 expression in these cells reduced latency to first wing extension and attempted copulation. Increased percentage of males performing courtship steps within allotted time. Multiple males courted simultaneously without delay (loss of inhibition). No increase in general locomotor activity.
ecological_context: Male courtship behavior timing and gating (process controlling sequence of courtship ritual)
techniques_used: ['Immunohistochemistry', 'RT-PCR', 'dpr-GAL4 loss-of-function mutation', 'UAS-fruRNAi', 'Courtship latency assays', 'Locomotor activity assays']
summary: dpr-expressing cells in the CNS (ascending median bundle, SOG, PI, VNC) and PNS regulate male courtship timing. Loss of dpr or fru P1 in these cells reduces latency to courtship initiation and copulation attempts without affecting general locomotion.

paper_title: A high-protein diet-responsive gut hormone regulates behavioral and metabolic optimization in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: High-protein diet (HPD), yeast autolysate, tryptone, and amino acid mixtures (specifically Non-Essential Amino Acid Mix3 containing Alanine and Glycine)
neural_activity: Increased intracellular Ca2+ signaling measured by CaLexA and GCaMP8f reporters; activity heterogeneous with only about half of cells responding
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown or inhibition of these cells increased total food intake and specifically increased consumption of protein sources (yeast, tryptone) without affecting sucrose intake (CAFÉ assay, FlyPAD)
ecological_context: Regulates suppression of excessive protein intake to prevent hyperammonemia and maintain lifespan under high-protein diet conditions
summary: Midgut CCHa1-producing EECs respond to high-protein diets with increased Ca2+ signaling and regulate protein-specific satiety; loss of function leads to protein overconsumption and reduced lifespan.

paper_title: A high-protein diet-responsive gut hormone regulates behavioral and metabolic optimization in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Gut-derived CCHa1 hormone released during high-protein diet feeding
neural_activity: Increased Ca2+ signaling (CaLexA) upon HPD; activation dependent on CCHa1 receptor (CCHa1-R) signaling as shown by knock-in-Tango system
behavioral_consequence: Enteric-specific RNAi knockdown of sNPF or sNPF-R resulted in significant preference shift towards protein (tryptone) over sucrose and increased yeast intake (CAFÉ, FlyPAD)
ecological_context: Forms part of a gut-enteric neuronal axis that suppresses protein overload and adapts feeding behavior to dietary protein levels
summary: Enteric sNPF neurons in the hypocerebral ganglion receive CCHa1 signals to suppress protein feeding; inactivation leads to protein preference and metabolic dysregulation.

paper_title: A high-protein diet-responsive gut hormone regulates behavioral and metabolic optimization in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation using TrpA1; synaptic input from enteric sNPF neurons
neural_activity: Established synaptic contact with enteric sNPF neurons via GRASP analysis; functionally identified as sugar-sensing neurons
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of sNPF-R in Gr43a neurons increased protein preference and yeast intake; Gr43a knockdown reduced yeast intake; TrpA1 activation increased yeast intake (FlyPAD)
ecological_context: Regulates the balance between sugar and protein intake; downstream target of gut-signal integration for nutritional decision-making
techniques_used: ['GRASP (Green Fluorescent Protein Reconstitution Across Synaptic Partners)', 'RNAi', 'FlyPAD system', 'Optogenetics (TrpA1)', 'Immunostaining']
summary: Hypocerebral ganglion Gr43a neurons receive input from enteric sNPF neurons and modulate sugar/protein intake balance; their activation promotes protein consumption.

paper_title: A gut-brain-gut interoceptive circuit loop gates sugar ingestion in Drosophila
cell_type: Enteric sensory neurons expressing Gr43a (foregut lumen)
experimental_stimulus: Sucrose ingestion (high ~1M vs low ~100mM), metabolic states (fed vs 24-hour fasted)
neural_activity: Rapidly responded to ingestion of high-concentration sucrose; activity transient and elevated only during ingestion; persistent activity positively correlated with sugar concentration, independent of metabolic state (measured by GCaMP6s fluorescence)
behavioral_consequence: Not directly manipulated for behavior in this study; function described as monitoring sugar content and conveying information to IN1s
ecological_context: Enables rapid sensory monitoring of sugar ingestion to optimise diet across varying metabolic states
techniques_used: ['Two-photon calcium imaging', 'Optogenetics', 'Anatomical imaging (confocal)', 'In vivo food ingestion assay']
summary: Foregut lumen Gr43a neurons monitor sucrose content during ingestion, showing transient responses to high sugar, and provide excitatory input to IN1s.

paper_title: A gut-brain-gut interoceptive circuit loop gates sugar ingestion in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: High-concentration sucrose ingestion, optogenetic activation/inhibition
neural_activity: Persistently activated upon ingestion of sugar-rich foods; receive excitatory input from enteric Gr43a neurons; recurrently connected (measured by GCaMP6s)
behavioral_consequence: Inhibition of synaptic release (IN1>TNT) blocked entry of high-concentration sucrose into the crop, restricting consumption to small volumes
ecological_context: Facilitates optimisation of diet and maximising energy acquisition in environments where food quality fluctuates
techniques_used: ['Two-photon calcium imaging', 'Optogenetics (Chrimson, TNT)', 'Connectomics (FlyWire)', 'In vivo food transport imaging']
summary: IN1s are persistently activated by high-sugar ingestion, receive input from enteric Gr43a neurons, and are required for transporting sugar-rich food into the crop.

paper_title: A gut-brain-gut interoceptive circuit loop gates sugar ingestion in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: High-concentration sucrose ingestion, optogenetic activation (Chrimson)
neural_activity: Persistently inhibited upon ingestion of high-concentration sucrose; inhibited by optogenetic activation of IN1s; glutamatergic (VGLUT-positive) (measured by GCaMP6s)
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation during ingestion prevented sucrose entry into the crop; inhibition by IN1s allows crop entry
ecological_context: Enables flies to consume large volumes of sugar by relaxing crop muscles during high-sugar ingestion
techniques_used: ['Two-photon calcium imaging', 'Optogenetics', 'Immunohistochemistry (VGLUT)', 'In vivo food transport imaging']
summary: CEM neurons are inhibited by IN1s during sugar ingestion; their activation blocks sucrose transport into the crop.

paper_title: Pharyngeal mechanosensory neurons control food swallow in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Mechanical force during swallowing (cibarium expansion), food ingestion (0.5M sucrose solution)
neural_activity: Increased calcium fluorescence in SEZ projections during swallowing (GCaMP6s imaging). No signal change with sucrose in saline alone (gustatory control).
behavioral_consequence: Inhibition (Kir2.1, TNT): Arrhythmic swallowing, decreased pump frequency, difficulty in cibarium emptying (dysphagia), decreased intake volume. Activation (CsChrimson, ReaChR): Difficulty in cibarium filling, incomplete filling, increased pump frequency, reduced volume per pump.
ecological_context: Regulates swallow rhythm and food ingestion efficiency; monitors physical properties of food (texture/viscosity) to coordinate filling and emptying.
summary: md-C neurons co-express nompC, Tmc, and piezo, wrap the cibarium, and sense mechanical force during swallowing. Inhibition causes dysphagia and emptying failure, while activation causes filling difficulty.

paper_title: Pharyngeal mechanosensory neurons control food swallow in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Activation of md-C neurons via P2X2/ATP
neural_activity: Calcium transients observed in MN12 and MN11 when md-C neurons were activated (GCaMP6s).
behavioral_consequence: MN11 associated with emptying (expelling food to foregut); MN12 associated with filling. Inhibition of md-C blocks emptying (MN11 not activated). Activation of md-C stimulates MNs.
ecological_context: Coordinated muscle contraction for food bolus movement through proboscis during swallowing.
summary: MD-C neurons form synaptic and functional connections with MN11/12 in the SEZ. Activation of md-C triggers calcium transients in MNs, linking sensory input to motor output for swallow coordination.

paper_title: Pharyngeal mechanosensory neurons control food swallow in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation of md-C neurons (with md-L ablated)
neural_activity: Not measured; used as control for md-C specificity.
behavioral_consequence: Ablation of md-L did not prevent md-C activation induced dysphagia, indicating md-L not responsible for mechanical sensation during swallowing.
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed.
summary: md-L neurons were labelled by the same intersection but were not responsible for mechanical sensation during swallowing, as ablation did not affect md-C mediated dysphagia.

paper_title: A single pair of pharyngeal neurons functions as a commander to reject high salt in Drosophila melanogaster
cell_type: IR60b-positive GRNs in the Labral Sense Organ (LSO)
experimental_stimulus: High salt (NaCl 300-1000 mM), Sucrose (2-100 mM), Capsaicin (with TRPV1), NaBr; Bitter compounds (5-50 mM) tested
neural_activity: Calcium imaging (GCaMP6f) showed activation by high salt and sucrose; response to Na+ (NaBr) but not Cl- (CaCl2, MgCl2, KCl); no response to low salt (50 mM NaCl) or bitter compounds
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson) inhibited Proboscis Extension Reflex (PER) to sucrose (aversive); Ir60b3 mutation increased high salt ingestion (DrosoX assay, 300 mM NaCl) and reduced avoidance in binary choice; TRPV1 expression caused capsaicin avoidance
ecological_context: Prevents ingestion of excessive salt levels which are detrimental to survival
techniques_used: ['Calcium imaging (GCaMP6f)', 'Optogenetics (CsChrimson)', 'Genetic mutants (Ir60b3)', 'DrosoX ingestion assay', 'Binary food choice assay', 'PER assay']
summary: IR60b-positive GRNs in the pharynx LSO detect high salt and sucrose, and their activation drives aversive behavior to prevent harmful salt ingestion.

paper_title: A single pair of pharyngeal neurons functions as a commander to reject high salt in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: High salt (300 mM NaCl)
neural_activity: Tip recordings showed action potentials in S4, S8, L4, L6 sensilla; responses dependent on Ir25a, Ir76b, and Ir7c
behavioral_consequence: Inactivation (Kir2.1) reduced high salt aversion behavior
ecological_context: Avoidance of toxic substances
summary: Labellar B and D GRNs are primarily responsible for high salt aversion in the external taste organs, dependent on Ir25a, Ir76b, and Ir7c.

paper_title: Control of Sugar and Amino Acid Feeding via Pharyngeal Taste Neurons
experimental_stimulus: Sugars (sucrose, glucose, fructose, glycerol, D-glucose, L-glucose), Amino acids (serine, threonine, phenylalanine, 3AA mixture), Mixtures (L-glucose + D-sorbitol/mannose/D-arabinose)
neural_activity: Not measured; functional role assessed via genetic protection in taste-blind background
behavioral_consequence: Restored intensity discrimination of sugars and amino acids in binary choice assays. Increased peak intensity and duration of first feeding event (FLIC). Drives preference for D-glucose over L-glucose. Does not drive preference based on nutritional value over sweetness.
ecological_context: Essential for dietary intake of nutritious chemicals for survival; guides short-term food choice decisions.
techniques_used: ['Binary choice feeding assays', 'FLIC (Fly Liquid-Food Interaction Counter)', 'Genetic protection (lexAop2-GAL80 strategy)']
summary: Pharyngeal Gr43a GRNs are sufficient to restore sugar and amino acid preference in taste-blind flies and drive feeding duration, but promote choice based on sweet taste rather than nutritional value.

paper_title: Control of Sugar and Amino Acid Feeding via Pharyngeal Taste Neurons
experimental_stimulus: Amino acid mixture (3AA)
neural_activity: Not measured; functional role assessed via genetic silencing
behavioral_consequence: Silencing alone had no effect. Double silencing with Gr64e abolished amino acid discrimination. Ir76b silencing reduced sensitivity.
ecological_context: Nutrient intake of amino acids.
summary: Pharyngeal Ir20a GRNs act with Gr43a GRNs for amino acid sensing, showing functional redundancy.

paper_title: Control of Sugar and Amino Acid Feeding via Pharyngeal Taste Neurons
experimental_stimulus: D-glucose (in combination with Gr43a)
neural_activity: Not measured; functional role assessed via genetic protection
behavioral_consequence: Protection with Gr43a significantly increased duration of first feeding event for D-glucose, but not L-glucose, compared to Gr43a alone.
ecological_context: Sugar consumption.
summary: Pharyngeal Ir67c GRNs differentially modulate Gr43a-driven feeding responses to D-glucose.

paper_title: Control of Sugar and Amino Acid Feeding via Pharyngeal Taste Neurons
experimental_stimulus: Red light (optogenetic activation)
neural_activity: Activation induced proboscis extension
behavioral_consequence: Induced proboscis extension in wild-type flies.
ecological_context: Initiation of feeding behavior.
summary: External sweet GRN activation causes proboscis extension.

paper_title: Control of Sugar and Amino Acid Feeding via Pharyngeal Taste Neurons
experimental_stimulus: Red light (optogenetic activation)
neural_activity: Activation did not induce proboscis extension
behavioral_consequence: No proboscis extension in Poxn mutants lacking external taste bristles.
ecological_context: Functional subdivision between pharyngeal and external neurons.
summary: Pharyngeal sweet GRN activation does not trigger proboscis extension, distinguishing them from external neurons.

paper_title: Control of Sugar and Amino Acid Feeding via Pharyngeal Taste Neurons
experimental_stimulus: Sugars, Amino acids, Bitter, Acid, Salt
neural_activity: Not measured; functional role assessed via genetic silencing
behavioral_consequence: Silencing abolished discrimination of sugar/amino acid concentrations and avoidance of aversive tastants.
ecological_context: Food selection decisions.
techniques_used: ['Binary choice feeding assays', 'Genetic silencing (Kir2.1 via Ir25a-GAL4)']
summary: All pharyngeal taste input is critical for guiding short-term food choice decisions.

paper_title: Control of Sugar and Amino Acid Feeding via Pharyngeal Taste Neurons
experimental_stimulus: Sugars, Amino acids
neural_activity: Not measured; functional role assessed via genetic protection
behavioral_consequence: Protection alone did not restore feeding preference for sucrose or amino acids in taste-blind background.
ecological_context: N/A
summary: Pharyngeal Gr32a and Ir60b GRNs are not sufficient alone to drive sugar or amino acid preference.

paper_title: Control of feeding by Piezo-mediated gut mechanosensation in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Ingestion of sucrose, sucralose, or water (inducing crop distension); Thermogenetic activation (Trpa1 at 30°C); Thermogenetic silencing (Shits at 32°C); Piezo knockout
neural_activity: CaLexA-mediated GFP expression increased with gut distension (sucrose, sucralose, water ingestion); No GFP increase in Piezo knockout flies despite distension; Trpa1 activation induced CaLexA response; Neurons innervate crop and anterior midgut
behavioral_consequence: Activation suppressed food intake and blocked meal-associated crop volume increase; Silencing caused ravenous feeding, gut bloating, and increased crop size; Knockout increased food intake, crop size, body weight, and feeding bout duration
ecological_context: Food storage during a meal occurs primarily in the anterior gut (crop); Food-induced gut distension serves as a signal for meal termination after ingestion of large food volumes
techniques_used: ['CaLexA reporter system', 'Gal4/UAS expression', 'Thermogenetics (Trpa1, Shibire)', 'Immunofluorescence/Confocal microscopy', 'Dye-based feeding assays', 'EXPRESSO automated feeding platform', 'Piezo knockout genetics', 'Histological crop size measurement']
summary: Piezo-expressing enteric neurons in the hypocerebral ganglion respond to anterior gut distension via CaLexA expression, control meal termination, and prevent gut bloating; their activation suppresses feeding while silencing or knockout increases consumption and crop volume.

paper_title: Control of feeding by Piezo-mediated gut mechanosensation in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Ingestion of sucrose, sucralose, or water
neural_activity: CaLexA-mediated GFP expression elevated after sucrose ingestion (nutrient conversion to fructose/glucose), but not after sucralose or water ingestion
behavioral_consequence: Not explicitly tested for behavioral consequences in this study
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed
summary: Gr43a-expressing enteric neurons respond specifically to sucrose ingestion consistent with sensing nutritional carbohydrates, distinct from Piezo-mediated distension sensing.

paper_title: A receptor and neuron that activate a circuit limiting sucrose consumption
cell_type: IR60b neuron (single pair of pharyngeal gustatory neurons in LSO sensillum 7)
experimental_stimulus: Sucrose (100-900 mM), Glucose (900 mM), Trehalose (300 mM), Bitter compounds (caffeine, coumarin, lobeline), Salts (NaCl, KCl), Amino acids, Water, Red light (optogenetic)
neural_activity: Increased Ca2+ fluorescence (GCaMP6s) in response to 900 mM sucrose and 900 mM glucose; no response to water or 300 mM trehalose; sucrose response eliminated in IR60b mutants
behavioral_consequence: Silencing or mutation increases total feeding time and volume ingested on sucrose; optogenetic activation decreases feeding time on trehalose; mutants have longer mean duration of individual feeding bouts in freely moving assays; does not affect pumping rate or thirst baseline
ecological_context: Prevents overconsumption of high-calorie sucrose to maintain nutrient homeostasis; may favor feeding on ripening fruits where sucrose levels decline and other sugars increase
techniques_used: ['Calcium imaging (GCaMP6s)', 'Pharyngeal pumping assay', 'Optogenetics (Chrimson)', 'CRISPR-Cas9 mutagenesis', 'FLIC assay', 'Confocal microscopy']
summary: The IR60b neuron in the pharynx detects sucrose and high-concentration glucose via the IR60b receptor, activating a circuit that limits feeding duration and prevents overconsumption of sucrose.

paper_title: Motor control of Drosophila feeding behavior
experimental_stimulus: Sucrose stimulation (anterior legs), Heat (TrpA1 activation), Red light (Chrimson activation), Heat (shibirets silencing)
neural_activity: Not directly measured; activity manipulated via TrpA1, Chrimson, and shibirets
behavioral_consequence: Activation causes rostrum lifting and labella spreading. Silencing prevents rostrum lifting during sucrose stimulation but not labella spreading
