ecological_context: Initiation of feeding behavior (Proboscis Extension Response); also involved in courtship licking
summary: MN9 controls rostrum lifting and contributes to labella spreading during the feeding motor sequence.

paper_title: Motor control of Drosophila feeding behavior
experimental_stimulus: Sucrose stimulation, Heat (TrpA1 activation), Red light (Chrimson activation), Heat (shibirets silencing)
neural_activity: Not directly measured; activity manipulated via TrpA1, Chrimson, and shibirets
behavioral_consequence: Activation causes haustellum extension. Silencing prevents haustellum extension during sucrose stimulation
ecological_context: Sequential step in feeding behavior (Proboscis Extension Response)
summary: MN2 controls haustellum extension during the proboscis extension sequence.

paper_title: Motor control of Drosophila feeding behavior
experimental_stimulus: Sucrose stimulation, Heat (TrpA1 activation), Red light (Chrimson activation), Heat (shibirets silencing)
neural_activity: Not directly measured; activity manipulated via TrpA1, Chrimson, and shibirets
behavioral_consequence: Activation causes labella extension. Silencing prevents labella extension during sucrose stimulation
ecological_context: Sequential step in feeding behavior; allows labella contact with food
summary: MN6 controls labella extension during the proboscis extension sequence.

paper_title: Motor control of Drosophila feeding behavior
experimental_stimulus: Heat (TrpA1 activation), Red light (Chrimson activation)
neural_activity: Not directly measured; activity manipulated via TrpA1 and Chrimson
behavioral_consequence: Activation causes labella spreading. Silencing was insufficient to prevent labella spreading
ecological_context: Prepares proboscis for food ingestion during feeding
summary: MN8 controls labella spreading to prepare for food ingestion.

paper_title: Motor control of Drosophila feeding behavior
experimental_stimulus: Heat (TrpA1 activation), Red light (Chrimson activation)
neural_activity: Not directly measured; activity manipulated via TrpA1 and Chrimson
behavioral_consequence: Activation causes active proboscis retraction into the head capsule. Silencing did not significantly impair retraction
ecological_context: Active retraction terminates feeding or responds to aversive stimuli
summary: MN1 mediates active proboscis retraction.

paper_title: Long-range projection neurons in the taste circuit of Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Sucrose applied to legs
neural_activity: Robust calcium signal increase (GCaMP6s); dendrites overlap with sugar GRN axons in VNC
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation increases Proboscis Extension Response (PER) in absence of taste stimulus; conditional silencing (Shibirets) does not affect PER or conditioned taste aversion memory
ecological_context: Detection of nutrients (sugar) critical for survival and feeding behaviors
techniques_used: ['GCaMP6s calcium imaging', 'Optogenetics (CsChrimson)', 'Proboscis Extension Response (PER) assay', 'Conditional silencing (Shibirets)']
summary: TPN1 neurons respond to sucrose on legs and their activation promotes PER, but they are not required for innate PER or learned taste aversion.

paper_title: Long-range projection neurons in the taste circuit of Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Sucrose applied to legs
neural_activity: Robust calcium signal increase (GCaMP6s); dendrites overlap with sugar GRN axons in VNC; axons project to SEZ and Superior Lateral Protocerebrum (SLP)
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation increases PER in absence of taste stimulus; conditional silencing impairs conditioned taste aversion (memory formation and retrieval); activation decreases PPL1 dopaminergic neuron calcium activity
ecological_context: Detection of nutrients (sugar) and mediation of learned associations (rewards/punishments)
techniques_used: ['GCaMP6s calcium imaging', 'Optogenetics (CsChrimson)', 'Proboscis Extension Response (PER) assay', 'Conditional silencing (Shibirets)', 'Two-photon calcium imaging of PPL1']
summary: TPN2 neurons respond to sucrose on legs and promote PER; they are required for conditioned taste aversion and convey sugar signals to higher brain centers.

paper_title: Long-range projection neurons in the taste circuit of Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Bitter mixture applied to legs or proboscis
neural_activity: Robust calcium signal increase (GCaMP6s); dendrites overlap with bitter GRN axons in SEZ; axons project to Superior Lateral Protocerebrum (SLP)
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation inhibits PER in presence of sucrose; activation serves as Unconditioned Stimulus (US) for aversive memory; conditional silencing during training impairs conditioned taste aversion; activation increases PPL1 dopaminergic neuron calcium activity
ecological_context: Avoidance of toxins and mediation of learned aversive associations
techniques_used: ['GCaMP6s calcium imaging', 'Optogenetics (CsChrimson)', 'Proboscis Extension Response (PER) assay', 'Conditional silencing (Shibirets)', 'Two-photon calcium imaging of PPL1', 'Multi-photon laser ablation']
summary: TPN3 neurons respond to bitter compounds, inhibit feeding behavior, and are essential for conveying punishment signals for learned taste aversion.

paper_title: Pharyngeal sense organs drive robust sugar consumption in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Sweet compounds (sucrose, fructose, glucose, glycerol, arabinose, L-fucose)
neural_activity: Robust calcium responses in SEZ projections (GCaMP3 imaging); responses sustained longer than labellar GRNs
behavioral_consequence: Necessary for preference for sweet compounds. Silencing (KIR2.1) abolishes preference for non-caloric sweeteners (arabinose, L-fucose) and reduces preference for nutritive sugars. Silencing reduces ingestion duration and number of feeding bouts
ecological_context: Directing sustained consumption of sweet compounds; providing feedback during feeding to maintain ingestion
techniques_used: ['Calcium imaging (GCaMP3)', 'KIR2.1 silencing', 'Binary choice feeding', 'Temporal consumption assay']
summary: Pharyngeal sweet GRNs respond to sweet compounds with sustained calcium activity and are necessary for sustaining ingestion and preference for sweet compounds.

paper_title: Pharyngeal sense organs drive robust sugar consumption in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Nutritive sugars (sorbitol preference context)
neural_activity: Silencing used (Gr43a-GAL4 driver)
behavioral_consequence: Silencing completely abolished preference for sorbitol, unlike pharyngeal silencing which only mildly reduced it
ecological_context: Taste-independent nutrient sensing mechanisms in short-term feeding
summary: Gr43a+ brain neurons are required for preference for sorbitol, indicating a role in taste-independent nutrient sensing.

paper_title: Pharyngeal sense organs drive robust sugar consumption in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Sweet compounds (on tarsi/labellum)
neural_activity: Proboscis Extension Reflex (PER) responses
behavioral_consequence: PER abolished in poxn mutants, confirming lack of peripheral sweet taste
ecological_context: Feeding initiation via PER
summary: External taste GRNs mediate PER responses to sweet compounds, which are absent in poxn mutants.

paper_title: Visual Projection Neurons Mediating Directed Courtship in Drosophila.
cell_type: LC10 visual projection neurons (LC10-SS1, LC10-SS2, LC10-SS3)
experimental_stimulus: Live females in courtship assays; tethered visual stimuli (gratings, long bars, virtual moving objects, looming discs, motion noise); optogenetic activation (CsChrimson, red light); thermogenetic P1 activation (TrpA1, 31°C)
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6m) showed preference for moving visual figures over full-field stimuli; motion-based center-surround receptive field; tuned to angular speeds ≤150°/s; broadly tuned to object sizes matching female courtship; responds to bright and dark edges; OFF full-field flicker elicited slow signal increase; unilateral activation triggers ipsilateral motor responses
behavioral_consequence: Silencing (TNT) reduces ability to orient toward/follow female and eliminates ipsilateral wing bias during singing; unilateral optogenetic activation elicits ipsilateral turning and wing extension in isolated males; P1-induced arousal state enhances frequency of LC10-induced wing extensions; silencing does not affect courtship motivation toward decapitated females
ecological_context: Males primarily use visual cues to orient toward and follow females during courtship; LC10 tuning matches visual signature (size/speed) of females during courtship behavior
techniques_used: ['Two-photon calcium imaging', 'Optogenetics (CsChrimson)', 'Genetic silencing (Tetanus toxin)', 'Automated video tracking (MateBook)', 'Fly-on-ball assay', 'Thermogenetics (TrpA1)']
summary: LC10 visual projection neurons are necessary for visually guided courtship tracking and ipsilateral wing extension. They detect moving objects via a motion-based center-surround mechanism tuned to female courtship cues, and their ability to elicit courtship actions is potentiated by P1-induced arousal.

paper_title: DANCE: An open-source analysis pipeline and low-cost hardware to quantify aggression and courtship in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Aggression assay context (social interaction)
neural_activity: Optogenetic silencing applied
behavioral_consequence: Silencing promotes aggression
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed
summary: Optogenetic silencing of dopaminergic neurons promotes aggression in the DANCE assay context.

paper_title: Visually induced initiation of Drosophila innate courtship-like following pursuit is mediated by central excitatory state
experimental_stimulus: Blue light (optogenetic stimulation via ChRWR), moving light spots, female abdomen tapping
neural_activity: Periodic Ca2+ rises corresponding to turns of the male fly during courtship (GCaMP3.0 Ca2+ imaging). Activated by female sex pheromones.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation initiates unilateral wing extension and vibration (on either side) and following pursuit of moving targets. Does not induce abdominal bending or proboscis extension.
ecological_context: Innate courtship ritual initiated by female-derived sensory stimuli. Includes male-specific fru-positive P1 subset functioning as a decision-making center for male courtship.
summary: pC1 cluster activation initiates early courtship behaviors including wing vibration and pursuit. Neural activity shows periodic Ca2+ rises during turning.

paper_title: Visually induced initiation of Drosophila innate courtship-like following pursuit is mediated by central excitatory state
experimental_stimulus: Blue light (optogenetic stimulation via ChRWR), moving light spots
neural_activity: Periodic Ca2+ increases coinciding with turns of the fly toward the hemisphere where clones were located (GCaMP3.0 Ca2+ imaging).
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation initiates contralateral wing displays, abdominal bending, and proboscis extension. Induces following pursuit of moving targets.
ecological_context: Mediates late-phase courtship behaviors such as licking genitalia and attempted copulation. Exhibits direction selectivity in motor programs.
summary: pC2l cluster activation initiates both early and late-phase courtship programs including contralateral wing displays and abdominal bending.

paper_title: Visually induced initiation of Drosophila innate courtship-like following pursuit is mediated by central excitatory state
experimental_stimulus: Moving multi-spot visual patterns, female tapping
neural_activity: Discrete Ca2+ rises upon female tapping. Enhanced Ca2+ responses to visual stimuli in group-reared fru mutant males compared to isolation-reared.
behavioral_consequence: Enhanced responsiveness correlates with visually induced courtship-like pursuit in fru mutant males without prior chemosensory priming.
ecological_context: Social experience (group rearing) modulates neural responsiveness. In fru mutants, this leads to prevalent male-to-male courtship and chaining behavior.
summary: LPR neurons show enhanced visual responsiveness in group-reared fru mutants, correlating with increased visually induced courtship behavior without pheromone priming.

paper_title: Neuronal control of locomotor handedness in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Symmetric Y-maze environment (unbiased, no asymmetric sensory cues)
behavioral_consequence: Acute silencing (Shibirets, GAL80ts;Kir2.1) or hyperactivation (dTRPA1) of c465, R16D01, or R73D06 PFNs caused large increases in the variability of turning scores (handedness) within a population. Data suggests PFNs projecting to No domain 3 specifically regulate this variability.
ecological_context: Discussion suggests these asymmetries may help the animal avoid analysis paralysis or be a feature of noisy biological systems during decision-making.
techniques_used: ['GAL4/UAS system', 'Temperature-sensitive neuronal silencing (Shibirets, GAL80ts;Kir2.1)', 'Temperature-sensitive neuronal activation (dTRPA1)', 'Y-maze locomotion assay', 'Immunohistochemistry and confocal microscopy']
summary: PFN neurons regulate variability in turn bias; silencing or hyperactivating them increases population variability in handedness.

paper_title: Neuronal control of locomotor handedness in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Symmetric Y-maze environment (unbiased, no asymmetric sensory cues)
behavioral_consequence: Silencing mushroom body neurons (using c465-GAL4;MB247-GAL80) had no effect on turn bias variability.
summary: Silencing c465-expressed mushroom body neurons had no effect on turn bias variability.

paper_title: Octopamine Neuromodulation Regulates Gr32a-Linked Aggression and Courtship Pathways in Drosophila Males
experimental_stimulus: Male cuticular hydrocarbon (CHC) extracts applied to legs; Male-male interaction in aggression chambers
neural_activity: Ca2+ changes were not reliably detected in foreleg neurons expressing GCaMP3.0. Project to distinct zones in the suboesophageal ganglion (SOG).
behavioral_consequence: Ablation increases latency to first lunge (aggression), reduces number of lunges, and significantly increases male-male courtship (wing extensions).
ecological_context: Pheromonal signals detected by these neurons enhance male aggression and regulate gender recognition (conspecificity) in social behavior like courtship and aggression.
summary: Gr32a neurons project to the SOG; their ablation delays aggression onset, reduces aggression intensity, and increases inappropriate male-male courtship.

paper_title: Octopamine Neuromodulation Regulates Gr32a-Linked Aggression and Courtship Pathways in Drosophila Males
experimental_stimulus: Male-male interaction in aggression chambers
neural_activity: Not directly measured; identified via tsh-Gal80 blocking Gal4 activity in legs to isolate mouth neurons.
behavioral_consequence: Ablation significantly increases latency to first lunge and reduces number of lunges (aggression). Unlike total Gr32a ablation, does not increase male-male courtship (wing extensions were less than control levels).
ecological_context: Male aggression initiation and social behavior selection.
summary: Mouth-specific Gr32a neurons promote male aggression (latency and lunge number) without suppressing male-male courtship, distinguishing them from the general Gr32a population.

paper_title: Octopamine Neuromodulation Regulates Gr32a-Linked Aggression and Courtship Pathways in Drosophila Males
experimental_stimulus: Male CHC extracts applied to legs
neural_activity: Significant increases in intracellular Ca2+ (GCaMP6s) in response to male CHC extracts. Response is abolished in the absence of Gr32a neurons.
behavioral_consequence: Elimination of OA (tbhM18 mutation) delays onset of aggression (2-fold increase in latency), reduces lunges, and increases male-male courtship (wing extensions).
ecological_context: Octopaminergic neuromodulatory neurons function as early as a second-order step in chemosensory-driven male social behavior pathways (aggression and courtship selection).
summary: OA neurons in the SOG respond to male CHCs in a Gr32a-dependent manner; OA signaling is required for timely aggression onset and suppression of male-male courtship.

paper_title: Octopamine Neuromodulation Regulates Gr32a-Linked Aggression and Courtship Pathways in Drosophila Males
experimental_stimulus: None (Anatomical focus)
neural_activity: Anatomical synaptic contacts with Gr32a neurons confirmed via GRASP (GFP Reconstitution Across Synaptic Partners).
behavioral_consequence: Absence of FruM results in increase in male-male courtship (citing prior study [21]). This paper links these neurons anatomically to the Gr32a pathway.
ecological_context: Male sex-specific modulation of behavior (behavioral choice between courtship and aggression).
summary: Three identified FruM-OA neurons form anatomical connections with Gr32a neurons, linking the chemosensory pathway to sex-specific behavioral choice circuits.

paper_title: Two-photon calcium imaging from motion-sensitive neurons in head-fixed Drosophila during optomotor walking behavior
cell_type: HS neurons (Horizontal-system lobula plate tangential cells)
experimental_stimulus: Horizontally rotating vertical grating pattern (1 Hz temporal frequency) in preferred (PD) and null (ND) directions
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging with GCaMP3.0 showed slow increases in fluorescence (calcium transients) in soma and dendrites during PD motion; slight decreases in fluorescence during ND stimulation recorded from soma
behavioral_consequence: Calcium transients correlated with robust optomotor turning behavior during walking; turning response lagged behind calcium transients (mean lag 4.37 ± 1.65 s)
ecological_context: PD corresponds to the direction of expected optic flow for a forward-walking animal; system well suited for studying fly navigation and orientation behavior
techniques_used: ['Two-photon calcium imaging', 'Head-fixed walking on air-supported ball', 'High-resolution ball tracking', 'LED visual arena']
summary: HS neurons (HS-North and HS-Equator) show calcium transients correlated with robust optomotor turning behavior during walking, with neural responses preceding behavioral turning.

paper_title: Identification of an aggression-promoting pheromone and its receptor neurons in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Synthetic 11-cis-vaccenyl acetate (cVA) and endogenously produced cVA from male flies
neural_activity: Activated by cVA; responds essentially exclusively to cVA
behavioral_consequence: Genetic activation (NaChBac) or exposure to cVA promotes male-male aggression (lunges, wing threats, tussling, chasing) and dispersal from food resources. Silencing (Kir2.1) or Or67d mutation blocks cVA-induced aggression and dispersal. Rescue of aggression deficit in Or83b mutants requires Or83b expression in Or67d-expressing OSNs.
ecological_context: Mediates negative-feedback control of male population density; promotes dispersal from food resources when male density is high to balance feeding, reproduction, and competition.
techniques_used: ['Behavioral assays (lunging count, dispersal count)', 'Genetic manipulation (GAL4/UAS-Kir2.1 silencing, UAS-NaChBac activation, GAL4 null mutants, Or83b rescue)', 'CADABRA software for behavior analysis']
summary: Or67d-expressing OSNs detect cVA to promote male-male aggression and dispersal from food resources, mediating negative-feedback control of male population density.

paper_title: Identification of an aggression-promoting pheromone and its receptor neurons in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Synthetic cVA
neural_activity: Identified as a cVA receptor
behavioral_consequence: Silencing (Kir2.1) did not impair cVA promotion of aggression or suppression of male-female mating. Genetic activation (NaChBac) did not promote aggression.
ecological_context: Not explicitly linked to aggression function or population density control in this study
summary: Or65a-expressing OSNs are cVA receptors but are not required for cVA-induced aggression or activation of aggression by genetic means.

paper_title: Neural circuitry of social learning in Drosophila requires multiple inputs to facilitate inter-species communication
experimental_stimulus: Interspecies social communication cues (olfactory pheromones and food odorants) during dialect training with D. ananassae
neural_activity: Not directly measured; necessity inferred through genetic inactivation
behavioral_consequence: Inactivation (Or69a mutants or Or69a-RNAi) prevents D. melanogaster from learning the dialect from D. ananassae (unable to reduce oviposition in response to wasp threat). Or47b and Or65a mutants show wild-type dialect training
ecological_context: Facilitates communication of parasitoid wasp threat between closely related Drosophila species through olfactory cues
techniques_used: ['Genetic inactivation (UAS-shits, Or69a-GAL4 > UAS-Or69aRNAi, Or69a-/-)', 'Egg-laying assay (oviposition rate)', 'Immunofluorescence (Or69a-GFP expression validation)']
summary: Or69a OSNs are necessary for dialect training; their inactivation prevents D. melanogaster from learning the wasp threat communication from D. ananassae.

paper_title: Neural circuitry of social learning in Drosophila requires multiple inputs to facilitate inter-species communication
experimental_stimulus: Visual cues (moving dark edges/wing movement) during dialect training
neural_activity: Not directly measured; necessity inferred through genetic inactivation
behavioral_consequence: Silencing L2 results in an inability of D. melanogaster to learn the dialect. Silencing L1 or L3 neurons results in wild-type dialect training
ecological_context: Detects visual cues required for interspecies communication of predator threat during social interaction
summary: L2 neurons are required for dialect training; inactivation results in an inability to learn the dialect from D. ananassae.

paper_title: Neural circuitry of social learning in Drosophila requires multiple inputs to facilitate inter-species communication
experimental_stimulus: Visual cues (moving dark edges/wing movement) during dialect training
neural_activity: Not directly measured; necessity inferred through genetic inactivation
behavioral_consequence: Silencing L4 prevents D. melanogaster from learning the dialect (validated with splitL4-GAL4). L1 and L3 silencing is dispensable
ecological_context: Detects visual cues required for interspecies communication of predator threat during social interaction
summary: L4 neurons are necessary for dialect training; inactivation hinders dialect learning from D. ananassae.

paper_title: Neural circuitry of social learning in Drosophila requires multiple inputs to facilitate inter-species communication
experimental_stimulus: Social interaction and dialect training period (weeklong cohabitation)
neural_activity: Not directly measured; necessity inferred through genetic inactivation
behavioral_consequence: Inactivation of Region 5 (R38E07 driver) prevents dialect learning. Inactivation of other FB regions (1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 9) is dispensable
ecological_context: Involved in consolidation of memory pertaining to social interactions and predator threat communication
techniques_used: ['Genetic inactivation (R38E07-GAL4 > UAS-shits)', 'Egg-laying assay (oviposition rate)', 'Immunofluorescence (FB driver validation)']
summary: Region 5 of the fan-shaped body is necessary for dialect training; inactivation prevents D. melanogaster from learning the dialect from D. ananassae.

paper_title: Mechanosensory Interactions Drive Collective Behaviour in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Gentle touch from neighbouring flies (inter-fly encounters); mechanical touch with a metallic disc on distal leg segments
neural_activity: Identified via R55B01-Gal4 and piezo-Gal4;cha3-Gal80 drivers showing GFP expression in distal leg structures; required for mechanosensory transmission (linked to NOMPC channel activity)
behavioral_consequence: Silencing neurons (UAS-Tnt) significantly diminishes Encounter Response frequency and abolishes collective odour avoidance; optogenetic activation (ChR2) elicits Encounter Response-like walking
ecological_context: Flies aggregate at high densities (>1 fly/cm2) to feed; this mechanism drives collective escape from aversive odour (5% CO2) in dense groups
techniques_used: ['Fluorescence Behavioural Imaging (FBI)', 'High-resolution video tracking', 'Genetic silencing (UAS-Tnt)', 'Optogenetics (ChR2)', 'Confocal microscopy', 'nompC mutants']
summary: Distal leg mechanosensory sensilla neurons mediate inter-fly touch sensing required for Encounter Responses and collective odour avoidance.

paper_title: Mechanosensory Interactions Drive Collective Behaviour in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Mechanical touch (inferred from screening of mechanosensory neurons)
neural_activity: Express R55B01-Gal4 and piezo-Gal4; anatomically identified in femoral and tibial leg structures
behavioral_consequence: Silencing these neurons alone had no effect on Encounter Response frequency
ecological_context: Present in leg contact scenarios during aggregation
summary: Leg chordotonal organs are not required for Encounter Responses, distinguishing them from distal leg mechanosensory sensilla neurons.

paper_title: Adjacent Neuronal Fascicle Guides Motoneuron 24 Dendritic Branching and Axonal Routing Decisions through Dscam1 Signaling
experimental_stimulus: Genetic manipulation (dscam1 knockout; cell-type specific dscam1 RNAi via hh-GAL4)
neural_activity: Morphological response: Significant reduction in primary dendritic branches (1.3 ± 0.4 vs 7.6 ± 0.3 in wild-type); disruption of axonal routing area (4.1 ± 4.4 μm2 vs 36.2 ± 3.6 μm2 in wild-type); medial shift of cell body position. Technique: Retrograde lipophilic dye labeling (DiD), Confocal microscopy, Immunofluorescence.
summary: MN24 requires dscam1 expression for primary dendritic outgrowth and proper axonal routing. Dscam1 acts cell-autonomously and non-cell-autonomously to guide dendrite formation at the lateral edge of the neuropile.

paper_title: Adjacent Neuronal Fascicle Guides Motoneuron 24 Dendritic Branching and Axonal Routing Decisions through Dscam1 Signaling
experimental_stimulus: Genetic manipulation (dscam1 RNAi via Scp2-GAL4)
neural_activity: Non-cell-autonomous effect on MN24: Reduction in MN24 primary dendritic branches (2.0 ± 0.4 vs 8.3 ± 0.6 in control); disruption of MN24 axonal routing area (12.6 ± 4.6 μm2 vs 35.6 ± 5.8 μm2 in control). Technique: Scp2-GAL4 driver, Confocal microscopy, Immunofluorescence.
summary: Scp2-positive neurons in the lateral fascicle provide Dscam1 signals necessary for MN24 dendritogenesis and axonal routing, acting non-cell-autonomously.

paper_title: The Hunchback temporal transcription factor determines motor neuron axon and dendrite targeting in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Misexpression of Hunchback temporal transcription factor to create ectopic U1 neurons
summary: U1-U5 neurons project axons sequentially, followed by sequential dendrite extension. Misexpression of Hunchback creates ectopic U1 neurons with axon muscle targeting and dendrite neuropil targeting consistent with U1 intrinsic temporal identity rather than their time of birth or differentiation.

paper_title: Gap junctions desynchronize a neural circuit to stabilize insect flight
experimental_stimulus: Tethered flight (natural behavior), optogenetic activation of cholinergic input or direct MN activation, somatic current injection (in situ patch-clamp)
neural_activity: Fires at low frequency (3–12 Hz) relative to wingbeat (~200 Hz), approximately every 20th to 40th wingbeat. Firing is splayed-out in time (splay state) with conserved phase relationships across individuals. MNs exhibit weak electrical coupling (gap junctions) and linear input-output relationship (f-I curves). Excitability dynamics near saddle homoclinic orbit (SNL) bifurcation point.
behavioral_consequence: MN firing frequency regulates wingbeat power (frequency and stroke amplitude). Splay state minimizes wingbeat frequency fluctuations (stabilizes flight power). Genetic disruption of gap junctions (shakB KD) or excitability (Shab OE) causes synchronous firing, which results in 8x higher wingbeat frequency fluctuations (instability).
ecological_context: Asynchronous flight is used by more than 600,000 insect species with wingbeat frequencies of 100–1,000 Hz. High wingbeat frequencies in small flyers require stable power output. Splayed-out firing is conserved across species, suggesting selective benefits over millions of years.
techniques_used: ['in vivo extracellular recordings (EMG)', 'tethered flight with laser-based wingbeat detection', 'dual in situ patch-clamp electrophysiology', 'optogenetics (UAS-XXL-ChR)', 'in vivo calcium imaging (GCaMP8f in muscle fibers)', 'genetic manipulation (RNAi, overexpression)', 'computational modelling']
summary: Drosophila flight motoneurons (MN1–5) form a minimal CPG where weak electrical synapses and specific excitability dynamics generate a splay state (desynchronized firing). This pattern stabilizes wingbeat power by minimizing fluctuations during flight, which is critical for the high-frequency asynchronous flight used by most flying insects.

paper_title: Distinct Aging-Vulnerable and -Resilient Trajectories of Specific Motor Circuit Functions in Oxidation- and Temperature-Stressed Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Electrical stimulation across the brain (eyes) using tungsten electrodes
neural_activity: Action potential propagation detected as Short-Latency Response (SLR) and Long-Latency Response (LLR) in DLM motor neurons
behavioral_consequence: Jump-and-flight escape reflex
ecological_context: Critical for survival
summary: GF neuron activation triggers escape reflex. SLR properties (latency, threshold) remain robust with age, while LLR afferent processing shows age-dependent decline and accelerated habituation.

paper_title: Distinct Aging-Vulnerable and -Resilient Trajectories of Specific Motor Circuit Functions in Oxidation- and Temperature-Stressed Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: GF pathway activation
neural_activity: Transmission of GF signal to DLM motor neurons (detected via DLM spiking)
behavioral_consequence: Jump-and-flight escape reflex
ecological_context: Critical for survival
summary: PSI acts as a downstream relay in the GF pathway, maintaining robust transmission properties during aging.

paper_title: Distinct Aging-Vulnerable and -Resilient Trajectories of Specific Motor Circuit Functions in Oxidation- and Temperature-Stressed Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: GF pathway stimulation or air puff
neural_activity: Action potentials recorded in DLM muscle fibers; firing rate increases significantly with age in WT flies
behavioral_consequence: Sustained tethered flight and jump-and-flight
ecological_context: Flight ability
summary: DLM motor neurons maintain flight performance despite aging by increasing firing rates to compensate for biomechanical changes, keeping Wing Beat Frequency stable.

paper_title: Distinct Aging-Vulnerable and -Resilient Trajectories of Specific Motor Circuit Functions in Oxidation- and Temperature-Stressed Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Electroconvulsive stimulation (ECS) (200 Hz, 2 s pulses)
neural_activity: Stereotypic seizure discharges (Initial Discharge, Paralysis, Delayed Discharge) monitored in DLM
behavioral_consequence: Seizure-like discharges and paralysis
summary: Seizure discharges evoked by ECS show decreased threshold with age, and Initial Discharge spike counts peak then decline across the life span.

paper_title: Postnatal Increases in Axonal Conduction Velocity of an Identified Drosophila Interneuron Require Fast Sodium, L-Type Calcium and Shaker Potassium Channels
experimental_stimulus: Electrical stimulation of GF interneuron; Postnatal age (1hPE vs 24hPE); Targeted RNAi knock-down of Para, DmCa1D, Shaker, Shal, Slowpoke channels in GF
neural_activity: Axonal action potential (AP) conduction velocity increased 80% during the first day of adult life (from 1.15 m/s at 1hPE to 2.07 m/s at 24hPE). Knock-down of Para, DmCa1D, or Shaker channels decreased conduction velocity and counteracted postnatal speeding. DmCa1D RNAi almost eliminated postnatal speeding.
behavioral_consequence: Circuit mediates the jump-and-flight escape reflex. Increased GF conduction velocity ensures jumping precedes flight initiation. Genetic manipulation reduced the postnatal speed increase, potentially affecting escape timing.
ecological_context: Optimized for fast escape responses. A 0.2 ms increase in conduction velocity provides a 1.6 mm advantage against a threat approaching at 30 km/h (e.g., frog tongue).
techniques_used: ['Electrophysiology (muscle potential latency recording)', 'Targeted RNAi knock-down', 'Confocal microscopy (axon diameter and channel localization)']
summary: The GF interneuron axon conduction velocity increases 80% postnatally due to adjustments in Para sodium, Shaker potassium, and DmCa1D L-type calcium channel expression, supporting the rapid jump-and-flight escape reflex.

paper_title: Postnatal Increases in Axonal Conduction Velocity of an Identified Drosophila Interneuron Require Fast Sodium, L-Type Calcium and Shaker Potassium Channels
cell_type: TTM Motoneuron (TTMn) and Dorsal Longitudinal Flight Muscle Motoneurons (MN1-5)
experimental_stimulus: Electrical stimulation of thoracic nerve cord (motoneurons); Postnatal age (1hPE vs 24hPE)
neural_activity: Axonal conduction speed and neuromuscular transmission delay did not undergo postnatal changes between 1hPE and 24hPE. TTMn-to-TTM latency remained stable (0.80 ms vs 0.84 ms).
behavioral_consequence: TTMn controls extension of mesothoracic legs during escape jumping. MN1-5 control wing downstroke during flight initiation.
ecological_context: Part of the giant fiber system circuit that executes the jump-and-flight escape reflex.
summary: Motoneurons (TTMn and MN1-5) show no postnatal changes in axonal conduction speed or transmission delay, indicating the GF interneuron is the primary site of postnatal speeding in the escape circuit.

paper_title: Autophagy within the mushroom body protects from synapse aging in a non-cell autonomous manner
experimental_stimulus: Olfactory associative conditioning (odor paired with electric shock)
neural_activity: Attenuation of autophagy (atg5/atg9 RNAi) led to accumulation of p62/Ref(2)p aggregates in cell bodies and reduced expression of short Neuropeptide F (sNPF) peptide. Synaptic active zone scaffold protein Bruchpilot (BRP) levels increased brain-wide. STED microscopy revealed increased BRP scaffold diameters at presynaptic active zones.
behavioral_consequence: Significant reduction in Short-Term Memory (STM) and Mid-Term Memory (MTM) scores, specifically the Anesthesia-Sensitive Memory (ASM) component. Innate smell scores and principal locomotion behavior remained unaffected.
ecological_context: Autophagy status in MB enables high-level control over brain-wide information processing strategy, steering life-strategy decisions such as whether or not to form memories. Long-term memory encoding is noted as energetically costly.
techniques_used: ['RNA interference', 'Immunostaining (p62, sNPF, BRP)', 'Olfactory conditioning behavioral assays (T-maze)', 'STED microscopy', 'Transmission Electron Microscopy (EM)']
summary: Mushroom Body intrinsic neurons (Kenyon Cells) require autophagy to maintain sNPF neuropeptide levels and prevent brain-wide synaptic metaplasticity (increased BRP active zone size). Impairing autophagy in these cells specifically reduces anesthesia-sensitive memory components without affecting innate smell.

paper_title: Autophagy within the mushroom body protects from synapse aging in a non-cell autonomous manner
experimental_stimulus: Olfactory associative conditioning (implied via memory testing)
neural_activity: Attenuation of autophagy (atg5/atg9 RNAi) caused accumulation of p62/Ref(2)p aggregates in PN cell bodies.
behavioral_consequence: Innate smell scores and aversive memory scores remained unaffected despite p62 accumulation.
summary: Autophagy impairment in Projection Neurons leads to p62 accumulation but does not affect olfactory memory or innate smell, indicating specificity of autophagy requirements to other regions.

paper_title: Autophagy within the mushroom body protects from synapse aging in a non-cell autonomous manner
experimental_stimulus: General brain function (memory testing)
neural_activity: Attenuation of autophagy (atg9 RNAi) caused strong buildup of p62 aggregates in targeted mNSCs/IPCs.
behavioral_consequence: Memory scores and brain-wide BRP staining levels were not significantly changed compared to controls.
summary: Autophagy suppression specifically in Median Neurosecretory Cells/Insulin-Producing Cells does not trigger brain-wide synaptic phenotypes or memory defects, suggesting these cells do not mediate the observed non-cell autonomous effects of MB autophagy.

paper_title: Newly Identified Electrically Coupled Neurons Support Development of the Drosophila Giant Fiber Model Circuit
experimental_stimulus: Iontophoretic neurobiotin injection into GFI; targeted apoptosis via UAS-hid (split-Gal4 10B11-AD 14A06-DBD); optogenetic activation (ChR2-H134R/ChOP-XXL)
neural_activity: Electrically coupled to GFI at the inframedial bridge (IB) with Shaking-B positive contacts; postsynaptic at IB (DenMark+, Syt::GFP-); presynaptic in leg neuropil (Syt::GFP+, DenMark-). Ablation resulted in loss of one GFI neuron in 65% of cases with complete ablation.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation did not produce behaviors. Ablation caused developmental circuit defects (GFI loss), not immediate behavioral assay changes.
ecological_context: Part of the GF escape circuit involved in rapid jump-and-flight response to threat.
summary: GFC1 is electrically coupled to GFI at the IB, postsynaptic at IB and presynaptic in leg neuropil. Ablation of GFC1 causes loss of one GFI neuron, compensated by contralateral axon extension. Optogenetic activation produced no behaviors.

paper_title: Newly Identified Electrically Coupled Neurons Support Development of the Drosophila Giant Fiber Model Circuit
experimental_stimulus: Iontophoretic neurobiotin injection into GFI; Immunohistochemistry
neural_activity: Electrically coupled to GFI at the IB and axonal bend (Shaking-B). Shows mixed polarity with colocalized DenMark and Syt::GFP in looped processes. Targets wing neuropil.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation did not produce behaviors.
ecological_context: Part of the GF escape circuit involved in rapid jump-and-flight response to threat.
summary: GFC2 is electrically coupled to GFI at IB and axonal bend with mixed synaptic polarity. Targets wing neuropil. Optogenetic activation produced no behaviors.

paper_title: Newly Identified Electrically Coupled Neurons Support Development of the Drosophila Giant Fiber Model Circuit
experimental_stimulus: Iontophoretic neurobiotin injection into GFI; Immunohistochemistry
neural_activity: Electrically coupled to GFI at the IB (Shaking-B+). Postsynaptic at IB (DenMark+), presynaptic in leg neuropil (Syt::GFP+). Targets leg neuropil.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation did not produce behaviors.
ecological_context: Part of the GF escape circuit involved in rapid jump-and-flight response to threat.
summary: GFC3 is electrically coupled to GFI at the IB, postsynaptic at IB and presynaptic in leg neuropil. Optogenetic activation produced no behaviors.

paper_title: Newly Identified Electrically Coupled Neurons Support Development of the Drosophila Giant Fiber Model Circuit
experimental_stimulus: Iontophoretic neurobiotin injection into GFI
neural_activity: Electrically coupled to GFI at the IB (inferred from GFC3/4 driver overlap). Polarity and electrical synapse contacts could not be tested due to marker lethality with 42A06-Gal4 driver. Predicted postsynaptic at IB and presynaptic in leg neuropil.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation did not produce behaviors.
ecological_context: Part of the GF escape circuit involved in rapid jump-and-flight response to threat.
summary: GFC4 is electrically coupled to GFI at the IB. Synaptic polarity could not be experimentally determined due to genetic marker lethality. Targets leg neuropil.

paper_title: Newly Identified Electrically Coupled Neurons Support Development of the Drosophila Giant Fiber Model Circuit
experimental_stimulus: Iontophoretic neurobiotin/TRITC injection; Targeted ablation of GFC1/PSI
neural_activity: Connects to GFC1-4 at the inframedial bridge (IB). When one GFI is lost due to GFC1 ablation, the remaining GFI always extends a compensating axon to the contralateral side (100%).
behavioral_consequence: Part of the escape circuit mediating jump in response to threat. Loss of GFI leads to circuit reorganization, not immediate behavioral quantification in this study.
ecological_context: Escape response circuit for rapid jump and flight in response to threat.
summary: GFI is the central interneuron of the GF escape circuit. Loss of one GFI due to GFC1 ablation is compensated by contralateral innervation from the persisting GFI.

paper_title: Inhibition of oxidative stress in cholinergic projection neurons fully rescues aging-associated olfactory circuit degeneration in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Eight different odors (three attractive: 2,3-butanedione, putrescine; five aversive: benzaldehyde, 3-octanol, CO2, etc.)
neural_activity: Single sensillum recordings (SSR) showed a significant decrease in elicited spikes for benzaldehyde and CO2 with age, but not for six other odors. No significant difference in OSN number, cell body size, or olfactory receptor (OR) expression between young and old flies.
behavioral_consequence: Olfactory preference index declined with age in T-maze assays. Knockdown or overexpression of SOD2 in OSNs (ORCO-Gal4) had no effect on olfactory preference behavior.
ecological_context: OSNs detect odors for approach or avoidance behavior; loss of OSN function is not the primary cause of age-related smell loss in this study.
summary: OSNs show decreased spike response to benzaldehyde and CO2 with age but no change in number or receptor expression. Manipulating SOD2 in OSNs does not affect olfactory behavior or rescue aging phenotypes.

paper_title: Inhibition of oxidative stress in cholinergic projection neurons fully rescues aging-associated olfactory circuit degeneration in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Odors (benzaldehyde, 2,3-butanedione, 3-octanol, 4-methylcyclohexanol, linalool)
neural_activity: In vivo calcium imaging (GCaMP3) revealed reduced odor response in antennal lobe glomeruli and a reduced number of responsive boutons in the mushroom body calyx in aged flies. Aging showed reduced ChAT, mitochondrial reporter (mito-mcherry), presynaptic active zones (BRP), and postsynaptic densities (Drep2) in the calyx.
behavioral_consequence: Aged flies showed reduced olfactory preference/aversion in T-maze. Knockdown of SOD2 in PNs (GH146-Gal4) reduced olfactory preference in young flies. Overexpression of SOD2 in PNs fully rescued olfactory performance in aged flies.
ecological_context: PNs transmit odor information for approach or avoidance behavior; their degeneration drives the functional decline of the olfactory circuit controlling these behaviors.
techniques_used: ['In vivo calcium imaging (epifluorescence, two-photon)', 'Confocal and STED microscopy', 'T-maze behavioral assay', 'RNAi', 'Overexpression', 'RNA-sequencing']
summary: Cholinergic PNs exhibit reduced odor response and axonal degeneration (reduced boutons, ChAT, mitochondria, synapses) with age. SOD2 expression in PNs is necessary and sufficient to prevent this degeneration and restore olfactory behavior.

paper_title: Reduced insulin signaling maintains electrical transmission in a neural circuit in aging flies
experimental_stimulus: Electrical brain stimulation (via supra-oesophageal ganglion); light air-puff to head
neural_activity: Response latency (conduction velocity) recorded via muscle extracellular electrodes; SHAK-B gap junction protein density in bilateral tracts quantified via immunostaining; IR expression confirmed via immunolabeling
behavioral_consequence: Escape response (leg extension followed by flight); reduced insulin signaling prevents age-related increase in response latency; neuronal IIS silencing increases mean frequency of DLM responses to air-puff
ecological_context: Rapid conduction has survival value; escape behavior involves legs extension and flight
summary: GF interneurons mediate fast escape response. Aging increases response latency and reduces SHAK-B gap junction density; reduced insulin signaling preserves SHAK-B density and prevents functional decline in aging flies.

paper_title: Reduced insulin signaling maintains electrical transmission in a neural circuit in aging flies
experimental_stimulus: Electrical brain stimulation; direct thoracic stimulation
neural_activity: Response latency recorded at TTM muscle; direct stimulation latencies unaffected by age or IIS status
behavioral_consequence: Jump response (tergotrochanteral muscle contraction); transmission delay occurs upstream of TTMn
ecological_context: Jump escape behavior
summary: TTMn mediate jump response. Age-related transmission slowing occurs upstream of TTMn, as direct stimulation latencies remain normal regardless of age or IIS status.

paper_title: Reduced insulin signaling maintains electrical transmission in a neural circuit in aging flies
cell_type: Dorsal Longitudinal Flight Muscle Motor Neurons (DLMn)
experimental_stimulus: Electrical brain stimulation; direct thoracic stimulation
neural_activity: Response latency recorded at DLM muscle; direct stimulation latencies unaffected by age or IIS status
behavioral_consequence: Flight response (dorsal longitudinal muscle contraction); transmission delay occurs upstream of DLMn
ecological_context: Flight escape behavior
summary: DLMn mediate flight response. Age-related transmission slowing occurs upstream of DLMn, as direct stimulation latencies remain normal regardless of age or IIS status.

paper_title: Reduced insulin signaling maintains electrical transmission in a neural circuit in aging flies
experimental_stimulus: Electrical brain stimulation
neural_activity: Part of bisynaptic DLM pathway; targeted by A307-GAL4 driver for IIS manipulation
behavioral_consequence: Contribution to DLM flight response pathway
ecological_context: Flight escape behavior
summary: PSI neurons relay signals to DLMn in the GFS circuit. Reduced IIS in the GFS circuit (including PSI) preserves SHAK-B density and prevents age-related functional decline.

paper_title: Constitutive Activation of Ca2+/Calmodulin-Dependent Protein Kinase II during Development Impairs Central Cholinergic Transmission in a Circuit Underlying Escape Behavior in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Direct electrical stimulation of brain; Indirect electrical stimulation of eye layers (visual pathway); Lights-off stimulus
neural_activity: Direct stimulation evokes Short Latency Response (SLR) in muscles. Indirect stimulation evokes Long Latency Response (LLR). Constitutive activation of CaMKII-T287D in GF neurons blocks LLR transmission at ColA/GF synapse but preserves SLR capability.
behavioral_consequence: Flies failed to exhibit light-induced escape behavior (jump and flight) due to blocked sensory input to GF.
ecological_context: Escape behavior triggered by sudden light dimming (startle response).
techniques_used: ['Electrophysiology (brain/thorax stimulation, muscle recording)', 'Behavioral assay', 'Gal4-UAS genetic manipulation', 'Confocal microscopy']
summary: GF interneurons transmit visual startle signals; postsynaptic CaMKII-T287D expression during development blocks input from ColA interneurons, preventing escape behavior.

paper_title: Constitutive Activation of Ca2+/Calmodulin-Dependent Protein Kinase II during Development Impairs Central Cholinergic Transmission in a Circuit Underlying Escape Behavior in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Electrical stimulation of GF neurons; Direct electrical stimulation of thorax
neural_activity: Evoked muscle action potentials in TTM. CaMKII-T287D expression increases Refractory Period (RP) and decreases Following Frequency 50% (FF50) in GF-TTM pathway; Neuromuscular junction (NMJ) function remains normal.
behavioral_consequence: Impaired jump response component of escape behavior.
ecological_context: Jump component of escape behavior.
summary: TTMns mediate jump escape; postsynaptic central cholinergic synapses are impaired by CaMKII-T287D during development, increasing RP and decreasing FF50.

paper_title: Constitutive Activation of Ca2+/Calmodulin-Dependent Protein Kinase II during Development Impairs Central Cholinergic Transmission in a Circuit Underlying Escape Behavior in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Electrical stimulation of GF neurons; Direct electrical stimulation of thorax
neural_activity: Evoked muscle action potentials in DLM. CaMKII-T287D expression increases SLR, RP, and decreases FF50 in GF-DLM pathway; NMJ function remains normal. Defects localized to PSI/DLMn central synapse.
behavioral_consequence: Impaired flight/wing depression component of escape behavior.
ecological_context: Flight component of escape behavior.
summary: DLMns mediate flight escape; postsynaptic central cholinergic synapses are impaired by CaMKII-T287D during development, increasing delays and reducing high-frequency response.

paper_title: Constitutive Activation of Ca2+/Calmodulin-Dependent Protein Kinase II during Development Impairs Central Cholinergic Transmission in a Circuit Underlying Escape Behavior in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Indirect electrical stimulation of eye layers (visual pathway)
neural_activity: Transmission to GF dendrites. Signal transmission at ColA/GF synapse blocked by postsynaptic CaMKII-T287D expression in GF neurons; LLRs failed almost completely.
behavioral_consequence: Failure of long-latency escape response.
ecological_context: Visually mediated startle response.
summary: ColA interneurons convey visual startle signals to GF; CaMKII-T287D expression in GF during metamorphosis (24-84 h APF) prevents synaptic transmission at ColA/GF.

paper_title: Constitutive Activation of Ca2+/Calmodulin-Dependent Protein Kinase II during Development Impairs Central Cholinergic Transmission in a Circuit Underlying Escape Behavior in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Electrical stimulation of GF neurons
neural_activity: No defects in SLR, RP, or FF50 in flies expressing CaMKII-T287D under cha-Gal4 driver.
behavioral_consequence: Robust response to light-off stimuli (escape behavior intact).
ecological_context: Escape behavior.
summary: General cholinergic neuron expression of CaMKII-T287D did not produce GFS deficits, unlike specific GFS neuron expression.

paper_title: Distinct Roles for Two Histamine Receptors (hclA and hclB) at the Drosophila Photoreceptor Synapse
experimental_stimulus: Light flashes (10 ms, varying intensities) and light steps (halogen lamp)
neural_activity: Intracellular recordings showed hyperpolarizing 'on' and depolarizing 'off' transients. Synaptic transients abolished in hclA (ort) null mutants. In hclB (hisCl1) null mutants, ERG 'on' transients were approximately twofold enhanced, while intracellular response kinetics were slower (rise time delayed by ~10-20 ms).
summary: LMCs express hclA receptors mediating synaptic transmission from photoreceptors. hclA null mutants lack synaptic transients. hclB is not expressed in LMCs, but its mutation alters LMC response kinetics, suggesting indirect glial modulation.

paper_title: Distinct Roles for Two Histamine Receptors (hclA and hclB) at the Drosophila Photoreceptor Synapse
neural_activity: hclB gene expression localized exclusively to epithelial glial cells surrounding lamina cartridges. No direct intracellular recordings made from glia. Mutation of hclB alters LMC response kinetics and ERG transients, implying glial signaling shapes LMC response.
summary: Lamina glial cells express hclB histamine receptors. Loss of hclB affects LMC electrophysiology, suggesting a previously unrecognized role for glia in shaping postsynaptic responses at the photoreceptor synapse.

paper_title: The Nicotinic Acetylcholine Receptor Dα7 Is Required for an Escape Behavior inDrosophila
experimental_stimulus: Visual stimulus (lights-off), electrical stimulation of eyes (low strength to activate presynaptic inputs; high strength to activate GF directly)
neural_activity: In Da7 mutants, the long-latency response (indicating synaptic transmission from visual/mechanosensory inputs) is absent, while the short-latency response (direct activation) is intact. Da7 protein is enriched in the dendrites of the giant fiber (ventral lateral dendrites and posterior lateral dendrites).
behavioral_consequence: Failure to perform a visually mediated escape jump in response to lights-off stimuli.
ecological_context: Escape behavior triggered by predator approach (rapid expansion of shadow at retina); essential for survival under intense selection pressure.
techniques_used: ['Immunohistochemistry', 'Electrophysiology (extracellular recording from TTM)', 'Genetic rescue (c17-GAL4 driver)', 'Behavioral assays (jump test)']
summary: The Giant Fiber is essential for the visually mediated escape jump. Da7 nAChR is required in GF dendrites for cholinergic synaptic transmission from visual and mechanosensory inputs, as loss of Da7 abolishes the long-latency response to eye stimulation.

paper_title: The Nicotinic Acetylcholine Receptor Dα7 Is Required for an Escape Behavior inDrosophila
experimental_stimulus: Giant fiber activation via electrical stimulation of eyes
neural_activity: In gfA1 mutants (Da7 allele), EPSP amplitude at the soma is significantly reduced (1.5 mV vs 4.7 mV in wild-type) with spike jitter (whole-cell patch clamp).
behavioral_consequence: Defective activation of dorsal lateral muscles (DLM), preventing flight initiation.
ecological_context: Flight motor control; synchronized DLM activation is required for flight but not jumping.
techniques_used: ['Whole-cell patch clamp electrophysiology', 'Backfilling with Texas red dextran', 'Intracellular muscle recording']
summary: DLM motor neurons show reduced EPSP amplitude at the synapse from the peripherally synapsing interneuron (PSI) in Da7 mutants, impairing flight motor control.

paper_title: The Nicotinic Acetylcholine Receptor Dα7 Is Required for an Escape Behavior inDrosophila
experimental_stimulus: Giant fiber activation via electrical stimulation of eyes
neural_activity: TTM activation via electrical synapses with the giant fiber remains intact (short-latency response) even in severe Da7 null mutants.
behavioral_consequence: The jump muscle itself is functional, but the behavioral jump is failed due to lack of GF activation from sensory inputs.
ecological_context: Escape jump initiation (leg extension).
summary: The TTMmn is electrically coupled to the GF and remains functional in Da7 mutants, indicating the behavioral escape defect arises from the failure of sensory input transmission to the GF rather than motor output failure.

paper_title: Targeted Expression of Truncated Glued Disrupts Giant Fiber Synapse Formation in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Extracellular brain stimulation to activate GFs; Repetitive stimuli at 100, 200, 250, and 300 Hz
neural_activity: When truncated Glued (Gl D) is expressed, GF axons fail to show characteristic bends in the second thoracic neuromere and exhibit terminal swellings with large vesicles. Dye coupling (neurobiotin) from TTMn to GF is reduced (25%) or absent compared to controls (88-93%). Electrophysiological recordings show increased response latency (0.8 ms to 2.38 ms), increased refractory period (>10 ms in strong expression), and reduced following frequency at 250 Hz (10-43% vs 100% in controls). Tetanus toxin expression in GFs mimics this phenotype, suggesting chemical transmission disruption.
behavioral_consequence: Muscle responses in the tergotrochanteral muscle (TTM) show reduced following frequency during repetitive stimulation. Direct stimulation of the thoracic ganglia bypasses the GF defect, restoring short latency and high frequency muscle response, indicating the defect is specific to the GF-TTMn synapse.
summary: Expression of truncated Glued in GF neurons disrupts synapse formation, causing terminal swellings, reduced dye coupling to TTMn, and weakened synaptic transmission (increased latency, reduced following frequency) to the TTM muscle.

paper_title: Targeted Expression of Truncated Glued Disrupts Giant Fiber Synapse Formation in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Activation via GF brain stimulation; Direct thoracic stimulation of motorneurons
neural_activity: TTMn morphology (soma, dendrites, axon) appears normal in mutants. Dye coupling from TTMn to GF is reduced when Gl D is expressed in GFs. Muscle responses (TTM) fail to reach threshold during repetitive GF stimulation in mutants.
behavioral_consequence: Tergotrochanteral muscle (TTM) contraction fails or is significantly reduced during repetitive GF stimulation (following frequency drops to 10-43%). Direct stimulation of the TTMn restores normal muscle response latency and frequency, confirming the neuromuscular junction is intact.
summary: TTMn morphology is normal, but synaptic input from GF is compromised. TTM muscle responses to repetitive GF stimulation are reduced, while direct motorneuron stimulation remains normal.

paper_title: Targeted Expression of Truncated Glued Disrupts Giant Fiber Synapse Formation in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Activation via GF stimulation (disynaptic pathway GF-PSI-DLMn)
neural_activity: DLM responses generally remain normal in GF mutants. Latency remains long (~1.4 ms) indicating disynaptic pathway integrity. In strong Gl D expression (A307 line), DLM latency increases, possibly due to poison subunit expression in DLMns.
behavioral_consequence: DLM muscle following frequency is less affected than TTM muscle. In strong expression, DLM response frequency reduced (16% vs 55% in controls).
summary: The GF-PSI-DLMn pathway is generally less affected than the GF-TTMn pathway, though strong Glued disruption can impact DLMns.

paper_title: Altered Habituation of an Identified Escape Circuit inDrosophila Memory Mutants
cell_type: Giant Fiber (GF) Neurons and Afferents (Giant Fiber Pathway)
experimental_stimulus: Electrical stimulation via eye electrodes (long-latency vs. short-latency), Visual stimuli (lights-off), Air puffs, Light flashes
neural_activity: Muscle spikes recorded from TTM (jump) and DLM (flight) muscles; long-latency response (afferent pathway) shows decreased response probability with repetitive stimulation; short-latency response (direct GF activation) does not show habituation characteristics; latency shifts observed in long-latency response
behavioral_consequence: Jump-and-flight escape response; Habituation (reduction in response probability over time); Dishabituation (recovery via novel stimulus); *rut* mutations reduce habituation rate; *dnc* mutations increase habituation rate; *dnc rut* double mutants show extremely rapid habituation
ecological_context: Escape response to visual stimuli; habituation modulates sensitivity of escape response
summary: The Giant Fiber Pathway mediates a jump-and-flight escape response to visual stimuli. Habituation is localized to afferents to the giant fiber (long-latency response), characterized by decreased response probability with repetitive stimulation, whereas direct GF activation (short-latency) does not habituate. *rut* mutants show reduced habituation rates, while *dnc* mutants show increased rates, with double mutants showing extremely rapid habituation.

paper_title: Higher-order olfactory neurons in the lateral horn support odor valence and odor identity coding in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Odors
neural_activity: Odors evoke reproducible, stereotypic, and odor-specific response patterns. Response amplitude is positively correlated with innate odor preferences (valence-specific).
behavioral_consequence: Not explicitly described as a result of neuronal activation or inactivation in the provided text.
ecological_context: Implicated in determining innate behavior as a higher olfactory center.
techniques_used: ['Two-photon functional imaging', 'GRASP (Genetic Reconstitution of Synaptic Pairs)', 'Micro-lesioning']
summary: Glutamatergic LHNs encode odor identity and hedonic valence, with response amplitudes correlating with innate odor preferences. They receive excitatory input from uniglomerular PNs and inhibitory input from multiglomerular PNs.

paper_title: Higher-order olfactory neurons in the lateral horn support odor valence and odor identity coding in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Odors (inferred from context of olfactory coding)
neural_activity: Uniglomerular PNs provide major excitatory input to LHNs. Inhibitory multiglomerular PNs provide odor-specific inhibition to LHNs.
behavioral_consequence: Not explicitly described in the provided text.
ecological_context: Not explicitly described in the provided text.
summary: PNs are identified as presynaptic partners to LHNs, providing both excitatory (uniglomerular) and inhibitory (multiglomerular) inputs.

paper_title: Serotonergic modulation of visual neurons in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Bath application of serotonin (100 μM); Visual stimuli (100 ms light and dark flashes)
neural_activity: Serotonin application increased basal intracellular calcium (GCaMP6f) in a 5-HT2B-dependent manner. Serotonin modestly decreased the decay time of calcium transients following repeated dark flashes. No effect on light flash transients or peak sizes.
behavioral_consequence: None tested
ecological_context: Paper discusses potential relevance to adaptation to varying light levels throughout the day and circadian rhythms, where serotonin levels change.
summary: L2 neurons express 5-HT2B receptors. Exogenous serotonin increases basal intracellular calcium in L2 terminals (5-HT2B dependent) and modestly reduces the decay time of dark flash-induced calcium transients.

paper_title: Serotonergic modulation of visual neurons in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Bath application of serotonin (100 μM)
neural_activity: Serotonin application increased basal intracellular calcium (GCaMP6f). No serotonin receptor expression was detected in L1 neurons via RNA-Seq or RT-qPCR.
behavioral_consequence: None tested
ecological_context: Not tested
summary: L1 neurons lack detectable serotonin receptors but show increased basal intracellular calcium following serotonin application, suggesting indirect modulation.

paper_title: Serotonergic modulation of visual neurons in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Bath application of serotonin (100 μM)
neural_activity: No significant change in basal calcium (GCaMP6f) or membrane potential (ArcLight) observed upon serotonin application. T1 cells express 5-HT1A and 5-HT1B receptors.
behavioral_consequence: None tested
ecological_context: Not tested
summary: T1 neurons express 5-HT1A and 5-HT1B serotonin receptors but do not show acute changes in calcium or voltage following serotonin application.

paper_title: Serotonin transporter dependent modulation of food-seeking behavior
cell_type: CSD neurons (Contralaterally projecting serotonin-immunoreactive deutocerebral neurons)
experimental_stimulus: Food odor blends containing acetic acid (AA), ethyl acetate (EtOAc), or ethanol (EtOH) at varying concentrations
neural_activity: Not directly measured; serotonin signaling increased via expression of mutated serotonin transporter (SertDN); activity manipulated via optogenetic activation (ChR2) and inhibition (TNT)
behavioral_consequence: Increased serotonin signaling (SertDN) enhanced attraction to low AA (0.0125%) and high EtOAc (0.025%). Trh-RNAi increased attraction to 3% AA. Optogenetic activation and TNT inhibition blocked attraction to 5% EtOH. No effect on olfactory learning, memory, food consumption, or climbing
ecological_context: Food seeking behavior in response to key odorants (yeast/bacteria byproducts) in food odor blends (apple mango juice); balancing protein needs with avoidance of ethanol intoxication
techniques_used: ['Binary choice odor trap assay', 'UAS-SertDN expression', 'RNAi (Trh-RNAi)', 'Optogenetics (ChR2, TNT)', 'Immunohistochemistry', 'GRASP analysis']
summary: CSD neurons regulate attraction to key food odorants (AA, EtOAc, EtOH). Increased serotonin signaling enhances attraction to specific concentrations, while activation or inhibition of neuronal activity blocks attraction to 5% EtOH. No impact on learning or food intake.

paper_title: Serotonin transporter dependent modulation of food-seeking behavior
cell_type: LP1, IP, and SE1 neuron clusters
experimental_stimulus: Food odor blends containing acetic acid (AA) or ethyl acetate (EtOAc) at varying concentrations
neural_activity: Not measured; serotonin signaling interfered via expression of mutated transporter (SertDN)
behavioral_consequence: Expression of SertDN blocked attraction to 3% AA and increased aversion to 10% AA. No significant effect on EtOAc attraction. Behavioral outcome dominated by CSD neurons when both sets are altered
ecological_context: Regulation of odorant-guided food seeking behavior
techniques_used: ['UAS-SertDN expression', 'Sert3-Gal4 driver', 'Binary choice odor trap assay']
summary: Altered serotonin signaling in LP1, IP, and SE1 neurons blocks attraction to high AA and increases aversion. CSD neuron signaling dominates behavioral outcome when both populations are targeted.

paper_title: Serotonin transporter dependent modulation of food-seeking behavior
experimental_stimulus: Food odor blends containing acetic acid (AA) or ethyl acetate (EtOAc) at varying concentrations
neural_activity: Not directly measured; serotonin receptor function manipulated via RNAi
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of 5-HT1B reduced attraction to low AA (0.0125%) and increased attraction to high AA (3%). Knockdown of 5-HT1A or 5-HT2A had no effect on AA attraction. No effect on EtOAc attraction
ecological_context: Modulation of attraction to acetic acid, a key odorant associated with bacteria in food sources
summary: 5-HT1B receptors in GABAergic neurons positively regulate attraction to low AA and negatively regulate attraction to high AA. 5-HT1A and 5-HT2A do not regulate AA attraction in these neurons.

paper_title: Serotonin transporter dependent modulation of food-seeking behavior
experimental_stimulus: Food odor blends containing acetic acid (AA) or ethyl acetate (EtOAc) at varying concentrations
neural_activity: Not directly measured; serotonin receptor function manipulated via RNAi
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of 5-HT1A or 5-HT2A increased attraction to high AA (3%). Knockdown of 5-HT1B had no effect on AA or EtOAc attraction
ecological_context: Modulation of attraction to acetic acid during food seeking
summary: 5-HT1A and 5-HT2A receptors in projection neurons negatively regulate attraction to high concentrations of acetic acid. 5-HT1B receptors are not involved in this regulation.

paper_title: Temporally specific engagement of distinct neuronal circuits regulating olfactory habituation in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Continuous 1X 3-octanol (OCT) exposure
neural_activity: Not measured directly; functional role inferred via genetic silencing
behavioral_consequence: Silencing causes premature habituation after 1 min of exposure; silencing does not prevent habituation after 4 min; expression of Rutabaga rescues premature habituation phenotype in these neurons
ecological_context: Essential for maintaining information content to allow association with other stimuli; prevents premature habituation

paper_title: Temporally specific engagement of distinct neuronal circuits regulating olfactory habituation in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Continuous 1X OCT exposure
neural_activity: Not measured directly
behavioral_consequence: Silencing has no significant effect on habituation latency or establishment
ecological_context: Not implicated in habituation latency or establishment under tested conditions

paper_title: Temporally specific engagement of distinct neuronal circuits regulating olfactory habituation in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Continuous 1X OCT exposure
neural_activity: Not measured directly
behavioral_consequence: Silencing causes premature habituation (1 min) and blocks dishabituation; silencing blocks habituation after 4 min (response remains naive); activation via TRPA1 does not alter response
ecological_context: Essential for maintaining stimulus value during latency; required for associative learning capacity

paper_title: Temporally specific engagement of distinct neuronal circuits regulating olfactory habituation in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Continuous 1X OCT exposure
neural_activity: Not measured directly
behavioral_consequence: Silencing blocks habituation after 4 min; activation via TRPA1 for 4 min without odor is sufficient to cause habituation
ecological_context: Necessary and sufficient for establishing habituation (devaluing stimulus) after prolonged exposure

paper_title: Temporally specific engagement of distinct neuronal circuits regulating olfactory habituation in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Continuous 1X OCT exposure
neural_activity: Not measured directly
behavioral_consequence: Ablation/silencing causes premature habituation (1 min) and blocks dishabituation; silencing does not block habituation after 4 min
ecological_context: Essential for response maintenance during latency and dishabituation; prevents premature habituation

paper_title: Temporally specific engagement of distinct neuronal circuits regulating olfactory habituation in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Continuous 1X OCT exposure
neural_activity: Not measured directly
behavioral_consequence: Silencing causes premature habituation (1 min) and blocks dishabituation
ecological_context: Indispensable for habituation latency and normal dishabituation

paper_title: Temporally specific engagement of distinct neuronal circuits regulating olfactory habituation in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Continuous 1X OCT exposure
neural_activity: Not measured directly
behavioral_consequence: Silencing causes premature habituation (1 min) but does not affect dishabituation
ecological_context: Required for stimulus value maintenance (latency) but not dishabituation

paper_title: Temporally specific engagement of distinct neuronal circuits regulating olfactory habituation in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Continuous 1X OCT exposure
neural_activity: Not measured directly
behavioral_consequence: Silencing has no effect on habituation or latency
ecological_context: Not implicated in habituation latency or dishabituation

paper_title: Temporally specific engagement of distinct neuronal circuits regulating olfactory habituation in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Continuous 1X OCT exposure
neural_activity: Not measured directly
behavioral_consequence: Silencing does not facilitate habituation (shorten latency)
ecological_context: Not the primary driver of habituation latency phenotype associated with GH146 driver

paper_title: Temporally specific engagement of distinct neuronal circuits regulating olfactory habituation in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: 1X vs 0.1X OCT exposure
neural_activity: Not measured directly
behavioral_consequence: Weaker stimulus (0.1X) leads to faster habituation; decrement not attributed to OSN adaptation
ecological_context: Stimulus strength determines latency; response decrement occurs downstream of OSNs

paper_title: A Single Pair of Serotonergic Neurons Counteracts Serotonergic Inhibition of Ethanol Attraction in Drosophila
cell_type: IP and LP1 serotonergic neurons (Sert3-Gal4 subset)
experimental_stimulus: Binary choice assay with mango-apple juice + 5% ethanol vs. mango-apple juice; Blue light activation (optogenetics)
neural_activity: Prolonged serotonin signaling in the synaptic cleft via UAS-SertDN expression (confirmed by reduced serotonin staining in soma); Optogenetic activation using ChR2 and blue light
behavioral_consequence: Expression of UAS-SertDN significantly reduced olfactory attraction to ethanol (Preference Index dropped); Optogenetic activation abolished preference for ethanol-containing food
ecological_context: Innate olfactory attraction to ethanol-containing food sources (fermenting fruit)
summary: These serotonergic neurons inhibit olfactory attraction to ethanol; prolonged serotonin signaling or optogenetic activation abolishes attraction to ethanol-containing food.

paper_title: A Single Pair of Serotonergic Neurons Counteracts Serotonergic Inhibition of Ethanol Attraction in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Binary choice assay with ethanol (5% and 10%); Combined with Sert3-Gal4 driver
neural_activity: Prolonged serotonin signaling in the synaptic cleft via UAS-SertDN expression
behavioral_consequence: Expression alone did not alter 5% ethanol preference; when combined with Sert3-Gal4 expression, it restored normal attraction levels (counteracted inhibition); Increased attraction to 10% ethanol observed
ecological_context: Modulation of innate olfactory attraction to ethanol-containing food sources
summary: These neurons counteract the inhibition of ethanol attraction caused by other serotonergic neurons; prolonged signaling strengthens olfactory information and promotes approach behavior.

paper_title: A Single Pair of Serotonergic Neurons Counteracts Serotonergic Inhibition of Ethanol Attraction in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Binary choice assay with mango-apple juice + 5% ethanol
neural_activity: Prolonged serotonin signaling in the synaptic cleft via UAS-SertDN expression in ~83% of serotonergic neurons
behavioral_consequence: Did not alter olfactory attraction to ethanol (normal preference observed), indicating compensatory mechanisms
ecological_context: Innate olfactory attraction to ethanol-containing food sources
summary: Broad manipulation of serotonergic neurons shows no net behavioral change, suggesting compensatory mechanisms on the network level.

paper_title: Decoding odor quality and intensity in the Drosophila brain
cell_type: Inhibitory Projection Neurons (iPNs) projecting to LH-PM (Posterior-Medial)
experimental_stimulus: Attractive odors (acetoin acetate, balsamic vinegar, 1-octen-3-ol) at median and high concentrations; repellent odors (benzaldehyde, acetophenone)
neural_activity: Ca2+ signals in the LH-PM domain via G-CaMP3.0 imaging; activity positively correlated with positive hedonic valence and independent of odor concentration
behavioral_consequence: Silencing GABA synthesis in MZ699+ iPNs (including this group) via GAD1-RNAi severely diminished flies' attraction behavior in T-maze assays; flies showed neutral or aversive responses to attractive odors
ecological_context: Processing of attractive byproducts of the yeast fermentation process (acetoin acetate) and attractive natural odor mixtures (balsamic vinegar); mediating innate odor-guided attraction
techniques_used: ['Calcium imaging (G-CaMP3.0)', 'RNAi silencing (GAD1-RNAi)', 'T-maze behavioral assay', 'Transection (2-photon laser)', 'PA-GFP tracing']
summary: iPNs projecting to the LH-PM domain encode positive hedonic valence (attraction) independent of intensity; silencing GABA synthesis in these neurons diminishes attraction behavior.

paper_title: Decoding odor quality and intensity in the Drosophila brain
cell_type: Inhibitory Projection Neurons (iPNs) projecting to LH-AM (Anterior-Medial)
experimental_stimulus: Odorants at various concentrations
neural_activity: Ca2+ signals in the LH-AM domain via G-CaMP3.0 imaging; activity significantly correlated with odor intensity (concentration)
behavioral_consequence: Silencing GABA synthesis in MZ699+ iPNs (including this group) via GAD1-RNAi diminished attraction behavior
ecological_context: Coding of odor intensity information within the lateral horn
techniques_used: ['Calcium imaging (G-CaMP3.0)', 'Transection (2-photon laser)', 'PA-GFP tracing', 'Cluster analysis of morphological similarity']
summary: iPNs projecting to the LH-AM domain encode odor intensity; they are part of the MZ699+ population whose silencing reduces attraction behavior.

paper_title: Decoding odor quality and intensity in the Drosophila brain
experimental_stimulus: Repellent odors (benzaldehyde, 1-octen-3-ol, acetophenone) at median and high concentrations
neural_activity: Ca2+ signals in the LH-AL domain; activity negatively correlated with hedonic valence and correlated with odor intensity
behavioral_consequence: Not explicitly tested; activity in LH-AL was significantly abolished by plF transection
ecological_context: Coding of aversive odors (repellents) in the lateral horn
summary: vlPr neurons project to the LH-AL domain and code for aversive odors (negative valence) and intensity; their activity is eliminated by plF transection.

paper_title: Farnesol-Detecting Olfactory Neurons in Drosophila
cell_type: Or83c-expressing olfactory sensory neurons (ai2a neurons in intermediate sensilla)
experimental_stimulus: Farnesol (dilutions 1:10,000 to undiluted), citrus fruit peels (grapefruit, orange), cVA
neural_activity: Robust activation (peaking at ~200 spikes/s) to farnesol; high sensitivity (down to 1:10,000 dilution); response requires Or83c, Orco, and Snmp1; Snmp1 mutants show slower deactivation kinetics
behavioral_consequence: Mediates attraction to farnesol in four-quadrant olfactometer and two-choice trap assay; Or83c mutants are defective in this attraction
ecological_context: Farnesol is produced by ripe citrus fruit peels; guides long-range attraction to food sources (yeast on fruit); not a fly pheromone (no volatiles from live flies activated neurons)
techniques_used: ['Single-sensillum electrophysiology', 'Electroantennography', 'Four-quadrant olfactometer', 'Olfactory trap assay', 'Genetic mutants and misexpression']
summary: Or83c-expressing ai2a neurons are narrowly tuned to farnesol, mediating attraction to citrus fruit peels, and require Snmp1 for normal response kinetics.

paper_title: Farnesol-Detecting Olfactory Neurons in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Farnesol (via Or83c input)
neural_activity: Axons target a region of the lateral horn that overlaps with projection neurons relaying pheromone information (VA1lm, VL2a)
behavioral_consequence: Not directly tested behaviorally; circuit maps farnesol signals to regions associated with pheromone perception
ecological_context: Relays food-derived farnesol signals to higher brain centers
techniques_used: ['Mosaic analysis with a repressible cell marker (MARCM)', 'Confocal microscopy', 'Clustering analysis of axon innervation']
summary: DC3 projection neurons relay Or83c signals to a lateral horn region previously implicated in pheromone perception.

paper_title: Spatial representation of odorant valence in an insect brain.
experimental_stimulus: 110 odorants (6 most attractive, 6 most aversive) at weak (10^-1) and strong (10^-3) concentrations
neural_activity: Functional calcium imaging showed multiglomerular activation; no significant separation of attractive and aversive odorants based on OSN activation patterns (PCA inconclusive)
