neural_activity: No increase in GCaMP3.0 fluorescence during sucrose stimulation in starved flies
behavioral_consequence: Selective laser illumination failed to induce proboscis extension or pump movement; laser ablation did not affect sucrose-induced proboscis extension response
summary: LPE neurons are adjacent to Fdg-neurons but show no calcium response to sucrose and are not required for feeding behavior.

paper_title: A single pair of interneurons commands the Drosophila feeding motor program
experimental_stimulus: Flip-out Gal80 screening for TrpA1-induced feeding
neural_activity: Not tested
behavioral_consequence: No statistically significant correlation between GFP expression in ALLH neurons and proboscis extension frequency; did not exhibit the skew seen in Fdg-neuron positive flies
summary: ALLH neurons are within the NP883 expression pattern but were not identified as driving the feeding behavior in the behavioral screen.

paper_title: The GABAA Receptor RDL Suppresses the Conditioned Stimulus Pathway for Olfactory Learning
cell_type: Mushroom Body (MB) intrinsic neurons (targeted by c772-Gal4 and c739-Gal4)
experimental_stimulus: Olfactory stimuli (benzaldehyde, 3-octanol) paired with unconditioned stimuli: electric shock (aversive) or sucrose (appetitive)
neural_activity: Not directly measured in this study (prior imaging cited shows RDL modulates calcium responses to odor but not shock); behavioral modulation observed
behavioral_consequence: RDL knockdown enhances aversive and appetitive olfactory learning; RDL overexpression impairs both. dDA1 overexpression enhances aversive learning. Combined RDL knockdown/dDA1 overexpression shows no synergistic enhancement. RDL knockdown fails to enhance learning in rut or NF1 mutants.
ecological_context: Not explicitly discussed in this study
summary: Manipulation of RDL and dDA1 expression levels in Mushroom Body neurons alters olfactory learning performance (RDL knockdown enhances learning, overexpression impairs it; dDA1 overexpression enhances aversive learning).

paper_title: Pigment Dispersing Factor-Dependent and -Independent Circadian Locomotor Behavioral Rhythms
cell_type: Lateral Ventral Neurons (LNv - lLNv and sLNv)
experimental_stimulus: Targeted expression of voltage-gated sodium channel NaChBac using pdf-GAL4 driver; Constant Darkness (DD) for 15 days
neural_activity: Whole-cell patch-clamp recording showed large amplitude spontaneous depolarizations (144–153 mV) at low frequency (0.03 Hz) in NaChBac-expressing lLNv, compared to normal firing (~2.3 Hz) in controls. Electrical properties did not recover to control values after 15 days in DD
behavioral_consequence: Initial arrhythmicity (days 1–5 DD) followed by emergence of two concurrent locomotor activity bouts (~22.5 h and ~25 h periods) in ~60% of flies. In pdf01 null background, only a short-period rhythm emerged
ecological_context: Activity/rest rhythm in constant darkness (absence of environmental time cues); wild-type flies normally show dawn and dusk peaks under light/dark regimens
summary: LNv hyperexcitation via NaChBac causes initial arrhythmia followed by polyrhythmic locomotor behavior with two concurrent periods, regulated by circuit-wide coordination.

paper_title: Pigment Dispersing Factor-Dependent and -Independent Circadian Locomotor Behavioral Rhythms
experimental_stimulus: Circadian circuit manipulation via LNv NaChBac expression; Constant Darkness (DD)
neural_activity: Immunocytochemistry for PER protein revealed two peaks of PER oscillation in DN1 and DN2 subgroups. One peak coincided with PDF-positive sLNv (short-period bout), the other with LNd (long-period bout)
behavioral_consequence: PER peaks in DN1 and DN2 coincided with the two activity bouts in NaChBac flies, whereas controls showed synchronized oscillations
ecological_context: Circadian pacemaker circuit organization in constant darkness
summary: DN1 and DN2 exhibit two peaks of PER oscillation in electrically manipulated flies, with peaks coinciding with the two concurrent activity bouts.

paper_title: Pigment Dispersing Factor-Dependent and -Independent Circadian Locomotor Behavioral Rhythms
experimental_stimulus: Circadian circuit manipulation via LNv NaChBac expression; Constant Darkness (DD)
neural_activity: Immunocytochemistry for PER protein showed a single peak in LNd that coincided with the long-period activity bout
behavioral_consequence: The long-period circadian activity component is PDF-dependent; LNd PER peak aligns with this long-period bout
ecological_context: Circadian pacemaker circuit organization in constant darkness
summary: LNd exhibits a single PER peak coinciding with the long-period activity bout, which is dependent on PDF.

paper_title: Pigment Dispersing Factor-Dependent and -Independent Circadian Locomotor Behavioral Rhythms
experimental_stimulus: Circadian circuit manipulation via LNv NaChBac expression; Constant Darkness (DD)
neural_activity: PER levels were out-of-phase compared to the manipulated PDF-positive sLNv subgroups
behavioral_consequence: Associated with the short-period activity bout component
ecological_context: Circadian pacemaker circuit organization in constant darkness
summary: Fifth sLNv PER peak is out-of-phase with manipulated sLNv and coincides with activity bouts in electrically manipulated flies.

paper_title: Electrical Hyperexcitation of Lateral Ventral Pacemaker Neurons Desynchronizes Downstream Circadian Oscillators in the Fly Circadian Circuit and Induces Multiple Behavioral Periods
experimental_stimulus: Transgenic expression of NaChBac (bacterial sodium channel) to induce electrical hyperexcitability; coexpression with Kir2.1 (inward-rectifier potassium channel)
neural_activity: Electrical hyperexcitability confirmed in muscle fibers via 2-electrode voltage clamp; Constitutively high PDF immunoreactivity in dorsomedial terminals (abolished cycling); Damped PDP1 oscillation amplitude in sLNV cell bodies
behavioral_consequence: Induced complex free-running locomotor activity rhythms with multiple superimposed periods (~22h and ~25.5h); Coexpression with Kir2.1 suppressed arrhythmicity induced by Kir2.1 alone
techniques_used: ['Transgenic expression (UAS-GAL4)', '2-electrode voltage clamp (muscle)', 'Immunocytochemistry (PER, PDP1, PDF)', 'Locomotor activity monitoring']
summary: LNVs expressing NaChBac exhibit electrical hyperexcitability, abolished PDF cycling, and induce complex behavioral rhythms with multiple periods.

paper_title: Electrical Hyperexcitation of Lateral Ventral Pacemaker Neurons Desynchronizes Downstream Circadian Oscillators in the Fly Circadian Circuit and Induces Multiple Behavioral Periods
experimental_stimulus: Indirect manipulation via electrical hyperexcitation of LNVs using NaChBac
neural_activity: DN1s showed earlier PDP1 peak (CT14 vs CT22 on day 2) and damped amplitude; DN2s showed advanced PDP1 peak (CT6 vs CT10-14 on day 5) and PER peak at CT3 (12h out-of-phase)
behavioral_consequence: Phase shifts in clock proteins correlate with multiple behavioral periods; DN2s suggested to drive short-period (~22h) component of complex rhythmicity
summary: Downstream dorsal neurons desynchronize from controls (DN1 peak shift, DN2 phase advance), correlating with complex locomotor rhythms.

paper_title: Functional Dissection of a Neuronal Network Required for Cuticle Tanning and Wing Expansion in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Genetic suppression of excitability (EKO), enhancement (NaChBac), PKA inhibition, and synaptic suppression (TNT, Shi ts1)
neural_activity: Suppression of excitability blocks bursicon release. Enhancement of excitability (NaChBac) has no effect on release or tanning, suggesting neurons are electrically quiescent until stimulated.
behavioral_consequence: Required for release of bursicon into hemolymph. Necessary for wing expansion and cuticle tanning after eclosion.
ecological_context: Post-eclosion adult maturation; failure results in 'juvenile' features (unexpanded wings, soft unpigmented cuticle).
techniques_used: ['Gal4/UAS expression', 'Confocal immunohistochemistry', 'Hemolymph Western blot', 'Behavioral wing expansion scoring', 'Cuticle tanning grayscale analysis']
summary: NCCAP-c929 neurons (specifically 14 abdominal ganglion neurons) express bursicon and release it into the hemolymph. They require excitability for release but are silent until stimulated, as enhancing their activity does not block release, whereas suppressing it does.

paper_title: Functional Dissection of a Neuronal Network Required for Cuticle Tanning and Wing Expansion in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Genetic suppression of excitability (EKO), synaptic transmission (TNT, Shi ts1), PKA activity (PKA inh), and enhancement of excitability (NaChBac)
neural_activity: PKA activity and synaptic transmission are required for bursicon regulation. Enhancement of excitability (NaChBac) depletes bursicon from central neuronal processes and blocks secretion.
behavioral_consequence: Regulates bursicon secretion from NCCAP-c929. Required for normal wing expansion and cuticle tanning.
ecological_context: Post-eclosion adult maturation; regulates the timing and release of the hormone required for wing expansion and tanning.
techniques_used: ['Gal4/UAS expression', 'Gal80 inhibition to isolate NCCAP-R', 'Confocal immunohistochemistry', 'Hemolymph Western blot', 'Behavioral wing expansion scoring']
summary: NCCAP-R neurons, defined as CCAP-expressing neurons outside the c929-Gal4 pattern, regulate bursicon secretion from NCCAP-c929 neurons. They require PKA activity and synaptic transmission, and their hyperexcitation depletes bursicon from CNS processes.

paper_title: Functional Dissection of a Neuronal Network Required for Cuticle Tanning and Wing Expansion in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Ablation (reaper), suppression of excitability (Shaw, EKO, Kir2.1), synaptic suppression (TNT, Shi ts1), and PKA inhibition
neural_activity: Membrane excitability, synaptic transmission, and PKA activity are required for bursicon release.
behavioral_consequence: Ablation or suppression results in unexpanded wings and unpigmented cuticles. Hemolymph lacks bursicon immunoreactivity.
ecological_context: Early adult maturation following eclosion; essential for transitioning from pupal to adult form.
techniques_used: ['Genetic ablation', 'Electroretinography (ERG) for NaChBac validation', 'Confocal immunohistochemistry', 'Behavioral wing expansion scoring', 'Cuticle tanning grayscale analysis']
summary: CCAP-expressing neurons (NCCAP) as a whole are required for bursicon release into the hemolymph, wing expansion, and cuticle tanning. Excitability, synaptic transmission, and PKA activity within these neurons are necessary determinants of secretion.

paper_title: Ir56d-dependent fatty acid responses in Drosophila uncover taste discrimination between different classes of fatty acids
experimental_stimulus: Short-chain (4C, 5C), medium-chain (6C, 7C, 8C), and long-chain (9C, 10C) fatty acids, 10 mM sucrose, water
neural_activity: In vivo Ca2+ imaging showed responsiveness to sucrose and all fatty acids in heterozygotes. Loss of IR56d disrupted Ca2+ responses specifically to medium-chain fatty acids (6C–8C), while responses to short/long-chain fatty acids and sucrose remained intact.
behavioral_consequence: Genetic silencing (Tetanus Toxin) or CRISPR/Cas9 deletion of IR56d significantly reduced Proboscis Extension Response (PER) to medium-chain fatty acids. No significant effect on PER to short-chain, long-chain fatty acids, or sucrose.
ecological_context: Fatty acids are natural by-products of yeast fermentation and abundance in fruit declines after ripening. Discrimination is likely important for determining fruit ripeness, degree of fermentation, and palatability of potential food sources/oviposition sites.
techniques_used: ['Proboscis Extension Response (PER) assay', 'In vivo Ca2+ imaging (GCaMP6.0)', 'Genetic silencing (Tetanus Toxin)', 'CRISPR/Cas9 knockout', 'Rescue experiments']
summary: IR56d-expressing neurons are required for taste responses to medium-chain fatty acids but dispensable for short- or long-chain fatty acids and sucrose.

paper_title: Ir56d-dependent fatty acid responses in Drosophila uncover taste discrimination between different classes of fatty acids
experimental_stimulus: 10 mM sucrose, 1% saturated fatty acids (4C–10C)
neural_activity: Co-expressed with IR56d in a subset of sweet-sensing neurons. Genetic silencing used to assess function.
behavioral_consequence: Genetic silencing significantly reduced PER to sucrose and medium-chain fatty acids (6C–8C). No significant difference in PER to short-chain (4C, 5C) or long-chain (9C, 10C) fatty acids compared to controls.
ecological_context: Sweet-sensing neurons promote feeding behavior on appetitive substances.
summary: GR64f-expressing neurons are necessary for PER to sucrose and medium-chain fatty acids, but dispensable for short- and long-chain fatty acids.

paper_title: Ir56d-dependent fatty acid responses in Drosophila uncover taste discrimination between different classes of fatty acids
experimental_stimulus: Water, 10 mM sucrose, 1% fatty acids (4C–10C)
neural_activity: In vivo Ca2+ imaging (intersectional strategy) showed robust response to hexanoic acid (6C) but not sucrose. Responsive to short-, medium-, and long-chain fatty acids. Response to short-chain fatty acids was lower than to medium- and long-chain fatty acids. No difference in activity between medium- and long-chain fatty acid presentation.
behavioral_consequence: Not explicitly tested in isolation for behavioral consequences in this study.
ecological_context: Differential response between anterior and posterior populations is discussed as a possible mechanism for discrimination between fatty acid classes.
techniques_used: ['In vivo Ca2+ imaging (GCaMP6.0)', 'Intersectional genetic labeling (GR64f-LexA > LexAop-GAL80)']
summary: Anterior IR56d+ taste neurons respond to fatty acids but not sucrose, with lower activity to short-chain fatty acids compared to medium- and long-chain fatty acids.

paper_title: Ir56d-dependent fatty acid responses in Drosophila uncover taste discrimination between different classes of fatty acids
experimental_stimulus: 10 mM sucrose, 1% fatty acids (4C–10C)
neural_activity: Functionally assessed via mutants; broadly expressed receptors implicated in taste.
behavioral_consequence: IR76b1 and IR25a1 mutants showed significantly reduced PER to medium-chain fatty acids. PER to short- and long-chain fatty acids was also significantly reduced in both mutants. PER to sucrose was normal.
ecological_context: Required for fatty acid taste perception across all chain lengths tested.
summary: IR76b and IR25a are required for taste responses to short-, medium-, and long-chain fatty acids, but not for sucrose.

paper_title: An expression atlas of variant ionotropic glutamate receptors identifies a molecular basis of carbonation sensing
experimental_stimulus: carbonated solutions (aqueous CO2), hexanoic acid (fatty acids), sugars, organic acids, salts, buffers of different pH
neural_activity: Calcium imaging (GCaMP3) in subesophageal zone (SEZ) showed strong response to carbonation in AMS1 projection. No response to sugars or other acids. Response to fatty acids abolished in Ir56d mutants.
behavioral_consequence: Two-choice positional preference for carbonated solutions abolished in Ir56d mutants. Carbonation does not robustly trigger proboscis extension reflex (PER). Optogenetic activation of taste peg neurons (E409-Gal4) does not induce PER. Carbonation does not enhance food ingestion volume in Expresso assay.
ecological_context: Carbonation is a product of microbial fermentation and is a modestly attractive stimulus for Drosophila.
techniques_used: ['Calcium imaging (GCaMP3)', 'CRISPR/Cas9 mutagenesis', 'Two-choice positional preference assay', 'Proboscis extension reflex (PER) assay', 'Optogenetics (CsChrimson)', 'Expresso food ingestion assay']
summary: IR56d taste peg neurons respond specifically to carbonation and fatty acids, mediating attraction to carbonated solutions but not triggering PER or increasing feeding volume.

paper_title: An expression atlas of variant ionotropic glutamate receptors identifies a molecular basis of carbonation sensing
cell_type: IR56d taste bristle neurons (labellar and leg)
experimental_stimulus: sucrose and other sugars, hexanoic acid (fatty acids), glycerol, carbonated solutions
neural_activity: Calcium imaging (GCaMP3) in SEZ (PMS4 projection) showed strong response to sugars. Response to hexanoic acid present. Variable response to carbonation. Leg neurons also respond to sucrose but not carbonation.
behavioral_consequence: Hexanoic acid triggers PER, which is abolished in Ir56d mutants. Ir56d-Gal4 activation induces PER. Leg stimulation with carbonation does not evoke PER.
ecological_context: Detection of nutritive content (sugars) and fatty acids, which are attractive taste stimuli.
summary: IR56d taste bristle neurons (including leg neurons) respond to sugars and fatty acids, mediating PER to fatty acids, but do not respond to carbonation or trigger PER to carbonation.

paper_title: Internal amino acid state modulates yeast taste neurons to support protein homeostasis in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: 10% yeast liquid on labellum
neural_activity: Strong calcium responses in AMS1 and PMS4 regions of SEZ (two-photon imaging); responses enhanced after 3-10 days of amino acid deprivation
behavioral_consequence: Acute silencing drastically reduced yeast preference index and total yeast sips; silencing did not affect sucrose feeding
ecological_context: Yeast is the primary source of dietary protein and amino acids for Drosophila melanogaster; supports homeostatic regulation of protein intake
summary: Ir76b-expressing GRNs on the proboscis respond to yeast taste and are required for yeast intake, with their sensory gain enhanced by amino acid deprivation.

paper_title: Internal amino acid state modulates yeast taste neurons to support protein homeostasis in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Yeast taste (liquid on labellum)
neural_activity: Calcium response in AMS1 region of SEZ (two-photon imaging)
behavioral_consequence: Closed-loop optogenetic silencing after feeding initiation reduced the number of sips per feeding burst (sustaining feeding); total feeding unchanged if sensillar GRNs intact
ecological_context: Ensures sufficient uptake of critical nutrients (protein) through flexible behavioral programs
summary: Taste peg GRNs are required to sustain yeast feeding bursts after initiation, mediating the duration of feeding episodes.

paper_title: Internal amino acid state modulates yeast taste neurons to support protein homeostasis in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Yeast presented to labellum
neural_activity: Calcium response in PMS4 region of SEZ (two-photon imaging)
behavioral_consequence: Silencing or mutation (Poxn loss-of-function) abolished Proboscis Extension Response (PER); essential for feeding initiation
ecological_context: Detection of food source to trigger feeding behavior
summary: Sensillar GRNs mediate the initiation of yeast feeding via the Proboscis Extension Response.

paper_title: Internal amino acid state modulates yeast taste neurons to support protein homeostasis in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Yeast taste
neural_activity: Not explicitly imaged for yeast response in detail
behavioral_consequence: Silencing Ir67c-GAL4 or Gr9a-GAL4 neurons did not reduce yeast feeding compared to controls
ecological_context: Act redundantly with other GRNs for total yeast intake
summary: Pharyngeal GRNs tested are not essential for total yeast feeding, suggesting redundancy with proboscis GRNs.

paper_title: Internal amino acid state modulates yeast taste neurons to support protein homeostasis in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Not directly tested (silencing removed from Ir76b line)
neural_activity: N/A
behavioral_consequence: Silencing Ir76b-GAL4 with tshirt-GAL80 (removing tarsal/wing GRNs) did not suppress yeast feeding phenotype
ecological_context: Dispensable for yeast feeding behavior
summary: Leg GRNs expressing Ir76b are dispensable for yeast feeding, as proboscis GRNs suffice.

paper_title: Internal amino acid state modulates yeast taste neurons to support protein homeostasis in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Sucrose
neural_activity: Calcium response to sucrose; response not enhanced by yeast deprivation
behavioral_consequence: Used as control; demonstrates specificity of amino acid deprivation modulation to yeast GRNs
ecological_context: Confirms nutrient-specific sensory gain modulation distinct from starvation effects
summary: Sweet-sensing GRNs do not show enhanced response to yeast deprivation, indicating specificity of AA state modulation to yeast GRNs.

paper_title: Functional dissociation in sweet taste receptor neurons between and within taste organs of Drosophila
cell_type: Ascending tarsal sweet taste receptor neurons (atGRNs)
experimental_stimulus: Sucrose (tarsal contact in PER assay; arena presence in preference assay)
neural_activity: Axonal projections terminate directly in the brain (GNG) via cervical connective; cell bodies in fifth tarsal segment (Immunohistochemistry)
behavioral_consequence: Silencing impairs sucrose preference, tarsal proboscis extension reflex (PER), and appetitive olfactory memory; does not abolish sugar-induced locomotion suppression
ecological_context: Legs are typically the first appendage to contact food; atGRNs promote feeding initiation
techniques_used: ['Genetic silencing (UAS-Kir2.1)', 'Immunohistochemistry (Confocal microscopy)', 'Sugar preference assay', 'Proboscis extension reflex (PER)', 'Olfactory memory assay']
summary: atGRNs project to the GNG and are critical for feeding initiation, sugar choice, and appetitive memory, but not locomotion suppression.

paper_title: Functional dissociation in sweet taste receptor neurons between and within taste organs of Drosophila
cell_type: Segmental tarsal sweet taste receptor neurons (stGRNs)
experimental_stimulus: Sucrose (tarsal contact in PER assay; arena presence in preference assay)
neural_activity: Axonal projections terminate in the ventral nerve cord (VNC)/thoracic ganglia; do not project directly to GNG (Immunohistochemistry)
behavioral_consequence: Silencing abolishes sugar-induced suppression of turning/locomotion; modest effect on preference; no effect on PER or appetitive memory
ecological_context: Inhibition of locomotion upon food encounter allows feeding to proceed; suppresses competing behaviors
techniques_used: ['Genetic silencing (UAS-Kir2.1)', 'Immunohistochemistry (Confocal microscopy)', 'Sugar preference assay', 'Locomotion quantification (Video tracking)']
summary: stGRNs project to the VNC and are required for sugar-dependent locomotion suppression to facilitate feeding.

paper_title: Functional dissociation in sweet taste receptor neurons between and within taste organs of Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Sucrose (labellar stimulation in PER assay; arena presence)
neural_activity: Projections to anterior gnathal ganglia (GNG) (Immunohistochemistry)
behavioral_consequence: Silencing causes modest preference impairment; does not abolish locomotion suppression or PER on tarsal stimulation
ecological_context: Involved in later stages of feeding rather than initial sugar choice; legs are the first appendage to contact food
summary: Labellar GRNs contribute modestly to sugar preference and PER but are not critical for initial sugar choice compared to leg GRNs.

paper_title: Functional dissociation in sweet taste receptor neurons between and within taste organs of Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Sucrose (arena presence)
neural_activity: Projections to GNG (Immunohistochemistry)
behavioral_consequence: Silencing shows normal sugar preference; not critical for sugar choice
ecological_context: Not critical for early appetitive responses; distinct from leg GRNs
summary: Pharyngeal/LSO GRNs show normal preference when silenced, indicating they are not critical for immediate sugar choice.

paper_title: Serotonin acts through multiple cellular targets during an olfactory critical period
cell_type: CSDns (Contralaterally projecting Serotonin immunoreactive Deuterocerebral neurons)
experimental_stimulus: Chronic 5% CO2 exposure during the critical period (4-day old pupae to day 5 post-eclosion)
neural_activity: Release of serotonin into the antennal lobe (AL) via Vmat-dependent transport
behavioral_consequence: Blocking 5-HT release via Vmat-RNAi prevents V-glomerulus volume increase (structural plasticity). Overexpression or knockdown of 5-HT1B autoreceptors on CSDns also prevents CPP.
ecological_context: CO2 sensing circuit identified as behaviorally relevant; modulation occurs during early postnatal development (critical period)
summary: CSDns release serotonin required for olfactory critical period plasticity. Serotonin levels are autoregulated by 5-HT1B receptors on CSDns; disruption of this regulation blocks V-glomerulus volume expansion in response to CO2.

paper_title: Serotonin acts through multiple cellular targets during an olfactory critical period
cell_type: R70A09 Local Interneurons (GABAergic, overlap with LN1)
experimental_stimulus: Chronic 5% CO2 exposure during the critical period
neural_activity: Expression of 5-HT7 receptors; activation involves cAMP signaling
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of 5-HT7 receptors or overexpression of cAMP phosphodiesterase (dunce) in these LNs prevents V-glomerulus volume increase
ecological_context: Part of the inhibitory local network in the antennal lobe modulating olfactory processing during development
summary: R70A09 GABAergic LNs express 5-HT7 receptors and are required for CPP. 5-HT7 signaling and subsequent cAMP elevation in these neurons facilitate structural plasticity in the V-glomerulus.

paper_title: Serotonin acts through multiple cellular targets during an olfactory critical period
experimental_stimulus: Chronic 5% CO2 exposure during the critical period
neural_activity: Expression of 5-HT2B receptors (peaks at 48 hours post-eclosion) and GABA receptors (GABAB/GABAA)
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of 5-HT2B receptors in CO2-sensing OSNs (Gr63a-Gal4) or all OSNs (Peb-Gal4) prevents V-glomerulus volume increase. Knockdown of GABAB receptors in all OSNs also blocks plasticity
ecological_context: Direct modulation of primary sensory afferents by serotonin during the critical period
summary: 5-HT2B receptors on OSNs are essential for structural plasticity. 5-HT2B expression peaks at the end of the critical period. GABAergic inhibition via GABAB receptors in OSNs is also required for CPP.

paper_title: Internal state affects local neuron function in an early sensory processing center to shape olfactory behavior in Drosophila larvae
experimental_stimulus: Red light (optogenetic activation), Odors (4-hexen-3-one, menthol), Starvation (2h dH2O), Insulin signaling manipulation (InR RNAi)
neural_activity: Calcium imaging (GCaMP6m) showed strong response to high concentration 4-hexen-3-one; weak response to low concentration 4-hexen-3-one and menthol. Immunocytochemistry confirmed InR expression, which was lower in starved larvae.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation triggers head sweeps. Starvation reduces head sweep magnitude. Reducing GABA impairs attraction to low concentration odor and aversion to menthol. Reducing InR reduces head sweep magnitude and results in straighter tracks.
ecological_context: Starved larvae have fewer head sweeps and travel straighter tracks to search for food (confirmed in this study).
summary: Keystone-LN activation triggers head sweeps, and its function is modulated by starvation and insulin signaling to adjust head sweep magnitude and track straightness.

paper_title: Internal state affects local neuron function in an early sensory processing center to shape olfactory behavior in Drosophila larvae
experimental_stimulus: Red light (optogenetic activation)
neural_activity: Not measured
behavioral_consequence: Activation did not increase probability of head sweeps
ecological_context: Not discussed
summary: Optogenetic activation of Orco-positive OSNs did not increase head sweep probability.

paper_title: Internal state affects local neuron function in an early sensory processing center to shape olfactory behavior in Drosophila larvae
experimental_stimulus: Red light (optogenetic activation)
neural_activity: Not measured
behavioral_consequence: Activation did not increase probability of head sweeps
ecological_context: Not discussed
summary: Optogenetic activation of GH146-expressing PNs did not increase head sweep probability.

paper_title: Internal state affects local neuron function in an early sensory processing center to shape olfactory behavior in Drosophila larvae
experimental_stimulus: Red light (optogenetic activation)
neural_activity: Not measured
behavioral_consequence: Activation did not increase probability of head sweeps
ecological_context: Not discussed
summary: Optogenetic activation of GH298-expressing LNs did not increase head sweep probability.

paper_title: Odor-Induced Multi-Level Inhibitory Maps in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Various odorants (ethyl-3-hydroxybutyrate, benzaldehyde, acetic acid, methyl hexanoate, etc.) applied via airflow
neural_activity: Odor-evoked Cl- influx (indicating inhibition) and Ca2+ influx (indicating excitation) measured in dendrites (antenna) and axonal termini (antennal lobe). Cl- signals often outlasted odor stimulation. Acetic acid induced Cl- influx without Ca2+ response. Some odors evoked both Ca2+ and Cl- signals in the same areas.
summary: OSNs exhibit odor-specific Cl- influx in both antenna and antennal lobe. Cl- signals can occur without Ca2+ influx, indicating inhibition independent of excitation. Signals persist longer than the odor stimulus.

paper_title: Odor-Induced Multi-Level Inhibitory Maps in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Methyl hexanoate, ethyl-3-hydroxybutyrate, benzaldehyde
neural_activity: Methyl hexanoate induced Ca2+ and Cl- influx. Ethyl-3-hydroxybutyrate and benzaldehyde induced Cl- influx without significant Ca2+ influx in the antenna. Cl- responses were significantly lower in the antennal lobe compared to the antenna for ethyl-3-hydroxybutyrate and benzaldehyde.
summary: Or22a-expressing OSNs show specific patterns of Cl- influx (inhibition) to certain odors (benzaldehyde, ethyl-3-hydroxybutyrate) without concurrent excitation. Cl- influx intensity varies between the antenna and antennal lobe.

paper_title: Odor-Induced Multi-Level Inhibitory Maps in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Ethyl-3-hydroxybutyrate (and methyl laurate reference)
neural_activity: Ethyl-3-hydroxybutyrate induced a strong and long-lasting Cl- influx. These OSNs are known to selectively respond to methyl laurate but are inhibited by other odors.
summary: Or47b-expressing OSNs exhibit strong odor-induced inhibition (Cl- influx) to ethyl-3-hydroxybutyrate, confirming inhibitory responses in this specific OSN population.

paper_title: Odor-Induced Multi-Level Inhibitory Maps in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Various odorants (same set as OSNs)
neural_activity: Odor-evoked Cl- influx in dendrites within the antennal lobe. Inhibition was stronger and more glomerulus-specific at the PN level than at the OSN level. GABAA receptor blockade with picrotoxin reduced odor-induced Cl- signals by 59%. PNs showed broader odor-evoked responses compared to OSNs.
summary: PNs display odor-specific inhibitory maps in the antennal lobe. Inhibition is mediated partly by GABAA receptors and is stronger at the PN level than the OSN level, suggesting distinct processing roles.

paper_title: Neuropeptides Modulate Female Chemosensory Processing upon Mating in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Volatile polyamines (putrescine, cadaverine at 1-10 ppm)
neural_activity: In vivo calcium imaging in antennal lobe (VC5 glomerulus) using GCaMP6f/5: Mating transiently decreases presynaptic calcium response to behaviorally relevant concentrations (10 ppm) at 1-6 h post-mating; SPR knockdown significantly increases response; SPR overexpression significantly suppresses response
behavioral_consequence: T-maze olfactory choice assay: Mated females show strong attraction to high polyamine concentrations (1 mM/10 ppm) whereas virgins show attraction only to low levels; SPR/MIP knockdown reduces attraction to polyamine odor in mated females; Overexpression of SPR/MIP in virgins converts preference to high polyamine levels
ecological_context: Polyamines are essential nutrients for cell proliferation, reproduction, and embryonic development; a polyamine-rich diet increases reproductive success and is found in fermenting fruit/decaying fruit
summary: IR41a OSNs detect volatile polyamines; mating reduces their sensitivity via SPR/MIP signaling to shift female olfactory preference toward higher polyamine concentrations found in fermenting fruit.

paper_title: Neuropeptides Modulate Female Chemosensory Processing upon Mating in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Taste polyamines (putrescine at 1-100 mM)
neural_activity: In vivo calcium imaging in subesophageal zone (SEZ, ROI 1) using GCaMP6f/5: Mating transiently increases presynaptic calcium response to putrescine at 1-6 h post-mating; SPR knockdown significantly decreases response
behavioral_consequence: Oviposition assay: Mated females prefer polyamine-rich agarose substrates over control; SPR/MIP knockdown in IR76b neurons abolishes this preference (indifference); Overexpression of SPR/MIP in virgins increases attraction to high polyamine levels
ecological_context: Polyamines enhance reproductive success and are required during reproduction; females use taste to select optimal egg-laying sites on polyamine-rich food
summary: IR76b GRNs detect taste polyamines; mating increases their sensitivity via SPR/MIP signaling to enhance female preference for polyamine-rich oviposition substrates.

paper_title: Another fly diuretic hormone: tachykinins increase fluid and ion transport by adult Drosophila melanogaster Malpighian ‘renal’ tubules
experimental_stimulus: Drosophila Tachykinin-1 (DTK-1) peptide application; Genetic knockdown of DTKR receptor using c724-GAL4 driver
neural_activity: Increased fluid secretion rate (up to 84% lower in knockdown vs control); Increased Cl- transport rate; Increased Na+ and K+ transport rates; No neural firing recorded (epithelial cell)
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of DTKR in stellate cells increased fly lifespan during desiccation stress (max 90 hours); No effect on starvation survival
ecological_context: Maintenance of hydromineral balance; Enhanced tolerance to desiccation stress in arid/dynamic environmental conditions
techniques_used: ['Single-nucleus RNA sequencing (snRNA-seq)', 'RT-qPCR', 'Fluorescence microscopy', 'In vitro fluid secretion assay', 'Ion-selective microelectrodes (ISME)', 'Desiccation and starvation survival assays']
summary: Malpighian tubule stellate cells express the DTK receptor (DTKR). DTK-1 stimulation increases fluid and ion (Na+, K+, Cl-) transport via DTKR, leading to diuresis. Genetic knockdown of DTKR in stellate cells reduces DTK-stimulated secretion and enhances survival during desiccation stress.

paper_title: Another fly diuretic hormone: tachykinins increase fluid and ion transport by adult Drosophila melanogaster Malpighian ‘renal’ tubules
experimental_stimulus: Genetic knockdown of DTKR receptor using uro-GAL4 driver
neural_activity: No DTKR expression detected; No change in fluid secretion rates compared to controls upon DTK-1 treatment (up to 4-fold increase observed in controls, unaffected in knockdown); No neural firing recorded (epithelial cell)
behavioral_consequence: No impact on desiccation or starvation survival compared to controls
ecological_context: Maintenance of hydromineral balance; Not a primary target for DTK-mediated desiccation response
summary: Principal cells do not express DTKR. Knockdown of DTKR in principal cells does not affect fluid secretion rates or stress survival, indicating DTK signaling is specific to stellate cells.

paper_title: Endocrine signals fine-tune daily activity patterns in Drosophila.
experimental_stimulus: Bath application of Adipokinetic Hormone (AKH) to isolated ventral nerve cord (VNC); genetic ablation/silencing under ad libitum feeding conditions
neural_activity: Significant increase in intracellular cAMP levels in mesothoracic neurons upon AKH bath application (Epac1-camps50A sensor). No significant change in intracellular Ca2+ levels upon AKH application (GCaMP6m sensor).
behavioral_consequence: Ablation (UAS-hid,rpr) or silencing (UAS-shits, UAS-ΔOrk) of OANs significantly reduced locomotor activity specifically during the day under ad libitum feeding. Rescue of AKHR expression in OANs increased locomotor activity during morning/evening bouts and night.
ecological_context: Part of a neuroendocrine axis that balances activity and rest under ad libitum access to food, shaping daily activity levels during the day.
techniques_used: ['Locomotor activity monitoring (DAM)', 'Genetic ablation (UAS-hid,rpr)', 'Neuronal silencing (UAS-shits, UAS-ΔOrk)', 'cAMP imaging (Epac1-camps50A)', 'Ca2+ imaging (GCaMP6m)', 'RNAi (AkhR)']

paper_title: Endocrine signals fine-tune daily activity patterns in Drosophila.
experimental_stimulus: Bath application of Octopamine (OA) to larval CNS preparations; genetic ablation and OA receptor knockdown
neural_activity: Significant increase in intracellular Ca2+ levels upon OA application (GCaMP6m sensor). CaLexA labeling showed increased activity upon starvation.
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of OA receptor (OAMBII) in APCs resulted in significantly increased nocturnal activity under ad libitum conditions. Ablation of APCs (Akh>hid,rpr) impaired starvation-induced hyperactivity and reduced daytime locomotor activity.
ecological_context: APCs integrate metabolic and endocrine signals to shape daily activity patterns; feedback signaling from OANs via OAMBII helps prevent abnormally high nocturnal activity levels.
techniques_used: ['Immunofluorescence (Tdc2 antibody)', 'RT-PCR', 'Ca2+ imaging (GCaMP6m)', 'CaLexA labeling', 'RNAi (OAMBII)', 'Genetic ablation (Akh>hid,rpr)', 'Locomotor activity monitoring (DAM)']

paper_title: A neuroendocrine pathway modulating osmotic stress in Drosophila
cell_type: Dorsal lateral peptidergic neurons (DLPs) / Crz-producing neurons
experimental_stimulus: Desiccation, starvation, and chill-coma exposure
neural_activity: Crz peptide immunoreactivity decreased in large DLP neurons after desiccation and rehydration, indicating peptide release; Crz transcript levels remained constant under osmotic and nutritional stresses.
behavioral_consequence: Global knockdown of Crz increased survival under desiccation and ionic stress but delayed recovery from chill-coma; acute Crz peptide injection delayed chill-coma recovery and decreased desiccation survival.
ecological_context: Responses to environmental stressors including desiccation, ionic imbalance, and cold exposure, as well as nutritional stress.
techniques_used: ['Immunohistochemistry (peptide quantification)', 'qPCR (transcript levels)', 'Crz-RNAi knockdown', 'Peptide injection', 'Survival assays (desiccation, ionic stress)']
summary: DLPs release Crz peptide during desiccation and starvation; modulating Crz signaling alters desiccation, ionic, and cold stress tolerance.

paper_title: A neuroendocrine pathway modulating osmotic stress in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Ex vivo Crz application, Optogenetic activation (ChR2), Thermogenetic activation (TRPA1), CrzR knockdown
neural_activity: Crz application inhibited cAMP production (Epac1-camps FRET sensor) in Va neurons without altering Ca2+ levels (GCaMP6m); optogenetic activation increased defecation.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation increased excretion and reduced whole body water content; CrzR knockdown in Va neurons increased survival under desiccation, ionic, and starvation stress but delayed chill-coma recovery.
ecological_context: Maintenance of water and ion homeostasis during desiccation, ionic stress, and cold tolerance.
techniques_used: ['FRET-based cAMP imaging (Epac1-camps)', 'GCaMP6m calcium imaging', 'Optogenetics (ChR2)', 'Thermogenetics (TRPA1)', 'CrzR-RNAi knockdown', 'Survival assays', 'Water content measurement']
summary: Va neurons express CrzR and are inhibited by Crz via cAMP reduction; activation of Va neurons promotes excretion, while CrzR knockdown enhances stress tolerance.

paper_title: Modulation of Drosophila post-feeding physiology and behavior by the neuropeptide leucokinin
experimental_stimulus: Starvation, desiccation, water consumption (rehydration after desiccation), and standard diet
neural_activity: Calcium activity measured using CaLexA system increased specifically following water consumption; no activity change during starvation, desiccation, or standard diet
behavioral_consequence: Regulation of water homeostasis; loss of LK signaling (affecting these neurons) promoted water retention and improved survival under desiccation
ecological_context: Maintenance of water balance and survival during desiccation and post-feeding physiology
techniques_used: ['CaLexA calcium imaging', 'Lk/Lkr mutant analysis', 'Water content measurement', 'Desiccation survival assays']
summary: ABLKs show increased calcium activity upon water rehydration, indicating a role in regulating water homeostasis and desiccation resistance.

paper_title: Modulation of Drosophila post-feeding physiology and behavior by the neuropeptide leucokinin
experimental_stimulus: Starvation, desiccation, water consumption, and standard diet
neural_activity: Calcium activity measured using CaLexA system showed no changes under tested conditions
behavioral_consequence: Disruption of LK signaling (including these neurons) reduced locomotor activity and metabolic rate (vCO2); global mutants showed reduced long-term food intake
ecological_context: Regulation of locomotor activity, metabolic rate, and feeding behavior
techniques_used: ['CaLexA calcium imaging', 'Drosophila Activity Monitor System (DAMS)', 'Indirect calorimetry', 'CAFE feeding assay']
summary: Brain LK neurons do not show calcium changes under stress but contribute to LK signaling that regulates locomotor activity, metabolic rate, and long-term feeding.

paper_title: Modulation of Drosophila post-feeding physiology and behavior by the neuropeptide leucokinin
experimental_stimulus: Lkr knockdown, normal diet, high sugar/high protein diet, low sugar/high protein diet
neural_activity: Altered expression of insulin-like peptides (DILPs); DILP3 and DILP5 transcript levels changed depending on diet; DILP2 and DILP3 immunoreactivity increased in Lk/Lkr mutants
behavioral_consequence: Targeted Lkr knockdown in IPCs increased starvation resistance; Lk/Lkr mutants showed increased starvation survival
ecological_context: Stress tolerance and metabolic regulation during starvation
techniques_used: ['Lkr-RNAi knockdown', 'qPCR for DILP transcripts', 'Immunohistochemistry', 'Starvation survival assays']
summary: IPCs express Lkr; LK signaling modulation of IPCs affects DILP expression and starvation resistance.

paper_title: A single pair of leucokinin neurons are modulated by feeding state and regulate sleep–metabolism interactions
experimental_stimulus: Feeding state (Fed vs 24-hr Starvation), Glucose application, 2DG application, AMPKα knockdown
neural_activity: Elevated Ca2+ activity during starvation (measured via GCaMP6m-mCherry); reduced activity with glucose application; increased activity with AMPKα knockdown in fed flies
behavioral_consequence: Required for starvation-induced sleep suppression and hyperactivity; silencing (TNT), ablation (laser), or AMPKα knockdown abolishes sleep loss in starved flies; no effect on sleep in fed state
ecological_context: Integration of sleep and metabolic state; starvation-induced sleep loss presumably to induce foraging behavior for survival
techniques_used: ['GCaMP6m Ca2+ imaging (ex vivo and in vivo)', 'Multiphoton laser microablation', 'Genetic silencing (TNT, RNAi)', 'DAMS behavioral assay']
summary: LHLK neurons show increased Ca2+ activity during starvation and are essential for starvation-induced sleep suppression and hyperactivity, but are dispensable for sleep under fed conditions.

paper_title: A single pair of leucokinin neurons are modulated by feeding state and regulate sleep–metabolism interactions
experimental_stimulus: Leucokinin signaling (via Lk receptor)
neural_activity: Lk receptor (Lkr) expression confirmed; direct neural activity not measured in this study
behavioral_consequence: Lkr knockdown in IPCs abolishes starvation-induced sleep suppression, indicating LHLK-IPC connectivity is critical
ecological_context: Critical modulators of both sleep and feeding states
summary: The leucokinin receptor in IPCs is required for starvation-induced sleep suppression, suggesting LHLK neurons signal through IPCs to regulate sleep.

paper_title: A single pair of leucokinin neurons are modulated by feeding state and regulate sleep–metabolism interactions
experimental_stimulus: Feeding state (Fed vs 24-hr Starvation)
neural_activity: No significant difference in Ca2+ activity between fed and starved states
behavioral_consequence: Not identified as the primary driver of starvation-induced sleep suppression (LHLK is)
ecological_context: N/A
summary: SELK neurons are labeled by Lk-GAL4 but do not show modulation of Ca2+ activity in response to starvation.

paper_title: A single pair of leucokinin neurons are modulated by feeding state and regulate sleep–metabolism interactions
experimental_stimulus: Laser ablation
neural_activity: N/A
behavioral_consequence: Unilateral ablation of ALK neurons did not affect starvation-induced sleep suppression
ecological_context: N/A
summary: ALK neurons are not required for metabolic regulation of sleep, serving as a control against LHLK-specific function.

paper_title: Characterization of a set of abdominal neuroendocrine cells that regulate stress physiology using colocalized diuretic peptides in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Genetic knockdown of Leucokinin (LK) or Diuretic Hormone 44 (DH44) using GAL4-UAS RNAi system; Environmental stress challenges including desiccation, starvation, ionic stress, and cold exposure
neural_activity: Decreased LK or DH44 immunoreactivity in the ventral nerve cord (VNC) confirmed by immunohistochemistry; Ex vivo Malpighian tubule fluid secretion rates measured (showed additive response to LK and DH44 peptides)
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of LK or DH44 in ABLKs increased survival under desiccation, starvation, and ionic stress; LK knockdown increased body water content, while DH44 knockdown decreased feeding; ABLK-specific DH44 knockdown delayed chill coma recovery, whereas global DH44 knockdown did not affect desiccation survival
ecological_context: Regulation of stress physiology and homeostasis (water and ion balance) to tolerate environmental stressors such as desiccation, starvation, and ionic stress
techniques_used: ['Immunohistochemistry', 'GAL4-UAS RNAi knockdown', 'Stress resistance assays (desiccation, starvation, ionic stress, chill coma)', 'Capillary feeding (CAFE) assay', 'Water content measurement', 'Malpighian tubule secretion assay']
summary: ABLKs co-express LK and DH44 and regulate stress tolerance and fluid homeostasis; LK knockdown in ABLKs increases water retention and stress resistance, while DH44 knockdown reduces feeding and increases stress resistance, with peptides acting additively on Malpighian tubule secretion.

paper_title: Regulation of starvation-induced hyperactivity by insulin and glucagon signaling in adult Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Starvation (2% agar, no sucrose) vs. Fed conditions (5% sucrose). Hormonal signaling via AKH (hunger) and DILPs (satiety).
neural_activity: Transcriptional profiling (single-cell RT-PCR and RNA-seq) confirmed co-expression of AKHR, dInR (insulin receptor), and TβH (octopamine synthesis enzyme). Direct electrophysiological activity recordings not reported; functional activity inferred from genetic silencing/activation.
behavioral_consequence: Necessary for starvation-induced hyperactivity: acute silencing (Shibirets1) eliminated increased locomotion. Activation (NaChBac) accelerated onset of hyperactivity. dInR knockdown accelerated hyperactivity; dInR overexpression delayed it. Not required for starvation-induced food consumption (MAFE and FLIC assays).
ecological_context: Starvation-induced hyperactivity facilitates food localization and acquisition (food-seeking behavior), which is crucial for survival and reproduction when energy sources are depleted.
techniques_used: ['Split-GAL4 system (nSyb:AD/AKHR:BD)', 'Genetic silencing (Shibirets1, RNAi)', 'Genetic activation (NaChBac, overexpression)', 'Single-cell RT-PCR', 'Single-cell RNA-seq', 'Immunostaining', 'DAMS (Drosophila Activity Monitor System)', 'Video-based locomotion tracking', 'MAFE (feeding assay)', 'FLIC (feeding assay)']
summary: A small group of octopaminergic neurons in the SEZ expressing AKHR and dInR regulates starvation-induced hyperactivity but not food consumption. Octopamine signaling mediates this effect. Insulin signaling (via dInR) suppresses hyperactivity, while glucagon-like signaling (via AKHR) promotes it.

paper_title: Regulation of starvation-induced hyperactivity by insulin and glucagon signaling in adult Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Starvation vs. Fed conditions (via AKHR knockdown).
neural_activity: Express AKHR (confirmed by previous reports and tested via RNAi).
behavioral_consequence: Knocking down AKHR in these neurons did not interfere with starvation-induced hyperactivity, indicating they are not the primary regulators of this behavior.
ecological_context: Involved in taste sensation but not in mediating starvation-induced locomotor activity.
summary: Gr5a+ gustatory neurons express AKHR but are not required for starvation-induced hyperactivity.

paper_title: Synaptic transmission parallels neuromodulation in a central food-intake circuit
experimental_stimulus: Thermogenetic activation (dTrpA1 at 32°C); chemosensory input in the subesophageal zone (SEZ)
neural_activity: Extensive synaptic contacts onto insulin-producing cells (IPCs); expression of choline acetyltransferase (ChAT); decreased pharyngeal pump activity upon activation
behavioral_consequence: Decreased food intake and reduced pharyngeal pumping motor patterns upon activation
ecological_context: Regulation of feeding behavior and energy homeostasis in response to chemosensory cues
techniques_used: ['serial section transmission electron microscopy (ssTEM)', 'thermogenetic activation (dTrpA1)', 'immunohistochemistry (ChAT)', 'behavioral food intake assay', 'electrophysiology (antennal nerve recording)', 'RNAi knockdown']
summary: hugin-PC neurons receive chemosensory input in the SEZ, express acetylcholine and hugin peptide, and suppress feeding and pharyngeal pumping upon activation.

paper_title: Synaptic transmission parallels neuromodulation in a central food-intake circuit
experimental_stimulus: Thermogenetic activation (dTrpA1); sensory input from SEZ and abdominal nerves
neural_activity: Receive chemosensory and abdominal sensory input; express ChAT and hugin peptide; form reciprocal connections within class
behavioral_consequence: Contribution to decreased food intake and pharyngeal pumping (as part of hugin population)

paper_title: Synaptic transmission parallels neuromodulation in a central food-intake circuit
experimental_stimulus: None tested behaviorally in this study
neural_activity: Efferent neurons projecting to ring gland; minimal presynaptic sites in CNS; weak chemosensory input
behavioral_consequence: Unknown specific effect described in this study

paper_title: Synaptic transmission parallels neuromodulation in a central food-intake circuit
experimental_stimulus: None tested behaviorally in this study
neural_activity: Efferent neurons projecting to pharynx; no presynaptic sites in CNS
behavioral_consequence: Unknown specific effect described in this study

paper_title: Synaptic transmission parallels neuromodulation in a central food-intake circuit
experimental_stimulus: Application of hugin neuropeptide (hug-PK2 at 1 µM or higher)
neural_activity: Increased calcium activity; expression of hugin receptor PK2-R1; extensive synaptic contacts from hugin-PC neurons (especially IPCs)
behavioral_consequence: N/A (Downstream neuroendocrine target)
ecological_context: Coordination of feeding and energy homeostasis via neuroendocrine signaling
techniques_used: ['calcium imaging (CaMPARI)', 'immunohistochemistry (GAL4 lines)', 'serial section transmission electron microscopy (ssTEM)']
summary: mNSCs express the hugin receptor and show increased calcium activity in response to hugin peptide, linking hugin neurons to the neuroendocrine system.

paper_title: The molecular basis for water taste in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Water, hypo-osmotic solutions (0.2M-1M NaCl, 0.5M-1M sucrose, 0.5M-1M ribose, 1M NMDG, 20% PEG)
neural_activity: Robust G-CaMP fluorescence increases and extracellular action potentials (12.0±0.9 spikes/sec) upon water stimulation. Response inhibited by salts, sugars, and high osmolarity solutions. Complete loss of water response in ppk28 null mutants.
behavioral_consequence: ppk28 mutants show significantly reduced drinking time (3.0 seconds vs 10.3 seconds in controls) and reduced proboscis extension reflex to water. Sucrose consumption and general drinking behavior unaffected.
ecological_context: Essential for regulation of water intake to maintain osmotic homeostasis.
techniques_used: ['microarray screen', 'in situ hybridization', 'immunohistochemistry', 'G-CaMP imaging', 'extracellular bristle recordings (electrophysiology)', 'behavioral drinking assays', 'proboscis extension reflex', 'HEK293 calcium imaging']
summary: ppk28-expressing gustatory neurons mediate cellular and behavioral responses to water; ppk28 is an osmosensitive ion channel required for water taste detection and normal water drinking behavior.

paper_title: The molecular basis for water taste in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Denatonium, caffeine (native); Water, NMDG, sucrose (with ectopic ppk28 expression)
neural_activity: No water response normally. Ectopic expression of ppk28 confers water sensitivity (G-CaMP increase and action potentials) and inhibition by solutes (NMDG, sucrose). Response to bitter compounds (denatonium, caffeine) unchanged.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested.
summary: Gr66a-expressing neurons normally lack water sensitivity; ectopic expression of ppk28 confers water sensitivity and hypo-osmotic inhibition to these bitter-sensing neurons.

paper_title: The Drosophila Receptor Guanylyl Cyclase Gyc76C Is Required for Semaphorin-1a-Plexin A-Mediated Axonal Repulsion
experimental_stimulus: Loss-of-function mutations in Gyc76C (gyc76C KG03723); overexpression of Gyc76C (UAS:Gyc76C, elav-Gal4); genetic interaction with Sema-1a and PlexA
neural_activity: Defasciculation of axon bundles from the intersegmental nerve (ISN) and segmental nerve (SN); navigation to specific muscle targets (muscles 6, 7, 12, 13, 24); failure to innervate targets in mutants
behavioral_consequence: Not tested (developmental axon guidance focus)
ecological_context: Establishment of neuromuscular connectivity in the developing Drosophila embryo
techniques_used: ['Genetic mutants (P-element insertion, excision)', 'Gal4-UAS system for overexpression', 'Immunocytochemistry (anti-fasciclin II)', 'Microscopy']
summary: Gyc76C is required for Sema-1a–PlexA-mediated repulsive axon guidance of motor axons. Loss of Gyc76C or Sema-1a leads to failure of motor axon defasciculation and target innervation in the ventral nerve cord.

paper_title: The Drosophila Receptor Guanylyl Cyclase Gyc76C Is Required for Semaphorin-1a-Plexin A-Mediated Axonal Repulsion
experimental_stimulus: Ectopic expression of high levels of Sema-1a in midline glial cells (UAS:Sema-1a, P52-Gal4) in sema1a mutant background
neural_activity: Midline crossing (anterior and posterior commissures); failure to cross midline in Sema-1a gain-of-function background
behavioral_consequence: Not tested (developmental axon guidance focus)
ecological_context: Formation of stereotypical ladder-like CNS architecture in the developing embryo
summary: Commissural axons are responsive to the repulsive effects of Sema-1a. Ectopic Sema-1a in midline glia prevents commissural axons from crossing the midline in a dosage-sensitive manner.

paper_title: A neuropeptide regulates fighting behavior in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Thermogenetic activation (dTRPA1 at 28°C), chemogenetic activation of upstream P1 neurons (ATP via P2X2), social competition (winner vs loser state)
neural_activity: Increased calcium activity in winners compared to losers (TRIC imaging); strong electrical and calcium responses to P1 activation (patch-clamp, GCaMP6m); no detectable TRIC signals in DSK-L neurons
behavioral_consequence: Activation increases lunge frequency and reduces fighting latency; inactivation (TNT/Kir2.1) reduces lunges and prolongs latency; conditional overexpression promotes social dominance
ecological_context: Aggression is critical for defense against conspecifics to obtain food resources and mating partners; involved in the establishment of social hierarchy during fly fighting
techniques_used: ['CRISPR-Cas9 knockouts', 'GAL4 knock-ins', 'Thermogenetics (dTRPA1, TNT, Kir2.1)', 'Calcium imaging (TRIC, GCaMP6m)', 'Patch-clamp electrophysiology', 'Behavioral assays']
summary: DSK-M neurons (Type I & II) show increased calcium activity in winning flies and respond strongly to P1 activation; their activation promotes aggression while inactivation suppresses it.

paper_title: A neuropeptide regulates fighting behavior in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Chemogenetic activation of upstream P1 neurons (ATP via P2X2)
neural_activity: Relatively weak electrical responses to P1 activation; smaller calcium response compared to DSK-M; TRIC signals not detectable
behavioral_consequence: Inactivation of Type III neurons did not cause significant changes in lunge frequency
ecological_context: Part of the Dsk system involved in aggression modulation, though Type III contribution is not fully established
summary: DSK-L neurons (Type III) show weak responses to P1 activation and their inactivation does not significantly affect aggression levels.

paper_title: A neuropeptide regulates fighting behavior in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Chemogenetic activation (ATP via P2X2), thermogenetic activation (dTRPA1)
neural_activity: Activation elicits robust electrical and calcium responses in downstream Dsk-expressing neurons; direct synaptic connections confirmed via transsynaptic tracing (Trans-Tango, GRASP)
behavioral_consequence: Activation promotes aggressive behavior and courtship; aggression-promoting effect is suppressed in ΔDsk mutants, while courtship effect remains intact
ecological_context: Control aggressive arousal in the central brain; facilitates both aggression and courtship behaviors
summary: A subset of P1 neurons directly connects to Dsk-expressing neurons; their activation drives aggression (Dsk-dependent) and courtship.

paper_title: A neuropeptide regulates fighting behavior in Drosophila melanogaster
experimental_stimulus: Genetic knock-out (ΔCCKLR-17D1), RNAi silencing
neural_activity: Expression visualized in central complex, lateral horn, optical lobe, suboesophageal ganglion, and ventral nerve cord
behavioral_consequence: Knock-out largely abolishes aggressive behavior; required for Dsk-mediated neuromodulation of aggression (activation of Dsk neurons fails to enhance aggression in mutant background)
ecological_context: Modulation of aggressive behavior; receptor necessary for Dsk signaling pathway
summary: CCKLR-17D1 receptor-expressing neurons are necessary for Dsk-mediated aggression modulation; loss of receptor function abolishes aggressive behavior.

paper_title: The neuropeptide Drosulfakinin regulates social isolation-induced aggression in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Social isolation
neural_activity: Downregulation of Dsk gene expression measured via RNA-seq following social isolation
behavioral_consequence: Dsk knockdown significantly increased social isolation-induced aggression; genetic activation or silencing of Dsk neurons each similarly increased isolation-driven aggression
ecological_context: Social isolation strongly modulates behavior across the animal kingdom
summary: Social isolation downregulates Dsk gene expression in head-expressed genes. Genetic manipulation (knockdown, activation, or silencing) of Dsk neurons significantly increases social isolation-induced aggression, suggesting a U-shaped dependence of aggression on Dsk signaling.

paper_title: Hierarchical Control of Drosophila Sleep, Courtship, and Feeding Behaviors by Male-Specific P1 Neurons
experimental_stimulus: Temperature shifts to 27°C (mild activation) or 30-32°C (strong activation) in flies expressing dTrpA1 in P1 neurons using five independent drivers (P1a-P1e)
neural_activity: dTrpA1-mediated activation (firing begins at >26°C, activity increases with temperature); P1e driver labeled ~23 P1 neurons, other drivers labeled 9-12 P1 neurons
behavioral_consequence: Sleep: Mild activation of P1a, P1b, P1c, P1e inhibited sleep (up to 80% loss); Stronger activation of all P1a-P1e inhibited sleep. Courtship: Mild activation did not induce unilateral wing extension; Stronger activation of P1a, P1c, P1d, P1e induced unilateral wing extension. Feeding: Mild activation had no effect; Stronger activation of P1e suppressed feeding (blue dye assay); P1a-d did not affect feeding. Locomotion: Activation increased walking velocity.
ecological_context: Competition between sleep, courtship, and feeding behaviors mediated by external sensory cues and internal states, essential for survival and reproduction; P1 neurons are male-specific and regulate sexually-dimorphic behaviors
techniques_used: ['Split-GAL4', 'Flip-out (LexA/Flp)', 'dTrpA1 thermoactivation', 'DAM2 locomotor monitoring', 'Video scoring of wing extension', 'Blue dye feeding assay', 'Confocal microscopy']
summary: Male-specific P1 neurons control sleep, courtship, and feeding in a threshold-dependent manner: mild activation inhibits sleep, stronger activation induces courtship, and strongest activation of a larger subset (P1e) suppresses feeding.

paper_title: How Food Controls Aggression in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Food substrate (apple juice + 100 mM sucrose + 1% agarose), sucrose solutions (100-800 mM), or no food (agarose)
behavioral_consequence: Silencing Gr5a+ GRNs (UAS-TNT, UAS-Hid) reduced food-promoted aggression but did not reduce aggression in the presence of females. Artificial activation (UAS-TRPA1, UAS-TRPV1, UAS-DTRPA1, UAS-ChR2, UAS-ReACh) did not increase aggression in the absence of food. Silencing reduced sucrose sensitivity in proboscis extension reflex (PER) assays.
ecological_context: Sucrose is present in fruits attractive to egg-laying females; flies compete over limiting food resources which may also signal female presence.
techniques_used: ['Genetic silencing (UAS-TNT, UAS-Hid)', 'Genetic activation (UAS-TRPA1, UAS-TRPV1, UAS-DTRPA1, UAS-ChR2, UAS-ReACh)', 'Behavioral assays (aggression lunges)', 'Proboscis extension reflex (PER) assay', 'Video tracking']
summary: Gr5a+ GRNs are necessary for food-promoted aggression but are not sufficient to drive aggression in the absence of food.

paper_title: Aggression and social experience: genetic analysis of visual circuit activity in the control of aggressiveness in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Chronic visual loss via ninaB or eya mutations; Acute synaptic blockade via temperature-sensitive shibire (shits) at 32°C; Social housing conditions (single vs. group housing).
neural_activity: Loss of vision confirmed by phototaxis assay; Blockade of synaptic transmission in photoreceptor cells at restrictive temperature (32°C).
behavioral_consequence: Chronic visual loss did not prevent social suppression of aggression or affect aggressiveness of single-housed flies. Acute blockade of visual circuit activity significantly increased aggressiveness of single-housed flies. Locomotor activity was unaffected in ninaB/shits manipulations but reduced in eya mutants.
ecological_context: Aggression is an innate behavior that allows animals to compete for limited resources, such as food, mating partners and habitats. Social experience plays an important role in modulating aggression.
techniques_used: ['Genetic manipulation (ninaB, eya mutants, rescue constructs)', 'Temperature-sensitive shibire (shits) expression', 'Aggression assay (counting lunges, wing threats, tussles, boxing, holding)', 'Phototaxis assay', 'Locomotion assay (travel distance)']
summary: Photoreceptor cell activity was manipulated genetically and acutely. Chronic visual loss did not affect social suppression of aggression or baseline aggression in single-housed flies, whereas acute blockade of visual circuit activity increased aggressiveness in single-housed flies.

paper_title: Optogenetic control of freely behaving adult Drosophila using a red-shifted channelrhodopsin
experimental_stimulus: Photostimulation (470 nm, 530 nm, 627 nm) or heat (dTrpA1)
neural_activity: Electrophysiology showed continuous spiking bursts during pulsed light (1 Hz); spiking decayed exponentially during continuous light (half-time ~1.5 sec); dTrpA1 activation triggered transient spiking with decay
behavioral_consequence: Proboscis Extension Reflex (PER) triggered by photostimulation; continuous activation produced only transient PER reaction
ecological_context: Sugar-sensing function
summary: Gr5a GRNs express opsins that trigger PER upon light or heat activation; pulsed activation maintains spiking and behavior, while continuous activation leads to spiking decay and depolarization block.

paper_title: Optogenetic control of freely behaving adult Drosophila using a red-shifted channelrhodopsin
experimental_stimulus: Red (627 nm) or Green (530 nm) light activation (ReaChR)
neural_activity: Calcium imaging (2-photon) showed larger ReaChR-evoked calcium influxes in socially isolated flies vs group-housed flies; no persistent calcium transients after photoactivation
behavioral_consequence: Wing extension evoked in a probabilistic/stochastic manner with variable latencies; behavior persisted after stimulation offset; social isolation lowered the light intensity threshold for wing extension
ecological_context: Male courtship song initiation; modulated by social experience (isolation)
summary: P1 neuron activation triggers probabilistic wing extension during male courtship; neural excitability is enhanced by social isolation, lowering behavioral thresholds.

paper_title: Optogenetic control of freely behaving adult Drosophila using a red-shifted channelrhodopsin
experimental_stimulus: Green (530 nm) light activation (ReaChR)
behavioral_consequence: Wing extension evoked in a deterministic manner, time-locked to stimulation onset and offset; not modulated by social isolation
ecological_context: Male courtship song generation
summary: pIP10 neuron activation triggers deterministic, time-locked wing extension during courtship; this response is not influenced by prior social experience.

paper_title: Optogenetic control of freely behaving adult Drosophila using a red-shifted channelrhodopsin
experimental_stimulus: Light activation (ReaChR)
behavioral_consequence: Side walking induced at specific intensities; paralysis (loss of postural control) at higher intensities
summary: ReaChR activation in HB9-GAL4 neurons induces side walking or paralysis in freely moving flies.

paper_title: Optogenetic control of freely behaving adult Drosophila using a red-shifted channelrhodopsin
experimental_stimulus: Light activation (ReaChR)
behavioral_consequence: Abdominal bending and ejaculation induced
summary: ReaChR activation in Crz-GAL4 neurons induces abdominal bending and ejaculation.

paper_title: Optogenetic control of freely behaving adult Drosophila using a red-shifted channelrhodopsin
experimental_stimulus: Light activation (ReaChR)
behavioral_consequence: Mating behaviors including wing extension and abdominal bending; paralysis at higher intensities
summary: ReaChR activation in Fru-GAL4 neurons elicits courtship behaviors and paralysis depending on light intensity.

paper_title: Contrast normalization affects response time-course of visual interneurons
experimental_stimulus: 1-second luminance step in receptive field (RF) center; surround conditions: uniformly gray, stationary grating, moving grating, stochastic noise; varying input step amplitudes (50%, 75%, 100%)
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6f) from axon terminals. Response amplitude scaled with input amplitude. Temporal kinetics (decay) remained unchanged regardless of surround dynamics or input amplitude. No contrast normalization effects observed.
ecological_context: Discussed as not exhibiting contrast normalization, unlike medulla cells, suggesting distinct roles in contrast encoding for natural scenes.
summary: L1 neurons do not exhibit contrast normalization; their response dynamics remain stable regardless of visual surround dynamics.

paper_title: Contrast normalization affects response time-course of visual interneurons
experimental_stimulus: 1-second luminance step in RF center; surround conditions: uniformly gray, moving grating; varying input step amplitudes (50%, 75%, 100%)
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6f) from axon terminals in medulla layer M10. Dynamic surround suppressed response amplitude (to 67% of static) and increased signal decay speed. Input amplitude affected amplitude but not kinetics. Some pre-step response present before luminance step, subsequently suppressed by dynamic surround.
ecological_context: Discussed as providing input to T4 cells; contrast normalization effects (faster kinetics) may adjust speed tuning to prevailing speeds in natural environments where global visual cues are abundant during active locomotion.
summary: Mi1 neurons show contrast normalization; dynamic surround suppresses amplitude and speeds up response decay, independent of stimulus amplitude.

paper_title: Contrast normalization affects response time-course of visual interneurons
experimental_stimulus: 1-second luminance step in RF center; surround conditions: uniformly gray, moving grating
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6f) from axon terminals in lobula layer Lo1. Dynamic surround suppressed response amplitude (to 59% of static) and increased signal decay speed.
ecological_context: Discussed as input to T5 cells; contrast normalization significantly improves robustness of motion detection in natural scenes with varying contrast.
summary: Tm1 neurons show contrast normalization; dynamic surround suppresses amplitude and speeds up response decay.

paper_title: Contrast normalization affects response time-course of visual interneurons
experimental_stimulus: 1-second luminance step in RF center; surround conditions: uniformly gray, moving grating
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6f) from axon terminals in lobula layer Lo1. Dynamic surround suppressed response amplitude (to 48% of static) and increased signal decay speed.
ecological_context: Discussed as input to T5 cells; contrast normalization improves robustness of motion detection in natural scenes.
summary: Tm2 neurons show contrast normalization; dynamic surround suppresses amplitude and speeds up response decay.

paper_title: Contrast normalization affects response time-course of visual interneurons
experimental_stimulus: 1-second luminance step in RF center; surround conditions: uniformly gray, moving grating, stationary grating, stochastic noise; varying input step amplitudes (50%, 75%, 100%)
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6f) from axon terminals in medulla layer M10. Dynamic surround suppressed response amplitude (to 24% of static) and increased signal decay speed. Input amplitude affected amplitude but not kinetics. Similar temporal profiles observed for moving grating and stochastic noise surrounds.
ecological_context: Discussed as providing input to T4 cells; contrast normalization alters filtering properties, potentially adjusting speed tuning to natural visual conditions.
summary: Tm3 neurons show contrast normalization; dynamic surround suppresses amplitude and speeds up response decay, independent of stimulus amplitude.

paper_title: Assessing olfactory, memory, social and circadian phenotypes associated with schizophrenia in a genetic model based on Rim
experimental_stimulus: Benzaldehyde (Bz) odor; High KCl bath application
neural_activity: Reduced evoked Ca2+ influx (GCaMP6f imaging); ~50% reduction in presynaptic terminal area in the Lateral Horn (LH)
behavioral_consequence: Reduced olfactory avoidance (lower olfactory index); Increased social distance (social space paradigm); No effect on olfactory memory
ecological_context: Innate olfactory behaviours (LH processing)
summary: Rim knockdown in AL PNs reduces presynaptic terminal area and evoked Ca2+ in the Lateral Horn, resulting in impaired olfactory avoidance and increased social distance.

paper_title: Assessing olfactory, memory, social and circadian phenotypes associated with schizophrenia in a genetic model based on Rim
experimental_stimulus: Odorant (3-octanol/4-methylcyclohexanol) paired with electric shock
neural_activity: Not measured
behavioral_consequence: No effect on Short-term Memory (STM) or Intermediate-term Memory (ITM); No effect on olfactory acuity or social space
ecological_context: Main memory centre
summary: Rim knockdown in MB neurons does not affect olfactory learning, memory, or baseline olfactory performance.

paper_title: Assessing olfactory, memory, social and circadian phenotypes associated with schizophrenia in a genetic model based on Rim
experimental_stimulus: Light/Dark (LD) cycles; Constant Darkness (DD)
neural_activity: Impaired day/night remodelling of dorsal s-LNvs terminals; Impaired circadian cycling of PDF neuropeptide release (immunohistochemistry)
behavioral_consequence: Reduced circadian rhythmicity strength; Shortened free-running period length; Reduced total locomotor activity in DD
ecological_context: Circadian pacemaker function
summary: Rim knockdown in LNvs disrupts circadian structural plasticity and PDF release, leading to deficits in circadian rhythmicity and locomotor activity.

paper_title: Serotonin modulates a depression-like state in Drosophila responsive to lithium treatment
experimental_stimulus: 300 Hz vibration stress (3 days), sucrose feeding
neural_activity: 5-HT immunoreactivity reduced at mushroom body (MB) a-lobes during stress; 5-HT levels elevated in brain after sucrose feeding (anti-5-HT labelling). Tonic depolarization prevents depression-like state.
behavioral_consequence: Vibration stress reduces climbing attempts, walking activity, and courtship latency; sucrose feeding restores these. Blocking chemical output (UAS-TNT) prevents sucrose relief. Depolarization (UAS-NaChBac) prevents depression-like state.
ecological_context: Stress response and modulation of behavioral motivation under continuous stress conditions.
summary: Trh493 serotonergic neurons release 5-HT at MB a-lobes; stress reduces this release while sucrose feeding elevates 5-HT levels. Blocking their output prevents sucrose-mediated relief of the depression-like state.

paper_title: Serotonin modulates a depression-like state in Drosophila responsive to lithium treatment
cell_type: Mushroom Body a-/b-lobes Kenyon Cells (5-HT-1A receptor expressing)
experimental_stimulus: Vibration stress, sucrose feeding, 5-HT-1A receptor knockdown (RNAi)
neural_activity: 5-HT release compromised at a-lobes during stress. 5-HT-1A receptor activation required for sucrose relief. Tonic activation of 5-HT-1A cells prevents depression-like state.
behavioral_consequence: Stress reduces climbing attempts. 5-HT-1A knockdown prevents sucrose relief. Tonic depolarization of these cells prevents depression-like state. Silencing output prevents depression-like state but also sucrose relief.
ecological_context: Modulation of motivation to perform voluntary behaviors (climbing, walking) under stress and reward conditions.
summary: 5-HT-1A receptors in MB a-/b-lobes mediate sucrose relief from the depression-like state. Activation enhances behavioral activity, while knockdown prevents sugar-mediated resilience.

paper_title: Serotonin modulates a depression-like state in Drosophila responsive to lithium treatment
cell_type: Mushroom Body g-lobes Kenyon Cells (5-HT-1B receptor expressing)
experimental_stimulus: Vibration stress, 8-OH-DPAT (5-HT-1B agonist), 5-HT-1B receptor knockdown (RNAi)
neural_activity: 5-HT-1B receptor activation controls behavioral inactivity. Knockdown prevents stress-induced depression. Tonic activation increases susceptibility to stress.
behavioral_consequence: Stress reduces climbing attempts. 5-HT-1B knockdown renders flies resilient to vibration stress. Tonic depolarization increases susceptibility. Feeding 8-OH-DPAT reduces climbing activity.
ecological_context: Control of behavioral inactivity and inhibition of voluntary motor output under stress.
summary: 5-HT-1B receptors in MB g-lobes inhibit climbing activity. Knockdown confers resilience to stress, while receptor activation reduces voluntary behavioral output.

paper_title: Transient Dysregulation of Dopamine Signaling in a Developing Drosophila Arousal Circuit Permanently Impairs Behavioral Responsiveness in Adults
experimental_stimulus: Transient heat-induced activation (31°C) during late pupal development
neural_activity: Transient increase in presynaptic dopamine activity; transient decrease in pupal dopamine levels; levels returned to normal by adulthood
behavioral_consequence: Permanently decreased responsiveness to mechanical stimuli in adults; impaired visual fixation (increased angle of deviation); increased pausing in response to moving gratings; increased sleep intensity (deeper sleep) without altering sleep duration
ecological_context: Regulates arousal and attention; implicated in allocating salience to external and internal stimuli
summary: Transient activation of dopaminergic neurons during development permanently impairs adult behavioral responsiveness and visual fixation, and increases sleep intensity.

paper_title: Transient Dysregulation of Dopamine Signaling in a Developing Drosophila Arousal Circuit Permanently Impairs Behavioral Responsiveness in Adults
experimental_stimulus: Transient activation (UAS-NachBac) or transient knockdown of Dop1R1/Dop1R2 receptors during late pupal development
neural_activity: Increased neuronal activity (activation); decreased Dop1R1/Dop1R2 gene expression (knockdown)
behavioral_consequence: Decreased responsiveness to mechanical stimuli in adults (D1/D2 knockdown in restricted 23E10 driver); increased sleep intensity (D1 knockdown); increased sleep duration (activation of GR23E10/GR55B01)
ecological_context: Sleep/wake regulation and arousal; involved in regulating arousal levels and sleep homeostasis
