summary: Transient knockdown of D1 receptors in dFB sleep-promoting neurons during development decreases adult behavioral responsiveness and increases sleep intensity.

paper_title: Transient Dysregulation of Dopamine Signaling in a Developing Drosophila Arousal Circuit Permanently Impairs Behavioral Responsiveness in Adults
experimental_stimulus: Transient knockdown of Dop1R1/Dop1R2 receptors during late pupal development
neural_activity: Decreased Dop1R1/Dop1R2 gene expression
behavioral_consequence: No significant effect on behavioral responsiveness to mechanical stimuli in adults
ecological_context: Associated with arousal and sleep regulation
summary: Transient knockdown of D1 receptors in mushroom body neurons does not affect adult behavioral responsiveness.

paper_title: Transient Dysregulation of Dopamine Signaling in a Developing Drosophila Arousal Circuit Permanently Impairs Behavioral Responsiveness in Adults
experimental_stimulus: Transient activation (UAS-NachBac) during development
neural_activity: Increased neuronal activity
behavioral_consequence: Decreased responsiveness to mechanical stimuli in adults; decreased sleep duration
ecological_context: Sleep/wake regulation
summary: Activation of GABAergic neurons during development decreases adult behavioral responsiveness and sleep duration.

paper_title: Transient Dysregulation of Dopamine Signaling in a Developing Drosophila Arousal Circuit Permanently Impairs Behavioral Responsiveness in Adults
experimental_stimulus: Transient activation during development
neural_activity: Increased neuronal activity
behavioral_consequence: Decreased sleep; increased responsiveness
ecological_context: Arousal and sleep regulation
summary: Activation of a dopaminergic sub-cluster targeting the dFB decreases sleep and increases responsiveness.

paper_title: Gene Dosage in the Dysbindin Schizophrenia Susceptibility Network Differentially Affect Synaptic Function and Plasticity
experimental_stimulus: Electrical stimulation of motor neurons; Acute incubation with philanthotoxin (PhTx); Stimulation at 3 Hz or 10 Hz in presence of bafilomycin A1
neural_activity: Intracellular recordings of Evoked Junctional Potential (EJP) and Miniature EJP (mEJP) amplitudes, quantal content; Vesicle pool depletion rates measured during high-frequency stimulation
summary: Larval motor neuron synapses showed increased mEJP amplitude and bouton number in Blos1-null alleles (dominant phenotype). Dysbindin and Blos1 were necessary for homeostatic plasticity (PhTx response) and mobilization of the reserve synaptic vesicle pool (high-frequency stimulation).

paper_title: Gene Dosage in the Dysbindin Schizophrenia Susceptibility Network Differentially Affect Synaptic Function and Plasticity
cell_type: Adult Local Interneurons (LNs) and Projection Neurons (PNs)
experimental_stimulus: 30 min exposure to ethyl butyrate (EB) odor (5% in paraffin oil)
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of dysbindin in these cell types prevented short-term olfactory habituation, resulting in maintained avoidance behavior after odor exposure (deficit in learned odorant-avoidance response)
techniques_used: ['Y-maze behavioral assay', 'Gal4/UAS RNAi knockdown (LN1 and GH146 drivers)', 'Temperature-sensitive repression (tub-Gal80 ts)']
summary: Dysbindin is necessary in adult local interneurons and projection neurons for short-term olfactory habituation. Cell-specific knockdown prevented the reduction in avoidance behavior typically seen after odor pre-exposure.

paper_title: Transient activation of dopaminergic neurons during development modulates visual responsiveness, locomotion and brain activity in a dopamine ontogeny model of schizophrenia
experimental_stimulus: Transient activation (UAS-TrpA1 at 31°C) or silencing (UAS-shibire at 31°C) during developmental windows (0-10 days post-embryo) or adulthood; moving visual gratings (3 Hz) in choice maze; moving visual bar in LFP arena; Methamphetamine or Haloperidol feeding
neural_activity: Confocal imaging showed no gross anatomical changes in DA clusters (PAM, PPL, etc.) after developmental manipulation. HPLC revealed significantly decreased dopamine content in heads of activated flies compared to controls (correlated with increased visual responsiveness). LFP recordings showed significantly reduced gamma-band activity (30-90 Hz) and decreased LFP responsiveness to moving visual stimuli in treated adults.
behavioral_consequence: Transient activation during the pupal stage (days 5-10) significantly increased visual responsiveness (optomotor response) in adult males and increased locomotion (hyperactivity). It also caused fragmented sleep with increased number of sleep bouts and shorter duration. Silencing DA neurons had no significant effect on visual responsiveness. Acute methamphetamine feeding increased visual responsiveness in controls but had no additive effect in developmentally manipulated flies (ceiling effect).
ecological_context: The paper links DA function to 'arousal states', 'responsiveness to stimuli', and 'attention-like behaviour'. It notes 'ethologically normal variability in DA content in wild-type flies correlates with performance in a visual paradigm'. No specific natural ecological niche or behavioral necessity for the developmental timing was explicitly described beyond general modulation of arousal and salience.
techniques_used: ['Gal4/UAS system', 'Thermal activation (UAS-TrpA1)', 'Thermal silencing (UAS-shibire)', 'Visual choice maze', 'HPLC', 'Confocal imaging', 'Local Field Potential (LFP) electrophysiology', 'Drosophila Activity Monitor Systems', 'Video tracking', 'Drug feeding (Methamphetamine/Haloperidol)']
summary: Transient activation of Th-expressing dopaminergic neurons during the pupal stage of development (days 5-10) permanently increases visual responsiveness, locomotion, and causes fragmented sleep in adult Drosophila. These developmental manipulations are associated with reduced head dopamine levels and reduced gamma-band brain activity (LFP) during visual stimulation, mimicking effects of methamphetamine exposure.

paper_title: Glia control experience-dependent plasticity in an olfactory critical period
experimental_stimulus: 15% [v/v] ethyl butyrate (EB) from 0 to 2 days post-eclosion (DPE)
neural_activity: 44% loss of terminal arbor volume and 39% reduction in presynaptic terminals (Brp puncta) in VM7 glomerulus. Terminal arbor volume recovers after 5 days in normal environment, but presynaptic number remains reduced.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested in this study.
ecological_context: Discussion suggests relevance for maintaining odor encoding in animals raised in environments saturated with cognate odorant.
summary: Or42a OSN terminals in VM7 undergo experience-dependent pruning (volume and synapse loss) following early-life EB exposure, with persistent reduction in presynapse number despite arbor recovery.

paper_title: Glia control experience-dependent plasticity in an olfactory critical period
experimental_stimulus: 15% [v/v] ethyl butyrate (EB) from 0 to 2 DPE (postsynaptic to Or42a OSNs)
neural_activity: Perforated patch-clamp recordings show spontaneous firing rate strongly decreased (near complete silence) at 2-3 DPE. Activity remains persistently reduced (8-fold lower) after a 5-day recovery period. Intrinsic membrane properties (spike onset latency, AHP decay) unchanged.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested in this study.
ecological_context: Persistent dampening of synaptic transmission downstream of Or42a OSNs affects odor processing.
summary: VM7 PNs exhibit persistently reduced spontaneous activity following early-life EB exposure, correlating with reduced presynaptic input from Or42a OSNs.

paper_title: Glia control experience-dependent plasticity in an olfactory critical period
experimental_stimulus: 15% [v/v] ethyl butyrate (EB) from 0 to 2 DPE
neural_activity: Upregulate Draper expression (66.7% increase in VM7 intensity). Extend processes into VM7 (24.8% volume overlap vs 3.9% control) to phagocytose Or42a OSN terminals (confirmed via MApHS pH sensor). Invasion and pruning require Draper.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested in this study.
ecological_context: Not explicitly tested in ecological context.
techniques_used: ['Confocal microscopy (Draper::GFP, mCD8::RFP)', 'MApHS pH sensor imaging', 'RNAi knockdown (GMR56F03-GAL4)']
summary: Ensheathing glia invade VM7, upregulate Draper, and phagocytose Or42a OSN terminals in a Draper-dependent manner during the critical period.

paper_title: Glia control experience-dependent plasticity in an olfactory critical period
experimental_stimulus: 15% [v/v] ethyl butyrate (EB) from 0 to 2 DPE
neural_activity: Knockdown of Draper in astrocytes does not suppress Or42a OSN terminal volume loss or presynapse reduction. Draper expression is low in adult astrocytes.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested in this study.
ecological_context: Not explicitly tested in ecological context.
summary: Astrocytic Draper is not required for experience-dependent pruning of Or42a OSN terminals in the VM7 glomerulus.

paper_title: FMRP activity and control of Csw/SHP2 translation regulate MAPK-dependent synaptic transmission
experimental_stimulus: Motor nerve stimulation (0.2 Hz basal, 10-20 Hz high-frequency trains), Acute depolarization (90 mM K+)
neural_activity: Elevated evoked EJC amplitudes and mEJC frequency (increased release probability) in csw, PTPN11, and dfmr1 mutants; elevated presynaptic pERK fluorescence intensity at rest with reduced activity-dependent increase upon stimulation; elevated neuronal Csw protein levels in dfmr1 nulls
techniques_used: ['Two-electrode voltage-clamp (TEVC)', 'Miniature EJC (mEJC) recording', 'Confocal immunocytochemistry', 'Western blot', 'RNA immunoprecipitation']
summary: Presynaptic motor neurons in csw, PTPN11, and dfmr1 mutants exhibit elevated glutamate release probability and presynaptic pERK signaling; FMRP binds csw mRNA to regulate Csw protein levels.

paper_title: Chronic exposure to odors at naturally occurring concentrations triggers limited plasticity in early stages of Drosophila olfactory processing
experimental_stimulus: Chronic exposure to 1s pulses of E2-hexenal (10^-7) every 20s for 2 days; Test stimuli: E2-hexenal concentration series (10^-10 to 10^-5)
neural_activity: Whole-cell current clamp recordings showed increased odor-evoked membrane depolarization and firing rates specifically to weak stimuli (10^-10 to 10^-9). Responses to moderate/strong stimuli (>10^-8) were unchanged. Input resistance and intrinsic excitability (f-I curves) were unchanged. Unitary ORN-PN synapse strength was unchanged.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested in this study
ecological_context: Stimuli chosen to match concentrations found in natural odor sources (fruit volatiles, ~1 ppb to 10 ppm).
techniques_used: ['Whole-cell current clamp electrophysiology', 'Permutation testing', 'Optogenetic minimal stimulation']
summary: DL5 PNs showed increased sensitivity to weak odor stimuli following chronic exposure, without changes in intrinsic properties or synaptic strength, suggesting central plasticity.

paper_title: Chronic exposure to odors at naturally occurring concentrations triggers limited plasticity in early stages of Drosophila olfactory processing
experimental_stimulus: Chronic exposure to 1s pulses of 2-butanone (10^-4) or E2-hexenal (indirect); Test stimuli: 2-butanone concentration series, odor mixtures (pentyl acetate + 2-butanone)
neural_activity: Whole-cell current clamp showed modest increases in odor-evoked depolarization to weak stimuli (10^-7) under direct exposure. Indirect exposure (E2-hexenal) increased depolarization and post-stimulus hyperpolarization. Mixture responses were stable, indicating lateral inhibition compensated for excitation.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested in this study
ecological_context: Stimuli chosen to match concentrations found in natural odor sources (fruit volatiles, ~13,000 ppb).
summary: VM7 PNs exhibited mild plasticity to weak stimuli and altered post-stimulus dynamics with indirect input, while maintaining stable coding for odor mixtures.

paper_title: Chronic exposure to odors at naturally occurring concentrations triggers limited plasticity in early stages of Drosophila olfactory processing
experimental_stimulus: Chronic exposure to geranyl acetate (10^-4 or 20%); Test stimuli: Geranyl acetate concentration series
neural_activity: Whole-cell current clamp showed no significant change in responses to naturalistic concentrations or high sustained concentrations. Indirect exposure (E2-hexenal) enhanced delayed excitation post-stimulus. Lateral excitatory coupling increased in E2-hexenal flies.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested in this study
ecological_context: Stimuli chosen to match concentrations found in natural odor sources.
summary: VA6 PNs showed no plasticity to direct odor exposure at naturalistic levels, but indirect exposure altered post-stimulus dynamics.

paper_title: Chronic exposure to odors at naturally occurring concentrations triggers limited plasticity in early stages of Drosophila olfactory processing
experimental_stimulus: Chronic exposure to E2-hexenal (10^-7); Test stimuli: E2-hexenal concentration series
neural_activity: Single-sensillum recordings and two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6f) showed no change in spontaneous or odor-evoked firing rates or calcium signals across a wide range of concentrations.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested in this study
ecological_context: Stimuli chosen to match concentrations found in natural odor sources.
summary: ORN sensitivity remained unchanged despite chronic exposure, indicating plasticity is central rather than peripheral.

paper_title: Chronic exposure to odors at naturally occurring concentrations triggers limited plasticity in early stages of Drosophila olfactory processing
experimental_stimulus: Chronic exposure to E2-hexenal or 2-butanone
neural_activity: Confocal microscopy showed inhibitory LN innervation was largely unchanged. Functional lateral excitatory coupling increased in E2-hexenal flies. Global glomerular volume reduced in E2-hexenal flies, but not glomerulus-specific.
behavioral_consequence: Not tested in this study
ecological_context: Stimuli chosen to match concentrations found in natural odor sources.
summary: LN anatomy was stable, but functional lateral excitatory coupling increased, suggesting a circuit mechanism for observed plasticity.

paper_title: Neuron-Specific FMRP Roles in Experience-Dependent Remodeling of Olfactory Brain Innervation during an Early-Life Critical Period
experimental_stimulus: Ethyl butyrate (EB) odorant exposure (0-2 days post-eclosion); Optogenetic cyan light stimulation (515 nm)
neural_activity: VM7 glomerulus innervation volume remodels (reduces) following EB exposure; Optogenetic activation drives similar reduction; FMRP loss in Or42a OSNs impairs remodeling; FMRP overexpression enhances remodeling; Blocking synaptic output (TeTxLc) does not impair remodeling
ecological_context: Early-life critical period adaptation to new sensory environment
techniques_used: ['Confocal imaging of innervation volume (mCD8::GFP)', 'Genetic manipulation (Gal4/UAS, RNAi, mutants)', 'Optogenetics (CsChrimson)', 'Odor exposure (EB)']
summary: Or42a OSNs undergo experience-dependent structural remodeling of VM7 glomerular innervation during an early-life critical period. This process requires FMRP specifically in Or42a OSNs and is driven by Or42a activity, but does not require OSN synaptic output.

paper_title: Neuron-Specific FMRP Roles in Experience-Dependent Remodeling of Olfactory Brain Innervation during an Early-Life Critical Period
experimental_stimulus: Ethyl butyrate (EB) odorant exposure (0-2 days post-eclosion)
neural_activity: Dendritic arborization volume remains largely unchanged despite presynaptic OSN remodeling
summary: VM7 Projection Neurons do not show significant structural remodeling during the Or42a OSN critical period odorant experience.

paper_title: Neuron-Specific FMRP Roles in Experience-Dependent Remodeling of Olfactory Brain Innervation during an Early-Life Critical Period
experimental_stimulus: Ethyl butyrate (EB) odorant exposure (0-2 days post-eclosion)
neural_activity: Silencing (TeTxLc) reduces Or42a OSN VM7 innervation remodeling
summary: GluLN synaptic output is required for normal Or42a OSN critical period remodeling, suggesting glutamatergic signaling mediates lateral modulation.

paper_title: Neuron-Specific FMRP Roles in Experience-Dependent Remodeling of Olfactory Brain Innervation during an Early-Life Critical Period
experimental_stimulus: Ethyl butyrate (EB) odorant exposure (0-2 days post-eclosion)
neural_activity: Silencing (TeTxLc) slightly enhances Or42a OSN innervation remodeling
summary: MB neuron activity has a minor modulatory effect on Or42a OSN remodeling, though interaction terms were not significant in all analyses.

paper_title: Glomerulus-Selective Regulation of a Critical Period for Interneuron Plasticity in the Drosophila Antennal Lobe
experimental_stimulus: Odor exposure to 20% ethyl butyrate (EB) or 5% carbon dioxide (CO2) for 4 days starting 0-12, 24-36, or 48-60 hours after eclosion; or geranyl acetate (GA) exposure.
neural_activity: Silencing Or83b-positive OSNs electrically (UAS-Kir2.1) extends the critical period (CP) for structural plasticity beyond 48 hours. Silencing glomerulus-selective OSNs (Or85a for EB/DM5, Gr21a for CO2/V) extends the CP for structural plasticity only in respective target glomeruli.
behavioral_consequence: Long-term olfactory habituation (LTH) to EB or CO2 (decrement in behavioral response) occurs if odor exposure starts before 48 hours post-eclosion. Silencing OSNs allows odor-induced structural plasticity to persist if exposure starts after 48 hours. GA-induced plasticity shows no critical period regardless of age.
ecological_context: Local control of closure mechanisms can impart evolutionarily adaptive, odorant-specific features to behavioral plasticity. Potentially allows varied critical periods for plasticity to stimuli of different ethological relevance, such as retaining ability to habituate to innately attractive but useless odorants.
techniques_used: ['UAS-Kir2.1 silencing', 'Gal80ts temperature shift', 'Confocal microscopy', 'Y-maze behavioral assay']
summary: OSN activity is required for closing the critical period for structural plasticity. Silencing specific OSN subtypes extends plasticity only in their target glomeruli.

paper_title: Glomerulus-Selective Regulation of a Critical Period for Interneuron Plasticity in the Drosophila Antennal Lobe
experimental_stimulus: Odor exposure to EB, CO2, or GA for 4 days during the critical period (0-48h post-eclosion).
neural_activity: PN processes (arborization) show increased fluorescence (CD8-GFP, Da7-GFP) in odorant-responsive glomeruli (DM5, V, VA6) during the critical period. Glomerular volume increases occur together with PN process elaboration.
behavioral_consequence: PN structural plasticity correlates with long-term olfactory habituation (LTH) to aversive odorants (EB, CO2). No critical period is observed for GA-induced PN plasticity in the VA6 glomerulus.
ecological_context: Structural plasticity in odorant-responsive glomeruli is associated with behavioral habituation to aversive and attractive odors.
techniques_used: ['Confocal microscopy', 'Immunohistochemistry', 'Glomerular volume measurement', 'GAL4/UAS expression (GH146, VPN, Or82a)']
summary: PN processes elaborate in odorant-selective glomeruli during the critical period, contributing to glomerular volume increases associated with habituation.

paper_title: Glomerulus-Selective Regulation of a Critical Period for Interneuron Plasticity in the Drosophila Antennal Lobe
experimental_stimulus: Odor exposure to EB or GA for 4 days. Genetic manipulation of rutabaga expression.
neural_activity: rutabaga (adenylate cyclase) function is required in LN1 neurons for odor-induced PN arborization and glomerular growth. Knockdown of rut in LN1 prevents plasticity.
behavioral_consequence: rutabaga function in LN1 neurons is necessary for long-term olfactory habituation and associated glomerular growth for both aversive (EB) and attractive (GA) odors.
ecological_context: Identical signaling pathways lead to increased inhibition onto odorant-responsive PNs following long-term odorant exposure for both aversive and attractive responses.
summary: rutabaga signaling in GABAergic LN1 neurons is necessary and sufficient for odor-induced elaboration of PN processes and glomerular growth.

paper_title: Activity-Dependent Remodeling of Drosophila Olfactory Sensory Neuron Brain Innervation during an Early-Life Critical Period
experimental_stimulus: Ethyl butyrate (EB) odorant exposure (15% or 25% v/v) during early-life critical period (0-2 days post-eclosion) vs. vehicle control (mineral oil)
neural_activity: Dose-dependent reduction in VM7 glomerular innervation volume; rapid synapse elimination (reduced BRP punctae and T-bar density) and axon retraction. No change in OSN cell body survival or Or42a receptor expression levels. Remodeling requires Or42a receptor activation, OSN electrical activity, and NMDA receptor signaling. Not dependent on GABAb R3 signaling or Wnt signaling.
ecological_context: Early-life critical period for brain circuit refinement
techniques_used: ['Confocal microscopy', 'Immunohistochemistry', 'Western blot', 'qRT-PCR', 'STaR labeling', 'Transmission electron microscopy (miniSOG)', 'Genetic manipulation (RNAi, mutants, TeTxLC, Kir2.1)']
summary: Or42a OSNs undergo experience-dependent structural remodeling of VM7 glomerular innervation during the early-life critical period. Exposure to EB odorant causes dose-dependent synapse elimination and axon retraction, which is reversible upon odor removal and requires Or42a receptor activity, OSN electrical activity, and NMDA receptor signaling but does not involve changes in receptor expression or GABAb signaling.

paper_title: Central Adaptation to Odorants Depends on PI3K Levels in Local Interneurons of the Antennal Lobe
experimental_stimulus: High concentration odorants (Ethyl Butyrate 10^-1 v/v or Isoamyl Acetate 10^-1 v/v) for 4-5 days of adulthood
neural_activity: Morphological changes in branching and synaptic reporter expression (increase with PI3K, decrease with PI3KDN/GSK3); no direct electrophysiological activity recorded
behavioral_consequence: Central adaptation (reduced avoidance to odorant) occurred normally despite synaptic manipulation in these neurons
ecological_context: Exposure to high odorant concentrations (model for experience-dependent plasticity)
summary: Manipulation of synapse numbers in Mushroom Body neurons does not prevent olfactory central adaptation.

paper_title: Central Adaptation to Odorants Depends on PI3K Levels in Local Interneurons of the Antennal Lobe
experimental_stimulus: High concentration odorants (Ethyl Butyrate 10^-1 v/v or Isoamyl Acetate 10^-1 v/v) for 4-5 days of adulthood
neural_activity: Morphological changes in size and GFP signal intensity (increase with PI3K, decrease with PI3KDN/GSK3); no direct electrophysiological activity recorded
behavioral_consequence: Central adaptation (reduced avoidance to odorant) occurred normally despite synaptic manipulation in these neurons
ecological_context: Exposure to high odorant concentrations (model for experience-dependent plasticity)
summary: Manipulation of synapse numbers in Projection Neurons does not prevent olfactory central adaptation.

paper_title: Central Adaptation to Odorants Depends on PI3K Levels in Local Interneurons of the Antennal Lobe
cell_type: Local Interneurons (GH298 inhibitory and krasavietz excitatory)
experimental_stimulus: High concentration odorants (Ethyl Butyrate, Isoamyl Acetate, or Benzaldehyde 10^-1 v/v) for 4-5 days of adulthood
neural_activity: Synapse number manipulated via PI3K constructs (increase with PI3K/AKT, decrease with PI3KDN/GSK3); no direct electrophysiological activity recorded
behavioral_consequence: Manipulating synapses in either subset individually prevented central adaptation (flies maintained strong avoidance like controls). Simultaneous manipulation in both subsets restored central adaptation (reduced avoidance) and odorant-specific glomerulus enlargement (DM2). Cross-adaptation was absent in both prevented and restored conditions
ecological_context: Exposure to high odorant concentrations (model for sensory perception and central adaptation)
techniques_used: ['Genetic manipulation (UAS-PI3K/GSK3)', 'Behavioral assay (T-maze olfactory choice)', 'Confocal microscopy (glomerular volume measurement)']
summary: Central adaptation requires a balanced inhibition/excitation ratio in Local Interneurons; synaptic manipulation in either inhibitory or excitatory subsets prevents adaptation, while simultaneous manipulation restores it.

paper_title: Hunger- and thirst-sensing neurons modulate a neuroendocrine network to coordinate sugar and water ingestion
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (Chrimson + 660 nm LED); dILP3 or amontillado RNAi knockdown
neural_activity: ISNs inhibit BiT (ISN activation increases ArcLight fluorescence in BiT, indicating BiT hyperpolarization). ISNs are peptidergic neurons that release dILP3.
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of dILP3 or amontillado in ISNs caused a decrease in sugar ingestion and an increase in water ingestion. ISN activation (via light) inhibits BiT.
ecological_context: Regulates the coordination of competing needs for food and water to maintain internal nutrient homeostasis based on hunger and thirst states.
techniques_used: ['RNAi screen', 'Temporal consumption assay', 'Two-photon voltage imaging (ArcLight)', 'Electron microscopy connectome (FAFB/FlyWire)', 'Trans-Tango']
summary: ISNs release the neuropeptide dILP3 to inhibit the downstream BiT neuron; loss of dILP3 in ISNs reduces sugar ingestion and increases water ingestion.

paper_title: Hunger- and thirst-sensing neurons modulate a neuroendocrine network to coordinate sugar and water ingestion
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (Chrimson + 635 nm laser); nSynaptobrevin (nSyb) RNAi knockdown; Optogenetic stimulation of ISNs
neural_activity: BiT becomes hyperpolarized upon ISN stimulation (voltage imaging). BiT inhibits Insulin Producing Cells (IPCs) (calcium imaging).
behavioral_consequence: Acute optogenetic activation of BiT decreased sugar ingestion and increased water ingestion. Reducing synaptic transmission (nSyb RNAi) increased sugar ingestion and decreased water ingestion.
ecological_context: Acts as a critical node in the ISN network to coordinate nutrient-specific ingestion based on internal nutritional needs.
techniques_used: ['Split-Gal4', 'Optogenetics', 'Two-photon voltage imaging (ArcLight)', 'Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6s)', 'Temporal consumption assay', 'RNAi']
summary: BiT reciprocally regulates sugar and water ingestion; activation reduces sugar and increases water, while inhibition reverses this effect.

paper_title: Hunger- and thirst-sensing neurons modulate a neuroendocrine network to coordinate sugar and water ingestion
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (Chrimson); Optogenetic stimulation of BiT
neural_activity: BiT inhibits IPCs (reduced calcium signal upon BiT stimulation, measured via GCaMP6s).
behavioral_consequence: Acute optogenetic activation of IPCs increased sucrose ingestion and decreased water ingestion.
ecological_context: Integrates multiple signals of nutrient status to regulate feeding and metabolism; contributes to sugar and water ingestion coordination.
summary: IPCs are inhibited by BiT; their acute activation promotes sucrose ingestion and limits water ingestion.

paper_title: Hunger- and thirst-sensing neurons modulate a neuroendocrine network to coordinate sugar and water ingestion
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic stimulation of ISNs; Optogenetic activation of CCHa2R-RA; nSyb RNAi knockdown
neural_activity: Robust calcium responses in CCHa2R-RA neurites upon ISN stimulation (measured via GCaMP6s).
behavioral_consequence: Activation of CCHa2R-RA neurons decreased water ingestion but did not change sugar ingestion. Inhibition increased water ingestion but did not change sugar ingestion.
ecological_context: Modulates water ingestion specifically as part of the downstream peptidergic network regulating nutrient-specific needs.
summary: CCHa2R-RA neurons receive input from ISNs and modulate water ingestion but not sugar ingestion.

paper_title: Hunger- and thirst-sensing neurons modulate a neuroendocrine network to coordinate sugar and water ingestion
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic stimulation of ISNs; Optogenetic activation of CCAP; nSyb RNAi knockdown
neural_activity: Activated upon ISN optogenetic stimulation (measured via GCaMP7b).
behavioral_consequence: Activation of CCAP neurons decreased water ingestion and increased sugar ingestion. Silencing decreased sugar ingestion and increased water ingestion.
ecological_context: Reciprocally regulates sugar and water ingestion downstream of ISNs to coordinate consumption based on internal needs.
techniques_used: ['Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP7b)', 'Optogenetics', 'Temporal consumption assay', 'RNAi', 'Electron microscopy connectome']
summary: CCAP neurons are activated by ISNs and reciprocally regulate sugar and water ingestion similarly to the ISNs.

paper_title: A rise-to-threshold process for a relative-value decision
experimental_stimulus: Egg-laying sequence (ovulation, search period), varying substrate sucrose concentrations (0, 200, 500 mM), optogenetic stimulation (CsChrimson), hyperpolarization (Kir2.1*)
neural_activity: Calcium signal (GCaMP) dips during ovulation, drifts up and down during search, peaks at ~0.35 ΔF/F before abdomen bend. Signal rise rate tracks relative substrate value (faster on higher-value/low-sucrose substrates). Membrane potential and spike rate increase on high-value substrates. Optogenetic activation induces rise-to-threshold behavior.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation triggers abdomen bend and egg deposition if egg available (threshold ~0.32 ΔF/F). Inhibition (Kir2.1, GtACR1, ablation) suppresses egg laying completely. Gentle hyperpolarization increases search duration and increases preference for higher relative-value substrate.
ecological_context: Egg-laying site selection is critical for progeny survival; flies search for substrates mimicking rotting fruit (ethanol, acetic acid, low sucrose) to optimize egg-laying environment.
techniques_used: ['Two-photon calcium imaging', 'Patch-clamp electrophysiology', 'Optogenetics (CsChrimson, GtACR1, Kir2.1)', 'High-throughput behavioral tracking', 'DeepLabCut pose estimation']
summary: oviDNs exhibit a rise-to-threshold calcium signal that guides egg-laying substrate choice; activity peaks before egg deposition and correlates with substrate relative value, with optogenetic threshold control determining motor program initiation.

paper_title: A rise-to-threshold process for a relative-value decision
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic inhibition (GtACR1)
neural_activity: Anatomically characterized as cholinergic (ChAT positive) neurons providing synaptic input to oviDNs; part of a recurrent circuit.
behavioral_consequence: Inhibition markedly reduces the total number of eggs laid by flies.
ecological_context: Required for egg-laying behavior critical for progeny survival.
summary: oviENs are cholinergic input neurons to oviDNs; their activity is required for egg laying as silencing them markedly reduces total eggs laid.

paper_title: A rise-to-threshold process for a relative-value decision
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic inhibition (GtACR1)
neural_activity: Anatomically characterized as tyrosine hydroxylase (TH) positive neurons providing synaptic input to oviDNs; part of a recurrent circuit.
behavioral_consequence: Inhibition markedly reduces the total number of eggs laid by flies.
ecological_context: Required for egg-laying behavior critical for progeny survival.
summary: Group U cells are TH-positive input neurons to oviDNs forming a recurrent circuit; silencing them markedly reduces egg laying.

paper_title: A rise-to-threshold process for a relative-value decision
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic inhibition (GtACR1)
neural_activity: Anatomically characterized as a mix of ChAT and TH positive neurons providing synaptic input to oviDNs; part of a recurrent circuit.
behavioral_consequence: Inhibition markedly reduces the total number of eggs laid by flies.
ecological_context: Required for egg-laying behavior critical for progeny survival.
summary: Group G cells are input neurons to oviDNs (mixed transmitter identity) forming a recurrent circuit; silencing them markedly reduces egg laying.

paper_title: Flexible neural control of transition points within the egg-laying behavioral sequence in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Substrate firmness (agarose concentrations 0.75% to 2.5%)
neural_activity: Identified as mechanosensory neurons (NOMPC staining); function tested via inhibition.
behavioral_consequence: Silencing increased transition probability from bending to burrowing regardless of substrate firmness. Reduced burrowing duration and persistence. Increased aberrant egg deposition on chamber walls. Reduced subterraneous egg deposition on firm substrates.
ecological_context: Tactile feedback modulates egg deposition sequence adaptively to substrate firmness to achieve subterraneous placement.
summary: ATB neurons provide tactile feedback during egg laying. Silencing them causes indiscriminate transition to burrowing and reduced ability to achieve subterraneous deposition on firm substrates.

paper_title: Flexible neural control of transition points within the egg-laying behavioral sequence in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Passage of egg through uterus into ovipositor
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6f) showed increased activity after egg entered ovipositor, returned to baseline after expulsion. No response to ovipositor extrusion without egg.
behavioral_consequence: Silencing reduced progression from burrowing to egg expulsion and egg output, causing spontaneous egg drop. Optogenetic activation prolonged burrowing beyond egg expulsion and delayed transition to reset.
ecological_context: PU activity signals egg position to drive burrowing persistence; decreased activity signals expulsion completion to initiate reset phase.
techniques_used: ['Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6f)', 'Optogenetic activation (CsChrimson)', 'Genetic silencing (Kir2.1)', 'High-resolution behavioral filming']
summary: PU neurons sense egg passage into ovipositor. Their activation promotes burrowing persistence, while inactivation signals expulsion completion to transition to the reset phase.

paper_title: Flexible neural control of transition points within the egg-laying behavioral sequence in Drosophila
cell_type: CMU neurons (circular muscle of the uterus motor neurons)
experimental_stimulus: Natural burrowing/expulsion; Optogenetic stimulation
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6f) showed acute increase in activity concurrent with egg expulsion.
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation reliably induced egg expulsion. Silencing prevented expulsion, causing 89% of eggs to be dropped spontaneously without burrowing.
ecological_context: CMU neuron activity is necessary to progress from burrowing to egg expulsion, the final decision point in the sequence.
summary: CMU neurons are uterine motor neurons that enact egg expulsion. Their activation triggers expulsion, while silencing blocks progression from burrowing to expulsion.

paper_title: Assessing the cognitive status of Drosophila by the value-based feeding decision
experimental_stimulus: Two-choice feeding assay: sucrose vs arabinose, sucrose vs plain water
neural_activity: Suppression of synaptic transmission using temperature-sensitive Shibirets (Shits)
behavioral_consequence: Competence to make proper value-based feeding decision (VBFD) was not affected
ecological_context: Evaluation of nutritional values of food substances to keep nutritional homeostasis
summary: Suppression of Gr5a-positive sweet sensing neurons did not affect the flies' ability to make proper VBFD between nutritious and non-nutritious sugars.

paper_title: Assessing the cognitive status of Drosophila by the value-based feeding decision
experimental_stimulus: Two-choice feeding assay: sucrose vs arabinose
neural_activity: Suppression of synaptic transmission using temperature-sensitive Shibirets (Shits)
behavioral_consequence: Slightly blunted the complex VBFD (reduced preference for sucrose)
ecological_context: Modulating feeding decisions based on nutritional value of sugar solutions
summary: Suppression of Gr66a-positive bitter-sensing neurons slightly blunted the complex VBFD efficacy.

paper_title: Assessing the cognitive status of Drosophila by the value-based feeding decision
experimental_stimulus: Two-choice feeding assay: sucrose vs arabinose
neural_activity: Suppression of synaptic transmission using temperature-sensitive Shibirets (Shits); reduced cell death in neurodegeneration model
behavioral_consequence: Moderate changes in VBFD; sucrose-ingesting population reduced to ~70% (control ~90%); DNIC-1 reduced cleaved Cas-3-positive cells in MB
ecological_context: Plays critical roles in diverse behaviors including olfaction, associative learning, sleep, and feeding; integration of hunger/satiety signals
summary: Silencing MB neurons moderately impaired complex VBFD. DNIC-1 treatment reduced cell death in the MB region during polyQ neurodegeneration.

paper_title: Assessing the cognitive status of Drosophila by the value-based feeding decision
experimental_stimulus: Two-choice feeding assay: sucrose vs arabinose
neural_activity: Suppression of synaptic transmission using temperature-sensitive Shibirets (Shits)
behavioral_consequence: Making of proper VBFD remained efficient
ecological_context: Part of central brain pathways dictating feeding decisions
summary: Perturbation of dopaminergic rewarding system neurons did not affect the making of proper VBFD.

paper_title: Assessing the cognitive status of Drosophila by the value-based feeding decision
experimental_stimulus: Two-choice feeding assay: sucrose vs arabinose
neural_activity: Suppression of synaptic transmission using temperature-sensitive Shibirets (Shits)
behavioral_consequence: Making of proper VBFD remained efficient
ecological_context: Part of central brain pathways dictating feeding decisions
summary: Perturbation of feeding-promoting NPF signals did not affect the making of proper VBFD.

paper_title: Assessing the cognitive status of Drosophila by the value-based feeding decision
experimental_stimulus: Two-choice feeding assay: sucrose vs arabinose
neural_activity: Suppression of synaptic transmission using temperature-sensitive Shibirets (Shits); loss of Gr43a receptors (mutants)
behavioral_consequence: Efficacy of complex VBFD was not affected
ecological_context: Sensing the levels of hemolymph fructose and modulating food consumption
summary: Loss of Gr43a receptors or inhibition of Gr43-positive neurons did not affect the efficacy of complex VBFD.

paper_title: Assessing the cognitive status of Drosophila by the value-based feeding decision
experimental_stimulus: Two-choice feeding assay: sucrose vs arabinose, sucrose vs water
neural_activity: Ectopic expression of 41Q-HA polypeptides (aggregates accumulating in brain)
behavioral_consequence: Progressive impairment of complex VBFD after 15 days (50% chose sucrose); simple VBFD and sweet sensation deteriorated
ecological_context: Model of neurodegenerative disorders affecting cognitive status
techniques_used: ['TARGET expression system', 'VBFD assay (two-choice feeding)', 'Immunohistochemistry (anti-HA, cleaved Cas-3)']
summary: Adult neurons expressing 41Q-HA showed progressive impairment of VBFD and sweet sensation over time, associated with polyQ aggregate accumulation and cell death.

paper_title: Acetic acid activates distinct taste pathways in Drosophila to elicit opposing, state-dependent feeding responses
experimental_stimulus: Acetic acid (1-10%), Sucrose
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6f) revealed strong GCaMP activation in both fed and starved states; peak response was about half of that observed with 500 mM sucrose.
behavioral_consequence: Genetic silencing (Kir2.1) or deletion of sugar receptors abolished proboscis extension response (PER) to acetic acid in starved flies. Required for the appetitive response to acetic acid.
ecological_context: Acetic acid from fermenting fruit is a valuable caloric resource; this pathway allows consumption during starvation when energy is needed.
summary: Sugar-sensing neurons are activated by acetic acid and mediate the appetitive feeding response in starved flies, enabling utilization of acetic acid as a calorie source.

paper_title: Acetic acid activates distinct taste pathways in Drosophila to elicit opposing, state-dependent feeding responses
experimental_stimulus: Acetic acid (1-5%), Lobeline
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP6f) showed significant GCaMP activation; S-b subclass exhibited significant peak response. Peak response was about 30% of that obtained with 1 mM lobeline.
behavioral_consequence: Genetic silencing (Kir2.1) eliminated acetic acid-induced suppression of sucrose-evoked PER in fed flies (reduced aversion) and enhanced PER to acetic acid in starved flies. Mediates the aversive response in fed flies.
ecological_context: Acetic acid can be toxic; this pathway prevents consumption when flies are fed to protect against potential toxicity.
summary: Bitter-sensing neurons are activated by acetic acid and mediate the aversive response in fed flies, preventing consumption due to toxicity concerns.

paper_title: Acetic acid activates distinct taste pathways in Drosophila to elicit opposing, state-dependent feeding responses
experimental_stimulus: Acetic acid
neural_activity: Not directly measured (organs surgically removed).
behavioral_consequence: Surgical removal of antennae and maxillary palps did not affect PER to acetic acid in fed or starved flies.
ecological_context: Confirms that the response to acetic acid is mediated by taste, not smell, distinguishing gustatory processing of the metabolite.
summary: Olfactory organs are not required for the proboscis extension response to acetic acid, indicating the response is gustatory.

paper_title: Acetic acid activates distinct taste pathways in Drosophila to elicit opposing, state-dependent feeding responses
experimental_stimulus: Acetic acid, various tastants
neural_activity: Two-photon calcium imaging showed broad responses inversely related to osmolarity; acetic acid did not activate neurons beyond the level elicited by PEG (osmolarity control).
behavioral_consequence: Acetic acid does not activate water-sensing neurons beyond osmolarity-sensing mechanism; not mediating specific acetic acid detection.
ecological_context: Rules out osmolarity as the mechanism for acetic acid detection in taste neurons.
summary: Water-sensing neurons respond to acetic acid solely via osmolarity, not specific acid recognition.

paper_title: Dietary L-Glu sensing by enteroendocrine cells adjusts food intake via modulating gut PYY/NPF secretion
experimental_stimulus: Dietary L-glutamate (1%), High-protein diet (5% yeast extract/paste), High-sugar diet (10% sucrose), High-fat diet (25% coconut oil)
neural_activity: mGluR-mediated deceleration of intracellular Ca2+ oscillations (GCaMP6f imaging); Increased retention of NPF peptide in dense-core vesicles (immunostaining/pANF-EMD reporter); Reduced peptide secretion
behavioral_consequence: Ablation (esgP>scRNAi, prosTCD>hid) or NPF knockdown (tap1.3-B>NPFRNAi) significantly increases food intake (CAFE/dye assays); Activation (TrpA1) decreases food intake; Inhibition (TNT) increases food intake
ecological_context: Mated female flies assess food quality (protein/AA availability) to regulate appetite and adapt to nutritional environments; mechanism conserved with mammalian PYY
techniques_used: ['Ca2+ imaging (GCaMP6f)', 'Immunostaining', 'Genetic manipulation (RNAi, Gal4, CRISPR, TNT, TrpA1, hid)', 'Feeding assays (CAFE, MAFE, dye-based)', 'Peptide injection']

paper_title: Dietary L-Glu sensing by enteroendocrine cells adjusts food intake via modulating gut PYY/NPF secretion
experimental_stimulus: Dietary L-Glu/High-protein (associated with low systemic NPF), Systemic NPF injection
neural_activity: Activity inhibited by L-Glu/High-protein diet (CaLexA intensity); Excited by systemic NPF injection (CaLexA intensity); Dopaminergic identity (co-labeling with Ddc, TH); Axons project to Subesophageal Zone (SEZ)
behavioral_consequence: Ablation (hid) or inhibition (shits) increases food intake; Activation (TrpA1) inhibits food intake; NPFR knockdown increases food intake, blocking NPF-mediated feeding suppression
ecological_context: Relay gut nutrient signals (protein status) to the brain to control feeding behavior; Analogous to mammalian vagal afferents
techniques_used: ['Ca2+ imaging (CaLexA)', 'Immunostaining', 'Genetic manipulation (RNAi, shits, TrpA1, hid, Gal4)', 'Trans-tango mapping', 'Feeding assays']

paper_title: Dietary L-Glu sensing by enteroendocrine cells adjusts food intake via modulating gut PYY/NPF secretion
experimental_stimulus: Indirect activation via NPFRENS neuron synapses
neural_activity: Synaptically connected to NPFRENS neurons (identified via trans-tango labeling); Identified as potential motor neurons and interneurons known to control feeding
behavioral_consequence: Part of circuit inhibiting food intake when NPFRENS neurons are activated
ecological_context: SEZ is a known brain center for feeding control

paper_title: Failure to mate enhances investment in behaviors that may promote mating reward and impairs the ability to cope with stressors via a subpopulation of Neuropeptide F receptor neurons
experimental_stimulus: Repeated sexual rejection (non-receptive females for 4 days); Optogenetic activation (3x 5 min/day for 2 days)
neural_activity: Disinhibited in rejected males (inferred from reduced NPF signaling); Optogenetic activation induces activity
behavioral_consequence: Increased sensitivity to starvation and oxidative stress; Knockdown of NPFR abrogates rejection-induced starvation sensitivity; Full population activation increases starvation sensitivity but does not alter male-male social interactions
ecological_context: Mediates stress response to failure in obtaining sexual reward; balances mating drive with survival costs in dynamic social environments
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics (csChrimson)', 'RNAi (NPFR knockdown)', 'Starvation resistance assay', 'Oxidative stress assay', 'FlyBowl behavioral tracking']
summary: NPFR neurons are disinhibited in rejected males, mediating sensitivity to starvation stress; optogenetic activation of the general NPFR population increases starvation sensitivity but does not induce social interaction changes.

paper_title: Failure to mate enhances investment in behaviors that may promote mating reward and impairs the ability to cope with stressors via a subpopulation of Neuropeptide F receptor neurons
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation (3x 5 min/day for 2 days)
neural_activity: Activation of a subset of 22–26 cells (specifically 12–16 cells negative for NPF and Dh44)
behavioral_consequence: Increased sensitivity to starvation; Increased male-male social arousal (higher touch/chase frequency, longer duration, chaining); Reduced social clustering; Does not require Tachykinin neuropeptide release for starvation sensitivity
ecological_context: Mediates 'frustrative-like' stress response to promote mating reward (aggression/persistence) while impairing ability to resist starvation stress
summary: Activation of a subpopulation of NPFR neurons co-expressing Tachykinin (NPFRTK) increases sensitivity to starvation and promotes social arousal and aggression behaviors mimicking the rejection state.

paper_title: Ionotropic Receptor-dependent moist and dry cells control hygrosensation in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Changes in relative humidity (switch from 7% to 90% RH and 90% to 7% RH)
neural_activity: Robust, non-adapting increases in GCaMP6m fluorescence upon switching to high humidity (90% RH); robust decreases upon switching to low humidity (7%). Responses eliminated in Ir68a, Ir25a, and Ir93a mutants.
behavioral_consequence: Loss of Ir68a reduces dry-seeking behavior in hydrated flies in a humidity gradient. In dehydrated flies, loss of Ir68a alone does not abolish moist-seeking, but combined loss of Ir68a and Ir40a abolishes moist-seeking.
ecological_context: Hygrosensation is used by Drosophila to avoid desiccation; hydrated flies prefer dry environments while dehydrated flies prefer moister environments.
summary: Moist cells (Ir68a-expressing neurons) in the antennal sacculus are activated by high humidity air (90% RH) via calcium imaging. They are required for dry-seeking by hydrated flies and contribute to moist-seeking by dehydrated flies alongside dry cells.

paper_title: Ionotropic Receptor-dependent moist and dry cells control hygrosensation in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Changes in relative humidity (switch from 7% to 90% RH and 90% to 7% RH)
neural_activity: Humidity-dependent calcium responses opposite to moist cells (activated by low humidity/dry air). Responses eliminated in Ir40a, Ir25a, and Ir93a mutants but unaffected by Ir68a loss.
behavioral_consequence: Loss of Ir40a reduces dry-seeking behavior in hydrated flies in a humidity gradient. In dehydrated flies, loss of Ir40a alone does not abolish moist-seeking, but combined loss of Ir68a and Ir40a abolishes moist-seeking.
ecological_context: Hygrosensation is used by Drosophila to avoid desiccation; hydrated flies prefer dry environments while dehydrated flies prefer moister environments.
summary: Dry cells (Ir40a-expressing neurons) in the antennal sacculus are activated by low humidity air (7% RH) via calcium imaging. They are required for dry-seeking by hydrated flies and contribute to moist-seeking by dehydrated flies alongside moist cells.

paper_title: A Novel Assay Reveals Hygrotactic Behavior in Drosophila
cell_type: Hygroreceptors in the third antennal segment
experimental_stimulus: Moist air / humidity gradient (water source proximity)
neural_activity: Not measured; functional necessity established via ablation
behavioral_consequence: Required for hygrotactic behavior (aggregation to water) and moisture-induced proboscis extension reflex (PER) in thirsty flies. Ablation abolishes these behaviors.
ecological_context: Locating water sources in thirst status; critical for water maintenance and survival.
techniques_used: ['Sensory organ ablation', 'Hygrotaxis assay', 'Proboscis extension reflex (PER) assay']
summary: Hygroreceptors in the third antennal segment are necessary for thirsty flies to locate water via hygrotaxis and exhibit PER, but are not required for moisture avoidance in hydrated flies.

paper_title: A Novel Assay Reveals Hygrotactic Behavior in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Moist air / humidity gradient
neural_activity: Not measured; functional necessity established via ablation
behavioral_consequence: Required for avoidance of moist air in hydrated flies (T-maze). Not required for water attraction in thirsty flies.
ecological_context: Avoiding moist air when hydrated; adaptive response to body osmolality status.
summary: Hygroreceptors in the arista mediate aversive behavior toward moist air in hydrated flies, distinct from the attraction mediated by the third antennal segment in thirsty flies.

paper_title: A Novel Assay Reveals Hygrotactic Behavior in Drosophila
cell_type: Mushroom Body α/β surface and posterior neurons (MB α/βsp)
experimental_stimulus: Moist air / humidity (thirsty vs hydrated states)
neural_activity: Synaptic output silenced using tetanus toxin light chain (TNT); expression confirmed via immunohistochemistry
behavioral_consequence: Required for hygrotactic behavior in thirsty flies and moisture-aversive behavior in hydrated flies. Silencing does not affect thirst perception (drinking, weight loss) or locomotor ability.
ecological_context: High-order brain center receiving and processing humidity stimuli to drive adaptive behaviors based on body water status (osmolality).
techniques_used: ['Neural silencing screen (Gal4 > TNT)', 'Immunohistochemistry (mCD8::GFP)', 'Hygrotaxis assay', 'T-maze humidity choice assay', 'Water-drinking behavior assay']
summary: MB α/βsp neurons integrate humidity stimuli to elicit both moisture-attractive behavior in thirsty flies and moisture-aversive behavior in hydrated flies.

paper_title: A Novel Assay Reveals Hygrotactic Behavior in Drosophila
cell_type: Olfactory Sensory Neurons (OSNs) and Projection Neurons (PNs)
experimental_stimulus: Moist air (tested for role in moisture sensation)
neural_activity: Synaptic output silenced using TNT (Orco-Gal4, GH146-Gal4)
behavioral_consequence: Blocking OSNs or PNs did not affect hygrotactic behavior. Deletion of ORCO did not affect behavior.
ecological_context: Olfactory system does not appear to participate in moisture sensation for hygrotactic behavior.
techniques_used: ['Neural silencing (Orco-Gal4, GH146-Gal4 > TNT)', 'ORCO mutant analysis', 'Hygrotaxis assay']
summary: Blocking olfactory neural circuits (OSNs/PNs) or deleting the odorant co-receptor ORCO does not impair hygrotactic behavior, suggesting olfaction is not involved in moisture sensation.

paper_title: A Novel Assay Reveals Hygrotactic Behavior in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Moist air / humidity
neural_activity: Synaptic output silenced using TNT (17d-Gal4)
behavioral_consequence: Did not affect hygrotactic behavior.
summary: Mushroom Body α/β core neurons are not required for hygrotactic behavior, unlike the α/β surface and posterior neurons.

paper_title: Graded Encoding of Food Odor Value in the Drosophila Brain
cell_type: Drosophila Neuropeptide F (dNPF) neurons (specifically a subset of 4 large dNPF-positive neurons)
experimental_stimulus: Complex food odors (yeast, banana, apple, mango, lemon, apple cider vinegar) and nonfood odors (rubber, pine needle extract, motor oil, male pheromone cVA, monomolecular yeast components, synthetic monomolecular odors)
neural_activity: In vivo two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP3) showed amplitude of food-odor-evoked activity strongly correlated with food-odor attractiveness. Activity levels were significantly higher for food odors than nonfood odors. Activity increased significantly in starved flies compared to fed flies for food odors.
behavioral_consequence: Inactivation (KIR2.1 at 30°C) or genetic ablation (hid using dNPF2-Gal4/cry-Gal80) of these neurons abolished attraction to food odors (yeast) in starved flies. Silencing dNPF receptors (npfr1dsRNA) abolished attraction. Optogenetic activation (dTRPA1 at 30°C) in fed flies increased attraction to normally attractive odors (acv) and promoted attraction to normally aversive odors (ethyl acetate, 2-methylbutanol). Activity levels predicted preference when two odors competed.
ecological_context: Food-seeking behavior; evaluating potential food sources and selecting the best options based on internal hunger state and odor value.
techniques_used: ['two-photon calcium imaging', 'genetic inactivation (KIR2.1)', 'genetic ablation (hid)', 'genetic activation (dTRPA1)', 'receptor knockdown (npfr1dsRNA)', 'behavioral olfactometer assays']
summary: A subset of 4 large dNPF neurons encodes food-odor value, with calcium activity amplitude correlating with behavioral attractiveness and modulated by hunger; manipulating these neurons alters food-odor attraction.

paper_title: Graded Encoding of Food Odor Value in the Drosophila Brain
experimental_stimulus: Food odors (yeast, banana, etc.) and monomolecular odors (acetaldehyde, 2-methylbutanol, etc.)
neural_activity: In vivo two-photon calcium imaging (GCaMP3) showed odor-specific response patterns that clustered by odor identity rather than food category. Activity magnitude and patterns did not change significantly with starvation state or correlate with behavioral attractiveness.
behavioral_consequence: Not directly manipulated for causality in this study (correlative imaging only); used as a contrast to dNPF neurons regarding value encoding.
ecological_context: Processing unique odor identity for olfactory perception.
summary: MB Kenyon cells encode odor identity with specific activity patterns but do not correlate with food-odor attractiveness or hunger state.

paper_title: A G-Protein-Coupled Neuropeptide Y-Like Receptor Suppresses Behavioral and Sensory Response to Multiple Stressful Stimuli in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Fructose (sugar), 40°C heat probe, BITC (benzyl-isothiocyanate), Capsaicin (via ectopic rat TRPV1 expression)
neural_activity: Fructose stimulation induces Ca2+ influx in thoracic PAIN neurons (measured by YC2.1 and G-CaMP imaging). NPFR1 overexpression attenuates this Ca2+ influx. Capsaicin-induced Ca2+ influx via TRPV1 is attenuated by NPFR1 coexpression (in HEK293 cells and larvae).
behavioral_consequence: Knockdown of NPFR1 triggers precocious sugar-averse grouping in feeding larvae. Overexpression of NPFR1 suppresses sugar-averse migration, clumping, and burrowing in postfeeding larvae. Overexpression suppresses rolling response to 40°C heat (delayed response from <1s to >3s). Overexpression suppresses BITC avoidance in adults. Overexpression suppresses capsaicin-averse migration/aggregation in TRPV1-expressing larvae. Overexpression suppresses PKA-induced sugar aversion.
ecological_context: PAIN-mediated sugar aversion drives postfeeding larvae out of aquatic feeding habitats (e.g., fallen overripe fruits) to soil, thereby preventing immobile pupae from microbial killing and drowning in liquid food.
techniques_used: ['In vivo calcium imaging (confocal laser scanning microscopy, YC2.1, G-CaMP)', 'Behavioral assays (migration, clumping, thermonociception, two-choice preference)', 'Immunohistochemistry', 'HEK293 transfection and calcium imaging (for mechanism validation)']
summary: PAIN neurons respond to sugar, heat, and chemical stressors with Ca2+ influx. NPFR1 expression in these neurons suppresses these responses, preventing sugar-averse migration, clumping, burrowing, and nociceptive behaviors in larvae and adults.

paper_title: Larval Defense against Attack from Parasitoid Wasps Requires Nociceptive Neurons
cell_type: Class IV neurons (multi-dendritic nociceptors: ddaC, v'ada, vdaB)
experimental_stimulus: Parasitoid wasp (Leptopilina boulardi) ovipositor attack; Artificial heat (32°C water droplet via dTRPA1-A)
neural_activity: Not measured
behavioral_consequence: Silencing class IV neurons (UAS-TNT) abolished nocifensive escape locomotion during wasp attacks; thermogenetic activation of 5-10 neurons triggered nocifensive escape locomotion
ecological_context: Defense against parasitoid wasp predation; nocifensive escape locomotion associated with reduced mortality (50%) compared to cuticle penetration rates (71%)
summary: Class IV neurons are necessary for nocifensive escape locomotion in response to natural wasp attacks, with activation of 5-10 neurons sufficient to trigger the behavior.

paper_title: Optogenetic Activation of the fruitless-Labeled Circuitry in Drosophila subobscura Males Induces Mating Motor Acts
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation via 632 nm red light (CsChrimson-mVenus expressed under native fru promoter)
neural_activity: Light-dependent activation of CsChrimson (visualized by mVenus fluorescence); no direct neural recording (e.g., electrophysiology) performed
behavioral_consequence: Induced mating motor acts including abdominal bending (attempted copulation), bilateral wing extension, and regurgitation (nuptial gift transfer)
ecological_context: Male courtship behavior in D. subobscura, specifically the unique nuptial gift transfer not shared by D. melanogaster
summary: Optogenetic activation of the fru-labeled circuitry in D. subobscura males induces specific mating motor acts, including nuptial gift regurgitation, demonstrating the circuit's sufficiency for these behaviors.

paper_title: Optogenetic Activation of the fruitless-Labeled Circuitry in Drosophila subobscura Males Induces Mating Motor Acts
cell_type: M neurons (Visual interneurons in optic lobe)
experimental_stimulus: None (Anatomical characterization only)
neural_activity: None (Anatomical characterization only)
behavioral_consequence: None (Function not tested in this study)
ecological_context: Vision is indispensable for successful mating in D. subobscura
summary: M neurons are anatomically expanded in D. subobscura males compared to D. melanogaster (2.5x more); they are present in both sexes in D. subobscura (unlike D. melanogaster where they are male-specific).

paper_title: Optogenetic Activation of the fruitless-Labeled Circuitry in Drosophila subobscura Males Induces Mating Motor Acts
experimental_stimulus: None (Anatomical and neurochemical characterization only)
neural_activity: None (Anatomical and neurochemical characterization only)
behavioral_consequence: None (Function not tested in this study)
ecological_context: Involved in contact chemical pheromone processing (inferred from established role in D. melanogaster circuitry)
summary: mAL cluster neurons in D. subobscura are GABAergic and exhibit sexually dimorphic neurite projections in the SOG.

paper_title: A Small Subset of Fruitless Subesophageal Neurons Modulate Early Courtship in Drosophila
cell_type: FruM(+) Subesophageal Ganglion (SOG) Neurons (specifically mSG>4-57 and aSG>4-57 subsets)
experimental_stimulus: Virgin female presentation in behavioral assays; Ambient light vs. Infrared light (visually deficient conditions)
neural_activity: Not directly measured (no electrophysiology/calcium imaging). Anatomical characterization (confocal microscopy/immunofluorescence) revealed sexually dimorphic tritocerebral arborizations (larger in males) and descending projections to the Ventral Nerve Cord (VNC). Presynaptic markers confirmed output to VNC.
behavioral_consequence: Silencing (RNAi or Tetanus Toxin) increased courtship latency and reduced courtship index primarily in Infrared light. Silencing prevented progression to copulation in Infrared light (copulation rate dropped to ~11-18% vs ~60% in controls). Activation (TRPA1 depolarization) did not induce courtship behaviors or alter latency/index compared to controls. Co-expression with Cha-Gal80 rescued courtship defects.
ecological_context: Courtship initiation relies on chemosensory cues when visual cues are compromised (infrared). Neurons likely integrate gustatory and higher-order signals to gauge female receptivity and initiate wing extension/copulation, weighing costs of rejection in open environs.
techniques_used: ['Behavioral assays (courtship latency, index, copulation)', 'RNAi knockdown', 'Synaptic inhibition (Tetanus Toxin)', 'Neuronal activation (TRPA1)', 'Confocal microscopy', 'Immunofluorescence (anti-FruM, anti-GFP)', 'Gal4/Gal80 intersectional genetics']
summary: A small subset of FruM(+) SOG neurons (mSG and aSG) modulate early courtship initiation and copulation, primarily under visually deficient (infrared) conditions. Silencing these neurons delays courtship and prevents copulation, while anatomical data shows sexually dimorphic tritocerebral projections and descending VNC innervation.

paper_title: Neural Representations of Courtship Song in the Drosophila Brain
experimental_stimulus: Pure tones (100–900 Hz), white noise (80–1000 Hz), Drosophila melanogaster and simulans courtship songs (pulse and sine modes), synthetic pulse trains
neural_activity: Extracellular local field potential (LFP) recordings show oscillatory steady-state responses (frequency doubling); response amplitude decreases with increasing intensity at steady state; onset and pulse responses increase linearly with intensity; pulse tuning is flat (100–900 Hz)
ecological_context: Acoustic communication is based on courtship songs which facilitate mating; pulse song is more salient for mate selection
summary: JON population responses show frequency doubling at steady state and linear intensity dependence for onset and pulse stimuli.

paper_title: Neural Representations of Courtship Song in the Drosophila Brain
experimental_stimulus: Pure tones (100–900 Hz), white noise (80–1000 Hz), Drosophila melanogaster and simulans courtship songs, synthetic pulse trains
neural_activity: Whole-cell patch clamp recordings show graded (nonspiking) responses; sustained depolarization (DC component) during steady state decreases with frequency; pulse responses match JON population (low-pass filter); DC shift occurs for interpulse intervals < 50 ms
ecological_context: Central auditory neurons preserve low-frequency temporal features (pulse spacing) for analysis by downstream circuits during courtship
summary: AMMC-A1 neurons produce graded responses that low-pass filter JON input, preserving pulse timing information for courtship song analysis.

paper_title: Neural Representations of Courtship Song in the Drosophila Brain
experimental_stimulus: Sound pulses
neural_activity: Whole-cell patch clamp recordings show graded responses to sound pulses (qualitatively similar to AMMC-A1)
ecological_context: Innervate AMMC zone B; likely responsive to courtship song as part of auditory pathway
summary: AMMC-B1 neurons produce graded potential responses to sound pulses similar to AMMC-A1 neurons.

paper_title: Neural Representations of Courtship Song in the Drosophila Brain
experimental_stimulus: Sound pulses
neural_activity: Whole-cell patch clamp recordings show graded responses to sound pulses (qualitatively similar to AMMC-A1)
ecological_context: Innervate AMMC zone B; likely responsive to courtship song as part of auditory pathway
summary: AMMC-B2 neurons produce graded potential responses to sound pulses similar to AMMC-A1 neurons.

paper_title: Neural Representations of Courtship Song in the Drosophila Brain
experimental_stimulus: Sound pulses, subthreshold and suprathreshold current injection
neural_activity: Whole-cell patch clamp recordings show graded responses to sound; produce Ca2+ spikes with current injection; sound responses summate with subthreshold current to elicit spikes
ecological_context: Part of the giant fiber pathway which senses visual and mechanosensory stimuli to trigger an escape response
summary: GF neurons produce graded responses to sound and can summate sound with current to elicit spikes, though they are not activated by sound alone.

paper_title:
Investigating the Neural Control of Social Behavior in
 Drosophila ⁠melanogaster
 Using a Low-Cost Optogenetics System
experimental_stimulus: Optogenetic activation using 655 nm red light (30 Hz pulsed for 1 minute) in solitary males and male pairs; all-trans-retinal supplemented in food
neural_activity: Activation induced via red-shifted opsin CsChrimson (light-gated cation channel depolarization)
behavioral_consequence: Solitary males: Significantly elevated unilateral wing extension (courtship) during and after photostimulation compared to controls. Male pairs: Elevated wing extension during stimulation; marked increase in lunging (aggression) following stimulus offset compared to controls (transition from courtship to aggression in social context)
ecological_context: Demonstrates how a single neuronal population regulates opponent behaviors (courtship vs aggression) and persistent internal states (arousal, motivation) depending on social context and integration of external cues
techniques_used: ['Optogenetics (CsChrimson)', 'FlyRig video recording system', 'Automated behavior analysis (FlyTracker, JAABA classifiers)', 'Mann-Whitney U statistical testing']
summary: Optogenetic activation of P1a neurons in Drosophila elicits courtship wing extension in solitary males and triggers aggression (lunging) in male pairs post-stimulation, demonstrating context-dependent regulation of internal states and behavior.

paper_title: Olfactory receptor and circuit evolution promote host specialisation
experimental_stimulus: Methyl hexanoate, noni juice, noni fruit extract
neural_activity: High sensitivity to methyl hexanoate in D. sechellia compared to D. melanogaster; responses abolished in DsecOr22a mutants (electrophysiology); three-fold increase in neuron population in D. sechellia (immunohistochemistry)
behavioral_consequence: Long-range attraction to noni lost in DsecOr22a mutants (wind tunnel assay); behavior restored by expressing DsecOr22awt in mutants
ecological_context: Critical for host-specialisation on toxic noni fruit (Morinda citrifolia) in D. sechellia
techniques_used: ['Single sensillum electrophysiology', 'Wind tunnel assay', 'CRISPR/Cas9 mutagenesis', 'Allele transfer', 'Immunohistochemistry']
summary: Or22a-expressing OSNs in D. sechellia show high sensitivity to methyl hexanoate and increased population size; mutation abolishes long-range attraction to noni fruit, linking receptor tuning to host specialisation.

paper_title: Olfactory receptor and circuit evolution promote host specialisation
experimental_stimulus: 2-heptanone, noni juice, 1-hexanol
neural_activity: Stimulated by 2-heptanone; responses lost in DsecOr85c/b mutants (electrophysiology); three-fold increase in neuron population in D. sechellia
behavioral_consequence: Long-range attraction to noni lost in DsecOr85c/b mutants (wind tunnel assay); no significant effect on short-range preference in single mutants
ecological_context: Contributes to host-specialisation on toxic noni fruit in D. sechellia
summary: Or85c/b-expressing OSNs respond to 2-heptanone; their mutation impairs long-range noni attraction in D. sechellia, supporting their role in host finding.

paper_title: Olfactory receptor and circuit evolution promote host specialisation
experimental_stimulus: Hexanoic acid, butyric acid, noni juice, grape juice
neural_activity: Selective loss of hexanoic and butyric acid responses in DsecIr75b mutants (electrophysiology)
behavioral_consequence: Short-range preference for noni reduced in DsecIr75b mutants (trap assay); long-range attraction less affected compared to Ir8a mutants
ecological_context: Acid sensing facilitates short-range host finding on noni fruit
summary: Ir75b-expressing OSNs detect acids; mutation reduces short-range noni preference in D. sechellia, indicating a role in close-range host assessment.

paper_title: Olfactory receptor and circuit evolution promote host specialisation
experimental_stimulus: None (circuit tracing)
neural_activity: Anatomical tracing reveals a prominent anterio-medial branch in the lateral horn specific to D. sechellia, independent of sensory input (photoactivation, dye-filling, confocal imaging)
behavioral_consequence: Not tested directly via manipulation
ecological_context: Central circuit changes linked to olfactory specialisation towards noni fruit
summary: DM2 PNs in D. sechellia exhibit a novel lateral horn projection pattern compared to D. melanogaster, suggesting central circuit rewiring accompanies receptor adaptation for host specialisation.

paper_title: Third-Order Neurons in the Lateral Horn Enhance Bilateral Contrast of Odor Inputs Through Contralateral Inhibition in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Symmetric (bilateral) vs asymmetric (unilateral) odor stimulation using acetophenone, isoamyl acetate, and cis-vaccenyl acetate
neural_activity: Calcium signals in VLPn on the ipsilateral side to the intact antenna significantly increased after removing the contralateral antenna compared to bilateral stimulation; calcium signals in contralateral VLPn were strongly reduced (contralateral suppression) in response to asymmetric odor stimulation
ecological_context: Drosophila survival and reproduction depend on determining odor source location; lateralized odor processing is proposed to facilitate odor lateralization for olfactory navigation behavior
summary: VLPn exhibit contralateral inhibition where ipsilateral responses increase and contralateral responses decrease during asymmetric odor stimulation, enhancing odor contrast via a mechanism occurring presynaptically in the lateral horn.

paper_title: Drosophila Avoids Parasitoids by Sensing Their Semiochemicals via a Dedicated Olfactory Circuit
experimental_stimulus: Leptopilina wasp (L. boulardi) body wash, headspace; synthetic (-)-iridomyrmecin, (R)-actinidine, nepetalactol
neural_activity: Strong spiking response in GC-coupled single sensillum recording (GC-SSR); calcium-dependent fluorescence increase in DL4 glomerulus (GCaMP3.0 imaging)
behavioral_consequence: Artificial activation (ChR2) induces oviposition avoidance in females and crawling avoidance in larvae; Inactivation (Shibirets) abolishes avoidance of parasitoid odor in both stages
ecological_context: Avoiding oviposition sites and crawling away from areas inhabited by Leptopilina parasitoid wasps that kill larvae (up to 80% parasitization rate in nature)
summary: The adult ab10B OSN co-expresses Or49a and Or85f, responds to parasitoid semiochemicals, and is necessary and sufficient for innate avoidance behavior in adults and larvae.

paper_title: Drosophila Avoids Parasitoids by Sensing Their Semiochemicals via a Dedicated Olfactory Circuit
experimental_stimulus: Leptopilina wasp body wash; synthetic (-)-iridomyrmecin (no response to actinidine or nepetalactol)
neural_activity: Strong spiking response in GC-coupled extracellular recordings from dorsal organ
behavioral_consequence: Artificial activation induces crawling avoidance; Inactivation abolishes avoidance of iridomyrmecin
ecological_context: Preventing larval death from parasitoid wasp eggs (larvae become killed, not just parasitized)
summary: Larval OSNs express Or49a only (not Or85f) and detect iridomyrmecin to mediate avoidance behavior essential for survival against lethal parasitism.

paper_title: Drosophila Avoids Parasitoids by Sensing Their Semiochemicals via a Dedicated Olfactory Circuit
experimental_stimulus: (-)-iridomyrmecin, (R)-actinidine, nepetalactol
neural_activity: Calcium-dependent fluorescence changes in DL4 glomerulus; stereotypic innervation pattern to lateral horn base overlapping with DA2 PNs
behavioral_consequence: Transfers information to lateral horn region known to be involved in mediating avoidance behavior
ecological_context: Processing innate aversive valence for enemy detection within the olfactory circuit
summary: DL4 PNs relay parasitoid odor signals from the ab10B OSN to the lateral horn, a region associated with innate aversion and avoidance behaviors.

paper_title: Asymmetric neurotransmitter release enables rapid odor lateralization in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Odors (fermented peach volatiles, pentanoic acid, ethyl acetate, trans-2-hexenal); Optogenetic light stimulation (ChR2 activation)
neural_activity: Each ORN spike releases ~40% more neurotransmitter from the ipsilateral axon branch compared to the contralateral branch; Calcium signals (GCaMP) in axon terminals significantly larger ipsilaterally; Synaptobrevin fluorescence 41% higher ipsilaterally
behavioral_consequence: Optogenetic activation of DM1 ORNs on one antenna elicited turning toward that antenna; Flies detected 5% asymmetry in total ORN input and turned toward odor within 70 ms (faster than stride period)
ecological_context: Navigating toward an odor by comparing signals from two antennae and turning in the direction of the stronger signal
summary: ORNs exhibit asymmetric neurotransmitter release (~40% more ipsilaterally), enabling rapid odor lateralization and turning behavior in less time than a stride.

paper_title: Asymmetric neurotransmitter release enables rapid odor lateralization in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Odors (pentanoic acid, ethyl acetate) with lateralized stimulation (one antenna removed)
neural_activity: Ipsilateral PNs fired 1.0-2.5 ms earlier and at 30-50% higher rates than contralateral PNs; Ipsilateral EPSCs were ~39-40% larger than contralateral EPSCs
behavioral_consequence: Ipsilateral firing asymmetry corresponds to the detection of odor lateralization and subsequent turning behavior; Behavior persists even with GABAergic inhibition blocked
ecological_context: Encoding lateralized odor information to guide navigation toward odor sources
summary: PNs encode lateralized odor input with faster and stronger ipsilateral activity, demonstrating that small differences in spike timing and rate produce reliable olfactory behavior.

paper_title: Origins of correlated activity in an olfactory circuit
experimental_stimulus: Odors (benzaldehyde, butyric acid, ethyl acetate, linalool, methyl salicylate, 1-octanol, pentyl acetate); spontaneous activity
neural_activity: Homotypic PNs in the same glomerulus exhibit highly correlated spontaneous and odor-evoked spike timing; odor stimulation increases correlated activity. Spontaneous EPSCs occur synchronously. Ipsilateral homotypic PNs show reciprocal electrical and chemical coupling. Axonal projections reconverge in the lateral horn but are non-overlapping in the mushroom body.
ecological_context: Discriminating a weak stimulus from spontaneous activity
summary: Homotypic Projection Neurons in the same glomerulus show correlated spontaneous and odor-evoked spikes due to shared ORN input and reciprocal PN-PN connections, with axonal projections reconverging in the lateral horn.

paper_title: Origins of correlated activity in an olfactory circuit
experimental_stimulus: Odors (1-butanol, ethyl acetate); spontaneous activity
neural_activity: ORNs fire spontaneously with irregular inter-spike intervals. Paired extracellular recordings show spikes occur independently (uncorrelated) in homotypic ORNs.
summary: Olfactory Receptor Neurons fire spontaneously with irregular intervals but spike independently in pairs, indicating shared input drives correlation in downstream PNs rather than coordinated ORN input.

paper_title: A circuit supporting concentration-invariant odor perception in Drosophila
experimental_stimulus: Ethyl butyrate concentration series (10^-7 to 10^-2 dilution); Panel of 22 odors (10^-2 dilution)
neural_activity: Calcium imaging (G-CaMP) at axon terminals showed differential response thresholds: Or42b most sensitive (10^-4), Or42a intermediate (10^-3), Or35a least sensitive (10^-2). No difference in sensitivity between wild-type and single OSN-functional animals.
behavioral_consequence: Larvae with a single functional OSN showed attraction only within narrow concentration ranges matching the OSN's sensitivity. Wild-type larvae (21 functional OSNs) showed attraction across a 500-fold concentration range. Single OSN larvae showed avoidance/circling at high concentrations.
ecological_context: Ethyl butyrate is an ester found in fruits; larvae feed on yeast growing on rotting fruit or plant parts. Mechanism allows tolerance of high odor concentrations while ignoring low concentrations.
techniques_used: ['Calcium imaging (G-CaMP)', 'Chemotaxis assays (single and multiple odor source devices)']
summary: Ethyl butyrate-tuned OSNs (Or35a, Or42a, Or42b) have distinct concentration thresholds. Wild-type larvae maintain attraction across a wide range, whereas single OSN larvae are restricted to narrow ranges and avoid high concentrations.

paper_title: A circuit supporting concentration-invariant odor perception in Drosophila
